## Chapter 2.1 Breathlessness and other respiratory symptoms

### 2.1 Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

##### 2.1.1 Der Doctor

Re: Mr John Thom, aged 56 years

Thank you for seeing this man who has become progressively breathless over six months. Three months ago he developed a lower respiratory tract infection that responded to amoxicillin and doxycycline but he continues to be breathless, particularly on exertion. He smokes and I wonder if he is developing chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. His peak flow is slightly lower than predicted at 420 l/min, although salutamol and pterutropium inhalers appear to have made little difference. A course of prednisone did not improve his breathing. Your assessment is appreciated.

##### 2.1.2 Young's sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr John Thom is a 56-year-old man who has been troubled by progressive breathlessness on exertion for the past 6 months, with a dry cough. He saw his GP 3 months ago when he developed purulent sputum and although this settled over about 2 weeks with two different courses of antibiotics, his breathlessness and dry cough persisted. He is not breathless at rest, or lying down. He thinks he may have lost a little weight, but has no other notable symptoms. He has smoked 20 cigarettes a day since he was a young man and attributes his symptoms to this. He has no other past medical history of note. He has worked for 30 years as a road-builder. He has never worked in shipyards or building construction and to his knowledge has not been exposed to asbestos. He is not prone to allergies. He has owned the same parrot for the last 8 years and has no other pets. Inhalers have not helped his breathlessness. A course of steroids helped his breathing only marginally. He is worried that if his breathing deteriorates he will no longer be able to visit his brother, who lives in America.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

##### 2.1.3 Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

##### 2.1.4 Elicit details of breathlessness.

Find out:

If it is chronic, and, if so, if it is progressive

If it came on suddenly or insidiously

If it is continuous, discrete or with stepwise episodes of worsening (pulmonary emboli; infective exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD))

If it is present only on exertion, or also at rest, and if there is orthopnoea (left ventricular failure).

##### 2.1.5 Ask about trigger factors.

Ask about exercise, cold, smoking and allergens.

##### 2.1.6 Explore associated symptoms.

Ask about:

Cough, with sputum (characteristics and volumes - large volumes suggest bronchiectasis) or dry (interstitial lung disease)

Haemopptosis (lung cancer, pneumonia, pulmonary tuberculosis, bronchiectasis, pulmonary embolus, vasculitis, pulmonary haemorrhage)

Wheeze (asthma, COPD)

Symptoms suggesting a cardiac cause (exertional chest pain, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, ankle swelling - which may also be due to cor pulmonale)

Alamm symptoms for malignancy such as weight loss, anorexia, haemoptysis or hoarseness.

##### 2.1.7 Patient perspective

Explore his concerns about the limitations caused by breathlessness, and reassure him that you will do all you can to improve his breathing to enable him to fly.

##### 2.1.8 Past medical history

Ask breathless patients about previous respiratory diseases, such as tuberculosis or asthma, cardiovascular disease or risk factors, and atopy (personal or family history of asthma, eczema or allergic rhinitis). 

### Drug and allergy history

Many drugs may contribute to dyspnoea, cough or wheeze; specifically ask about non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors.

#### Family history

Ask about atopy or respiratory disease.

#### Social history

Ask about:

* when started/stopped, quantity and pack years)
* Occupations past and present (specifically about the shipyard and building industries for asbestos exposure and road-building for silicosis)
* Travel (atypical pneumonia)
* Pets (cats and dogs commonly trigger asthma, and birds can trigger atypical pneumonia and extrinsic allergic alveolitis).

Consider the effects of symptoms on daily activities and the effects of daily activities on symptoms.

#### Summary Box - Assessment and Plan

* Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) seems likely in a smoker, but pulmonary fibrosis should always be suspected in a patient with progressive exertional dyspnoea and early on it may be the only feature of disease, with examination and chest X-ray unremarkable; the occupation is important here.
* Pneumonia that fails to resolve in a smoker should always prompt investigation for possible lung cancer. Exertional dyspnoea also has cardiac causes including ischaemic and valvular heart disease.
* Chest X-ray and spirometry are mandatory initial investigations in suspected COPD or fibrosis.
* Spirometry would confirm obstructive or restrictive lung disease.
* Exertional breathlessness can be the result of cardiac rather than respiratory disease; coronary disease may present as exertional dyspnoea rather than chest pain and if no persuasive respiratory cause is found then exercise tolerance testing is advised.

## Discussion

### What is silicosis?

Silicosis is a pneumoconiosis caused by inhalation of crystalline silicon dioxide (quartz), which is highly fibrogenic. Many occupations (e.g. stonemasons, road-builders) still involve exposure to quartz dust, which is concentrated in rocks such as granite and sandstone. Particles are engulfed by macrophages and swept to the hilar nodes where inflammation, fibrosis and blockage to lymphatic drainage occur. A similar process can occur in the lungs, provoking small fibrotic nodules. Silicosis does not usually occur with brief exposure; most patients have been exposed for years or decades. With high exposure, small lung nodules can coalesce into large, destructive masses with surrounding bullae. Nodules continue to grow and merge, even after exposure has ceased, causing dyspnoea. Very high exposure occasionally causes an acute inflammatory destructive process with respiratory failure in the absence of nodules. The chest X-ray usually shows 'eggshell' hilar calcification and small (< 5 mm) diffuse nodules. The history, chest X-ray and workplace inspection by an occupational physician usually suffice for diagnosis. Biopsy is seldom necessary, but reveals quartz particles.

### What is meant by the terms total lung capacity (TLC), vital capacity (VC) and residual volume (RV)?

These are shown in Figure 2.1.

* TLC is the total volume of air in the lungs at full inspiration. TLC is limited by the elasticity of the thoracic cage and the fibrous and elastic tissues of the lungs, and by inspiratory muscle strength. TLC varies little with age in healthy people.
* VC is the volume of air expired from full inspiration to full expiration. VC varies widely between healthy people depending upon height, gender and race (3-6 litres) and tends to decrease with age.
* RV is the volume always left in the lung after breathing out the VC. RV is reached when the ribs cannot move closer together. It tends to increase with age and in obstructive lung disease when airways close early in expiration.

### Which dynamic volumes are helpful in pulmonary function testing?

The above are static volumes. What we measure mostly in practice are dynamic volumes (volumes which change) because patients are breathing!

* _Forced vital capacity (FVC)_ is the forced maximal volume of air expired from full inspiration (TLC) to full expiration (RV), the patient breathing out as hard and for as long as they can. FVC depends

Figure 2.1: Useful lung volumes. to an extent on the duration of expiration, forced expiratory time varying from normally less than 5 seconds to over 20 seconds in airflow obstruction. Attempts to standardise the FVC using the forced expiratory volume at 6 seconds (FEV6) have been hindered by the limitations of FEV6; although FEV6 (and indeed slow and relaxed VC) seems more reproducible than FVC, it considerably underestimates FVC.
* _Forced expiratory volume over 1 second (FEV1)_ is the amount of air expired over the first second of FVC. FEV1 is normally > 75-80% of normal VC. FEV1 is a useful indicator of the severity of obstructive lung disease and a screening test for cardiorespiratory health as it correlates with maximal exercise capacity.

The normal _FEV1_/_FVC_ ratio is around 70-80%. It is highest in young non-smokers, and declines with age, although < 65% under age 65 is definitely abnormal. Spirometry measures this and differentiates two major groups of lung disease: obstructive or restrictive (Table 2.2).

A mixed obstructive/restrictive pattern occurs when both the FEV1/FVC and TLC are low, typically in mild COPD with interstitial lung disease.

### What happens to flow rates and flow rate curves in obstructive and restrictive lung diseases?

As well as plotting expiratory flow rate on a volume-time curve, it is possible to plot flow rate (e.g. expiratory flow rate during a FVC manoeuvre) against lung volume (which changes as a person breathes out), as shown in Figure 2.2.

It may seem from Figure 2.2 that the expiratory flow rate in restrictive lung diseases is decreased, but if we correct this for the decreased lung volume in restrictive lung diseases then it is normal or even increased. This is because a fibrotic lung provides more traction than usual on airways, holding them open. Nevertheless, lungs are shrunken and airways are narrowed, increasing their

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c}  & **OLD** & **RLD** \\ Diseases & COPD & **Intrapulmonary disease:** \\  & Asthma & Intestinal lung disease \\  & & Lung or lobe resection \\  & & **Extrapulmonary disease:** \\  & Thoracic deformity, pleural disease or inspiratory muscle weakness \\ Static volumes & There is air trapping or hypentiflation & The lungs are small and fibrotic \\  & TLC \(\downarrow\) & TLC \(\downarrow\) \\  & RY \(\uparrow\) (early small airway closure and VC \(\downarrow\)) & RY \(\downarrow\) (alveolar obliterated) \\  & Tidal volume can vary widely & Tidal volume often \(\downarrow\) \\ Dynamic volumes & FEV, markedly \(\downarrow\) (\(<\) 80% predicted in COPD) & FEV, \(\downarrow\) \\  & FVC normal or \(\downarrow\) (because emptying is delayed by airway narrowing and small airways close early in expiration) & FVC \(\downarrow\) (VC is low in RLD, not because of airway problems but because lung volumes are small) \\  & FEV, \(\uparrow\) / FVC \(\downarrow\) (\(<\) 70% diagnostic) & FEV, \(\uparrow\) / FVC normal or high (\(>\) 80–85%) \\  & Expiratory flow rates \(\downarrow\) & Normal \\ Transfer factor & \(\downarrow\) in COPD, normal in asthma & Normal or \(\downarrow\) \\ COPD, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease; FEV, forced expiratory volume over 1 second; FVC, forced vital capacity; RV, residual volume; TLC, total lung capacity, VC, vital capacity. & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.2: Obstructive lung disease (OLD) and restrictive lung disease (RLD)

Figure 2.2: Expiratory flow rate plotted against lung volume–normal. FEV, forced expiratory volume over 1 second; FVC, forced vital capacity, OLD, obstructive lung disease; RLD, restrictive lung disease; RV, residual volume; TLC, total lung capacity. resistance and hence the work of breathing and sensation of inspiratory effort or breathlessness.

In obstructive lung disease, expiratory flow rates are decreased. Although the lungs are hyperinflated, inflammation, oedema and mucus in the smaller airways cause these to close earlier than usual despite normal traction from the lung parenchyma attempting to keep them open.

Note from Figure 2.2 that expiratory flow rates in normal and diseased lungs vary according to lung volume. For example, in normal lungs the maximum flow rate, about 6 litres per second, occurs when about 4 litres of air are left in the lungs. This _peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR)_ is normally 300-700 1/min, varying between people as for VC, but reproducible to within 5% for an individual. PEFR tends to be reached shortly after the onset of expiration and subsequently declines as airways narrow and elastic recoil, the effective driving pressure for maximal flow, declines as the lungs get smaller, PEFR reflects airway calibre and respiratory effort, and is most useful for monitoring asthma.

Sometimes, rather than plotting flow rate against volume, flow rate is plotted against percentage VC. This is essentially the same thing simply plotting flow rate through the various stages of exhaling the VC. For example, when 75% of the VC has been exhaled, the flow rate is called Vmax75% or forced expiratory flow (FEF)75. These measurements are less useful to interpret than the shapes of the curves produced, the former exhibiting large inter-individual variation but the latter characteristic in normality and disease and reproducible within individuals.

Note from Figure 2.3 that in healthy individuals the maximal expiratory flow rate (MEFR) decreases steadily throughout forced expiration - as the lungs empty, the airways decrease in size. Note also that the flow rate is low in obstructive lung disease at all stages of exhaling the VC, in other words at all lung volumes. Also note that in obstructive lung disease the curve is scooped out. This dip in MEFR during the middle of forced expiration is one of the earliest signs of small airway damage and is seen in young smokers before clinical features of disease, often with a normal IVC and FEV1.

### What do you understand by compliance and elastic recoil?

Compliance is essentially a measure of the 'give' of the lung when expanding, and elastic recoil is a measure of its ability to shrink to its original size again, like a deflating balloon. Obstructive lung disease lungs are very compliant, but have too little elastic recoil because there is too much airspace and not enough parenchyma. In emphysema elastic recoil is particularly reduced, and FEV1 is reduced even when the airways are patent. Restrictive lung disease lungs are not very compliant (they are too stiff), having too much recoil, if anything.

### What are flow-volume loops?

Complex mathematics has been applied to produce the shapes of the expiratory flow curves for normal and diseased lungs. Fortunately, physicians do not need to understand the mathematics but need merely to recognise the shapes of certain curves and what they imply. Curves have been created not just for expiration, but also for inspiration. Therefore, as well as flow-volume curves, flow-volume loops (Fig. 2.4) tracking the full inspiration-expiration cycle can be plotted, and these too have characteristic shapes. The expiratory and inspiratory components of the curves are different shapes because of differing forces applied to airways inside and outside the thorax; intrathoracic airways are squeezed during forced expiration, but distended during forced inspiration, whilst extrathoracic airways are 'blown out' on expiration and 'sucked in' on expiration. Flow limitation in expiration is by airway closure; flow limitation in inspiration is by the maximal force and speed of contraction of muscles, and although extrathoracic airways are sucked in, they never become flow limiting.

### What do you understand by the term transfer factor and which diseases affect it?

The transfer factor (TLCO) measures total transfer of carbon monoxide across the lung during 10 seconds of breath-holding after full inspiration. It is a measure of alveolar-capillary exchange (surface area and thickness) or effective ventilation/perfusion (V/Q) matching. TLCO is also known as diffusing capacity (DLCO). The KCO, or transfer coefficient, corrects TLCO for lung volume. For example, a TLCO that is half that predicted may occur after a pneumocometory but the KCO will be normal.

A decreased TLCO (and KCO) occurs in COPD, interstitial lung disease, pulmonary vascular disease (pulmonary emboli, pulmonary hypertension, pulmonary oedema), right-to-left shunts and anaemia. In emphysema, TLCO is misleadingly elevated because CO lingers in the bullae,

Figure 2.3: Flow rate variation in normal lung and in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). when in fact the obliterated alveoli cause markedly diminished gas exchange.

CO has a high affinity for blood cells, and so transfer is apparently increased in pulmonary haemorrhage, e.g. in the setting of pulmonary vasculitis. TLCO and KCO are also increased in polycythaemia, left-to-right shunts and exercise.

A decreased TLCO and normal KCO is the result of extrapulmonary restriction or 'functional' volume loss, e.g. neuromuscular disease, or volume loss post-pneumonectomy.

### What is respiratory failure?

Arterial \(p\text{CO}_{2}\left( p_{\text{a}}\text{CO}_{2}\right)\) is normally about 5 kPa (40 mmHg) and is regulated by chemoreceptors. Arterial \(p\text{O}_{2}\left( p_{\text{a}}\text{O}_{2}\right)\) is more variable, between 10 and 16 kPa (80-120 mmHg). In type 1 respiratory failure, \(p_{\text{a}}\text{O}_{2}\) is < 8 kPa (60 mmHg) and \(p_{\text{a}}\text{CO}_{2}\) is normal or decreased. Causes include interstitial lung disease, pulmonary vascular disease, asthma and COPD. In type 2 (hypoventlatory) respiratory failure, \(p_{\text{a}}\text{CO}_{2}\) is increased to > 6 kPa (50 mmHg). Causes include COPD, asthma in a tiring patient, and neuromuscular disease.

## Case 2.2 Asthma

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the respiratory clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

Dear Doctor

Re: Mrs Claire Pickles, aged 30 years

Thank you for seeing this lady with episodes of cough, breathlessness, wheeze and chest 'tightness' for the last few months. Although her symptoms seem to be responding to salutamol and steroid inhalers 1 am always a little apprehensive about making a new diagnosis of asthma in adults. I am grateful for your advice as to the likelihood of the diagnosis.

Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mrs Claire Pickles is a 30-year-old woman who has had episodes of cough, breathlessness, wheeze and chest 'tightness' for the last 4 months, partially responsive to a blue 'treatment' and a brown 'prevention' inhaler. She had eczema as a child but no history of apparent asthma until now and has no other significant past medical history. She moved into a country cottage 6 months ago with her husband and two sons. The cottage is extremely dusty and

Figure 2.4: Flow-volume loops (maximal flow). (A) Normal. Normally, maximal expiratory flow rate (MFR) decreases during expiration (above), but inspiration is more rapid. Maximal flow rates are much greater than those of tidal breathing (not shown). (B) Obstructive lung disease (OLD) and restrictive lung disease (RLD). In OLD, MFR decreases during the middle of forced expiration (above) because the small airways collapse just after the onset of forced expiration. For this reason, forced expiration may be less effective than quiet breathing. (C) Fixed upper airway (extrathoracic) obstruction (e.g. tracheal stenosis). Here, MFR and maximal inspiratory flow rate (MFR) are reduced equally because of non-compliant or fixed extrathoracic obstruction. (D) Intrathoracic variable obstruction (e.g. tracheobronchial collapse as a consequence of emphysema, tracheomalacia, rare bronchial cartilage disorders). Here, the main problem is with ‘breathing out’, MFR being reduced from the start of expiration (MFR may be constant for the first few hundred millilitres of expiration, after which it behaves like fixed upper airway obstruction). (E) Extrathoracic variable obstruction (e.g. bilateral vocal cord palsy, gotre, acromegally, laryngeal polyp, episodic in obstructive sleep opnoea). Here, the main problem is with ‘breathing in’, MFR being reduced but not MFR, as extrathoracic airways are narrowed (but remain compliant or flexible) by external compression, luminal obstruction or wall weakness, and flow limited on inspiration but not expiration. they are in the process of renovating and rebuilding some of it. Local cats tend to wander in. During house moving she injured her knee and has taken regular ibuprofen for a few months. She cannot understand why she has asthma, as she does not smoke.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

The history should cover similar points to Case 2.1. Asthma not uncommonly starts in adults, and not always with identifiable triggers, and here symptoms are characteristic. She should be asked about diurnal variation of cough (often worse at night and the early hours of the morning. e.g. 02.30-05.00 when endogenous steroids are at their lowest levels), wheeze and shortness of breath. Clinical features that influence the probability of asthma are listed in Box 2.4.

#### Explore precipitating factors

These include pollens, dust (house dust mite), animal allergens (cats!), pollens, exercise, viral infection, chemicals (fumes, sprays and perfumes) and irritants (including cigarettes!). Has she, for example, from recently moving house, come into increased contact with more house dust mites? Has she bought a new pet! Has she taken up a new form of exercise! Has she come into contact with any new materials at work? Has she moved in with a smoker?

#### Patient perspective

Explore her ideas about asthma precipitants.

#### Past medical history

Ask about previous hospital admissions and particularly whether or not assisted ventilation has ever been needed.

#### Drug and allergy history

Sometimes symptoms appear or worsen with aspirin, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, beta blockers or angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors. Ask specifically about cumulative oral steroid use with consideration of the need for osteoporosis prophylaxis.

#### Family history

Ask about a personal or family history of asthma or atopy (eczema, allergic rhinitis).

#### Social history

Exclusion of smoking is of course essential, as is an occupational history. In addition to a full home and work history (occupational asthma should always be considered in adult-onset asthma, symptoms worse at work), the following three questions should be asked about the last week/month:

1. Have you had difficulty sleeping because of your asthma symptoms (including cough)?
2. Have you had your usual asthma symptoms during the day (cough, wheeze, chest tightness or breathlessness)?
3. Has your asthma interfered with your usual activities (e.g. housework, work, school)?

## Discussion

### What is asthma?

Asthma is characterised by airway inflammation (often failing to settle between attacks and now appreciated as a core problem in asthma) giving rise to reversible airway narrowing, and airway hyper-responsiveness giving rise to cough, wheeze and dyspnoea. Alveoli and lung parenchyma are spared. 

## Summary Box - Assessment and Plan

### What do you know about the pathophysiology of asthma?

Asthma and allergic diseases are increasing in prevalence in the Western world, and asthma is predominantly such a disease.

#### Atopy and allergy

Atopy refers to the predisposition to generate allergic antibodies (i.e. immunoglobulin E; IgE) in response to environmental triggers. The immune system responds to foreign antigens by their being presented to naive T-helper (Th) lymphocytes, which differentiate either to a Th1 response driving intense inflammation or to a Th2 response driving B lymphocytes to switch to IgE production. The optimal scenario is a well-balanced ability to produce Th1 and Th2 responses.

Allergy is associated with a Th2-weighted imbalance.

Atopic predisposition may start in early life, with impaired interferon-g (IFN-g) production leading to impaired suppression of Th2 activity involved in IgE-mediated allergy. Healthy neonates have a weak Th2-based immune response that is subsequently suppressed in early life. Exposure to bacteria in early life may increase IFN-g levels, lending weight to the 'hygiene' hypothesis, which suggests that reduced exposure to bacteria together with antibiotics and modern infant diets result in the persistence of a dominant Th2 pathway. Mycobacterial species are known to stimulate powerful IFN-g production and research has considered bacterial vaccines to promote switching from Th2 to Th1 pathways.

#### Allergens in asthma

In asthma, allergens and other unknown antigens are presented by antigen-presenting cells and activate Th2 lymphocytes to produce the Th2 profile of cytokines including interleukins (ILs) 3, 4, 5, 9, 10 and 13. Acutely, these promote histamine release from mast cells. IL-4 and IL-13 have sustained roles that include collaboration with B cells to promote class-switching and release of IgE (which promotes degranulation of mast cell contents - histamine, prostaglandins and leukotrienes), upregulation of adhesion molecules on the capillary endothelium of airway mucosa, and actions on epithelial cells, fibroblasts and airway smooth muscle cells to produce airway narrowing and remodelling. IL-5 promotes eosinophil recruitment to airway mucosa. Eosinophils damage mucosa by protein release, and exacerbate bronchospasm, vascular leakage and oedema (inflammation) by leukotriene release; they also contribute to remodelling through release of fibrogenic mediators such as transforming growth factor-b. It is increasingly appreciated that this intense Th2 eosinophilic activity can lead to persistent inflammation and airway remodelling that may cause accelerated lung function decline.

### Are there different types of asthma?

Asthma is a spectrum of disease with asectrum of triggers.
* Allergic asthma is most common, in which IgE promotes release of mast cell granules immediately and over minutes (e.g. histamine, prostaglandins, leukotrienes), provoking nasal congestion, a runny nose, itchy and watery eyes, wheezing and bronchospasm, followed by, over hours, Th2 cytokine and eosinophil activity. It may be associated with eosinophils and high blood levels of specific IgE and positive skin prick tests to allergens including grass (particularly perennial rye and timothy grass) and tree pollens, house dust mites, cats, dogs, bird feathers, and sometimes _Aspergillus fumigatus,_ the last sometimes being an allergen for asthma without provoking frank allergic bronchopulmonarypergillosis.
* Food allergens may be non-IgE mediated, and include sulphites and salicylates.
* Occupational asthma is triggered by a wide range of chemicals and foodstuffs.
* Exercise-induced asthma is mostly an expression of poorly controlled asthma.
* Thunderstorm asthma occurs later in the year than many other seasonal triggers and is due to wet conditions affecting activity of the fungus _Alternaria alternate_; it is increasingly recognised and may result in very severe asthma of rapid onset with numerous presentations to hospital in triggering weather conditions.

### How is asthma diagnosed?

Where asthma is highly probable a therapeutic trial is appropriate. Where the diagnosis is not absolutely clear, spirometry should be performed to confirm forced expiratory volume in 1 second / forced vital capacity (\(\text{FEV}_{\text{i}}/\text{FVC}\)) < 0.7, followed by reversibility testing.

* Asthma is most commonly diagnosed by the peak expiratory flow (PEF) exhibiting > 20% diurnal variation on >= 3 days in a week for 2 weeks.
* An increase in \(\text{FEV}_{\text{i}}\geq 15\%\) (and 200 ml) after a short-acting b-agonist (e.g. salbutamol 400 mg by metered dose inhaler (MDI) + spacer or 2.5 mg by nebuliser) or steroids (prednisolone 30 mg/day for 14 days) is also diagnostic.
* A decrease in FEV1 >= 15% after 6 minutes of exercise (running) or a bronchoconstrictor methacholine (or in the future mannitol) challenge may help in difficult cases.

Although percentage changes are quoted, absolute improvements in FEV1 of > 400 ml with bronchodilator are more specific for asthma; steroid responsiveness is often associated with higher levels of eosinophilic airways inflammation. No significant reversibility suggests chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).

What do you know about the British Thoracic Society and Scottish Intercollegiate Guideline Network (BTS/SIGN) guidelines for the management of chronic asthma in adults?

Diagnosis may be of high, intermediate or low probability. A treatment trial is indicated for high probability and investigations for alternative causes for low probability. For intermediate probability, especially where FEV1/FVC is > 0.7, further investigation is preferred alongside a treatment trial for a defined period.

### Standard therapies

Treatment aims are to control asthma, with control defined as (with minimal side effects):

* No daytime symptoms
* No night-time awakening due to asthma
* No need for rescue medication
* No exacerbations
* No limitations on activity including exercise
* Normal lung function (in practical terms FEV1 and/or PEF > 80% predicted or best)
* Minimal side effects from medication.

Treatment is stepwise (Table 2.3), starting at the most appropriate step to achieve early control, stepping up as necessary and down when control is good. Patients should be maintained at the lowest possible doses of inhaled steroid, but reductions should be slow, by around 25-50% at 3-monthly intervals.

### Omalizumab and emerging therapies

Asthma is often atopic, with aeroallergens interacting with IgE causing release of inflammatory mediators. Evidence is accumulating that the extent of eosinophilic airway inflammation predicts future exacerbations. Single inhaler therapy (e.g. with Symbicort - butosedonide/formoterol) appears to improve lung function, reduce exacerbations and produce better symptom control.

Omalizumab is a humanised monoclonal antibody to IgE given subcutaneously, the dose determined by baseline IgE and body weight. It may be used in patients not controlled by optimised standard therapy with positive skin pick tests or specific IgE to perennial aeroallergen(s). The place of other biological agents in asthma is undetermined, and immunosuppressant drugs such as ciclosporin and methotrexate may be considered under specialist supervision.

Increased airway smooth muscle mass may be an important factor in severe or fatal asthma. Bronchial thermoplasty - controlled thermal energy delivered to the airway wall during several bronchoscopy procedures - produces prolonged reduction of smooth muscle mass and can reduce symptoms, reduce use of medication and improve quality of life and lung function.

## How would you explain to a patient how to use an MDI?

### Instructions are as follows:

* Shake the inhaler and remove the dust cap
* Breathe out gently and fully
* Place the mouthpiece between your teeth and seal your lips around it
* Breathe in steadily, and at the moment you start breathing in ....
* Press the inhaler to release the dose immediately after beginning to breathe in and keep breathing in to full inspiration (until your lungs are full)
* Remove the inhaler from your mouth and hold your breath for 10 seconds or as long as is comfortable
* If a second dose is needed, wait for 30-60 seconds.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Step 1: Mild intermittent asthma & Inhaled short-acting β2-agonist as required \\ Step 2: Regular prevention therapy & Add inhaled steroid 200–800 μM μg / day (bedometasone or equivalent) \\ Step 3: Add-on therapy & Add inhaled long-acting β2-agonist: if good response continue; if benefit but incomplete control increase inhaled steroid dose to 800 μM; and if no response stop and increase inhaled steroid to 800 μM; if control remains inadequate consider other therapies such as a leukotriene receptor antagonist or SR theophylline \\ Step 4: Persistent poor control & Consider trials of increasing inhaled steroid to 2000 μM qualify or addition of a fourth drug such as a leukotriene receptor antagonist, SR theophylline or a β2-agonist tablet \\ Step 5: Continuous or frequent use of oral steroids & Daily steroid tablets in lowest dose possible oral steroids \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.3: Stepwise approach to asthma in adults 

### How should patients with asthma be monitored?

It is unclear how best to monitor patients, although regular follow-up by doctors and nurses trained in asthma management may be beneficial, but asthma control can be assessed by well-validated questions, such as those in the asthma control test with a specific scoring system:

How much of the time did your asthma keep you from getting as much done at work, school, or home?

How often have you had shortness of breath?

How often did your asthma symptoms wake you up at night or earlier than usual in the morning?

How often have you used your rescue inhaler or nebulised drug?

How would you rate your asthma control?

### How would you recognise acute severe asthma (ASA) and life-threatening asthma?

Features of moderate exacerbations, ASA and life-threatening asthma are listed in Table 2.4.

### How would you manage acute asthma?

High-flow oxygen and b2-agonist bronchodilators by nebuliser are administered to all patients. Nebulised piratropium bromide (0.5 mg 4- to 6-hourly) may be added to the b2-agonist for ASA or life-threatening asthma or when initial response to b2-agonists is poor. Systemic steroid (e.g. prednisolone 40-50 mg daily) is administered to all patients. A single dose of intravenous magnesium sulphate (1.2-2 g by 20-minute infusion) is considered in ASA without a good initial response or in life-threatening asthma. Referral to the ITU should be made in those circumstances outlined in Box 2.5.

### Does asthma management differ in pregnancy?

The b2-agonists, steroids and theophylline are each used as normal, but leukotriene receptor antagonists have not been adequately researched. Poorly controlled asthma is worse for the baby than any risk from oral steroids. ASA management is unchanged.

### When would you consider it safe to discharge a patient from hospital after ASA?

Discharge is appropriate under those criteria listed in Box 2.6.

### What might you consider when a patient has chronic asthma that is difficult to control?

There is no clear definition of difficult asthma but it is reasonable to consider it when there are persistent symptoms and frequent exacerbations despite being treated at steps 4 or 5 of the guidelines. Patients typically need high-dose inhaled steroids (>=800 mg bcometasone equivalent), a long-acting b2-agonist, plus add-on treatment. Several considerations are needed with difficult asthma.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Moderate & Increasing symptoms \\ exacerbation & PFF 50–75\% of best or predicted \\ of asthma & No features of ASA \\ Acute severe & Any one of: \\  asthma & PFF 33–50\% of best or predicted \\ Respiratory rate ≥ 25 / min \\ Heart rate ≥ 110 / min \\  inability to complete sentences in one breath \\ Life-threatening & PFF \textless{} 33\% of best or predicted \\ asthma & S\textless{} 92\% \\ Respiratory failure on arterial blood gases – \\ \(p_{\text{0}}\)2 \textless{} 8 kPa (rising \(p_{\text{0}}\)CO2 a sign of \\ near-fatal asthma) \\ Silent chest \\ Cyanosis \\ Poor respiratory effort \\ Arrhythmia \\ Exhaustion, altered conscious level \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.4: Features of asthma of varying severity 

#### Alternative diagnosis

These include cardiac diseases such as heart failure and other lung diseases, including the systemic vasculitis Churg-Strauss syndrome, the severity of which may be masked by steroids. Investigations that may be needed in patients with difficult asthma may include:

* _Blood tests_: Eosinophil count (and induced sputum eosinophil count), total serum IgE and allergen skin prick testing (or radiodallergosorbent test) especially to _Aspergillus_, _Aspergillus_ serology, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody, \(\alpha_{1}\)-antitrypsin
* _Pulmonary function_: Spirometry, flow-volume loops, bronchodilator reversibility with 400 mg of inhaled salbutamol if airflow obstruction present, and if airflow obstruction absent consideration of exercise testing or bronchial challenge testing using inhaled methacholine or mannitol, and exhaled nitric oxide (raised in eosinophilic airway inflammation)
* _Radiography_: Chest radiography, high-resolution computed tomography of the thorax (for bronchiectasis or interstitial lung disease), computed tomography of sinuses (for sinus disease after nasal endoscopy or a trial of nasal steroids)
* _Other tests (as clinically indicated)_: Nasal endoscopy (for polyps and rhinosinusitis), bronchoscopy (for tumours or foreign bodies), laryngoscopy during symptomatic episodes (for vocal cord dysfunction), echocardiogram (where wheeze may be 'cardiac asthma').

#### Coexisting conditions

Asthma often occurs alongside other conditions, and bronchiectasis, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, rhinosinusitis and psychological disorders are more common in people with frequent versus infrequent severe exacerbations of asthma. Vocal cord dysfunction (paradoxical adduction during inspiration) is a disorder that can mimic or coexist with asthma.

#### Persistence of aggravating factors

These include:

* Psychological factors and stress
* Upper airway disease (asthma and allergic rhinitis are a continuation of the same inflammatory process)
* Allergy
* Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease
* Drug side effects
* Occupational exposure
* Smoking
* Obesity.

#### Concordance

Reasons for this include treatment considered unnecessary or ineffective, poor understanding of the regimen and poor inhaler technique

## Is asthma preventable?

Breastfeeding, parental non-smoking and weight reduction may be important in primary prevention. Nutritional intake in pregnancy, dietary manipulation, the role of microbiological exposure and immunotherapy are all being studied. Not smoking is helpful in secondary prevention, and allergen or air pollution avoidance seems logical and may be useful where dust mites are a cause, although studies on pet removal are not robust; there may be a role for immunotherapy.

### Are inhaled medications dangerous?

Any inhaled substance (not necessarily the drug itself) will cause bronchospasm in some people; b-agonists can cause severe exacerbations and deaths in asthma, but not in COPD. Anticholinergics are generally very safe in COPD with small concern about vascular events. Inhaled corticosteroid predisposes to pneumonia in COPD but not asthma, and benefits outweigh risks.

## Additional problems

### Case 2.3 Dyspepsia and Upper Gastrointestinal bleeding

### Candidate information

### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

### Scenario

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mrs Elizabeth Richards is a 67-year-old woman who is anaemic. She has lost around 1 stone in weight over the last few months and feels that her abdomen is getting 'fuller'. She has a little discomfort in her chest after eating but no vomiting or alteration in bowel habit. She has type 2 diabetes, which is controlled by diet. Four years ago she had heartburn and endoscopy revealed moderately severe oesophagitis and she has taken omeprazole since. She takes no other medications, and in particular has not taken non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs). She does not drink alcohol or smoke. She has a family history of breast cancer, her mother and sister both being affected. She feels well, apart from the above, and wonders why she cannot simply take iron tablets to correct the anaemia. She will ask the doctor why further tests are necessary and has not considered malignancy as a possibility needing exclusion.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

\(\blacksquare\) **Consider the causes of dyspepsia**. Around 25-40% of adults in the general population have dyspepsia. Dyspepsia refers to a group of symptoms arising from the upper gastrointestinal tract including epigastric or upper abdominal discomfort, post-prandial fullness, early satiety, heartburn, and regurgitation. Many definitions exist. The National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence considers dyspepsia as any chronic and recurrent symptoms referable to the upper gastrointestinal tract. Patients with heartburn and regurgitation only should be considered to have gastro-oesophageal reflux disease. The symptoms may indicate one of four possibilities (Box 2.7).

\(\blacksquare\) **Consider the differential diagnoses of dyspepsia**. A cardiac history is essential because cardiac pain and dyspepsia can be difficult to distinguish. Other causes of upper abdominal pain include aerophagy, biliary disease, abdominal wall pain, chronic pancreatitis, mesenteric ischaemia, diabetes and hypercalaemia. Individual dyspeptic symptoms or subgroups of symptoms poorly predict the presence of underlying organic lesions, although the presence of predominant heartburn and regurgitation may help predict underlying gastro-oesophageal reflux disease.

#### Patient perspective

Explore her ideas and expectations about simply treating anaemia with iron and explain the reasons for wanting to investigate further.

#### Past medical history

The history of oesophagitis is important, even though she has taken a proton pump inhibitor (PPI), because it can transform to oesophageal malignancy. 

### Drug and allergy history

Ask specifically about NSAIDs or corticosteroids (often for chronic inflammatory conditions such as asthma, inflammatory bowel disease and connective tissue disease). Aspirin, antibiotics, calcium antagonists, theophylline and bisophosphonates also provoke dyspepsia.

### Family history

The family history of breast cancer may be relevant because it may link with ovarian cancer. The latter must be in the differential diagnosis as a cause of weight loss, abdominal fullness and unexplained anaemia that may not be iron deficient.

### Social history

Ask about alcohol and smoking, both risk factors for peptic ulceration.

## Discussion

### What do you know about the types of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD)?

The Montreal classification defines gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) as 'a condition that develops when the reflux of stomach contents causes troublesome symptoms and/or complications'. It is potentially, although rarely, serious. A clear history of heartburn (a rising feeling from the stomach to the neck) is unlikely to be caused by anything else and quality of life is affected in proportion to the frequency and severity of this, irrespective of the presence or severity of oesophagitis. The acid component of the gastric refluxate is responsible for most of the symptoms and pathology associated with GORD, although other components, such as bile, may also contribute (known as non-acid reflux). The prevalence of reflux symptoms is around 44% with 24% of individuals experiencing symptoms for 2 days or more per week.

### Non-erosive reflux disease (NERD)

Nearly 70% of patients with heartburn do not have evidence of erosive changes on endoscopy. Of these, a proportion has increased acid reflux on 24-hour pH monitoring and is classified as having NERD.

### Functional heartburn

A proportion of patients describe typical reflux symptoms without evidence of reflux. It is likely that acid exposure is not the major cause of symptoms and the pathophysiology remains obscure.

### Reflux oesophagitis

Reflux oesophagitis implies reflux of gastric contents causing mucosal damage (inflammation or ulceration) of the oesophagus, and may be symptomatic or asymptomatic. Attempts have been made to classify the extent of damage, and the Los Angeles classification is now commonly used: A - mucosal breaks of < 5 mm not extending between folds; B - mucosal breaks of > 5 mm not extending between folds; C - mucosal breaks extending between at least 2 folds; D - mucosal breaks extending between > 2 folds involving > 75% of the circumference.

### What is the pathophysiology of GORD?

GORD is associated with transient lower oesophageal sphincter (LOS) relaxation unrelated to swallowing or to failed swallows - those not followed by normal peristalsis. The LOS is a 3-4 cm region of smooth muscle in the distal oesophagus and externally, the crural diaphragmatic striated muscle, the latter being incompetent in a hiatus hernia. Other factors contributing to the magnitude of reflux include oesophageal clearance and neutralisation mechanisms. Relaxation of the LOS and crura are normal physiological processes during swallowing and gas venting. Relaxations not initiated by a swallow are known as transient lower oesophageal sphincter relaxations (TLOSRs). TLOSRs probably account for 90% of reflux episodes. The TLOSR reflex is initiated by tension receptors in the stomach and mediated by a vagovagal pathway via the brainstem; these responses are inhibited by gamma-aminobutyric acid-B (GABA-B) receptor agonists, a future potential therapeutic target. Hiaatus hemias appear to increase the magnitude of reflux during TLOSRs. TLOSRs appear to be less significant in more severe reflux oesophagitis. Factors which relax the LOS, such as caffeine, fat, smoking, drugs (calcium channel antagonists and nitrates) and gastric distension, increase the likelihood of reflux. The association of increased body mass index and GORD remains unclear.

### What are the complications of GORD?

GORD can lead to the complications listed in Box 2.8.

The prevalences of oesophagitis and Barrett's oesophagus are 12% and 1.3%, respectively, irrespective of symptoms. Traditionally, oesophagitis was seen as a continuous spectrum of disease, with endoscopy-negative symptoms labelled as mild and Barrett's oesophagus labelled as very severe. However, progression from normal to erosive oesophagitis to Barrett's oesophagus is seldom seen and symptoms are tending to replace mucosal findings in determining severity. 

### What is the relationship between a hiatus hernia and GORD?

A hiatus hernia does not diagnose GORD. The majority of hiatus hernias are asymptomatic (rarely, a hiatus hernia may undergo torsion to cause acute pain and vomiting) although 90% of patients with severe oesophagitis will have a hiatus hernia.

### How may GORD be investigated?

Most patients with typical symptoms of GORD do not require investigation and will respond to a trial of PPI. High-dose PPI (40 mg omeprazole twice daily for 1 week) has a sensitivity of 80% as a diagnostic test for GORD.

#### Oesophagagostroudodenoscopy (OGD)

Patients of any age presenting with reflux symptoms and alarm symptoms should have an urgent OGD. OGD should also be performed before oesophageal pH studies. Otherwise OGD in the assessment of heartburn is not indicated. Further, a negative OGD does not exclude NERD. Screening for Barrett's oesophagus in patients of any age with a history of chronic heartburn cannot be recommended as the absolute risk of adenocarcinoma is less than 1: 1000 and 40% of patients who develop adenocarcinoma in association with Barrett's oesophagus do not have a history of heartburn.

#### Oesophaggeal ambulatory pH studies

Acid exposure of the distal oesophagus can be measured either by placing a naso-oesophageal probe 5 cm above the LOS or by placing a wireless capsule probe endoscopically. Measurements of pH can be taken for 24 or 48 hours. PPIs or histamine receptor antagonists (H2A) should be stopped 1 week before the procedure. Acid exposure above normal ranges is considered pathological. The indications for pH studies are:

* Patients with typical symptoms of GORD who fail to respond to a trial of PPIs (it is alternatively reasonable to escalate PPI dose in these patients)
* Patients with typical symptoms of GORD without oesophagitis or an adequate response to PPIs who are contemplating anti-reflux surgery
* Patients with persistent GORD symptoms after anti-reflux surgery.

#### Oesophaggeal manometry

Oesophageal manometry seldom influences GORD management It may be useful where oesophageal motility abnormalities are suspected, such as achalasia.

#### Multichannel intraluminal impedance

This measures changes in electrical resistance within the oesophagus, which can imply the direction and duration of refluxate. It may be useful in the investigation of PPI-resistant reflux symptoms although its role is not well defined.

### Why is Barrett's oesophagus important and how is it managed?

It can be a precursor to adenocarcinoma of the oesophagus (relative risk around 80-fold). Cancer is heralded by high-grade dysplasia and is more likely with a long segment (> 7 cm) of Barrett's oesophagus. Endoscopic surveillance is appropriate every 1-2 years if there is no significant dysplasia. There is no evidence that PPIs or anti-reflux surgery lead to regression of Barrett's oesophagus.

### Other than GORD, what other causes of oesophageal pain are there?

GORD is the most common cause, but motility disturbances, increased or decreased - as in achalasia - can occur, as hypersensitivity and often an overlap of these problems. Oesophageal spasm, relieved with nitrates, is very rare.

### List some causes of duodenal and gastric ulceration

These are listed in Table 2.5.

### What do you know about Helicobacter pylori?

_Helicobacter pylori_ is a Gram-negative bacillus that colonies only the mucus layer of the human stomach. It is one of the most common human infections; about half of the world's population carries this organism, although the prevalence varies widely, with more than 80% of adults being infected in Japan and South America compared with around 40% in the UK. Most infected people (> 70%) are asymptomatic. However, there is strong evidence that _H. pylori_ has an important causative role in > 90% of duodenal ulcers and a role in benign gastric ulcers that are not attributable to NSAIDs. There is evidence of a causative role in gastric cancer and gastric- mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue lymphoma. It may have a role in functional (non-ulcer) dyspepsia. Although _H. pylori_ initially causes inflammatory gastritis, the immunological response is not sufficient to clear it and it becomes chronic. Polyclonal strains are common. It is, however, decreasing in prevalence. What do you know about the pathophysiology of peptic ulceration and gastric cancer, and their links with _H. pylori?_

#### Normal gastric secretion

The stomach comprises a proximal fundus, a body and a distal antrum. Parietal (oxyntic) cells in the body and fundus produce hydrochloric acid and intrinsic factor. Chief (peptic) cells secrete pepsinogen, a pepsin precursor. Stimulation of these and other gastric secretions has neuronal (vagal), hormonal (gastrin) and histamine (stimulated by gastrin) inputs. Inhibition of secretion is mediated by sympathetic nerve activity, low pH and negative feedback from small bowel enzymes. Gastrin is produced from G cells in the antrum. Its secretion is stimulated by food, distension, vagal input and high pH. Gastrin promotes acid, intrinsic factor and pepsin secretion (the H2 receptor being the final common pathway). gastric emptying, pancreatic bicarbonate release and secretin release. Somatostatin in the antrum inhibits gastrin release. The pieces of information important here to the development of peptic ulceration and gastric cancer are that:
* Acid is produced from the body and fundus of the stomach
* Gastrin, which stimulates acid, is produced in the antrum
* Somatostatin, which inhibits gastrin, is also produced in the antrum.

#### How peptic ulceration develops

Patients with duodenal ulcers have excessive numbers of parietal cells with acid hypersecretion. That acid hypersecretion is virtually always due to _H. pylori_ is evidenced by the fact that secretion returns to normal when _H. pylori_ is eradicated. A predominantly antral gastritis due to _H. pylori_ occurs in _H. pylori_-provoked duodenal ulcers (the vast majority are _H. pylori_ provoked). This tends to cause suppression of somatostatin cells and a consequent increase in gastrin release from G cells leading to acid hypersecretion. Further, areas of gastric metapalsia in the duodenum can be colonised by _H. pylori_, causing duodenitis; gastric metaplasia increases with the amount of acid entering the duodenum and tends to be lowest in pericious anaemia (in which acid-producing areas are destroyed) and highest in patients with gastrin-secreting tumours (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome). Unlike patients with duodenal ulcers, patients with gastric ulcers and functional dyspepsia have normal numbers of parietal cells and acid secretion, and the link with _H. pylori_ is variable.

#### How gastric cancer develops

_H. pylori_ also predisposes to gastric cancer, but patients have acid hyposecretion. This was once thought to be due to gastric body gastritis leading to atrophy and loss of acid-producing parietal cells. However, acid hyposecretion can in part be reversed by _H. pylori_ eradication, suggesting that _H. pylori_ causes gastric body gastritis/inflammation rather than permanent cell loss. Acid hyposecretion may be self-perpetuating because it supports the presence of _H. pylori_ in the gastric body and so worsening gastric body gastritis, maintaining suppression of acid secretion. Acid hyposecretion predisposes to cancer by several mechanisms including impaired vitamin C absorption and overgrowth of bacteria in the stomach.

### Is the location of _H. pylori_ important in determining clinical outcome?

Given the above, the location of _H. pylori_ (together with environmental, bacterial and host factors) thus determines whether a person with _H. pylori_ infection tends towards a state of acid hypersecretion (predominantly antral gastritis) or acid hyposecretion (predominantly gastric body gastritis) and therefore the clinical outcome - asymptomatic infection, duodenal ulceration, gastric ulceration or gastric cancer.

### Are there any implications from the presence of _H. pylori_ for the use of PPIs?

Acid normally protects the gastric body from _H. pylori_ colonisation. A PPI, by reducing acid production in the gastric body, can allow _H. pylori_ to shift from the antrum to the gastric body and fundus causing a chronic active pangastritis that may progress to atrophic gastritis and, over time, gastric adenocarcinoma. PPIs thus have the potential to improve symptoms by reducing 'acid' symptoms but promote an increased risk of gastric cancer. Eradicating _H. pylori_ should therefore be considered concurrently, although in practice this theoretical risk has not appeared to translate to substantial concern, and the widespread empiric use of PPIs appears to be relatively safe provided guidelines for when to test for _H. pylori_ and when to contemplate endoscopy are followed.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Duodenal ulceration & Gastric ulceration \\ Helicobacter pylori infection (\textgreater{} 90\%) & Helicobacter pylori infection (non-atrophic gastritis, although the latter is usually autoimmune) \\ Hypercalcatenin & NSAIDs \\ Granulomatous disease (Crohn’s disease, sarcoidosis, Wegener’s granulomatosis) & Neoplasia (carcinoma, Lymphoma, Lymphoma, leiomyonymousarcoma) \\ Neuplasia (carcinoma, lymphoma, leiomyonymousarcoma) & Granulomatous disease (Crohn’s disease, sarcoidosis, Wegener’s sarcoidosis, Wegener’s granuloma tuberculosis) \\ herpes simplex, virulence, cytomegalovirus, Candida) & Infection (tuberculosis, syphilis, herpes simplex, cytomegalovirus, Candida) \\ Ectopic pancreatic tissue & cytomegalovirus, Candida) \\ RSAD, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug. \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.5: Causes of duodenal and gastric ulceration 

### What is Zollinger-Ellison syndrome?

This is peptic ulcer disease resulting from a gastrin-secreting adenoma, usually of pancreatic origin. It may be part of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 syndrome.

### How is _H. pylori_ infection diagnosed?

#### Invasive tests

Endoscopically, histology is very useful, culture unreliable and urease tests (CLO test) indicate only the presence or absence of infection.

#### Non-invasive tests

_H. pylori_ elicits a local and systemic antibody response and circulating immunoglobulin (Ig) G antibodies can be detected, but individuals vary in antibody responses and titres fall slowly after eradication so serology cannot distinguish eradication from reinfection (there are no reliable IgM tests). Serology is thus not recommended in patients with uninvestigated dyspepsia. The 13C-urea breath test is the most accurate non-invasive test with a sensitivity and specificity > 90% - urea is hydrolysed by _H. pylori_ urease to carbon dioxide and ammonia and, following ingestion of carbon radio-labelled urea, radio-labelled carbon dioxide is detected in the breath of patients harbouring _H. pylori_. The faecal antigen test has a similar sensitivity and specificity to the urease breath test; however, PPIs should be stopped at least 2 weeks before and antibiotics at least 4 weeks before performing either of these tests because they can lead to false-negative results.

### When is OGD indicated in patients with dyspepsia?

British Society of Gastroenterology guidelines suggest, for the first presentation of dyspepsia, the following.

#### People under 55 years

Those without alarm symptoms should have _H. pylori_ serology checked (or urea breath testing). A positive _H. pylori_ test is a good predictor of peptic ulcer and patients testing positive should receive _H. pylori_ eradication therapy, and those with persisting symptoms after eradication therapy or patients testing negative whose symptoms persist after anti-secretory treatment should proceed to OGD. No action is required for patients rendered asymptomatic.

#### People 55 years or over or those with alarm symptoms

People 55 years or over with uncomplicated dyspepsia may receive empirical treatment first with OGD recommended if symptoms persist after 1-2 months, or they may proceed directly to OGD. People 55 years or over or patients with alarm symptoms should proceed to OGD. It is the 'gold standard' investigation of the upper gastro-intestinal tract; its accuracy in detecting organic lesions is higher than 95%, and biopsies can be taken to diagnose _H. pylori_ infection, precancerous conditions, and malignancy. Anti-secretory drugs should be stopped at least 2 weeks before OGD because treatment may heal early malignant ulcers and promote overgrowth of normal mucosa in sites of malignant tissue and mask early gastric cancer.

### What empirical treatments are useful in dyspepsia?

#### Predominant epigastric pain or discomfort

Two strategies are available:

1. A non-invasive test for _H. pylori_ and treatment of positive patients with eradication therapy (test and treat)
2. Empirical anti-secretory therapy (a Cochrane review showed that PPIs are the most effective anti-secretory drug).

The main benefit of test and treat over PPIs is that it cures most patients with underlying peptic ulcer and a small group of those with functional dyspepsia. However, the prevalence of _H. pylori_ and peptic ulcer has decreased in the past decade, and it is not clear whether test and treat is still the best first-line strategy. PPIs are recommended as a second-line strategy in patients with persistent symptoms after _H. pylori_ eradication and in those who are _H. pylori_ negative.

#### Heartburn or regurgitation (GORD)

Test and treat is not recommended because these patients most likely have GORD, although evidence may be changing in favour of test and treat in reducing symptoms in patients with GORD. _H. pylori_ eradication is not thought to heal oesophagitis, and in the presence of Barrett's oesophagus _H. pylori_ might even, it has been suggested, protect against malignant transformation. However, guidelines suggest that eradication is sensible because of _H. pylori_'s links with peptic ulceration and gastric cancer. The aim of GORD treatment is to control symptoms and prevent GORD-associated complications:

* Lifestyle changes include dietary changes (reducing fat, caffeine and alcohol), avoiding late meals and weight loss.
* Attacks/alginate combinations improve reflux symptoms but do not heal oesophagitis and are indicated for very mild symptoms where step-up treatment is not necessary.
* PPIs are pro-drugs activated in the parietal cell, inhibiting the final pathway of acid secretion and suppressing acid production for several days. Histamine-2-receptor antagonists (H2A) reversibly bind H2 gastric parietal cell receptors. Both H2As and PPIs heal and prevent relapse of oesophagitis, although PPIs are superior to H2As and prokinetic agents. Patients with typical reflux symptoms who do not respond to initial courses of PPIs may have an alternative diagnosis, inadequate acid suppression or functional heartburn. If symptom control is not achieved after 4 weeks it may be worth extending PPI therapy to 2 months.
* Prokinetic agents with correction of the anti-reflux barrier is the most obvious target for the treatment of GORD, but existing drugs such as domperidone have not shown promise. GABA-B agonists may have future potential.

### How are patients managed following OGD investigation?

Biopsy specimens should be taken from the antrum and the body of the stomach for diagnosing _H. pylori_ infection.

#### Oesophagitis or peptic ulceration

OGD may show oesophagitis or peptic ulceration, which should be treated with PPIs, together with _H. pylori_ eradication for duodenal ulcers, gastric ulcers not due to NSAIDs, and mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue lymphoma; eradication is not indicated in peptic ulcers due to NSAIDs. However, most patients have no structural lesion.

#### No structural lesion and GORD symptoms

Patients should be treated with PPIs. Eradicating _H. pylori_ does not usually provide symptom relief in these patients, but may be considered because it does not worsen symptoms and may have other long-term benefits. The rationale for surgery in GORD is to reverse the physiological LOS abnormalities associated with GORD, achieved by lengthening and increasing the proportion of intra-abdominal LOS. The most common technique is the Nissen fundopplication, where the gastric fundus is wrapped around the distal oesophagus. There is no statistical difference between outcomes of medical therapy (PPI or H2A) and surgery for GORD, but, since there is operative risk, surgery is considered second to medical therapy.

#### No structural lesion and functional dyspepsia

Eradication of _H. pylori_ should be the first-line treatment; 10% of patients with functional dyspepsia benefit from _H. pylori_ eradication. PPIs should be given to _H. pylori_ negative patients and those who fail to respond to eradication therapy, although reassurance of the benign nature of symptoms may be the best treatment overall.

### For which conditions should _H. pylori_ infection definitely be treated?

Several conditions have been linked to _H. pylori_ infection, for which eradication is beneficial (Table 2.6).

### How can _H. pylori_ eradication be confirmed?

The urea breath test is the optimum test to confirm eradication. Eradication should be confirmed at least 4 weeks after treatment ends. If positive, an alternative eradication regimen could be used. Eradication of _H. pylori_ should always be confirmed in cases where a duodenal ulcer has biled, to ensure complete epithelialisation of the crater. Invasive methods to confirm _H. pylori_ eradication should be limited to cases where repeat OGD is indicated (such as gastric ulcer, where OGD is mandatory, with initial targeted biopsies of the rim and base, and repeat endoscopy performed 8 weeks later to confirm healing and to obtain biopsies from the original ulcer site).

### List some causes of upper gastrointestinal bleeding

Causes and frequencies are shown in Table 2.7. Small bowel (e.g. angiodysplasia, investigated by wireless capsule endoscopy or computed tomographic angiography) and right-sided colonic disease may present with melanena.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} \hline
**Cause** & **Frequency (\%)** \\ \hline Peptide ulceration & 35–50 \\ \hline Oesophagitis & 20–30 \\ \hline Erosive disease (duodenitis, gastritis, erosions) & 10–20 \\ \hline Variees & 5–10 \\ \hline Mallory–Weiss tear & 2–5 \\ \hline Neoplasm & 2–5 \\ \hline Vascular malformations & 2–3 \\ \hline Aorto-gastrointestinal fistula & \textless{} 1 \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.7: Causes of upper gastrointestinal bleeding

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} \hline
**Condition linked to** & **Benefits of _H. pylori_ infection** \\ _H. pylori_ infection & eradication \\ \hline Peptide ulcer (active or confirmed history) & Healing; prevention of recurrence; prevention of recurrent bleeding \\ \hline Gastric mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue lymphoma (low grade) & Durable remission \\ \hline Uninvestigated dyspepsia without alarm symptoms & Management of symptoms \\ \hline Patient at increased risk of gastric cancer (first-degree relative of patient with gastric cancer; after gastric cancer resection) & Prevention of development or recurrence of gastric cancer \\ \hline The association between gastric cancer and _H. pylori_ infection is strong but it is not known whether eradicating _H. pylori_ infection reduces the risk of developing gastric cancer. 

### How should peptic ulcer bleeding be managed?

PPIs appear not to significantly reduce re-bleeding or need for surgery. Several endoscopic modalities are available to achieve haemostasis for bleeding ulcers. Combination therapy of epinephrine (adrenaline) injection with mechanical methods of haemostasis reduce re-bleeding compared with a single treatment. A minority of patients with refractory bleeding need surgical oversewing of an artery or partial gastrectomy.

### How might you assess risk in a patient who presents with upper gastrointestinal bleeding?

Haematemesis (bright red, dark clots or coffee-ground) or melena indicate an upper gastrointestinal bleed. The Rockall score is a clinical and endoscopic predictor of risk of re-bleeding and death (Table 2.8). The Glasgow-Blatchford score (Table 2.9) does not rely on endoscopy; its place is more to predict the need for intervention than to predict mortality, and so has greater emphasis in less severe bleeding.

### Can bradycardia be a sign of acute haemorrhage?

A normal or low heart rate and hypotension may occur in acute haemorrhage, often after gastrointestinal surgery. The first response to maintain arterial pressure despite a falling cardiac output involves tachycardia and baroreceptor reflex vasoconstriction. A second phase, however, occurs when about a third of the circulating volume has been lost, in which sympathetically mediated vasoconstriction and cardiac drive fall abruptly and cardiac vagal drive increases.

\begin{table}

## Chapter 2.4 Dysphagia

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the gastroenterology clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

##### Peer Doctor

Re: Mr James Morton, aged 62 years

Thank you for seeing this gentleman who reports difficult swallowing over the last 3 months. He is now frequently vomiting and his routine blood tests show he has a sodium of 130 mmol/l and potassium of 2.9 mmol/l. His renal and liver functions are normal and he is not anaemic. A routine chest X-ray is reported as showing a hugely dilated oesophagus. Please see and advise on urgent endoscopy.

##### Years sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr James Morton is a 62-year-old retired, rather stoical, farmer with a history of reflux symptoms treated by his GP with omeprazole for the last 12 months (although he has taken it sparingly). He takes no other medications. For the last 3 months he has had difficulty swallowing and some vomiting to the extent that he can become dehydrated. He has lost around 10 kg in weight, but attributes this to not being able to eat. He is otherwise previously well. He wishes to avoid further investigation or treatment if possible because he believes 'treatment does more harm than good and these things are best left to nature to heal'.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

##### Presenting problem/s and symptom exploration

Dysphagia refers to delay in the passage of solids or liquids from mouth to stomach. It should be distinguished from odonyphagia, which is discomfort or pain on swallowing hot or cold liquids. The history should take a similar approach to that of dyspepsia, in particular asking about alarm features:

* A anaemia
* L loss of weight (a worry, prompting check of inflammatory markers) or anorexia
* A abdominal mass
* R recent onset of progressive symptoms
* M melanoma or haematenesis
* D dysphagia.

Although attempted localisation can be misleading clinically, it is useful to separate those causes that predominantly affect the pharynx and proximal oesophagus (high dysphagia) from those that affect the oesophageal body and oesophago-gastric junction (Table 2.10).

#### Swallowing assessment

Ask about the following factors that can influence swallowing:

* Medications
* Nutritional intake
* Teeth
* Posture
* Neck problems, e.g. cervical spondylosis
* Neck or limb weakness
* Respiratory problems.

#### Patient perspective

Explore his reasons for preferring to avoid treatment and explain the possible causes, and risks if left untreated.

##### Past medical history

The history of oesophagitis is important, even though he has taken a proton pump inhibitor, as it can transform to oesophageal malignancy.

##### Drug and allergy history

Ask specifically about non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs or corticosteroids.

##### Family history

Relevance here is unlikely.

##### Social history

Ask about alcohol and smoking - both risk factors for peptic ulceration.

## Discussion

### What are the phases of swallowing?

Swallowing has cortical input and is mediated in the brainstem, which receives sensory information from the tongue, pillars of fauces and pharynx. Following pre-oral anticipation of food there are three phases of swallowing: oral, pharyngeal and oesophageal.



#### Oral phase

This involves propulsion of the bolus backwards along the tongue past the fauces and into the pharynx, triggering the swallow reflex and initiation of the pharyngeal phase. The tongue tip and blade press the bolus against the hard palate, the sides of the tongue rising in smooth progression from front to back to force the bolus along a central groove, and swallowing is triggered when the bolus reaches the tongue base, the soft palate rising to occlude the nasopharynx and avoid nasal regurgitation. In the oral phase the airway is open and the circopharyngeal sphincter closed. The timing from the start of lingual peristalsis to trigger of swallow is normally < 1 second. Once swallow is triggered, voluntary control is surpassed.

#### Pharyngeal phase

This is rapid, also < 1 second. It involves movement of the bolus through the pharynx by pharyngeal muscle contraction, with elevation of the larynx to fit under the tongue base, the vocal cords coming together and the epiglottis covering the laryngeal opening. All of this is seen externally as the Adam's apple rising to protect the airway. The bolus may sit transiently in the valleculae then pharyngeal peristalsis squeezes it downwards as the circopharyngeal sphincter relaxes to admit the bolus into the oesophagus. The soft palate and larynx are then lowered and breathing recommences.

#### Oesophageal phase

This is reflexic, not under voluntary control, and no longer speech and language therapy territory!

### Outline your approach to managing dysphagia

This is outlined in Box 2.9.

### What is the role of speech and language therapists in a swallow assessment?

Bedside screening (e.g. water swallow test) to identify patients at risk of aspiration can be carried out by any doctor. It can miss silent aspiration and pharyngeal function can only be inferred.

Referral to a speech and language therapist is appropriate in patients who are alert, able to sit upright for at least 15 minutes, have a stable chest status and in whom there is evidence of oral or facial weakness or sensory disturbance, a wet/gurgly voice or drooling.

Speech and language therapists more fully assess swallow stages and safety and may recommend videofluoroscopy (the gold standard for identifying aspiration and features of dysphagia).

A full history takes account of the normal level of independence with feeding to avoid overly optimistic expectations. Problems with co-operation and cognition (e.g. awareness of spoons, cups and straws in patients with dyspraxia or dementia or volition to eat or swallow in dementia), communication (dysphasia, dysarthria,

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Type of dysphagia & Causes & Pointers in history to possible causes \\ Low (oesophageal) & Oesophageal or oesophago-gastric junction malignancy & Malignancy suggested by alarm symptoms and short history with dysphagia for solids more than liquids \\  & Oesophagitis secondary to candida, cytomegalovirus or herpes simplex virus (consider AIDS) & Otherwise, achalasia more likely, patients often describing chest pain and minor dysphagia \\  & Oesophageal motility disorders, e.g. achalasia, diffuse & Reflux-induced strictures common, often without a history of GORD \\  & & Barrett’s oesophagus often characterised by diminished oesophageal sensitivity and lack of pain \\ High (pharyngeal) & Stroke & Known neurological disease, e.g. multiple sclerosis, motor neurone disease \\  & Dementia & Tendency for spillage into trachea on eating, causing coughing or choking \\  & Bulbar palsy, e.g. motor neurone disease, myasthenia gravis & \\  & Oropharyngeal malignancy & \\  & Crico-oesophageal spasm & \\  & Pharyngeal pouch & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.10: Causes of dysphagia 

#### Low (oesophageal) dysphagia

Endoscopy is the investigation of choice. Where endoscopy is normal, ambulatory pH studies and oesophageal manometry and occasionally oesophageal provocation tests are indicated.
* A benign stricture is confirmed histologically and managed by dilation if possible and maintenance treatment with a proton pump inhibitor. Oesophagitis due to infection warrants specific treatment.
* An oesophageal or gastric tumour is managed operatively by resection in fit patients where there is no evidence of metastases, stenting or laser treatment, or pure palliation.

Achalasia may be detected as a widened oesophagus on chest X-ray and is characterised by absent distal oesophageal peristals and abnormal relaxation of the lower oesophageal sphincter; it may be managed by pneumatic balloon dilatation, often repeatedly, or, failing this, Heller's myotomy of the lower oesophageal sphincter.
* Diffuse oesophageal spasm is rare and characterised by simultaneous contractions seen on barium swallow as a 'orkscrew' oesophagus.

#### High (pharyngeal) dysphagia

Radiology is generally more rewarding than endoscopy. Most problems are due to failure of pharyngeal contraction or circopharyngeal relaxation or both and cincardiography with barium (liquid or bread soaked in) can give functional as well as anatomical information.
* A pouch or pharyngeal bar can be managed by surgical myotomy of the overactive circopharyngeals, sometimes with pouch excision.
* Neuromuscular problems (e.g. post-stroke) may require consideration of percutaneous endoscopic gastrootomy feeding.

#### Low (oesophageal) dysphagia

* An oesophageal or gastric tumour is managed operatively by resection in fit patients where there is no evidence of metastases, stenting or laser treatment, or pure palliation.

Neuromuscular problems (e.g. post-stroke) may require consideration of percutaneous endoscopic gastrootomy feeding. members are nearby and he was rushed into hospital by ambulance before care could be arranged. He has good neighbours, and needs to contact them.
