* Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors should be considered for people with stable angina and diabetes and continued in patients taking these for other indications.

### When symptoms are not controlled satisfactorily on optimal medical treatment

* Revascularisation should be considered, with coronary angiography to guide treatment strategy. Additional noninvasive (e.g. dopamine stress echocardiography, myocardial perfusion imaging) or invasive functional testing (measurement of coronary artery fractional flow reserve) may be required to evaluate angiographic findings and guide treatment decisions. The options are percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) or coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery. The risks of PCI and CABG surgery for people with anatomically less complex disease should be explained, and where the person does not express a preference, PCI is offered in preference to CABG surgery as the more cost-effective strategy. There is potential survival advantage associated with CABG surgery compared with PCI for people with multivessel disease who have diabetes, are over 65 years, or have anatomically complex three-vessel disease, with or without involvement of the left main stem.

### When symptoms are controlled satisfactorily on optimal medical treatment

The following should be considered and discussed:

* Prognosis without further investigation
* The low likelihood of their having left main stem disease or proximal three-vessel disease

## Case 2.9 Acute coronary syndrome

### Candidate instructions

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

* The availability of CABG surgery to improve prognosis in the small subgroup of people with left main stem or proximal three-vessel disease
* e.g. in people with left main stem disease, such surgery may extend survival by a mean of 19 months over 10 years.

After discussion, a functional test or non-invasive anatomical test (computed tomography angiography) may be considered to identify people who might gain survival benefit from surgery. If functional testing indicates extensive ischaemia or non-invasive anatomical testing demonstrates the likelihood of left main stem or proximal three-vessel disease, and if revascularisation is acceptable and appropriate, coronary angiography is considered, and if it indicates left main stem disease or proximal three-vessel disease, CABG surgery.

#### General principles of revascularisation

A multidisciplinary team including interventional cardiologists and cardiac surgeons should discuss the risks and benefits of continuing drug treatment or revascularisation strategy (CABG surgery or PCI) for people with stable angina. Treatment strategy should be considered for, but not be limited to, people with left main stem or anatomically complex three-vessel disease, and people in whom doubt exists about the best method of revascularisation because of the complexity of the coronary anatomy, the extent of stenting required, or other relevant clinical factors and comorbidities. The main purpose of revascularisation is to improve the symptoms of stable angina, but repeat revascularisation may be necessary after either procedure, although the rate is lower after CABG surgery. Stroke is an uncommon complication of each of the two procedures (incidence similar). There is a potential survival advantage with CABG surgery for some people with multivessel disease.

#### Stable angina that has not responded to treatment

For people whose stable angina has not responded to drug treatment and/or revascularisation, re-evaluation should include exploring alternative diagnoses and the person's perceptions of the condition and its pain. There is no evidence for transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation, enhanced external counterpulsation, or acupuncture to manage stable angina.

### Scenario

#### Dear Doctor

Re: Mr William Hawwood, aged 62 years

Thank you for admitting Mr Harwood, who has had 2 hours of chest pain this morning. This came on while gardening and did not settle with his usual glycerol trinitrate spray but has now settled with morphine 5 mg at the surgery. He had a coronary stent inserted in his right coronary artery 6 weeks ago and I am deadly concerned about unstable coronary disease. I have given him aspirin 300 mg. I have taken an ECG, which shows I-wave inversion in the inferior leads, but I think this may be old. Yours sincerely Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient instructions

Mr William Harwood is a 62-year-old man who has just arrived at the hospital after experiencing angina-like chest pain for 2 hours this morning. This came on while gardening and did not settle with three sprays ofylyed trinitrate (GTN) but it eventually settled with morphine at the GP's surgery, to where he managed to drive. He did not want to call an ambulance because he did not want to come to hospital. His wife has severe osteoporosis and has been troubled recently with severe back pain because of a vertebral compression fracture rendering her immobile; she is awaiting a review by the orthopaedic specialist. Mr Harwood is her main carer. He has had a previous heart attack and had a coronary stent inserted in his right coronary artery 6 weeks ago and knows in himself that there is very likely a problem with the stent because if anything his angina has been worse since it was inserted. He has tried to ignore it, hoping that his medications will work. He was told by the hospital doctor last time that the medications he takes should 'protect the heart' and he had hoped that this would be the case even if he were experiencing pain. He does not smoke. His medications are aspirin 75 mg. clopidogrel 75 mg, simvastatin 40 mg. atenolol 50 mg, ramipril 10 mg, all daily, and GTN.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of data gathered

##### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

The definition of an acute coronary syndrome (ACS) is given in Box 2.24.

**Ask about the chest pain.** Ask about:

* The site, character and radiation of chest pain
* The onset, evolution and duration of symptoms
* Any precipitating, exacerbating or relieving factors
* though GTN responsiveness is not very helpful as chest pain waxes and wanes anyway, or was it only with the oxygen and morphine, and has it now settled completely?)
* Its intensity (was this as bad as his original heart attack?).

**Ask about associated symptoms.** Ask about:

* Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea and ankle swelling

* Syncope or presyncopal symptoms (aortic stenosis).

**Ask about progress.** Ask about details of any action/treatment en route to hospital by the ambulance crew and ask what he has been told so far about what is suspected to be wrong.

**Consider the differential diagnosis of acute chest pain.** The differential diagnosis includes all causes of chest pain (Case 2.8), not least dyspepsia from aspirin and clopidogrel. If aortic dissection is considered in the differential diagnosis, immediate imaging with computed tomography (CT)/magnetic resonance imaging is needed, a chest radiograph being unhelpful in this context.

##### Patient perspective

Reassure him that he did the right thing in agreeing to come to hospital, and that, while the medications are somewhat protective, if he is having pain it may well mean that there is a problem with one of the arteries that needs urgent attention.

##### Past medical history

His cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk factors will need to be reviewed to ensure that optimal medical therapy is on board but immediately he needs exclusion of and presumptive treatment for an ACS. 

### Drug and allergy history

His medications are all very reasonable.

#### Family history

There may or may not be a family history of coronary disease, but the relevance of that is historical; he has coronary disease and is by definition at high risk.

#### Social history

Explore who might be in a position to care for his wife if he is in hospital for a few days.

## Discussion

### What are the causes of a raised cardiac troponin level and how do you interpret results?

Cardiac causes include myocardial infarction, arrhythmias, coronary vasospasm, cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, amyloidosis, contusion, coronary intervention, surgery and cardioversion/implantable cardioverter defibrillator shocks. Non-cardiac causes include critical illness, chemotherapy, primary pulmonary hypertension, pulmonary embolism, hypertensive crises, renal failure, subarachnoid haemorrhage, sepsis, stroke and ultra-endurance exercise.

Troponin predictive accuracy is very dependent on the pre-test probability of ACS. If troponin is positive in a typical patient ACS treatment should be started. If positive in an atypical patient an open mind should be kept. A slightly elevated troponin at 12 hours unchanged from an earlier one is probably not due to an ACS unless clinical features are compelling. Troponins should not undermine the value of serial electrocardiograms.

### Are other markers of myocardial damage on the horizon?

Troponin testing has improved identification of high-risk patients but there is room for refinement of markers. The vascular inflammation that occurs at the time of an ACS may yield new markers and the CD40 ligand is a marker of future events in ACSs.

### Which electrocardiogram leads are affected in each type of myocardial infarction?

Most ACSs have no acute electrocardiogram changes. II, III, aVF (inferior leads) indicate right coronary artery (RCA) territory disease. I, aVL, V5, V6 (lateral leads) indicate left circumflex artery territory disease. V1-V5 (anterior leads) indicate left anterior descending (IAD) territory disease. Posterior ST elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMIs) can produce profound reciprocal ST depression in V1-V4 with prominent R waves and/or ST elevation in leads V7-V9 placed horizontally under the left scapula at the level of V4-V6.

### What systems are there for stratifying ACS risk?

The Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction (TIMI) score is an objective means of risk stratification intended for acute 'through the door' decision making based on seven variables (Box 2.25).

The Global Registry of Acute Coronary Syndromes (GRACE) system uses the following predictors of death - age. Killip class (heart failure), heart rate, blood pressure, ST deviation, cardiac arrest, raised creatinine or raised cardiac enzymes. It is used at initial assessment to predict 6-month mortality and risk of future adverse cardiovascular events and so guide management (Table 2.18).

### Summarise antiplatelet therapy in non-ST elevation acute coronary syndrome (NSTE-ACS)

Platelets were once considered little more than inert smuldges on a blood film with a role in clotting. Over 80 surface receptors have now been identified, many involved in platelet activation. Platelet function may be assessed by various laboratory techniques involving addition of activation agonists to a blood sample and assessing platelet 'clump-ability'. Equally, by adding platelet antagonistic drugs to blood samples and assessing the variable responses, it is possible to determine individual likelihoods of responding to particular drugs; for example, the level of breakdown products of thromboxane A2 in urine can help determine whether or not aspirin is effective, lower levels indicating greater effect. Potential sites determining variability of response to antiplatelet drugs include gut absorption, liver metabolism, and platelet receptor genetic polymorphisms. Platelet adhesion, activation and aggregation occur at sites of endovascular damage (Case 2.34). Platelet activation results from the binding of various agonists (e.g. thomboxane A2, collagen, adenosine diphosphate (ADP), thrombin, arachidonic acid) to specific receptors leading to intracellular changes. During activation the platelet CPIIbIIIa receptor undergoes a conformational change, allowing it to bind to the dimeric fibrinogen molecule, and irreversible platelet aggregation occurs. This platelet plug is the troublemaker in ACSs and other thrombotic events. Drugs interfere with platelet function usually by inhibiting platelet activation, although some inhibit platelet aggregation. These are illustrated in Figure 2.5 and may be broadly classified as follows.

### 1. Aspirin

Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) inhibits isoenzyme cyclooxygenase 1 (COX-1) leading to suppression of thromboxane A2 production. This action is irreversible, but platelet lifespan is only up to 10 days and new recruits continuously exit their bone marrow barracks, and so the need for daily dosing. But, as seen in Figure 2.5, aspirin only partially interferes with platelet activation; an army of alternative receptors and molecules are waiting to activate platelets.

### 2. ADP pathway antagonists - P2Y12 receptor inhibitors

ADP is a powerful amplifying trigger of platelet activation. Many pathways that activate platelets result in platelet granule release and production of ADP, which via the platelet adenosine nucleotide receptor P2Y12 amplifies the activation. Thienopyridine drugs such as diclodipine (not used now on account of side effects), clopidogrel and prasugel are selective and irreversible inhibitors of this receptor and so inhibit binding of ADP to platelet receptors and so inhibit ADP-induced adenylcyclase activity. Reversible inhibitors of this receptor include AZD6140/cangerd/ticagrelor. Inhibition of ADP receptor binding also deactivates the platelet GPIbIIIa complex, thereby inhibiting platelet aggregation.

Since clopidogrel and aspirin act on different platelet receptors, dual use confers additive effects. The CURE study was the first to demonstrate this and showed a 20% relative risk reduction in NSTE-ACS with the combination of clopidogrel (300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg daily) and aspirin compared with aspirin alone, whether or not patients proceeded to percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) or coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). The CHARISMA study did not reproduce these results, but was flawed by the inclusion of patients following an ACS (who did benefit from clopidogrel) and those at high risk taking clopidogrel for primary prophylaxis (who did not benefit) - in other words, those who had a clot benefited. The CREDO study showed that clopidogrel did not start to produce benefits until after around 15 hours. The CLASSICS trial established the role of clopidogrel following PCI. The PCI-CURE study, a subgroup analysis of the CURE trial, further established the role of clopidogrel after coronary stenting.

Prasugel produces more rapid and consistent platelet inhibition than clopidogrel and does not appear to be affected significantly by proton pump inhibitors, or loss-of-function variants of the _CYP2C19_ gene and evidence for benefit comes from trials including the TRial to Assess Improvement in Therapeutic Outcomes by Optimizing Platelet Inhibitors with Prasugel-Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction (TRITON-TIMI) trial. The rate of adverse effects, such as thrombocytopenia or neutropenia, was similar with prasugel and clopidogrel.

Ticagrelor evidence for benefit comes from trials including the PLATelet inhibition and patient Outcomes (PLATO) trial in moderate- to high-risk NSTE-ACS (planned for

Figure 2.5: Antiplatelet agents. ADP, adenosine diphosphate; ATP, adenosine triphosphate; GP glycoprotein; 5-HT, 5-hydroxytryptamine; PAR-1, protease activated receptor-1; P2Y, a type of purinergic receptor; Tr, thromboxane platelet receptor.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Predicted 6-month & Risk of future adverse \\ mortality (\%) & cardiovascular events \\ \(\leq 1.5\) & Lowest \\ \(>1.5\)–3.0 & Low \\ \(>3.0\)–6.0 & Intermediate \\ \(>6.0\)–9.0 & High \\ \(>9.0\) & Highest \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.18: Global Registry of Acute Coronary Syndromes (GRACE) risk stratification either conservative or invasive management) or STEMI planned for primary PCI randomised to either clopidogrel or ticagrelor. Co-administered with aspirin, the PLATO trial showed that, for every 72 patients with an ACS treated for 12 months with ticagrelor instead of clopidogrel, one extra life was saved.

### 3. Emerging platelet receptor antagonists

There is a need to find alternative ways around the problems of partial inhibition of platelet activation and platelet receptor genetic polymorphism-induced resistance. As well as evaluating different doses of the above drugs, researchers are looking at alternative platelet receptors such as protease-activated receptor-1 (PAR-1).

#### 4. GPIIbllla inhibitors

GPIIbllla inhibitors block the platelet receptor for fibrinogen and hence prevent sandwiching of fibrinogen between platelets and the final common pathway of aggregation. These were demonstrated to be effective in ACS, especially in those people with troponin positivity or undergoing PCI, in trials such as CAPTIURE, PURSUIT, PRISM, PRISM-PLIUS, PARAGON-B, ACUIITY Timing and EARLY-ACS. Oral GPIIbllla inhibitors have not been shown to be very effective. The drugs in current use are abiciximab, eptifibatide and tirofiban.

Recommended usage of antiplatelet therapy in NSTE-ACS is given in Box 2.26.

### Summarise the use of anti-ischaemic drugs in NSTE-ACS

Anti-ischaemic drugs either decrease myocardial oxygen demand (by decreasing heart rate, lowering blood pressure, reducing preload, or reducing myocardial contractility) or increase myocardial oxygen supply (by inducing coronary vasodilatation). 

#### Beta blockers

These competitively inhibit the myocardial effects of circulating catecholamines and reduce myocardial oxygen consumption by lowering heart rate, blood pressure, and contractility. The evidence for benefit is extrapolated from early studies in STEMI and stable angina patients, more recent double-blind randomised trials compared with placebo in unstable angina, and meta-analysis studies suggesting a 13% relative risk reduction of progression to STEMI. No significant effect on mortality in NSTE-ACS has been demonstrated. Reservations about giving beta blockers came from the Chinese Clopidogrel and Metoprolol in Myocardial Infarction Trial (COMMIT) study in mostly STEMI patients, which resulted in a significantly higher rate of cardiogenic shock with bacteroprolol, but overall meta-analysis data favour beta blockers.

#### Nitrates

The use of nitrates in unstable angina is largely based on pathophysiological considerations and clinical experience. The major therapeutic benefit is probably related to the venodilator effects that lead to a decrease in myocardial preload and left ventricular end-diastolic volume, resulting in a decrease in myocardial oxygen consumption. In addition, nitrates dilate normal as well as atherosclerotic coronary arteries and increase coronary collateral flow.

#### Calcium channel blockers

These are vasodilators. In addition, some have direct effects on atrioventricular conduction and heart rate. There are three subclasses of calcium blockers: dihydropyridines (such as nifedipine), benzothiazepines (such as diltiazem), and phenylethylamines (such as verapamil). Atrioventricular block may be induced by non-dihydropyridines. Nifedipine and amlodipine produce the most marked peripheral arterial vasodilatation, whereas diltiazem has the least vasodilatory effect. All subclasses cause similar coronary vasodilatation. Evidence of the effect on prognosis in NSTE-ACS patients is limited. Most of the data with dihydropyridines show no significant benefit in either myocardial infarction or post-myocardial infarction secondary prevention but a trend for harm, whilst studies with verapamil and diltiazam suggest reductions in sudden death, re-infarction and total mortality, with the largest benefit in patients with preserved left ventricular function.

#### Other anti-anginal drugs

Nicorandil, a potassium channel opener, reduced the rate of the primary composite endpoint in patients with stable angina, but was never tested in ACS patients. Ivabradine selectively inhibits the primary pacemaker in the sinus node and may be used in selected patients with beta-blocker contraindications. Ranolazine exerts anti-anginal effects by inhibiting the late sodium current and was not effective in reducing major cardiovascular events in the Metabolic Efficiency With Randazine for Less Ischemia in Non-ST-elevation Acute Coronary Syndromes (MERLIN) study.

Recommended use of anti-ischaemic drugs in NSTE-ACS is given in Box 2.28.

## Summarise revascularisation strategies in NSTE-ACS

These may relieve symptoms and improve prognosis. Risk stratification should be performed as early as possible.

#### Invasive versus conservative approach

Those who may benefit from an invasive strategy are outlined in Box 2.29.

#### PCI versus CABG

There are no specific trials comparing PCI with CABG in patients with NSTE-ACS. In patients stabilised after an episode of ACS, the choice of revascularisation modality can be made as in stable angina (Case 2.8). The revascularisation strategy (culprit lesion \(\text{PCI}/\text{multivessel PCI}/\text{CABG}\)) should be based on clinical status as well as disease severity, i.e. distribution and angiographic lesion characteristics. PCI of non-significant lesions is not recommended. 

### Box 2.28 Use of anti-ischaemic drugs in non-ST elevation acute coronary syndrome

* Patients on chronic beta-blocker therapy admitted with acute coronary syndrome should be continued on beta-blocker therapy if not in class 2 ill heart failure. Oral beta-blocker treatment is indicated in all patients with left ventricular dysfunction without contraindications. Intravenous beta-blocker treatment at the time of admission should be considered for patients in a stable haemodynamic condition (class < III) with hypertension and/or tachycardia.
* Calcium channel blockers are recommended for symptom relief in patients already receiving nitrates and beta blockers (dihydropyrophe type), and in patients with contraindications to beta blockade (non-dihydropyrophe type). Calcium channel blockers are recommended in vasospastic angina. Niedipine or otherdihydropyridines are not recommended unless combined with beta blockers.
* Oral or intravenous nitrate treatment is indicated to relieve angina; intravenous nitrate treatment is recommended in recurrent angina and/or signs of heart failure.

### Box 2.29 Invasive management of non-ST elevation acute coronary syndrome

### National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence guidelines

Recommendations are:

* Angiography (with follow-on percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) if indicated) as soon as possible for patients who are clinically unstable or at high ischaemic risk
* Angiography within 96 hours of admission for those at intermediate or higher risk when initially assessed (predicted 6-month mortality > 3.0%) if there are no contraindications (such as active bleeding or comorbidity)
* Conservative management without early coronary angiography for those at low risk when initially assessed (predicted 6-month mortality < 3.0%). Angiography (with follow-on PCI if indicated) is indicated if ischaemia is subsequently experienced or demonstrated by ischaemia testing.

### European Society of Cardiology guidelines

Primary criteria for invasive management are:

* Relevant rice or fall in troponin
* Dynamic ST- or T-wave changes (symptomatic or silent). Secondary criteria for invasive management are:
* Diabetes mellitus
* Renal insufficiency (estimated glomerular filtration rate < 60 ml/min/1.73 m3)
* Reduced left ventricle function (ejection fraction < 40%)
* Early post-infarction angina
* Recent PCI
* Prior coronary artery bypass grafting
* Intermediate to high Global Registry of Acute Coronary Syndromes (GRACE) risk score.

### What do you understand by the term drug-eluting stent (DES)?

PCI DESs reduce the rate of in-stent stenosis and the overall need for further revascularisation. However, they do this at the price of an increased risk of stent thrombosis, not seen in bare metal stents. The risk of DES thrombosis is much increased by the premature cessation of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT); DAPT is currently recommended for 12 months after DES implantation, and does not seem to be associated with excessive bleeding although thrombotic risk appears to continue after 12 months. Stent technology is ever changing.

## Is bleeding risk from antithrombotic treatment a concern?

As a broad generalisation, patients at highest ischaemic risk are also those who are at higher risk of bleeding complications associated with the use of multiple antiplatelet and antithrombin agents. The addition of warfarin for those who need it poses further risk, with little evidence for guidance, although expert opinion recommends reducing the dose of aspirin to 75 mg daily, minimising the duration of DAPT and a lower international normalised ratio target; temporary discontinuation of warfarin or use of one antiplatelet agent is considered only if the risk of bleeding is deemed to exceed the benefits of treatment. Gastrointestinal prophylactic treatment should be initiated.

### What special patient group or comorbidity considerations are there in ACS?

These include:

#### Older people

The clinical presentation of NSTE-ACS in older people is often atypical and they are more likely to have mild symptoms. This is a more heterogeneous population in terms of disease, comorbidity and functional status. Technical issues with PCI include the fact that diffuse, multivessel disease of calcified, tortuous arteries is more likely. As with many diseases in older people there may be more to gain but more risk.

#### Women

Women presenting with NSTE-ACS are older than men and have a higher frequency of diabetes, hypertension, heart failure, and other comorbidities.

#### Diabetes

Approximately 20-30% of patients with NSTE-ACS have known diabetes, and at least as many have undiagnosed diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance.

#### Renal disease

Renal dysfunction is present in 30-40% of patients with NSTE-ACS. 

### Heart failure

Heart failure is one of the most frequent and deadly complications of NSTE-ACS.

#### Extreme body weight

Low body weight is associated with an increased risk of death, particularly of bleeding, frequently owing to inappropriate dosing of drugs. Obese patients have more risk factors, but are younger and more likely to receive evidence-based therapies and paradoxically may have better outcomes.

#### Non-obstructed coronary arteries

The pathophysiology of NSTE-ACS is not homogeneous and possible mechanisms include: a coronary artery spasm (Prinzmetal's angina), an intramural plaque complicated by acute thrombosis with subsequent recanalisation, coronary emboli and 'syndrome X'.

#### Anaemia

Anaemia is associated with a worse prognosis (cardiovascular death, myocardial infarction, or recurrent ischaemia) across the spectrum of ACS.

#### Bleeding

Bleeding is the most frequent non-ischaemic complication in the management of NSTE-ACS.

#### Thrombocytopenia

Thrombocytopenia can occur during treatment of NSTE-ACS, defined as a decrease in platelet count to < 100 000/ml or a drop of 50% from baseline. Thrombocytopenia is considered moderate if the platelet count is between 20 000 and 50 000/ml, and severe if < 20 000/ml. In the ACS setting, the two main types of drug-induced thrombocytopenia are heparin-induced thrombocytopenia and GPIlb/Illia receptor inhibitor-induced thrombocytopenia.

### What is Takotsubo cardiomyopathy?

Also called stress-induced cardiomyopathy, apical ballooning or broken heart syndrome, Takotsubo cardiomyopathy was first described in Japan and is characterised by acute, reversible left ventricular dysfunction without obstructive coronary artery disease and usually resolves spontaneously over a period of weeks. The pathophysiology seems to include endocrine, hormonal, neuropsychological, and microvascular factors. The characteristic appearances seen on angiography are a ballooned apical segment and a hypercontractile basal portion of the left ventricle reminiscent of the traditional fishing pot used in Japan to trap octopus - 'tako-tsubo'. Once considered rare, it could possibly be responsible for 1-2% of admissions for ACS. It mainly affects post-menopausal women and may be triggered by psychological or physical stress. Typical ischaemic cardiac pain is the usual symptom, and the electrocardiogram may show ST elevation or negative T waves with QT prolongation. These ST segment changes may be widespread. The troponin level is usually slightly raised. Diagnosis requires coronary angiography to exclude obstructive coronary artery disease. Treatment is supportive, and prognosis generally good.

## Why are patients with diabetes at higher risk of an ACS?

Diabetes is associated with risk factors for accelerated CVD and risk of ACS. These include a prothrombotic state mediated by increased plasminogen activator inhibitor 1 and increased GPIlbIIIa receptors, and increased endothelial dysfunction mediated by hyperglycemia and dyslipidaemia. Whilst it is desirable to maintain blood glucose levels <= 11.1 mmol/l in an ACS, intensive glucose lowering has not been shown beneficial.

### What are the immediate management principles in a STEMI?

In the absence of contraindications, reperfusion therapy should be instituted without delay if there is ST elevation, new left bundle branch block or evidence of posterior infarction. Either thrombolysis or primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PPCI) is acceptable, though PPCI is considerably preferable. 'Time is muscle'. Electrocardiogram criteria for thrombolysis (in the absence of a long list of contraindications) are 1 mm ST elevation in at least two contiguous leads or 2 mm ST elevation in at least two contiguous precordial leads or new left bundle branch block (or evidence of a posterior myocardial infarction). Advantages of PPCI include a higher patency rate of the artery, reduced short- and long-term mortality, reduced re-infarction and stroke rates, shorter hospital stay and likely cost effectiveness. Early PCI in cardiogenic shock is also beneficial.

### Are there other causes of ST elevation?

Causes of ST elevation are listed in Box 2.30.

### What do you know about right ventricular infarction (RVI)?

The V4R lead and other chest leads placed on the right side of the chest symmetrical to where they are usually placed may help diagnose RVI. V4R should be performed in the setting of an acute inferior territory infarct when it may reveal ST segment elevation that is absent in its usual V4 placement. RCA occlusion usually threatens the inferior wall of the heart. Unlike LAD territory, which supplies much of the anterior wall (scarring of which impairs left ventricular function), RCA territory supplies the atrioventricular node in 80% of cases (the circumflex artery supplying it in the remaining 20%) and RCA infarcts tend to cause bradyarrhythmias. Temporary pacing is often life saving but, because these arrhythmias often represent reversibly ischaemic penumbrae, long-term pacing is not inevitable. ST elevation in right-sided leads implies involvement of the right ventricular branch (RVB) of the RCA. This occurs if the RCA occlusion is proximal to its RVB offshoot. An infarcted right ventricle will impair left heart filling and cause hypotension. Bradyarrhythmias will also cause hypotension, as will a more extensive infarct with left ventricular involvement, as will hypotensive drugs such as beta blockers. Unlike most causes of hypotension in the setting of acute myocardial infarction, RVI demands high filling pressures. Fluids, rather than diuretics, are needed to maintain right ventricular output and left ventricular filling.

### Which complications may follow myocardial infarction?

These are listed in Box 2.31.

### What are the important secondary preventive measures after an ACS?

Secondary preventive measures are described in Table 2.19.

'Uncomplicated' patients may be discharged from hospital at day 5. Patients should mobilise at home for the following week, and gradually increase their exercise thereafter. Cardiac rehabilitation programmes are now well developed.

### How might you investigate acute severe interscapular pain?

The three important diagnoses to exclude (the 'triple rule out') are myocardial infarction, pulmonary embolism and an acute aortic syndrome (aortic dissection or intramural haematoma). Correct diagnosis is imperative because of widely differing treatment options.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Treatment & Comment \\ \hline Antiplatelet therapy & Continued indefinitely unless contraindications (DAPT for 12 months after moderate to high-risk NETEMI and at least 4 weeks after STEMI) \\ \hline Beta blocker & Continued indefinitely unless contraindications \\ \hline Station & Continued indefinitely unless contraindications \\ Intensive lipid lowering more beneficial than moderate (high-dose at devastatin recommended for first few months at least) \\ \hline ACE inhibitor & All patients indefinitely unless contraindications (angiotensin receptor blockers if intolerant) \\ All should have echocardiogram to assess left ventricular function and exclude mutual thrombus \\ Blood pressure, lipid and glycaemic targets & As for all patients at high cardiovascular disease risk \\ \hline Rehabilitation & Mobilization days 2–3, with risk factor modification, lifestyle assessment and introduction to cardiac rehabilitation \\ \hline Aldosterone antagonists & All patients with left ventricular systolic dysfunction post myocardial infarction and diabetes or heart failure without significant renal impairment or hyperkalaemia, after institution of beta blocker and ACE inhibitor (from day 3–14) \\ indefinitely (spironolactone greatest evidence in heart failure, eplemenone evidence post myocardial infarction) \\ \hline Anticoagulation & Considered for patients with atrial fibrillation, large anterior wall myocardial infarction, severe left ventricular dysfunction or echocardiographic evidence of mural thrombus or left ventricular aneurysm \\ \hline Implantable cardiac & Considered for patients at risk of sudden cardiac death \\ defibrillator & Antiarrhythmic drugs other than beta blockers exhibit harm or no benefit \\ \hline ACE, angiotensin-converting enzyme; DAPI, dual antiplatelet therapy; STEMI, ST elevation myocardial infarction; NSTEMI, non-ST elevation myocardial infarction. \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.19: Secondary preventive measures following an acute coronary syndrome 

#### Serial electrocardiogram and 12-hour troponin testing

This has a high accuracy for ruling in or out an ACS but does not inform about pulmonary embolism or aortic dissection.

#### Echocardiography

Transthoracic echocardiography does not adequately visualise the distal ascending, transverse or descending aorta and has only 60% sensitivity and 30% specificity for detecting aortic dissection. Transoesophageal echocardiography improves these detection rates to 97-99% and 77-85%, respectively, because of the close proximity of the oesophagus to the thoracic aorta, but carries a 2% complication rate (bacteraemia, oesophageal rupture, bleeding). It can only detect the haemodynamic consequences of obstructive embolism such as increased right ventricular size, flattening of the interventricular septum and tricuspid regurgitation, present in only 30-40% of patients. It has high sensitivity for detecting wall motion defects following myocardial infarction but low specificity.

#### Electrocardiogram gated multidetector CT

Performed in late diastole when the heart is relatively static, this informs about the heart, lungs and pulmonary vasculature, and great vessels in a single study. Sensitivity and specificity for atheromatous coronary disease are 80-99% and 97%, respectively, and after myocardial infarction it will detect occlusion and the corresponding perfusion defect. Multidetector CT is the imaging modality of choice for an acute aortic syndrome, usually more readily available and less invasive than transoesophageal echocardiography, and detects a dissection flap or intra-mural haematoma with 83-98% sensitivity and 87-100% specificity; additional electrocardiogram gating improves image quality and allows coronary artery analysis. This investigation is also very sensitive in detecting pulmonary embolism; the sensitivity and specificity of CT pulmonary angiography for pulmonary embolism are 90% and 95%, respectively. This test is thus optimal for excluding the three important diagnoses in selected patients.

### What do you know about aortic dissection?

Aortic dissection is caused by an intimal and medial tear in the aorta with propagation of a false lumen within the aortic media. It is part of the 'acute aortic syndrome' - an umbrella term for aortic dissection, intramural haematoma, and symptomatic aortic ulcer. Acute dissection is the most common aortic emergency, with an annual incidence of 3-4 per 100 000 in the UK, which exceeds that of ruptured aneurysm. The prognosis is grave, with 20% pre-admission mortality and 30% in-hospital mortality. Aortic dissection is categorised according to the time between the onset of symptoms and diagnosis and the site of the entry tear. A dissection is considered acute when diagnosis is made within 14 days of onset, and thereafter chronic. The location of the entry tear plays a key role in treatment and outcome, and it is classified by being in the ascending aorta (Stanford type A dissection) or distal to the origin of the left subclavian artery (Stanford type B dissection). Type A dissection carries a far worse prognosis than type B dissection and urgent surgical intervention is often needed. By contrast, acute type B dissection is usually managed conservatively if uncomplicated and surgically if complicated.

#### Causes

The causes of aortic dissection are multifactorial, with both inherited susceptibility and acquired degenerative disease.

White men aged over 40 years with hypertension, or those under 40 with Marfan syndrome or bicuspid aortic valves, are at highest risk.

Systemic hypertension is one of the most important risk factors, along with male sex and being white. Various connective tissue diseases predispose to the inherent weakening of the aortic wall and subsequent dissection, these diseases especially important in patients under 40 years, and include Marfan syndrome with fibrillin defects, Ehlers-Danlos type IV with abnormal synthesis of type III procollagen, and other connective tissue disorders associated with cystic medial necrosis. Congenital cardiovascular abnormalities such as bicuspid aortic valve increase the risk.

#### Presentation

Patients typically present with the abrupt onset of sharp tearing or stabbing chest pain, which may improve slightly over time, but pain may be absent in 10% of patients. Asymptomatic presentation is more common in diabetes. The pain may radiate to the neck in type A dissection or to the interscapular area in type B aortic dissection. Acute rupture or inadequate perfusion may cause a patient to become unconscious. Interrupted perfusion may result in neurological deficits, symptomatic limb ischaemia, or visceral ischaemia. Many differential diagnoses exist, including for acute chest pain, an ACS, pulmonary embolism or spontaneous pneumothorax.

#### Management

The emergency management of patients with suspected aortic dissection is largely resuscitative, aiming for a heart rate of 60-80 beats/min and systolic blood pressure of 100-120 mmHg. A careful balance must be maintained between suppressing tachycardia and hypertension and ensuring adequate end-organ perfusion (monitoring urine output, mental and neurological state and peripheral vascular status). Twelve-lead electrocardiography is essential to exclude an ACS in the context of a potential aortic dissection.

CT aortography is the first-line diagnostic investigation, followed by transoesophageal echocardiography; magnetic resonance angiography is preferred for surveillance.

Management of a proximal (type A) dissection is surgically if possible. Uncomplicated distal (type B) dissection is best managed with intensive drug treatment; complicated type B dissection requires surgical intervention. All patients need lifelong antihypertensive therapy and surveillance imaging.

## Case 2.10 Heart Failure

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

##### **C**lear Doctor

Re: Mrs Julie Havelock, aged 78 years

This lady has become increasingly dyspnoea over the last 6 months. She has no cough. She has been sleeping with extra pillous for 2 months and her exceptional tolerance has worsened. She gets occasional angina, relieved with a GTN spray. Her full blood picture, urea and electrolytes, glucose and liver and thyroid function tests are normal. Creatinine is slightly elevated at

128 mol/1 and total cholesterol is 5.3 mmol/1. She takes aspirin, furosemide 40 mg daily and ramipril 5 mg daily. I would appreciate your further assessment.

Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mrs Julie Havelock is a 78-year-old woman whose main symptomatic problem is shortness of breath. This has been progressive over the past 6 months, initially just on exertion but now she feels breathes lying in bed without three pillous. She has found it increasingly difficult to walk to the shops over the past 6 months. She gets very occasional angina on exertion that is rapidly relieved with a spray. This is a problem for her because she does not drive on account of visual impairment from age-related macular degeneration and her husband, who is 10 years older than she, suffers from dementia. She is his main carer. They live in a house with stairs, which she still manages to climb. She has never smoked, but has a family history of 'heart trouble'. There is a parrot at home, which they have had for 4 years. She is otherwise well but takes a substantial amount of ibuprofen for long-standing back pain.

#### How to approach the case

##### **D**ata gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of data gathered

##### **Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration**

##### **D**icit details of symptoms.

Try to confirm that symptoms are consistent with heart failure rather than other common causes of dyspnoea such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), recurrent pulmonary emboli, lower respiratory tract infection and pleural effusion/malignancy. Left ventricular systolic dysfunction (LVSD) on an echocardiogram does not exclude alternative causes or the possibility of multiple causes of breathlessness. Hypoalbuminaemia due to liver disease (impaired synthesis) or renal disease (e.g. nephrotic syndrome), lower limb venous insufficiency and drug-induced fluid retention (e.g. non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers causing ankle swelling) may also masquerade as heart failure. Renal artery stenosis may cause 'flash floods' of pulmonary oedema.

Left ventricular failure (LVF) may result in dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, nocturnal cough or fatigue (signs include a third heart sound, tachycardia, hypotension, fibbasal crackles). Remember that symptoms that are worse when lying flat do not necessarily imply LVF. Symptoms from gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, post-nasal drip and bronchial secretions can also be worse supine.

Right ventricular failure may result in ankle swelling (signs include raised jugular venous pulse, pulsatile tender hepatomegaly, sacral and peripheral oedema).

Biventricular failure leads to both sets of symptoms.

##### **E**stablish symptom severity.

The New York Heart Association (NYHA) classification is summarised in Box 2.32.

*Consider causes.** There are many causes of heart failure, although the usual is ischaemic heart disease (Table 2.20).

##### **Box 2.32 New York Heart Association classification of heart failure symptoms**

* no limitations
* slight limitation of physical activity
* marked limitation of physical activity
* symptoms at rest 

#### Patient perspective

Explore her thoughts about the cause of symptoms and her expectations of treatment. What can she no longer do that she would like to do? The impact on the care for her husband is paramount.

#### Past medical history

This usually unveils some cardiovascular risk factors.

#### Drug and allergy history

Ask about NSAIDs.

#### Family history

It is unlikely that this is relevant in this case.

#### Social history

Exploring support from family, and any need for and her willingness to accept adjunctive support from social services, occupational therapy (e.g. stairlifts) and of course her GP is pivotal, as is a predictive approach to future care pathways for her husband, with the support of her GP, and possibly community psychiatric services.

## Summary BOX - Assessment and PLAN

Patients with suspected heart failure and previous myocardial infarction should have transthoracic Doppler two-dimensional echocardiography and specialist assessment.

Patients with suspected heart failure without previous myocardial infarction should have measured serum natriuretic peptide (B-type natriuretic peptide or N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide) levels if the B-type natriuretic peptide level is > 400 pg/ml (116 pmol/l) or N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide level is > 2000 pg/ml (236 pmol/l), urgent echocardiography and specialist assessment are needed as very high levels of serum natriuretic peptides carry a poor prognosis.

If the B-type natriuretic peptide level is 100-400 pg/ml (29-116 pmol/l), or N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide level is 400-2000 pg/ml (47-236 pmol/l), echocardiography and specialist assessment are warranted soon. If levels are less than these a diagnosis of heart failure is unlikely in an untreated patient.

Situations that may reduce serum natriuretic peptide levels include obesity or treatment with diuretics, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, beta blockers, or aldosterone antagonists. Conditions other than heart failure that may raise serum natriuretic peptide levels include diabetes, sepsis, age > 70 years, and those affecting the heart (left ventricular hypertrophy, myocardial

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Causes & What to ask about \\ ischaemic heart disease & Symptoms of angina Risk factors \\ Pressure overload, especially due to hypertension or aortic stenosis & High blood pressure known valve disease \\ Volume overload, especially due to aortic or mitral regurgitation & Known valve disease \\ Arrhythmias, especially atrial fibrillation & Palpitations \\ Cor pulmonale (right heart failure secondary to lung disease) & COPD & Breathlessness on exertion and other respiratory symptoms \\ Pulmonary emboli (recurrent multiple pulmonary emboli remain underdiagnosed) & \\ Cardiomyopathy & Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy & Ask if history suggests a possibility, e.g. alcohol diabetes \\ Restrictive cardiomyopathy, e.g. sarcoid, amyloid \\ Myocarditis & Ask if history suggests a possibility, e.g. viral illness before symptoms, chemotherapy drugs \\ Pericardial disease & Ask if history suggests a possibility, e.g. viral illness, tuberculosis, connective tissue disease, malignancy \\ High output failure & Ask if history suggests a possibility, e.g. thyrotoxicosis, anaemia, Paget’s disease, left-to-right cardiac shunts \\ COPD, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.20: Causes of heart failure ischaemia, tachycardia, right ventricular overload), the lungs (hypoxaemia - from causes including pulmonary embolism and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), the kidneys (glomerular filtration rate < 60 ml/min) and the liver (cirrhosis).
* Electrocardiography and tests to evaluate possible aggravating factors and/or alternative diagnoses are important including chest radiography, pulmonary function tests, urine analysis, and blood tests for full blood count and film plus biochemical assessment of the liver, kidneys, thyroid, glucose, and lipids.
* Once heart failure has been diagnosed, severity, aetiology, precipitating factors and type of cardiac dysfunction (whether it is associated with left ventricular systolic dysfunction or preserved left ventricular ejection fraction) are important.

## Discussion

### What is heart failure?

Heart failure is a complex syndrome resulting from any structural or functional cardiac disorder that impairs the ability of the heart to act as a pump to support physiological circulation, and is characterised by symptoms such as breathlessness and fatigue and signs such as fluid retention. Chronic heart failure affects over 1% of people in the UK, almost a million, with a prevalence of about 6-10% in people aged over 65 years, and overall prevalence is increasing. It carries a high morbidity and a 10% annual mortality. About a third of patients have a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) <= 35%, up to 40% of whom are at risk of more severe heart failure and worse outcomes identified by conduction delay (QRS duration on a surface electrocardiogram of > 120 milliseconds).

### What is the pathophysiology of heart failure?

The shape of the normal left ventricle is designed for maximal efficiency of ejection. Remodelling from scarring, hypertrophy (pressure overload) and dilatation (volume overload) compromises this ideal shape, and ushers in a vicious cycle of decreased cardiac output and compensatory neurohumoral stimulation (sympathetic nervous system and the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system; RAAS) with positive ionotropy, salt and water retention and vasoconstriction. This leads to increased myocardial work, relative hypoxia, cell death and further left ventricular dysfunction. Arrhythmias are more likely.

### What are the two main types of heart failure?

The two main types of heart failure are left ventricular systolic dysfunction or heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HF-REF) and heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HF-PEF). HF-PEF may be as common as HF-REF. Both types have a poor prognosis, although the introduction of effective treatments has led to a fall in mortality from heart failure caused by left ventricular systolic dysfunction (from 26% at 6 months in 1995 to 14% at 6 months in 2005). Diastolic heart failure is a term often used synonymously with HF-PEF, although they are not necessarily the same, and most patients with diastolic heart failure have some systolic dysfunction, too. HF-PEF refers to people with symptoms or signs of heart failure, a LVEF of > 50% and evidence of diastolic dysfunction (biomarkers, catheter evidence). The typical patient is female, elderly, overweight and hypertensive. The pathophysiology of HF-PEF is myocyte hypertrophy and fibrosis and a failure of or resistance to ventricular filling. A stiff ventricle causes compensatory atrial hypertrophy with a fourth heart sound (S4). The pathophysiology is more complex than mere diastolic dysfunction. In the normal heart, left ventricular twist during systole (which stores energy), motion of the mitral annulus towards the apex during systole (which also helps suck blood into the atrium) and the corresponding untwisting process and recoil in early diastole when that energy is released to generate the negative intraventricular pressure gradient or suction in early diastole are tightly coordinated. In patients with HF-PEF this coordination is disrupted, especially on exercise. A comparison of systolic and diastolic heart failure is given in Table 2.21.

### Which pharmacological treatments improve the prognosis in heart failure?

Optimal treatments (Box 2.33) serve to modulate the RAAS and upregulate or re-sensitive b-receptors.

### How do angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors work?

ACE inhibitors inhibit ACE, which converts angiotensin I to angiotensin II (All). All has numerous actions including systemic vasoconstriction and stimulation of aldosterone. ACE inhibitors promote systemic vasodilation, which reduces afterload and preload. This reduces intracardiac pressures and unfavourable ventricular remodelling. Formerly, the combination of nitrates and hydralazine was used to respectively reduce preload and afterload.

### Why are ACE inhibitors relatively contraindicated in aortic stenosis?

Reducing afterload increases the pressure drop or gradient across the aortic valve. Reducing preload limits the heart's capacity to compensate by increased cardiac output. Nitrates are thus also contraindicated in aortic stenosis, and antihypertensives other than ACE inhibitors/angiogenesis receptor blockers (ARBs) such as calcium channel blockers need to be used with caution.

### Why are ACE inhibitors relatively contraindicated in renal artery stenosis?

Numerous molecules act on the afferent and efferent arterioles in the kidney to maintain glomerular filtration rate. All is activated by reduction in renal artery perfusion 

## Chapter 2.21 Comparison of systolic and diastolic heart failure

### 2.2.1 Heart failure - reduced ejection fraction (HF-REF) and systolic heart failure

The following chapters describe the methodology used in this chapter are described in Chapter 2.1. The first chapter is devoted to the analysis of the physiological and physiological properties of the cerebral cortex. The second chapter is devoted to the analysis of the physiological properties of the cerebral cortex. The second chapter is devoted to the analysis of the physiological properties of the cerebral cortex. The third chapter is devoted to the analysis of the physiological properties of the cerebral cortex. heart failure, and the WATCH study examined aspirin, clopidogrel and warfarin in such patients and failed to find significant differences in endpoints other than a trend favouring warfarin in non-fatal stroke. Heart failure patients were excluded from many statin trials, and there is no evidence for statin use in heart failure, and indeed some data even suggest that cholesterol may be beneficial, perhaps by mapping up endotoxin. This illustrates the dangers of extrapolating results from trials to different populations.

### Anticoagulation

This is indicated in atrial fibrillation and in sinus rhythm with a history of thromboembolism, left ventricular aneurysm or intracardiac thrombus.

### Practical recommendations (National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence guidance)

Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction

ACE inhibitors and beta blockers licensed for heart failure should be offered to all patients with heart failure caused by left ventricular systolic dysfunction. For beta blockers, this includes older adults as well as patients with peripheral vascular disease, erectile dysfunction, diabetes mellitus, interstitial pulmonary disease, or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease without reversibility. For patients who have intolerable side effects with ACE inhibitors, an ARB licensed for heart failure is an alternative. For patients intolerant of both ACE inhibitors and ARBs, hydralazine in combination with a nitrate may be considered.

If a patient remains symptomatic despite optimal treatment with an ACE inhibitor and a beta blocker, second-line treatment may be considered: an aldosterone antagonist licensed for heart failure, especially in moderate to severe heart failure (NYHA class III or IV) or if there has been a myocardial infarction within the past month; or an ARB licensed for heart failure, especially in mild to moderate heart failure (NYHA class II or III); or hydralazine in combination with nitrate (especially if the patient is of African or Caribbean descent with moderate to severe heart failure (NYHA class III or IV).

Digoxin may be considered for worsening or severe heart failure caused by left ventricular systolic dysfunction despite first-line and second-line treatment.

Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction

Treatment of congestive symptoms is with diuretics, alongside optimal management of comorbidities such as hypertension, ischaemic heart disease and diabetes. Insufficient evidence exists to recommend on the role of ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and beta blockers.

### When might you suspect renal artery stenosis clinically?

The presence of a renal bruit (epigastric or loin) is helpful. Peripheral vascular disease may be surrogate evidence.

### How do ARBs work?

ACE inhibitors effectively act as a 'dam' to the production of AII, which gives rise to two potential problems:

1. Accumulation of substrates such as bradykinin, which promote cough, potential angioedema and other side effects (it is unclear whether all substrates which build up are potentially harmful or whether indeed some may confer benefit)
2. Alternative ways 'around the dam' to promote AII.

ARBs provide a molecular spanner limiting the effects of AII. However, numerous AII receptor sites have been identified in humans, including \(\Delta\text{T}_{1}\) and \(\Delta\text{T}_{2}\). Blocking \(\Delta\text{T}_{1}\) appears to be important in treating heart failure, whereas \(\Delta\text{T}_{2}\) may have important endothelial roles whose blockade may be best avoided. Selective ARBs may be developed in patients who have intolerable side effects with ACE inhibitors, an ARB licensed for heart failure is an alternative. For patients intolerant of both ACE inhibitors and ARBs, hydralazine in combination with a nitrate may be considered.

If a patient remains symptomatic despite optimal treatment with an ACE inhibitor and a beta blocker, second-line treatment may be considered: an aldosterone antagonist licensed for heart failure, especially in moderate to severe heart failure (NYHA class III or IV) or if there has been a myocardial infarction within the past month; or an ARB licensed for heart failure, especially in mild to moderate heart failure (NYHA class II or III); or hydralazine in combination with nitrate (especially if the patient is of African or Caribbean descent with moderate to severe heart failure (NYHA class III or IV).

Digoxin may be considered for worsening or severe heart failure caused by left ventricular systolic dysfunction despite first-line and second-line treatment.

Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction

Treatment of congestive symptoms is with diuretics, alongside optimal management of comorbidities such as hypertension, ischaemic heart disease and diabetes. Insufficient evidence exists to recommend on the role of ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and beta blockers.

### Should ACE inhibitors and ARBs be used in combination?

The CHARM study programme of 7601 patients using can-desartan compared with placebo in a broad range of patients with heart failure irrespective of systolic function or background therapy had three facets. In CHARM Alternative (LVEF >= 40%, patients intolerant of ACE inhibitors) there was unequivocal risk reduction in cardiovascular death or hospitalisation from heart failure. In CHARM Added (LVEF >= 40%, patients on ACE inhibitors for at least 30 days) there was a small but significant risk reduction in cardiovascular death or hospitalisation from heart failure, but no effect on all-cause mortality. Benefit was also seen in patients taking a beta blocker, contrasting with previous data suggesting that the triple combination of ACE inhibitor, ARB and beta blocker was associated with adverse outcomes. In CHARM Preserved (LVEF >= 40%, history of hospitalisation for a cardiac reason with or without current use of ACE inhibitor) there was a trend towards reduction in cardiovascular death or hospitalisation from heart failure. There is general consensus that ARBs should be used in heart failure in patients intolerant of ACE inhibitors but combination therapy is not without debate.

### How do beta blockers work in heart failure?

Beta blockers reduce sympathetic drive on the heart. In chronic heart failure, cardiac b-receptors are down-regulated, but b2-receptors to a lesser extent than b1. It might, therefore, be that b2-receptors are relatively more active in heart failure than in the normal heart, and that non-selective beta blockers are preferable to b1-selective drugs. The Carevelliol Or Metoprolol European Trial (COMET) compared metoprolol (a beta1-selective blocker) with carvedilol (a non-specific beta blocker that blocks both b1 and b2 and that has alpha-blocking effects). Carevelliol produced a striking reduction in all-cause mortality, favouring non-selective blockade, but there has been concern that the dose and formulation of metoprolol used in COMET was different from that in the MERIT-HF study, which showed the benefit of metoprolol. Bisoprolol is a selective beta1-blocker. One should never assume class effects, and current evidence favours bisoprolol (highly cardioselective) or carvedilol (not cardioselctive and produces significant peripheral vasodilation) in heart failure.

### How does spironolactone work in heart failure?

Spironolactone inhibits aldosterone but also seems to reduce cardiac filling pressures and stiffness and enhance compliance. The RALES trial demonstrated the benefit of spironolactone in heart failure and EPHESUS showed that eplerenone improved outcome in LVSD following myocardial infarction.

### List some non-pharmacological interventions you might consider for a patient with heart failure

* Management of other modifiable risk factors (smoking cessation, hypertension, diabetes)
* Stopping drugs which precipitate fluid retention (e.g. NSAIDs, corticosteroids, calcium channel blockers) or depress myocardial function
* Salt restriction (ready-to-cook meals and convenience foods have a high salt content)
* Fluid intake restriction to 1.5-2 litres daily in advanced heart failure
* Exercise and cardiac rehabilitation programme
* Weight reduction (unless cardiac cachexia)
* Vaccination (influenza annually, pneumonococcal once only)

The role of coronary artery bypass grafting revascularisation in patients with heart failure from coronary disease is uncertain, and the HEART-UK study aims to further inform in the setting of angina and heart failure. Underlying precipitants such as anaemia may need to be corrected. Heart failure is frequently associated with anaemia of chronic disease.

### What do you know about devices in heart failure?

Cardiac resynchronisation therapy (CRT), also known as biventricular pacing, can overcome the dyssynchrony resulting from left bundle branch block (LBBB) and subsequently improve cardiac output in the failing heart. Despite numerous imaging studies, LBBB on the 12-lead electrocardiogram is still the best way to identify patients who may respond to CRT. Implantable cardioverter defibrillators (ICDs) prevent sudden cardiac death from ventricular arrhythmias and prolong life in specific subgroups of patients.

#### Cardiac resynchronisation therapy (CRT)

Conduction system disease is common in heart failure, identified by a broad QRS complex and accompanied by dyssynchronous, inefficient cardiac contraction, increased mitral regurgitation, and regional ischaemia, all contributing to further adverse remodelling and worsening cardiac function. Around 20% of patients with heart failure have LBBB, resulting in desynchronisation of left ventricular systolic contraction (left ventricular dyssynchrony) because of delayed contraction of the posterior left ventricle relative to the septum. CRT is an effective treatment for heart failure with LVSD and conduction delay, and can reduce symptoms and admission to hospital and improve quality of life and prognosis.

#### How CRT works

CRT is a form of cardiac pacing that improves coordination of the atria and both ventricles. The device is similar to a conventional dual-chamber pacemaker but has an additional ventricular lead. Pacing leads are placed through the subdavian vein into the right atrial appendage, at the right ventricular apex (the anterior wall of the left ventricle) and into the left ventricular posterolateral free wall (via a tributary of the coronary sinus). The leads are connected to a subcutaneous generator programmed to deliver simultaneous right and left ventricular pacing (with simultaneous pacing of the septum and left ventricular free wall) in response to each sensed sinus beat. Pacing both sides of the left ventricle improves coordination of left (and right) ventricular contraction and cardiac output, reduces mitral regurgitation during rest and exercise, and improves regional perfusion defects. To override intrinsic conduction, the atrioventricular delay is set shorter than the intrinsic PR interval. In selected patients CRT is combined with protection from sudden cardiac death with an ICD.

#### Patient selection

Improvement in symptoms was experienced by 60-70% of patients with heart failure and LBBB after CRT. The best predictor of symptomatic response remains an electrocardiogram showing a QRS duration of >= 120 milliseconds. Evidence (from numerous studies including CARE-HF) is clear that mortality and morbidity outcomes are improved, too, such that a failure to improve symptomatically after CRT does not imply failure to respond. Whether those with a shorter QRS duration (< 120 milliseconds) benefit remains unclear. American and European guidelines recommend CRT for patients with NYHA classes III and IV heart failure, an ejection fraction <=35% and QRS duration of >= 120 milliseconds. National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence (NICE) guidelines recommended CRT with a pacing device (CRT-P) as a treatment option for people with heart failure who fulfil all the following criteria:

* Experiencing or have recently experienced NYHA class III-IV symptoms
* _either_ with a QRS duration of 150 milliseconds or longer _or_ with a QRS duration of 120-149 milliseconds and mechanical dyssynchrony confirmed by echocardiography
* LVEF <= 35%
* Receiving optimal pharmacological therapy.

CRT with a defibrillator device (CRT-D) may be considered for people who fulfil the criteria for implantation of a CRT-P device and who also separately fulfil the criteria for the use of an ICD device. Those with atrial fibrillation probably benefit as much provided that intrinsic conduction is suppressed pharmacologically or by atrioventricular node ablation. Guidelines do not distinguish between patients with right and left bundle branch block, both of whom may be considered for biventricular pacing. Age is unrelated to outcomes from heart failure treatments, including CRT; but is related to outcomes from ICDs.

#### Implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD)

The use of ICDs in ischaemic heart disease and severe heart failure without a previous arrhythmic event (primary prevention) remains controversial. Evidence suggests mortality benefit only in those with mild heart failure (NYHA class I or II). ICDs are recommended by NICE in the following situations.

#### Secondary prevention

For patients who present, in the absence of a treatable cause, with one of:

* Having survived cardiac arrest due to ventricular tachycardia (VT) or ventricular fibrillation (VF)
* Spontaneous sustained VT causing syncope or haemodynamic compromise
* Sustained VT without syncope or cardiac arrest, and an associated reduction in ejection fraction (LVEF < 35%) (no worse than NYHA class III).

#### Primary prevention

* For patients who have a history of previous (more than 4 weeks) myocardial infarction and _either_ an LVEF < 35% (no worse than NYHA class III) and non-sustained VT on electrocardiogram monitoring and inducible VT on electrophysiological testing _or_ LVEF of < 30% (no worse than NYHA class III) and QRS duration >= 120 ms.

NICE guidelines for CRT and ICDs therefore overlap only in patients with class III heart failure, although many cardiologists think a single procedure sensible in patients with class II symptoms as many will deteriorate. An ICD in class III symptoms may reduce sudden death but there is increased mortality from heart failure, and ICDs are also associated with a higher complication rate than CRT alone, including inappropriate discharges, and so those with class III symptoms, those aged over 75 years, and those with very poor ventricular function (< 10%) should be considered very carefully. In non-ischaemic cardiomyopathy the evidence is neutral for the benefit of ICDs.

## What do you know about surgery in heart failure?

In Western countries 70% of heart failure patients have coronary artery disease with transient ischaemic dysfunction, hibernating myocardium or scar. The remainder have hypertension, valvular heart disease or idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM). Left ventricular shape and volume are important determinants of symptomatic status and outcome. Whilst LVEF alone is a poor predictor of survival in the presence of reversible ischaemia, the onset of mitral regurgitation portends a poor prognosis.

#### Surgery to vessels, valves and ventricle

Surgery addresses the three V's: vessels (obstructed coronary arteries), valve (mitral) and left ventricle (dyskinesia or scar). Revasulariation of hibernating myocardium can improve left ventricular function and longevity with outcomes superior to transplantation. The benefits of mitral repair and left ventricular remodelling are less certain. Aortic valve disease responds to conventional valve surgery and DCM patients with secondary mitral regurgitation may improve after valve repair. Surgery must be considered before comorbidity, such as renal dysfunction, substantially increases operative risk. In the presence of poor left ventricular function (LVEF 10-30%) hospital mortality can be avoided by elective deployment of an intra-aortic balloon pump or left ventricular assist device.

#### Transplantation and left ventricular assist devices (LVADs)

Donor hearts are a scarce commodity but mechanical blood pump bioengineering is improving. The observation that pulse pressure is of limited functional importance (compared with flow) has allowed the development of miniaturised continuous-flow devices for long-term use, whilst unloading (resting) the deteriorating left ventricle has been shown to reverse many cellular and molecular changes in heart failure promoting improvement in myocardial function. Temporary extracorporeal assist devices are used to salvage patients with cardiogenic shock after myocardial infarction, those with myocarditis and after surgery. Implantable IVADs are emerging as an alternative to transplantation. For ischaemic cardiomyopathy the combination of a LVAD with autologous or allogenic bone marrow stem cells to improve vascularity is being considered. How might you differentiate between heart failure and cor pulmonale in a patient with significant peripheral oedema?

A history of COPD, a bounding pulse and warm peripheries favours the latter; heart failure is often associated with cool peripheries.

### How would you define 'acute' heart failure and what are its causes?

This is not as easy as it may seem, given that heart failure is one of the most common medical discharge diagnoses. Heart failure, acute or chronic, has no universally agreed definition. The term 'acute' heart failure is often used to mean worsening breathlessness leading to breathlessness (at rest when sitting upright), synonymous with the term acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (ACPOE). Some suggest that the term 'acute heart failure' might be abandoned or should refer to new-onset heart failure regardless of its severity. The European Society of Cardiology proposed six subgroups of 'acute' patients: cardiogenic shock; hypertensive heart failure; heart failure with acute coronary syndrome; isolated right heart failure; worsening chronic heart failure; ACPOE. Many clinical trials have been tempered by tempestuous interpretation of definition. About two-thirds of patients have a prior diagnosis of heart failure. Many admissions reflect worsening in underlying heart function, but myocardial ischaemia, arrhythmias (especially atrial fibrillation), hypertension, sepsis, medication changes or excessive dietary salt or alcohol may all precipitate acute deterioration of chronic heart failure or cause new-onset heart failure. Probably only about 50% of cases are associated with a reduced LVEF.

### What is the pathophysiology of ACPOE?

ACPOE reflects pulmonary venous (left atrial) hypertension. The precise left atrial pressure (LAP) at which ACPOE develops varies, dependent upon alveolar capillary membrane permeability, plasma oncotic pressure and the efficiency of pulmonary lymphatic drainage. Patients with chronic heart failure may be less likely to develop ACPOE at a given LAP because of hypertrophy and constriction of
