Increases in LAP may be due to fluid retention or fluid translocation. The former, but not the latter, is associated with weight gain and peripheral oedema. Sudden severe mitral regurgitation, severe myocardial ischaemia, atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response or severe systemic hypertension associated with a stiff and hypertrophic-phied left ventricle may all lead to pulmonary oedema without weight gain or obvious fluid retention.

### How is 'acute heart failure' diagnosed?

The typical patient is pale, cool to touch and sweaty, reflecting intense sympathetic activation, obviously breathless and sitting upright clutching the bedrails. Coughing up pink frothy sputum is relatively uncommon. There may be central or peripheral cyanosis and reduced oxygen saturations. The venous pressure is often hard to determine. Most patients do not have peripheral oedema. Auscultation reveals bilateral fine basal pulmonary crackles that do not clear on coughing and there may be high-pitched expiratory wheeze.

A chest X-ray should exclude pulmonary causes of breathlessness and identify pulmonary oedema. An electrocardiogram will confirm heart rhythm and any evidence of conduction abnormalities, ventricular hypertrophy or myocardial ischaemia. Until recently the gold standard for confirming a diagnosis has been echocardiography showing a reduced LVEF. However, many people have all the features of heart failure, respond to treatment and yet have a normal LVEF. This appears to be a heterogeneous group of patients, for example those with rarer causes of heart failure such as constrictive pericarditis, those with paroxysmal disease (ischaemia or arrhythmia), those with a forme fruste of systolic dysfunction (long-axis dysfunction) and those with genuine diastolic dysfunction. There is also operator dependence.

Biomarkers, especially brain natriuretic peptide (BNP or NT-proBNP), may be helpful. Diagnosis can be refuted with some confidence if peptide levels are low. If levels are substantially elevated, the patient is in sinus rhythm and renal function not grossly abnormal, the diagnosis of acute heart failure may be made with confidence. Levels are increased in atrial fibrillation even in the absence of heart failure. Intermediate levels pose some uncertainty but some symptoms and signs combined with a modest elevation in levels most likely represent cardiac dysfunction, whereas severe symptoms combined with a slight elevation in levels suggest that the main diagnosis is not heart failure. BNP/NT-proBNP provides the best prognostic marker in heart failure.

### How is ACPOE treated and what is the evidence for treatments?

The reason for ACPOE should be sought and managed. Many patients are hypertensive and have high LAPs but normal cardiac output. Treatment of ACPOE has changed little over the past few decades and conventional treatment is usually highly effective (Table 2.22).

## Case 2.11 Participants

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

### Scenario

#### Dear Doctor

Re: Mr Walter Cartwright, aged 74 years

Thank you for seeing this gentleman with a 2-month history of intermittent, fast palpitations. Episodes occur without warning, and during these he feels dizzy but has never lost consciousness. He has a history of ischaemic heart disease and I wonder if he is now describing paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. Past medical history comprises asbestos with a mild restrictive picture (under annual review at the respiratory clinic) and giant cell arteritis diagnosed a year ago, under control on prednisolone 5 mg daily. He also takes aspirin, netool 50 mg daily (for infrequent angina), sinusvastatin 40 mg daily, elendronate and calcium/vitamin D. He lives with his wife and is an ex-smoker. On examination his heart rate is 44 beats per minute (his ECG shows sinus rhythm), blood pressure is satisfactory at 146/84, heart sounds are present with no murmus and he has scattered chest crackles consistent with asbestos. I would value your advice as to need for further investigation of his palpitations.

#### yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr Walter Cartwright is a 74-year-old man who for the last 2 months has experienced episodes of fast, regular palpitations. He also experiences light-headedness, but has never lost consciousness. These differing episodes do not always occur together, and can occur at any time of day, at rest or during exercise. He has even been aware of symptoms in bed. He has a past medical history of chronic stable angina and asbestos-related lung disease with minor reduction in lung capacity. An ex-smoker of 40 cigarettes per day, he seldom drinks alcohol, takes moderate caffeine and is fully independent, living with his wife in their two-bedroom house. He has no real concerns and will be happy to be 'dismissed' without further tests.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

_Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration_

Consider possible causes. Palpitations are common, and many patients do not have a proven cardiac arrhythmia. Older patients are more likely to have structural heart disease provoking atrial fibrillation (AF) or (non-sustained) ventricular tachycardia (VI). An older man with ischaemic heart disease and left ventricular dysfunction with intermittent fast, regular palpitations is more likely to have VT. Irregular palpitations may indicate AF. A 40-year-old woman with irregular, fast palpitations at rest and on exertion, who also feels sweaty, should be checked for thyrotoxicosis.

Younger patients are more likely to be describing sinus tachycardia or supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). Symptomatic ventricular ectopic beats (VEBs) are extremely common. Sinus tachycardia may be the result of anxiety, thyrotoxicosis, phaechromocytoma or sympathomimetic drugs. A 30-year-old woman with fast, regular palpitations lasting several minutes to several hours is most probably describing SVT, and the baseline electrocardiogram may show evidence of Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome but will more likely be normal. Ambulatory electrocardiogram monitoring is indicated unless there is a clear precipitant such as caffeine or alcohol. A 25-year-old man with a history of feeling that his heart 'misses a beat', and whose episodes last a few seconds, often before falling asleep, is probably describing occasional ventricular or supraventricular ectopic beats, and can be reassured if there are no clinical signs of structural heart disease.

#### Elicit details of the palpitations.

Ask:

* If these are regular (sinus tachycardia, SVT, VT) or irregular (AF, VEBs); patients can sometimes tap out the beat
* If these are fast (sinus tachycardia, AF, atrial flutter, SVT, VT) or slow
* If these are intermittent or persistent; if intermittent, ask about the frequency of attacks, abruptness of onset of each attack and duration of each attack
* For how long attacks have been recurring (since childhood suggests SVT)
* If there are any trigger or relieving factors such as exercise, caffeine, alcohol or drugs.

Ask about associated symptoms. Ask about chest pain, dyspnoea and syncope.

Ask about risk factors for AF. These include coronary heart disease, hypertension, valvular heart disease (history of rheumatic fever), alcohol intake and thyrotoxicosis.

#### Patient perspective

He may have no particular concerns, but that should not dissuade you from recognising that he could be describing tachyarrhythmias with or without significant sinus pauses, warranting an implantable cardiac defibrillator or pacemaker.

#### Past medical history

The history of ischaemic heart disease should alert you to episodes of AF or VT or a tachy-brady syndrome.

#### Drug and allergy history

Antihypertensive drugs, notably calcium channel blockers, may precipitate reflex tachycardia. Ask if any drug treatments have been tried for the palpitations.

#### Family history

This can be very relevant in younger patients with inherited structural heart disease (e.g. hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM)) or an inherited conduction disturbance.

#### Social history

Explore the impact of his symptoms on his daily routine, notably driving.

When palpitations are the primary symptoms, the most useful tests are a 12-lead electrocardiogram and electrocardiogram monitoring (exercise electrocardiogram testing if symptoms are produced on exercise). Echocardiography is unlikely to add information if clinical examination and the electrocardiogram are normal.

This patient should have routine blood chemistry including potassium, magnesium and calcium levels, a 12-lead electrocardiogram and an ambulatory electrocardiogram recording (a patient-activated event recorder if episodes are sufficiently infrequent that 24-48-hour measurement is unlikely to capture any disturbance).

He is likely to have sick sinus or 'tachy-brady' syndrome, ischaemic disturbance of the sinus node and its pathways.

Presyncopal episodes are more likely to be the result of bradyarrhythmias than atrial fibrillation, although non-sustained ventricular tachycardia is a possibility. Sinus pauses of 3 or more seconds warrant a permanent pacemaker, as may shorter pauses that are symptomatic. Atenolol should be stopped, but if there is combined tachy-brady disturbance then beta blockers may be a necessary adjunct to a pacemaker.

## Discussion

### List some types of tachyarrhythmia

* AF
* Atrial flutter
* Atrial ectopy (focal/multifocal)
* Atrioventricular re-entry tachycardia (overt accessory pathway as in WPW syndrome/concealed accessory pathway) * Atrioventricular nodal (junctional) re-entry tachycardia
* VT

### How may SVT arise in WPW syndrome?

WPW syndrome is caused by an accessory conduction pathway that bypasses the atrioventricular node (AVN).

The accessory pathway may not always conduct and so the electrocardiogram may be intermittently normal. When it does conduct, impulses reach the ventricle from both the accessory pathway and the AVN. Impulses via the accessory pathway are faster than those through the AVN, giving rise to the delta wave on the electrocardiogram (pre-excitation). This does not usually provoke tachycardia.

There may, however, be retrograde conduction through the accessory pathway. This can arise during the transient window of opportunity when the accessory pathway is refractory (having delivered its impulses to the ventricle), and the slower AVN pathway is still conducting (an extrasystole is a common precursor, because it delays AVN conduction). At this time, AVN impulses may loop back up into the accessory pathway, setting up a loop of continuous conduction, which results in a re-entry SVT.

This antegrade conduction through the AVN and retrograde conduction through the accessory pathway is termed orthodromic conduction and produces a narrow complex tachycardia. Less commonly, conduction is down the accessory pathway, termed antedromic conduction, producing a broad complex tachycardia. These types of SVTs are referred to as atrioventricular re-entrant tachycardias.

### How may SVT be treated?

Vagal manoeuvres (carotid sinus massage, Valsalva manoeuvre) or adenosine (3 mg then 6 mg then 12 mg) may terminate SVT by transiently blocking the AVN. Adenosine should be used with caution in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and is contraindicated in asthma. Vera-pamil 5 mg intravenously over 1 minute, repeated once if necessary, is an alternative regimen. Beta blockers may be used.

### Why is digoxin contraindicated in WPW syndrome?

AF occurs in up to 15% of patients with WPW. Digoxin must never be used because it decreases the refractory period of the accessory pathway and allows rapid antegrade conduction from atrium to ventricle. The very fast ventricular rate may degenerate to ventricular fibrillation.

### What other types of SVT are there?

The AVN itself may contain both a slow and fast pathway and other SVTs may arise through the ability of the AVN to conduct in an antegrade direction down one of these and a retrograde direction along the other, giving rise to atrioventricular nodal re-entry tachycardias (AVNRTs). SVT is common in patients without structural aberrations in whom there may be numerous triggers such as caffeine or alcohol.

Which clinical or electrocardiographic features can help to determine whether a broad complex tachycardia is the result of VT or SVT with aberrant conduction?

VT is suggested by:

* History of ischaemic heart disease (the strongest predictive factor)
* QRS > 140 milliseconds
* Capture or fusion beats (intermittent sinoatrial beats, or sinoatrial beats fused with ventricular beats)
* Concordance in precordial leads (all of the V lead complexes point in the same direction)
* Absence of bundle branch block on a previous electrocardiogram in sinus rhythm.

### How might you manage VT?

Drug therapy for ventricular arrhythmias is limited by the pro-arrhythmic potential of antiarrhythmic drugs. Monomorphic VT is usually due to ischaemic heart disease. Acutely, amidaronare is the only antiarrhythmic that may be used to treat VT, along with intravenous magnesium, and direct current cardioviesion is needed in cardiogenic shock. An implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) may be considered prophylactically. For patients with VT for whom ICD insertion is not contemplated, antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone or beta blocker) is a second choice; amiodarone has no evidence for primary prevention after myocardial infarction. Torsades de pointes VT should be distinguished from polymorphic VT with a normal QT interval, the latter treated as standard VT but QT prolonging antiarrhythmics are contraindicated in torsades, which may be caused by metabolic disturbances and drugs. If VT continues, intra-aortic balloon counter-pulsation/angiography/VT ablation may be necessary.

### What may cause bradycardia?

Causes include acute ischaemia or chronic degeneration of conducting pathways, metabolic disturbances, hypothermia, hypothyroidism, beta blockers, digoxin, verapamil, diltiazam and Lyme disease.

### What is meant by the term sudden cardiac death (SCD)?

SCD refers to death from a cardiac cause within 1 hour of symptoms without a known prior condition. Sixty per cent of all cardiac death is SCD, with a male to female ratio of 4 : 1. Causes are listed in Box 2.34.

### How might patients be evaluated?

Investigations are largely to exclude coronary heart disease and may include identification of risk factors (hypertension, dyslipidaemia, smoking, diabetes, early family history), echocardiography, nuclear cardiography, coronary angiography, electrophysiological studies, computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, positron emission tomography scanning and coronary calcium scoring. In patients clinically affected by a cardiomyopathy the resting electrocardiogram is rarely normal. In gene carriers who seem to be clinically unaffected (incomplete gene expression) it is usually normal. Classically, large-voltage QRS complexes with ST depression and deep T-wave inversion are seen in patients with HCM, although non-specific changes may be seen. In arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy (AVRC), subtle abnormalities may be evident in the right precordial leads (V1-V3) including T-wave inversion, broadening of the QRS and the 'epsilon wave' (notch in the ST segment). Patients with dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) may also have non-specific abnormalities. Echocardiography or magnetic resonance imaging can help make a definitive diagnosis in HCM and DCM.

### What are the indications for an electrophysiological study?

Persistent palpitations, recurrent syncope or pre-syncope with impaired left ventricular function are symptoms suitable for investigation. Radiofrequency ablation (accessory pathways, junctional tachycardias, atrial flutter, AF), and investigation of narrow or broad complex arrhythmias, with or without radiofrequency ablation, are other indications.

### Which treatments may be considered for patients at risk of SCD?

Management of patients depends on the cause:

* Treatments for coronary heart disease are by far the biggest intervention overall.
* Cardiac surgery may be indicated, notably coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) for coronary disease and valve replacement for valvular heart disease. Cardiac surgery carries a higher risk if there is left ventricular dysfunction, hypertension, renal failure or carotid disease. Patients aged over 80 years have a significant immediate risk of death from cardiac surgery (up to 10%), but, for example, a history of blackouts attributable to aortic stenosis may confer an 80% risk of death within the next year and patients with symptomatic valve disease and no significant comorbidity should be referred for consideration of surgery. CABG benefit is harder to prove, except for left main stem disease, three-vessel disease and proximal left anterior descending artery disease (less evidence).
* The risk of SCD from arrhythmias can be reduced by lifestyle changes tailored to removing triggers. For example, participating in competitive sport is universally discouraged. Drugs such as beta blockers and angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors are commonly used to reduce the risk of death in patients with DCM, but drugs have not been shown to be useful in reducing the risk of sudden death in patients with HCM or ARVC, although in ARVC symptomatic ventricular arrhythmias may be reduced by sotalol and amiodarone.
* 1CDs may be considered (Box 2.35).

### What is the role of genetic mutation analysis?

Because genetic testing is less than 100% sensitive, it is recommended only after confirmation of a clinical diagnosis, and even then usually in a limited number of genes that cause disease. Once a definite disease-causing mutation is identified in an affected patient, the main diagnostic role for genetic testing is identification of carriers within a pedigree, a process known as cascade testing. Risk prediction depends mainly on clinical assessment in the case of cardiomyopathy; mutational analysis may determine risk - one specific subgroup of mutations in the gene encoding lamin A/C (LMNA) associated with DCM increases the SCD risk.

### What is the value of tracing relatives?

Assuming an autosomal dominant inheritance, all first-degree blood relatives of a proband with cardiomyopathy (the first identified affected member in a family) should undergo cardiogenetic evaluation because they have a 50% chance of being a disease carrier. Case finding can prevent future premature deaths through the timely delivery of 

### How they work

Since the 1990s transvenous ICD systems have been implanted like a permanent pacemaker. The ICD lead is placed in the right ventricle and has one or two shock coils. It delivers a shock between the coils and the casing of the device. Devices not only defibrillate but also can recognise and treat ventricular tachycardia (VT) by overdrive pacing and have all the functions of a bradycardia pacemaker. Ventricular fibrillation is induced at implantation to ensure function and the device is programmed with several rate zones to respond to a patient's individual arrhythmia rates. Modern devices can incorporate QRS analysis and analyse to minimise the risks of inappropriate shocks in patients with rapidly conducted supraventricular arrhythmias. ICD malfunction includes inappropriate device therapy and failure of therapy.

### Evidence

Evidence for ICDs in primary and secondary prevention comes from trials including AVID (sunvivors of VT or ventricular fibrillation), MADIT (at-risk patients after myocardial infarction with an impaired left ventricular ejection fraction (UEF) who had not had an arrhythmia incorporating ambulatory electrocardiogram monitoring and electrophysiological testing), MADIT-2 (patients after myocardial infarction with a UEF < 30% without electrophysiological testing), DINAMIT (timing after myocardial infarction - at least 1 month after), CABC-PATCH (inherited cardiac conditions) and SCD-HET (New York Heart Association class III heart failure due to left ventricular systolic dysfunction).

### Indications

* The evidence translates to use of ICDs mostly in the setting of ischaemic cardiomyopathy or heart failure (Case 2.10)
* ICDs are also used in familial cardiac conditions with high risk of sudden cardiac death (e.g. long QT syndrome, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy, Brugada syndrome) and are the only intervention shown to reduce sudden cardiac death in most patients with an inherited cardiomyopathy.

Proplyadic use of an ICD in high-risk patients (UEF <= 35% and impaired cardiac autonomic function manifested as depressed heart rate variability or an elevated average heart rate) who have recently had a myocardial infarction does not reduce overall mortality - reduction in arrhythmic death is offset by increased non-arrhythmic death.

## Case 2.12 Atrial fibrillation

### Candidate information

### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

### Scenario

#### Dear Doctor

Re: Mrs Dorothy Brooks, aged 78 years

This 78-year-old lady presents with fast palpitations and an irregular pulse. Her current ECG shows atrial fibrillation (AF) but she was in sinus rhythm last year when admitted with chest pain under your team's care. Her current medications are aspirin, atenol 25 mg, sinusostatin 20 mg and ramipril 5 mg, all daily. She experiences intermittent palpitations but is otherwise well. I wonder if she has paroxysmal AF and whether or not she should be considered for anticoagulation.

#### Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mrs Dorothy Brooks is a 78-year-old woman who has just been admitted to hospital with fast palpitations. She has had numerous episodes of irregular, fast palpitations over the last year since being admitted to hospital a year ago with a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction. She experiences no ongoing chest pains but does feel moderately breathless if the episodes last for more than an hour or so. Episodes have no diurnal pattern or obvious precipitant. She is usually well, although sometimes has hot flushes during which she feels hot and sweaty. She takes aspirin 75 mg, atenolol 25 mg, simvastatin 20 mg and ramipril 5 mg, daily. Otherwise, she has no significant past medical history of note. She is widowed and lives with her cat. She smoked many years ago and does not drink. She has no particular concerns.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and exploration of symptoms

The history should follow similar lines to that in Case 2.11. In addition, explore the reasons that atrial fibrillation (AF) matters to patients and physicians, namely its causes, symptoms and complications. 

**Explore symptoms**. Atrial fibrillation may result in any of the symptoms listed in Box 2.36.

These symptoms occur as a result of loss of atrial systole/atrioventricular asynchrony (which reduces left ventricular filling and so cardiac output by up to 50%), variable stroke volume (a slow rate allows a longer filling time and thus improved stroke volume, hence the importance of rate control in AF), the irregular ventricular rate (which patients can be aware of), dyschronotropy (usually fast, and related to activity), mitral regurgitation (ventricular systole may begin when the mitral valve is still open) or the underlying cause.

**Consider potential complications**. AF means atrial 'quivering' or disordered contraction and this predisposes to two important complications (Box 2.37).

Total mortality is doubled, largely because of stroke, which is increased six-fold and up to 18-fold for those with rheumatic heart disease. Stasis leads to thrombus formation, especially in the left atrial appendage, and thrombi are often large, provoking devastating strokes. Morbidity relates to age and underlying cause, and annual stroke risk is 1.5% for patients aged 50-59 rising to 23.5% in patients aged 80-89.

#### Patient perspective

She has no particular concerns.

#### Past medical history

AF alerts physicians to underlying disease. The leading causes are listed in Box 2.38.

#### Drug and allergy history

She is taking a range of secondary prophylaxis, but at suboptimal doses.

#### Family history

Relevance here is unlikely.

#### Social history

There are no obvious worries, but she lives alone and should be asked if things are all well at home.

#### Summary BOX - Assessment and PLAN

If atrial fibrillation (AF) is confirmed, an initial decision to rate control is probably best as it cannot be unequivocally established that this episode came on within the last 48 hours; further, she has probably had numerous previous episodes and asymptomatic paroxysmal AF is common. Digoxin loading at 500 mg followed by 500 mg 6 hours later and then 125 mg daily is appropriate, as would be increasing the dosage of beta blockade (after to use a short-acting agent such as metoprolol in acute settings).

A troponin assay as well as routine haematobiochemistry, including potassium, magnesium and calcium levels, and thyroid function tests should be obtained, although troponin levels are sometimes elevated by AF alone.

Low-molecular-weight heparin at a therapeutic dose for thromboembolic prophylaxis should be commenced, and warfarin subsequently considered.

The should have an echocardiogram in the near future.

Her simvastatin should be increased to 40 mg and her ramipril to 10 mg if there are no contraindications.

## Discussion

### How common is AF?

AF is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, occurring in 1-2% of the population. The prevalence increases with age, from 0.5% at 40-50 years to 5-15% at 80 years. Men are more often affected than women. 

## Why is AF important?

* Death rates are independently doubled by AF. Only antithrombotic therapy has been shown to reduce AF-related deaths.
* AF confers a five-fold increased risk of stroke. Stroke in AF is often severe and results in long-term disability or death, and in survivors is more likely to recur. Approximately every fifth stroke is due to AF; furthermore, undiagnosed 'silent AF' is a likely cause of some 'cryptogenic' strokes. Paroysmal AF carries the same stroke risk as permanent or persistent AF. Cognitive dysfunction, including vascular dementia, may be related to AF.
* Hospitalisations due to AF account for one-third of all admissions for cardiac arrhythmias. Acute coronary syndrome (ACS), aggravation of heart failure, thromboembolic complications and acute arrhythmia management are the main causes.
* Quality of life and exercise capacity are impaired in patients with AF.
* Left ventricular function is often impaired by the irregular, fast ventricular rate and by loss of atrial contractile function and increased end-diastolic left ventricular filling pressure.

## What is the pathophysiology of AF?

### How AF is sustained

The electrical properties of the atria are altered by sustained AF, making the atria more susceptible to initiation and maintenance of AF. Sustained AF then induces further electrophysiological and structural alterations known as atrial remodelling:

* Tachycardia causes calcium overload in atrial myocytes; this alters gene expression leading to downregulation of one type of calcium current, a shortening of the refractory period and a decrease in wavelength, promoting multiple wave re-entry (electrical remodelling).
* Increased cell volume, sarcomere misalignment, proteolysis, loss of contractile elements and accumulation of glycogen lead to atrial myopathy (structural remodelling).

These factors explain why AF may recur early after electrical cardioversion, why paroxysmal AF may progress to chronic AF and why chronic AF is harder to treat.

### Sequelae

All of these factors lead to an increasing tendency for irregular, often fast atrial activity, and thromboembolic risk due to stasis of blood flow and endothelial dysfunction.

### Atrial factors

Any structural heart disease may trigger progressive structural remodelling in both the ventricles and the atria. In the atria, proliferation and differentiation of fibroblasts into myofibroblasts and enhanced connective tissue deposition and fibrosis occur. Electrical remodelling contributes to the increasing stability of AF during the first days after its onset. The main cellular mechanisms underlying the shortening of the refractory period are downregulation of the L-type calcium inward current and upregulation of inward rectifier potassium currents.

### Electrophysiological mechanisms

The initiation and perpetuation of a tachyarrhythmia requires triggers for its onset and a substrate for its maintenance. Various mechanisms are likely to coexist. _Focal mechanisms_ might involve both triggered activity and re-entry. Single rapid macrore-entry circuits produce wavefronts emanating from a primary driver (rotor, which may be fixed or moving) which break against regions of varying refractoriness; this gives rise to the irregular global activity that characterises the arrhythmia, a bit like a large mother wave breaking against rocks of all different shapes and sizes, producing all sorts of smaller, diversely directed waves. The single focus also fires at an automatic, regular but very rapid rate that cannot be followed by the rest of the atrial tissue in a 1:1 fashion, so producing fibrillatory conduction. Because of shorter refractory periods as well as abrupt changes in myocyte fibre orientation, the pulmonary veins have a stronger potential to initiate and perpetuate atrial tachyarrhythmias. Arrhythmogenic foci in the pulmonary veins may be amenable to ablation, but access is difficult and through puncture of the atrial septum. In the _multiple wavelet hypothesis_, AF is perpetuated by continuous conduction of several independent wavelets propagating through the atrial musculature in a seemingly chaotic manner. A combination of single and multiple foci may be at play.

### Genetic predisposition

AF has a familial component, especially AF of early onset. Numerous inherited cardiac syndromes associated with AF include short and long QT syndromes and Brugada syndrome.

### How is AF defined?

AF is defined as a cardiac arrhythmia with the following characteristics:

* The electrocardiogram shows 'absolutely' irregular RR intervals
* There are no distinct P waves on the electrocardiogram
* The atrial cycle length (when visible) is usually variable.

### What types of AF are there?

Clinically, there are five types of AF (Table 2.23).

### What are the acute management priorities in AF?

The acute management of AF focuses on relief of symptoms and assessment of AF-associated risks and should include the following. 

#### Determination of symptoms

Determination of the European Heart Rhythm Association (EHA) score is useful: I 'No symptoms'; II 'Mild symptoms' - normal daily activity not affected; III 'Severe symptoms' - normal daily activity affected; IV - 'Disabling symptoms' - normal daily activity discontinued.

#### Estimation of stroke risk and consideration of other complications

Most patients with acute AF will require anticoagulation unless they are at low risk of thromboembolic complications (no stroke risk factors) and no cardioversion is necessary (e.g. AF terminates within 24-48 hours).

#### Search for causes

Underlying causes should be sought. An echocardiogram is useful to detect ventricular, valvular and atrial disease. Thyroid function tests should be included in the initial blood screen. Patients with persistent signs of left ventricular dysfunction and/or signs of myocardial ischaemia are candidates for coronary angiography.

### What do you know about antithrombotic therapy in AF?

Risk stratification is a precursor to treatment decisions.

#### Risk stratification for stroke and thromboembolism

Various stroke risk schemes have (artificially) categorised stroke risk into 'high', 'moderate' and 'low' risk strata, but as these have low predictive value there is now increased emphasis on the presence of stroke risk factors. The simplest risk assessment scheme is the CHADS2 score (cardiac failure, hypertension, age, diabetes, stroke (doubled)) (Table 2.24).

The CHA2DS2-VASC score refines and extends the CHADS2 score by including additional common stroke risk factors (Table 2.25). The CHADS2 score does not include many stroke risk factors such as some types of valvular heart disease (mitral stenosis or prosthetic heart valves), which would categorise such 'valvular' AF patients as 'high risk'. Risk factors are cumulative and the presence of one 'major' risk factor (stated in Table 2.24) or the simultaneous presence of two or more 'clinically relevant non-major' risk factors (those others in Table 2.25) would justify a stroke risk that is high enough to require anticoagulation.

#### Antithrombotic therapy

Recommendations for antithrombotic therapy should be based on the presence (or absence) of risk factors for stroke and thromboembolism, rather than on an artificial division into high-, moderate- or low-risk categories (Box 2.39).

#### Evidence for antithrombotic agents

Numerous clinical trials have provided an extensive evidence base for antithrombotic therapy in AF:

* Anticoagulation with a vitamin K antagonist (VKA) (versus control) for patients with AF with >= 1 stroke risk factor produces a 64% risk reduction provided there are no contraindications, especially with careful assessment of the risk-benefit ratio and an appreciation of the patient's values and preferences. * Data from all comparisons of antiplatelet agents and control groups show 22% risk reduction. Pharmacologically, near-complete platelet inhibition is achieved with aspirin 75 mg, and low-dose aspirin is safer than higher doses.
* Direct comparison between a VKA and aspirin demonstrates that a VKA is significantly superior. In the Atrial fibrillation Clopidogrel Trial with Irbesartan for prevention of Vascular Events -Warfarin arm (ACTIVE W) trial, anticoagulation therapy was superior to the combination of clopidogrel plus aspirin. The Aspirin arm (ACTIVE A) trial found that major vascular events were reduced in patients receiving aspirin-clopidogrel, compared with aspirin monotherapy, but major bleeding was significantly increased and broadly similar to that seen with VKA therapy.
* broadly two classes, oral direct thrombin inhibitors (e.g. dabigartan ecaliate and AZD0837) and oral factor Xu inhibitors (e.g. riravaroaban, apixaban, edoxaban, betrixaban, YM150)
- are being developed for stroke prevention in AF. Dabigartan may be used to prevent stroke and systemic embolism in people with non-valvular AF with at least one of the following risk factors: previous stroke or transient ischaemic attack (TIA) or systemic embolism; left ventricular ejection fraction <= 40%; symptomatic heart failure; age >= 75 years; age >= 65 years with one of diabetes, coronary artery disease or hypertension.

### Risk of bleeding

An assessment of bleeding risk should be made before starting anticoagulation. Despite anticoagulation of more elderly patients with AF, rates of intracerebral haemorrhage are considerably lower than in the past, typically between 0.1% and 0.6%, which may reflect lower anticoagulation intensity, more careful dose regulation or better control of hypertension. Intracranial bleeding increases with international normalised ratio (INR) values 3.5-4.0, and there is no increment in bleeding risk with INR values between 2.0 and 3.0 compared with lower INR levels. Various bleeding risk scores have been validated for bleeding risk in anticoagulated patients. A HAS-BLED score (Table 2.26) of >= 3 indicates 'high risk', and some caution and regular review is needed following initiation of antithrombotic therapy, whether with VKA or aspirin.

### Optimal INR

The level of anticoagulation is expressed as the INR, derived from the ratio between the actual prothrombin time and that of a standardised control serum. Based on achieving a balance between stroke risk with low INRs and an increasing bleeding risk with high INRs, an INR of 2.0-3.0 is the likely optimal range for prevention of stroke and systemic embolism in patients with non-valvular AF. Whilst a lower target INR range (1.8-2.5) has been proposed for the elderly, this is not based on evidence and studies suggest a two-fold increase in stroke risk at an INR of 1.5-2.0. The maintenance, safety and effectiveness of the INR within range can be influenced by the pharmacokinetics of VKA therapy, particularly the cytochrome P450 2C9 gene (_CYP2C9_) and the vitamin K epoxide reductase complex 1 gene (_VKORC1_).

### AF in special situations

#### Paroxysmal AF

Stroke risk in paroxysmal AF is not different from that in persistent or permanent AF and thus patients with paroxysmal AF should receive oral anticoagulation (OAC) according to their risk score.

#### Perioperative anticoagulation

Patients with AF who are anticoagulated require temporary interruption of VKA treatment before surgery or an invasive procedure. The risk of clinically significant bleeding should be weighed against the risk of stroke and thromboembolism. If the VKA is warfarin, which has a half-life of 36-42 hours, treatment should be interrupted 5 days before surgery. In patients with a mechanical heart valve or AF at high risk for thromboembolism, 'bridging' anticoagulation with therapeutic doses of either low-molecular-weight heparin or unfractionated heparin should be considered.

### Stable vascular disease

Many anticoagulated AF patients have stable coronary or carotid artery disease and/or vascular disease, and common practice is to treat such patients with a VKA plus one antiplatelet drug, usually aspirin. This does not reduce the risk of stroke or vascular events (including myocardial infarction), but substantially increases bleeding events.

### Acute coronary syndrome and/or percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI)

Guidelines for ACS and/or PCI recommend combination antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel) after an ACS, and a stent (4 weeks for a bare-metal stent, 6-12 months for a drug-eluting stent). VKA non-treatment is associated with an increase in mortality and major adverse cardiac events, with no significant difference in bleeding rates. Thus triple therapy seems to have an acceptable risk-benefit ratio provided it is kept short (e.g. 4 weeks) and the bleeding risk is low. Consensus is that drug-eluting stents should be avoided and triple therapy (VKA, aspirin and clopidogrel) used in the short term, followed by longer therapy with VKA plus a single antiplatelet drug (either clopidogrel or aspirin). In patients with stable vascular disease (e.g. with no acute ischaemic events or PCI/stent procedure in the preceding year),VKA monotherapy should be used.

### Elective PCI

Triple therapy (VKA, aspirin, and clopidogrel) should be administered for a minimum of 1 month after implantation of a bare-metal stent, but for much longer with a drug-eluting stent.

### Acute stroke

There are limited trial data to guide management, and there is concern that patients within the first 2 weeks after cardioembolic stroke are at greatest risk of recurrent stroke because of further thromboembolism. However, anticoagulation in the acute phase may result in intracranial haemorrhage or haemorrhagic transformation of the infarct. In patients with AF and an acute TIA, OAC therapy should be considered as soon as possible in the absence of infarction or haemorrhage. In patients with AF presenting with an acute stroke or TIA, uncontrolled hypertension should be appropriately managed before antithrombotic treatment is started, and cerebral imaging performed to exclude haemorrhage. In the absence of haemorrhage, anticoagulation should begin after 2 weeks, but in the presence of haemorrhage anticoagulation should not be given.

### Cardioversion

Increased risk of thromboembolism following cardioversion is well recognised. Therefore anticoagulation is considered mandatory before elective cardioversion for AF of > 48 hours or AF of unknown duration. VKA treatment (INR 2.0-3.0) should be given for at least 3 weeks.

### Atrial flutter

The risk of stroke linked to atrial flutter is similar to that seen in AE. Thus, thromboprophylaxis in patients with atrial flutter should follow the same guidelines as in AF patients.

### What do you know about acute rate and rhythm management in AF?

The acute management of patients with AF is driven by acute protection against thromboembolic events and acute improvement of cardiac function. The severity of AF-related symptoms should drive the decision for acute restoration of sinus rhythm (in severely compromised patients) or acute management of the ventricular rate (in most other patients).

### Acute rate control

In stable patients this can be achieved by oral beta blockers or non-dihydropyridine calcium channel antagonists. In severely compromised patients, intravenous verapamil or metoprolol can be used. Either oral or intravenous digoxin or intravenous amiodarone is preferable in patients with concomitant heart failure or hypotension. If the ventricular rate is very fast (200-300) or delta waves are seen, a rapidly conducting accessory (pre-excitation) pathway is likely and intravenous flecainide and/or direct current cardioversion (DCCV) is recommended; atrioventricular node blockers (e.g. adenosine, digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem) are contraindicated in this situation as they potentially further accelerate the ventricular response.

### Pharmacological cardioversion

Many episodes of AF terminate spontaneously within the first hours or days. If medically indicated (e.g. compromised patients, patients who remain symptomatic despite adequate rate control), a bolus administration of an antiarrhythmic drug may be considered. The conversion rate is lower than with electrical cardioversion, but does not require sedation or anaesthesia. Class I or class III antiarrhythmics are commonly used for cardioversion and maintenance of sinus rhythm. Flecainide, propagtenone and amiodarone may be effective in descending order, but class Ic drugs (flecainide and propagtenone) should be avoided in ischaemic heart disease and impaired left ventricular function. Class Ic antiarrhythmics are also dangerous in atrial flutter. Flutter has an average atrial rate of 300 and with 2:1 block an average ventricular rate of 150. Class Ic antiarrhythmics may slow the atrial rate to, for example, 200, which can then be conducted via an intact atrioventricular node to produce a similar ventricular rate. lbutilide and vernakalant may also be used, with their own contraindications. Ibutilide and sotalol are more effective for conversion of atrial flutter. Repeat oral pharmacological cardioversion ('pill-in-the-pocket' therapy) may be appropriate for selected ambulatory patients once the safety of such an intervention has been established.

#### Direct current cardioversion (DCCV)

DCCV is an effective method of converting AF to sinus rhythm. Unless adequate anticoagulation has been documented for 3 weeks or AF is < 48 hours from a definite onset, transesophageal echocardiography should be performed to rule out atrial thrombi. Complications include thromboembolic events and arrhythmias. Recurrences of AF may be immediate within minutes, early during the first 5 days or late. Factors that predispose to AF recurrence include:

* Duration of AF (especially more than 3 months)
* Heart failure/structural heart disease
* Hypertension
* Age
* Left atrial diameter > 6.5 cm.

### When might you consider long-term rate control in AF and what might you use?

Rate control is the only approach for permanent AF but has gained popularity for persistent AF. Numerous randomised controlled trials, including the AFFIRM study, have demonstrated no difference in the endpoints of mortality or disabling stroke between rate versus rhythm control, with more side effects from drug treatment in the rhythm control groups. Furthermore, sinus rhythm is often not maintained and rhythm control does not lower the need for antithrombotic therapy. Rate control may now be considered for patients aged > 65 years; Table 2.27 lists factors that can aid decision making about rhythm versus rate control. It is important to understand, however, that younger patients, especially those with symptoms or heart failure, benefit more from rhythm control. 

### What non-pharmacological therapies are you aware of for AF?

These include radiofrequency catheter ablation of the atrioventricular node and permanent pacing ('ablate and pace', best reserved as a last resort), obliteration of the left atrial appendage and implantable atrial defibrillators. Catheter ablation is recommended after failure of at least one antiarrhythmic drug or where antiarrhythmic drugs are contraindicated or not suitable. Pulmonary vein isolation to prevent induction of AF (by rapid repetitive pulmonary vein ectopic activity emanating from 'sleeves' of the atrial myocardium inside the pulmonary veins) has a high success rate.

### How might you manage atrial flutter?

Recommendations for atrial flutter are similar to those for AF. Carotid sinus massage or adenosine may transiently slow atrioventricular conduction, making the flutter waves more obvious. Atrial flutter ablation, a definitive cure, should be considered at an early stage for recurrent atrial flutter.

### CASE 2.13 Dyslipidemia

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

```
Dear Doctor Re: Mrs Jean Buchanan, aged 64 years Thank you for advising on the treatment of this lady/s lipid profile. She has type 2 diabetes and her total cholesterol 6 months ago was 7.5 mmol/l. She is now on the maximum dose of a atorvastatin, and her cholesterol has fallen to 6.0 mmol/l with a high-density lipoprotein of 0.9 mmol/l and raised triglycerides. She is overweight. She also takes metformin, twice daily insulin, pentodirul, bendrolfumethiazide and aspirin. She has a slightly raised thyroid-stimulating hormone but a normal thyroxine, which 1 am monitoring.
```

#### Years sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mrs Jean Buchanan is a 64-year-old woman with raised cholesterol, 7.5 mmol/l, discovered at her diabetes check at the surgery 6 months ago. Her atorvastatin was increased to a higher dose of 80 mg and when rechecked recently her cholesterol was 6.0 mmol/l. She feels well in herself and is fully independent. She was diagnosed with diabetes 10 years ago and since then has developed numerous complications - she has had laser therapy for diabetic retinopathy and had to give up work as a secretary because of eye disease. She has proteinuria, recently discovered. She has numbness of both feet and a small diabetic foot ulcer. She has hypertension, treated with perindopril 4 mg once a day and bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg once a day. She also takes aspirin 75 mg once a day, metformin 1 g t.d.s. and twice-daily insulin. She is overweight, despite advice from the practice on diet and exercise, and eats three meals a day: breakfast of cereal and buttered toast, lunch of soup with bread and butter and dinner of meat, potatoes and vegetables. She snacks between meals, especially if she increases her insulin dose. Home blood glucose monitoring reveals levels of 9-14. Her glycated haemoglobin (HbAic) is 10 mmol/l. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. She is very concerned about her cholesterol level, as she been told it is a risk factor for ischaemic heart disease and both her parents died of heart attacks.

## How to approach the case

### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

Take a full history of her:

* Dyslipidemia (many patients know their exact levels)
* Diabetes, including complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy)
* Cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk (age and sex, level of diabetes control, hypertension, smoking status, family history, excess weight, alcohol intake, lack of exercise).

She very probably has dyslipidemia secondary to diabetes, but it is important to be aware of the types of dyslipidaemia (Table 2.28) and consider other potential contributors. The World Health Organization numerical classification is largely superseded in practice by a descriptive one. Hypercholesterolaemia usually results from nutritional factors such as a diet high in saturated fats combined with an underlying polygenic predisposition; monogenic causes are rare. Hypercholesterolaemia may have an entirely genetic cause, as in monogenic familial hypercholesterolaemia (Table 2.28) in which low-density lipoprotein (LDL) is raised from birth.

#### Patient perspective

Acknowledge and explore her concerns about ischaemic heart disease.

#### Past medical history

Explore all the usual CVD suspects, including her diabetes complications.

#### Drug and allergy history

Review her lipid-lowering, blood pressure and diabetes drugs.

#### Family history

Ask about the ages of family members when they suffered CVD events.

#### Social history

Explore her reasons for giving up work and the impact of this.

## Discussion

### Why is cholesterol important?

Cholesterol is essential for cell membrane structure and a precursor for steroid hormone and bile acids.
