## Summary Box - Assessment and Plan

### Unfortunately, she is already taking a good range of evidence-based primary prophylaxis.

Her diet should be reviewed, and there may be an indication for considering a second lipid-lowering agent.

Her overall cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk management is more important than focusing only on her cholesterol level.

Tighter control of her diabetes is imperative in reducing her overall CVD risk, including its benefits on her lipid profile; she might be considered for a long-acting insulin analogue such as glargine and short-acting analogues.

### What are lipoproteins?

Lipoproteins are complexes of lipid and protein that carry hydrophobic plasma lipids, notably cholesterol and triglycerides. Abundance of larger, triglyceride-containing lipoproteins can turn plasma milky. Cholesterol and triglycerides form the core of lipoproteins and phospholipids and apolipoproteins form the surface; the latter are synthesised largely in the liver and are pivotal in lipid transport and metabolism. Lipoproteins are classified, through escalating density, as chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoprotein (IDL), low-density lipoprotein (LDL) and high-density lipoprotein (HDL).

### What types of apolipoprotein are there?

Apo B100 is the major apolipoprotein of VLDL and LDL. It is the ligand for the removal of LDL by the LDL receptor, a cell surface molecule that binds and internalises lipoproteins containing apo B100 or apo E. Apo B48 is the major apolipoprotein of chylomicrons. Apo Cs are present in all plasma lipoproteins (traces in LDL) and tend to inhibit the removal of plasma chylomicrons and VLDL remnants by the liver. Apo CII activates lipoprotein lipase (LPL). LPL hydrolyses triglycerides in chylomicrons and VLDL. Deficiency causes severe hypertriglyceridaemia. Apo CIII inhibits LPL activity. Apo E is found in chylomicrons, VLDL and HDL and is a ligand for their uptake by both the LDL receptor and LDL-receptor-related protein (LRP). There are three major alleles, E2, 3 and 4; E2 binds most easily to the LDL receptor. Apolipoproteins AI, AII and AIV are found mostly on HDL. Apo AI is integral to HDL and its deficiency causes HDL deficiency. It also activates lecithin-cholesterol acetyltransferase (LCAT).

### What happens to dietary (exogenous) lipids? (Fig. 2.6A)

Dietary triglycerides and cholesterol are incorporated into chylomicrons in gut mucosa. Chylomicrons are fat droplets with a very high triglyceride concentration, and the means by which cholesterol is transported from the gut to the liver. They enter the circulation via the thoracic duct, where HDL donates apo CII molecules, activating hydrolysis of triglycerides by LPL to free fatty acids (FFAs) and glycerol on capillary endothelial cells in fat and muscle. FFAs are used for energy in adjacent tissue or stored as fat. Insulin helps stimulate LPL and so diabetes impairs triglyceride clearance, as can LPL deficiency. Most circulating LPL is associated with LDL. Apo E allows chylomicron remnants (after the triglyceride core has been hydrolysed and apo CII has recirculated back to HDL) to bind to hepatic LDL receptors (or similar lipoprotein receptor proteins referred to as LRP). The overall results are utilisation or storage of triglycerides and uptake of cholesterol by the liver. Cholesterol can also be synthesised _de novo_ in the liver by the action of 3-hydroxy-3-methyl-glutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA) reductase, and to a lesser extent cholesterol can be synthesised _de novo_ in the gut or central nervous system. Liver cholesterol can be used for bile acid formation through the action of 7a-hydroxylase, excreted as cholesterol in bile, incorporated into membranes or resected into the circulation either directly as VLDL or by conversion to triglyceride by the action of acyl-CoA:cholesterol acyltransferase and microsomal triglyceride transfer protein.

### What is endogenous lipid transport and metabolism?

This refers to the transfer of lipids from the liver to the peripheral tissues and back.

### How is lipid transported from the liver to peripheral tissues? (Fig. 2.6B)

This is via the apo B100 system, and concerns VLDL, IDL and LDL. In the liver, cholesterol is taken up from chylomicron remnants or synthesised _de novo_ and triglycerides are made from FFAs taken up from plasma or synthesised _de novo_. These core lipids are packaged with apo B100 and phospholipid into VLDL and secreted into plasma where apolipoproteins C and E are added. VLDL is triglyceride rich, even more so in states of triglyceride excess such as overeating, diabetes and alcohol consumption. In the plasma, LPL hydrolyses VLDL triglycerides to create smaller, denser VLDL remnants (also known as IDL). VLDL remnants (IDL) are still relatively triglyceride rich (more so than LDL and less so than VLDL), but progressively lose triglyceride as more and more triglyceride is hydrolysed by LPL. VLDL remnants can re-enter the liver via apo E binding to the LDL receptor or, after step-wise LPL activity, become LDL, apo B100 being the only remaining surface protein on LDL. LDL is the means by which cholesterol is delivered to most tissues. Some LDL is taken up by the liver via the LDL receptor. Some is delivered to tissues such as adrenals and gonads and used for steroid hormone synthesis.

### What influences LDL levels as well as diet?

LDL levels are influenced by LDL receptor availability or activity; they are downregulated when liver cholesterol is replete. Figure 2.6: Lipid transport and sites of drug action. (A) What happens to dietary lipids. (B) Transport of lipid from the liver to peripheral tissues. (C) Transport of lipid from peripheral tissues to the liver. ABCA, ATP-binding cassette transporter A; ACAT, acyl-CoA:cholesterol acyltransferase; CETP, cholesterylester transfer protein; FFA, free fatty acid; HDL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol; HMG-CoA, 3-hydroxy-3-methyl-glutaryl-CoA; LCAT, lecithin-cholesterol acetyltransferase; LDL, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol; MTP, microsomal triglyceride transfer protein; SRA, scavenger receptor; VLDL, very-low-density lipoprotein cholesterol. 

### Why are increased LDL levels dangerous?

Increased LDL and apo B100 are risk factors for CVD, because excess LDL that is not taken up by tissues or the liver and cannot be removed by HDL pathways (below) undergoes lipid peroxidation to become modified LDL which becomes a ligand for alternative, scavenger receptor pathways (e.g. via steroid receptor RNA activator 1 - SRA1 (see Fig. 2.6B) or thrombospondin receptor CD36) present on endothelial cells and macrophages. Oxidised lipoproteins (often the cholesterol esters in LDL or chymomicron remnants) within macrophages transform these into foam cells, the first phase of the atherosclerosis mechanism (Case 2.8).

### How is lipid transported from peripheral tissues to the liver? (Fig. 2.6C)

This is through the apo A1 system, and concerns HDL. It is known as the reverse cholesterol pathway. HDL particles are formed in the plasma from the coalescence of phospholipid-apolipoprotein complexes. Free plasma cholesterol is transferred by ATP binding cassette transporter A1 (ABCA1) to these small, cholesterol-poor HDL particles and esterified by LCAT, which is bound to HDL, to cholesterol ester (CE). This enables HDL to accept more cholesterol and larger HDL particles develop which can be metabolised in one of two ways. The entire HDL particle can be removed from plasma, or its CEs can be exchanged with triglyceride in VLDL, mediated by cholesterol ester transfer protein. The CEs are returned to the liver via VLDL conversion to LDL and LDL liver reception. The triglyceride transferred to HDL is a substrate for LPL and HDL particles are condensed down again to their smaller form. When apo B100 is removed by the liver, reverse cholesterol transfer is complete.

### What is the association between CVD and cholesterol?

As total cholesterol (TC) (and LDL) increases so does CVD risk, the relative risks being similar for those with and without CVD. CVD risk is principally determined by the concentrations of LDL and HDL and to a lesser extent by triglycerides. Lowering cholesterol by diet, drugs or other means decreases CVD risk. A 1 mmol/l decrease in LDL reduces coronary death risk by 11% in the first year, 24% in the second, 33% in the third to fifth and 36% in the sixth and subsequent years. Of course, lipoproteins are only one part of overall CVD risk (Case 2.7) but hyper-cholesterolaemia is the permissive factor that allows other risk factors to operate.

### What is the average plasma TC concentration in the UK?

The average plasma cholesterol concentration in the UK is 5.9 mmol/l, much higher than the 4 mmol/l in rural China and Japan where heart disease is uncommon. Furthermore, there seems to be no lower limit of cholesterol at which coronary risk stops - although the relation become progressively steeper as cholesterol rises - and so it is rational to base desirable targets as those above which coronary risk seems unacceptably high. Most plasma cholesterol is present in LDL, with smaller amounts in VLDL and HDL making up the TC.

### How would you investigate hypercholesterolaemia?

Investigation must be in the context of a detailed clinical assessment, and the likelihood of it being secondary or primary, and in the context of other risk factors. Fasting for 10 hours avoids the post-prandial contribution to plasma triglycerides.

### Are there other ways of assessing dyslipidaemia as well as serum lipid profiles?

Computed tomography scan calcification scoring is one way of further assessing risk in asymptomatic people.

### What are the common dyslipidaemias?

Elevation of TC and LDL has received most attention, particularly because it can be modified by lifestyle changes and drug therapies. The evidence showing that reducing TC and LDL can prevent CVD is strong, based on many randomised controlled trials. Several other types of dyslipidaemia predispose to premature CVD. A particular pattern, termed the atherogenic lipid triad, is more common than others, and consists of VLDL remnants manifested as mildly elevated triglycerides, increased small dense LDL particles, and reduced HDL levels. However, evidence is limited on the effectiveness and safety of intervening in this pattern to reduce CVD risk.

### What do you know about risk levels and treatment targets?

#### Risk levels and assessment

The concept of total CVD risk is discussed in Case 2.7. If a person has a history of coronary heart disease, angina, stroke or transient ischaemic attack, peripheral vascular disease (includes carotid artery disease, aortic aneurysm), diabetes or a monogenic lipid disorder, risk assessment is unnecessary as they are already considered at high risk. Otherwise National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence (NICE) guidelines recommend consideration of intervention for those with a 10-year risk >= 20%.

#### Treatment targets

NICE guidelines recommend 40 mg simvastatin (or drug of similar efficacy and cost) as part of the management strategy for adults over 40 who have a 20% or greater 10-year risk of developing CVD. There is no target level for TC or LDL for primary prevention; 40 mg simvastatin (or drug of similar efficacy and cost) should be offered to all adults with clinical evidence of CVD, increasing to 80 mg simvastatin or a drug of similar efficacy and cost if the TC does not fall to <= 4 mmol/l or the LDL does not fall to <= 2mmol/l.

### How strong is the evidence for lipid-lowering therapy in reducing CVD risk?

The compelling evidence is with statins. The early major statin trials in people with established CVD using simvastatin and pravastatin (4S, CARE, LIPID) and in asymptomatic people at risk of developing CVD (WOSCOPS, AFCAPS/TEXCAPS) showed significant reductions in coronary events, coronary mortality and often all-cause mortality. A meta-analysis of these five trials showed significant reductions of major coronary events by 31%, coronary mortality by 29% and all-cause mortality by 21%. There was no difference in non-CVD mortality. Later statin trials with atorvastatin (GREASE, ASCOT, CARDS, PROVE-IT, TNT, IDEAL, 4D), fluvastatin (LIPS, ALERT), pravastatin (ALIHAT, PROPSER, PROVE-IT) and simvastatin (HPS, A to \(Z\), IDEAL) extended the evidence base to many populations including older people, people with acute coronary disease, people with diabetes, renal transplantation, and in populations hitherto thought to have low TC and LDL concentrations (the Heart Protection Study randomised people with TC > 3.5 mmol/l). Trials looking specifically at women have been absent. Intensive dose statin trials (TNT, IDEAL, PROVE IT, A to _Z_) show conflicting results. The Cholesterol Trialists' Collaboration, in a meta-analysis of 14 statin trials with data from 90 056 patients, showed safe reduction in the 5-year incidence of major coronary events, coronary revascularisation and stroke by about one-fifth (21%) per mmol reduction in LDL, accompanied by decreased all-cause mortality; the linear decrease extends down to the current LDL targets of 2 mmol/l, and those with pre-treatment levels < 3mmol/l have the same relative risk reduction. Efficacy of different statins varies: at a maximum dose of 40 mg pravastatin reduces LDL by 29%, and at a maximum dose of 80 mg simvastatin, atorvastatin and rosuvastatin reduce LDL by 42%, 55% and 58%, respectively. Each doubling of statin dose produces a further 5-6% LDL reduction.

### What lipid-lowering therapies are there?

Lipid-lowering therapies are summarised in Table 2.29.
* Drugs for treatment of hypercholesterolemia include statins, bile acid sequestrants, cholesterol absorption inhibitors and nicotinic acid, and drug combinations include statins and cholesterol absorption inhibitors and statins and bile acid sequestrants.
* Drugs for treatment of hypertriglyceridaemia include fibrates (with substantial evidence of benefit), nicotinic acid and n-3 fatty acids. Mixed dyslipidaemia with elevated triglycerides and low HDL is common. The main strategy is still a statin to treat LDL to target.
* Drugs affecting HDL include statins, fibrates, nicotinic acid and cholesterylester transfer protein (CETP) inhibitors (e.g. torcetrapib, withdrawn because of risks).

### Might statins work in ways other than lipid lowering?

Accelerated atherosclerosis in chronic inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis is not explained by traditional risk factors. Such patients may have increased levels of a CD4 subset of T cells that lack CD28 expression called CD4 null cells, which can also be activated by infection and are more abundant in older people. These cells express interferon-y and may cause a number of effects including endothelial cell death via engagement of their Toll-like receptor TLR2. TLR2 may be regulated by statins and there is evidence for endothelial stabilisation and regression of atherosclerosis with statin therapy, the anti-inflammatory effects of which are being examined in a range of non-CVD conditions including rheumatoid arthritis.

### What is the clinical spectrum of statin-induced myopathy?

The spectrum includes: myalgia (painful muscles without elevated creatine kinase level), myositis (painful muscles with elevated creatine kinase level, generally up to 10x upper limit of normal), rhabdomyolysis (painful muscles with elevated creatine kinase level, generally over 10x upper limit of normal, often with compromised kidneys), and asymptomatic increase in creatine kinase level. The clinical features include fatigue, muscle pain, muscle tenderness, muscle weakness, nocturnal cramping, and tendon pain. Symptoms tend to be proximal, generalised, and worse with exercise.

### What are the risk factors for statin-induced myopathy?

How statin myopathy arises is uncertain, but risk factors include age, female sex, low body mass index, vigorous exercise, comorbidity (diabetes, kidney disease, liver disease, hypothyroidism, intercurrent infection, surgery or trauma), drug interactions especially with drugs that are inhibitors or substrates of the cytochrome P450 pathway (e.g. fibrates, nicotinic acid, calcium channel blockers, ciclosporin, amiodarone, thiazolidinedones, macrolide antibiotics, azole antifungals, protease inhibitors, warfarin), diet (excessive grapefruit or cranberry juice), alcohol and genetic factors (e.g. polymorphisms of the cytochrome P450 isoenzymes). Lipophilic statins (e.g. simvastatin, atorvastatin) are more likely to induce myopathy than hydrophilic ones (e.g. pravastatin, rosuvastatin). 

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt}} \hline
* reduction in intracellular cholesterol & & & & \\  & induces LDL receptor expression & & & & \\  & on & & & & \\  & hepatocyte surface and increased extraction of LDL from blood & & & & & \\  & Should creatine kinase be measured before starting statin therapy and monitored during statin therapy?

Guidance differs. A reasonable compromise is to measure baseline creatine kinase before starting therapy in high-risk groups, but it is unclear what constitutes an acceptable rise in creatine kinase with statins. Slightly raised concentrations of creatine kinase are common in the general population. Physical activity, especially when unaccustomed, may also increase creatine kinase. Routine monitoring in asymptomatic patients is not generally recommended.

### Should liver enzymes be checked in people on statin therapy?

Baseline transaminases are often checked when starting therapy. Routine monitoring is not necessary in the absence of symptoms. If transaminases are raised but less than three times the upper limit of normal, statins are not necessarily precluded.

### How should we react to HDL levels on current evidence?

Low HDL (< 1.0 mmol/l in men, < 1.2 mmol/l in women) remains a strong independent risk factor for CVD. Levels may be improved by exercise and stopping smoking. High levels of HDL are often associated with elevated LDL. While the ratio of the two is often regarded as a useful index of CVD risk, a normal ratio in the setting of raised HDL and LDL has been shown to increase risk, and the efficacy of statins in the Heart Protection Study was independent of HDL levels. The combination effect of statins and torcetrapib may have additive effects on lowering LDL and raising HDL.

### Is HDL cholesterol always good?

HDL-mediated reverse cholesterol transport is thought to be the major mechanism by which HDL protects against atherosclerosis, and plasma levels of HDL and apo AI are inversely proportional to CVD risk. Almost 40% of HDL variation between individuals is genetically determined, one-quarter of which is attributable to polymorphisms of the _CETP_ gene. Lower CETP levels mean higher HDL levels (or at least higher levels of large, cholesterol-rich HDL). Evidence as to whether these mutations are protective is conflicting. Some studies have shown increased CVD risk. Adverse effects of CETP deficiency may be attributable to impairment of reverse cholesterol transport and loss of the antiatherogenic properties of HDL resulting from its increased cholesterol content and larger size. A few studies have even shown CVD reduction with increased HDL particle size.

### Is hypolipidaemia a problem?

It may be secondary to malabsorption. Primary hypolabilipoproteinaemia (low HDL) is a common correlate of hypertriglyceridaemia (prevalence 10-25%). Isolated low HDL due to, for example, _ABCA1_ mutations, is very rare. Hypobetaliopoproteinamias are rare, but the very rare abetaliopproteinaemia with severe hypocholesterolaemia is associated with retinitis pigmentosa, fatty liver, acanthocytes and a Friedrich's ataxia-like syndrome. There is an uncertain but observational relationship between low cholesterol levels and cancer. 

### Does management differ in familial hypercholesterolemia compared with common hypercholesterolemia?

The possibility of familial hypercholesterolemia should always be suspected, especially if the TC is > 7.5 mmol/l, which is typical in familial hypercholesterolemia, and if there is a personal or family history of CVD. High-intensity statin therapy is needed to achieve a > 50% reduction in LDL, increasing to maximum tolerated doses. Ezetimibe is often needed additionally. Early referral to a cardiologist is important if coronary disease is suspected.

### Is the benefit of statin therapy as clear for stroke as for coronary heart disease?

The correlation between cholesterol and stroke is less clear than for coronary heart disease, but statin therapy is recommended.

## Case 2.14 Hypertension

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

##### Peer Doctor

Re: Mr Thomas Roberts, aged 39 years

Thank you for seeing Mr Roberts, who was diagnosed with hypertension 2 months ago at an insurance medical, urgently. Blood pressure then was 190/114 and baseline investigations revealed normal urinalysis, normal electrolytes and creatinine and a normal ECG. He did admit to occasional palmitopathies. His fasting blood glucose was normal and his cholesterol was 5.4 mmol/l. He was started on atenolol 50 mg and reviewed 1 month later, at which time blood pressure was 180/110 and amlodipine was added. He has noticed intermittent headaches and sweats since then, and blood pressure today is 200/120.1 am concerned that there may be a secondary cause for his hypertension and that the headaches may represent worsening incipient complications.

##### Years sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr Thomas Roberts is a 39-year-old solicitor recently diagnosed with hypertension at an insurance medical for a second mortgage as he and his wife were buying a new house for their expanding family of three children and two dogs. He has no past medical history of note but has seen his GP regularly over the last 2 months for first-line investigations (blood tests and electrocardiogram, which he has been advised are normal) and started treatment, initially atenolol, and now additional amlodipine. He has noticed occasional episodes of his heart racing over the last few months, which he has attributed to stress, and occasional sweats, which he has put down to being 'less fit than he used to be'. He has developed headaches since starting the amlodipine, and a small amount of ankle swelling. He is concerned about being on medication potentially lifelong and also being declined life assurance.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

##### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

The objectives when evaluating hypertension are: assessment for possible causes, assessment for target organ damage and planning of appropriate investigation and treatment. Referral to secondary care is indicated in the following circumstances:

* A need for urgent treatment, as in accelerated or malignant hypertension (blood pressure > 180/110 with papilloedema or retinal haemorrhage)
* Suspected phaeochromocytoma (labile or postural hypotension, headache, palpitations, pallor or diaphoresis)
* Other symptoms suggesting a secondary cause
* Young age (< 20 years or needing treatment < 30 years)
* Sudden onset or worsening hypertension
* Resistance to a multidrug regimen (three or more drugs) or problems with intolerance or multiple contraindications)
* Special situations such as unusual blood pressure variability, possible white-coat hypertension or pregnancy.

##### Consider causes

Most hypertension is 'idiopathic', a conspiracy of nefarious and poorly understood genetics and a rogue environment. Secondary causes are listed in Table 2.30.

##### Patient perspective

Explore his concerns about hypertension and its effects on life assurance for a mortgage.

##### Past medical history

##### Consider target organs

Possible target organ damage in hypertension is listed in Box 2.40. 

**Consider risk factors for cardiovascular disease.** The usual suspects include age and sex, smoking, diabetes, cholesterol and family history. Contributory factors include excess weight, excess alcohol (> 3 units/day for men, > 2 units/day for women), excess salt, lack of exercise and environmental stress.

#### Drug and allergy history

Ask about drugs exacerbating hypertension. These include non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, steroids and ciclox-sporin. Ask about possible side effects of antihypertensives (calcium channel blockers can cause headaches, flushing and ankle swelling).

#### Family history

Ask about renal disease (e.g. adult polycystic kidney disease).

#### Social history

Explore his concerns about hypertension and its effects on life assurance for a mortgage. A detailed social history, including alcohol intake and substance misuse (such as cocaine), is important.

## Summary BOX - Assessment and PLAN

* All hypertensive patients should have a thorough history and examination, urinalysis, serum creatinine and electrolytes, fasting blood glucose, lipid profile with a total cholesterol-high-density lipoprotein ratio (ideally fasted for evaluation of triglycerides) and an electrocardiogram.
* Further investigations in this case should include screening for Conn's syndrome, measurement of urinary catecholamines on three separate occasions to exclude pheochromocytoma, measurement of thyroid function and possibly renal imaging and echocardiogram. While a renal cause is less likely with normal urinalysis, renal artery stenosis is possible and definitive exclusion would require computed tomography angiography or magnetic resonance angiography.
* This patients' headaches may be the result of amlodipine.
* Assure him that investigations will determine the optimum treatment and that, while hypertension needs to be declared in his application, it should not deny him life assurance.

## Discussion

### How would you investigate hypertension in a young person?

Hypertension warrants further investigations for secondary causes in young people (aged < 40 years), those with blood pressure resistant to treatment, and those with a family history of hypertension or stroke at age < 50 years. Before further investigations hypertension needs to be

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} \hline
**Secondary causes** & **Clinical clues** \\ \hline Renal & & Higenaturia \\  & & Known history or family history of renal disease \\ Endocrine & Primary & Consider in this patient’s age group \\  & hyperalgalosternism & Symptoms rare, but may include tetany, muscle weakness and polyuria \\  & (Conn’s syndrome) & Sodium retention increases the extracellular fluid volume but water retention is usually insufficient for development of oedema \\ \hline Phaeochromocytoma & Sweats and palpitations \\  & Paroxysmal symptoms \\  & 90\% have at least one of three symptoms: sweats, palpitations or anxiety \\  & headaches & The 10\% “rule” of these catecholamine-secreting tumours is that 10\% are bilateral, 10\% extra-adrenal, 10\% malignant and 10\% familial \\ \hline Cushing’s syndrome & Unusual without other symptoms or signs \\ \hline Aortic coarctation & Blood pressure discrepancy between right and left \\ Genetic & Defined genetic causes should be considered in young patients (e.g. aged 20) and include those in Box 2.41. \\ Drugs & \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 30: Secondary causes of hypertension established by blood pressure measurements on at least three occasions (cuffs too small give false high readings); 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure measurement may be warranted.

#### Untrnalysis, creatinine and electrolytes

These are essential to screen for hypokalaemia and renal disease. Only a third of patients with primary hyperalosteronism exhibit frank hypokalaemia, although potassium concentrations in most are at the lower end of the reference range and sodium concentrations at the higher end, with hypokalaemic alkalosis common.

#### Electrocardiography, chest radiography and renal ultrasonography

Electrocardiography and chest radiography may show signs of left ventricular hypertrophy if hypertension is long-standing. With normal urinalysis and renal function renal parenchymal disease is unlikely and renal ultrasonography of limited benefit. None of these tests help to establish cause.

#### Measurement of plasma aldosterone and renin

Primary hyperalosteronism is the most prevalent form of secondary hypertension, accounting for 5-10% of all hypertensive patients. Screening identifies a high plasma aldosterone concentration and usually markedly suppressed plasma renin activity in response to aldosterone-mediated volume increase, giving a high ratio of plasma aldosterone concentration to plasma renin activity (or a low ambulant or erect renin-aldosterone ratio). Exact cut-off values depend on the laboratory and assays used, a ratio of > 750 (aldosterone in pmol/l and plasma renin activity in ng/ml/h) with an absolute aldosterone concentration of > 400 pmol/l highly suggestive.

Antihypertensive drugs do not have to be stopped routinely before measurements (except for spironolactone, eplerenone and amiloride) but in some patients may contribute to false-positive and -negative results. Calcium channel blockers are suitable antihypertensives pre-investigation as they do not interfere with the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS).

Positive screening warrants confirmation of the diagnosis, usually by the observation that aldosterone fails to suppress with oral or intravenous sodium loading or a fludrocortisone suppression test (salt tablets, saline or fludrocortisone 0.2 mg daily for 5 days). Then to differentiate between the two principal causes - a unilateral aldosterone-producing adenoma (Conn's adenoma) and idiopathic hyperalosteronism - selective adrenal venous sampling is the optimal test. Imaging is of limited value because of the high incidence of adrenal 'incidentalomas'.

#### 24-hour urinary-free catecholamines or plasma-free metanephrines

Tumours that produce catecholamines, such as phaochromocytomas and paragangliomas, are rare (0.1-0.6% of hypertensive patients), but important to exclude even if only mild headaches and no other classic symptoms (paroxysms of palpitations, sweating, and pallor) are present. Testing for plasma-free metanephrines is now the optimal test (sensitivity 99%, specificity 86%) but of limited availability and so screening is still predominantly based on excretion of urinary catecholamines (epinephrine (adrenaline), norepinephrine (noradrenaline), dopamine, metanephrine, normetanephrine, and vanillylmandelic acid). False-positive test results can be prevented if the patient avoids consumption of caffeine, nuts, and chocolate for 24 hours before and during urine collection.

#### Renal imaging

Magnetic resonance angiography is the investigation of choice to exclude renal artery stenosis. In the absence of a renal artery bruit, it should be performed if screening tests for primary hyperalosteronism and phaochromocytoma are negative.

### How may the RAAS contribute to the pathophysiology of hypertension?

Hypertension can result from raised peripheral resistance, excessive activation of the RAAS, excessive sympathetic nervous system activity or alteration in the balance of endothelial-derived vasoactive molecules. A range of genetic factors contributes. Renin is released from the juxtap glomerular apparatus of the kidney in response to low sodium or reduced renal perfusion. Renin converts angiotensioner to angiotensin I (AI). AI is converted in the lung to angiotensin II (AII) by the action of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE). AI restores circulating volume via release from the adrenal cortex of aldosterone, which promotes sodium and water retention at the distal renal tubule in exchange for potassium and hydrogen, vasoconstriction and thirst. Aldosterone secretion is controlled almost exclusively by the RAAS and not by adrenocorticotrophic hormone. Numerous abnormalities of the RAAS can lead to hyperalosteronism (Box 2.41).

### How is phaechromocytoma managed?

Clonidine suppression testing may be used for suspected phaechromocytoma where urinalysis gives equivocal results. Where confirmed, phaechromocytoma requires specialist management, including a search for the tumour with structural or functional imaging. Treatment with beta blockers is contraindicated without a-blockade (e.g. phenoxybenzamine) because unopposed a-blockade can cause severe vasoconstriction and pulmonary oedema; this is especially important perioperative.

### Why is hypertension important?

Hypertension is one of the most important preventable causes of premature morbidity and mortality.

### How is hypertension defined?

Definitions are given in Box 2.42. 

### Primary hyperalosteronism

This is usually caused by an aldosterone-secreting adenoma (Conn's syndrome). Rarely it can result from bilateral hyperplasia or carcinoma. Renn production is suppressed.

### Secondary hyperalosteronism

This is hyper-retinaemic, usually in response to low sodium or reduced renal perfusion. The kidney perceives low circulating volume, which may be apparent as in renal artery stenosis or true as in dehydration.

### Pseudohyperalosteronism (Liddle's syndrome)

This occurs when the sodium channel (ENac) in the tubule normally stimulated by aldosterone is switched permanently 'on' in the absence of aldosterone by an autosomal dominant mutation in its gene. Aldosterone levels are low, but renin is suppressed. Hypertension may respond to amiloride. Liddle's syndrome is one of a number of genetically determined causes of low-reinin hypertension. Two others are glucocorticoid-remediable hypertension and apparent mineralocorticoid excess.

### Glucocorticoid-remediable/suppressive aldosteroneism (GRA)

Two closely related enzymes, 11B-hydroxylase and aldosterone synthase, normally coordinate synthesis of glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid in the adrenal gland. In GRA a chimeric gene (autosomal dominant), comprising the aldosterone synthase gene with an upstream adrenocorticotrophic hormone (ACTH)-dependent promoter, results in expression of aldosterone synthase under ACTH regulation. Aldosterone levels are high and renin is suppressed. Hypertension may respond to dexamethasone, which suppresses ACTH.

### Box 2.42 Definitions of hypertension

#### Stage 1 hypertension

Clinic blood pressure is >= 140/90 mmHg and subsequent ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) daytime average or home blood pressure monitoring (HBPM) average blood pressure is >= 135/85 mmHg.

#### Stage 2 hypertension

Clinic blood pressure is >= 160/100 mmHg and subsequent ABPM daytime average or HBPM average blood pressure is >= 150/95 mmHg.

#### Severe hypertension

Clinic systolic blood pressure is >= 180 mmHg, or clinic diastolic blood pressure is >= 110 mmHg.

### How should blood pressure be measured?

Devices for measuring blood pressure must be properly validated, maintained and regularly recalibrated. An appropriate cuff size should be used. The environment should be standardised and relaxed, with the person quiet and seated, and their arm outstretched and supported. The radial or brachial pulse should be palpated before measuring blood pressure, since automated devices may not measure blood pressure accurately if there is pulse irregularity (e.g. atrial fibrillation) when blood pressure should be measured manually, using direct auscultation over the brachial artery.

#### Postural hypotension

In people with symptoms of postural hypotension (falls or postural dizziness) blood pressure should be measured either supine or seated, and again standing for at least 1 minute prior to measurement. If the systolic blood pressure falls by 20 mmHg or more when the person is standing medication should be reviewed and subsequent blood pressures measured standing with specialist investigation if symptoms of postural hypotension persist. 

### How is hypertension diagnosed?

#### Clinic blood pressure

Blood pressure is measured in both arms. If the difference in readings between arms is more than 20 mmHg, they should be repeated. If the difference in readings between arms remains more than 20 mmHg on the second measurement, subsequent blood pressures in the arm with the higher reading should be measured. If blood pressure measured in the clinic is >=140/90 mmHg. a second measurement should be taken and if substantially different from the first, a third. The lower of the last two measurements should be recorded as clinic blood pressure.

#### Confirming the diagnosis

If clinic blood pressure is >=140/90 mmHg, ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM) should be offered to confirm the diagnosis of hypertension. If a person is unable to tolerate ABPM, home blood pressure monitoring (HBPM) is a suitable alternative. While waiting to confirm the diagnosis, investigations for target organ damage and a formal assessment of cardiovascular risk should be obtained.

#### Severe hypertension

Anthhypertensive drug treatment should be considered immediately, without waiting for results of ABPM or HBPM, for people with severe hypertension.

#### Specialist investigations

Referral to specialist care should be the same day if there is accelerated hypertension (blood pressure usually higher than 180/110 mmHg with signs of papilloedema and/or retinal haemorrhage) or suspected phenochromocytoma (labile or postural hypotension, headache, palpitations, pallor and diabetes). Referral to specialist care should be considered in people with signs and symptoms suggesting a secondary cause of hypertension.

#### Using ambulatory or home blood pressure monitoring

ABPM requires at least two measurements per hour during usual waking hours, and the average value of at least 14 measurements taken during the person's usual waking hours to confirm the diagnosis. HBPM requires that: for each blood pressure recording, two consecutive measurements are taken, at least 1 minute apart and seated; blood pressure is recorded twice daily, ideally morning and evening: blood pressure recording continues for at least 4 days, ideally for 7 days; measurements taken on the first day are discarded and the average value of all the remaining measurements used to confirm the diagnosis.

#### If hypertension is not diagnosed

Blood pressure measurement should be undertaken at least every 5 years, more so if clinic blood pressure is close to 140/90 mmHg. If there is evidence of target organ damage such as left ventricular hypertrophy, albuminuria or proteinuria, investigations for alternative causes of the target organ damage should be considered.

#### How are cardiovascular risk and target organ damage assessed?

A formal estimation of cardiovascular risk is needed to discuss prognosis and health-care options with people with hypertension, both for raised blood pressure and for other modifiable risk factors. Target organ damage assessment should include:

* Testing for the presence of proteinuria by a urine sample for estimation of the albumin-creatinine ratio and testing for haematuria using a reagent strip
* A blood sample to measure plasma glucose, electrolytes, creatinine, estimated glomerular filtration rate, serum total cholesterol and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol
* Examination of the fundi for the presence of hypertensive retinopathy
* A 12-lead electrocardiogram.

For people aged under 40 with stage 1 hypertension, specialist evaluation of secondary causes of hypertension and a more detailed assessment of target organ damage should be considered. This is because 10-year cardiovascular risk assessments can underestimate the lifetime risk of cardiovascular events in these people.

### What lifestyle interventions should be considered for hypertension?

These include: improving diet and exercise patterns; reducing alcohol consumption, stopping excessive consumption of coffee and other caffeine-rich products; keeping salt intake low; smoking cessation; relaxation therapies (evidence debatable).

#### How should antihypertensive drug treatment be initiated and titrated?

If possible, drugs should be taken once daily. Treatment for isolated systolic hypertension (systolic blood pressure >=160 mmHg) should be the same as for both raised systolic and raised diastolic blood pressure. Treatment in people aged over 80 years should be the same as treatment for people aged 55-80 years, taking into account any comorbidities. An ACE inhibitor should not be combined with an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB). Management in pregnancy or breastfeeding is a specialised area. There are four treatment steps to consider (Fig. 2.7, Table 2.31).

### How is treatment monitored?

Clinic blood pressure measurement should be used to monitor the response to treatment. For people identified as having a 'white-coat effect' - a discrepancy of more than 20/10 mmHg between clinic and average daytime ABPM or average HBPM blood pressure measurements at the time of diagnosis - ABPM or HBPM may be considered as an adjunct. concept of 'beyond blood pressure' whereby different drug classes are believed to be advantageous over and above their blood-pressure-lowering effects. This is certainly true for targeted patients (e.g. ACE inhibitors if there is diabetic nephropathy).

The HOPE trial analysed rampiril versus placebo in people at high risk of cardiovascular disease (CVD) events; 80% had known coronary disease. Average blood pressure was 139/79 mmHg at entry. Blood pressure reduction achieved was modest, 3.3/1.4 mmHg. In other words, for a very small blood pressure reduction there was a very large decrease in CVD events, promoting the notion of the added value of ACE inhibitors beyond blood pressure lowering. Risk reductions were as follows: myocardial infarction 20%, stroke 32%, CVD death 26%, all deaths 16%, new-onset diabetes 25%.

The LIFE trial compared losartan with aenolol in older hypertensive people with left ventricular hypertrophy and showed that for similar reduction in blood pressure the ARB produced better results with respect to stroke. On closer analysis it appears that this can be largely explained by ARB reduction of new-onset atrial fibrillation rates (33%).

The Blood Pressure Lowering Treatment Trialists' Collaboration 2003 meta-analysis assessed data from 29 trials involving 162 341 patients and concluded that there was no significant difference in cardiovascular events or mortality between ACE inhibitors, beta blockers, calcium channel blockers or diuretics. A caveat was that ACE inhibitors might have more benefits than other agents in stroke.

However, the ASCOT blood pressure trial was stopped early because of a reduction in all-cause mortality and additional cardiovascular and stroke benefits in favour of amlodipine and perindopril over atenolol and a thiazide. Amlodipine and perindopril did result in an overall slightly greater blood pressure reduction, suggesting again that blood pressure lowering is key. ASCOT impacted significantly blood pressure guidelines, which had previously recommended an AB/CD rule giving beta blockers equal weight with other agents.

It seems sensible to conclude that differences between treatment regimens can largely be explained by their blood-pressure-lowering effects. Beta blockers, while not preferred as initial routine therapy, are not ineffective but may merely be less effective than other agents. There may still be compelling indications for their use as first-line treatment (see Table 2.31), and they may be preferable in younger patients, for women of childbearing age, patients with increased sympathetic overdrive and patients intolerant of ACE inhibitors or ARBs. Refractory hypertension not responsive to modern prescribing occasionally may be tackled with centrally acting sympatholytics such as maxonidine or methyldopa, or vasodilators such as hydralazine.

#### Prescribing steps if no compelling indications or contraindications

These have been outlined (Fig. 2.7, Table 2.31).

Figure 2.7: Summary of antihypertensive drug treatment . (From National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence 2011 CG 127 Hypertension, with permission.) 

#### Compelling and potential indications and contraindications

In many, particularly older, patients there may be compelling reasons to choose or not to choose a drug that deviates from the prescribing steps. These are shown in Table 2.32.

### What is the concern about ACE inhibitors and ARBs being used in combination?

In the ONTARGET trial of ramipril versus telmisartan versus both in reducing CVD death, myocardial infarction, stroke and hospitalisation for heart failure, there was no apparent benefit from both agents, despite a modest further reduction in blood pressure; the combination also provoked increased adverse effects such as syncope and increased need for dialysis. Renal outcomes in the ONTARGET study were similarly not beneficial for the combination of drugs. Further, in the TRANSCEND study of telmisartan in high-risk patients intolerant of an ACE inhibitor, there was no benefit seen from telmisartan. And so this creates an apparent puzzle: in HOPE, an ACE inhibitor was shown to be better than placebo; in ONTARGET, an ARB and an ACE inhibitor were shown to be equally effective; yet in TRANSCEND, an ARB was not shown to be better than placebo when an ACE inhibitor was not tolerated. The answer to this might lie in the fact that in the TRANSCEND group of high-risk patients a great deal of preventive treatment was already being applied and the ceiling of benefit had already been reached. The current best conclusions from this are that ACE inhibitors and ARBs have been shown to be equally effective, but that the combination of an ACE inhibitor and ARB is associated with more adverse outcomes without an increase in benefit and cannot be recommended. Likewise, direct renin blockers are unlikely to be superior to other RAAS blockers. It appears unwise to totally block the RAAS, which exists for a reason, such as keeping us upright!

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Treatment & Comments & Treatment \\ Step 1 & This should be offered to: & People aged under 55 years should be offered an ACE inhibitor or ARB. \\ People aged under 80 with stage 1 & People aged over 55 years and blade people of African or Caribbean family & origin of any age should be offered a CCB. If a CCB is not suitable, for example because of oedema or intolerance, or if there is evidence of heart failure or a high risk of heart failure, a thiazole-like diuretic should be offered. If treatment with a diuretic is being started, or changed, a thiazole-like diuretic, such as chloridation (12.5–25.0 mg once daily) or indapamide (1.5 mg modified-release once daily or 2.5 mg once daily), is preferred to a conventional thiazole diuretic such as bendrolfumethiazide or hydrochlorothiazide. \\  & Beta blockers are not preferred in step 1. However, they may be considered for younger people if ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated or not tolerated or there is evidence of increased sympathetic drive, and for women of childbearing potential. \\ Step 2 & If blood pressure is not controlled by step 1 & A CCB is not suitable, a thiazole-like diuretic should be offered. For black people of African or Caribbean family origin, an ARB in preference to an ACE inhibitor should be considered, in combination with a CCB. \\ Step 3 & Before step 3 treatment, medication should be reviewed to ensure step 2 treatment is at optimal or best-tolerated doses. \\ Step 4 & Clinic blood pressure that remains & Further diuretic therapy with low-dose (25 mg once daily) spironolactone if blood potassium level is 4.5 mmol / l or lower (caution in people with reduced renal function) because of increased risk of hyperkalaemia. \\  & Should be regarded as resistant hypertension. & Further diuretic therapy with a higher dose thiazol-like diuretic diuted blood potassium level is higher than 4.5 mmol / l with diuretic therapy blood sodium and potassium and renal function should be measured within 1 month and repeated as required. \\  & If further diuretic therapy is not tolerated or contraindicated or is ineffective, an alpha or beta blocker may be considered. \\  & If blood pressure remains uncontrolled with optimal or maximum tolerated doses of four drugs, expert advice is needed. \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.31: Antihypertensive treatment if no compelling indications or contraindications 

## Is isolated systolic hypertension (ISH) important in older people?

Left ventricular contraction unleashes an arterial pressure wave, which on meeting points of natural resistance such as vessel bifurcation produces a smaller backward pressure wave that is reflected back towards the aorta. This reflected wave is greater in states of arterial stiffness or arteriosclerosis, and indeed in ISH, and provides a general measure of fitness of the vascular tree. It increases aortic systolic pressure and reduces stroke volume and sets up, to an extent, a vicious cycle; arterial stiffness and increased pulse wave velocity increase systolic blood pressure with age.

ISH was once wrongly interpreted as a benign part of ageing, like greying hair, and it was thought that there was a J curve such that if systolic blood pressure fell below a certain point diastolic blood pressure would be so low as to compromise coronary filling during diastole. These were incorrect assumptions, and abundant evidence reveals a strong linear relationship between blood pressure and stroke risk.

Systolic blood pressure is the strongest predictor of CVD events in people over 40 years, and heart failure and CVD events are effectively prevented by treating ISH.

## Acuological problems

### Case 2.15 HEADACHE

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When
