* Previous fragility fracture, particularly hip, wrist or spine (vertebral deformity/collapse, height loss/kynbosis)
* Parental history of hip fracture
* Ever long-term use of glucocorticoids (any oral dose for at least 3 months)
* Current smoking
* Daily alcohol consumption of three or more units
* Secondary osteoporosis including: rheumatoid arthritis, untreated hypogonadism in men and women, organ transplantation, type 1 diabetes, hyperthyroidism, gastrointestinal disease, chronic liver disease and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

Although falls are an important risk factor for fracture they are not used in the FRAX algorithm because the effect of treatment on reducing falls risk is unclear. Other secondary causes of osteoporosis include: rheumatological disease (osteogenesis imperfecta, Marlan's syndrome, Ehlers-Danios syndrome, homocystinuria); endocrine disease (primary hyperthyroidism, Cushing's disease, hyperprolactinaemia); malignancy (myeloma, lymphoma, leukaemia, mastocytosis); drugs (heparin, aromatase inhibitors, androgen deprivation therapy, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, proton pump inhibitors, thiazolidenedines). Other risk factors for osteoporosis include low dietary calcium, vitamin D deficiency, physical inactivity and high caffeine intake.

The three most important factors independent of BMD are age over 65 years, a previous fragility fracture and a strong family history of fracture.

#### 2.5.1 Past medical history

#### 2.5.2 Explore factors for risk of osteoporosis and fractures

Risk factors are listed in Box 2.57.

#### 2.5.3 Drug and allergy history

Ask about drug treatments, in particular repeated courses of corticosteroids for conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease or inflammatory bowel disease.

#### 2.5.4 Family history

Elicit any family history of osteoporosis.

#### 2.5.5 Social history

Ask about smoking or heavy alcohol ingestion. Ask how she is coping at home.

## 3 Summary BOX - Assessment and PLAN

She has strong osteoporosis risk factors and may have sustained a thoracic compression fracture.

There is nothing other than back pain, which is a very common symptom, to suggest malignancy.

A full screen of blood tests including inflammatory markers should be requested, together with a thoracic spine X-ray.

She merits a bone densitometry scan.

## 4 Discussion

### What is the scale of the osteoporosis problem?

It is estimated that around 2 million women in the UK have osteoporosis. After the menopause, the prevalence of osteoporosis increases markedly with age, from approximately 2% at 50 years rising to more than 25% at 80 years. One in two women and one in five men over 50 years will develop an osteoporotic fracture, the lifetime risk of a vertebral fracture one in three, and a hip fracture one in five. The incidence of an osteoporotic fracture increases markedly with age in women after 45 years, mainly forearm fractures up to 65 years, hip fractures then rising exponentially, and in men fragility fractures after 75 years. Post-menopausal women with an initial fracture are at substantially greater risk of subsequent fractures, e.g. a woman with a vertebral fracture has an increased relative risk of 4.4 for a further vertebral fracture, 2.3 for a hip fracture and 1.4 for a wrist fracture. It is estimated that annually there are 180 000 osteoporosis-related symptomatic fractures in England and Wales, 70 000 hip, 25 000 clinical vertebral and 41 000 wrist. There are marked geographic variations, osteoporosis being most common in white and Asian populations.

### What do you understand by bone remodelling?

Bone comprises collagen (mostly type 1) and other proteins and minerals (mostly calcium and phosphate) within hydroxyapatite crystals. There are two types - cortical bone (80%) is dense, envelops the marrow cavity and forms Haversian systems or concentric lamellae around a central canal of blood vessels; trabecular bone (20%) is 'spongy', less dense and forms the centre of long bones, flat bones and vertebrae as a meshwork of trabeculae and marrow-filled spaces and is more metabolically active. Bone provides structural support, is a reservoir for mineral exchange and is dynamic, about 10% being remodelled at any time; it is remodelled in multicellular bone-remodelling units (BMIs), beginning with resorption and ending in new bone formation within the cavity.

#### Bone resorption

Remodelling begins with bone resorption. Osteoclast precursors in blood, derived from the monocyte-macrophage lineage, are attracted to skeletal micro-damage, where they differentiate into mature osteoclasts by activation of their RANK (receptor activator of nuclear factor-kB) receptors by RANK ligands (RANKL) expressed on osteoblasts and other bone cells. The interaction is inhibited by osteoprotegerin (OPG), a 'decoy' molecule that is analogous to RANK and also binds RANKL. RANK and OPG are members of the tumour necrosis factor (TNF) receptor family, and RANKL is part of the TNF family. Other molecules are involved in differentiation of myeloid precursors to osteoclasts and in transducing signals from RANK to nucleus. Osteoclasts contain membrane pumps that release acid, which dissolves hydroxyapatite and allows proteolytic enzymes to degrade bone matrix, one of the important ones being cathepsin K. When resorption is complete, osteoclasts undergo apoptosis, heralding bone formation.

#### Bone formation

This begins with attraction of osteoblast precursors derived from marrow stroma to the resorption site. Osteoblasts mature in response to transcription factors Cbfa1 and osterix (Oxx), which bind to the promoter of osteoblast-specific genes such as osteocalia, type-1 collagen and alkaline phosphatase. 'Bone morphogenic proteins' are other growth factors thought to contribute to the formation and differentiation of osteoblast precursors. Lipoprotein receptor-related protein 5 is a further key regulator of bone formation acting downstream of Cbfa1 and Oxx and stimulates bone formation when activated by the Wnt protein family, effects opposed by a protein called sclerostin produced by osteoclasts which inhibits bone formation. Mature osteoblasts lay down uncalcified matrix (osteoid) on the bone surface, and this calcifies in about 10 days to form mature mineralised bone. Alkaline phosphatase, produced by osteoblasts, promotes mineralization by degrading pyrophosphate, an inhibitor of mineralisation. During formation, some osteoblasts become trapped in bone matrix and differentiate into osteocytes, the most abundant cells in bone. Osteocytes produce signalling molecules in response to mechanical loading and have a role in sensing mechanical strain.

### What regulates bone remodelling?

It is regulated by circulating hormones and local cytokines. Mechanical loading increases formation whereas pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-1 (IL-1) and TNF increase remodelling. Calciotropic hormones such as parathyroid hormone and 1,25-hydroxyvitamin D increase remodelling to maintain plasma calcium homeostasis. Remodelling is increased by thyroid hormone and growth hormone and suppressed by oestrogen, androgens and calcitonin. Many hormones act by modulating the expression of local cytokines, e.g. sex hormone deficiency enhances the expression of IL-1 and IL-6. Ultimately, the final common pathway of regulation by hormones and cytokines appears to be the RANKL-RANK-OPG system.

### How do problems with bone remodelling lead to disease?

Most bone diseases result from abnormal remodelling (Table 42) that compromises its structure and strength and leads to pain, deformity, fractures and abnormal mineral homeostasis.

### What is the pathophysiology of osteoporosis?

#### Bone mineral density (BMD)

Low BMD is one of the key predisposing factors for osteoporotic fractures. Adult BMD is determined by both _acquisition of peak bone mass_ during adolescence and the _degree of subsequent bone loss_. These processes are regulated by genetic and environmental factors at the level of BMIs. Each remodelling cycle is balanced - resorption equalling formation - and lasts 90-130 days. Remodelling cycles can become imbalanced, invariably owing to excess resorption, often because of changes in hormones, dietary intake or mechanical loading.

#### Acquisition of peak bone mass

Acquisition of BMD is most rapid at 12-15 years and peak BMD is attained by 20-25 years. Genetic factors, sex hormone status and dietary calcium are the main regulators of peak BMD. Impaired acquisition of peak BMD is responsible for 60-70% of the variance in BMD at any age.

#### Bone loss

Hormonal and environmental factors are the strongest determinants of bone loss after the fourth decade in men and women. During the menopause, oestrogen deprivation enhances bone loss, causing around 1% loss per year, but in a small proportion of women a loss of spine BMD up to 5% per year may occur. It is not possible to prospectively identify this group. Although formation accelerates in an attempt to match resorption around the menopause, it does not catch up. Young amenorthoice athletes are at greater risk of osteoporosis because of impaired acquisition of peak BMD and accelerated bone loss. In the elderly, there is chronic uncoupling or imbalance of resorption and formation - resorption normal or slightly increased and formation suppressed, normal or only slightly increased - together with oestrogen or androgen deprivation and often calcium and vitamin D deficiency (dietary, and reduced activation of vitamin D) and secondary hyperparathyroidism accounting for bone resorption at least equivalent to menopausal levels.

#### Fractures

An osteoporotic fracture occurs as a result of major or minor trauma to a bone of reduced quantity and quality. Fractures in virtually all sites in post-menopausal women can be considered osteoporotic excepting facial and compound road traffic accident trauma. Although there is a strong inverse relationship between BMD and fracture risk, other mechanical factors contribute to bone strength and absolute fracture risk and include the rate of turnover, cortical and trabecular thickness and bone shape; trabecular perforation occurs with increased turnover and leads to disproportionate loss of strength for the amount of bone lost. The lower the BMD, the less force is needed to fracture.

### How is osteoporosis defined?

The definition is based on the standard deviation of BMD, called a T score, in relation to the young adult normal mean BMD (Table 2.43).

### How would you measure BMD?

Dual-energy X-ray absorption (DXA) employs two energy peaks of X-rays that are absorbed to different extents by bone and soft tissue, and BMD is calculated in g/cm2 using simultaneous equations. The result is compared with two reference ranges - young adults and age-matched adults. Those at risk of osteoporosis may be targeted for DXA scanning. Although osteophytes and aortic calcification may affect reliability, DXA scanning is the most reliable test available for BMD. Quantitative calcaneal or patella ultrasound and quantitative computed tomography of spine, femur or radius are sometimes used to assess BMD.

### Are there any other investigations you might consider in osteoporosis?

Over 90% of patients have no secondary cause. Investigations in selected patients, often men, in whom a secondary cause is suspected are listed in Table 2.44.

### Do biochemical markers of bone turnover have an investigative place?

Neither markers of formation (e.g. bone alkaline phosphatase, serum osteoclast) nor markers of resorption are used in clinical practice.

### What treatments are there for osteoporosis?

Drug treatments are described in Box 2.58. Smoking and excessive alcohol should be discouraged. Weight-bearing exercise such as walking is beneficial and non-weight-bearing exercise may reduce fracture risk by improving

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{|l l l|} \hline
**Investigation** & **Reason** \\ \hline \hline \multicolumn{1}{|l}{follicle-stimulating hormone} & \multicolumn{1}{l}{Detects menopause in hysterectomised women} \\  & with conserved ovaries – osteoporosis risk higher despite ovarian presence \\ \hline \hline \multicolumn{1}{|l}{Immunoglobulin electrophoresis} & \multicolumn{1}{l}{Myeloma} \\ \hline \hline Renal and liver function & Chronic kidney disease / liver disease \\ \hline \hline Calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase & \multicolumn{1}{l}{‘Bone profile’} \\ \hline Thyroid function tests & Hyperthyroidism \\ \hline \hline Testosterone & Male hypogonadism \\ \hline Full blood picture / biochemistry / coeliac autoantibodies & Malabsorption \\ \hline \hline Partiallyroid hormone & If hypercalaemia \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.44: Secondary investigations in osteoporosis

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{|l l l|} \hline
**T score** & **Bone disease** & **Fracture risk** \\ \hline \hline \(>\) –1.0 & Normal & Low \\ \hline –1 to –2.5 & Osteopenia & Average \\ \hline \(<\) –2.5 & Osteoprocis & High \\ \hline \(<\) –2.5 and fracture & Severe osteoporosis & Very high \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.43: T score strength and coordination and reducing the risk of falls. Loss of the effect of gravity on the skeleton (e.g. immobilisation) produces dramatic loss of BMD because of uncoupling (resorption increasing dramatically and formation markedly suppressed), the evidence greatest in younger people. Physiotherapy can help at risk patients in reducing falls and with pain management. Hip protectors may be used in institutionalised people but otherwise there is limited evidence of benefit. Analgesia, kyphoplasty or vertebroplasty, the injection of 'cement', decrease pain risk of breast cancer, venous thromboembolism and cardiovascular disease.

### Dual action treatment - strontium ranelate

Two pivotal studies, Spinal Osteoporosis Therapeutic Intervention (SOTI) and Treatment of Peripheral Osteoporosis (TROPOS), developed the place of strontium in osteoporosis. In SOTI 1649 post-menopausal women with osteoporosis were treated for 3 years leading to a 41% relative risk reduction for vertebral fractures with benefits seen after 1 year. In TROPOS treatment of 5091 post-menopausal women with osteoporosis led to a significant 16% reduction in all non-vertebral fractures after 3 years; for women at high risk of hip fracture there was a 36% risk reduction. Stomtium is administered orally, 2 g once daily as a sachet dissolved in water.

Stimulation of bone formation - teriparatide (recombinant human parathyroid hormone (PTH) peptide 1-34)

Continuous exposure to exogenous PTH causes bone resorption. Paradoxically, PTH stimulates bone formation when administered intermittently, increasing BMD, strength and connectivity. This anabolic effect is achieved both by _de novo_ formation on resting bone surfaces and by a positive remodeling balance at the bone-remodelling unit. In a randomised controlled trial of 1637 women with osteoporosis, teriparatide 20 or 40 mg daily by subcutaneous injection over a mean treatment period of 18 months resulted in significant increases in BMD in the spine and proximal femur and significant reductions of 65-70% in vertebral fractures and 53-54% in non-vertebral fractures, but without evidence for reduction in hip fractures. The risk of two or more new vertebral fractures was reduced by 77% in the 20 mg group and 86% in the 40 mg group.

### Denoumab

This targets the receptor activator of nuclear factor-kB (RANK)/RANK ligand interaction, and may be effective, although the number needed to treat appears high and a track record of evidence is yet needed and there are concerns about jaw osteonecrosis.

### Calcium and vitamin D3 supplementation

These should always be used as supplementary therapy, at doses of around 1200 mg calcium (which is anti-resorptive by suppressing PTH) and 800 IU vitamin D3/calcitri daily, in those at risk such as older patients.

How might you in practice approach the use of BMD measurement and primary and secondary prevention of osteoporosis fragility fractures?

Guidelines by the National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence (NICE) on secondary prevention in post-menopausal women were first published in 2005, with subsequent revisions including the addition of primary prevention, but these continued to leave gaps (men, primary prevention in post-menopausal women regardless of age, secondary prevention in men and women regardless of age, glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis, more robust risk stratification and thresholds for BMD measurement) that were filled by the National Osteoporosis Guideline Group (NOGG).

### NICE Guidelines

Guidance focuses on post-menopausal women and recommends a range of treatments depending upon age. BMD and number of indicators of fragile bones or clinical risk factors for fractures.

#### Primary prevention (opportunistic)

Alendronate is recommended first-line therapy for primary prevention for women aged >= 70 years with at least one clinical risk factor for osteoporosis or indicator of low BMD confirmed to have osteoporosis (defined by NICE as a T-score of < -2.5 on DXA). In women >= 75 years with >= 2 independent clinical risk factors or indicators of low BMD, a DXA scan may not be required if considered clinically inappropriate or unfeasible. Women under 75 years must have a diagnosis of osteoporosis, and the number of clinical risk factors and indicators of low BMD depends upon the age. Risedronate or etidronate may be used where alendronate is not tolerated or compliance is not possible, and strontium ranelate where alendronate and either risedronate or etidronate are not tolerated or compliance is not possible, but indications in each situation depend upon a combination of T-score, age and number of independent clinical risk factors for fracture.

### Secondary prevention

Alendronate is recommended first-line therapy for secondary prevention of osteoporotic fragility fractures in post-menopausal women confirmed to have osteoporosis. Risedronate and etidronate may be used where alendronate is not tolerated or compliance is not possible, and strontium ranelate or raloxifene where alendronate and either risedronate or etidronate are not tolerated or compliance is not possible, but indications in each situation depend upon a combination of T-score, age and number of independent clinical risk factors for fracture. In all cases for women >= 75 years a DXA scan may not be required if considered clinically inappropriate or unfeasible. Denoumab may also be considered. Teriparatide may be considered where alendronate and either risedronate or etidronate are not tolerated or compliance is not possible, or with a contraindication to or intolerance of strontium ranelate, or after an unsatisfactory response to treatment with alendronate, risedronate or etidronate, with stringent combinations of age, T scores and clinical risk factors.

For the purposes of NICE guidance:

* Indicators of low BMD are low body mass index (BMI) (defined as less than 22 kg/m2), medical conditions (such as ankylosing spondylitis, rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn's disease), conditions that result in prolonged immobility, and untreated premature menopause
* Independent clinical risk factors for fracture are parental history of hip fracture, alcohol intake of 4 or more units per day, and rheumatoid arthritis
* Intolerance of alendronate, risedronate or etidronate is defined as persistent upper gastrointestinal disturbance, and intolerance of strontium ranelate, persistent nausea or diarrhoea
* An unsatisfactory response is defined as another fragility fracture despite adhering fully to treatment for 1 year and evidence of a decline in BMD below pre-treatment baseline.

Guidance that BMD scanning may not be required in women aged >= 75 who have had a fragility fracture presumes that most women aged >= 75 with fragility fractures have osteoporosis. But DXA scans of women aged >= 75 with fragility fractures evaluated by the Lothian and Glasgow fracture liaison services in 2007 showed that only half of them had osteoporosis and bisphosphonates do not appear to prevent fractures in patients with osteopenia.

### NOGG guidelines

NICE thus imposes differing clinical risk factor number and T-score requisites for a range of differing ages for all drugs other than alendronate, and also restricts what is considered to be a risk factor. This restricts prevention for many at ongoing risk of further fractures. The NOGG essentially translates widely accepted Royal College of Physicians (RCP) guidance into the setting of the World Health Organization's Fracture Risk Assessment (FRAX) and carries forward two important principles from RCP guidelines. Firstly, since there is no universal screening policy for osteoporosis, a case-finding strategy that identifies patients through previous fracture or clinical risk factors as outlined in the FRAX tool (Box 2.57) is recommended. Secondly, in post-menopausal women with a fragility fracture, treatment may be considered without BMD measurement.

A management algorithm used by NOGG starts by assessing 10-year fracture probability on the basis of sex, age, number of clinical risk factors and BMI (used as a surrogate for BMD). This first set of charts categorises people into high, intermediate or low risk of a major osteoporotic fracture (red, orange or green). What is considered high risk differs for different ages and the intervention threshold at each age is set at a risk equivalent to that associated with a prior fracture and therefore rises with age; for example, the guideline suggests treating a 50-year-old with a fracture risk of 7.5%, but sets the treatment threshold at 30% for an 80-year-old; this avoids unnecessarily treating an active 80-year-old and not treating a fragile 50-year-old. Furthermore, since a previous fragility fracture is considered a clinical risk factor by NOGG and contributes to the risk calculation, whilst it is likely that a person with a fragility fracture will qualify for treatment, this is not inevitable. The FRAX score and NOGG guideline does not therefore discriminate cleanly between primary and secondary prevention, but rather makes the following intervention suggestions:

* _Red (high risk)_: these women and men should be considered for treatment without BMD testing.
* _Orange (intermediate risk)_: these women and men should have BMD testing and their probability recomputed with the result; a second set of charts for this purpose shows 10-year fracture probability on the basis of sex, age, number of clinical risk factors and BMD (BMD at femoral neck substituted for BMI); where probability exceeds one or other of the intervention thresholds treatment is recommended and where below further assessment is recommended in 5 years.
* _Green (low risk)_: these women and men may be reassured with reassessment in 5 years depending upon the context.
* _Women and men with a previous fragility fracture (secondary prevention)_: all women should be considered for treatment without BMD measurement although BMD measurement may be desirable in some younger women. The diminished emphasis on BMD measurement reflects modern consensus that other factors independently contribute to fracture risk. All men should have BMD measurement because of the high incidence of underlying secondary causes of osteoporosis in men.

The guidelines, like NICE, recommend alendronate as first-line therapy but are less restrictive on second-line treatment, which may include other bisphosphonates (including risedronate, ibandronate or zolendronate), strontium, raloxifene or PTH; the guidelines do caution that the high cost of PTH should restrict its use to those at very high risk, particularly of vertebral fractures.

### Is monitoring of BMD during treatment sensible?

Treatment aims to reduce the rate of fractures, but if DXA cannot detect a significant change in BMD within a time scale that enables effective changes in management or if the change in BMD does not predict a reduction in fractures after treatment, then monitoring BMD does not appear sensible. Although nearly all women gain BMD with treatment, the large variability associated with the measurement of BMD obscures true treatment response in an individual. Further, only a small proportion of reduction in fractures attributable to treatment is explained by a change in BMD.

### When does glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis begin?

Glucocorticoids simultaneously suppress bone formation and increase resorption, and do so rapidly, within the first few months. Even small oral doses (< 7.5 mg prednisolone) cause some bone loss. Risk increases rapidly in the first 3-6 months and so bone protection should start as soon as long-term corticosteroids are started.

### Case 2.21 Joint pain

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

#### Dear Doctor

Re: Mrs Elaine Tilney, aged 52 years

Mrs Tilney has been complaining of worsening polyarthalgia in her hands and wrists for 6 months. Her blood results are as follows: full blood count normal except for a borderline normochlorinormotorix anaemia; creatinine of 135 mmol/l, otherwise urea, electrolytes and liver function tests normal; rheumatoid factor positive. I would be grateful for your further assessment.

#### Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

#### Patient information

Mrs Elaine Tilney is a 52-year-old woman who has had increasing pain in the joints of her hands for 6 months. The joints, especially the metacarpals (knuckles), also tend to become spongy and to swell. Pain, and in particular stiffness, is worse in the mornings and starts to subside after an hour or so. Before this started she an episode of swelling in her right knee which lasted around 3 days. She has been increasingly fatigued over the last 6 months, and has taken many days off work. She works as a part-time secretary at the hospital. She lives with her husband and two 'grown up' children in a small house, which she had been trying to improve before putting on the market for sale. However, she has increasingly had to stop work on the house, decorating and so forth, as her hands are too painful to use. Her husband works full time as a nurse on night shifts, and there are tensions at home. She feels he is seldom around, and is always too tired because of his 'full-time job' to decorate or do the housework. Mrs Tilney feels she is trying to cope with her part-time job, home improvement, demanding children who should be out to work and a husband seldom there, against a backdrop of increasing pain and fatigue, and financial worries. If she does not work they will have to sell the house sooner rather than later.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

_Presenting problem/s and symptom exploration_

Determine if symptoms are localised to joints and the pattern and features of joint involvement. As if pain is localised to joints, and not diffuse. The latter could imply a chronic pain syndrome such as fibromyalgia. Myalgic symptoms can occur in rheumatoid arthritis. Determine as precisely as you can which joints are affected. Determine if there is joint stiffness as well as pain. Morning stiffness, improving with exercise, characterises an inflammatory arthroplasty. Mechanical pain is usually relieved by rest. It is essential to know if there is joint swelling (polyarthritis) as well as pain (polyarthalgia) and stiffness. Any soft-tissue swelling is highly suggestive of inflammatory joint disease.

Consider the differential diagnoses.The next step is to test each differential diagnosis in turn by asking about symptoms from the diagnostic criteria. In this woman, you should consider rheumatoid arthritis (rheumatoid factor is positive) and systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) (given the raised creatinine) first. The normochromic-normocytic anaemia supports an inflammatory condition but does not help diagnostically. For rheumatoid arthritis, ask about morning stiffness of >1 hour, hand joint involvement, symmetrical involvement and nodules. For SLE, ask about rashes, sunlight sensitivity, mouth ulcers and Raynaud's phenomenon.

#### Patient perspective

Clearly very relevant here is her fatigue, contributed to largely by active inflammatory disease but compounded by her stress and lifestyle. To her, what is going on at home and the impact her symptoms have had on her work are probably more significant than the symptoms themselves. Explore the huge impact her symptoms have had, and try to reassure her that you will try to confirm the diagnosis quickly and get her condition under good control with treatment, reducing symptoms and thereby hopefully improving everything else.

Figure 2.8: Approach to the differential diagnosis of an older person with decreased mobility and painful joints or muscles. CK, creatine kinase; ESR, erythrocyte sedimentation rate; SLE, systemic lupus erythematosus. 

#### Past medical history

She is previously well.

#### Drug and allergy history

Ask about prescription and non-prescription medications.

#### Family history

Seek a family history of any autoimmune disease.

#### Social history

Explore her duties at home with her family and her work in detail.

## Discussion

Patients, especially older patients, often present with non-specific aches and decreased mobility. Do you have an approach to the differential diagnosis?

An approach to the differential diagnosis of an older person with decreased mobility and painful joints or muscles is given in Figure 2.8.

### What do you know about septic arthritis?

Septic arthritis is extremely serious. It is often caused by entry of _Staphylococcus_ from a breach in the skin, or sometimes from another source such as discitis, or sometimes from urine. Organisms tend to seed to damaged joints, as in rheumatoid arthritis, just as they may seed to damaged heart valves causing endocarditis. Multiple joints may be affected, and methicillin-resistant _Staphylococcus aureus_ septic arthritis has a very high mortality.

## Case 2.22 Type 1 diabetes Mellitus

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

### Scenario

#### Dear Doctor

Re: Ms Jill Masters, aged 19 years

Thank you for admitting Ms Masters, who has been complaining of increasing thist and polyuria over the last few weeks. Her blood glucose monitor reading is 28 and her urine is positive for ketones, although she is relatively well in herself. I have told her that I suspect she has diabetes and an grateful for your ongoing management.

Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Ms Jill Masters is a 19-year-old medical student in her first year at medical school. She has been increasingly thirsty over the last 4 weeks, and passing larger volumes of urine, and her GP has just admitted her with a new diagnosis of diabetes. She has no past medical history of note and does not take any medications. She is usually very active, leading the university swimming team. Her mother has hypothyroidism but otherwise there is no clear family history of autoimmune disease.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

##### Initial priorities in assessing a patient with newly diagnosed diabetes.

A decision as to whether immediate rehydration and insulin are needed should be made, determined by the presence of dehydration, vomiting or ketonuria. Thirst, polyuria and initial glucose levels correlate less well with the need for immediate insulin.

##### Presentation of type 1 diabetes.

Presentation varies according to the rate and degree of beta cell failure. The classic presentation is outlined in Box 2.59.

##### Consider if admission to hospital is warranted.

Admission can be a very negative first experience of newly diagnosed diabetes. Patients invariably proceed to insulin but this can be instituted in a diabetes clinic if there are no features to suggest that insulin is urgently needed and patients are not acutely unwell. Indications for admission are listed in Box 2.60.

#### Patient perspective

She may initially be very afraid of a diagnosis that has important implications for lifestyle adjustment and carries long-term complications. Some of the skills for breaking bad news may be important, and it is important to emphasise the advances in monitoring and treatment that are less intrusive on lifestyle.

#### Past medical history

A new diagnosis in this age group is usually type 1 diabetes.

#### Drug and allergy history

Although certain drugs such as corticosteroids exacerbate hyperglycemia, drug causes of diabetes are very rare.

#### Family history

A family history of autoimmune disease is common.

#### Social history

As well as asking about smoking (future cardiovascular disease risk) and alcohol (which can affect glycaemic control), enquire about university social life and sports activities.

## Discussion

### What is diabetes?

Diabetes is not a single disorder but a range of autoimmune, metabolic and genetic disorders associated with hyperglycemia in which defects in insulin secretion or action cause premature atherosclerosis with microvascular (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) and macrovascular (ischaemic heart disease, stroke, peripheral vascular disease) complications.

### How is diabetes defined?

The prevalence of diabetes has been rising in the last few decades, fuelled by the global rise in the prevalence of obesity. The classification and diagnosis of diabetes are complex and have been the subjects of much consultation, debate and revision over the decades. Expert committees from the World Health Organization (WHO) and American Diabetes Association have varied in their position on the diagnostic criteria for diabetes, based on the measurement of fasting or 2-hour post-load glucose, and more recently there has been debate on whether glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) should be used. Diagnostic definitions of diabetes mellitus, impaired fasting glucose and impaired glucose tolerance by the WHO are given in Table 2.45.

### How is diabetes classified?

The aetiological classification of diabetes has now been widely accepted. Type 1 diabetes and type 2 diabetes are the two main types, type 2 diabetes accounting for the majority (> 85%) of total diabetes prevalence, together with a range of rarer disorders (Table 2.46).

What do you know about maturity onset diabetes of the young (MODY) and how to recognise when other types of diabetes than types 1 or 2 may be the present?

MODY comprises a heterogeneous group of monogenic disorders characterised by beta cell dysfunction. It is estimated to cause 1-2% of cases of diabetes. It is important to distinguish MODY from type 1 and type 2 diabetes because optimal treatments are different. Furthermore, first-degree relatives have a 50% probability of inheriting the same mutation, with a greater than 95% lifetime risk of developing diabetes.

#### Genetic mutations

Mutations in the genes encoding the enzyme glucokinase (GCK) and the nuclear transcription factors hepatocyte nuclear factor 1a (HNIF1A) and hepatocyte nuclear factor 4a (HNIF4A) are the most common causes of MODY, and in the UK represent 32%, 52%, and 10% of MODY cases, respectively. The prevalence of MODY subtypes differs across countries, with GCK mutations more commonly diagnosed in countries where glucose testing of asymptomatic people is routine. Mutations in at least 10 different genes have been associated with a MODY phenotype.

#### Clinical features

Features to consider when assessing the cause of diabetes are listed in Box 2.61.

Patients with MODY typically display one or more of:

* A strong family history of diabetes (of any type)
* Onset of diabetes in the second to fifth decade
* Insulin independence (insulin may be needed for optimal control)
* Absence of features of insulin resistance
* Absence of beta cell autoimmunity.

The specific genetic subtype determines the clinical features, prognosis, and treatment response. Table 2.47 summarises similarities and differences between common MODY subtypes and type 1 and type 2 diabetes.

#### Diagnosis

Diagnostic genetic testing for MODY, by direct sequencing of the genes, is widely available, but largely opportunistic. Guidelines recommend genetic testing for people who match specific clinical criteria: diabetes presenting before the age of 25 years, a strong family history of diabetes, and evidence of insulin independence.

* < 0.5 units/kg/day, no tendency to ketoacidosis when insulin is omitted, and persistent C-peptide production of > 0.2 nmol/1 outside the honeymoon period. C-peptide is less useful for distinguishing MODY from type 1 diabetes close to diagnosis, when endogenous insulin production is still present. MODY is not usually associated with pancreatic autoantibodies. Parental history of diabetes is more common in MODY than in type 1 diabetes, although family history does not discriminate well between the two.

**Patients diagnosed with type 2 diabetes who should be referred for genetic testing.** Beta cell dysfunction in MODY typically occurs in the absence of insulin resistance. Patients with young onset (< 45 years) apparent type 2 diabetes, without features of insulin resistance, may have MODY. Clinical features such as acanthosis nigricans, central obesity, hypertension, and dyslipidemia can be used as surrogate markers of insulin resistance in practice. A family history of diabetes is common in people with young onset type 2 diabetes and does not discriminate well between type 2 diabetes and MODY. Marked sensitivity to sulphonylureas with hypoglycaemia on low or normal doses of sulphonylureas is common with the HNF1A or HNF4A mutation.

#### Treatment

The mild increase in HbA1c generally seen in GCK-MODY suggests that drugs do not improve glycaemic control in GCK-MODY and insulin and oral hypoglycaemic agents can usually be stopped in patients with GCK-MODY. Sulphonylureas are the optimal treatment in HNF1A/HNF4A-MODY, but patients are very sensitive to them.

### What is diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and how is it treated?

DKA is defined as metabolic acidosis with a plasma bicarbonate of < 15 mmol/l and a total ketone body concentration of > 5 mmol/l, but in practice it is identified by dehydration, hyperglycaemia, acidosis and ketonuria. It is caused by insulin deficiency in the presence of elevated catabolic hormone levels (catecholamines, glucagon, cortisol, growth hormone) and usually occurs in type 1 diabetes but can occur in type 2 diabetes. There may be precipitating causes such as infection or myocardial infarction, all having the common denominator of insulin (anabolic hormone) deficiency and catabolic hormone excess promoting the problems in Table 2.48.

DKA is a life-threatening condition requiring immediate hospitalisation and treatment. The aims of treatment are to rehydrate and give insulin. Potassium supplementation is invariably necessary and careful monitoring is necessary. Referral to the critical care team while resuscitating may be appropriate if there is dehydration > 10%, coma, a pH < 7.1 or a plasma sodium < 120 mmol/l or > 160 mmol/l. Fluid resuscitation is with isotonic saline at a rate exceeding losses (e.g. 1 litre first hour, 1 litre next 2 hours, 1 litre next 4 hours, 1 litre 6-hourly) switched to 5% dextrose (and insulin dose halved) when glucose is < 15 mmol/l.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c c}  & **Type 1 diabetes** & **Type 2 diabetes** & **GCK-MODY** & **HNF1A (30–70\% of MODY)/** \\ Typical age of diagnosis (years) & 10–30 & \textgreater{} 25 & Present from birth; presents at any age & 15–45 \\ Parental history of diabetes & \textless{} 15\% & \textgreater{} 50\% in young onset type 2 diabetes & One parent usually has impaired fasting glycaemia if tested & 60–90\% \\ Obesity / other distinguishing clinical features & Uncommon & Common & Uncommon & Uncommon \\  & & & Mild fasting & Low / normal renal threshold for glycosuria; hyperglycaemia (5.5–8 mmol/l) throughout life; small glucose rise after carbohydrate load & \\ Insulin restance & Uncommon & Common & Uncommon & Uncommon \\  & & & & Hyperglycaemia (5.5–8 mmol/l) throughout life; small glucose rise after carbohydrate load & \\ Insulin dependent & Yes & No & No & No \\ Insulin resistance & Uncommon & Common & Uncommon & Uncommon \\ Presence of beta-cell autoantibodies & \textgreater{} 90\% & Negative & Rare & Rare \\ C-peptide concentrations & Undetectable / low & Normal / high & Normal & Normal \\ Diabetic ketoacidosis & Common & Rare & Rare & Rare \\ Optimal first-line treatment & Insulin & Metformin & None & Sulphonylurea \\ GCK, glucokinase; HNF1A / 4A, hepatocyte nuclear factor-1α / 4α. & & & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.47: Clinical and biochemical features associated with type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and the common subtypes of maturity onset diabetes of the young (MODY) It is possible that faster rates are associated with an increased risk of cerebral oedema and slower rates of correction of bicarbonate. When acidosis persists it is probably best to maintain the same dose of insulin and prevent hypoglycaemia with 10% glucose infusion. Insulin may be delivered as 50 units short-acting insulin in 50 ml saline via syringe pump on a sliding scale according to blood glucose (e.g. < 4.0 mmol/1 - 0 insulin units/h, 4.1-6.0 - 1 insulin unit/h, 6.1-1.2.0 - 3 insulin units/h, 12.1-17.0 - 5 insulin units/h, > 17.1 - 8 insulin units/h). The sliding scale may be stopped when there is no ketonuria acidosis or dehydration and the patient is eating and drinking, ideally having continued to receive their basal insulin if it is pre-existing diabetes. It is possible that rates of fall in plasma glucose > 5 mmol/h may increase the risk of cerebral oedema. A suitable potassium replacement is 40 mmol KCl/1 if <3.5 mmol/l, 20 mmol KCl/1 if 3.5-5.5 mmol/l. Complications occur including vomiting with aspiration, thrombotic events, and cerebral oedema.

### How is type 1 diabetes managed?

Treatment involves the administration of exogenous insulin to avoid diabetic ketoacidosis, maintaining glycaemic control and preventing the long-term complications. Insulin therapy (Box 2.62) presents a challenge to both patient and health-care professional and a collaborative approach encouraging 'self-management' is necessary. Insulin preparations with differing pharmacokinetic profiles have provided greater flexibility in the management of type 1 diabetes and technological advances in continuous glucose monitoring and continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion via insulin pumps show promise in helping to achieve treatment goals. Other potential therapies include amylin analogues, pancreatic transplantation and islet transplantation, the last a potential modality for possible future delivery of 'engineered' beta cells from human stem cells. The use of immunotherapy for prevention of type 1 diabetes has been disappointing.

### What are the principles of nutrition in diabetes?

The old-fashioned approach was a diet low in carbohydrate and high in fat. The modern trend is lower fat and higher carbohydrate, with the concept of knowing the carbohydrate content of foods and responding to carbohydrate units and adjusting insulin accordingly rather than limiting eating.

### Does glycaemic control in type 1 diabetes reduce vascular complications?

The Diabetes Control and Complications Trial (DCCT) showed significant relative risk reductions with intensive treatment (HbAlc 7%) of 76% for the onset of retinopathy, 54% for retinopathy progression, 39% for nephropathy and significant reduction in neuropathy. Continuous benefit was suggested at lower levels but at the cost of a higher risk of hypoglycaemia. Evidence for microvascular benefit appeared greater than for macrovascular benefit because there were few macrovascular events in short-term follow-up, but time has demonstrated macrovascular benefit. Indeed, relative cardiovascular risk is very high in type 1 diabetes, particularly in younger patients, and all of the complications of type 2 diabetes (Case 2.23) may occur in type 1 diabetes.

### Why does glycaemic control in diabetes tend to destabilise in hospital?

Stress causes a surge of counter-regulatory hormones including epinephrine (adrenaline), glucagon, cortisol and growth hormone.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c} Hormone problem & **Resulting pathophysiology** & **Biochemical problem** & **Symptoms** \\ Insulin deficiency & Hepatic glucose mobilisation (e.g. gluconeogenesis) in attempt to compensate for perceived glucose deficiency Decreased peripheral glucose storage (glucose cannot be taken up by cells peripherally) & Hypercaemia & Polynia \\ Catabolic hormone excess (often due to precipitate cause) & Triglyceride breakdown in adipose tissue resulting in fatty acids which are used to produce ketone bodies (acetone, acetoacetate and 3-hydroxymethyl) in the ketotic pathway as an alternative source of fuel. The mechanism is the same as that seen in prolonged starvation (Case 4.34) but in starvation insulin levels fall in response to carbohydrate deficiency and alternative energy pathways are sought, in DKA insulin levels are low owing to pancreatic insufficiency and although this results in hyperglycaemia, without insulin glucose cannot be incorporated into cells and is unavailable for energy production mandating a switch to the ketotic pathway as an alternative source of fuel Protein catabolism in muscle & Ketoacidosis & Vomiting Ketonuria \\ Ketonuria Abdominal pain (often due to precipitate cause) & & Abdominal pain \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.48: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)



###### Abstract

The high-resolution imaging of the human brain is a key factor in the development of the brain. 

## What is the normal response to hypoglycemia?

The autonomic response is protective by both producing counter-regulatory hormone release (glucagon and the catecholamine epinephrine,which stimulate hepatic glucose release and reduce glucose uptake by fat and muscle) and generating symptoms that alert the individual (Table 2.49). Growth hormone and cortisol, also produced during hypoglycemia, play a relatively minor role during insulin-induced hypoglycaemia.

### How problematic is hypoglycaemia in diabetes?

Hypoglycaemia is a potential problem when aiming for tight glycaemic control. Major hypoglycemia was experienced by 27% of intensively treated patients in the DCCT and only 2% of patients in the _UK Prospective Diabetes Study_ (_UkPDS_) (Case 2.23 Type 2 diabetes) despite broadly similar levels of glycaemic control. In the UKPDS over the first 10 years, major hypoglycemia rates varied depending upon treatment modality, but were highest at 2.3% for patients on insulin. Hypoglycaemia remains very common in older people who may eat little and still take their tablets. Long-acting analogues reduce the risk of hypoglycaemia but cause sustained hypoglycaemia when it does occur and effects may occur days after the last dose.

### What are hypoglycaemia-associated autonomic failure (HAAF) and hypoglycaemic unawareness?

Difficulty recognising hypoglycaemia affects about 25% of patients with type 1 diabetes and almost 50% who have had the disease for over 20 years. In insulin-deficient diabetes, exogenous insulin levels do not fall as glucose levels fall and the integrity of counter-regulatory hormones assumes greater significance. The glucagon response begins to fail within the first year or two of type 1 diabetes and is generally absent after 5 years, the epinephrine response becoming the crucial line of defence. The epinephrine response itself, however, is progressively blunted by antecedent hypoglycaemia (usually later in disease than glucagon failure), as is hypoglycaemic awareness because of loss of sympathetic symptoms. This combination of glucagon and epinephrine failure and unawareness because of antecedent hypoglycaemia is known as HAAF. By shifting glucose thresholds downwards for the symptomatic and sympatho-adrenal responses (to a level of around 2.5 mmol/l), antecedent hypoglycaemia creates a self-perpetuating cycle of recurrent hypoglycaemia without warning symptoms and further blunting of counter-regulation. The cycle can be broken by scrupulous avoidance of hypoglycaemia, which restores epinephrine (but not glucagon activity) and demonstrates that hypoglycaemic unawareness is not caused by autonomic neuropathy but rather HAAF.

### How is hypoglycemia treated?

A conscious patient should be given 10 g of quick-acting glucose (two teaspoons of sugar, three dextrose tablets, 50 ml original Lucozade, jam or honey) followed by long-acting carbohydrate such as bread, biscuits or milk. An unconscious patient should receive 50 ml of 50% dextrose intravenously or 1 mg of intramuscular glucagon.

### Can hypoglycaemia be predicted and prevented?

Predictors include previous severe hypoglycemia, recurrent asymptomatic hypoglycemia (often nocturnal), diabetes of long duration and intensified insulin therapy. Education is vital. Hypoglycaemic awareness is more likely when patients have autonomic symptoms (sweats, shakes, palpitations) but less likely for patients relying on neurohypoglycaemic symptoms (confusion, dysarthria, unsteadiness). For those with impaired awareness, more physiological insulin and relaxation of glycaemic targets (e.g. pre-meal 4 mmol/l and pre-bed 7 mmol/l) may help, and scrupulous avoidance of hypoglycaemia may break the HAAF cycle.

### How would you tackle nocturnal hypoglycaemia?

Nocturnal hypoglycaemia affects over 50% of people taking insulin, and more physiological replacement with insulin analogues and matching replacement to carbohydrate intake may lessen the problem, as may a bedtime snack. Snacking between meals may also help patients with non-physiological insulin profiles. Overnight insulin should be reduced after an overactive day. Alcohol should be avoided to excess, and overnight insulin should sometimes be reduced.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Approximate blood glucose (mmol / l) at which response starts & Response \\
3.7 & Adrenaline release \\
3.5 & Start of neuronal dysfunction \\ Sweating, tremor, palpitations, hunger (autonomic symptoms) \\
3.0 & Start of cognitive dysfunction \\
2.5 & Confusion, loss of concentration, speech difficulty, old behaviour, incoordination, occasionally focal neurological deficits (neuroglycaemic symptoms) \\
1.0 & Coma or seizure \\ Sustained hypoglycaemia & Permanent brain damage \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.49: Normal responses to hypoglycemia 

### Other than overly tight glucose control in diabetes, what other causes of hypoglycaemia are there?

Hypoglycaemia may occur as a result of hepatic or adrenal failure. Severe hypoglycaemia is more commonly due to a person taking excess insulin (or sulphonylurea) deliberately (or, exceptionally rarely, malicious institution by a third party) or an insulinoma. Detection of elevated insulin and C-peptide levels during hypoglycemia will determine if there is excess exogenous insulin (C-peptide absent) or an insulinoma (insulin and C-peptide levels high). Sometimes tumours including ovarian cancers and pleural fibromas also induce hypoglycaemia by producing insulin-like growth factors. Steroids may be a helpful treatment.

### What is hyperosmolar non-ketotic coma?

This is an insidious rise in glucose to often very high levels, which predisposes to thrombotic complications; it is more common in older people with unrecognised type 2 diabetes and may present with polydipsia, polyuria or confusion. Ketones are absent or minimally present as insulin is not completely deficient, present in sufficient amount to incorporate some glucose into organs and thus prevent switching to alternative ketotic pathway sources of energy. However, glucose levels build up slowly in the bloodstream to impressive levels with a high osmolarity. Treatment is rehydration, insulin and anticoagulation.

## Case 2.23 Type 2 diabetes Mellitus

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the diabetes clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes are elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

#### Patient information

Mrs Lorna Dunne is a 60-year-old retired pharmaceutical representative who was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes 10 years ago and has been on insulin at night for 2 years together with metformin. She now has a small ulcer on her right big toe, which has turned black and painful. She has a history of sensory neuropathy with numbness, and poor peripheral circulation, and is an ex-smoker of 20 cigarettes a day. She has high cholesterol despite simvastatin. Her previous history comprises angina on exertion and problems with obesity. She had laser eye surgery last year but has no visual impairment. She feels her diet is reasonable but admits that exercise is difficult on account of her neuropathy and that she spent much of her working life in the car. She is now horrified by the black toe. Her husband is unwell with back problems, and needs help with washing and dressing.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

#### Elicit the range of symptoms and complications

Presentation of type 2 diabetes is usually with symptoms of hyperglycaemia or cardiovascular disease (CVD) complications after many years of silent development. The classic presentation and features are outlined in Box 2.63.

Take a full history of events surrounding the foot ulcer, and for:

* home monitoring and knowledge of glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c)
* Blood pressure
* Lipid levels
* Microvascular complications
* Macrovascular complications.

#### Patient perspective

A self-management programme should aim to develop understanding of diabetes, and promote the specific diabetes (e.g. monitoring) and general self-management (e.g. goal setting) skills needed for effective self-management. Areas include: what diabetes is; means of managing diabetes; consequences and personal risk; monitoring and taking action (including hypoglycaemic awareness); food choices; physical activity; stress and emotion; and screening and complication surveillance.

#### Past medical history

Explore the history of angina, investigations performed and treatment.

#### Drug and allergy history

Beta blockers and diuretics are associated with a significantly increased incidence of diabetes, and the former exacerbate peripheral vascular disease.

#### Family history

A family history of type 2 diabetes is common.

#### Social history

Enquire about the needs of her husband were she to be admitted to hospital.

## Discussion

### What is the scale of the problem of type 2 diabetes?

The worldwide prevalence of diabetes continuously escalates. The International Diabetes Federation's e-atlas and regularly updated 'Diabetes Maps' from the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention graphically illustrate the scale of the problem. Type 2 diabetes accounts for about 90% of all diabetes. It is a worldwide epidemic, tripling in prevalence over the last 30 years and affecting 6-8% of middle-aged to older adults in North America and Europe. The number of adults predicted to have diabetes by 2025 is 333 million, and it is increasingly a problem in South East Asia. Adolescent- and childhood-onset type 2 diabetes is increasingly recognised. The pandemic has major economic implications and the need is great for people to be physically active and have less easy access to energy-rich foods. Type 2 diabetes is strongly associated with obesity, physical inactivity and other risk factors for CVD, and may be part of the metabolic syndrome (Case 2.7).

### What causes type 2 diabetes?

There is a strong albeit poorly understood genetic susceptibility, and environmental factors crucial to development include excessive food intake, obesity and physical inactivity. Type 2 diabetes is a chronic, progressive metabolic disorder characterised by insulin resistance and impaired secretion.

#### Insulin resistance

Insulin acts on cells in muscle, fat and liver by binding to membrane receptors and triggering a series of intracellular phosphorylation steps leading to glucose uptake, and effects on fatty acid and protein metabolism. In type 2 diabetes, basal hepatic glucose output is increased, suppression of hepatic glucose output after meals is impaired and insulin-induced glucose uptake in muscle and fat is reduced. Increased triglycerides and low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and decreased high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C), together with uric acidaemia and increased plasminogen activator inhibitor 1, are also effects of insulin resistance and increase CVD risk. Both the insulin molecule and cell receptors are normal, but intracellular pathways are aberrant after insulin binding. Insulin resistance leads to elevated insulin concentrations in contrast to type 1 and other forms of diabetes in which endogenous insulin is low or absent.

#### Beta-cell failure

Despite hyperinsulinaemia, the beta cell response to hyperglycemia is inadequate and insulin secretion is always abnormal once type 2 diabetes is fully established. Insulin secretory responses to meals are severely blunted, with post-prandial hyperglycemia early in disease leading to fasting hyperglycemia as disease progresses and insulin concentrations cannot overcome hepatic insulin resistance. Ultimately, hyperinsulinaemia gives way to beta-cell failure and hyposinulinaemia.

### How is diabetes care best coordinated?

Diabetes is a long-term condition requiring a multidisciplinary approach across health settings that should consider the areas outlined in Table 2.50. 

### Why is glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes important?

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a complex chronic condition associated with hyperglycemia and long-term end-organ damage. This damage, due to exposure to hyperglycemia, leads to increased risk of CVD and excess mortality. Advanced glycation end products are of pathogenic importance. Glucose binds with protein to produce glycated proteins such as HbA1c. The property of these proteins is altered by, for example, cross-links, and this is very important for molecules that are not readily renewed such as collagen, which become advanced glycation end products.

#### Acute glycaemic management

Short-term glycaemic control in hospitalised patients might be expected to improve well-being, reduce infection rates, aid wound healing, prevent severe hyperglycemia and reduce vascular complications. The Dextrose Insulin and Glucose in Acute Myocardial Infarction (DIGAMI) study showed that tight control at the time of infarction and for 3 months reduced 3-year mortality by 11%, but other studies have been conflicting and in DIGAMI 2 the benefits of DIGAMI were not replicated.

#### The UK Prospective Diabetes Study (UKPDS) and long-term glycaemic control

The UKPDS was a 20-year study of over 5000 patients with type 2 diabetes. It showed that the intensive glycaemic control (average HbA1c 7.0%) produces considerably fewer microvascular complications (Table 2.51) than conventional control (average HbA1c 7.9%). Good glycaemic control also reduces the risk of stroke and myocardial infarction. There were no differences in glucose control or microvascular endpoints between patients treated with chlorpropamide, glibenclamide or insulin. Metformin, used as primary therapy for overweight patients, reduced microvascular and microvascular endpoints at similar levels of glucose control to other therapies, and was the only therapy not associated with weight gain. Improving glycaemic control with insulin or a sulphonylurea had only marginal macrovascular benefit but an explanation for this may be that longer term follow-up was needed; UKPDS-PTD (post-trial monitoring data) in 2008 on macrovascular outcomes 10 years on reported that tight blood glucose control with sulphonylurea and insulin versus conventional therapy as well as metformin versus conventional therapy have reduced myocardial infarction and all-cause mortality. UKPDS also showed that controlling hypertension had an important impact on macrovascular events. UKPDS also showed that HbA1c tended to rise every 5 years by around 1% even in tightly controlled patients, perhaps reducing the temptation to 'blame' patients or doctors for disease progression.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Patient-centred care and patient education & Structured, individually tailored self-management plan and education programme \\ Dietary advice & Nutritional advice \\ Assessment and treatment of blood glucose & Individually tailored targets for HbA1c (general target 6.5, measured every 2–6 months) and self-monitoring as part of the self-management plan \\ Management of cardiovascular disease risk & Estimation of risk not recommended as most with type 2 diabetes at high risk \\ Blood pressure and lipid-lowering treatment \\ Diabetic nephropathy & Measurement of albumin–creatinine ratio on first-pass morning urine (or spot urine) annually; if \textgreater{} 2.5 mg / mmol for men or \textgreater{} 3.5 mg / mmol for women or if glomerular filtration rate \\  & abnormal, non-diabetic renal disease should also be considered \\  & if diabetes likely cause, angiotensin-converting enzyme or angiotensin receptor blocker \\ Diabetic neuropathy & Annual review of symptoms \\  & Specialist management of foot problems \\  & Review issue of erectile dysfunction \\  & Consideration of possible contributory damage to the sympathetic nervous system if impaired \\  & hypoglycaemic awareness, and autonomic dysfunction contributing to orthostatic hypotension, diarrhoea or bladder emptying difficulties \\ Diabetic retinopathy & Retinal screening at least annually (25–30\% with type 2 diabetes have retinopathy at presentation, correlating with 7 years of antecedent hyperglycemia) \\ HbA1c, glycated haemoglobin. \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.50: Diabetes care The ACCORD study showed that those with high HBA1c levels (> 8.5%) who struggle to lower it are at mortality risk from intensive glucose lowering to achieve levels < 6%.

### Which groups of hypoglycaemic agent are used in type 2 diabetes?

Management of type 2 diabetes is complex and aims to prevent and reduce the impact of complications by means of a healthy lifestyle, controlling blood pressure and blood lipids, and lowering blood glucose concentrations. While diet and exercise and weight loss remain more effective than pharmacotherapy, only a minority of patients achieve optimal control through lifestyle efforts alone. As well as traditional oral hypoglycaemic agents (Table 2.52), in recent years new drugs for glycaemic control have become available, including long-acting insulin analogues, and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) mimetics and dipeptidylpeptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitors (Box 2.64).

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c c c} Drugs & Action & Indications & Dose & Advantages & Side effects \\ Metformin & Reduces hepatic gluconeogenesis & First line in overweight or obese & 500 mg b.d. to optimum 850 mg & No weight gain & Gastrointestinal (20\%) – \\  & Enhanced peripheral utilisation of glucose & Sutable & t.d.s. if tolerated & Hypoglycaemia & after years of treatment \\  & Only effective in presence of endogenous insulin (some residual beta-cell function) & Suitable monotherapy & Available in modified release formulation & May be cardioprotective (UKPDS) & Lactic acidosis (contraindicated if acutely ill, renal failure – (creatinine > 150 mmol / 1, decompensated liver disease, severe heart failure) \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & \\  & & & & 

### How would you approach using glycaemic control agents in type 2 diabetes in practice?

A stepwise approach is used.

#### First-line therapy - metformin

A joint consensus statement from the American Diabetes Association and European Association for the Study of Diabetes emphasises metformin as the preferred option for initial monotherapy.

#### Second-line therapy - sulphonylureas, DPP-4 inhibitors, thiazolidinediones

Owing to progressive beta-cell failure, most patients eventually fail monotherapy. The consensus statement does not specify second-line therapy, but National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence recommendations are as follows.

#### Sulphonylureas

It is usual to use a sulphonylure second-line if metformin alone is inadequate, or a sulphonylurea as monotherapy if metformin is not tolerated or contraindicated.

#### DPP-4 inhibitors and thiazolidinediones

* A DPP-4 inhibitor or thiazolidinedione should be considered second-line with metformin instead of a sulphonylurea when blood glucose control remains or becomes inadequate (HbA1c 2 6.5% or higher level agreed with the individual), if there is serious risk of hypoglycaemia or a sulphonylurea is not tolerated or contraindicated (e.g. severe renal impairment).
* If metformin is not tolerated or contraindicated (e.g. renal impairment), a DPP-4 inhibitor or thiazolidinedione should be considered second-line with a sulphonylurea when blood glucose control remains or becomes inadequate.

A DPP-4 inhibitor or a thiazolidinedione should be continued only if a beneficial response occurs (reduction of >= 0.5 in HbA1c in 6 months). A thiazolidinedione is contraindicated in heart failure or where there is a high risk of fracture. A DPP-4 inhibitor may be preferable to a thiazolidinedione if further weight gain would provoke serious problems, or if a thiazolidinedione is not tolerated or contraindicated. A thiazolidinedione may be preferable to a DPP-4 inhibitor if reduced insulin sensitivity is likely or if a DPP-4 inhibitor is not tolerated or contraindicated. Sometimes either a DPP-4 inhibitor or a thiazolidione may be suitable, the choice determined by patient preference.

#### Third-line therapy - sitagliptin, GLP-1 mimetics
* If metformin with a sulphonylure does not adequately control blood glucose (HbA1c 2 7.5% or higher level agreed with individual) and insulin is unacceptable or inappropriate (e.g. employment, social reasons, recreational problems related to hypoglycaemia, injection anxieties, other personal matters, obesity) sitagliptin or a thiazolidinedione may be considered as third-line treatment.
* A GLP-1 mimetic (exenatide, liraglutide) may be considered as third-line treatment (HbA1c 2 7.5% or higher level agreed with individual) to metformin and a sulphonylurea if a person has a body mass index (kg/m2) >= 35.0 and specific psychological or medical problems associated with high body weight, only continued if a beneficial response occurs (reduction of >= 1.0 in HbA1c and weight loss of at least 3% of initial body weight over 6 months).

#### The role of insulin

Box 2.65 describes the indications for insulin use in type 2 diabetes. Combining pioglitazone with insulin may be considered where there has been marked glucose lowering with a thiazolidinedione or where high-dose insulin does not inadequately control blood glucose.

### What other measures are important in reducing complications in type 2 diabetes?

Apart from glycaemic management, it is essential to target other contributors including diet and weight, smoking, blood pressure and lipids effectively. Atherosclerosis is a complex pathological process that is accelerated in diabetes and accounts for a higher proportion of total mortality than in the general population. It results from one or more established and/or 'novel' risk factors. Type 2 diabetes is characterised by obesity and insulin resistance and frequently associated with hypertension and dyslipidaemia. Other predictors of cardiovascular risk include microalbuminuria, elevated B-type natriuretic peptide, hypoadiponectinemetinaemia, vitamin D deficiency and testosterone deficiency. Endothelial dysfunction is an important antecedent of many complications of diabetes and is present in states of pre-diabetes.

Recent years have seen a paradigm shift in understanding the best strategies to lower cardiovascular risk in type 2 diabetes. In the past, considerable emphasis was placed on reduction of plasma glucose and there were misplaced perceptions of benefit from aspirin in all patients. There is now overwhelming evidence that lowering cholesterol with statin therapy and lowering blood pressure with antihypertensive agents, at least to a systolic value of 130 mmHg, are the keys to success in achieving such benefits. Trial and meta-analytical evidence has shown that aspirin for primary prevention of vascular events is probably unwarranted, intensive glucose lowering achieves limited reductions in CVD, and fibre therapy has no clear benefit with regard to reducing vascular events in type 2 diabetes despite favourable changes in lipid subfractions.

### Blood pressure

Elevated blood pressure (> 140/90 mmHg) is twice as common in diabetes and greatly increases an already increased cardiovascular risk. The combination of hypertension and diabetes doubles the risk of developing microvascular and macrovascular complications, and doubles mortality compared with non-diabetic people with hypertension. Evidence for blood pressure reduction is largely from trials that included people with diabetes shown to reduce or prevent major cardiovascular events including heart failure, cardiovascular death and total mortality. There is also evidence of reduced progression to retinopathy and nephropathy in people with diabetes but without CVD. Blood pressure targets are < 130/80 mmHg, possibly lower in diabetic nephropathy, the greater the reduction the greater the benefit and there appears to be no threshold below which risk no longer declines. Strict blood pressure control is the most important factor preventing diabetic nephropathy and its progression. Almost all patients need a combination of drugs and the evidence for nephropprotection is greatest with renin-angiotensin system blockade.

#### Lipid lowering

Diabetic dyslipidaemia is characterised by high LDL-C and triglycerides and low HDL-C. Covert hypothyroidism may coexist with diabetes and produce similar dyslipidaemia. Evidence for lipid lowering in diabetes reducing cardiovascular risk is largely from study subgroup analyses including people with diabetes. More recent trials exclusively in diabetes have shown significant reductions in cardiovascular events in people with diabetes comparable to those without diabetes. The Heart Protection Study included more people with type 1 and type 2 diabetes than previous studies combined and showed that patients with type 2 diabetes, one other risk factor for coronary heart disease and a total cholesterol > 3.5 mmol/1 had 33% fewer CVD events (coronary or stroke) with 40 mg simvastatin. ASCOT-LLA similarly had a large diabetes cohort and the 19% non-significant reduction in fatal coronary heart disease and myocardial infarction probably reflected reduced statistical power because it was stopped early. CARDS evaluated statin therapy exclusively in diabetic patients and compared atorvastatin 10 mg with placebo and was terminated early because the 37% reduction in CVD events (reductions in acute coronary heart disease events by 37% and stroke by 48%) was larger than that observed in the Heart Protection Study. All patients with diabetes should therefore be considered for statin therapy, with a minimum dose of simvastatin 40 mg or equivalent.

### What do you know about diabetic nephropathy?

Diabetic nephropathy is summarised in Box 2.66.

can usually be detected many years before development of advanced renal failure through the detection of raised urinary AER - microalbuminuria. This allows time for the intensive treatment of glycaemic control, blood pressure and other cardiovascular risk factors to reduce the morbidity and mortality. Significant structural damage accompanies microalbuminuria, often with hypertension, and although the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) may not be affected until the high microalbuminuria range is reached, microalbuminuria is strongly predictive of cardiovascular disease death, especially in older patients with type 2 diabetes.

In type 1 diabetes, proteinuria developing after a very long duration more likely reflects cardiovascular disease than diabetic nephropathy. Persistent albuminuria or overt diabetic nephropathy is usually accompanied by atherosclerosis and, untreated, a LETIERS decline in GFR with death after an average 7-10 years.

### Treatment

Good glycaemic control can prevent diabetic nephropathy but there is less evidence that it prevents progression once established. Strict blood pressure control is the mainstay of treatment, with blockade of the renin-angiotensin system.

In type 1 diabetes, angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors reduce progression from microalbuminuria to overt nephropathy and from overt nephropathy to end-stage kidney disease.

In type 2 diabetes, ACE inhibitors reduce mortality in those with microalbuminuria, and angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) reduce progression from microalbuminuria to overt nephropathy and progression thereof. There is no evidence that ARBs reduce mortality, however, and so ACC inhibitors remain the first-line treatment for all forms of diabetic nephropathy in types 1 and 2 diabetes, although some favour ARBs in type 2 diabetes. The combination of an ACE inhibitor and ARB may be beneficial in thwarting nephropathy, but large studies are lacking. 

### Diabetic polneuropathy

This is one of the commonest long-term complications of diabetes and the commonest form of neuropathy in the developed world. It encompasses a number of neuropathic syndromes, the commonest of which is chronic distal symmetrical polneuropathy, also known as diabetic peripheral neuropathy (DPN). DPR is a cause of considerable morbidity, including foot ulceration, amputations and neuropathic pain, and is associated with cardiovascular disease and increased mortality. Risk factors for DPN include poor glucose control and markers of macrovascular disease, such as hypertension. There is strong evidence for nerve ischemia as the cause. Painful DPN affects 15-26% of all people with diabetes and can lead to considerable disability. First-line therapies for painful DPN are tricyclic antidepressants, serotonin norepinephrine (nondrenaline) reuptake inhibitors or anticonvulsants such as pregabalin or gabapentin.

### Diabetic autonomic neuropathy

This results in considerable morbidity and reduction in quality of life, and is associated with increased mortality. It may involve cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, urogenital, pupil pollinotot, thermoregulatory and sudomotor function.

### Other neuropathies

These include acute focal neuropathy, femoral neuropathy and diabetic amyotrophy.

### What do you know about diabetic neuropathy?

#### What do you know about diabetic foot?

Foot problems remain the commonest cause of hospital admission amongst patients with diabetes in Western countries. As many as 50% of older patients with type 2 diabetes have risk factors for foot problems.

Complications of diabetic neuropathy in the foot include ulceration, callus formation, deformity, and Charco's joints and wasting. Sensory neuropathy leads to unawareness of injury, autonomic neuropathy leads to anhidrosis with dry, fissuring skin and susceptibility to infection. Motor neuropathy leads to altered muscle tone and deformity, often with pes cavus and pressure areas.

The lifetime risk of a patient with diabetes developing an ulcer is 25%, and up to 85% of all lower limb amputations in diabetes are preceded by foot ulcers. The key to management of diabetic neuropathic foot ulceration is aggressive debridement with removal of callus and dead tissue, followed by application of some form of cast to offload the ulcer area. Most ulcers will heal if pressure is removed from the ulcer site, if the arterial circulation is sufficient and if infection is managed and treated aggressively.
