Any patient with a warm swollen foot without ulceration should be presumed to have acute Charco neuroarthropathy until proven otherwise.

The approach to reducing ulceration is regular screening, patient education and a team approach to management, both in the community and in hospital. Diabetic foot clinics utilise the expertise of diabetes, surgeons, chloroplasts and physiotherapists. Radiological investigations include plain radiographs for evidence of osteomyelitis (e.g. erosion, periostial reaction), nuclear imaging, computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging, the last being good for detecting extent and localisation of infection and inflammation.

### What do you know about sexual dysfunction in diabetes?

Erectile dysfunction affects over a third of diabetic men, caused by failure of nitric oxide-mediated smooth muscle relaxation of the corpus cavernosum secondary to endothelial dysfunction and autonomic neuropathy. Phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors are first-line treatment.

#### Eye problems

#### Case 2.24 Visual Loss

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the diabetes clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario 

### Patient information

Mr John Knight is an 80-year-old widowed man with type 2 diabetes mellitus who has experienced several episodes of disturbed vision over the past 3 months - sometimes blurred vision, sometimes glare whilst driving and sometimes indistinct letters when reading. Both eyes appear to be affected. Episodes resolve spontaneously and last for up to an hour. There is no associated limb weakness or speech difficulty or altered sensation. He feels his vision has generally become less distinct over the past year. He is still driving. He has a past medical history of type 2 diabetes but since a stroke with right arm weakness 3 years ago he has been very strict about keeping himself healthy. He made a full recovery from his stroke. He takes metformin 850 mg twice daily, aspirin 75 mg, simvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 4 mg and bendrolumenthaidze 2.5 mg. He monitors his blood glucose and knows that his glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) is 7.5%. He monitors his blood pressure and average readings are around 140/70 mmHg. His total cholesterol is 4.8 mmol/l. He has background diabetic retinopathy and at retinal screening 6 months ago the ophthalmology team were happy.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

##### Presenting problem/s and symptom exploration

\(\blacksquare\) **Explore visual loss.** Visual loss may be acute or gradual, and affect one or both eyes. It may commonly be due to the causes listed in Table 2.53.

\(\blacksquare\) **Ask about colours or shapes.** Patients frequently complain of _spots_ in their vision. It is important to differentiate between _flashing lights_ (a sign of incipient retinal detachment) and _floaters_ (opacities in the vitreous). Haloes (a waterlogged cornea breaks up white light as does the sky after a rainstorm, and because of the shape of the cornea the 'rainbow' is round) may be a sign of incipient acute glaucoma. Wavy lines on a grid may be due to wet age-related macular degeneration.

##### Patient perspective

He is extremely well motivated and an excellent historian.

##### Past medical history

His past medical history of stroke and diabetes immediately alert you to diabetic retinopathy or transient ischaemic attacks (TIAs), but the bilateral symptoms suggest alternative diagnoses.

##### Drug and allergy history

He is concordant with a good range of secondary vascular prophylaxis.

##### Family history

Relevance here is unlikely.

##### Social history

He should not drive until TIAs have absolutely been excluded and until his vision has been fully assessed.

##### Summary box - Assessment and PLAN

* **Likely diagnoses are cataracts (related or unrelated to his diabetes) or age-related macular degeneration or a combination of both. Glaucoma is possible. He should certainly be referred to an ophthalmologist.**
* **Transient ischaemic attacks seem less likely, but if there is concern about focal neurological symptoms/amaurosis fugax then a carotid Doppler study should be undertaken promptly. Hypoglycaemic episodes are possible.**

## Discussion

### What is glaucoma?

This is described in Case 5.30.

#### What are the visual requirements for driving?

These are described in Case 4.49.

#### Renal and metabolic problems

##### Case 2.25 Acute Kidney Injury

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Type of visual loss & Causes \\ Gradual loss of central vision in one or both eyes & Cataract Age-related macular degeneration \\ Gradual loss of peripheral vision in one or both eyes & Primary open angle glaucoma Retinal detachment Retinitis pigmentosa \\ Acute loss of central vision in one eye & Optic neuritis \\ Acute loss of vision (total or variable distribution) in one eye & Central / branch retinal vein occlusion Central retinal artery occlusion Retinal haemorrhage Amaurosis fugax \\ Acute homonymous visual field loss & Stroke \\ Case 2.25 Acute kidney injury \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.53: Causes of visual loss (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

### Scenario

#### 2.6.1 Re: Mr Philip Martin, aged 72 years

Thank you for admitting this gentleman who appears to be oligoauric. He seems to be developing oedema in his ankles and his blood pressure, usually well controlled, is 190/110 mmHg. His blood tests show a creatinine of 750 mmol/l and a potassium of 6.7 mmol/l. Urinalysis shows a trace of protein but no blood. His creatinine was 130 mmol/l if a year ago, at which time he also had a normal renal ultrasound. He has a past medical history of hypertension for which he takes ramipril and bendroflumethiazide, and benign prostatic hypertrophy under surveillance.

#### 2.6.2 Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr Philip Martin is a 72-year-old man who for the last 2 weeks has noticed difficulty passing urine and gradual swelling of his ankles. He now feels uncomfortable suprapubically and has been admitted to hospital. He has a history of hypertension for which he takes ramipril 10 mg and bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg, both daily. Last year he was investigated for possible prostate cancer, which turned out to be benign. He normally has a little trouble with urinary flow but nothing as problematic as this week. He feels generally weak and lethargic. He put off seeing his doctor because his wife has moderately severe Alzheimer's dementia and there is no one to look after her. He is a very active man who finally decided he must see his doctor because his symptoms had become so severe.

### How to approach the case

#### 2.6.3 Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### 2.6.4 Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

Most acute renal failure, now termed acute kidney injury (AKI), is managed by general physicians; it is essential to be able to assess and treat AKI in the first few hours of admission and know when to seek nephrology input. Over recent years it has been recognised that even small degrees of renal dysfunction are associated with worse patient outcomes. Unfortunately, as confirmed by the UK's National Confidential Enquiry into Patient Outcome and Death, recognition is often delayed and management frequently sub-optimal. Recognition of risk factors, accurate history taking careful examination and meticulous monitoring of volume balance are enough to reverse this process, although AKI does present a unique set of metabolic derangements that, if untreated, will result in death. AKI is defined in Box 2.68. These definitions, proposed by the Acute Dialysis Quality Initiative and the Acute Kidney Injury Network, aimed to promote earlier detection and recognition of AKI triggering appropriate treatment prior to progressive injury and kidney failure. The application of these definitions in more than 500 000 patients has validated the increased risk of mortality associated with developing AKI and indicated that the incidence of AKI in hospitalised patients may be as high as 18%. The definitions have recently been harmonised by the Kidney Diseases: Improving Global Outcomes International Guideline Group.

Clinical assessment should consider firstly patient safety, secondly risk factors and possible causes, and thirdly the effects of AKI.

* **Ensure safety**. Before full assessment, immediate management of hyperkalaemia (below), hypoxia (usually due to pulmonary oedema) or restoration of circulating volume may be needed.
* **Consider risk factors and possible causes**. These are summarised in Box 2.69 and Box 2.70.
* **Consider effects**. The following areas should be considered.

#### 2.6.1 Symptoms

Patients are usually asymptomatic until advanced stages and so lack of symptoms is not a reassurance. The syndrome of uraemia is complex and results from retention of multiple substances, many not yet established. The role of urea in uraemic syndrome is uncertain. Uraemic symptoms may include anorexia, nausea and 

#### Box 2.69 Risk factors for developing acute kidney injury

* Age > 75 years
* Chronic kidney disease (especially stage 3 or below)
* Heart failure
* Atherosclerotic peripheral vascular disease
* Liver disease
* Diabetes mellitus
* Nephrotoxic medication
* Hypovolaemia
* Sepsis

#### Box 2.70 Causes of kidney disease (acute kidney injury (AKI) and chronic kidney disease (CKD))

Patients presenting with renal disease may present with AKI, sub-acute renal disease or CKD. In all cases the following lists of causes should be considered and combinations of causes are common.

#### Pre-renal causes

Pre-renal AKI is an expected physiological response to:
* Hypovolaemia, e.g. haemorrhage, gastrointestinal losses, renal losses, burns
* cardiogenic shock or distributive shock, e.g. sepsis/the systemic inflammatory response syndrome, anaphylaxis
* Renal hypoperfusion, e.g. non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDS), angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, renal artery stenosis
* Oedematous states, e.g. heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome.

Pre-renal AKI may compound CKD, as in heart failure and renovascular disease, where glomerular filtration rate may fall sharply owing to worsening cardiac output or nephrotoxic drugs.

### Renal causes

The kidney comprises glomeruli, tubules and interstitium, and blood vessels. Disease in any of these can cause renal failure.

#### Glomerular disease
* Glomerulonephritis (GN) and primary glomerular diseases are discussed in Case 2.27. Most causes lead to CKD, but rapidly progressive CN because of vasculitis, lupus or glomerular basement membrane disease may cause AKI.
* Secondary glomerular diseases are common worldwide, e.g. bacterial, viral (especially hepatitis and human immunodeficiency virus), tumours and drugs, often presenting with nephrotic syndrome
* Diabetic nephropathy affects more than one-third of patients with type 1 diabetes and an ever-increasing proportion of patients with type 2 diabetes, and is the single most common cause of end-stage renal failure in Western countries
* Other systemic diseases affecting the kidney, often including the glomerulus, include the thrombotic microangiopathies, multi-system autoimmune diseases, including systemic lupus erythematosus and vasculitis, and paraprotein-related renal disease and amyloid.

#### Tubular injury and interstitial nephritis and tubule-interstiital disorders

Causes of tubular injury include:
* acute tubular necrosis
* Toxins, e.g. aminoglycosides, herbal remedies, radiological contrast
* Metabolic, e.g. hypercalaemia, immunoglobulin light chains
* Crystals, e.g. urate, oxalate
* Rhabdomyolysis (crush injury, status epilepticus, drug overdose. Causes of interstitial nephritis include:
* Drugs, e.g. NSAIDs, antibiotics

* sarcoidosis, tuberculosis
* post infertile, pyelonephritis.

CKD is accompanied by tubule-interstitial atrophy and fibrosis, regardless of the underlying cause. Disorders of the renal vasculature, the glomeruli or urinary drainage all culminate in chronic tubule-interstitial damage. Drug-induced renal disease can range from renal vasculitis to progressive CKD to ureteric obstruction.

#### Atherosclerotic renovascular disease (ARVD)

* _Macrovascular renal artery disease or renal artery stenosis (RAS)_ is atheromatous in 90% of cases, usually at the renal ostia (within 1 cm of the aorta-renal junction) and because the juxtalomperular apparatus perceives low volume it stimulates hyper-reniennic hyperplasia/discosterism. Fibromuscular dysplasia accounts for around 10% of RAS, drastically in a distal, "sausage-sting" pattern, usually presenting as hypertension in young patients, most often women, and there is often a successful response after angioplasty. Occasionally a large renal embolism may result from infective endocarditis or cardiac thrombus. Clinical assessment may detect a renal artery bruit (common) at the epigastrium) and an atrophic kidney (> 1.5 cm disparity) may be seen on ultrasound. Confirmation is by magnetic resonance or computed tomography angiography. RAS lesions are often functionally insignificant. The Angioplasty and Sterting for Renal Artery Lesions (ASTRA) trial showed no benefit of revascularisation for renal function or blood pressure, and consensus reserves intervention for those with severe RAS and AKI or flash pulmonary oedema. Otherwise medical therapy appears to be as effective. Where hyperalosteroism is refractory to medical therapy, angioplasty, stents, artery bypass or nephrectomy may be necessary.
* _Microvascular or 'intratrenal'rovascular disease_ may be atherosclerotic. There is usually long-standing parenchymal injury rather than reversible ischaemia, reflected in the variability in renal function outcome after revascularisation for renal artery stenosis. Thrombolysis, anticoagulants or invasive procedures shearing endothelial plaques occasionally trigger embolic showers which scatter through the renovascular system. Episodes of cholesterol emboli may be associated with eosinophilia.
* whilst ARVD promotes hypertension, chronic inadequately controlled hypertension causes _nephrosclerosis_, which may promote or accelerate CKD. Heart failure with RAS may be experienced as 'flash floods' of pulmonary oedema. There is usually severe hypertension and bilateral RAS.

_Other renovascular disease_ includes kidney involvement in systemic vasodilattices, _renal artery dissection_, which can cause back pain, and _renal vein thrombosis_, which may occasionally complicate malignancy or nephrotic syndrome.

### Post-renal causes

Obstructive (urologica) causes include:

* Postal hypertrophy
* many conditions causing kidney, ureteric or bladder stones including metabolic and inherited disorders, anatomical defects and chronic urinary infection; however, most are idiopathic and present with lain or abdominal pain, and macro
- or microscopic haematuria
* Transitional cell carcinoma of the bladder obstructing the ureters
* Ovarian and other pelvic masses obstructing the ureters
* Urethral strictures
* Retroperitoneal and radiation fibrosis
* Urinary tract infection, one of the commonest infections in humans, does not usually cause kidney injury unless part of a complicated infection in patients with anatomical or functional abnormalities of the urinary tract, or severe sepsis.

### Box 2.71 Assessment of fluid status

It is important to try to decide whether a patient is fluid depleted, fluid overloaded or normozoalemic based on the following:

* Blood pressure (high or low, including presence of a postural drop)
* Jugular venous pulse assessment (misleading in cor pulmonale)
* Examination for oedema (preipheral, sacral or pulmonary)
* Daily weight (1 litre = 1 kg) and fluid balance charts.

These are better signs of fluid status than examining skin turgor and mucous membranes (dehydration used to be assessed as: < 3% - no signs, 5% - dry mucous membranes and reduced skin turgor, 10% - sunken eyes, poor capillary return, > 10% - hypotensive, tachycardia).

### Metabolic status

Important renal blood tests (Box 2.72) establish haematobiochemical complications that can be life-threatening or clues to the severity of renal failure and its duration. Renal failure also causes platelet dysfunction, which may respond to synthetic antidiuretic hormone (DDAVP/desmopressin).

### Patient perspective

Explore his concerns about his wife and establish her needs, assuring him that you can make all necessary arrangements to secure her safety, for example by contacting relatives, the GP or social services.

### Box 2.72 Important blood tests in kidney disease

* Potassium
* Bicarbonate: \(\lambda\) with a high anion gap metabolic acidosis if severe
* Urea and creatinine*: \(\uparrow\) proportionately
* Calcium: \(\lambda\) then normalised +/- \(\uparrow\) in chronic kidney disease (CKD)
* Phosphate: \(\uparrow\) in CKD
* Partiallyrold hormone: \(\uparrow\) in CKD
* Haemoglobin: \(\lambda\) in CKD due to failure of erythropoietin synthesis
* White cell count: neutrophilia in sepsis; eosinophils suggests interstitial nephritis
* Blood film: thrombotic microorganisms
* A rise in urea disproportionately higher than a rise in creatinine may be caused by dehydration, diuretics, gastrointestinal bleeding, high protein intake, catabolic states such as stress, starvation and steroids, or tetracolynes. A disproportionate creatinine rise occurs in rhabdomyolysis, in which myoglobin released from damaged muscle in severe crush injury, peripheral ischaemia or infarction 'closg' renal tubules. There is usually other evidence of cell lysis such as raised potassium, phosphate and creatine kinase. 

#### Past medical history

The history of hypertension probably explains his CKD and obstruction from prostatic enlargement the acute presentation.

#### Drug and allergy history

The angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor and bendroffumethiazide are now contributing to the mischief and must be stopped.

#### Family history

It is always important to ask about hereditary kidney disease, notably polycystic kidney disease, although it is unlikely here.

#### Social history

Establish more details about the care his wife needs and, if necessary, invoke the help of a social worker.

## Discussion

### What investigations are important in newly detected renal failure?

Tests aim to cause, determine whether acute or chronic, assess severity (Table 2.54) and monitor progress.

#### Urinalysis

Urinalysis can provide very helpful information in AKI:

* Positive protein values of 3+ and 4+ on reagent strip testing suggest intrinsic glomerular disease.
* A reagent strip positive for blood suggests the presence of red blood cells (> 5 per high-power field). Although red cell morphology may not be particularly useful the observation of large numbers of red cells in the presence of proteinuria suggests a glomerular aetiology for AKI, suspicion strengthened by the finding of red cell casts on a freshly collected sample of urine (this is rarely performed in the UK). Haematuria may also be found in lower urinary tract pathology including tumours, calculi and infection, and in severe renal ischaemia. Characteristically myoglobinuria will cause a positive reagent strip reaction for haematuria without evidence of red cells on urine microscopy.
* Increased numbers of white cells (> 5 per high-power field) are non-specific but found commonly with infection, acute interstitial nephritis, and glomerulonephritis. Eosinophiluria is not a specific test for interstitial nephritis but has value in ruling out the disease.
* Urine microscopy can be informative in particular cases of poisoning, e.g. oxalate crystalluria in ethylene glycol poisoning, urate crystalluria in tumour lysis syndrome, and crystalluria from drugs causing AKI such as sulphonamides, acylovir,

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c}  & **Stage** & **Serum creatinine (Scr)** & **Urine output** \\ \hline KDGO & Stage 1 & \(\uparrow\) Scr \(\geq\) 1.5–1.9 x baseline reference Scr or & \(<\) 0.5 ml / kg / h \(\geq\) 6 hours \\ steering & & \(\uparrow\) Scr \(\geq\) 26 μmol / l within 48 hours \\ SpMen & Stage 2 & \(\uparrow\) Scr \(\geq\) 2.0–2.9 × baseline reference Scr & \(<\) 0.5 ml / kg / h \(\geq\) 12 hours \\ \cline{2-4}  & Stage 3 & \(\uparrow\) Scr \(\geq\) 3.0 × baseline reference Scr or & \(<\) 0.3 ml / kg / h \(\geq\) 24 hours \\ Tscr \(\geq\) 44 μmol / l from baseline reference Scr \(\geq\) 354 μmol / l or & or anuria ≥ 12 hours \\  & commenced on renal replacement therapy irrespective of stage \\ \hline Riki & Risk & \(\uparrow\) Scr \(\geq\) 1.5 × baseline or \(\downarrow\) GFR \(\geq\) 25\% & \(<\) 0.5 ml / kg / h \(\geq\) 6 hours \\ \hline classification & Injury & \(\uparrow\) Scr \(\geq\) 2.0 × baseline or \(\downarrow\) GFR \(\geq\) 50\% & \(<\) 0.5 ml / kg / h \(\geq\) 12 hours \\ \cline{2-4}  & Failure & \(\uparrow\) Scr \(\geq\) 3.0 × baseline or \(\downarrow\) GFR \(\geq\) 75\% & \(<\) 0.3 ml / kg / h \(\geq\) 24 hours \\  & Loss & Complete loss of kidney function \(>\) 4 weeks \\  & End-stage kidney disease & End-stage kidney disease \(>\) 3 months \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.54: Acute kidney injury staging system and classification triamterene, indinavir and cathartics high in phosphates.

### Blood tests

These should include 'renal blood tests' (Box 2.72), full blood count and clotting screen, and inflammatory markers. An autoimmune screen (antinuclear antibodies, double-stranded DNA, antineutotrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, complement components, anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies) and paraprotein screen (immunoglobulin levels, serum electrophoresis, urine for light chains/Bence Jones proteins or serum free light chains) may be important. Uric acid concentration and creatine kinase if tissue damage is possible (rhabdomyolysis) may be requested, and a blood film for microangapathic haemolytic anaemia. Hepatitis B virus/hepatitis C virus and human immunodeficiency virus status are needed if renal replacement therapy is likely.

#### Electrocardiogram and chest X-ray

These are essential for assessing cardiac disease, metabolically driven electrical disturbances and fluid status.

#### Renal tract ultrasound

Ultrasound is the gold standard test for diagnosis of upper tract obstruction through the finding of hydronephrosis and/or hydroturer. However, upper urinary tract obstruction may not be initially detected by ultrasound in a patient who is volume depleted. There are other circumstances when ultrasound may not be diagnostic, such as in retroperitoneal fibrosis or early in the course of obstructive disease, in which case additional imaging studies may be considered.

#### Renal biopsy

Renal biopsy is of fundamental importance in nephrology and transplantation. Using appropriate guidelines for including or excluding patients it is generally safe with the commonest complication being bleeding. The biopsy must be processed and stained for standard haematoxylin and eosin sections as well as for immunohistochemistry and electron microscopy to provide a complete picture of the underlying pathology. Renal biopsy should be considered in patients with normal-sized, unobstructed kidneys where acute tubular necrosis (ATN) causing AKI does not seem likely, and without delay in patients in whom rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis due to vasculitis seems likely.

### What do you understand by the term acute tubular necrosis or injury (ATN or ATI)?

ATN (injury rather than necrosis reflects potential reversibility) refers to ischaemic tubular damage that is a consequence of pre-renal AKI. Various mechanisms play a role, including intense renal vasoconstriction, formation of tubular casts and reperfusion injury.

### How may pre-kidney AKI be distinguished from established ATN or oliguric AKI?

In pre-renal AKI, the tubules can reabsorb and thus concentrate urine. In ATN, the tubules are damaged and 'everything leaks out'. However, pre-kidney AKI often leads to ATN and results become indeterminate. Various measures have been claimed to aid in the differentiation of AKI including urinary sodium, fractional excretion of sodium, urine osmolality, and urine/plasma creatinine and urea ratios, fractional excretion of urea, freewater clearance and creatinine clearance. All of these have limitations and their specificity and sensitivity in clinical practice often means that a single measurement may be inconclusive except in extreme circumstances. In pre-renal AKI there is increased urinary sodium reabsorption and increased urinary urea reabsorption. This should therefore be reflected by low urine sodium concentrations, low fractional excretion of sodium and low fractional excretion of urea, and increased blood urea-creatinine ratios. Urinary electrolytes should be interpreted with caution, particularly in the elderly (who may already have an impaired concentrating ability), and in patients on diuretics (which increase urinary sodium excretion) or with a salt-losing state. In such patients the fractional excretion of urea may be a more useful index. One clinical situation where measurement of urinary electrolytes may have clinical use is in the diagnosis of hepatorenal syndrome as the cause of AKI in patients with liver disease. The diagnostic criteria for hepatorenal failure include a urine sodium of less than 10 mmol/l (Table 2.55).

### What are the management principles in an acute uraemic emergency?

The facts of greatest importance are the urine output and serum creatinine (which has an approximate inverse relationship to urine output in the steady state).

#### Treat the effects

Both rate of deterioration and severity are proportional to the urgency of this response. Urgent attention may be needed for:

* Serum potassium (Box 2.73)
* Fluid status (Box 2.74)
* Acidosis (give intravenous bicarbonate)
* Encephalopathy from uraemia
* Anaemia (occasionally).

#### Establish and treat the cause

This depends on the setting, which may be expected, as in septic shock or heart failure, or unexpected. Urinalysis for active sediment with blood is an essential first step in considering vasculitis. Nephrotoxic drugs should be stopped. An urgent renal ultrasound helps to distinguish between three possibilities:

* Obstruction, in which hydronephrosis is invariably present * [2] **Box 2.73 (Training hyperkalaemia)**

There is no absolute level of risk. Rate of change is more important, and chronic rises are better tolerated such that extreme levels up to 8 or 9 mmol/l are sometimes seen (although these should still be treated ( urgently). Fasting lowers insulin levels and may exacerbate hyperkalaemia in chronic kidney disease. Cardiac arrest is rare under 7 mmol/l but 6.5 mmol/l is dangerous and a level > 6 mmol/l warrants close consideration of treatment.

Calcium (calcium gluconate) is administered intravenously if there are electrocardiogram changes. This promotes intracellular shift of potassium and is cardioprotective. Tented T waves are usually not fit the threatening but may progress to immediately file-threatening broadening of QRS complexes and Twouse disappearance and a bizarre electrocardiogram. Calcium should be administered immediately with an immediate venous blood gas to urgently estimate serum potassium.

Dectrose and insulin (50 mmol 50% dextrose with 10 units of Actrapid intravenously over 15-30 minutes) shifts potassium intracellularly and should also be given with or without electrocardiogram changes.

Salbutamol 10-20 mg by nebuliser acts similarly but causes tachycardia and agitation and may not be necessary if intravenous treatment can be established quickly.

Bicarbonate may help and is popular in some renal units.

These measures are temporary, causing potassium shift, but have no effect on total body potassium and levels can swing upwards again within a few hours. Definitive lowering of potassium requires a diuresis (fluid resuscitation if dehydrated, furosemide if fluid overload) but if oligonaturia persists then haemodialysis may be needed.

Calcium resonium 1 g t.d.s orally may sustain safe levels, but evidence is weak. This is more likely where there is tissue damage (e.g. rhabdomyolysis) sustaining the problem.

Stopping exacerbating drugs (e.g. angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, gyniolactone, angiotensin receptor blockers) and a low-potassium diet are essential.

* [3] **Box 2.74 (Training hyperkalaemia)**

* [4] **Volume status must be optimised urgently. Dopamine is of no value, and there is no specific pharmacological therapy proven to effectively treat acute kidney injury (AKI) secondary to hypoperfusion injury and/or sepsis. A central venous line is seldom needed.
* **Prescription of intravenous fluid should follow a careful assessment of volume status, i.e. hypovolaemic, euolaemic, hyperolaemic. A patient with hypoperfusion, potvatal hypoperfusion, decreased skin turgor and dry mucous membranes will be dry. A dry patient may develop acute tubular necrosis and need dialysis. The most important principle is to put fluid in and 'fill' the bath; and if too much is put in it can be removed and the 'plug pulled' with loop duresis. Loop duresis should otherwise be avoided. A patient with a raised jugular venous pulse and oedema will be overloaded.**
* **Daily sodium intake in health is between 70 and 100 mmol/day. Following surgery or trauma the body's physiological response is to retain sodium and water. The selection of the type of fluid to be prescribed is important as excessive 0.9% sodium chloride (Na 154 mmol/l, Cl 154 mmol/l) can potentially lead to hyperolaemic acidosis, sodium, chloride and water overload which contributes to postoperative morbidity and mortality whereas excessive 5% dextrose will increase the risk of hyponatraemia. Fluid replacement prescriptions should be tailored to the needs of the patient. Potassium-containing solutions (fiartmann's and Ringer's lactate) should be used cautiously owing to the potential risk of hyperkalaemia. follow relief of obstruction, requiring large volume fluid replacement.

#### Establish reversibility

If this is not clear, a nephrologist should be consulted. Errors in managing an acute uraemic emergency include failure to identify the cause, delayed referral if renal function is deteriorating and fiddling with irrelevant issues such as watching urine output without acting.

How would you manage a patient with renal and heart failure who has pulmonary oedema but is hypotensive?

Cardiorenal failure is extremely common, with hypotension from left ventricular failure with or without pulmonary oedema, and renal failure from low cardiac output and often renovascular disease. Such patients are fluid overloaded, but the notion of being 'wet but intravascular dry' is frequently mooted. Treatment of the situation depends upon the goal. To improve breathing, the patient will need to be dry, aided by loop diuretics, and if creatinine rises from, say, 150 to 210, then that is of course perfectly acceptable. But without respiratory symptoms of overload, adequate fluid replacement to maintain acceptable renal function is preferred. What is absolutely illogical and should not be instituted in combination is fluid replacement and diuretics. Occasionally, pure cor pulmonale without left ventricular failure is the cause. There is no evidence in this situation for augmenting treatment with ACE inhibitors when the position has stabilised.
