\begin{tabular}{c c c c} Hypokalaemia & & **Hyperkalaemia** \\ \hline Redistribution & Alkalosis & Redistribution & Haemolysed sample \\ Catecholamines & cognition & Sample stored on ice \\ Insulin & First denching \\ B\({}_{r}\)-agonists & Hyperentialation & Inorganic acids \\ Periodic paralyses & Diabetic ketoradiosis & Lactic acidosis \\ Hyperbolic & & Hyperkalaemic periodic paralysis \\ Cell lysis, e.g. rhabdomyolysis, haemolysis, tumour lysis, chemotherapy \\ \hline Total body & Decreased intake, e.g. anorexia & Total body & Increased intake, e.g. bananas, \\ mediastinum & mediastinum & mediastinum & Genes \\ depletion & Increased loss & Gastrointestinal: urine K\textless{} 20 mmol / day; & \\ \hline Renal: urine K\textless{} 20 mmol / day & Renal: urine K\textless{} 20 mmol / day & \\ \hline Altogether & Hyperalostorension with hypertension, fluid overload & Primary (renin suppressed), e.g. Conn’s syndrome, Cushing’s syndrome (cortisol has mineralocorticoid activity before metabolised to cortisone) & Affectorne deficiency \\  & & & \\ \(K^{*}\textgreater{} 20 mmol / day\) & Secondary, e.g. renal artery stenosis (renin stimulated), genetic syndrome (renin suppressed) & \\  & & (ludde); glucocorticoid remediable hyperalostorension, mineralocorticoid excess (Case 2.14) & \\ \hline Aldosterone not primarily involved, with normotension and urine & Acidosis (plasma bicarbonate low) & Aldosterone resistance: congenital, e.g. types 1 or 2 renal tubular acidosis & e.g. pseudophyoalostorension; acquired, e.g. angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, potassium-paraine districts (spironolactone, amiloride), renal tubular disease (systemic lupus erythematosus, amyloid, diabetes mellitus, obstructive uropathy) \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.56: Causes of hypokalaemia and hyperkalaemia 

### What are the indications for urgent haemodialysis?

Indications for urgent haemodialysis via a temporary central venous catheter include:

* Life-threatening hyperkalaemia
* Pulmonary oedema in an anuric patient
* Symptomatic uraemia, e.g. nausea, drowsiness
* Severe acidosis (e.g. bicarbonate < 12 mmol/l) if unwell or an additional indication is present
* Serositis (now rare), e.g. pericarditis, pericardial rub, pleuritic.

How is serum potassium concentration regulated and what are the causes of hypokalaemia and hyperkalaemia? (Table 2.56)

Acute changes in potassium concentration are usually the result of redistribution between the extracellular fluid and intracellular fluid. Alkalosis, catecholamines (potassium is often slightly low in patients because of the stress response, which increases sympathetic drive and releases glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids, e.g. after an acute coronary syndrome) and insulin and b2-agonists tend to redistribute potassium ions into cells. The hypokalaemic and hyperkalaemic periodic paralyzes are the result of redistribution abnormalities. Rapid cell growth (e.g. in the treatment of megaloblastic anaemia) may also cause shift of potassium ions into cells. Chronic regulation of potassium concentration is undertaken by the kidney, in the distal tubule. Potassium disorders may be evaluated with three questions:

* Is redistribution likely?
* Is the kidney involved in the pathogenesis (by measurement of urinary potassium)?
* If the kidney is involved, what is the role of aldosterone?

### How would you treat hypokalaemia?

Treatment depends upon duration. Chronic hypokalaemia may be associated with a total body potassium deficit > 100 mmol, which should be replaced orally. Intravenous potassium should not exceed 20 mmol/h. If there is concurrent metabolic acidosis, then hypokalaemia should be treated first.

## Case 2.26 Chronic Kidney Disease

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

Dear Doctor

Re: Mrs May Russell, aged 68 years

Thank you for seeing this lady whose creatinine is 175 mmol/l, haemoglobin 9.9 g/dl, albumin 32 and estimated glomerular filtration rate 24. She is otherwise well but her creatinine was 125 mmol/l 12 months ago and I wonder if she now merits a renal opinion. Her blood pressure is 160/92 mmHg and her total cholesterol is 5 mmol/l. Her myeloma screen is negative. She has proteinuria but no blood, glucose or nitrites on urinalysis.

#### Young sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mrs May Russell is a 68-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease (CKD). She feels well but has been referred to the hospital for an opinion as to whether further investigations are needed because her kidney function has deteriorated moderately since a year ago. Her past medical history comprises hypertension, angina and a possible transient ischaemic attack; she recently had an episode of transient loss of vision in one eye but did not mention this to her GP. She does not have diabetes. Her medications are aspirin 75 mg, simvastatin 20 mg, perindopril 4 mg and atenolol 50 mg, all daily. She also takes non-prescription ibuprofen for back pain, a problem she has had for 30 years since a road-traffic accident. She smokes 10 cigarettes per day. She is very afraid of ending up on 'dialysis' since her husband died of advanced kidney failure due to prostate disease.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

A similar approach to considering causes to Case 2.25 (Acute kidney injury) should be taken.

#### Patient perspective

Harness her strong desire to avoid dialysis by emphasizing the significant benefits of stopping smoking and explain that there are things you can do to modify her risk, including optimising her blood pressure and cholesterol. 

#### Past medical history

The history of hypertension and cardiovascular disease (CVD) imply likely renovascular disease.

#### Drug and allergy history

The angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor can continue but an alternative to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs should be sought.

#### Family history

Relevance here is unlikely.

#### Social history

She should stop smoking because this contributes to her coronary disease, likely renovascular disease and possible carotid atheroma.

## Discussion

### Why is CKD important?

CKD is associated with substantial comorbidity including hypertension, CVD, anaemia and renal bone disease. People with it are far more likely to die from CVD than progression to renal failure requiring dialysis or kidney transplantation.

### What causes CKD?

There are many causes of CKD, the most common being diabetes mellitus (50%) and hypertension (25-50%). Glomerulonephritis is now a relatively small group.

### How is CKD defined and classified and how does this aid management?

CKD is diagnosed if evidence of kidney damage has been present for more than 3 months; it is divided into five stages depending on the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) (Table 2.57). The prevalence of stages 3-5 CKD in the UK is around 10%. Stratifying CKD helps to identify patients in whom metabolic complications are likely to arise (stage 3) and guides preparation for dialysis and transplantation. Patients should not be allowed to 'crash land' to haemodialysis as late referral is associated with poorer outcomes (e.g. central-line complications, sepsis) and higher cost.

### What do you understand by the term glomerular filtration rate (GFR)?

The glomerulus is a filter producing a cell-free and protein-free ultrafiltrate of plasma which enters the tubule. GFR is determined by the driving force of hydrostatic pressure from the heart and counteractive forces including Bowman's space hydrostatic pressure, oncotic pressures, filter permeability and surface area. GFR is greatly reduced in inflammatory glomerular disease. Normal GFR is around 125 ml/min (around 150-180 litres of ultrafiltrate daily), most of which is reabsorbed by the tubules. GFR is tightly autoregulated by constriction or dilatation of the afferent or efferent arterioles in response to changes in renal artery perfusion. Measuring GFR is unhelpful in acute kidney injury because of rapidly changing function.

### What do the renal tubules do?

Glomerular filtration and tubular transport are the two sequential mechanisms underlying renal function. Renal clearance of a substance refers to its net handling and is the sum of urine concentration x urine flow rate/plasma concentration (ml/min). Most reabsorptive processes occur in the proximal tubule and loop of Henle; the distal tubule and collecting duct are important in determining final urinary excretion of water and solutes. Most tubular transport processes are directly or indirectly coupled to reabsorption of sodium ions. Na+ ions leave the lumen and passively cross the apical surface of the tubular cell down a concentration or electrochemical gradient via Na+ channels or transporters and exit across the basolateral membrane via a sodium-potassium ATPase pump (ultimately required for most reabsorptive and secretory processes).

### How is GFR derived?

Isotope measurement of GFR is the gold standard measurement of renal function. Since measuring it is cumbersome, GFR is estimated (eGFR) by creatinine clearance, a correlate of GFR determined by either the Modification of Diet in Renal Disease or the Cockroft-Gault formula. eGFR is now calculated on all samples sent for creatinine measurement, and creatinine concentration has an inverse relationship to it.

### Why is GFR a better marker of renal function than serum creatinine?

The kidneys have enormous reserve. Mildly elevated creatinine may imply that three-quarters of functioning renal tissue is lost. Because of the reciprocal relationship between GFR and creatinine concentration, a small GFR change close to the normal range will have much less effect on creatinine than a small change when the GFR is markedly reduced. Further, changes in creatinine lag behind changes in GFR. Serum creatinine alone is a poor indicator of the degree of renal failure because it is influenced by muscle mass, age, sex, weight, diet and drugs. An elderly patient with a small body mass index and creatinine of

300 might have uracemic symptoms while a young muscular patient with a creatinine of 1000 might not. Creatinine may also exhibit a modest decrease because of haemodilution in rapid fluid resuscitation and increase with trimethoprim.

In addition to GFR, detection of kidney disease typically involves measurement of urinary protein (or albumin) concentration. The use of urinary protein-creatinine (or albumin-creatinine) ratios obviates the need for 24-hour urine collections.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c c c c} Stage & GFR ml / min / 1.73 m2 & Description & Monitoring & Referral to nephrologist & Management \\  & & As a general rule, eGFR is a measure of percentage of overall kidney function & & & & \\
1 & ≥90 & Kidney damage with normal or slightly T GFR & 12-monthly Referral if: Proteinuria & & & \\  & & & Mitochondria / macroscopic haematuria (urrologist if older, nephrologist if younger) & & & \\  & & & Multi-system disease suspected, e.g. systemic lupus erythematosus Family history, e.g. polycystic kidney disease Uncontrolled hypertension GRR declining rapidly & & & \\
2 & 60–89 & Kidney damage with mild Λ GFR & 12-monthly & As for stage 1 & As for stage 1 \\
3 & 30–59 & 3a & Moderate Λ GFR & 6-monthly & Referral if: Any of the conditions for stage 1 or 2 above are inert & As for above stages (but note that extensive investigation unlikely to be rewarding if stable stage 3, asymptomatic and normal urinary) + evaluation and treatment of CKD complications (anaemia, calcium, and phosphateobacillus) \\
4 & 15–29 & Severe ↓ GFR & 3-monthly & Urgent referral, especially if any of the conditions for stage 3 are met, diabetes present, GFR is falling rapidly or there are metabolic disturbances & As for above stages + preparation for RT - likely to be needed in 6–8 months \\  & & & & & & 

## Why is proteinuria important?

Proteinuria is:

* A marker of renal disease and of progression of renal disease
* A predictor and risk factor of CVD.

The importance of this is emphasised in the modern CKD classification, and the suffix (p) should be used when denoting the stage of CKD.

### Types of proteinuria

Normally low-molecular-weight proteins and albumin filtered from the plasma are reabsorbed in the proximal tubule resulting in a total daily proteinuria of less than 150 mg, of which only around 10 mg is albumin. More than these levels is abnormal, although early renal disease (especially in diabetes) results in low-level albuminuria of 30-300 mg daily known as microalbuminuria, although this is undetectable on most dipstick tests. Proteinuria occurs in a number of states:

* Functional proteinuria (occurs in exercise, fever and heart failure probably due to glomerular haemodynamic changes)
* Overflow proteinuria (increased filtration of low-molecular-weight proteins through a normal glomerular barrier as in light chain proteinuria in myeloma)
* Orthostatic proteinuria (only when upright, not on early morning sample)
* Tubular proteinuria (loss of proteins normally reabsorbed and proteins released by damaged cells)
* Glomerular proteinuria (increased permeability of the glomerular barrier).

### Quantification of proteinuria

Quantification of proteinuria has historically been by 24-hour urine collection, but this is inconvenient and thus often inaccurate. Current best practice involves measuring the albumin and/or protein concentration on a spot, ideally early morning, urine sample (Table 2.58). To correct for the effects of urine concentration (hydration state), the albumin or protein concentration is normalised to the creatinine concentration and expressed as the albumin to creatinine ratio (ACR) or protein to creatinine ratio (PCR), respectively expressed as mg albumin or protein/mmol creatinine. An average person excretes 10 mmol of creatinine daily so the ACR and PCR can be multiplied by 10 to give an estimate of the daily albumin or protein loss in mg.

Those with an ACR >= 30 and < 70 or a PCR >= 50 and < 100 should have confirmation on an early morning sample with a specialist opinion desirable for an ACR >= 70 or PCR >= 100 unless there is appropriately treated diabetes, or an ACR >= 30 or PCR >= 50 with haematuria.

### Why is early detection of CKD important?

CKD is generally asymptomatic until stage 4, but renal disease is often progressive once the GFR falls below 25% of normal. The haemodynamic changes (including angiotensin II (AII) activity), which sustain renal function initially in surviving nephrons, are ultimately detrimental. People at high risk should be evaluated for markers of CKD (albuminuria, abnormal urine sediment, elevated serum creatinine, decreased GFR) because of the potential to modify disease progression and because of the accelerated CVD associated with CKD.

## Which people should be targeted for early detection of CKD?

Those with the following should be offered testing:

* Diabetes
* Hypertension
* CVD (ischaemic heart disease, heart failure, peripheral vascular disease, cerebrovascular disease)
* Structural renal or post-renal disease
* Multi-system disease with renal involvement (e.g. systemic lupus erythematosus)
* Family history of stage 5 CKD

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c}  & **Normal** & **Microalbuminuria** & **Non-nephrotic range proteinuria** & **Nephrotic-range proteinuria** \\ * Opportunistic haematuria or proteinuria
* Those taking potentially nephrotoxic drugs.

### How may acute kidney injury be distinguished from CKD?

Clinical history and knowledge of previous blood tests are most valuable. Calcium and phosphate disturbances may suggest renal osteodystrophy and anaemia may be the result of erythropoietin deficiency, both reflecting CKD. On ultrasonography, small kidneys, thinning of cortices and increased echogenicity favour a diagnosis of CKD.

### What strategies can slow the rate of progression of CKD?

Factors causing progressive decline in renal function and strategies to slow progression are outlined in Table 2.59.

### How should hypertension be managed in CKD?

The management of hypertension in renal disease is best understood by classifying patients according to the stage of their CKD. In CKD stages 3 and 4 there is good evidence for slowing the rate of progression of renal disease and reducing cardiovascular morbidity and mortality, and evidence suggests reducing blood pressure to less than 130/80 mmHg. In CKD stage 5, evidence of benefit is more controversial, several studies showing worse outcomes in patients with low blood pressure. In renal transplant recipients additional factors need to be considered such as corticosteroid and calcineurin sparing to reduce hypertension, and blood pressure lowering as in CKD stages 3-4 reduces cardiovascular morbidity and mortality and prolongs graft survival.

What is the rationale for using ACE inhibitors or angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) in diabetic nephropathy?

The strong rationale is discussed in Case 2.23 (Type 2 diabetes).

### What is the rationale for using ACE inhibitors or ARBs in non-diabetic nephropathy?

ACE inhibitors slow the progression of non-diabetic nephropathy. In the Ramipril Efficacy in Nephropathy (REIN) studies, ramipril was so effective in slowing the rate of GFR decline in patients with nephrotic-range proteinuria that the trial was stopped early; in patients with 1-3 g/day of proteinuria the median time to established renal failure almost doubled, benefit seen across a wide range of initial GFRs. A meta-analysis by Jafar et al confirmed the benefit and the COOPERATE trial showed that combination of ACE inhibitors and ARBs safely slows the progression of non-diabetic nephropathy.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Factors causing progressive & Strategies to slow progression \\ decline in renal function & \\ \hline Persistent activity of underlying cause & Treat cause \\ Hypertension & Meticulous blood pressure control – the most important intervention to slow progression – with any agent unless proteinuria \\
120–139 / \textless{} 90 mmHg recommended by NICE in CKD (120–129 / \textless{} 80 if PCR \geq 100\) or ACR \(\geq 70\)) \\ Proteinuria, the best predictor of decline & ACE inhibitors or ARBs reduce proteinuria and slow progression of CKD and are first line if: PCR \(\geq 50\) or ACR \(\geq 30\) if hypertensive \\ PCR \(\geq 100\) or ACR \(\geq 70\) if not hypertensive \\ Diabetes if microalbuminuria – ACR 2.5–30 (men) or 3.5–30 (women) – even if not hypertensive \\ Combining an ACE inhibitor and ARB may be beneficial but may be harmful in minimal proteinuria \\ Poor glycaemic control & Meticulous glycaemic control as well as blood pressure control \\ Dyslipidemia & Use of statins for primary prevention of CVD as for people without CKD \\ Status for people with CVD \\ Smoking & Smoking cessation \\ High phosphate or protein diet & But maintain good nutrition and avoid low-protein diet because malnourishment leads to a poorer outcome with dialysis \\ Hyperphosphataemia & Phosphate binders \\ Anaemia (may cause high cardiac output and left ventricular hypertrophy) & Erythropoietin \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.59: Strategies to slow progression of chronic kidney disease (CKD) 

### How do ACE inhibitors and ARBs delay progression of CKD?

In CKD perfusion of the glomerulus is often maintained by AII-mediated efferent arteriole vasoconstriction. Whilst this is immediately beneficial, it is ultimately destructive as it raises pressure in the glomerulus and predisposes to glomerular sclerosis. Decreasing AII-mediated vasoconstriction with ACE inhibitors or ARBs lowers glomerular pressure and is ultimately beneficial but does cause a reversible rise in serum creatinine or fall in GFR.

### Should ACE inhibitors or ARBs be stopped if serum creatinine rises?

Given the above, in CKD creatinine may rise by up to 30% on these treatments but if it subsequently plateaus and serum potassium is within safe limits then ACE inhibitors and ARBs should be continued with close monitoring.

### Why might ACE inhibitors or ARBs be dangerous in renal artery stenosis (RAS)? (Fig. 2.9)

Numerous molecules act on the afferent and efferent arterioles in the kidney to maintain GFR. AII is activated by a reduction in renal artery perfusion pressure. This occurs in situations that would cause pre-renal renal failure (hypovolamelia, left ventricular failure, sepsis), which lead to a fall in glomerular hydrostatic pressure, a fall in GFR and in renin release from the juxtap glomerular apparatus. All promotes aldosterone action and promotes systemic vasoconstriction and renal vasoconstriction (the efferent arteriole is particularly affected and constricts to maintain intradomerular pressure).

ACE inhibitors and ARBs cause afferent and efferent arteriole vasodilatation. In RAS, the afferent arteriole cannot effectively dilate but the efferent arteriole can. Blood then effectively bypasses the glomerulus.

However, decreased GFR or elevated creatinine do not necessarily occur, even with bilateral RAS, and decreased GFR or elevated creatinine often occur with ACE inhibitors in people without RAS. In any low blood pressure state, GFR becomes AII dependent and thus ACE inhibitor sensitive (RAS is a perceived low-volume state where blood pressure is usually high), ACE inhibitors removing the partially protective effect of AII-mediated efferent arteriolar vasoconstriction.

Furthermore, in CKD of any cause there is an overall drop in GFR because of nephron loss triggering angiotensin-mediated compensatory increased GFR in surviving nephrons; this increase in GFR seldom exceeds 30%.

### What do you know about renal metabolic bone disease or osteodystrophy in CKD?

This is summarised in Box 2.75.

### Why is CKD associated with accelerated CVD?

CVD is the main cause of death in CKD. Traditional risk factors are common but do not explain an independent very substantial increased risk. In patients with end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis the risk of CVD is approximately 20 times that of the general population. Proteinuria, impaired calcium-phosphate homeostasis, anaemia and inflammation probably contribute. Possible mechanisms for increased vascular calcification include hyper-phosphataemia and increased calcium-phosphate product (Ca-PP) - vascular smooth muscle cells exposed to high phosphate enhance osteoblast gene expression; arteriolarecrosis and calcification increase pulse wave velocity and left ventricular hypertrophy with reduced coronary flow in diastole.

### What do you know about anaemia in CKD?

This is summarised in Box 2.76.

### What forms of renal replacement therapy (RRT) are there?

There are haemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis and renal transplantation.

#### Haemodialysis (Fig. 2.10)

A temporary double-lumen catheter may be inserted into the internal jugular or subclavian vein (the femoral vein is acceptable in an emergency but suboptimal) for emergency haemodialysis. Permanent haemodialysis requires an arteriovenous fistula (AVF) or Gortex graft. An AVF is an anastomosis created surgically between an artery and vein, commonly at the forearm, which dilates over a number of weeks to become suitable for twin-needed

Figure 2.9: Why angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors are contraindicated in renal artery stenosis (RAS). "The obstruction is really in the trunk artery but the effect is the same – the ratio of supply to EA run-off is reduced and the glomeruli are deprived of blood. All, angiotensin II; AA, afferent arteriole; EA efferent arteriole; RAS, renal artery stenosis. 

### Types of renal MBD

Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is associated with various types of MBD, which can start early in CKD (stage 2), and are common in stage 3.

### Secondary hyperparathyroidism

In early CKD a slightly raised phosphate (retention) is associated with the potential for mild hypocalcaemia. Decreased 1,25-dihydroxycholesterol/ vitamin D production by the kidney leads to reduced gut calcium and phosphate absorption and secondary hyperparathyroidism, which normalises serum calcium but increases phosphate. Alkaline phosphatase may be raised. This is, in essence, a high-turnover form of MBD, the drivers being failure of renal biodegradation of vitamin D, phosphate retention and, in some cases, hypocharcaemia.

Metality fibrosis cystic refers to the effect of secondary hyperparathyroidism on trabecular bone, with accelerated turnover and fibrosis of marrow, excessive osteoblast and osteoclast activity is driven by parathyroid hormone (PTH) and cytokines. Radiological correlates of hyperparathyroidism are osteopenia and areas of sclerosis ("ruger jersey spine", 'pepper-pot skull") and subperisotest erosions (starting in the phalanges) and bone cysts.

### Tertiary hyperparathyroidism

As CKD progresses, insensitivity of the parathyroids to calcium and skeletal resistance to PTH worsen the situation, ultimately with parathyroid hyperplasia and autonomous PTH secretion - tertiary hyperparathyroidism - provoking hypercalcaemia.

### Osteomalacia

Steomalacia (reduced bone mineralisation) is more readily induced by hypophosphataemia, hypocalaemia, chronic acidosis and aluminium toxicity.

### Mixed renal osteopathy

This refers to the combination of increased osteoid (due to hyperparathyroidism) and reduced mineralisation (due to osteomalacia).

### Adynamic bone disease

As renal impairment becomes more severe, some patients, particularly under the influence of treatment and especially if diabetic, evolve in a different direction with the development of low-turnover adjuvant bone disease associated with relative suppression of the parathyroid glands. This is characterised by reduced trabecular bone formation and resorption, with thinned trabeculae.

### Other

Other problems include osteoporosis (especially post-transplantation), dialysis-associated amyloid and metastatic calcification. Uracinic patients develop an internal milieu that favours soft-tissue calcification involving peri-articular tissue, skin and the vasculature. Arterial calcification is associated closely with arterial stiffening, left ventricular disease and increased cardiovascular morbidity and mortality.

### Monitoring

Abnormal biochemical parameters of CKD-MBD can be observed relatively early in the progression of CKD, although the rate of change and severity of abnormalities are highly variable among patients and there are no data showing that routine measurement of bone parameters improves outcomes. Current strategies aim to minimise disturbances to skeletal integrity by setting calcium, phosphate, vitamin D and PTH within defined target ranges. It is hoped, but not yet established, that these will also reduce cardiovascular events. Serum calcium, phosphate and PTH concentrations are measured in people with stage 4 or 5 CKD, but not routinely in stages 1-3b. Some recommend measuring alkaline phosphatase and 1,25-dihydroxycholesterol/ vitamin D.

### Treatment

#### Phosphate

Hyperphosphateemia is one of the commonest biochemical abnormalities through the course of CKD. Hyperphosphateaemia contributes to development of low vitamin D levels, hyperparathyroidism, abnormal bone remodelling and vascular calcification. There is a strong association between hyperphosphataemia and mortality in dialysis patients and it is now recognised, from the Framingham Offspring study, that serum phosphate is associated with the risk of cardiovascular disease in the general population. Hyperphosphataemia is generally corrected first of all, and agents include calcium carbonate or calcium acetate (e.g. calcium carbonate or calcium acetate two tablets thrice daily) and lanthanum carbonate. Calcium-based phosphate binders were traditionally first choice, but concerns about increasing the calcium phosphate product have led to the increasing use of lanthanum carbonate and sevelamer. Aluminum toxicity is generally of lesser concern these days and aluminium hydroxide is sometimes used. Serum phosphate in patients with CKD stage 3b-5 should be maintained between 0.9 and 1.5 mmol/l.

### Calcium

Serum calcium, adjusted for albumin concentration, in patients with CKD stage 3-5, should be kept within the normal reference range. Calcium load and episodes of hypercalcaemia are associated with a relative increase in vascular calcification and arterial stiffness, although trials of calcium binders have not demonstrated a significant reduction in mortality, despite a reduction in the amount, and progression, of vascular calcification.

### 1,25-dihydroxycholesterol/vitamin D

Vitamin D levels are seldom measured in CKD, and vitamin D replacement tends to be given after phosphate correction if PTH levels are raised. Vitamin D supplementation may be with chocalcide/l for expedacified in stage 1-3b CKD, or 1-alpha-hydroxycholesterol/ (aflactin) or 1.25-dihydroxycholesterol (calatrion) in stage 4-5 CKD (reason more fully explained in Case 2.29 under treatment of hypocalcemia). However, calcitrial can elevate the serum calcium-phosphate product by increasing gut calcium and phosphate absorption. Calcimimetric agents (e.g. Chnacle), which reduce PTH release by direct action on calcium-sensing receptors in the parathyroid glands, may provide future, less calciacmic and less phosphatase alternatives to calcitral.

### Parathyroid hormone

If vitamin D does not correct PTH levels then parathyroidectomy may need to be considered. Parathion/lectctomy may also be needed for people not on dialysis in whom serum PTH levels are progressively increasing and remain persistently higher than the upper reference limit for the assay, despite correction of modifiable factors, and this may be the case in tertiary hyperparathyroidism.

Osteoporosis treatment may be given if indicated for the prevention and treatment of osteoporosis in stage 1-3b CKD. dialysis access. By convention, the 'out' line is termed the 'A' line and the 'in' line the 'V' line. The needles point in opposite directions to minimise mixing of outgoing and incoming blood and hence the dialysing of freshly dialysed incoming blood.

From the point of vascular access, blood leaves the body and runs to a pump and thence to the dialyser ('artificial kidney'). The pump is designed not to crush blood cells. In the dialyser, blood enters thousands of semi-permeable microtubules, not dissimilar in diameter to a capillary. Between these microtubules and running in the opposite direction is _dialysis fluid,_ against which the blood is dialysed. Dialysis fluid contains concentrations of substances such that an osmotic or ionic gradient is set up from blood to dialysis fluid. Since most patients with CKD are electrostatically in the same vein, the dialyser is a 'in' line the 'V' line. The needles point in opposite directions to minimise mixing of outgoing and incoming blood and hence the dialysing of freshly dialysed incoming blood.

From the point of vascular access, blood leaves the body and runs to a pump and thence to the dialyser ('artificial kidney'). The pump is designed not to crush blood cells. In the dialyser, blood enters thousands of semi-permeable microtubules, not dissimilar in diameter to a capillary. Between these microtubules and running in the opposite direction is _dialysis fluid,_ against which the blood is dialysed. Dialysis fluid contains concentrations of substances such that an osmotic or ionic gradient is set up from blood to dialysis fluid. Since most patients with CKD are electrostatically in the same vein, the dialyser is a 'in' line.

Adjunctive treatments for dialysis patients are as for any patient with CKD.

Complications include access problems, sepsis, haemodynamic instability and b2-microglobulin accumulation. b2-microglobulin is a non-dialysed molecule that, over many years, forms amyloid deposits in musculoskeletal tissues. Current definitions of dialysis adequacy, based on urea clearance, need to be broadened to encompass parameters such as b2-microglobulin clearance, salt and water balance, and phosphate control. Despite major advances in technology, long-term clinical outcomes from haemodialysis are disappointing, even in low-risk patients. More frequent treatments may be necessary to adequately control uraemia and improve survival. Haemodafiliration improves b2-microglobulin compared with haemodialysis, and may improve survival.

#### Peritoneal dialysis

Peritoneal dialysis (PD) is a home-based RRT for patients with end-stage kidney disease (ESKD), offering some degree of autonomy and flexibility of lifestyle. After the placement of a catheter into the peritoneal cavity, the patient is instructed how to perform dialysis exchanges where dialysate is instilled into the peritoneal cavity. These exchanges can either be performed manually (continuous ambulatory or CAPD), or using a machine (automated or APD). During exchange small solutes (such as urea, potassium and creatinine) diffuse from the circulation into the dialysate and are removed when the effluent is drained. On CAPD the standard approach is four exchanges during a 24-hour period using 2 litres of dialysate on each occasion. With APD the dialysis machine performs repeated exchanges overnight and the patient may have additional daytime bags if necessary. Water is removed via the osmotic effect of the glucose in the dialysate, although other osmotic agents can also be used. The most common complication is peritonitis.

#### Renal transplantation

Renal transplantation is the preferred form of RRT. It restores quality of life and increases longevity for individuals with ESRD. Unfortunately, only 30% of patients who develop ESRD are fit enough to be listed for transplantation, because of excessive comorbidities. The advent of potent immunosuppressive has markedly improved 1-year graft survival rates to over 30%. Nevertheless, chronic deterioration of graft function after the first year remains a problem. This is compounded by deterioration in cadaveric donor organ quality, as donors have become progressively older with increasing comorbidity. Kidneys are usually obtained from deceased donors, but the proportion of live donors has substantially increased. Kidney transplant recipients (KTRs) swap one chronic illness for another, with increased risks of vascular disease, neoplasia and infection.

### Why is dialysis use increasing?

There is large variation in dialysis prevalence between countries, even across Europe. Dialysis is increasing as a mode of treatment because of an ageing population (now being considered for people in their eighties and nineties) and a result of strategies designed to slow progression of CKD also tending to treat CVD, leading to improved survival from CVD. The context to this is also an increase in diseases that cause CKD, notably diabetes.

### When should dialysis be started?

Improved modification of progression of CKD has made predicting the time for dialysis more challenging. Clinical pointers such as appetite, nutrition and cognition are often more helpful than numerical parameters.

### When might dialysis be considered inappropriate and 'maximal conservative treatment' appropriate?

The main reason is when patients are dying with, rather than because of, renal failure. Some evidence suggests that, over the age of 75 years with comorbidity, dialysis is of more questionable mortality benefit and that selected patients with high comorbidity live just as long with maximal conservative management as if they had dialysis. However, to withhold RRT from patients with multiple comorbidities without proper appraisal would be ethically questionable. The decision for non-dialysis should be made only after shared decision making between the patient and health-care team. Dialysis is not a true form of RRT; typically it might improve GFR from 5% to 12%, and thus maintains a state of chronic ill health. The aim of RRT is to prolong quantity of life without diminishing quality of remaining years but this is far from always

Figure 2.10: Principles of haemodialysis. AV, arteriovenous. possible. Maximal conservative management is the support of patients with ESKD without resorting to RRT. This support addresses the physical, emotional and spiritual needs until the end of life through a multidisciplinary approach. Medical therapy includes the treatment of underlying renal pathology and other manoeuvres to prolong residual renal function, such as antihypertensive medication and, as renal function declines, treatment of renal anaemia with erythropoietin and optimisation of fluid balance with diuretics.

## Case 2.27 Glomerulonephritis

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

Dear Doctor

Re: Mr Sam Spider, aged 48 years

Thank you for seeing Mr Spider who has had three episodes of painless macroscopic haematuria recently. His creatinine is mildly elevated at 135 mmol/l and urinalysis continues to show a trace of blood and protein. Otherwise he is a well gentleman. I would value your advice on further investigation.

#### Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr Sam Spider is a 48-year-old long-haul airline pilot. In the last 3 months he has had three episodes of macroscopic haematuria. On the first occasion he had a mild, concurrent sore throat. All episodes have been without loin or abdominal pain. His GP told him that his blood pressure was very slightly high and that his kidney blood test was slightly raised. He has no other past medical history. He is divorced, does not smoke, drinks occasional alcohol and enjoys keeping active. He is concerned about the possibility of cancer.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

\(\blacksquare\)Establish the range of symptoms.Establishing these often leads to consideration of differential diagnoses of each symptom then looking for a diagnosis common to each. Here the combination of symptoms points immediately to the unifying diagnosis of a glomerulonephritis (GN), possibly immunoglobulin A (IgA) nephropathy. It is nevertheless imperative to keep an open mind, consider all possibilities and obtain a reasonably thorough but succinct systematic enquiry.

\(\blacksquare\)Consider possible causes of haematuria and the significance of haematuria and proteinuria.These are described in Table 2.60 and Box 2.77.

#### Patient perspective

Tell him that you think a form of kidney inflammation, perhaps provoked by the initial sore throat illness, to be

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} \hline \hline Transient (usually non-visible) & Urinary tract infection \\  & Exercise \\  & haematuria \\ \hline Spurious haematuria & Menstrual \\  & Rhabdomyolysis \\  & Foods, e.g. beetroot, blackberries, rhubarb \\  & Drugs , e.g. doxorubicin, chloroquine, rifampicin \\  & Chronic lead or mercury poisoning \\ \hline “Nephrologicala” & Glomerulonephritis (nephritic syndrome, e.g. \\  & IgA nephropathy) \\  & Thin basement membrane disease \\  & Vasculitis \\  & Alport’s syndrome \\  & Polycystic kidney disease \\  & Haemolytic-uterine syndrome \\  & Renal ischaemia / infarct (renal artery thrombosis/renal vein thrombosis) \\  & Papillarity necrosis \\  & Loin pain haematuria syndrome \\  & Medullary sponge kidney \\ \hline “Urologicala” & Cancer (bladder, kidney, prostate, ureter) \\ causes & Calculus disease or nephrolithiasis \\  & Cysititis or prejonephritis \\  & Prostatitis or urethritis \\  & Schistosoma haematobium infection \\  & Tuberculosis \\  & Radiation cytitis \\  & Cyclophosphamide-induced cyystitis \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.60: Causes of haematuria much more likely than cancer but that your tests will look at all possibilities.

#### Past medical history

He is previously well, typically the case in many types of GN.

#### Drug and allergy history

He appears not to be taking any medications but a history taken from anyone with renal disease should ask specifically about non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and non-prescription drugs.

#### Family history

A family history of renal disease should be sought, especially polycystic kidney disease.

#### Social history

His occupation is important, and he should be advised to consult with his occupational medical team, who will be familiar with Civil Aviation Authority regulations, to establish his fitness for flying.

#### Summary box - Assessment and PLAN

The combination of blood and protein on urinalysis, hypertension and mildly impaired renal function suggests nephritic syndrome, and the synbhangnific (sore throat concomitant with renal effects) history and episodes of macroscopic haematuria strongly suggest immunoglobulin A nephropathy.

The next steps are to arrange examination of urine for casts and dysmorphic red cells, up-to-date renal function and a renal tract ultrasound.

The ultrasound occasionally suggests an alternative diagnosis such as renal cell carcinoma; if symptoms suggest ureteric calculi (pain) then an intravenous pelylegram and referral to a urologist might also be indicated.

If microscopy reveals casts or dysmorphic red cells or the renal ultrasound and history do not suggest an alternative cause then he should be referred to a nephrologist for early consideration of a renal biopsy to confirm the type of glomerulonephritis. 

## Discussion

### To whom should patients with haematuria be referred?

#### Urology referral

A urological cause is more likely in patients with either visible haematuria or symptomatic non-visible haematuria, whatever their age, and those with symptomatic non-visible haematuria aged 40 or more. In all these cases, initial referral to urology is important to exclude malignancy. An exception is young adults with colalooured (rather than red or pink) urine, particularly after a respiratory tract infection, who are more likely to have acute GN.

#### Nephrology referral

Patients under 40 with asymptomatic non-visible haematuria are more likely to have renal disease, particularly IgA nephropathy or thin membrane disease. A urology referral is unnecessary and a nephrology opinion is often not required either, a definitive diagnosis requires renal biopsy, and, unless management would be altered, it is hard to justify. In patients under 40, the presence of other risk factors for severe GN does warrant nephrology assessment, namely proteinuria >=0.5 g/day (albumin-creatinine ratio >=30 mg/mmol or protein-creatinine ratio >=50 mg/mmol), an estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFR) <= 60 ml/min, or hypertension.

#### Surveillance

In those referred initially for urological assessment, if no abnormalities are found, nephrological assessment should be obtained if manifestations of kidney disease are present or develop such as deteriorating renal function or proteinuria >=0.5 g/24 hours.

### What is glomerular disease?

The glomerular basement membrane (GBM) is the vital barrier between blood and glomerular filtrate. Diseases that damage it generally cause haematuria initially, but as architecture becomes disordered proteinuria becomes an increasing feature. Inflammatory GN most commonly does this, but genetic abnormalities of GBM components are another important mechanism.

### How may GN be classified?

GN is confusing because nomenclature attempts to encapsulate aetiology, pathology and clinical presentation. Aetiology descriptions refer to primary causes (unknown but thought to be autoimmune) or secondary causes (associated with autoimmunity, infection, malignancy or metabolic disturbance). Pathological descriptions address glomerular involvement, cell involvement and changes in non-cellular components. Although each clinical presentation can be the result of several types of GN as defined by aetiology or pathology, clinical classification best directs management (Table 2.61).

### How prevalent is GN?

The overall incidence and prevalence of GN is unknown because it often causes mild, asymptomatic, unrecognised illness. Population studies have shown that evidence of kidney damage - proteinuria, haematuria, low GFR or combinations of these - affects around 16% of adults in Australia and the USA. Diabetic and hypertensive nephropathy are two major causes, but GN is likely to be the cause in a substantial proportion. Non-visible haematuria is present in about 2.5% of the general population, but it can be as high as 20%, depending on the study population.

### What are the common types of GN?

IgA nephropathy is the most common GN, followed by focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis and vasculitis. Defined populations are at increased risk of types of GN. Australian Aboriginals are at increased risk of infection-associated GN after streptococcal throat or skin infections, now rare in developed countries. Chronic infections, notably hepatitis B or C viruses or human immunodeficiency virus, increase the risk of several types of immune-mediated GN.

### Which types of GN tend to progress to stage 5 chronic kidney disease (CKD)?

Types that can progress to stage 5 (end-stage) CKD include IgA nephropathy, focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis, rapidly progressive GN with crescents (vasculitis and anti-GBM disease), membranous nephropathy and lupus nephritis. Male sex, hypertension, heavy proteinuria and reduced GFR tend to carry a worse prognosis for most types of GN.

### What treatments are there for GN?

Recent understanding of the types of pathology, locations of pathology, important diseases that can result and their clinical presentations, treatments and prognoses are summarised in Table 2.62.

### What is anti-GBM disease or Goodpaste's disease?

This is an autoimmune pulmonary-renal syndrome, but not a vasculitis, characterised by anti-GBM antibodies that target renal and pulmonary type IV collagen. Histologically, there is linear GBM immunoglobulin G deposition. This causes rapidly progressive glomeruloonephritis and pulmonary haemorrhage, although renal involvement is sometimes the overwhelming problem in isolation. Confusingly, the term Goodpaste's syndrome was used historically for what is now best termed Goodpaste's or anti-GBM disease, while Goodpaste's disease was often reserved for those patients with Alport's syndrome (in 

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{p{142.3pt} p{142.3pt} p{142.3pt}} \hline \hline
**Location of pathology** & **Important diseases** & **Clinical presentation** & **Treatment and prognosis** \\ \hline
**Epithelial cell** & **Minimal change disease** & Selective proteinuria or & Oral prednisolone 0.5–1 mg / kg \\ Epithelial cells, in conjunction with the basement membrane, allows filtration of plasma solutes but not proteins and cells. Diseases are typified by flattening or effacement of the food processes (podocytes) that engage the basement membrane (minimal change disease) and subepithelial / basement membrane immune deposition with spikes and thickening (membranous nephropathy) resulting in disruption of the filtration barrier and proteinuria. & 30\% progress to end stage over 10 years \\ \hline

**Nephrotic syndrome –**_the glomerular filter is damaged and more leaky than usual. This leads to hypolabuniumuta and oedema._ & & \\ Nephrotic-range proteinuria (> 3.5 g/ 24 hours), hypolabuntiameria, oedema and hyperlipidemia, sometimes with predisposition to venous thrombosis and bacterial infection. The selective nature of the proteinuria tends to alter lipoprotein profiles causing loss of natural anticoagulant proteins and immunoglobulin subtypes. & \\ \hline
**Rapidly progressive GN** & & Emergency demanding urgent tissue diagnosis (typically extensive cresenteric GN) and treatment. & \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}

This results in proliferation (increase in cell number) leading to haematuria, proteinuria and renal impairment. & **Class II lupus nephritis** & Nephritic syndrome & Treatment not always indicated as prognosis good \\ Endothelial cell & Infectious-associated GN & Nephritic syndrome, tending to be 7–10 days after infection, unlike IgA, hence & Good prognosis if infection treated \\ Infectious-associated GN and mesangial GN, antibody attachment to the basement membrane as in antidepineral basement membrane disease, or trauma and activation of coagulation as in haemolytic uremic syndrome. Endothelial cell proliferation and necrosis are accompanied by leucocyte accumulation, and rupture of the basement membrane, crescent formation and disruption of glomerular architecture can develop with nephritic syndrome or rapidly progressive GN. & Nephritic syndrome & Rituximab (anti-CD20) may be useful in cryoglobulin-associated disease \\ Mesangial cell & IgA nephropathy & Power prognosis as tends to cause slowly progressive renal failure, predicted by rate of \textless{}4GFR and degree of proteinuria \\ Inhibition of pulmonary architecture can develop with nephritic syndrome or rapidly progressive GN. & **Class III and IV lupus nephritis** & Nephritic syndrome & Biopsy essential as type of lupus nephritis not clear clinically but determines therapy \\ Polsed methylprednisolone then monthly intravenous cyclophosphamide and oral prednisolone for 3–6 months (tension induction) & & \\ Azathioprine or myorphenolate & motefell and reduced steroid (maintenance) & \\ Vasculitis & Rapidly progressive GN & Case 2.28 (Vasculitis) \\ Antiglomerular basement membrane disease & Rapidly progressive GN & Plasmapheresis, pulsed methylprednisolone followed by oral cyclophosphamide and prednisolone \\ Thrombotic microangiopathies & Thrombotic thrombocytopenia & \\ Pumpura/haemolytic uremic syndrome & & \\ syndrome & & \\ syndrome & & \\ syndrome & & \\ syndrome & & \\ syndrome & & \\ secondary to many other infections, including GSE. & & \\ infections, including GSE. & & \\ \end{tabular}
