* _Antitrombin_ binding to and inactivation of thrombin and Xa; antithrombin is one of several 'serpins' that inhibit clotting proteases, including the recently discovered protein Z-dependent protease inhibitor
* _Tissue plasminogen activator_ secretion by endothelial cells.

### List some coagulation disorders that cause a bleeding tendency

These are listed in Table 2.83.

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

## Patient information

Mr Nick Adler is a 39-year-old man with haemophilia A and a usual factor VIII level of around 10% of normal. This tends to result in continuous bleeding after mild to moderate trauma. Today he fell onto his left knee off a bicycle and sustained a laceration to it that is continuing to ooze after 6 hours of pressure. He has therefore been admitted to hospital. His knee joint is swollen but he has had multiple previous haemathroses of both knees and the left is usually a little more deformed and swollen than the right. He does not have pain. He has no history of intracranial bleeding and did not bang his head on this occasion. He is otherwise well but does carry the hepatitis C virus as a result of blood transfusions many years ago. He does not have human immunodeficiency virus infection. He is married, without children. He has come to terms with his condition very well, and sees a liver specialist 6-monthly for monitoring of what is currently inactive hepatitis C. He works as a computer graphics designer. He is well informed about haemophilia and feels he will need factor VIII replacement on this occasion.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Congenital disorders & Haemophilia A and B von Willebrand disease factor XI deficiency Deficiencies of all other coagulation factors described but rare \\ Acquired disorders & Anticoagulant drugs Vitamin K deficiency Liver disease Disseminated intravascular coagulation Acquired factor inhibitors \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.83: Coagulation disorders that cause a bleeding tendency 

## How to approach the case

### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### 2.3.1 Presenting problem/s and symptom exploration

##### 2.3.2 Evaluate symptoms

Haemophilia typically causes joint and muscle bleeding and symptom severity depends significantly on the factor level (Table 2.84).

##### 2.3.3 Explore risks and complications

As well as blood transfusion risks, the significant problems of haemophilia relate to joint damage and comprehensive assessments often involve a haematologist, rheumatologist, orthopaedic surgeon and physiotherapist. Intracranial haemorrhage and visceral bleeds can occur with minimal trauma and are an important source of morbidity and mortality.

#### 2.3.4 Patient perspective

Discover what he knows about his condition. He is probably more likely than most doctors to know if he has sustained a haemarthrosis and what will be needed on this occasion to stem the bleeding.

#### 2.3.5 Past medical history

Explore the risk consequences of previous blood transfusions.

#### 2.3.6 Drug and allergy history

He is likely to know which drugs to avoid in haemophilia.

#### 2.3.7 Family history

The mode of haemophilia A and B inheritance is X-linked, such that all males carrying the affected X chromosome will have haemophilia and all females are carriers (unless, rarely, she is the daughter of a haemophilia male and carrier female). Carrier females may exhibit mild disease if factor production is sufficiently impaired. Around half of sons of carriers have haemophilia and half of daughters are carriers. The severity of disease tends to be similar between generations.

#### 2.3.8 Social history

Establish if he is doing all that he can (within the limits of his wishes) to minimise injuries. Presumably the cycling was not competitive racing?

## 3 Summary Box- Assessment and PLAN

You would arrange for baseline blood tests including an urgent haemoglobin and ensure that blood is available in the event that transfusion were absolutely necessary.

You would liaise with his haematology team and arrange treatment, probably in the form of recombinant factor VIII.

You would consult the orthopaedic team if you felt he had a significant haemarthrosis.

## 4 Discussion

### What are the commonest haemophilias?

Haemophilia A and B are the commonest inherited coagulopathies but are rare (1 : 10 000). Haemophilia A (deficiency of factor VIII : C) is about five times more common than haemophilia B (deficiency of factor IX). Rarer deficiencies have been described for all clotting factors.

#### 4.1.1 How might laboratory investigations be useful in haemophilia?

The activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) is usually prolonged ('intrinsic' pathway) but the prothrombin time (tissue factor or 'extrinsic' pathway) is normal. APTT corrects with the addition of normal plasma in a 50 : 50 mix in inherited haemophilia but not in the less common acquired factor VIII disease where there is an inhibitor (autoantibody) against factor VIII. Specific assays demonstrate reduced factor VIII : C or IX levels.

### How is bleeding in haemophilia managed?

A normal bleeding time is less than 10 minutes. In haemophilia continuous bleeding often occurs, or becomes apparent hours or days after injury. Replacement of the missing factor is the key to therapy, freeze-dried factors from large donor pools historically used but gradually being replaced by recombinant factors. A formula for dosage is:

Factor replacement = % increase in factor required

Factors have only a short half-life (8-12 hours for VIII and 12-24 hours for IX) _in vivo_.

Desompressin (DDAVP/synthetic antidiuretic hormone) combined with an antifibrinolytic agent increases factor VIII levels in patients with mild haemophilia A. Antifibrinolytic agents have been combined with factor concentrates for external bleeding but not usually with factor IX because of the risk of thrombosis or in haematuria because of risks of clot retention.

### What is von Willebrand disease (vWD)?

This is caused by mutations in the von Willebrand factor (vWF) gene on chromosome 12, resulting in quantitative or qualitative abnormalities in vWF. vWF is synthesised in endothelial cells (to an extent bone marrow) and acts as a subendothelial binding protein for platelets via GP1b and as a carrier molecule for factor VIII in the bloodstream to sites of vascular injury. There are three types of vWD:

1. Type 1 (70% of cases, autosomal dominant) is a partial quantitative deficiency of vWF, with a propensity to mucocutaneous bleeding (epistaxis, memorhraglia). Laboratory findings are reduced levels of vWF antigen (protein) and activity (by ristocetin cofactor or RCo assay) with normal or increased ristocetin-induced platelet aggregation (RIPA), which measures sensitivity of ristocetin-induced interaction of the patient's vWF with their own platelets.
2. Type 2 has four subtypes:
* Type 2A mutations result in a failure of vWF multimer assembly (group 1) or increased sensitivity to degradation by the vWF cleavage protease ADAMTS13 (group 2), resulting in loss of circulating high- and medium-molecular-weight multimers, with a corresponding low-vWF antigen level and disproportionately lower RCo level.
* Type 2B is a mutation of the platelet GP1b binding site that results in higher affinity of vWF for GP1b, so that vWF binds spontaneously to platelets in the circulation, causing loss of high-molecular-weight circulating multimers, with a corresponding low-vWF antigen level and disproportionately lower RCo level.
* Type 2M results in decreased affinity for the GP1b receptor. There is a corresponding low-vWF antigen level and disproportionately lower RCo, and markedly reduced RIPA activity. Multimer analysis reveals a full set of multimers.
* Type 2N (autosomal recessive) results in decreased affinity for the factor VIII binding site. Factor VIII levels in homozygotes may be in single figures; vWF antigen and activity levels, RIPA and multimer studies are normal.
3. Type 3 (5% of cases) is caused by two null vWF genes, with no detectable vWF antigen or activity levels, absent RIPA activity and no detectable multimers; factor VIII levels are also low (prolonged APTT) and bleeding is of haemophilia and mucocutaneous type.

Treatment for all types may be with factor VIII concentrate containing vWF, or in types 1, 2A and 2B desmopressin.

## CASE 2.36 DEEP VEIN THROMBOSIS

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

Dear Doctor

Re: Mrs Esther Summers, aged 60 years

Thank you for seeing this lady with a short history of leg swelling and breathlessness. She had a mastectomy for breast cancer 2 years ago and a perioperative DVT and I am concerned that this is further venous thromboembolism.

#### Years sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mrs Esther Summers is a 60-year-old woman with right-sided calf and leg swelling for the last 3 days. She usually has mild swelling of both legs but this does not unduly trouble her and she has been talking a small dose of favorable for a couple of years on an occasional basis. She has also been more breathless in the last week. She does not have chest pain or other cardiorespiratory symptoms. She has a history of breast cancer with a mastectomy and axillary clearance 2 years ago and takes tamoxifen. She is not taking any other medications. She does not smoke. She recently visited her sister in the USA, returning 2 weeks ago. She is a widow, and is currently caring for one of her grandchildren. She is not particularly concerned about the possibility of another deep vein thrombosis (DVT).

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

\(\blacksquare\) **Evaluate symptoms**. Unilateral leg swelling represents a DVT until proven otherwise, unless an alternative such as cellulitis is very evident. Bilateral leg swelling may be the result of heart failure, nephrotic syndrome and other low-protein states, hypothyroidism or a saddle thrombosis (the last usually in the setting of malignancy).

\(\blacksquare\) **Consider risk factors for venous thromboembolism.** Risk factors include: * Acute phase of illness
* Trauma, surgery
* Immobility/inactivity including long-haul flights
* Dehydration
* Pregnancy (oral contraceptive pill/hormone replacement therapy slightly increase risk)
* Obesity
* Malignancy, e.g. pancreas, prostate
* Myeloproliferative disorders and hyperviscosity syndromes
* Nephrotic syndrome
* Heart failure
* Antiphospholipid syndrome and hereditary thrombopphilis
* Hyperhomocysteinaemia.

**Clinical model.** Post-test probability in venous thromboembolism (VTE) relates to pre-test clinical probability and the nature of the test, e.g. V/Q, computed tomographic pulmonary angiogram. Accurate diagnosis of DVT can be a challenge, and various clinical scoring systems are used including the (maximum) nine-point version of the Canadian Wells score for predicting pre-test probability for DVT (Table 2.85). Note that this differs from the Wells score used in diagnosing pulmonary embolus. Clinical intuition has a strong place for experienced physicians.

The Wells DVT score probably performs differently in different clinical settings, but clinical judgement still has final veto.

### Patient perspective

It is probably best to advise her that a DVT is likely but that you will need to confirm this with an ultrasound scan, and, if confirmed, consider whether or not the long-haul flight is enough to explain it, but that you think it would also be sensible to recheck things from the point of the breast cancer to make absolutely certain things are still all clear.

#### Past medical history

The history of breast cancer and DVT is highly relevant.

#### Drug and allergy history

Tamoxifen is not a contributor.

#### Family history

Establish any family history of blood clots.

#### Social history

Establish who will look after her grandchild while she is in hospital.

#### Summary box - Assessment and PLAN

* Her risk factors for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) induce previous DVT and previous malignancy, and a recent long-haul flight.
* The diagnosis should be established by ultrasound scan; it might be suggested by a d-dimer although here a negative D-dimer is not likely to dissuade you from further testing.
* Low-molecular-weight heparin is the initial treatment of choice for DVT and pulmonary embolism, with subsequent warfanisation and if this proves to be recurrence of DVT then indefinite anticoagulation with warfarin is indicated.
* The possibility of recurrent malignancy should be explored.

## Discussion

### Where is a DVT usually located?

Distal veins in the leg are most common, followed by the femoral and popliteal veins and sometimes the iliac vein. Axillary vein thrombosis may occur.

#### What do you know about the role of d-dimer testing in suspected VTE? (Table 2.86)

Plasma d-dimers are specific cross-linked derivatives of fibrin (fibrin degradation products). They are sensitive but not specific for VTE and arise in many other situations including sepsis, trauma, surgery, pregnancy and post-partum. The usefulness also varies with age and comorbidity.

### Does the absolute level of a positive d-dimer correlate with the likelihood or severity of the VTE?

No. There is no evidence that any value above the cut-off correlates with the likelihood of VTE. It may

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c}
**Clinical feature** & **Score** \\ Active cancer (treatment ongoing or within previous 6 months or palliative) & 1 \\ Paralysis, parses or recent plaster immobilisation of legs & 1 \\ Recent bed rest for \textgreater{} 3 days or major surgery within 4 weeks & 1 \\ Localised tenderness along distribution of deep vein system & 1 \\ Entire leg swollen & 1 \\ Calf swelling by \textgreater{} 3 cm compared with asymptomatic leg (measured 10 cm below tibial tuberosity) & 1 \\ Printing oedema (greater in symptomatic leg) & 1 \\ Collateral superficial veins (non-varicose) & 1 \\ Previous documented DVT & 1 \\ Alternative diagnosis as or more likely than that of DVT & −2 \\ Low probability \textless{} 2 & High probability \textgreater{} 2 \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.85: Wells scoring system for predicting pre-test probability for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) correlate with the size of the DVT, age or likelihood of malignancy.

### How is a DVT treated?

After a first DVT or pulmonary embolism without ongoing risk factors, 3 months of anticoagulation is advised.

There is no evidence supporting 6 months of treatment. Daily wearing of compressive stockings for 2 years and immediate mobilisation are important. A second DVT or pulmonary embolism (or a first with ongoing risk factors) suggests a need for lifelong anticoagulation. Anti-coagulation protects against pulmonary embolism but post-thrombotic syndrome is common after DVT.

### What is post-thrombotic syndrome?

This is a chronic debilitating condition seen in about 43% of patients with DVT within 2 years. Patients present with a painful heavy leg and may also have cramps, paraesthesia and pruritus. Catheter-directed thrombolysis for DVT is a new treatment under study that may improve quality of life and reduce the risk of post-thrombotic syndrome.

### What treatment is advised for VTE in pregnancy?

Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) is safe and effective in the treatment and prophylaxis of VTE in pregnancy.

### How does heparin work?

Heparin forms a high-affinity complex with antithrombin and accelerates its action. Antithrombin inactivates both thrombin (IIa) and Xa when it binds to these (Fig. 2.11C, Case 2.34), but the rate of inactivation is relatively low. Heparin strongly catalyses the action of antithrombin by inducing a conformational change in antithrombin within the heparin-antithrombin-thrombin complex leading to irreversible binding of antithrombin to thrombin. Heparin can interfere with diagnostic testing for antithrombin deficiency.

### Unfractionated heparin (UFH)

UFH is a long molecule which complexes with antithrombin, causing it to bind with and accelerate the action of both coagulation factors. The anti-Xa:anti-IIa antithrombin binding ratio of UFH is 1:1.

### Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH)

LMWH complexes with and accelerates mostly that region of antithrombin binding to Xa. In other words, LMWH exerts more effect on the proximal part of the coagulation pathway. This results in higher bioavailability, a longer half-life, and lower propensity to thrombocytopenia. There may also be a lower osteoporosis risk. Monitoring partial thromboplastin time is not required with LMWH. LMWH are administered subcutaneously in the treatment and prophylaxis of DVTs, pulmonary emboli and acute coronary syndromes.

### What is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)?

This is induced by heparin-platelet factor 4 complexes. It is more likely with UFH than LMWH. It usually occurs in the first few days of heparin use but may occur up to around 14 days. Platelet levels often fall to below half their baseline, though extreme falls (to single figures) are rare. Bleeding is uncommon. Patients are at much greater risk of new thrombotic events (venous or arterial) or worsening thrombosis, and thus mortality is high if heparin is continued in the face of HIT. A 'HIT' screen to identify the heparin-platelet factor complexes should be undertaken and an alternative antithrombotic agent used.

### How do warfarin and vitamin K antagonists work?

Warfarin inhibits the procoagulant factors II, VII, IX and X but also the anticoagulant protein C. Since the half-life of protein C is short, a procoagulant state may precede warfarin's anticoagulant effect. This is seldom clinically significant but may cause problems in patients with

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c} Pre-test & Likelihood of VTE & Likelihood of VTE & Implications of p-dimer testing \\ probability of & venography (gold & with negative \\ DVT / VTE & standard) (\%) & d-dimer (\%) & \\ High & 75 & 21 & D-dimer unhelpful because still need to ultrasound scan a 21\% probability \\ Intermediate & 17 & 1.8 & Physicians might accept a 1.8\% probability as sufficiently low to not test further, but the 1.8\% figure relies on the more sensitive β-dimer assays \\ Low & 3 & 0.3 & D-dimer converts low to very low probability, i.e. no further testing needed \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.86: The role of β-dimer testing in suspected venous thromboembolism (VTE) underlying protein C underactivity and is responsible for the phenomenon of warfarin-induced skin necrosis. It is also the reason that heparin should be continued at least until and probably for some days after the international normalised ratio (INR) is therapeutic in those starting warfarin for VTE. Warfarin can interfere with diagnostic testing for protein C and S deficiency.

### How may anticoagulation be reversed?

Heparin can be reversed if absolutely necessary with protomaine, although LMWH reversal is incomplete. Over anticoagulation with warfarin may need no treatment (INR < 10 and no bleeding) or vitamin K; it can be reversed rapidly with a protrombin complex concentrate (Beriplex) in a life-threatening bleeding, the dose being proportional to the INR and administered with vitamin K.

### Do you know of any new anticoagulants?

These include indirect thrombin inhibitors, direct thrombin inhibitors and direct Xa inhibitors.

#### Indirect thrombin inhibitors - fondaparinux

Fondaparinux is a synthetic form of the heparin pentasaccharide molecule responsible for binding and activating antithrombin, resulting in only anti-Xa effect. In contrast to heparins which also inactivate thrombin to varying degrees, fondaparinux has no direct effect on thrombin. Rather, by specifically targeting Xa, fondaparinux exerts its antithrombotic effect by inhibiting thrombin generation. It had similar indications to LMWH, but its use is limited by cost other than in acute coronary syndromes where it has been shown to be superior.

#### Direct thrombin inhibitors (DTIs)

DTIs bind directly to IIa (thrombin) and block its interactions by binding to one of its three domains - the active or catalytic site or two exosites. Exosite 1 is a dock for fibrin. Exosite 2 is the heparin-binding domain. As well as heparin's ability to act in the heparin-antithrombin-thrombin complex, heparin can bind to both exosite 2 and fibrin. Because both thrombin exosites are occupied within this fibrin-heparin-thrombin complex, thrombin's activity is relatively protected from inactivation by the heparin-antithrombin complex. Thus, heparins have less capacity to inhibit fibrin-bound thrombin, which appears detrimental. DTIs act independently of antithrombin, and can inhibit thrombin bound to fibrin or fibrin-degradation products. DTIs, by reducing thrombin-mediated activation of platelets, also have an antiplatelet effect. Bivalent DTIs (e.g. hirudin, bivalirudin, lepirudin) block the active site and exosite 1, and dissociate from thrombin, leaving a small amount of free active thrombin. Univalent DTIs (e.g. dabigataran) block only the active site and inactivate fibrinogen-bound thrombin. DTIs have had an uneven history, early agents discontinued because of concerns about liver toxicity. Dabigataran is an oral agent now used for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation and treatment of VTE, with efficacy similar to warfarin.

#### Direct Xa inhibitors

Rivaroxaban is an oral agent that directly inhibits Xa and is used for VTE prophylaxis post-hip or knee surgery; it is also being considered for use in prevention and management of VTE more widely (approved for treating pulmonary embolus, but with concerns about reversibility limitations), atrial fibrillation and acute coronary syndromes. Other oral Xa inhibitors in development include apixaban and edoxaban. Danaparoid is an inhibitor of Xa and thrombin used post surgery.

### Do you know of any roles of therapeutic activated protein C?

Intravenous infusion of activated protein C is administered in selected patients with severe sepsis and multi-organ failure.

## Case 2.37 Thrombophilic Tendency

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

### Scenario

#### Dear Doctor

#### Re: Mr Edward Ferras, aged 28 years

Thank you for seeing this gentleman who appears to have a family history of venous thromboembolism. He himself had an axillary deep vein thrombosis 2 years ago diagnosed in South East Asia but this was thought to be due to prolonged carriage of a rucksack. He was warfarinised for 6 months. He is a new patient to my surgery and at a routine medical examination he mentioned that a brother has just been diagnosed with a deep vein thrombosis. I wonder if you would consider thrombophilia testing and/or long-term anticoagulation.

#### Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient. 

## Patient information

Mr Edward Ferrars is a healthy 28-year-old who 2 years ago had an axillary vein thrombosis while backpacking in Thailand. This was thought to have been caused by prolonged axillary pressure of the backpack in combination with severe dehydration. He has two siblings, one of whom has just had a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) diagnosed at a slightly older age than he, but without any obvious cause. His mother had a DVT during pregnancy. To his knowledge his grandparents died of strokes and heart attacks at reasonable ages although he thinks a grandmother might have had a DVT when she sustained a hip fracture. There is no family history of recurrent miscarriage. He takes no medications, and does not drink or smoke. He is concerned that there appears to be a family history of blood clots and wonders if there is anything he might do to reduce his risk, especially as he is a professional wind-surfer and takes frequent long-haul flights.

## How to approach the case

### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

Ask about:

* The circumstances and robustness of the diagnosis of the axillary vein thrombosis (diagnosis likely because he was taking warfarin)
* Family history (below)
* His general health now with a full systematic enquiry.

The inherited thrombophilias are summarised in Box 2.88.

#### Patient perspective

His concerns are entirely understandable.

#### Past medical history

The history of an axillary vein thrombosis is highly relevant because this is unusual without a lesion obstructing axillary flow.

#### Drug and allergy history

Ask about prescription and non-prescription drugs, including anabolic steroids.

#### Family history

The family history of at least two first-degree relatives with venous thromboembolism (VTE) is significant, and almost certainly warrants further assessment (see Case 2.36). Particular features to elicit are the age of family members when they developed symptoms and whether or not there were any clear provocative factors.

#### Protein C and S deficiencies and activated protein C resistance

Protein C is activated by thrombomodulin, an endothelial molecule that complexes with thrombin in homeostatic response to coagulation. Activated protein C cleares factors Va and Vila to prevent untamed clotting. Protein C deficiency may be autosomal dominant or recessive, the latter causing problems at birth. Protein S is a cofactor for protein C and protein S deficiency may cause similar disturbance. Factor V Leiden is a mutated form of factor V that is resistant to the effects of protein C. This is the commonest inherited thrombophilia and is also known as activated protein C resistance.

### Antithrombin deficiency

Antithrombin deficiency is autosomal dominant and similarly common.

#### Prothrombin gene variant

G to A transposition at position 20210 in the 3' untranslated region leads to elevated levels of prothrombin.

#### Dysfibrinogenaemia

This is rare and associated with low plasma fibrinogen levels.

#### Hyperhomocysteinaemia

This is discussed below.

#### Social history

His active lifestyle may be protective although long-term anticoagulation could carry particular risks.

#### Summary box - assessment and plan

There is probably an inherited thrombophilia exerting a modestly increased risk of venous thromboembolism.

His active lifestyle may be protective against blood clots except for the long-haul flights, which merit advice about adequate hydration and mobility.

Long-term anticoagulation might be considered pending the results of a thrombophilia screen (below).

### What do you understand by the term thrombophilia?

This refers to an inherited or acquired disorder of coagulation, with the potential for thrombocompolism. The most important risk factors for VTE are a personal or family history, with an approximate 10-fold increased relative risk if there is a first-degree relative with a history of VTE. It has been difficult to quantify risk attributable to inherited thrombophilias, largely because these do not act alone but conspire with other factors. Thrombotic risk in factor V Leiden heterozygotes, for example, is increased dramatically by smoking and the oral contraceptive pill. Estimated increases in relative risk are given in Table 2.87.

### How does hyperhomocysteineaemia lead to a prothrombotic tendency?

There are strong links between hyperhomocysteineaemia and occlusive vascular disease. The reasons are speculative, but homocysteine is atherogenic and causes oxidative damage to low-density lipoprotein. It may modify apolipoproteins, and is hypertensive, interfering with endothelium-derived relaxation factor. It also seems to downregulated anticoagulation, including protein C and thrombomodulin expression, and activate coagulants such as factor V.

### How does hyperhomocysteineaemia arise?

The conversion of 5,10-methylenetetrahydrofolate (5,10-MTHF) to 5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF) through methylene-tetrahydrofolate reductase (MTHRF) is a crucial starting point for normal homocysteine metabolism and DNA synthesis. The 5-MTHF is converted to tetrahydrofolate (THF) by MTHF:homocysteine methyltransferase, generating a methyl group that is transferred to homocysteine, converting homocysteine to methionine. It is the THF that is crucial to DNA synthesis. This series of reactions, known as the remethylation pathway of homocysteine metabolism, is folate dependent.

The mutated C677T MTHF, known as the thermolabile variant, in its heterozygous form, is associated with a moderate (20%) increase in serum homocysteine and a modestly increased risk of arterial and venous thrombosis. The prevalence varies between populations but in many Western countries may be around 15%. Homozygous polymorphism is rare, but promotes markedly elevated homocysteine levels and vascular risk.

Homocysteine is not metabolised exclusively to methionine. In the alternative transulphuration pathway it is metabolised to cysteine by the action of cystathione b-synthase. Mutations in cystathione b-synthase give rise to homocystinuria, in which homocysteine levels and thrombotic risk are very high.

### Why is folate important?

The B vitamins, especially folate, may protect against vascular disease, birth defects (neural tube defects), recurrent pregnancy loss and cancer by lowering homocysteine or by epigenetic mechanisms (several mechanisms pivotal to genomic machinery are sensitive to B vitamin status).

Dietary methionine cannot provide all the methyl groups needed for cellular methylation reactions and so _de novo_ production of methionine is important. Folate stabilises the C677T variant gene by preventing release of its flavin cofactor and, as MTHR is a flavin protein, riboflavin (B2) supplements may also lower homocysteine.

Precursors to DNA are dependent upon a healthy MTHR pathway with adequate folate. If folate is low, uracil misincorporation occurs, leading to breaks in DNA and a predisposition to cancer.

### Is there a role for folic acid in cardiovascular disease prevention?

High homocysteine levels contribute to arterial and venous thrombosis. Some evidence (not without some contradictions) suggests that lowering homocysteine levels by 3 mol/1 from current levels (achievable with folic acid 0.8 mg daily) reduces the risk of ischaemic heart disease by 16%, of stroke by 24% and of VTE by 25%, and that folate should be considered for primary or secondary prophylaxis in people at risk. Testing homocysteine levels may not be necessary to institute therapy because of the high rate of occurrence of hyperhomocysteineaemia in people with vascular disease.

### Is folate supplementation a good idea for the general population?

Folate used in food fortification is not a natural coenzyme and the long-term effects of unmodified synthetic folate are unknown. Furthermore, there is an increasing tendency for clinicians to recommend 5 mg of folate daily, which is more than 10 times that needed to give maximal methylfolate concentrations.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c}
**Approximate** & **Prevalence** & **Prevalence in** & **prevalence in** \\
**increased risk** & **in patients** & **normal** \\
**(relative risk) of VTE** & **with VTE (\%)** & **population (\%)** \\ \hline Protein C deficiency (heterozygous) & 3–15× & 3 & 0.3 \\ Protein S deficiency (heterozygous) & 3–15× & 3 & 2 \\ Antithrombin deficiency (heterozygous) & 25–50× & 1 & 0.2 \\ Heterozygous factor V Leiden mutation & 5× (homozygous 10–80×) & 25–50 & 5 \\ Heterozygous prothrombin G20210A & 1.5–4× (homozygous 5–15×) & 6 & 2 \\ Heterozygous combined factor V Leiden and prothrombin G20210A & 20× & & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.87: Risk of venous thromboembolism ( VTE) attributable to inherited thrombophilia 

### Who should be considered for thrombophilia screening?

A normal thrombophilia screen does not exclude thrombombic defects that may yet be discovered and cannot be assumed to imply 'normal' or 'no increased risk' in the future or in other family members. There is no evidence that identifying an abnormality influences the type of anticoagulant treatment, its intensity or its duration. Therefore, identifying a thrombophilia will not influence a patient's immediate treatment. Whether it may be of value in preventing further thrombosis and in counselling other family members, notably any family members contemplating pregnancy, is an area of uncertainty and possible indications for thrombophilia testing include:
* Patients with apparently spontaneous thrombosis, particularly if young or if pregnant, e.g. stroke at a young age without risk factors
* Patients with thrombosis at an unusual site, e.g. sagittal sinus thrombosis
* Patients with recurrent thrombosis
* Patients with VTE and a history of VTE in a first-degree relative
* Recurrent pregnancy loss
* Pre-pregnancy counselling.

### What do you know about antiphospholipid syndrome (APS)?

APS was first described by Hughes (Hughes syndrome) in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and positive anticardiolipin antibodies, who presented with a clotting syndrome of arteries and veins. Females had a high risk of recurrent miscarriage and late fetal loss. It is under-recognised.

#### What it is

APS is a systemic autoimmune disorder characterised by arterial and venous thrombosis, adverse outcomes in pregnancy (for mother and fetus), and raised titres of antiphospholipid antibodies. It occurs in isolation (primary APS) in more than 50% of patients, but can be associated with other autoimmune diseases. SLE being the most common - 20-35% of patients with SLE develop secondary APS. The population prevalence of primary APS is estimated to be 0.5%. APS occurs mainly in young women of fertile age.

#### Classification

Classification criteria are outlined in Box 2.89.

A rare (< 1% of all APS, but often presents _de novo_) acute variant of APS - catastrophic APS - results in widespread thrombotic microangiopathy and multiple organ failure, typically affecting brain, kidneys, lungs and skin. Thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic haemolysis, schistocytes and activation of the coagulation system occur, and so thrombotic thrombocytopenia purpura, haemolytic uraemic syndrome, and disseminated intravascular coagulation are differential diagnoses. Mortality is high.

#### Antiphospholipid antibodies

Antiphospholipid antibodies are a heterogeneous group of autoantibodies directed at plasma proteins that bind to phospholipids. Some such antibodies have a paradoxical effect on coagulation: _in vivo_ they are associated with thrombosis, but _in vitro_ they increase phospholipid-dependent clotting times, a phenomenon known as 'lupus anticoagulant' activity. The lupus anticoagulant assay is a functional assay based on a combination of several clotting tests. Two other antibodies useful for diagnosing antiphospholipid syndrome are anticardiolipin antibodies and anti-b-glycoprotein 1 antibodies, both of which can be detected by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs). Antiphospholipid antibodies are found in 1-5% of apparently healthy people, although persistent positivity is rare. The correlation between antiphospholipid antibody and clinical symptoms is variable. But lupus anticoagulant seems strongly associated with venous thrombosis and stroke in SLE and in the general population, and with fetal loss at greater than 10 weeks' gestation, and predicts venous thrombosis and fetal loss more strongly than anticardiolipin antibodies. The anticardiolipin ELISA has high sensitivity but low specificity. The relation between anti-b2-glycoprotein 1 antibodies and clinical symptoms and the clinical relevance of isolated anti-b2-glycoprotein I antibodies are uncertain. People with positivity for all three antibodies have a particularly high risk for pregnancy morbidity or thromboembolism.

#### How antiphospholipid antibodies might cause APS

The cause of the production of autoantibodies to phospholipid-bindingproteins such as anti-b2-glycoprotein I is unknown. Antiphospholipid antibodies affect the coagulation cascade and inflammation. In a process mediated by b2-glycoprotein I, antiphospholipid antibodies bind to platelets and endothelial cells, activating endothelial cells and inducing a procoagulant state. Antibody binding also activates complement with recruitment of more inflammatory cells, and tissue factor, leading to endothelial damage and thrombosis. Although cerebral involvement is thought to be thrombotic antiphospholipid antibodies may have direct effects on nerves. Thrombosis in placental vasculature was thought to be the cause of adverse outcomes in pregnancy, but antiphospholipid antibodies may bind directly to trophoblast cells.

#### Presentation

The clinical features of antiphospholipid syndrome are diverse and can affect all organ systems:

* Venous thrombosis is more common than arterial thrombosis, especially DVT and pulmonary embolism.
* Other vessels such as renal, hepatic, subclavian and retinal veins, cerebral sinuses and vena cava are sometimes involved. The most common arterial thrombotic events are stroke and transient ischaemic attack. Recurrent thrombotic events are common.
* cerebral ischaemia, migraine, cognitive dysfunction, seizures, chorea, transverse myelitis, psychosis, depression and Guillain-Barre syndrome.
* The most common cardiac abnormality is non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis, which may be complicated by stroke. There is also an increased risk of myocardial infarction.
* Thrombosis can occur anywhere in the renal vasculature.
* Haematological manifestations include thrombocytopenia and haemolytic anaemia.
* Skin symptoms include livedo reticularis.
* Maternal and fetal effects in pregnancy.

#### Whom to test for antiphospholipid antibodies

Indications for testing for antiphospholipid antibodies are as follows.

#### Thrombosis

* Arterial thrombosis before age 50
* Unprovoked venous thrombosis before the age of 50
* Recurrent thrombosis
* Thrombosis at an unusual site
* Both arterial and venous thrombosis
* Thrombotic microangiopathy of unknown cause

#### Obstetric problems

* One or more unexplained fetal loss after 10 weeks' gestation
* Unexplained severe intrauterine growth restriction
* Early or severe pre-eclampsia
* Three or more spontaneous miscarriages before 10 weeks' gestation

#### Systemic lupus erythematosus

* At baseline

#### Treatment

Antithrombotic agents are the mainstay of treatment.
* For a first episode of unprovoked venous thrombosis or thromboembolism associated with persistent positive antiphospholipid antibodies, long-term anticoagulation with vitamin K antagonists, such as warfarin, is recommended. If a reversible risk factor (e.g. surgery, immobilisation, oestrogen therapy, pregnancy) is reliably eliminated then indefinite anticoagulation may not be justified.
* There are insufficient data on treating arterial cerebral events and either long-term anticoagulation with warfarin or low-dose aspirin is advised.
* There are few data guiding obstetric management. Warfarin crosses the placenta and is teratogenic in the first trimester of pregnancy so low-molecular-weight heparins are the agents of choice for antenatal thromboprophylaxis.
* In patients with SLE and antiphospholipid antibodies, low-dose aspirin may be considered for primary prevention of thrombosis and pregnancy loss.
* Treatments for catastrophic APS include anticoagulation, high-dose corticosteroids and plasma exchange, with or without intravenous immunoglobulins.

## Case 2.38 Myeloma

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation. Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr Frank Boyd is a 66-year-old with a 6-month history of intermittent watery diarrhoea. Episodes last for a few days, although he may be well for weeks in between. He thinks it all began after a blood pressure tablet was started, but this was stopped soon afterwards and the diarrhoea has continued. He has a history of high blood pressure going back some years and takes amlodipine and ramipril, although his GP has recently stopped the ramipril because of worsening kidney tests. Mr Boyd is aware that his GP also obtained an abnormal blood test 12 months ago that can sometimes indicate a blood disease called myeloma. He refused referral to a hospital specialist at the time because he was afraid of a diagnosis of 'cancer'. However, the more recent tests indicate a worsening of that abnormality. He is a retired aircraft mechanic, lives happily with his wife and has smoked heavily since teenage years until recently. He has not been overseas for 30 years and his wife is well.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

Where confronted with a single problem you can delve deeply into its nuances. Here, you do not know which of the paraproteinaemia and hyperviscosity, the renal failure or the diarrhoea are more significant, whether they are equally important, whether they represent separate underlying pathologies or whether there is a unifying diagnosis. This case illustrates the importance of steering close to a standard history-taking framework. Ask particularly about:

* Symptoms of myeloma (Table 2.88)
* The nature of the diarrhoea
* Causes of renal failure (dehydration, symptoms of heart failure, renovascular risk factors, drugs, prostatic symptoms).

#### Patient perspective

Acknowledge and explore his earlier concerns about investigation.

#### Past medical history

His background kidney disease renders his kidneys more susceptible to threat from any new insult.

#### Drug and allergy history

The angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor might have been an important contributor to the renal failure.

#### Family history

Relevance here is unlikely.

#### Social history

This could be relevant, especially with respect to chronic infectious diarrhoea (overseas travel, unwell family members).

## Discussion

### What is myeloma?

Myeloma refers to a malignant clone of B-cell-derived plasma cells in the bone marrow producing high concentrations of one particular (monoclonal) immunoglobulin. In 80% of patients there is a paraprotein in the serum, usually immunoglobulin G (IgG) or IgA class, but the abnormal cells may also produce free light chains small enough for excursion through glomeruli and tubules and appear in the urine as Bence Jones proteins (BJPs). In 20% of patients free light chains are present only without paraprotein in serum but with BJPs in the urine. The serum free light chain (SFLC) immunoassay is a newer test that detects and measures light chains in serum. Rarely, the cell clone does not secrete immunoglobulin or light chains (non-secretory myeloma), but the SFLC test may reveal an M-protein in apparently non-secretory myeloma.

The clinical features of myeloma result from malignant plasma cells infiltrating bones or other organs (e.g. myelosuppression, hepatosplenomegaly), increased paraprotein levels (e.g. hyperviscosity symptoms, paraesthesia from peripheral neuropathy) or light chains in the serum (e.g. provoking light chain nephropathy).

\begin{tabular}{p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt}} \hline
**Problem** & **Prevalence** & **How it arises** & **Symptoms** \\ \hline Bone disease & The most common presenting symptom (60\% of patients) & Melanoma cells migrate from vessels and adhere to marrow stromal cells under the influence of cytokines; this causes stromal cells to produce IL-6, an important growth and survival factor for myeloma cells & Bone pain, especially back Osteoporosis and pathological fractures, especially vertebral Hyperclaemia symptoms \\  & & I-4, IL-6 and TNF-α, increased in the myeloma milieu, mediate osteoclastic resorption through RANK-L whilst osteoproteteerin, a RANK-L inhibitor, is reduced Adhesion also causes myeloma cells to secrete vascular endothelial growth factor leading to new vessel formation, which may encourage tumour growth & \\  & & Alkaline phosphatase levels are normal, reflecting normal or reduced osteoblast activity & \\ \hline Renal impairment & Up to 50\% of patients & Dehydration (unwell, pain, hypercalcaemia) Hypercalcaemia & \\  & & Hyperunicaemia (increased tumour cell turnover) Sepsis & \\  & & Direct proximal tubule damage from BIP Amyloid deposition & \\ Anaemia & Commonly present (70\% of patients) & Bone marrow infiltration Anaemia of chronic disease Cytokines such as IL-1 impair red cell production, iron incorporation and metabolism & \\ Hyperclaemia & Around 15\% of patients & Widespread bone destruction, as cytokine expression by myeloma cells results in osteoblastic bone resorption & Bone pain Renal impairment \\ Infection & Common & Myeloma impairs B-cell immunity with suppression of other normal immunoglobulins (immune paresi\({}^{\circ}\)) and also suppresses T cells A large quantity of immunoglobulin is around but is monoclonal and usually non-functional and suppresses residual normal polyclonal B cells & Lower respiratory tract infections common \\ \hline Amyloidosis & 10\% of myeloma & Primary amyloidosis Kidney usually affected (nephrotic syndrome) Heart failure Peripheral neuropathy & \\ \hline Constitutional symptoms & Common symptoms & Cytokines produced by myeloma cells, e.g. TNF-α Weight loss & Fatigue Weight loss \\ Hyperviscosity symptoms & More common with hyper-lQM disease (Waldenström’s macrophages) & IgA load & Headache Blurred vision Dilated retinal veins and haemorrhages \\ Neurological & Less common & Parasesthesia Spinal cord compression \\ Other & Uncommon & Bleeding hepatomegaly Epidemiology Lymphadenopathy & \\  & & Patients are increasingly diagnosed by way of raised inflammatory markers (plasma viscosity, ESR) & \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.88: Clinical features of myeloma 

### What is the spectrum of plasma cell dyscrasias?

There is a range of plasma cell disorders (Box 2.90).

### How common is myeloma?

The UK incidence is around 60 per million (around 3000 new cases per year), and it constitutes 10% of haematological malignancies and 1% of all malignancies. The incidence increases with age, median age of presentation 60-65, peaking at 65-85. Less than 2% are under 40 years of age. It is more common in males, and Afro-Caribbean people.

### What causes myeloma?

This is unknown, but there are associations with ionising radiation, occupation (railroad workers and agriculture), rheumatoid arthritis and viruses such as human immunodeficiency virus and human herpes virus 8, together with familial clustering.

### How is myeloma diagnosed?

The Duric and Salmon criteria published in 1975 have been widely used. These comprise three major criteria (plasmacytoma on biopsy, bone marrow plasma cells > 30%, monoclonal band - IgG > 35 g/l, IgA > 20 g/l, BJP > 1 g/24 hours) and four minor criteria (bone marrow plasma cells 10-30%, paraprotein present but less than above, lytic bone lesions, low immunoglobulins - immune pareis); diagnosis requires one major criterion plus one minor criterion, two major criteria or three minor criteria. New diagnostic criteria for myeloma by the _International Myeloma Working Group_ endorsed in British haematology Society guidelines are now standard (Table 2.89).

### Blood tests

There should be an index of suspicion. Test results warranting consideration of myeloma include unexplained anaemia with rouleaux on the blood film, raised inflammatory markers, renal impairment and hypercalcaemia. When myeloma is suspected, baseline screening investigations should include:

* A full blood count to detect cytopenias resulting from marrow infiltration
* Inspection of the peripheral blood film to look for rouleaux, which may suggest paraproteinaemia
* Serum biochemistry to detect the presence of renal impairment, hypercalcaemia and raised uric acid, and liver function for the total protein concentration (occasionally an unexpectedly raised total protein concentration may be the first clue).

A markedly raised erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) may also suggest myeloma. The C-reactive protein concentration can be mildly raised in patients with myeloma, but typically is not as useful as the ESR as a screening test.

To confirm the presence of a monoclonal paraprotein, serum (and urine) protein electrophoresis is used and can quantify the amount of detectable paraprotein. Testing of serum or urine for free light chains is also important. Immunohistochemistry is used to confirm the monoclonal nature of the protein and characterise the specific immunoglobulin subclass; other plasma cell disorders (such as plasma-cytomas) and lymphoproliferative disorders (such as chronic lymphocytic leukaemia and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma) can also be associated with an M protein. In 97% of patients with myeloma, the combination of serum and urine electrophoresis and immunofixation can detect a monoclonal paraprotein (M protein).

#### Bone marrow examination

Diagnostic confirmation is by bone marrow examination, traditionally aspiration but increasingly with biopsy and staining for CD138 antibody to detect plasma cells. Plasma cells in myeloma may be morphologically different as well as abundant.

#### Radiology

A skeletal survey to demonstrate lytic lesions including the classic 'pepperpot skull' has low sensitivity and is less frequently performed. Computed tomography improves sensitivity but is not routine. Magnetic resonance imaging is good for detecting soft-tissue masses, spinal cord compression and the pattern of bone marrow involvement, which has as yet postulated prognostic implications. Positron emission tomography scanning may be used in the future.

### What treatments are there for myeloma?

Myeloma is incurable except by bone marrow transplantation although even then cure rates are very small. Median survival is 3-4 years. Treatment of myeloma is currently indicated for symptomatic disease or for myeloma-related organ and tissue impairment. Patients with smouldering myeloma are generally monitored closely as there has been no evidence that early treatment confers a better long-term outcome, although with new therapeutic agents with more favourable toxicity profiles this may change. Patients with myeloma are broadly divided into two treatment groups, depending upon eligiblity for haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (usually autologous).

#### Patients not eligible for autologous stem cell transplantation

Patients are typically treated with combination chemotherapy until resolution of organ damage and absence of detectable paraprotein. Initial treatment achieves a response (decreased symptoms and tumour burden) in around two-thirds of patients, but the paraprotein is seldom completely dispelled and tends to plateau at a low level after a few months of treatment. Melphalan is very effective, usually given in combination with prednisolone for a few days each month, and more recently with thalidomide. Thalidomide achieves a response in up to 30% of patients with refractory disease, and improves survival in combination with melphalan and response rates in combination with prednisolone but with increased thromboembolic risk. An alternative chemotherapy regimen is cyclophosphamide, dexamethasone and thalidomide. Thalidomide analogues have also been developed (e.g. lenalidomide). Interferon-a maintenance treatment can prolong the plateau by a few months but has no significant survival effect and frequent side effects. When paraprotein levels start to rise again, indicating relapse, further treatment, often with a different drug or combination, may achieve a further response although remission is usually sustained for a shorter time and ultimately the disease becomes refractory. Bortezomib, a protease inhibitor, is increasingly used.

#### Patients eligible for autologous stem cell transplantation

Patients often receive repeated cycles of induction chemotherapy to reduce disease bulk (though not excessive as alkylating agents are toxic to stem cells) - usually in combination with thalidomide, lenalidomide, or bortezomib - before stem cell mobilisation and autologous transplantation. Then intensive chemotherapy with autologous stem cell support prolongs remission and survival,

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c}  & **MGUS (common;** & **Asymptomatic or** & **Symptomatic myeloma** \\
**1\% per year** & **‘smouldering’ myeloma** & **(12–32\% per year progress** \\
**myeloma)** & **to symptomatic myeloma** & **(13)** \\ \hline Monoclonal paraprotein (M protein) & \textless{} 30 g / I & \textgreater{} 30 g / I & \textgreater{} 30 g / I \\ in serum (or urine) & & & \\ Bone marrow plasma cell percentage & \textless{} 10\% & \textgreater{} 10\% & \textgreater{} 10\%, commonly \textgreater{} 30\% \\ \hline Organ or tissue involvement & No & No & CRAB features of hyperCalcaemia from bone destruction, Renal impairment, **A**naemia, and lytic \\  & & & Bone lesions on imaging anaemia Renal disease \\  & & & Bone lesions \\  & & & Other \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.89: Diagnostic criteria for myeloma and monoclonal gammopathy of uncertain significance (MGUS) although it does not cure. Allogeneic transplantation, suitable for patients up to 55 years of age with a matched sibling donor, is potentially curative. It carries high transplant mortality but lower relapse rates because of graft versus myeloma effect. Reduced intensity conditioning regimens may extend the use of allogeneic transplantation to older patients.

#### Other treatments

All patients require bisphosphonates, which reduce pathological fractures and skeletal related events, and zoledronic acid may be the bisphosphonate of choice.

Adjuvant treatments in the form of localised radiotherapy may also be used for patients with symptomatic lytic lesions.

Patients with recurrent infections resulting from secondary hypogammaglobulinaemia may benefit from regular intravenous immunoglobulin replacement.

### What prognostic factors are there in myeloma?

A prognosis staging system is outlined in Table 2.90, as are other prognostic factors in symptomatic myeloma.

Untreated myeloma can lead to debilitating complications such as pathological fractures from lytic lesions, irreversible renal failure, or rarely spinal cord compression from extramedullary plasmacytomas. Delays in diagnosis are associated with a higher rate of complications and reduced disease-free survival.

### How would you manage suspected monoclonal gammopathy of uncertain significance (MGUS)?

MCUS is common, and 10% of older people have a small detectable paraprotein. If anaemia, renal impairment, hypercalcaemia and evidence of organ involvement are excluded people can be observed. The combination of anaemia and a raised ESR or plasma viscosity should always prompt a paraprotein search. Referral to a haematologist is indicated if there is a high paraprotein, immune paresis or evidence of organ or tissue effects.

### What is a plasmacytoma?

This is a localised cluster of myeloma cells that may produce any of the usual symptoms or pressure effects such as spinal cord compression (may also occur from vertebral collapse).

### What is amyloidosis?

Amyloidosis refers to the infiltration of various organs with insoluble eosinophilic protein fibrils composed of aminoacid chains arranged in a cross-beta pattern together with other components such as serum amyloid P. Amyloidosis is now classified according to the fibrillary protein.

#### Classification

* In _AL amyloidosis_ the protein is an immunoglobulin light chain or fragment produced by a clone of plasma cells, although in most AL amyloidosis the plasma cell burden is typically low and only about 10% of AL amyloidosis is due to myeloma. Heavy chain amyloidosis is described.
* Reactive amyloidosis (_AA amyloidosis_), formerly termed secondary amyloidosis, is the most common systemic amyloidosis. It is associated with chronic inflammation and occurs in various inflammatory, infective and neoplastic disorders. The precursor amyloidogenic protein is a normal-sequence apo-SAA (serum amyloid A protein), which is an acute-phase reactant.
* Those formerly classified as 'senile' amyloid and many with familial amyloidosis are associated with fibrils of transport protein transthyretin (TTR) and are now termed ATTR.

In all forms of amyloidosis the cell secretes the precursor protein in a soluble form that becomes insoluble at a tissue site, compromising organ function. Many patients with AL, AA or ATTR amyloidosis have multi-system involvement. Some conditions, however, are due to localised amyloid fibril production; these include Alzheimer's disease (b-protein precursor), Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (prion protein), hereditary cerebral haemorrhage with amyloidosis, various forms of ocular amyloidosis , pituitary amyloidosis (prolactam), cutaneous amyloidosis (keratin), medually thyroid cancer (calcitonin) and isolated aortic and atrial amyloidosis.

#### Clinical features

The kidney is the most common site of damage in AL and AA amyloidosis, usually manifested as nephrotic syndrome often with massive proteinuria and oedema resistant to diuretics. Renal failure is inevitable unless amyloid formation can be halted and is rare without marked proteinuria unless the amyloid is predominantly confined to the tubulo-interstitium and blood vessels, sparing glomeruli. Amyloidosis (with the exception of AA amyloidosis) also causes concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, and a restrictive cardiomyopathy results from restriction to dilation and filling that reduces cardiac output despite a

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Stage & Features \\ I & Serum β2-micoglobulin \textless{} 3.5 mg / 1 (and albumin \textgreater{} 35 g / 1) \\ II & Neither I nor III \\ III & Serum β2-micoglobulin \textgreater{} 5.5 mg / 1 \\ Other therapeutic factors \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.90: Prognosis in myeloma relatively preserved ejection fraction. Clinical features of amyloidosis are given in Table 2.91.

#### Diagnosis

Initial investigation is aimed at confirming amyloidosis by tissue biopsy. Biopsy of an affected organ is usually diagnostic, although fat aspiration or rectal biopsy may be considered. Amyloid fibrils classically produce red-green birefringence under polarised light when stained with Congo red.

Once amyloidosis is confirmed, the amyloid type is established. Antibodies have been developed for most known amyloid proteins. In situations where immunohistochemistry is inconclusive, DNA analysis or fibril protein sequencing can be used. In AL amyloidosis it is important to look for evidence of an underlying plasma cell dysracasia such as myeloma.

As well as the type of amyloidosis, it is imperative to establish the extent of organ involvement. Serum amyloid P (SAP) scintigraphy is routinely performed in most individuals referred for assessment but is unable to image amyloid in the moving heart, and echocardiograms are essential. Nerve conduction studies may be required where neuropathy is the predominant symptom, and nerve biopsy is occasionally required to establish the diagnosis.

Poor prognostic factors at presentation are echocardiographic evidence of cardiac involvement (left ventricular hypertrophy in the absence of hypertension is very suggestive, as are right ventricular thickening without pulmonary pathology, and thickening of valves and the interatrial septum), a large amyloid load on SAP scintigraphy, autonomic neuropathy, hyperbilirubinemia and associated myeloma.

#### Treatment

This depends upon the type of amyloidosis. AL amyloidosis may be considered for chemotherapy, including high-dose chemotherapy with peripheral autologous stem cell rescue. AA amyloidosis often responds to reduction of the SAA load with treatment of the underlying inflammatory condition, increasingly with cytokine-modulating therapies that suppress the acute-phase response such as tumour necrosis factor and interleukin-1 inhibitors. Specific organ treatments such as treating heart failure are also needed.

## Other problems in acute and general medicine and dietary care

### Case 2.39 HUMAN

IMMUNODEFICIENCY

VIRUS INFECTION

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

Dear Doctor

Re: Mr Jason Moriarty, aged 35 years

Thank you for admitting this gentleman with human immunodeficiency virus who has become breathless over a few days. He is slightly tachynoeic, his temperature is 38degC, and he looks sweaty and unwell. I am concerned about pneumonia.

Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr Jason Moriarty is a 35-year-old man who is human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)-positive, the infection acquired 12 years ago through homosexual intercourse. He has taken highly active antiretroviral treatment (HAART) for the past few years, although concordance with treatment has recently been problematic because of life stresses and side effects of treatment, and he has missed a number of follow-up appointments with his specialist. His last documented CD4 count was 250/ml, measured around 4 months ago. He now presents with a short respiratory illness comprising a dry cough, breathlessness and fever. He is worried about the possibility of _Pneumocystis ijwoveti_ pneumonia. His past medical history other than HIV is uneventful. In terms of HIV he has had problems with recurrent herpes zoster, recurrent candidiasis and white oral plaques. He has recently separated from his long-term partner and lives alone and describes himself as very depressed. He has given up work as a store designer. He is not infected with the hepatitis C virus.

## How to approach the case

### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

Consider HIV-associated respiratory disease (but also the usual causes of breathlessness). HIV-associated respiratory disease is common, affecting more than 50% of patients. Typical respiratory infections that affect non-HIV-infected people are common in early disease, but may be more severe. These include upper respiratory tract infections, sinusitis, bronchitis and bacterial pneumonia. With CD4 counts under 200/ml, _P. jiwoveti_ is a problem (Box 2.91). Tuberculosis can occur at any stage. Non-infectious respiratory diseases in HIV include Kaposi's sarcoma, lymphoma, lung cancer and non-specific interstitial pneumonitis, the last of these being possible when the CD4 count is normal.

#### Patient perspective

Explore his reasons for stopping treatment, not least his depression.

#### Past medical history

Explore the duration of his diagnosis, how it came to be diagnosed and the likely mode of transmission and explore his list of symptoms and opportunistic infections to date and what treatments he has received for these.

#### Drug and allergy history

He has been taking HAART, but his poor concordance recently will have exposed him to a high risk of vigorous mutation of HIV with a further fall in the CD4 count and opportunistic infections, notably _P. jiwoveti_. Enquire about side effects of HAART that might have contributed to his stopping treatment, rather than assuming it was exclusively depression.

#### Family history

Relevance here is unlikely.

#### Social history

Explore what risk modification behaviour he has used to reduce the risk of transmission.

#### Summary box - assessment and plan

He should have all the usual investigations first - a full screen of haemidenochemistry, a chest X-ray and arterial blood gases. The chest X-ray appearances in _Pneumocystis ijwoveti_ pneumonia (formerly known as PCP) range from mild, bilateral, perihilar interstitial infiltrates to confluent, diffuse alveolar opacification, which can progress over a few days. Other appearances are possible, including upper zone infiltrates, pulmonary nodules or lobar consolidation.

While the differential diagnosis in patients not known to be infected with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) includes atypical pneumonia, a careful history in HIV usually reveals other signs of immune compromise such as oral candidiasis.

Iympanopenia is common. _P. jiwoveti_ pneumonia may be confirmed by Giemsa staining or polymerase chain reaction of induced sputum or bronchalveolar lavage fluid.

Treatment is usually started empirically in patients with a presumptive diagnosis of _P. jiwoveti_ pneumonia and a CD4 count below 200/ml. First line is co-trimoxazole (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole), initially intravenously except in mild disease without hypoxia. Alternatives induce dindamycin with primaquine, dapsone with trimethoprim or aloxouane.

Pentamidine is now seldom used. Corticosteroids are given for moderate to severe disease.

Prophylaxis uses co-trimoxazole first line. Primary prophylaxis is indicated for a CD4 count below 200/ml, HIV-associated constitutional features or a history of any acquired immunodeficiency syndrome defining diagnosis. Secondary prophylaxis is indicated for all patients. Prophylaxis may be discontinued if patients on highly active antiretroviral treatment exhibit sustained increases in CD4 counts greater than 200/ml and suppression of viral load below the limits of detection.

## Discussion

### What do you know about the epidemiology of HIV infection?

HIV affects 40 million adults worldwide. The major route of transmission is heterosexual (> 75%). About 5-10% of new infections are in children, usually acquired during pregnancy, birth or breastfeeding. Injecting drug users represent a smaller proportion. Increasingly, as an effect of longer term survival, HIV is seen as a chronic disease and in older people.

### What types of HIV are there?

HIV-1 has multiple groups and genetic subtypes, and mutation of strains is part of its ability to survive. HIV-1 is derived from the simian immunodeficiency virus of chimpanzees. HIV-2 is quite dissimilar, common in parts of Africa and genetically closer to the sooty mangabey virus and inherently resistant to non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors. Related viruses causing immunodeficiency infect other animals including cats, sheep, goats and horses.

### Describe the structure of the HIV virion

HIV is a retrovirus comprising two RNA copies of the genes _env, gag_ and _pol, env_ encodes glycoprotein gp160, which is cleaved to form the gp120 surface molecule and the gp41 transmembrane molecule; _gag_ encodes the precursor to the capsid molecules p24, p17, p9 and p6, the latter enabling budding of the virus; _pol_ encodes the enzymes reverse transcriptase, protease and integrase. There are also regulatory genes - _tat,_ which regulates transcription, _rev,_ which aids translation, and _nef,_ which recruits lymphocytes to infected macrophages to aid spread.

### How does the virus replicate?

gp120 binds to CD4 surface antigens on T lymphocytes and macrophages. Entry of the HIV virion into host cells also requires the interaction of gp120 with chemokine co-receptor CCR5, found predominantly on macrophages, and with chemokine co-receptor CXCR4, found predominantly on lymphocytes. Binding leads to a conformational change in the envelope molecule such that a hydrophobic sharp tip of the transmembrane protein gp41 is exposed, which can penetrate the host cell (Fig. 2.12-1). Viral capsid is then released into the cytoplasm of the host cell. Reverse transcriptase converts the RNA genome into double-stranded DNA. DNA is then integrated into the host genome under the control of viral integrase. The integrated gene is treated by the host as a normal gene, transcribed and translated into a new virion aided by the regulatory genes, before budding out of the cell. Replication is rapid, with perhaps 100 viruses produced daily and 108 host cells destroyed, especially at lymph node sites.

### What is the pathogenesis of HIV infection and why do some people appear to be 'immune' to HIV?

#### Entry of HIV into the body

The virus transmitted sexually across mucous membranes is almost exclusively macrophage tropic, and on infecting macrophages is transported to draining lymph nodes. People homozygous for CCR5 mutations appear to be protected from acquiring HIV by this route, and heterozygotes progress slowly. Macrophage-tropic HIV can infect central nervous system microangalia and probably accounts for HIV brain disease. Lymphocyte-tropic HIV may be acquired directly from contaminated blood but in most patients appears later in the course of infection as the macrophage-tropic virus evolves to harness the CXCR4 receptor.

#### Early immune responses

Innate immune responses are activated immediately but cannot stop the virus. Acquired, adaptive immune responses involve clonal expansion of B cells to produce antibodies and T-cell responses. Acute infection with HIV leads to rapid replication and within weeks several million copies of the HIV genome are found in plasma, the peak level determined by host immunity. However, this number rapidly declines to a steady state, because initial numbers comprise a wide variety of genetically different viruses because reverse transcriptase allows significant mutations as an in-built survival mechanism. Ultimately, the mutation potential of HIV is an important factor in the failure of host immunity to contain it, and why a cure remains elusive.

#### Antibody responses

Antibodies against _gag_ p24 and p17 tend to appear first within a few weeks, but are non-neutralising and non-sustained. Antibodies against envelope proteins needed for cell entry appear later - against a V3 loop variable region of gp120, transmembrane protein gp41 and binding sites for CD4 and chemokine co-receptors CCR5 and CXCR4. However, these later antibodies tend to be directed against less crucial sites of these envelope proteins and, even when neutralisation occurs, HIV can simply mutate.

#### Cellular responses

CD8 (cytotoxic) lymphocytes interact via their T-cell receptor (TCR) with MHC or human leucocyte antigen (HLA) class I molecules of infected cells presenting HIV peptides (Fig. 2.12-2). CD8 cells lyse infected cells directly and produce cytokines such as interferon-g and tumour necrosis factor-a and chemokines such as macrophage inflammatory proteins 1a and 1b and RANTES (regulated on activation normal T-cell expressed and secreted) that suppress replication or block entry of HIV into CD4 cells. CD4 (T-helper) lymphocytes interact via their TCR with the MHC class II molecules of specialised antigen-presenting cells (APCs) presenting shed protein particles of the HIV virus (Fig. 2.12-3). CD4 cells produce cytokines that perpetuate the T-helper response and collaborate with B cells in the production of specific antibodies (Case 2.46).

The importance of long-term non-progressors (LTNPs) in understanding HIV pathogenesis and research into treatments and vaccines

HIV induces chronic immune hyperactivity that causes the development of acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS). This process occurs readily in some individuals but is absent in others. A small proportion of HIV-infected individuals remain symptom free and with low viral loads and stable CD4 counts for up to 20 years, and are known as LTNPs. Many have strong neutralising antibody responses. Many have mutant chemokine co-receptors. Thus, host immune genetics are vital in Figure 2.12: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). 1. HIV entry into host cell. 2. CD8 response to HIV. 3. CD4 response to HIV. 4. Immune activation by HIV. 5. Alloactivation and CD8 killing of host cells. AIDS, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome; gp, glycoprotein; HLA, human leukocyte antigen; TCR, T-cell receptor. development of AIDS. Chimpanzees and human LTNPs appear to be resistant to the inappropriate immune activation that characterises HIV infection and progression to AIDS. This suggests potential for the immune system to be manipulated in susceptible individuals to induce a LTNP status.

#### HIV - the paradox of evading host immune responses yet provoking immune activation

#### Evading host immunity

HIV employs many mechanisms to evade immune responses. These include: antigenic variability, latent infection of CD4 cells such that these are not visible for CD8 cytotoxicity; masking of target structures, e.g. non-immunogenic proteins coat more crucial envelope proteins and protect these from antibody neutralisation; putting host proteins into the virion itself to protect itself from complement; downregulation of HLA in infected cells; mutation. Minimal HIV mutations are enough to prevent HLA binding of HIV particles for antigen presentation, for HIV particles to evade TCR recognition or for HIV to evade antibody responses; the V3 loop of the envelope protein gp120 exhibits many mutations whilst antibodies to the V3 loop tend to be specific to a particular strain, and whilst the CD4-binding domain of gp120 is highly conserved between strains, antibodies to it tend to exhibit poor neutralising ability.

#### Immune activation

The paradox is that HIV evades host immune responses, and yet provokes the exact opposite, inappropriately activating many parts of the immune system such as APCs, HIV-specific T lymphocytes, other T lymphocytes, B lymphocytes, and natural killer cells.

How HIV triggers immune activation is unclear. HIV destroys a large number of CD4-CCR5 memory T lymphocytes in the intestinal mucosa during acute infection, and it might be at this time that the immune system becomes chronically activated, although against this theory is that measles and other viruses also cause massive T-lymphocyte losses and transient immunodeficiency without provoking an AIDS-like syndrome. Other theories support the longer term influence of the virus and its gene products in promoting chronic immune stimulation. The envelope glycoprotein gp120 is most implicated in the pathogenesis of immune activation since it interacts with CD4 and chemokine co-receptors such as CCR5. Chemokine receptor interactions may influence activation, signals induced as a result of any interactions may trigger activation, and susceptibility to an additional inappropriate stimulus may be the difference between progressors and LTNPs (Fig. 2.12-4).

Autoimmune responses resulting from molecular mimicry may contribute to immune activation and AIDS pathogenesis. Partial sequence mimicry can be sufficient to induce this. There is, for example, structural mimicry between a region of the V3 loop of gp120 and a binding region of certain chemokines, allowing either to potentially interact with chemokine co-receptors; however, any response would probably only selectively activate a few autoreactive T lymphocytes against the select host protein and not provoke widespread immune activation. A more compelling theory for molecular mimicry in HIV's pathogenesis involves HLA repertoires, which almost certainly underlie the differing responses between progressors and LTNPs. Some HLA types afford greater protection against HIV. This may be because some HLA types are more likely to present antigenic peptides of HIV more suitable for long-term viral suppression or it may be because some HLA types are more likely to select a T-cell repertoire more responsive to HIV. However, a region of HIV's envelope protein gp120 known as C5 is homologous with a region of host HLA-DR (class II), such that both appear capable of binding to CD4. CD4 cells may respond to gp120-C5 by a variety of possible mechanisms. HIV or HIV-infected cells may directly stimulate CD4 with gp120-C5 or by engaging the TCRs of a CD4 cells with gp120-C5 complexed with HLA-DR; APCs may indirectly stimulate CD4 (or CD8) cells by engaging the TCR of CD4 cells with gp120-C5 presented by HLA-DR (Fig. 2.12-5).
