It may then be that the TCRs of both infected and uninfected T lymphocytes and other host cells become capable of being activated not just by HIV but by host HLA-DR, the latter known as auto- or allactivation (HLA-DR acting as an allonantigen), similar to the phenomenon seen in graft versus host disease. There may then be CD8 cytotoxic responses against uninfected native HLA-DR cells normally presented as part of the host self-repertoire (Fig. 2.12-5). Antibodies against the gp120-C5 region strongly correlate with slower HIV disease progression, raising the question of a non-neutralising antibody targeting the gp120-C5 domain being used as a vaccine.

#### Immune deficiency

Immune deficiency in HIV and AIDS has traditionally been attributed to direct infection and loss of cells by HIV, notably CD4 cells.

However, immune activation is associated with energy and apoptosis involving uninfected cells, and loss of antigen-specific responses, long before CD4 T lymphocyte counts decline. And so it appears that immune activation is key to AIDS pathogenesis, contributing significantly to cell loss and immune deficiency. The problem is that it is the immune system itself that is attacked by this immune activity, and so HIV might be considered the ultimate autoimmune disease in which HIV 'tricks' the immune system into destroying itself rather than the virus. Consequently, immune activation markers may have greater prognostic significance than viral load or CD4 counts, high viral loads leading to disease only if accompanied by inappropriate immune stimulation, but the latter leading to disease even with low viral loads. Further, the reduced pathogenesis of HIV-2 appears to reflect lower immune activation, also the case with LTNPs who either lack appropriate receptors to engage in inappropriate immune responses or can neutralise triggers to such responses. 

### Why are the symptoms of opportunistic disease in HIV often atypical?

The eloquent workings of the immune system are illustrated best when at fault. HIV, which parasitises the immune system, affirms that it is ravaged defence rather than microbiological attack that causes the clinical manifestations of HIV disease and AIDS. Just as it is the powerful pro-inflammatory response causing fever, pharyngitis and pain in a common cold and not the furtive adenovirus, so impoverished inflammatory potential limits symptoms. Whilst meningococcal meningitis in immunocompetent individuals rapidly results in severe headache with a life-threatening acute-phase response, cryptococcal meningitis in HIV-infected individuals may be insidious with minimal signs despite a cerebrospinal fluid overrun with organisms.

### What is the natural history of HIV infection?

The stages of HIV infection are illustrated in Table 2.92.

### Other than _P. jirovecli_, what other fungal infections can cause lung disease in HIV?

Cryptococcus neoformans, Histoplasma capsulatum and _Cocchioides immitis_ are uncommon in the UK but occur in Africa and the USA. Treatment is with fluconazole or amphotericin.

### What do you know about mycobacterial infection in HIV?

Tuberculosis may occur at any stage in HIV infection. There is a higher risk of tuberculosis latent reactivation, new acquisition, developing primary progressive disease or military, extrapulmonary or disseminated disease. More than two-thirds with tuberculosis present with pulmonary tuberculosis.

In early disease with near normal CD4 counts its behaviour is similar to that in non-HIV-infected adults with post-primary disease, i.e. cough, haemopysis, dyspnoea, fever, night sweats or weight loss. The chest X-ray may show upper zone opacification, the tuberculin test is positive and expectorated sputum or bronchoalveolar fluid analysis is often smear positive.

More advanced HIV can cause atypical tuberculosis presentation, often with weight loss or fever that may be mistaken for HIV 'constitutional' disease. The chest X-ray may resemble primary tuberculosis with hair lymphadenopathy, diffuse shadowing or even pleural effusion, or may be normal, the tuberculin test is usually negative and sputum or bronchoalveolar fluid analysis is often smear negative but culture positive.

Extrapulmonary tuberculosis is common with CD4 counts under 150/ml and often affects the lymph nodes, bone marrow, liver or pericardium.

Treatment for tuberculosis in HIV uses the standard four-drug regimens for 6 months used in non-HIV-infected individuals, but side effects are higher and HAART should be started.

Multidrug resistant tuberculosis is common, often as a result of poor concordance with therapy.

Prophylaxis against tuberculosis in HIV-infected individuals is without consensus.

Tuberculosis is a potent stimulator of cell-mediated immunity and there have long been concerns that it may accelerate HIV replication in infected cells; this is also a concern with some other HIV opportunistic infections such as syphilis.

Other mycobacterial diseases occur in HIV. Disseminated _Mycobacterium avium intracellular_ causes fevers, weight loss and anaemia, and may cause diarrhoea. It is a late manifestation of HIV with CD4 counts less than 50/ml. Treatment is with azalide antibiotics such as azithromycin.

### What HIV-related oral diseases do you know of?

Oral candidiasis and oral herpes simplex are common. Oral hairy leucoplakia refers to asymptomatic white corrugations on the side of the tongue and is the result of Epstein-Barr virus infection; it is highly suggestive of HIV infection, whereas oral candidiasis is common in many hospitalised and unwell patients.

### What HIV-related oesophageal diseases do you know of?

Oesophageal candidiasis is the most common cause of oesophageal symptoms in HIV and an AIDS-defining condition, although it does occur not infrequently outside the setting of HIV. It causes pain on swallowing and dysphagia. It usually responds to single-dose fluonazole and, if that fails, irtaconazole. Relapses are uncommon after HAART.

Oesophageal ulceration may be the result of cytomegalovirus or herpes simplex virus infections. Cytomegalovirus produces discrete ulcers of the lower oesophagus or haemorrhagic esophagitis. Treatments include ganicolovir or froscanter, and relapses are uncommon after HAART. Herpes simplex virus produces discrete, vesicular lesions and is treated with acidovir.

### What HIV-related diarrhoeal diseases do you know of?

Diarrrhoea can occur at any stage of HIV disease and may be caused by various pathogens. It occurs in almost all patients at some point and is a presenting feature of AIDS in a considerable proportion.

Bacterial infections are common and include _Salmonella_ and _Campylobacter_ spp.

Cryptococcusarium parvum_ is a zoonotic intestinal parasite. Cryptosporidiosis causes self-limiting diarrhoea in HIV-negative people and those with CD4 counts > 200/ml. Chronic diarrhoea with weight loss and malabsorption occur in immunodeficiency. It is identified on stool * [19] A. culture. _Microsporidium_ causes less severe but similar symptoms. Treatment is with HAART aiming for a CD4 count over 200/ml.

Cytomegalovirus may affect the entire bowel, including the oesophagus, but most commonly causes inflammatory colitis with bloody diarrhoea in highly immunosuppressed patients.

### What HIV-related liver diseases do you know of?

These include associated viral hepatitis-related diseases, cholestatic liver disease, malignant liver disease and drug-related hepatitis and cholestasis.

### Is kidney disease common with HIV?

Kidney disease is increasingly seen in HIV, although usually not due directly to HIV or HAART, but to the ageing population of patients with HIV and associated vascular disease, or to drugs used to treat HIV complications.

### What is HIV-associated wasting?

Loss of body weight by more than 10% without an obvious cause is an AIDS-defining condition but is now uncommon in the developed world. A pathogen should therefore generally be sought.

### What is the range of HIV-related neurological disease?

HIV may affect any part of the neuro-axis during evolution from seroconversion to late stage HIV, from direct viral infection, opportunistic infections, HIV-related malignancy or AIDS-defining cancers, antiretroviral drug therapy or other less well-elucidated associations such as vascular events. HAART has altered the epidemiology though not the range of diseases encountered. Neurological disease is a presenting feature in 2-3% of cases (15% pre-HAART). Around two-thirds result from opportunistic infection or HIV-related malignancy, and represent a late stage with profound immunodeficiency (CD4 count < 100/ml). The type and severity of disease depend on the CD4 count and timing of presentation, although age, ethnicity, drug therapy and comorbidities are other influences. The range of HIV-related neurological disease is outlined in Box 2.92.

### What is Kaposi's sarcoma?

This is the most common malignancy associated with HIV-1 and is caused by human herpes virus 8. It is probably derived from lymphovascular endothelium. Most patients present with mucocutaneous disease with discrete, non-itchy, non-tender, purple papules that are often arranged symmetrically on the limbs, trunk, face or hard palate. These may turn brown over time. Oedema from lymphatic involvement can precede lesions. Disease may be indolent or rapidly progressive with visceral as well as cutaneous involvement, especially of the lungs or gastro-intestinal tract. HAART and local radiotherapy or intra-lesional chemotherapy are used for cutaneous disease and systemic chemotherapy, with liposomal doxorubicin or paclitaxel, may be needed in systemic disease.

### What other AIDS-related cancers do you know of?

As HAART has reduced infectious disease complications and improved survival in HIV, longer term malignant complications have become more prevalent. High-grade B-cell non-Hodgkin's lymphoma is 100 times more common in HIV-infected individuals. Systemic non-Hodgkin's lymphoma accounts for about 30% of malignancy-related death in HIV-positive individuals. HAART has not led to a dramatic decrease in the incidence. Primary central nervous system lymphoma is rare in the general population but was common in HIV before HAART. It is strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus, occurs with CD4 counts less than 50/ml and may be difficult to distinguish from cerebral taxoplasmois. Seropositivity for _Toxoplasma_ antibodies favours toxoplasmois and detection of Epstein-Barr virus DNA in cerebrospinal fluid suggests primary cerebral lymphoma, but diagnosis may be confirmed by open or stereotactic biopsy. Survival is usually only a few months. Human papillomavirus infection and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia are common in HIV.

### Is HIV associated with cardiovascular disease (CVD)?

Multiple factors contribute to increased CVD risk in HIV-infected patients. HIV itself, probably as a consequence of its pro-inflammatory and procoagulatory effects and unfavourable changes in HDL, and HAART, through its metabolic impacts, including insulin resistance and dyslipidemia, increase the risk of CVD. Protease inhibitors particularly within ART seem to increase the risk of myocardial infarction, but evidence suggests that the relative risk is of less significance than instituting important lifestyle measures. The combination of HAART and statins may increase the risk of complications such as rhabdomyolysis.

### What is HIV lipodystrophy?

Up to 30-50% of HIV-infected patients develop a form of lipodystrophy. This may be loss of subcutaneous fat (lipoatrophy) from the face and limbs, and increased central obesity. It is multifactorial, and may be the result of the virus, genetic factors, hypertriglyceridaemia, hypercholesterolaemia or anti-HIV drugs, protease inhibitors especially. Mitochondrial toxicity is mostly nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor related and insulin resistance is linked to protease inhibitors.

### How would you test for HIV?

An antibody test in an HIV-infected individual is usually positive within 6-12 weeks after exposure. Antibody tests may be to various core or envelope proteins. Plasma HIV-1 load testing assesses disease progression and response to antiretroviral therapy; various assays include reverse transcriptase polymerase chain reaction. Specific genotypes can be detected by nucleic acid sequencing.

### What antiretroviral drugs are available?

This is specialised treatment. The two broad categories of drug are reverse transcriptase inhibitors (RTIs) and protease inhibitors.

#### Reverse transcriptase inhibitors (RTIs)

These prevent conversion of viral RNA to DNA. Only DNA can be incorporated into the host genome.
* _Nucleos(t)ide RTIs_ inhibit reverse transcriptase (RT) and act as DNA chain terminators. They include zidovudine, didanosine, stavudine, lamivudine, abacavir, emtricitabine and tenofovir. Fixed-dose combinations include Combivir (zidovudine and lamivudine), Kivexa (abacavir and lamivudine), Trizivir (zidovudine, abacavir and lamivudine) and Truvada (tenofovir and emtricitabine). Side effects include hypersensitivity reactions, peripheral lipoatrophy, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis and lactic acidosis.
* _Non-nucleos()ide RTIs_ bind to RT at a site distant to the active site but lead to conformational changes in RT which render it inactive. They include etravirine, nevirapine and efavirenz, available in a triple combination (Atripla). Side effects include hypersensitivity reactions.

#### Protease inhibitors

These bind competitively to the substrate site of viral protease. They include atazanavir, darunavir, foasmprenavir, indinavir, kaletra, ritonavir, saquinavir and tipranavir. Side effects include gastrointestinal intolerance, dyslipidaemia, accumulation of visceral fat and insulin resistance.

#### Entry or fusion inhibitors

These interfere with binding, fusion and entry of HIV into host cells. CCR5 inhibitors bind to CCR5 (although as disease advances patients are more likely to harbour viruses that utilise CXCR4 and there is also the potential for CCR5 inhibitors such as Maraviroc to promote switching to CXCR4 utilisation). Entrivtide binds to gp41 and interferes with its potential to interact with the surface of host cells.

#### Integrase inhibitors

These target the integrase enzyme and include raltegravir.

### What antiretroviral drug treatment strategies are there?

HAART or combination antiretroviral therapy (cART)

The optimum time to start antiretroviral therapy is still not known but HAART, using combinations of at least three drugs for advanced HIV-1 disease, has dramatically improved life expectancy, maintaining viral RNA load at undetectable levels. The lower limit for starting HAART is considered a CD4 count 200/ml because below this the chances of developing an AIDS-defining illness increase dramatically. Potentially, a considerable time span between counts of 350 ml/1 and 200 ml/1 exists, around 2-5 years given an average viral load.

#### Effects of HAART / cART on survival

HAART has reduced the incidence of AIDS and AIDS-related mortality by over 40% in the 10-year period 1996 to 2006, with an 80% reduction in mortality in industrialised nations.

#### HAART side effects

There are concerns about cardiovascular mortality as HAART can induce endothelial dysfunction, increase plasma cholesterol and insulin resistance and possibly cause hypertension. HAART, using three antiviral agents, can suppress viraemia and restore normal CD4 counts and when critical 'cut-off levels have been exceeded, prophylaxis against opportunistic infections may be stopped (although cellular specific immunity against HIV cannot be adequately restored). HAART also reduces bone density.

#### Treatment failure and emerging treatments

This is not uncommon and is indicated by an increase in plasma viral load. Failure is more likely with successive regimens. Drug-resistant viruses emerge when susceptibility of the virus to one or more drugs is reduced, and HIV's capacity to mutate vigorously means that stopping HAART even for a short time (often poor concordance) is very bad news. Transmitted drug resistance is now common. The problem is lessened with once-daily combined drug pills. Why is the profile of opportunistic infection and malignancy similar in patients with HIV to that of patients with renal transplants?

Impaired T-cell and B-cell immunity occurs in HIV, because T-helper lymphocytes are affected. The profile of opportunistic organisms reflects those that normally require T-helper lymphocytes for elimination such as _Mycobacterium,_ viruses such as cytomegalovirus and fungi such as _Candida_ and cryptococci. Neutrophil-mediated bacterial elimination is still largely intact. Many post-transplant immunosuppressionpressive anti-rejection therapies inhibit interleukin-2, which is central to T-helper lymphocyte proliferation.

### What is the state of play with developing HIV vaccines?

Current therapies are able to reduce the viral load to extremely low levels and reduce HIV-driven immune activation, but do not eliminate immune activation entirely. Multiple interactions between gp120 and cellular proteins remain targets for neutralisation and current vaccines with neutralising antibodies, either passively transferred or induced (e.g. stimulated by immunisation with gp120), lack good efficacy.

Antibodies against the gp120-C5 region strongly correlate with slower HIV disease progression, raising the question of a non-neutralising antibody targeting the gp120-C5 domain being used as a vaccine. The other main area of vaccine research is in trying to induce a CD8 cell response through delivery of 'safe' HIV antigens, which might hopefully suppress viral replication and slow disease progression.

### Is post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) important?

Overall it appears to confer a significant reduction in rates of seroconversion, but evidence varies. Following percutaneous injury the risk of transmission of HIV may be around 1:300, compared with 1:30 for hepatitis C and 1:3 for hepatitis B. Factors increasing the risk of transmission include deep injury, visible blood on the device, venepuncture instrumentation, terminal HIV-related illness in the source and possibly viral load in the source, although sources with undetectable viral loads are still capable of infecting others through procedures. The principles for selecting which regimen to use for PEP are similar to those for the treatment of chronic HIV infection: potency, pill burden, dosing schedule and tolerability. The current UK Department of Health guidelines recommend a combination of Truvada (a fixed-dose combination of tenofovir disoproxil and emtricitabine) and Kaletra for 4 weeks as soon as possible after exposure (the use of these agents for PEP is outside their licence.) A health-care worker may not be limited from work and procedures given the low risk of disease acquisition and transfer, but cannot be considered HIV negative until a negative HIV test 3 months after completion of PEP. Pre-exposure prophylaxis in preventing sexual transmission has been considered.

### How might immunodeficiency arise other than through HIV infection?

Immunodeficiency disorders are outlined in Table 2.93. HIV infection is the most common secondary immunodeficiency. Common variable immunodeficiency is the most common primary antibody deficiency, IgA deficiency the most common type. Complement, neutrophil and primary T-lymphocyte disorders are rare. Non-infectious features such as splenomegaly, granulomatous or autoimmunity increase the likelihood of identifying an immunodeficiency. FISHing (full blood count, immunoglobulins, serum complement, HIV test) will detect the most common disorders, and should be undertaken either where recurrent or unusual infections warrant further enquiry or where non-infectious features raise suspicion, analogous to coeliac disease.

## Case 2.40 Fails

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

##### **Dear Doctor**

Re: Mrs Marianne Wood, aged 74 years

Thank you for seeing this lady who is having an increasing number of falls. She has sustained numerous injuries as a result.

Last year she sustained a fractured ankle and humerus. She has no other medical history of note. She denies any loss of consciousness but I wonder if she merits investigation to search for possible causes.

##### **Yours** sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examines will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mrs Marianne Wood is a 74-year-old ex-primary school teacher with a lifelong history of falls. She describes herself as a 'clumsy child who always had a tendency to fall', but in adulthood these became more frequent. She now falls approximately monthly. Her husband has adjusted to keeping close to her when walking, ready to break any fall. Last year she fractured her humerus and ankle, required intensive physiotherapy and was unable to write for 2 months or drive for 6 months. She also has a history of a vertebral compression fracture following a fall down stairs. She does not think she loses consciousness when she falls, but does not seem to be able to coordinate her legs, which seem to be clumsy. She has no apparent difficulty with sensation in the feet and legs, is able to sense pain, hot and cold and joint position, and has no apparent difficulty with muscle strength. She has no other medical history of note, and takes no medications. She is extremely worried about having to give up walking, which she loves.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

##### **Presenting problem(s)** and symptom exploration**

##### **Explore the frequency and time course of falls.**

Ask the following:

* When did the falls start?
* or is it the first fall? (How many in the last 12 months?)
* Are they becoming more frequent?
* Is there a circadian pattern?

##### **Explore the characteristics of falls.**

Patients often report one of light-headedness, a sense of the room spinning, or feeling unsteady on their feet (Table 2.94).

Falls may occur:

* Cases 2.41 and 2.42)
* _Without loss of consciousness_, when they may be associated with neuromusculoskeletal instability or disequilibrium (Box 2.93) or they may be purely accidental ('the cat or the carpet'). Vertigo seldom

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Question & Interpretation \\ \hline Do you feel light-headed? & Pre-syncope or syncope \\  & (Case 2.41) \\ Is it a sense of the room spinning & Vertigo (Case 5.49) \\ (rotational or ‘to and fro’)? & \\ Do you feel unsteady on your feet (or & Disequilibrium \\ wobbly on your legs)? & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.94: Characteristics of the causes of falls 

### Box 2.93 Postural equilibrium and disequilibrium

The body has a small base compared with its height and must constantly take automatic corrective actions to overcome destabilising gravitational forces and maintain postural stability. These actions can produce body sway and limits of sway are known as limits of stability. When the centre of mass is displaced beyond the limits a fall occurs unless there is rapid corrective action, e.g. moving the foot.

### Neuromuscular mechanism for postural equilibrium

Balance is a complex sensorimotor process integrated centrally in the brainstem, cerebral cortex, cerebellum and extrayramidal system, with stabilising sensory information from the vestibular system, vision and somatosensory afferents (e.g. joint position receptors in the lower limbs and spine and touch and presure receptors in the sole). Somatosensory afferents are most important in postural stability when standing on a hard surface, and vision is important on unstable ground. The vestibular system largely detects head movement and is less perceptive for body sway. Much of this sensory information converges centrally and is used to produce corrective responses at the joints to keep the centre of mass within the limits of body sway, i.e. stabilisation of gaze during head movements and control of posture and equilibrium during movement. Effector pathways include the vestibulo-ocular reflex (stabilises retinal images with head movement) and vestibulospinal pathways to neck and trunk muscles and muscles in the lower limbs.

### Postural disequilibrium

Postural unsteadiness or disequilibrium occurs when one of the sensory inputs is perturbed. Normally minor slips or trips provoke rapid motor responses to restore postural equilibrium. Some adaptation can occur to compensate for sensory perturbation, e.g. impaired proprioception compensated for by vision. When sensory compensation is inadequate, motor responses are inadequately evoked and this results in a fall.

### Ageing and disequilibrium

Visual acuity, vestibulo-ocular reflexes, vestibular systems, proprioception and cutaneous sensation tend to decline with age or with disuse. These, combined with reduced cerebellar and cerebral processing and increased muscle stiffness, can all contribute to increased body sway over 60 years of age and an increased risk of falls.

### Disease and disequilibrium

Many diseases contribute to disequilibrium, e.g. diabetic neuropathy, osteoporosis, stroke, dementia, postural hypotension, cataracts, glaucoma, macular degeneration.

### Drugs and disequilibrium

Polypharmacy may contribute to disequilibrium.

provokes loss of consciousness. Most falls are non-syncopal, and in older people are the result of poor mobility and frailty and organ (notably brain, e.g. dementia) vulnerability, or acute illness (intrinsic factors), often combined with extrinsic (environmental) factors. The role of an ageing brain with chronic brain disease and ultimately brain failure is underappreciated as a contributor and cause of falls.

Assessment in patients who are admitted must include cognitive assessment, an electrocardiogram and lying and standing blood pressure if relevant (although most falls on standing are caused by weakness):

Ask about warning symptoms, whether there is recollection of falling or hitting the floor and whether protective responses to injury are used. Patients may lose consciousness so transiently as to be unaware of having done so. Associated symptoms, such as chest pain, palpitations or breathlessness, should be elicited.

Ask about direction of fall (in diffuse cerebrovascular disease, for example, gait tends to be upright or even tottering backwards a little, resulting in backward falls).

Ask how long she is usually on the floor, and how she gets back up.

Ask about vision and hearing.

Ask about sensory symptoms.

Ask about muscle weakness.

Ask about joint symptoms (pain or swelling).

Ask if there is vertigo.

### Explore environmental factors.

Environmental factors can contribute to falls risk, especially in the setting of disequilibrium. Indoor hazards include bad lighting, steep stairs, lack of edge of step demarcations, lack of rails, slippery floors, loose rugs, pets, grandchildren's toys and cords of telephones and appliances. Outdoor hazards include uneven pavements, streets or paths, lack of safety equipment, snow and ice, traffic and public transportation. A description of footwear should always be elicited.

**Assess falls risk.** The falls risk assessment tool predicts a high risk of falls if three or more of the five risks are present (Box 2.94).

People with syncope, recurrent falls in the past year, unexplained high-risk features or disturbance of gait or balance should be offered a specialist multifactorial falls assessment with a view to individualised multifactorial intervention. Assessment in outpatient settings should take account of the number and description of falls, environmental and functional assessment, balance and gait assessment, confidence, cognition, medical disorders, past history, medications and osteoporosis risk. The Morse falls risk assessment tool is suited to and validated for inpatients and applies six categories for consideration in arriving at an overall score - history of falling, secondary diagnoses, ambulatory aids, intravenous therapy, gait and mental state. Patients are classified as low, medium or high risk, with management guidance incrementally devised for categories.

Falls are often multifactorial. Chronic disorders causing problems with posture, balance and gait (e.g. peripheral neuropathy, joint disease, stroke, Parkinson's disease) or with cognition may conspire with acute illness (e.g. pneumonia, urinary tract infection, acute confusion or dehydration), drug side effects (e.g. hypotension, sedation or electrolyte disturbances) and environmental hazards. In other words, if a cause of falls is found, assessment should continue.

#### Patient perspective

Explore any ideas, concerns and expectations about her falls, especially her concerns about stopping work.

#### Past medical history

Establish the list of injuries sustained.

#### Drug and allergy history

Important medications contributing to falls risk include:
* All that provoke drowsiness, e.g. antipsychotics, antibiotics, antidepressants, muscle relaxants, opiates, antihistamines, centrally acting antihypertensives
* All that provoke hypotension, e.g. antihypertensives, dopaminergic drugs, anticholinergics, antiemetics
* Others, e.g. antiylexemics, antiarrhythmics.

#### Family history

There is occasionally a familial neurodegenerative disorder.

#### Social history

Enquire about type of accommodation (stairs etc.), environmental hazards, hazardous tasks (e.g. climbing, reaching), functional abilities (mobility, transfer, activities of daily living, e.g. shopping, driving), support services and work.

## Discussion

### Why is falls assessment important?

Falls assessment is important for numerous reasons, as follows.

#### Frequency

Falls are common, although most are not reported. One in three people aged over 65 will have at least one fall in a year, and one in two over 80, more if in a care home. Older people should be asked routinely if they have fallen in the last year and about the frequency, characteristics and context of the fall.

#### Possibility of underlying disorder

Falls may also be the manifestation of an underlying cardiac or neurological disorder.

#### Consequences

Approximately 3-5% of all falls may result in a fracture, and 10-15% of falls in people over 65 result in serious injury.

#### Value of intervention

Dedicated falls assessment and intervention services screen for medically treatable causes, assess using a clear and comprehensive pathway and reduce falls risk even where cause is multifactorial by organised exercise programmes and occupational environmental adjustments. Falls assessment and management are all about identifying causes, managing falls risk and reducing fracture risk with appropriate osteoporosis management.

### Why are older people at increased risk

Older people are at increased risk of the features in Box 2.95, and when assessing older people with any condition it is useful to consider the special considerations highlighted by the mnemonic 'RAMPS'.

#### Box 2.95 Considerations in assessing older people

* Reduced body reserve
* Atypical presentation
* Multiple medical problems
* Polpharmacy
* Social adversity 

### Which patients should undergo immediate brain imaging after a fall?

Those with a head injury and:

* Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score < 13 when first assessed in the emergency department
* GCS < 15 when assessed in the emergency department 2 hours after the injury
* Suspected open or depressed skull fracture
* Sign of fracture at the skull base (haemotympanum, 'panda' eyes, cerebrospinal fluid leakage from ears or nose, Battle's sign)
* Post-trumatinic seizure
* Focal neurological deficit
* > 1 episode of vomiting
* Amnesia of events > 30 minutes before impact.

Those who sustained loss of consciousness or amnesia and:

* Age >= 65 years
* Coagulopathy (history of bleeding, clotting disorder, current anticoagulation)
* Dangerous mechanism of injury, e.g. pedestrian or cyclist struck by a motor vehicle, occupant ejected from a motor vehicle, fall from > 1 m or 5 stairs.

### How might a patient's overall falls risk, gait, balance and strength be assessed?

#### Overall falls risk

A full examination should include cardiovascular examination, neuromuscular-culoskeletal examination and cognitive assessment, together with assessment of gait and balance in conjunction with a physiotherapist.

#### Gait

Perhaps the easiest initial assessment of falls risk is the 'get up and go' test. The patient is asked to get up from a chair without using their arms, walk a few paces (6 metres standard), turn around and return to the chair. If this is normal falls risk is low, but if unsteady a full multidisciplinary falls risk assessment is warranted. Impaired 'sit to stand' is probably the most sensitive indicator of falls risk. Walking can also be combined with distraction techniques that involve multitasking such as walking and talking.

#### Specific balance tests

Methods of assessing balance include: functional reach (how far a patient can reach out without falling, a measure of the range of stability correlating with falls which improves with rehabilitation); body sway (affected mostly by leg strength, peripheral sensation, visual acuity and reaction times and tested by swayometers); step tests, the number of steps to complete a 180deg turn increasing with unsteadiness; FICSIT 4 testing (to determine if a patient can stand with feet together side by side, semi-tandem, tandem or stand on one foot). Balance assessment tools include the Berg balance scale and Tinetti balance assessment tool.

#### Strength

Strength may be assessed by dominant knee extension or hand dynamometry, standing from a chair without hands, gait speed or climbing stairs. Muscle force (strength x speed of contraction) reduces by 20% after a week spent in bed!

### List some interventions that reduce falls

Falls research has revealed that the only effective interventions are multidisciplinary, but can reduce up to 30% of falls, and these may include:

* Diagnosing and managing specific causes and risk factors (e.g. cardiac pacing)
* Strength and balance training (physiotherapist)
* Home hazard assessment and intervention (occupational therapist)
* Vision assessment and correction of visual impairment as part of multifactorial intervention
* Footwear assessment
* Medication review and modification/withdrawal. For inpatients who have sustained a fall, using the Morse management approach the following are performed:
* For those at low risk: documentation of circumstances, communication with the multidisciplinary team and referral as needed, bed kept at lowest setting, appropriate seating provided, items within reach, information leaflets, footwear assessment, environmental safety hazard check, assistance with toilet access if needed
* For those at medium risk: as for low risk plus frequent re-orientation if confused, assessment of risks and benefits of bedrails and types of bedrails, education of patient and care
* For those at high risk: as for medium risk plus full medical review, patient located in easily observed area, consideration of 1 : 1 nursing, multidisciplinary team involvement, consideration of sensor alarm, consideration of falls team.

### List some interventions that cannot be recommended to reduce falls because of insufficient evidence

These include:

* Low-intensity exercise combined with incontinence programmes in older people in extended-care settings
* Group exercise (not a reason to discourage, but little evidence for non-individualised programmes in older people)
* Cognitive/behavioural interventions
* Referral for correction of visual impairment as a single intervention
* Hip protectors in fracture prevention. 

### Does vitamin D reduce falls risk in older people?

Vitamin D deficiency is common in older people and there is evidence that it impairs muscle strength and possibly neuromuscular function. However, evidence that replacement reduces falls risk is not yet strong. Vitamin D and calcium correction does reduce fracture risk in older people in care.

Which patients might be safely discharged following admission with a fall, and which patients admitted?

Discharge may be considered for patients who are continent, ambulant, rational, able to dress unaided and with safe social circumstances. Patients with injury, an abnormal electrocardiogram or a family history of sudden death should be admitted.

### What is rehabilitation?

This is the process of actively restoring function. It is focused and purposeful and aims to improve quality, engagement and participation in life and decrease morbidity and mortality. It is not the same as convalescence, which implies spontaneous recovery. Conditions suitable for rehabilitation are wide ranging and include stroke, fractured neck of femur, heart failure and chronic obstructive disease. Rehabilitation generally starts on acute medical wards and may continue in community hospitals, care settings, outpatient units or at home with the community rehabilitation team.

### List some challenges to rehabilitation

* Diagnostic uncertainty (the most important and, surprisingly, common, e.g. confusion in the setting of lung cancer when, for example, brain metastases should first be excluded)
* Constipation
* Incontinence
* Pain
* Sedation
* Pressure sores
* Parietal lobe neglect / inattention
* Cognitive impairment
* Communication problems (including hearing impairment)
* Depression

### Which patients should not ideally be referred for rehabilitation?

* Those without a diagnosis
* Those needing complex further investigation
* Those who are medically unstable
* Those awaiting placement

### Which patients should be referred for rehabilitation?

#### Is posture an important element of rehabilitation?

Muscle imbalance (due to neuromusculoskeletal problems, cognitive impairment or reduced activity) leads to disordered posture and sustained segmental (pelvis, hips, knees, feet, ankles, spine) malalignment. Physiotherapists assess posture by examining daily routines, the factors limiting stable posture and inhibiting neutral posture and the effects of posture (e.g. on function, comfort, contracture risk and pressure sore risk). Physiotherapy aims to limit deformity and discomfort and optimise function by, for example, moving all muscles and joints regularly and determining individually optimal bed and seating types.

### Case 2.41 Syncope

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

Dear Doctor

Re: Mr Walter Connelly, aged 74 years

Thank you for seeing Mr Connelly, who collapsed last week. He got up from his chair in the public library and promptly lost consciousness. He recovered rapidly but tells me this has happened before. He has a past medical history of hypertension and takes bendfulmenthaizide and losartan. Examination is unremarkable except for an ejection systolic murmur. I would appreciate advice on further management.

#### Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.



## Patient information

Mr Walter Connelly is a usually well 74-year-old man who last week lost consciousness in the public library. He got up from his chair and with very little warning fell to the floor. He thinks his chest hit the back of the chair as he fell, as he had some discomfort on recovery. He recovered within minutes. He has had two or three similar episodes in the last year. He takes bendroflumethiazide and losartan for hypertension. He has no other past medical history of note, has never smoked, seldom drinks alcohol and lives with his wife in a house with stairs. He drives a car and is keen to continue doing so.

## How to approach the case

### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

Patients are often referred to outpatient clinics or admitted to hospital with 'dizziness', 'giddiness', 'faintness', 'blackouts' or 'collapse', or attacks of 'altered awareness'. It usually means one of the problems in Box 2.96.

The initial evaluation in syncope should answer three key questions:

1. Is it a syncopal episode or not?
2. What is the aetiological diagnosis?
3. Are there data suggestive of a high risk of cardiovascular events or death?

#### Is it a syncopal episode or not?

\(\blacksquare\) **Syncope.** Syncope is defined in Box 2.97. Establish the following:

* Was loss of consciousness (LOC) complete?
* Was LOC transient with rapid onset and short duration?
* Did the patient recover spontaneously, completely and without sequelae?
* Did the patient lose postural tone?

If the answers to these questions are positive, the episode was likely to be syncope.

\(\blacksquare\) **Syncope in the context of transient loss of consciousness (T-LOC).** The term T-LOC (transient, with rapid onset, short duration and spontaneous recovery) encompasses all disorders characterised by self-limited LOC and is divided into traumatic and non-traumatic forms. Concussion usually causes LOC but the presence of a trauma is usually clear. Non-traumatic T-LOC is divided into:

* Syncope
* Seizures
* Psychogenic pseudosyncope
* Rare miscellaneous causes (e.g. cataplexy) or those whose presentation resembles other forms of T-LOC only in rare circumstances (e.g. excessive daytime sleepiness).

\(\blacksquare\) **Disorders resembling syncope.** Two groups of disorders may superficially resemble syncope:

* Those where consciousness is truly lost, but the mechanism is not global cerebral hypoperfusion, e.g. epilepsy (tone maintained), metabolic disorders (including hypoxia and hypoglycemia, which will not reverse without treatment), intoxication and trauma induced. Hyperventilation, which is associated with hypocapnia and cerebral vasoconstriction, may result in light-headedness and reduced mental function along with characteristic digital and period paraesthesia, but it is only rarely associated with LOC in the absence of an accompanying vasovagal response.
* Those where consciousness is not or only apparently lost, e.g. falls without LOC, drop attacks and psychogenic pseudosyncope. The term drop attack is generally unhelpful, but refers to a sudden fall with the legs giving way without LOC. Contrary to the belief of many doctors, transient ischaemic attacks (TIAs) and cerebrovascular disorders almost never cause syncope (Box 2.98). If a patient has not lost consciousness, but has experienced altered awareness (best corroborated by a witness), then seizure activity seems more likely.

\(\blacksquare\) **The two main causes of LOC - syncope or seizure ('faint or fit').** Most LOC is due to syncope and not seizures or other causes. Syncope is usually triggered; epilepsy rarely is. The value of a description of episodes (patient or witness) cannot be overstated. Valuable discriminators are given in Table 2.95 but the key is to build up a picture rather than relying on one fact to make the diagnosis. Further, the differentiation can be more difficult in older people.

### 2. What is the aetiological diagnosis?

\(\blacksquare\) **Causes of syncope**. Figure 2.13 and Table 2.96 provide a classification based on the pathophysiological causes of syncope. Syncope is common. There is a very high prevalence of first faints in patients between 10 and 30 years. Reflex syncope is the most frequent cause of syncope in any setting. Syncope secondary to cardiovascular

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c}  & **Syncope** & **Seizure** \\ \hline \hline ‘Hedofune’ & Minutes (often 1–10 minutes), e.g. nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, feeling hot or cold or sweaty, light-headedness, blurring of vision & A brief (often 10–30 seconds, and sometimes only 3–4 seconds) aura is typical of a complex partial seizure, e.g. funny smell \\ \hline Duration of blood & Usually \textless{} 1 minute (typically 20–30 seconds), and almost invariably \textless{} 5 minutes & Several minutes (usually 2–5 minutes) \\ \hline \hline ‘Hedofune’ & Rapid recovery (wakes at scene) & More prolonged recovery (wakes in ambulance at around 20 minutes) \\ \hline \hline ‘Hedofune’ & Often ‘deathly’ pale & Red or blue (cyanosed) in tonic–clonic seizure \\ \hline \hline ‘Hedofune’ & Shaking or jerking can occur with syncope in the form of minor & Rhythmic tonic–clonic movements are common for 1–2 minutes and their onset coincides with the loss of consciousness \\ \hline (curliness according) & & \\ \hline \hline ‘Hedofune’ & & \\ \hline \hline ‘H 4.2 The \(\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{ \mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{ \mathbf{\mathbf{ }}}}}}}}{{\mathbf{ \mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{}}}}}}}} {{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{}}}}}}}} {{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{}}}}}}}}} {{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{\mathbf{}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}} \ \ \ \ \ disease is the second most common cause. In younger people orthostatic hypotension is a rare cause of syncope. The most common causes of syncope in older people are orthostatic hypotension, reflex syncope (especially carotid sinus syncope (CSS)), and cardiac arrhythmias.

A sudden cessation of cerebral blood flow for only 6-8 seconds is sufficient to cause complete LOC. Experience from tilt testing shows that a decrease in systolic blood pressure (BP) to 60 mmHg or lower is associated with syncope. Systemic BP is determined by cardiac output and total peripheral vascular resistance, and a fall in either can cause syncope, but a combination of both mechanisms is often present in any type of syncope.

### Features helpful in differentiating the causes of syncope

Initial evaluation is able to define the cause of syncope in 23-50% of patients. Table 2.97 lists clinical features that may suggest a diagnosis.

The '5 Ps' approach to features in the history may also help differentiate causes of syncope (Table 2.98).

## 3 Are there data suggestive of a high risk of cardiovascular events or death?

Arrhythmias and structural heart disease are major risk factors for sudden cardiac death and overall mortality in patients with syncope (Box 2.99).

### Patient perspective

Explore his ideas, concerns and expectations, especially about driving.

### Past medical history

Establish the list of injuries sustained. A past medical history of anterior myocardial infarction should alert you to possible ventricular arrhythmias.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Groups of causes & Types & Causes \\ Reflex syncope (sometimes known as neutrally mediated syncope or neurocardiogenic syncope or neurocardiovascular syncope) & Vasovagal syncope (‘Common faint’) & Emotional stress (fear, pain, instrumentation, blood phobia) \\ Reflex syncope refers to a heterogeneous group of conditions in which cardiovascular reflexes become intermittently inappropriate in response to a trigger, resulting in vasodilatation and/or bradycardia. Reflex syncope may be classified by cause or by response (vasodepressor if hypotension, cardioinhibitory if bradycardia or systole, mixed if both). & Crustal syncope (cough, sneeze) \\  & 

### Drug and allergy history

Diuretics and angiotensin modulators can provoke syncope by postural hypotension, and can derange electrolytes.

#### Family history

Relevance here is unlikely, but ask about sudden cardiac death.

#### Social history

Ask about driving.

## Discussion

### What is the mechanism of reflex syncope?

The basic problem is afferent pathways feeding to brainstem cardioinhibitory and vasodepressor centres leading to syncope by hypotension (vasodepressor) and variable bradycardia or asystole (cardioinhibitory).

Stimulation of the brainstem medullary vasodepressor region may follow activation of cardiac C-fibre mechanoreceptors (hypovolaemia causing reduced preload, Valsalva manoeuvre), cardiopulmonary baroreceptors (coughing, sneezing, carotid massage), cranial nerves (glossopharyngeal neuralgia), the cerebral cortex (anxiety, panic, pain) or gastrointestinal or genitourinary mechanoreceptors (defectation, micturition). In hypovolaemia and emotional states such as pain and anxiety the starting point may be increased sympathetic tone, leading to hypercontractility (increased ionotropy and chronotropy) of the volume-depleted left ventricle and subsequent stimulation of C-fibres.

In all cases the common endpoint of receptor activation is compensatory parasympathetic (vagal) enhancement with bradycardia or transient asystole and decreased sympathetic tone with peripheral vasodilatation, reduced venous return, reduced cardiac filling, decreased cardiac output and cerebral hypoperfusion.

## Summary Box - Assessment and PLAN

* Symptoms are consistent with reflex syncope, although orthostatic hypotension may be a cause, and is a much more common cause than aortic stenosis, which has to be very severe to cause syncope and then often associated with other symptoms. However, the gradient across the valve in aortic stenosis is exacerbated by angiotensin receptor blockers.
* Echocardiography is important on account of the murmur, as is ambulatory electrocardiogram monitoring. The limitations of diagnostic tests such as electrocardiogram monitoring are that capturing a spontaneous event is unusual and a causal relationship between a diagnostic abnormality and syncope is often presumptive. The cause of syncope remains unknown in a substantial proportion of patients.
* Further cardiac investigation might be helpful because it is unclear whether his chest pain was secondary to trauma.
* A tilt table test will be helpful.
* If postural recordings confirm orthostatic hypotension, his antihypertensive should be weaned.
* The evidence base for investigations in syncope is limited, without gold standards (the diagnostic yield of tests being measured indirectly by the reduction of recurrence of events after administration of therapy as indicated by the test). Further, the evidence basis for many treatments is also limited.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Groups of causes & Types & Causes \\ Cardiac syncope (cardiovascular) & Arrhythmias & Bradycardia \\ Arrhythmias are the most common cardiac causes. & Sinus node dysfunction (including bradycardia / tachycardia syndrome) \\ damaged and syncope is due to long pauses caused by sinus arrest or sinonidial block and a failure of an escape mechanism. These pauses are most frequently encountered when an atrial tachyarrhythmia suddenly stops (brady–tachy syndrome). The more severe forms of acquired atherosclerotic bladder (Mobitz II block, ’high grade’, and complete atrioventricular block) are most closely related to syncope, as cardiac rhythm may be dependent on often-unreliable escape pacemaker sites with delay before these pacemakers begin to ‘fire’ and relatively slow rates. Bradycardia also prolongs repolarisation and predisposes to polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, especially torsade de pointes. Several drugs can cause brady- and tachyarrhythmias. Structural cardiovascular diseases cause syncope when circulatory demands outweigh the impaired ability of the heart to increase its output. & Carflac \\ Stroke syncope & Stroke syncope & Value disease \\ Stroke syncope & Acute myocardial infarction / ischaemia \\ Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy & Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy \\ Cardiac masses (atrial myxoma, tumours) & Cardiac masses (atrial myxoma, tumours) \\ Periardial disease / transpomade & Periardial disease / transpomade \\ Congenital anomalies of coronary arteries \\ Prosthetic valve dysfunction & Prosthetic valve dysfunction \\ Other \\ Other \\ Pulmonary embolus & Pulmonary embolus \\ Acute aortic dissection \\ Pulmonary hypertension \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.96: Causes of syncope—cont’d 

## What tests might be useful in syncope?

Initial evaluation of T-LOC requires careful history, examination (including orthostatic BP measurements) and electrocardiogram. Based on these findings, additional assessment may include:

* Electrocardiographic monitoring
* Echocardiography when there is known or suspected heart disease
* Orthostatic challenge (lying-to-standing orthostatic test and/or head-up tilt testing) when syncope is

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} \hline \hline Relig. & Absence of heart disease \\ Syncope & Long history of recurrent syncope \\ After sudden unexpected unpleasant sight, sound, smell, pain or sensation \\ Prolonged standing or crowded, hot places \\ Nausea and vomiting \\ During a meal or post-prandial \\ After exertion (though beware cardiac syncope) \\ With head rotation or pressure on carotid sinus (shaving, tight collars, tumours, head turning) \\ Orthostatic. & After standing up \\ Hyperlocation & Temporal relationship with start or change of dose of vasodepressor drugs leading to hypotension \\ Prolonged standing, especially in crowded, hot places \\ Presence of autonomic neuropathy or Parkinsonism \\ Standing after exertion \\ Various clinical syndromes of orthostatic intolerance are recognised: \\
**Classical OH** & This is defined as a decrease in systolic BP >= 20 mmHg and in diastolic BP >= 10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing. It occurs commonly in old age, with autonomic failure, drugs or hypovolaemia. \\
**Initial OH** & This is characterised by a BP decrease immediately on standing of > 40 mmHg, BP then spontaneously and rapidly returns to normal, so the period of hypotension and symptoms is short (< 30 seconds). It may occur in old or young people, and in CSS. \\
**Delayed (progressive) OH** & This is not uncommon in older people, probably due to age-related impairment of compensatory reflexes and stiffer hearts, and characterised by a slow progressive decrease in systolic BP with upright posture. The absence of a bradycardia reflex (wagh) differentiates delayed OH from reflex syncope although delayed OH may be followed by reflex bradycardia. \\
**Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome** & This mostly occurs in young women, presenting with severe orthostatic intolerance, but not syncope, with very marked heart rate increases (> 30 bpm or to > 120 bpm) and instability of BP. \\
**Reflex OH** & This may be triggered by standing, and occurs mostly in healthy young people. \\ Orthostatic & Cardiac syncope such as prolonged sinus pauses may be so brief that patients genuinely do not believe they lost consciousness, although they may not have an adequate explanation for what happened. \\ Syncope & **Electrocardiogram abnormalities that may suggest arrhythmic syncope** \\ Bifasciicular block (left or right bundle branch block with left anterior or posterior fascicular block) \\ Other intraventricular conduction abnormalities (QRS duration ≥ 0.12 seconds) \\ Mobile 1 second-degree antiventricular block \\ Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia (< 50 bpm), sinoatrial block or sinus pause ≥ 3 seconds in the absence of negatively chronotropic drugs \\ Non-sustained ventricular tachycardia \\ Pre-excitation QRS complexes \\ Long or short QT intervals \\ Early repalisation \\ Right bundle branch block pattern with ST elevation in leads VI–3 (Rrugada syndrome) \\ Negative T waves in right precordial leads, epsilon waves and ventricular late potentials suggesting arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia \\
**Q waves as legacy of myocardial infarction** \\
**Electrocardiogram abnormalities diagnostic of arrhythmic syncope** \\
**Sinus bradycardia with a heart rate < 40 bpm** \\ Repetitive sinusardial block or sinus pauses > 3 seconds in the absence of negatively chronotropic medications \\ Mobile II second- or third-degree block \\ Alternating right and left bundle branch block \\ Rapid ventricular or supraventricular tachycardia \\ Pacemaker malfunction \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.97: Features helpful in diagnosing the cause of syncope related to the standing position or there is suspicion of a reflex syncope
* Carotid sinus massage (CSM) in patients > 40 years.

#### Electrocardiographic monitoring (non-invasive and invasive)

As a general rule, electrocardiogram monitoring is indicated only when there is a high pre-test probability of identifying an arrhythmia associated with syncope. However, in patients > 40 years with recurrent syncope, without significant structural heart disease and with a normal electrocardiogram, an arrhythmia, usually assig-tole, is present during syncope in up to 50%. In-hospital monitoring (in bed or telemetry) is warranted only when the patient is at high risk. The gold standard for diagnosis of syncope is when correlation between symptoms and a documented arrhythmia is recorded. The presence of some asymptomatic significant arrhythmias, defined by

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Features & Suggested diagnosis \\ \hline Precipitants & Warm or crowded environments & Vasovagal syncope \\ \cline{2-3} Specific situation & Situational syncope \\ \cline{2-3} Dehydration & Orthostatic hypotension \\ \cline{2-3} Head movements, tight collars, shaving & Carotid sinus syncope \\ \cline{2-3} During exercise, no obvious precipitant & Cardiac syncope \\ \hline Prodrome & Light-headedness, dizziness, disturbances (including blurring, enhanced brightness, tunnel vision), hearing disturbances (including impaired hearing, crackles, tinnitus) & Orthostatic hypotension \\ \cline{2-3} Nausea, sweating, abdominal pain & Vasovagal syncope \\ \cline{2-3} None & Nose & Vasovagal syncope in older people \\ \cline{2-3} Crest pain, breathlessness, no prodtome & Cardiac syncope \\ \hline Palpitations & Cardiac syncope \\ \hline Position & Prolonged standing & Vasovagal syncope \\ \cline{2-3} Orthostatic hypotension & Sudden change in posture & Orthostatic hypotension \\ \cline{2-3} Supine & Cardiac syncope \\ \hline Post-event phenomena & Nausea, vomiting, fatigue & Vasovagal syncope \\ \cline{2-3} Immediate complete recovery & Any, common in arrhythmia \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.98: The 5 Ps’ approach to diagnosing the cause of syncope prolonged asystole (>=3 seconds), rapid supraventricular tachycardias (>=160 beats / min for >32 beats) or ventricular tachycardias, is considered by some diagnostic. The absence of documentation of an arrhythmia during a syncopal episode allows exclusion of an arrhythmia as a reason for syncope.

Electrocardiographic monitoring is traditionally undertaken with conventional 24-48-hour, or even 7-day, Holter recorders (for episodes occurring at least weekly). However, since in most patients symptoms do not recur during the monitoring period, the true yield of Holter in syncope may be very low. Event recorders are external devices applied by the patient when symptoms occur. Whilst useful in investigating palpitations they have no role in the evaluation of syncope. External loop recorders (for episodes occurring every 1-2 weeks) are connected to the patient through cutaneous patch electrodes and are suitable when there is warning of symptoms, with an increased diagnostic yield compared with Holter monitoring they have a loop memory that continuously records and deletes electrocardiograms, but when activated by the patient, typically after a symptom has occurred, 5-15 minutes of pre-activation electrocardiographic monitoring is stored and can be retrieved for analysis. Implantable loop recorders (for episodes occurring less frequently) are implanted subcutaneously under local anaesthesia and have a battery life of up to 36 months.

#### Echocardiography and other imaging

Echocardiography plays an important role in risk stratification on the basis of left ventricular ejection fraction. In the presence of structural heart disease, other tests to evaluate a cardiac cause of syncope should be performed. Echocardiography identifies the cause of syncope in very few patients when no more tests are needed (e.g. aortic stenosis, atrial myxoma, tamponade). Transosephagal echocardiography, computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging may be performed in selected cases (e.g. aortic dissection and haematoma, pulmonary embolism, cardiac masses, pericardial and myocardial diseases, congenital anomalies of coronary arteries).

#### Other cardiac investigations

Electrophysiological studies may be indicated in syncope with ischaemic heart disease (unless there is an established indication for an implantable cardiac defibrillator), bundle branch block where other tests have not revealed another cause, palpitations where other tests have not revealed a cause, selected patients with Brugada syndrome, arrhythmiagenic right ventricular dysplasia or hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and people in high-risk occupations where knowledge of cause could have a major impact.

Exercise-induced syncope is infrequent. Exercise testing should be performed in patients who have experienced episodes of syncope during or shortly after exertion.

Cardiac catheterisation techniques (e.g. coronary angiogram) should be carried out in suspected myocardial ischaemia or infarction or ischaemia-driven arrhythmias.

The role of endogenous adenosine release in triggering some forms of syncope due to otherwise unexplained paroxysmal atrioventricular block (the so-called 'adenosine-sensitive syncope'), diagnosed by adenosine triphosphate testing remains unclear.

#### Orthostatic challenge tests

Changing from supine to upright position produces a displacement of blood from the thorax to the lower limbs that leads to a decrease in venous return and cardiac output. In the absence of compensatory mechanisms, a fall in BP may lead to syncope.

#### Active standing

This is used to diagnose different types of orthostatic intolerance.

The sphygmomanometer is adequate, used to measure BP supine and during active standing for 3 minutes, as initial evaluation when orthostatic hypotension is suspected. Continuous beat-to-beat non-invasive pressure monitoring may be helpful in cases of doubt. The test is diagnostic when there is a symptomatic fall in systolic BP from a baseline value >=20 mmHg or diastolic BP >=10 mmHg or a decrease in systolic BP to <90 mmHg. It may be considered diagnostic with these values even in asymptomatic people.

Orthostatic hypotension may be worse after getting out of bed and not present in clinic. It may be delayed and only occur after a period of standing, diagnosed by a head-up tilt table test. Whilst the terms postural hypotension and orthostatic hypotension are often used interchangeably, postural hypotension strictly refers to a drop immediately after standing whilst orthostatic hypotension may be delayed, often so in older people. Ambulatory BP recordings may be helpful in identifying abnormal diurnal patterns. These recordings may also help identify supine or nocturnal hypertension in treated patients.

#### Tilt table testing

This is an orthostatic challenge test used when reflex syncope is suspected. It should ideally be performed with beat-to-beat monitoring of BP - the basic problem with vasovagal syncope is a drop in BP, chased very quickly, within seconds, by a dramatic drop in heart rate. The clinical situation corresponding to tilt testing is reflex syncope triggered by prolonged standing. However, this test can also be positive in patients with other forms of reflex syncope and in patients with sick sinus syndrome.

Prolonged standing is estimated to lead to an effective 700 ml drop in plasma volume in healthy adults. This may cause a slight compensatory rise in heart rate, but ultimately this paradoxically slows in patients with vasovagal attacks.

A modern tilting protocol comprises 20 minutes of passive 70deg head-up tilt followed by glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) spray. Exaggerated responses with progressive hypotension without bradycardia are an effect of GTN and more common in older people. Patients are supine for 5 minutes before the tilt and fast for 2 hours before the tilt. Tilts are best performed in the morning, in a quiet, darkened room to reduce sympathetic drive.

Uright tilting in people without reflex syncope reduces venous return, but subsequent baroreceptor stimulation and increased a- and b-adrenergic tone averts syncope. In reflex syncope there is decreased venous return, but sympathetic tone increases with the stimulation of C-fibres leading to stimulation of the medullary vasodepressor region, sudden reduction in sympathetic tone (vasodilatation) and concurrent increase in vagal tone (bradycardia) causing syncope. In other words, a BP drop may be chased rapidly by an inappropriate heart rate drop. The prodromal phase occurs at a systolic BP of around 90 mmHg then systolic BP rapidly falls and precedes a decrease in heart rate. Approximately 50% of patients with a positive baseline test become negative on repeat testing.

The endpoint of tilt testing is the induction of either reflex hypotension/bradycardia or delayed orthostatic hypotension associated with syncope or pre-syncope. When a reflex is induced, according to the predominance of vasodepressor or cardionhibitory components, the responses have been classified as cardioinhibitory, vasodepressor or mixed (Table 2.99).

Tilt testing is not usually needed in patients whose reflex syncope is diagnosed by clinical history. A negative test does not exclude reflex syncope. It has no value in assessing treatment efficacy. It is safe. It is usually combined with carotid sinus testing.

Carotid sinus massage (CSM) for carotid sinus hypersensitivity (CSH) and carotid sinus syncope (CSS)

Pressure at the site where the common carotid artery bifurcates - the anterior margin of the sternocleidomastoid muscle at the level of the cricoid cartilage (angle of the jaw where the carotid pulse is maximally felt) - produces a slowing in heart rate and fall in BP - the _cardiinhibitory_ component due to parasympathetic overactivity causing sinus bradycardia or atrioventricular block and the _vasodepressor_ component due to sympathetic underactivity causing loss of vascular tone and hypotension independent of heart rate changes. In some individuals there is an exaggerated reflex response to carotid sinus stimulation. A ventricular pause lasting > 3 seconds and/or a fall in systolic BP of > 50 mmHg defines CSH. When associated with spontaneous syncope, CSH defines CSS.

Diagnosis of CSS requires the reproduction of spontaneous symptoms during 10 seconds of sequential right and left CSM performed supine and erect, under continuous monitoring of heart rate and periodic measurement of BP, permitting better evaluation of the vasodepressor component. In up to 30% of patients, an abnormal reflex is present only in the upright position, especially in those with vasodepressor responses. Highly sensitive patients may be symptomatic with minimal provocation (even listening for fruits!). CSH is a common finding in older male individuals, but patients with CSS is more unusual. CSS is exceptional in patients < 40 years old.

Neurological complications from CSM are reported with an incidence of 0.17-0.45%, and CSM should be avoided in patients with a carotid bruit (unless imaging has excluded significant stenosis), previous stroke, myocardial infarction in the past 3 months or a history of sustained ventricular tachycardia. It should always be performed with recorded monitoring to avoid unnecessary repeat testing if positive to prove bradycardia or asystole and persuade the cardiology team to consider a pacemaker! A carotid ultrasound is safe practice prior to CSM where there is concern about possible carotid atheroma.

### How may reflex syncope be treated?

#### Education

In general, initial treatment comprises education about awareness and avoidance of triggers (e.g. hot crowded environments, volume depletion), early recognition of prodromal symptoms, and performing manoeuvres to

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c} \hline
**Type** & **Response** & **Definition** & **Treatment** \\ \hline Type 1 & Mixed cardioinhibitory and vasodepressor & Heart rate falls but not to \textless{} 40 bpm & Cardiac pacing (less effective in mixed with important vasodepressor component) \\ \hline Type 2A & Cardioinhibitory without asystole & Heart rate \textless{} 40 bpm for \textgreater{} 10 seconds & Cardiac pacing (considered for CSH more than vasovalgal syncope) \\ \hline Type 2B & Cardioinhibitory with asystole & Aystole \textgreater{} 3 seconds with symptoms & Cardiac pacing (considered for CSH more than vasovalgal syncope) \\ \hline Type 3 & Vasodepressor & Fall in SBP of \textgreater{} 50 mmHg with symptoms HR does not fall by more than 10\% of its peak at the time of syncope & More difficult to treat and evidence for treatments lacking Compression hosiery Salt tablets \\  & & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.99: Tilt table testing responses and treatments – reflex syncope abort the episode (e.g. supine posture, physical counter-pressure manoeuvres). If possible, triggers should be addressed directly, such as cough suppression in cough syncope. Careful avoidance of agents that lower BP (including alpha blockers, diuretics and alcohol) is important.

#### Physical counterpressure manoeuvres (PCMs)

PCMs are emerging as a new front-line treatment of reflex syncope. Isometric PCMs of the legs (leg crossing and gluteal clenching). or of the arms (hand grip and arm tension), are able to induce a significant BP increase during the phase of impending reflex syncope that allows the patient to avoid or delay losing consciousness in most cases.

#### Tilt training

In highly motivated young patients with recurrent vasovagl symptoms triggered by orthostatic stress, the prescription of progressively prolonged periods of enforced upright posture (so-called 'tilt training') may reduce syncope recurrence.

#### Drugs

Many drugs have been tested in the treatment of reflex syncope, most with disappointing results. Fluorocristions has been widely used in adults with reflex syncope, but there is no trial evidence to support this. Beta blockers have been presumed to lessen the degree of ventricular mechanoreceptor activation owing to their negative inotropic effect in reflex syncope, but the theory is not supported by evidence and they are not now recommended. Paroxetine was shown to be effective in one placebo-controlled trial but not confirmed in other studies.

#### Pacing

Pacing plays a small role in reflex syncope, unless severe spontaneous bradycardia is detected during prolonged monitoring, but appears to be beneficial in CSS.

### How may orthostatic hypotension and orthostatic intolerance be treated?

#### Treating cause

Through assessment to identify possible causes should consider volume status, diseases that can affect the autonomic nervous system, and drugs. Dehydrated patients should be considered to have hypoadrenalism until a short synachen test proves otherwise, particularly if there is hypoglycaemia or hyponatraemia, although these are late disturbances. Eurolaemic patients may be on anti-hypertensives or other hypotension-inducing agents, or may have long-standing diabetes or primary autonomic failure as in Parkinson's disease, multi-system atrophy, or primary autonomic failure. Orthostatic hypotension with tachycardia suggests hypovolaemia, but failure to produce heart rate compensation suggests autonomic dysfunction.

#### Education

Education as outlined for reflex syncope can improve orthostatic symptoms markedly, even though the rise in BP is relatively small (10-15 mmHg); raising the standing BP just enough to be within the autoregulatory zone can make a substantial functional difference. Expansion of extracellular volume is important. In the absence of hypertension, patients should be instructed on sufficient salt and water intake, targeting 2-3 litres of fluid per day and 10 g of NaCl. Rapid cool water ingestion is reported to be effective in combating orthostatic intolerance and postprandial hypotension.

#### Physical counterpressure manoeuvres (PCMs)

PCMs such as leg crossing and squatting should be encouraged in patients with warning symptoms able to perform them.

#### Sleeping position and gravitational measures

Sleeping with the head of the bed elevated prevents nocturnal polyuria, maintains more favourable distribution of body fluids, and ameliorates nocturnal hypertension. Gravitational venous pooling in older patients can be treated with abdominal binders or compression stockings.

#### Drugs

In contrast to reflex syncope, the a-agonist middorine is a useful addition to the first-line treatment in some patients with chronic autonomic failure. There is no doubt that middorine increases BP in both supine and upright posture and that it ameliorates the symptoms of orthostatic hypotension. Middorine (2.5-20 mg, three times daily) has been shown to be effective in three randomised placebo-controlled trials. Caution is needed with prostatism. Fluorocristone (0.1-0.3 mg once daily - higher doses usually limited by hypokalaemia) is a mineralocorticoid that stimulates renal sodium retention and expands fluid volume. The evidence in favour of fundrocrtisone is from small studies. Less frequently used treatments, alone or in combination, include desmopressin in patients with nocturnal polyuria, osteotriide in post-prandial hypotension, erythropoietin in anaemia, pyridostigmine, use of walking-sticks, frequent small meals, and judicious exercise of leg and abdominal muscles, especially swimming.

### How would you manage orthostatic hypotension in heart failure?

More challenging are patients with heart failure or fluid overload and postural hypotension. This may be the result of heart failure drugs or simply a failing heart. Here, the above drug therapies are contraindicated (other than cautiously withdrawing antihypertensive heart failure drugs because of balance of risk) and it is counterintuitive to prescribe furosemide and fludrocrtisone. Salt tablets and middorine should also be used with extreme caution in patients with supine hypertension or a history of stroke. 

### How would you manage orthostatic hypotension with supine hypertension?

Some patients have supine hypertension and orthostatic hypotension; theoretically it may be possible to re-sensitise cerebral autoregulation such that antihypertensives can be used without orthostatic hypotension but in practice it may be necessary to accept hypertension and reduce falls risk by withdrawing drugs.

### How may cardiac syncope be treated?

Specialist cardiology input is usually needed for further investigation and consideration of pharmacological or device therapy. Sinus pauses >= 3 seconds (less if symptomatic) warrant consideration of pacing.

### What driving regulations are there after syncope?

For Group 1 (private) drivers:

* For unexplained syncope with low risk of recurrence there are no restrictions but absence of a prodrome, occurrence whilst driving, or the presence of severe structural heart disease warrants restriction and investigation. Frequently syncope versus other diagnoses has not been established without doubt and patients should be advised to inform the driving licensing authority.
* For reflex syncope there are no restrictions after a single mild episode but recurrent or severe symptoms must first be controlled.
* For cardiac syncope successful treatment must first be established for arrhythmias. Group 2 (professional) drivers have steeper restrictions.

## Case 2.42 seizures

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

##### **Dear Doctor**

Re: Mr Julian Green, aged 40 years

Thank you for seeing Mr Green who had an episode of collapse last week. He was found confused in the kitchen by his wife, and had cut his head on a kitchen unit. She called the ambulance but he was recovering by the time it arrived. He had bitten his tongue but there was no incontinence. He was discharged from the emergency department following a normal computed tomography brain scan. He remembers going into the kitchen but then nothing until the ambulance arrived.

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr Julian Green is a usually well 40-year-old taxi driver who recently had an episode of collapse at home. He remembers walking into the kitchen at around 14.00 hours but then has no recollection of events until an hour later, waking up in an ambulance. He sustained a laceration on the side of his head from a kitchen unit, together with a bite to the side of his tongue. He was taken to the emergency department where he received five stuures and underwent a computed tomography (CT) brain scan that he was told was normal.
