**Predictive clinical features** & & \\ No features have a high negative likelihood ratio, because no feature (even headache) is reliably present in all cases. Several symptoms are moderately predictive of a positive biopsy result: Jaw claudication (34%); claudicant pain comes on gradually during chewing, whereas temporomandibular pain or dental pain is immediate & \\ Diplopia (8\%) & & \\ Any abnormality on palpation of the temporal artery – absent, beaded, tender or enlarged (65\%) & Other useful predictive features are: Temporal headache & \\ Scalp tenderness & ESR \textgreater{} 100 mm / h & \\ Anaemia & & \\ Differential diagnoses & & \\ Herpes zoster & & \\ Migraine or other causes of headaches & & \\ Serious intracranial pathology causing, for example, headache and raised inflammatory markers & & \\ Other causes of acute vision loss, e.g. transient ischaemic attack & & \\ Cervical spine disease & & \\ Ear, nose and throat pathology, e.g. sinus disease, temporomandibular joint disturbance & & \\ ESR, erythrocyte sedimentation rate. & & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.115: Symptoms of giant cell arteritis (GCA) 

### Drug and allergy history

This is less relevant in this case.

#### Family history

There are no suggestions of a familial illness other than possible breast cancer, although this patient was only diagnosed with breast cancer in recent years.

#### Social history

Explore her active lifestyle, what her symptoms limit her from doing and whether the steroids have helped with some of these activities.

## Summary BOX - Assessment and PLAN

The two important differential diagnoses are giant cell arteritis and metastatic breast cancer.

Urgent investigations should include routine haematobiochemistry and a computed tomography (CT) brain scan with contrast enhancement.

If the CT scan does not reveal a structural lesion she should be considered for a temporal artery biopsy.

If it is positive or if symptoms are persuasive corticosteroids should continue; a suggested regimen in Box 2.108. Bone protection is essential.

## Discussion

### How is GCA diagnosed and treated?

The British Society for Rheumatology and British Health Professionals in Rheumatology has produced guidelines (summarised in Box 2.108) for the diagnosis and management of GCA. Most treatment recommendations are based on expert opinion.

### Which tests might help confirm a diagnosis of GCA?

Ninety-five per cent of patients with GCA have raised inflammatory markers, notably an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) > 40 or plasma viscosity > 1.8 or C-reactive protein (CRP) > 10; the CRP is less likely to be raised because GCA does not involve a vigorous T-helper-1-mediated immune acute-phase reaction. Thrombocytosis, normochromic-normocytic anaemia and hypoalbuminaemia are common in GCA.

Temporal artery biopsy is positive in around 70-80% of cases of GCA (especially if biopsies more than 2 cm long are taken to avoid missing skip lesions) but there is uncertainty about how long after starting corticosteroids a biopsy might be useful. The most popular view is that changes might disappear within 3-7 days, but some experts feel that histopathological legacies of GCA can remain for a long time afterwards.

## Social history

Explore her active lifestyle, what her symptoms limit her from doing and whether the steroids have helped with some of these activities.

## Summary BOX - Assessment and PLAN

The two important differential diagnoses are giant cell arteritis and metastatic breast cancer.

Urgent investigations should include routine haematobiochemistry and a computed tomography (CT) brain scan with contrast enhancement.

If the CT scan does not reveal a structural lesion she should be considered for a temporal artery biopsy.

If it is positive or if symptoms are persuasive corticosteroids should continue; a suggested regimen in Box 2.108. Bone protection is essential.

## Discussion

### How is GCA diagnosed and treated?

The British Society for Rheumatology and British Health Professionals in Rheumatology has produced guidelines (summarised in Box 2.108) for the diagnosis and management of GCA. Most treatment recommendations are based on expert opinion.

### Which tests might help confirm a diagnosis of GCA?

Ninety-five per cent of patients with GCA have raised inflammatory markers, notably an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) > 40 or plasma viscosity > 1.8 or C-reactive protein (CRP) > 10; the CRP is less likely to be raised because GCA does not involve a vigorous T-helper-1-mediated immune acute-phase reaction. Thrombocytosis, normochromic-normocytic anaemia and hypoalbuminaemia are common in GCA.

Temporal artery biopsy is positive in around 70-80% of cases of GCA (especially if biopsies more than 2 cm long are taken to avoid missing skip lesions) but there is uncertainty about how long after starting corticosteroids a biopsy might be useful. The most popular view is that changes might disappear within 3-7 days, but some experts feel that histopathological legacies of GCA can remain for a long time afterwards.

## References

- - 

### Early recognition and diagnosis

GCA should be considered in patients > 50 years presenting with the symptoms listed in Table 2.115, with particular attention to the predictive features of jaw claudication, diplopia and temporal artery abnormalities.

### Confirmation of diagnosis - temporal artery biopsy

Temporal artery biopsy (TAB) should be considered whenever GCA is suspected but should not delay prompt institution of high-dose glucocorticoid therapy. Biopsy specimens should be no less than 1 cm, ideally greater than 2 cm, in length. Contrralateral biopsy is usually not required. Patients with negative TAB but a typical clinical picture and response to steroids should be regarded as having GCA. Imaging techniques do not replace TAB for diagnosis of cranial GCA but may be important to exclude intracranial causes of symptoms.

### Large-vessel GCA

Large-vessel GCA should be suspected in patients with prominent systemic symptoms, limb claudication or persistently high inflammatory markers despite adequate glucocorticoid therapy. Imaging techniques, such as positron emission tomography and magnetic resonance imaging scanning, should be reserved for the assessment of suspected large-vessel involvement.

### Initial corticosteroid therapy

High-dose glucocorticoid therapy should be initiated immediately when clinical suspicion of GCA is raised. In uncomplicated GCA (no jaw/tonage claudication or visual symptoms) oral prednisone 40-60 mg (not less than 0.75 mg /kg) should be used daily until resolution of symptoms and laboratory abnormalities. In complicated GCA (evolving visual loss or history of manuscripts fugay intravenous methylprednisolone 500 mg to 1 g daily for 3 days should be administered before prednisolone. For established vision loss at least 60 mg prednisone daily is needed. Bone and gastrointestinal protection should be considered.

### Corticosteroid reduction

Steroid reduction should be considered only in the absence of clinical symptoms, signs and laboratory abnormalities suggestive of active disease, balanced against the need to use the lowest effective dose, patient wishes and side effects. The standard regimen is 40-60 mg prednisolone (not less than 0.75 mg/kg) continued for 4 weeks (or until resolution of symptoms and laboratory abnormalities), reduced by 10 mg every 2 weeks to 20 mg, then by 2.5 mg every 2-4 weeks to 10 mg, and then by 1 mg every 1-2 months, provided there is no relapse. Some patients may benefit from a more gradual reduction, or a period of treatment at a stable dose, such as 5 mg for 3 months.

### Low-dose aspirin

This should be considered in all patients with GCA, if there are no contraindications.

### Monitoring of therapy

Patients should be monitored for evidence of relapse, and disease-related and steroid-related complications. Monitoring should be clinical, supported by measurement of inflammatory markers. In particular the following features should be sought: headaches, jaw and tongue claudication, visual symptoms, vascular claudication of limbs, bruits and asymmetrical pulses, polymylagic symptoms, osteoporotic risk factors and fractures, or other corticosteroid-related complications, other symptoms that may suggest an alternative diagnosis. Frequency of follow-up is advised as: weeks 0, 1, 3, 6 then months 3, 6, 9, 12 in the first year (extra visits if relapse or adverse events). At each visit a full blood count, inflammatory markers, urea and electrolytes and glucose should be obtained, and every 2 years a chest radiograph to monitor for aortic aneurysm (echocardiography, magnetic resonance imaging or positron emission tomography may also be appropriate). Bone mineral density measurement is important.

### Relapse

Disease relapse should be suspected in patients with a return of symptoms of GCA, ischaemic complications, or unexplained constitutional or polymylagic symptoms. Relapse is usually associated with a rise in erythrocyte sedimentation rate/C-reactive protein, but may occur with normal inflammatory markers. For headache only, treatment is the previous higher steroid dose. For headache and jaw claudication, treatment is 40-60 mg daily prednisolone. For eye symptoms treatment is either 60 mg prednisolone or intravenous methylprednisolone. For large-vessel GCA investigation with imaging techniques (magnetic resonance imaging or positron emission tomography) and treatment should be considered using systemic vasculitis protocols.

### Recurrent relapse

Recurrent relapse or failure to wear corticosteroids requires consideration of adjuvant therapy, such as methotrexate or other immunosuppressive agents should be started at the third relapse.

### Box 2.109 Summary of diagnosis and management of polymylagia rheumatica (PMR)

#### Diagnostic approach

A stepped diagnostic process for the evaluation of PMR comprises assessment for core inclusion and core exclusion features, exclusion of mimicking conditions, assessment of the response to a standardised dose of 15 mg prednisolone and confirmation of diagnosis at early follow-up.

Core inclusion features are:

\[\bullet\]

Bilateral shoulder and/or pelvic pain

Morning stiffness > 45 minutes

Aburut onset

Age > 50 years

Duration > 2 weeks

Acute inflammatory response. 

### Case 2.48 Pyrexia and Sepsis

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Monitoring and follow-up

Early follow-up is necessary at 1-3 weeks post steroid commencement with a follow-up schedule of weeks 0, 1-3, 6 and months 3, 6, 9, 12 in the first year (extra visits if relapses or adverse events). Clinical monitoring at each visit should include consideration of disease activity and response to treatment, complications of disease (including GCA and large-vessel vasculitis), steroid-related side effects, atypical features or alternative pathology, and persistent pain (may arise from degenerative or soft-tissue pathology such as factor cuff tears). Laboratory monitoring at each visit should include full blood count, inflammatory markers, urea and electrolytes and glucose or urine dipstick.

#### Relapse

Relapse is the recurrence of PMR symptoms or the onset of GCA, not just unexplained raised inflammatory markers. For clinical features of GCA treatment is as for GCA. For clinical features of PMR prednisolone should be increased to the previous higher dose or a single dose of intramuscular methylprednisolone 120 mg given. Adjuvant immunosuppressive therapy may be considered after two relapses.

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

##### Dear Doctor

Re: Dr John Watson, aged 60 years

Thank you for admitting this 60-year-old ex-hospital

ophthalmologist who is now engaged in regular overseas aid work. He presents with pyrexia of unknown origin. He has had night sweats and fever for the past few months. He has a documented temperature of 38degC. He has lost 2 kg in weight. He is usually well, although was briefly an inpatient in your hospital 3 months ago for incision and drainage of a staphylococcal abscess in his Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Dr John Watson is a 60-year-old ex-hospital ophthalmologist with a month or so of fever, drenching night sweats and weight loss of around 2-3 kg. He has had occasional blood in his urine for around 2 months. He is usually well but 3 months ago developed an abscess in his groin, which required a short hospital admission to the surgical wards for incision and drainage. While in hospital he was found to be anaemic, and is awaiting outpatient endoscopies to exclude a source of covert bleeding. He has no symptoms of indigestion, change in bowel habit or family history of colorectal cancer. He has had a cough for the last few months, but it is mild and he had not thought much of it. His other past history is removal of a skin melanoma 5 years ago, thought to be completely excised. He does not take any medications. He is now semi-retired but does intermittent overseas medical work, largely cataract surgery. Six months ago he was in Africa, where he did not take adequate anti-malarial prophylaxis because the tablets made him feel sick, but was well on return. Nevertheless, he feels he could still be at risk from malaria, and is a little concerned about the possibility of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) having sustained occasional minor needlestick injuries overseas. He has never smoked. He is married with two children, and all are well at home although there has been a 'throat bug' in the family.

### How to approach the case

#### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

Consider the causes of pyrexia. Fever is a state of elevated core temperature above 38 degC, often part of the defensive resistance to invasion of live (micro-organisms) or inanimate matter recognised as pathogenic or alien by the host. The term 'pyrexia' is often used synonymously with fever. Fever should be distinguished from hyperthermia, an unregulated rise in body temperature due to a failure of temperature homeostasis. The classic definition of pyrexia of unknown origin (PUIO) or fever of unknown origin (FIO) is a febrile illness of at least 3 weeks' duration, with repeated temperature measurements of 38.3degC or higher, that defies diagnosis after 1 week of hospital inpatient evaluation. This has subsequently been modified to remove the requirement that investigations be inpatient. Unfortunately, many clinicians use the term PUO loosely to include any fever for which a cause has not been found, which should be discouraged because the most likely causes of short-term fevers are different from those of PUO. He therefore may have PUO, although strictly his temperature has not been documented and there are numerous potential causes that could be rapidly confirmed or excluded by tests. A full history should be taken of the nature of symptoms and their chronology, thinking of potential causes (Table 2.116).

There are many potential causes of PUO so it is not possible to list a standard battery of tests for every case, and it is preferable to tailor investigations according to clues from history and repeated examinations.

Infectious in immunocompromised people (transplant recipients, HIV-infected patients, neutropenic patients) can present in an atypical fashion, as can infections in patients taking antipyretic, corticosteroids or antibiotics. It has been suggested that PUO can be subclassified into four different types - classic, nosocomial, immune-deficient and HIV-related.

Causes vary geographically and demographically. In developing countries PUO is more frequently due to infections. The relative likelihood of PUO being due to specific causes is related to age. In children, infections are the most common cause. Haematological malignancies and solid tumours are more common causes in elderly patients than in younger adults. Infectious and connective tissue disorders are also frequent causes in the elderly, and giant cell arteritis and polymylalgia rheumatica are particularly common. Bacterial endocarditis is now a less common cause because it can now be diagnosed relatively quickly.

#### Patient perspective

His thoughts are entirely reasonable.

#### Past medical history

The recent staphylococcal abscess must be considered as a potential source of sepsis, perhaps with secondary subacute bacterial endocarditis, although staphylococcal endocarditis is invariably acute and aggressive. The history of melanoma cannot be ignored.

#### Drug and allergy history

It is unlikely that this is relevant in this case. 

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{p{56.9pt} p{113.8pt} p{113.8pt}} \hline
**Category** & **Causes / examples** & **Possible clinical clues** \\ \hline Infection (30\%) & Localised bacterial (+ / – pyogenic) & Intra-abdominal: Subphenic, diverticular, hepatic abscess \\ \hline Ostecomyelitis, e.g. vertebral & Prostheses \\ \hline Septic arthritis & Exquisitely tender joint \\ \hline Skin and soft-tissue infection & History of oedema or czema Intravenous drug abuse (lemon juice and the practice of licking needles dissolves drugs but is a perfect culture medium for serious organisms); necrotising fascitis and deep tissue infections such as psoas abscesses (causing pain on leg straightening and the ’bent-knee’ sign) are now common \\ \hline Dental abscess & Tendermess of a tooth \\ \hline Sinusitis & Tendermess of facial sinuses or mastoid \\ \hline Chronic otitis media & Otalgia \\  & Conductive hearing loss \\ \hline Unresolving pneumonia & Chest symptoms \\ \hline Unresolving urinary tract infection & Frequency \\  & Dysuria \\  & Loin pain \\ \hline Gentitorunary infection & Vaginal or urethral discharge \\  & Dysuria, \\  & Dyspareunia \\  & Pelvic pain \\ \hline Brain abscess & Headache, focal neurological signs \\ \hline Tuberculosis meningitis & Confusion \\ \hline Systemic bacterial & Rheumatic fever & Streptococcal throat infections \\ \hline Infective endocarditis & Prosthetic valve or native valve disease \\  & Invasive (including dental) procedures \\ \hline Salmonellosis / typhoid & Gastroenteritis \\ \hline Atypical pneumonia, e.g. _Coxella_ infection, psttatcosis & Usually very unwell \\  & Contact with animals \\ \hline Bruellosis & Unpasteurised milk \\ \hline Leptospirosis & Occupational history (sewers) \\ \hline Listeriosis & Dairy products, soft cheeses, etc. \\ \hline Lyme disease (sometimes also _Bartonella_ infection) & Tick bite \\ \hline Tuberculosis & Cough \\  & Night sweats \\  & Weight loss \\ \hline Syphilis & Index of suspicion \\ \hline Viral & HIV infection & lymphadenopathy \\  & Recurrent fungal / viral infections \\ \hline Hepatitis B or C & Risk factors \\ \hline Epstein–Barr virus & Sore throat \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
