**Category** & **Causes / examples** & **Possible clinical clues** \\ \hline \hline Fungal & Systemic candidiasis & Usually immunocompromised \\ \cline{2-3}  & Cryptococcus Cryptococcus Cryptococcus meningitis & Known HIV infection \\ \cline{2-3}  & Protozonal & Malaria & Travel to malaria zone \\ \cline{2-3}  & Giardiaids & Common \\ \cline{2-3}  & Amoebiasis & Swimming in infected water \\ \cline{2-3}  & Toxoplasma Toxoplasma Toxoplasma & Petsc cat \\ \cline{2-3}  & Worm & Toxoplasma Toxoplasma & Petsc dog \\ \hline Neoplasm (15–20\%) & Lymphoproliferative disease & Night sweats \\ Lymphadenopathy & Lymphadenopathy \\ \cline{2-3}  & Renal cell carcinoma & Lack of early warning symptoms \\ Classic triad of flank pain (40\%), haematuria (40\%) and flank mass (25\%) rare; 25–30\% discovered as incidental finding \\ Weight loss and night sweats & Hypertension \\ Varicocoele & Paraneposistic – hypercalcemia (PTHrp), polycythaemia (EPO), iron deficiency anaemia, non-metastatic liver dysfunction, neuropathy \\ \cline{2-3}  & Lung cancer & Cough, haemoplysis, weight loss, smoker \\ \cline{2-3}  & Hepatocellular carcinoma & Complication of cirrhosis \\ \cline{2-3} Colorectal cancer & Especially elderly \\ \cline{2-3}  & Carcinomatous meningitis & Headache, confusion \\ \cline{2-3}  & Metastatic disease & Symptoms of primary, e.g. gastrointestinal, lung, breast, prostate, skin \\ \cline{2-3}  & Atrial myxoma & Very high ESR \\ \hline Inflammatory disease & Systemic vasculitides & Renal impairment \\ – autoimmune / connective & Rheumatoid arthritis & Arthritis \\ tissue & Lupus & Skin rashes \\ (15–20\%) & Arthritis & Arthralgia \\ \cline{2-3}  & Polymorphisms & Muscle tenderness \\ \cline{2-3}  & Adult Still’s disease & Arthralgia, fever, rash, raised white cell count, sore throat, lymphadenopathy, abnormal liver function, negative serology \\ \cline{2-3}  & Giant cell arteritis & Headache \\ Visual disturbance & Jewish pain \\ \cline{2-3}  & Sarcoid meningitis & Confusion \\ \cline{2-3} Miscellaneous and unknown & Deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary thromboembolism & Tachypnoea \\ Drugs / toxins & Antibiotics, NSAIDs, AEs \\ \cline{2-3}  & Periodic fever syndromes & Family history \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.116: Causes of pyredia of unknown origin—cont’d 

### Family history

A family history of colorectal or renal cancer should be explored.

#### Social history

His occupational history with overseas travel is potentially highly relevant, exposing him to malaria and needlestick injuries. A history of exposure to animals including household pets and farm animals is part of routine enquiry for PUO, as is a history of insect bites, including tick bites, a food history (poorly cooked meat, etc.), a history of unwell contacts, a sexual history and enquiry about intravenous drug taking.

#### Summary box - asseessment and plan

The differential diagnosis remains wide without further tests but possible infectious causes include staphylococcal bacteria (with endocarditis essential to exclude after his recent abscess and a possible new heart murmur), malaria and tuberculosis. Malignant causes include renal cell carcinoma and metastatic melanoma.

Routine blood tests include full blood count, urea, electrolytes and creatinine, liver function tests, calcium, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, C-reactive protein and multiple blood cultures from separate sites. Blood film examination for malarial parasites should be performed. A range of blood serology testing for viruses and autoantibodies may be indicated in a true pyrexia of unknown origin.

Urinolysis for blood is essential, and if present should be analysed for casts.

A chest X-ray and echocardiogram are important early tests. Further tests are almost inevitable, but dictated by the preliminary information. A contrast-enhanced computed tomography scan of chest, abdomen and pelvis would be important to look for metastatic disease or carcinoma, and differentiates between cystic and solid masses.

Empirical antibiotics, steroids or antipyretic usually thwart successful investigation and should be avoided if possible.

## Discussion

### What causes pyrexia?

The release of certain cytokines and acute-phase reactants involved in the immune response.

### What is sepsis?

This refers to systemic illness caused by microbial invasion of parts of the body that are normally sterile. The term septicaemia is old-fashioned and encompasses the better terms:

* Infection
* Systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS), which may be due to sepsis, characterised by a temperature > 38 degC or < 36 degC, tachycardia > 90 beats/min, a respiratory rate > 20 breaths/min and a white cell count > 12 000/mm3 or < 400/mm3; it may also occur in trauma, burns and acute pancreatitis
* Sepsis (inflammation with evidence or suspicion of a microbial cause)
* renal, respiratory, hepatic, haematological, central nervous system, cardiovascular
- leading to lactic acidosis, renal impairment, acute lung injury, liver dysfunction, thrombocytopenia, confusion and cardiac dysfunction)
* Septic shock (a subset of severe sepsis with hypotension despite volume replacement).

Severe sepsis and septic shock have mortality rates of 25-30% and 40-70%, respectively, partly because early phases may not be recognised as sepsis or vigorously treated.

### What host factors may predispose to sepsis?

Host factors that affect susceptibility to sepsis include immune status, illness severity, damage to tissues and indwelling lines.

### How do bacteria induce sepsis and septic shock?

Lipopolysaccharide-binding protein on host cells (e.g. monocytes) binds to lipopolysaccharide on Gram-negative bacteria and transfers it to CD14, which resides on the host cell membrane. Cellular activation requires CD14 and a co-receptor known as a Toll-like receptor (TLR-4 for lipopolysaccharide). Gram-positive bacteria may induce similar pathways via other TLRs (TLR-2, TLR-6, TLR-9) or by producing exotoxins that act as superantigens or by directly stimulating immune cells via their proteins; superantigens are molecules that bind to major histocompatibility complex class II molecules of antigen-presenting cells and T-cell receptors activating large numbers of T cells and inciting a powerful pro-inflammatory response. All cases of septic shock involve overt pro-inflammatory responses, often with initial monocyte release of mediators (e.g. tumour necrosis factor-a (TNF-a), interleukins (ILs), platelet-activating factor, prostaglandins) provoking neutrophil activation and T-helper-1 lymphocyte-mediated inflammation, with vasodilatation and intravascular coagulopathy, and organ damage.

### What are some important clinical and laboratory markers of sepsis and its associated inflammation?

Important clinical markers may include fever (or hypothermia), tachycardia, tachyonnoea, hypotension, confusion, oliguria, positive fluid balance and hypoxia.

Easily accessible laboratory markers may include leucocytosis, elevated C-reactive protein, hyperglycaemia, elevated creatinine, elevated plasma bilirubin, thrombocytopenia and elevated international normalised ratio. Lactate is invariably elevated.

The pro-inflammatory response provoked after the initial recognition of lipopolysaccharide is complex, and it is increasingly understood that genetic factors often determine susceptibility or protection by the severity of this response. Many markers of this response are recognised, but few are yet clinically available.

### What are early warning scoring systems?

These are systems used to identify serious illness, notably sepsis, early, using scoring charts with parameters such as heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, conscious level and urine output.

### What are the general approaches to managing sepsis?

Questions that should always be posed include: What is the likely organism? Where might it have come from? Why has this patient got it? Where else might the infection have gone? What is the likely resistance profile? Managing sepsis requires early recognition, supportive care, source control, antibiotics (the earlier the better) and specific non-antibiotic adjuvant therapies. Adjuvant therapies have achieved increasing prominence in severe sepsis, since mortality is 30-60% even with all other measures in place. These include:

* Early goal-directed therapy, which refers to early protocols in emergency departments designed to achieve early physiological stability before transfer to an intensive care unit, in such parameters as central venous pressure, mean arterial pressure and urine output; it refers to doing all basic things well such as timely use of fluids and blood transfusion and ionotropes and evidence is compelling
* Corticosteroids (many patients in intensive care have adrenal insufficiency though there is no value in synacthen testing as results are chaotic)
* Safe but not tight glycaemic control
* Consideration of recombinant human activated protein C, recommended in adults with severe sepsis with two or more major organs involved in the setting of optimum intensive care support but not effective in adults with severe sepsis but at low risk of death (acute physiology and chronic health evaluation (APACHE) < 25 or single organ failure) since APC is a natural anticoagulant and increases the rate of serious bleeding
* Gastric protection against stress ulceration
* Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis.

Staphylococcus is commonly detected in blood cultures. Which factors alert you to its reflecting true bacteriaemia rather than being a 'contaminant'?

These include the presence of local or systemic signs of infection, detection of bacteria in both culture bottles, a time to blood culture positivity less than 14 hours, coagulase-negative _Staphylococcus_ (although not always the case!) and previous colonisation with staphylococci.

### What types of disease can _Staphylococcus aureus_ cause?

_S. aureus_ is a versatile pathogen carrying significant morbidity and mortality. Infection is usually endogenous in origin, with approximately one-quarter of the population carrying one or more strains, principally in the nose. It can cause a broad spectrum of disease from mild skin and soft-tissue infections (SSTIs) to severe life-threatening bacteriaemic disease.

_S. aureus_ is a leading cause of community- and hospital-acquired bacteriaemia, and rates have risen with increased use of intravascular catheters. The overall mortality of 20-40% has remained largely unchanged over several decades. Complications of bacteriaemia include infective endocarditis and metastatic seeding to almost any body site. Bones, joints, meninges and prosthetic devices are particularly prone to metastatic seeding of _S. aureus_ following bacteriaemia. In adults, haematogenous _S. aureus_ osteomyelitis most often involves the vertebral bodies. Patients over the age of 50 with community-acquired bacteriaemia and no identifiable primary focus are at highest risk. The joint synovium is highly vascular and susceptible to haematogenous infection. Septic arthritis usually involves a single joint but may be polyarticular in people with rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Patients with immunosuppression and/or RA are at particular risk.

Host risk factors for complications include community acquisition of bacteriaemia, absence of identifiable infective focus, presence of an indwelling foreign body and comorbidities such as malignancy and HIV. Complications are often occult and it is impractical to investigate all _S. aureus_ bacteriaemia extensively, but factors that predict complications developing (by no means robust) include a time to blood culture positivity less than 14 hours, positive follow-up blood cultures 48-96 hours after the first positive blood culture result, persistent fever 72 hours after initial positive blood culture result, skin findings suggestive of an acute systemic infection and a new murmur.

The pathogenicity of _S. aureus_ is also related to a large number of virulence factors that include bacterial components such as surface-associated molecules. _S. aureus_ also produces a number of secreted products, including superantigens, cytotoxins such as Panton-Valentine leucocidin (PVL), and secreted enzymes. 

### What is toxic shock syndrome?

Toxic shock syndrome is an acute, potentially fatal condition characterised by temperature > 38.9degC, rash and desquamation (especially palms and soles), hypotension and multi-system involvement. Toxic shock syndrome often develops from a site of colonisation and is related to the production of exotoxins. The proportion of cases related to menstruation has dropped and non-menstrual cases may be associated with localised infections, surgery or insect bites.

### What do you know about 'MRSA?

This is meticillin-resistant (but more appropriately multi-resistant) _S. aureus_ because of its multi-resistant cassette gene; b-lactamase production was one of the traditional modes of antibiotic resistance, followed by production of antibiotics that inhibited b-lactamase. MRSA simply changed its target to become resistant again. In the early 1990s MRSA was a rare problem; now all doctors must be conscious of MRSA. Historically, Gram-negative organisms were the main cause of sepsis, then in the 1990s the high emergence of MRSA put Gram-positives in the limelight; with better control of MRSA in recent years Gram-negative and fungal organisms are seeing new-found predominance.

#### Risk factors for MRSA colonisation

These include recent hospitalisation, recent (3-6 months) antimicrobial use, invasive lines (intravenous, urinary catheters), recent surgery, nursing home residence, advanced age, underlying severe disease, exposure to a colonised or infected patient, morphid obesity and orthopaedic implant surgery.

#### Indications for MRSA eradication treatment

Clinical indications are stronger than treatment for those with colonisation. Isolated nasal carriage has a reasonable chance of clearance, but skin carriage and colonisation of indwelling catheters cannot be cleared.

#### Staff screening for MRSA carriage

This was popular, but it is apparent that many staff can have negative skin carriage at the start of a shift and be positive by the end of a shift.

#### Patient screening for MRSA carriage

Eighty-five per cent of carriage of MRSA is asymptomatic, but these people have a much higher risk of clinical infection when unwell; 15% of people have clinical infection. Same-day polymerase chain reaction testing has improved detection, and is used increasingly in at-risk patients.

#### MRSA prevalence control

Handwashing and prudent use of antibiotics (e.g. avoidance of quinolones which select MRSA) are imperative.

### What do you know about community-associated _S. aureus?_

MRSA is classically acquired during contact with healthcare institutions and is known as hospital-associated MRSA (HA-MRSA). However, community-associated MRSA (CA-MRSA) is an increasing problem and CA-MRSA strains are different, often susceptible to a wider range of antibiotics, and resemble community strains of meticillin-sensitive _S. aureus_ (MSSA). Most of them produce the PVL toxin.

### What do you know about PVL _S. aureus?_

PVL toxin is encoded by a mobile phage that produces two gene products that assemble with each other to form a membrane-perforating complex that destroys white blood cells and causes tissue necrosis. PVL-related disease is often associated with severe infections including necrotising pneumonia, usually in healthy young adults, often with a history of recurrent 'boils'. Currently in the UK, PVL-related disease is mostly associated with MSSA, with small clusters of MRSA. It is rapidly increasing in prevalence. Risk factors for acquiring PVL _S. aureus_ are the five 'C's:

* Contaminated items
* Close contact, such as with contact sports
* Crowding, such as in households, gyms, prisons and military training camps
* Cleanliness (lack thereof)
* Cuts and other compromised skin integrity.

Clinical features suggestive of PVL _S. aureus_ are given in Box 2.110.

#### Box 2.110 Clinical features of Panton-Valentine leucoidin (PVL) _Staphylococcus aureus_ disease

#### Skin and soft-tissue infections (often recurrent and may occur in clusters)

* Frununculosis, carbuncles, folliavitis, cellulitis
* Cutaneous lesions > 5 cm
* Pain and erythema out of proportion to severity of skin findings
* Necrosis

#### Invasive infections (may occur in young, immunocompetent host)
* Necrotising, cavitating pneumonia (suspect with respiratory symptoms and sepsis following a flu-like illness, haemoprysis, hypotension, marked neurogenic, very high inflammatory markers, raised creatine kinase or numerous staphylococci on Gram film of sputum); the CURB-65 (confusion, urea, respiratory rate, blood pressure) score can underestimate the severity of this condition with a mortality of around 75%
* Necrotising fascitis
* Osteomyelitis, septic arthritis and pyomysitis
* Purpura fulminans
* Cardiac abscesses Swabs from infected skin or nares may reveal the presence of PVL-positive _S. aureus_.

Neither fludloxacillin nor vancomycin will stop toxin production, and clindamycin and linezolid are often used, although it is the toxin, rather than ongoing infection, which gives rise to the high mortality.

### What do you know about vancomycin intermediately and fully resistant

_S. aureus_ (VISA)?

VISA does not reliably respond to glycopeptides and is not detected by routine microbiological methods. The mechanism of resistance is probably related to a thicker capsule. VISA should be suspected in any patient who has persistent _S. aureus_ infection despite prolonged glycopeptide treatment.

### What are the treatments for staphylococcal infection?

Most SSTIs can be dealt with in the community using oral antibiotics. Severe disease requires parenteral antibiotics.

* Fludloxacillin is the treatment of choice for MSA infection and parenteral glycopeptides are preferred for MRSA in most situations.
* Other antibiotics include rifampicin, fusidic acid, clindamycin (very effective for staphylococcal and streptococcal SSTIs but resistance rapidly occurs during treatment and _Clostridium difficile_ may occur) and tetracyclines.
* glycopeptide-resistant _S. aureus_ has occurred, the gene jumping from _Enterococcus;_ linezolid (a novel, bacteriostatic oxazolidinone antibiotic which inhibits MRSA toxicity, useful for vancomycin-resistant _Enterococcus_, usually reserved for severe SSTI or pneumonia, but may cause bone marrow suppression or peripheral and optic neuropathy); daptomycin (a bactericidal lipophilicopeptide for severe SSTIs but not respiratory infections as it is inhibited by secretions); and tigecycline (a glycolyclin, structurally related to tetracyclines, with broad-spectrum activity, for treatment of complicated SSTIs and complicated abdominal infections caused by multiple-antibacterial-resistant organisms).
* Combination therapy with agents such as clindamycin and linezolid are needed to suppress PVL and alpha-toxin production, with rifampicin for intracellular clearance of staphylococci. Intravenous immunosuppressive by considered because of their action in neutralising exotoxins and superantigens.

### How is resistance to antibiotics tackled?

Health-care-associated infection is now a very serious issue.

Resistance to infection is common but not necessary. Some countries, such as the Netherlands, have very low rates of it; for example the rate of MRSA here is around 25-50-fold less than in the UK.

Resistant infections include _C. difficile_, MRSA, vancomycin-resistant _Enterococcus_, extended spectrum betac-lactamase _Klebsiella pneumoniae_, multi-resistant _Acinetobacter baumannii_, coagulase-negative staphylococci, _Legionella pneumophila_, viral hepatitis and HIV infection, and emerging infections that could become resistant include _Acinetobacter_.

The prudent use of antibiotics is essential. Resistance occurs because resistant organisms are selected by antibiotics that kill other harmless or protective bacteria and allow resistant, nefarious ones to flourish. Antibiotics should be started only with good cause or indication, using small-spectrum agents where possible, and stopping these if it becomes apparent that infection is not present. Where infection is confirmed, the regimen should be modified if microbiological results dictate so. Dosages should be correct and not so low as to create selection of resistant organisms. The shortest duration of treatment possible should be used.

Prevention of transmission or cross-infection is very important, requiring personal and hand hygiene, aseptic approaches, the use of devices only with strict indication and removal of unnecessary devices; this requires always questioning why a device is _in situ_.

Surveillance and action includes isolation of infected patients, the use of gloves and gowns and sometimes masks, and involvement of the infection-control team.

### What tropical diseases should acute physicians be aware of?

#### Malaria

Malaria is one of the most common imported infections, increasing in prevalence in the UK with an increasing travelling population, and the threat of warming European climates alluring mosquitoes.

The five species are: _Plasmodium falciparum_ (common in sub-Saharan Africa and Papua New Guinea), _Plasmodium vivax_ (Central and South America, North Africa, Middle East, Indian subcontinent), _Plasmodium ovale_ (West Africa), _Plasmodium malaria_ (Africa) and _Plasmodium knowlesi_ (South East Asia).

Clinical features are produced from asexual forms in the blood which destroy red cells, localise in organs, obstruct the micro-circulation and release toxins leading to the classical rigors and fevers. The incubation period ranges from 7 days to many years, but _P. falciparum_ generally less than 6 weeks. After a period of fatigue and non-specific symptoms, abrupt fever develops with hot, cold and sweat phases; the periodicity with _P. falciparum_ is variable.

Features of severe malaria (caused by _P. falciparum_) include cerebral malaria (encephalopathy and coma), respiratory failure (pulmonary oedema), hypoglycemia, shock, acidosis, bleeding, renal involvement and blackwater fever and severe anaemia.

Diagnosis definitively is made by thick and thin blood film examination. Treatments for non-falciparum malaria include chloroquine and primaquine. Treatments for falciparum malaria include quinine, doxycycline, clindamycin, Malarone, Riamet and Lariam for mild malaria, and artesunate, quinine and quinidine for severe malaria. There can be extensive side effects.

### Dengue

Dengue fever is a common mosquito-borne arboviral infection seen in many parts of the world such as the Far East. There are four types, none conferring immunity against another. It typically produces high fevers with raised inflammatory markers, and often tachycardia or hypotension, in the context of a patient looking surprisingly well. The dangerous effects are dengue shock syndrome and dengue haemorrhagic shock syndrome, the latter less common than the former, either occurring generally with a second or subsequent infected strain. Shock syndrome is usually successfully treated with fluid resuscitation until it passes (if in hospital), whilst haemordiag shock syndrome has a higher mortality rate even with intensive care. Shock syndromes tend to occur days or weeks into the illness at the time fever is settling, and so the most risky time is when patients look well enough for discharge from hospital; they should not be!

### Diarrhoeal diseases

Acute water diarrhoeal diseases include viral diseases (rotavius, norovirus, adenovirus, cytomegalovirus), bacterial diseases (enterotoxigenic _Escherichia coli_, _Vibrio cholera_, _Aeromonas hydrophila_, _Salmonella enterica_, _Shigella_ spp., _Campylobacter jejuni_) and protozoan diseases (_Cryptosporidium_ spp., _Giardia intestinalis_, _Cyclospora cayetanensis_).

Acute bloody diarrhoeal diseases include bacterial diseases (_Shigella_ spp., enterohaemorrhagic _E. coli_, enteroinvasive _E. coli_), protozoan diseases (_Entamoeba histolytica_, _Balantidium coli_) and helminths (_Schistosoma mansoni_).

Persistent diarrhoeal diseases include enteropathogenic _E. coli_, enteroadherence _E. coli_, _Mycobacterium tuberculosis_, _Cryptosporidium_ spp., _G. intestinalis_, _Isospora belli_, micro-sporidia and _Strongfoides steroralis_, conditions also common in immunosuppressed people.

### Leishmaniasis

_Leishmania_ protozoan parasites are transmitted by phlebotomus sandfly, and though the condition is uncommon in the UK it affects 1.5-2 million people per year worldwide.

Visceral leishmaniasis is common in India, the Sudan and the Mediterranean basin. It causes chronic fever, splenomegaly and pancytopenia, sometimes with hepatomegaly, lymphadenopathy, hypocalbuminemia and hypergammaglobulinaemia. Symptoms are less obvious in immunosuppressed people, and visceral leishmaniasis in HIV is a major problem worldwide. Diagnosis is by direct visualisation of parasites on microscopy (the best tissues being bone marrow or splenic), although direct agglutination and dipstick tests are fairly sensitive. Treatments include amphotericin.

Cutaneous leishmaniasis occurs in Africa, the Middle East and Central Asia (Old World cutaneous leishmaniasis species) and South and Central Aemrica (New World cutaneous leishmaniasis species). It presents with one or more nodules on exposed areas (hands or face) that enlarge and ulcerate over weeks, after an incubation period of usually months. Diagnosis is by direct visualisation of parasites on microscopy, or polymerase chain reaction testing. Mucocutanous leishmaniasis occurs in South America and causes nasal stuffiness, bleeding, ulceration and septal perforation from mucosal inflammation. Treatments for cutaneous leishmaniasis and mucocutaenous leishmaniasis include sodium stibglucanate.

### Schistosoma schistosomiasis

Schistosomiasis is a predominantly African trematode (fluke) helminth disease whose intermediate host is an aquatic snail as it cannot complete its life cycle in humans. Free swimming cercariae invade skin or mucous membranes, develop into schistosomiasis, and migrate via the circulation to mesenteric or vesical veins. _Schistosoma haematobium_ lives in the bladder venous plexus and deposits eggs in the bladder mucosa. _Schistosoma mansoni_ lives in the mesenteric and portal veins and deposits eggs in the bowel mucosa. Symptoms include haematuria and haematospermia, and acute manifestations due to ectopic worm migration include neurological schistosomiasis. Diagnosis is supported by history and eosinophil count. Treatment is with praziquantel, sometimes preceded by corticosteroids.

## What do you know about renal cell carcinoma?

This constitutes 2-3% of all adult malignancies, is twice as common in men, and usually presents in the fourth to sixth decades.

The cause is usually unknown. Smoking doubles the risk, and it is associated with obesity, unopposed oestrogen, hypertension, petroleum products, heavy metals, tuberose sclerosis and acquired cystic kidney disease or chronic kidney disease, dialysis and immunosuppression (relative risk x 80). It also occurs in rare hereditary syndromes such as von Hippel-Lindau (autosomal dominant, 3p deletion, multiple cancers).

It usually arises in the proximal tubule epithelium and histologically may be clear cell 75% (proximal tubule origin, 3p deletion), chromophobic, chromophilic, oncolytic or of collecting duct type.

Renal cell carcinoma is often an incidental finding. Haematuria, a flank mass or loin tenderness may occur, but symptoms may equally be the result of cytokines such as erythropoietin or IL-6. Renal cell carcinoma is a great mimicker. It can produce a raised haemoglobin or iron deficiency anaemia (a cytokine inhibits iron incorporation into the haem synthetic pathway), thrombocytosis or leucocytosis including eosinophilia (the tumour releases II-5). Thirty-five per cent of renal cell carcinoma presents with metastases, most commonly lung, but sometimes soft tissue, bone, liver, skin or central nervous system. Symptoms often improve after resection of the primary tumour.

The mode of treatment depends upon staging because the disease may be confined to the kidney or extend to perinephric fat, the renal vein and inferior vena cava, regional nodes, adjacent viscera including adrenals, or more distantly with distant metastases. Surgery is the only potentially curative treatment for localised tumour, by radical nephrectomy. Palliative nephrectomy may alleviate symptoms such as pain, hypercalaemia and polycythaemia. Regression of metastases has been reported after nephrectomy but nephrectomy cannot be considered adjuvant treatment. The disease is relatively resistant to radiotherapy or chemotherapy but the former may palliate bone or cerebral involvement. Immunotherapy has shown promise.

### What are periodic fever syndromes?

Periodic fever syndromes are founding members of a family of conditions known as autoinflammatory syndromes. Autoinflammatory is explained in Box 2.111.

Periodic fever syndrome refers to a group of hereditary periodic fevers that may be autosomal dominant or recessive. These are listed in Table 2.117. The pathophysiology is complex and differs between disorders. IL-1b secretion depends on recognition of antigen and initial intracellular signalling by TLRs, the first structures of the innate immune system which recognise infective antigens or tissue damage, leading to production of pro-IL-1b. Pro-IL-1b is further processed by protein complexes containing MEFV (pyrin) and CISA1 (cryopyrin) genes. Additional 'danger signals' are usually required to activate these processing protein complexes to cleave pro-IL-1b into the mature cytokine. Mutated cryopyrin can self-activate cleavage of pro-IL-1b. Pyrin inhibits cleavage of pro-IL-1b and when mutated results in increased levels of IL-1b. Syndromes and their features of attacks are shown in Table 2.117. Inflammatory markers may be raised during attacks. Steroids and biological therapies may help symptoms.

Other hereditary autoinflammatory conditions include deficiency of interleukin-1 receptor antagonists, pyogenic sterile arthritis/poyderma gangrenosum/acne and chronic recurrent multifocal osteomyelitis. 

### Case 2.49 Weight Loss and Maligianancy

#### Candidate information

##### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

##### Dear Doctor

Re: Mr George Ross, aged 86 years

Thank you for reviewing this gentleman, who was admitted to hospital last year with anaemia while staying with relatives. He was apparently transposed and on his return I referred him to the hospital here for further investigation. Unfortunately his appointment was superseded by a family bereavement and he missed it. He did, however, see a rheumatologist on account of joint pains and shoulder discomfort who felt the overall position to be anaemia of chronic autoimmune disease. He has been on steroids, with some improvement initially, for 6 months. But he has now become more unveil again, tried and, significantly, losing weight. On examination I did wonder about a mass in the right iliac fossa. He has type 2 diabetes and his blood glucose has risen to around 20. I wonder if you have any further advice on diagnosis and management.

Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

##### Patient information

Mr George Ross is an 86-year-old man who has become tired and has lost around 10 kg in weight over the last 6 months. His clothes have become extremely loose. He and his wife went to stay with their daughter and son-in-law in Scotland last year; during their visit, Mr Ross became 'unwell' (a little light-headed and confused) and was admitted to hospital. There he was treated for a urinary tract infection and noted to be anaemic and was transfused with blood. Referral for investigation of his anaemia was interrupted when his brother died in London and family support was needed there. Mr Ross did see a rheumatologist, having also been troubled with joint pains, who felt he might have 'rheumatoid or polymyalgia'. He denies any history of proximal girdle pain or morning stiffness. Mr Ross also has type 2 diabetes and takes metformin and simvastatin, but is on no other medications. He has no other symptoms. He was a very active and well-maintained man until last year, playing golf most days.

##### How to approach the case

##### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

##### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

##### Select the important presenting symptoms

This case very typically highlights the sort of presentation seen in elderly care. There is more than one thing going on, and it is important to keep an open mind about previous opinions and focus on what are important and potentially threatening symptoms now. These are undoubtedly tiredness (likely to be the result of anaemia) and weight loss.

##### Take a thorough systematic enquiry for causes of weight loss (Box 2.112)

Take a full systematic enquiry but pay particular attention to:

Gastrointestinal symptoms - abdominal pain, nausea and vomiting, altered bowel habit (diarrhoea, constipation), gastrointestinal bleeding

Steatorrhoea - type 2 diabetes is occasionally the result of pancreatic infiltration

Prostatic symptoms - difficulty getting started, thin stream, nocturia, incomplete emptying, frequency

Adrenal insufficiency symptoms - dizziness, nausea and vomiting.

##### Decide what to do with less urgent symptoms and problems

The joint symptoms might be less pressing than the abdominal mass and weight loss.

##### Patient perspective

Establish if he has had any thoughts about what his symptoms might represent. 

#### Past medical history

There are no clues here.

#### Drug and allergy history

High-dose corticosteroids can mask symptoms of a wide range of diseases including cancer. Metformin is of course anorexiagenic.

#### Family history

Ask about a family history of colorectal cancer.

#### Social history

As well as checking his alcohol and smoking history, aim to discover how his life has changed in the last few months, what he cannot do now that he could, any difficulties that might be posed by investigation (given recent family circumstances) and how he and his wife are currently managing.

## Discussion

### Is the C-reactive protein (CRP) usually raised in cancer?

Inflammatory markers are often normal, and if elevated seldom very high, especially CRP (CRP often under 50); the plasma viscosity or erythrocyte sedimentation rate may be elevated significantly in certain cancers such as renal cell cancer and some lymphoproliferative states, and in metastatic disease, but low or normal levels should not provoke false reassurance.

### What are the commonest malignancies?

Leading cancers worldwide in approximate order of frequency are lung stomach, breast, colorectal, prostate, liver, oropharyngeal, gynaecological, oesophageal, leukaemia and bladder.

### How might you approach the problem of cancer of unknown primary (CUP) site?

CUP accounts for 3-9% of patients, and issues raised include whether or not to investigate, the primary site involved, the histological type, which investigations to consider, the prognostic implications of these investigations and possible treatments.

#### Presenting sites

The most common sites of presentation (excluding head and neck), with decreasing prevalence, are lymph nodes, bone, liver and other abdominal sites, lung and pleura, central nervous system, skin and adrenals. Identification of the primary site (as well as the histology and degree of spread) is central to management but needs to be tailored to the patient's likely prognosis and prospects for treatment.

#### Whether or not to investigate?

Exhaustive investigation of poor performance status patients is often counterproductive because of the diminishing likelihood of identifying a primary site, the increasing expense of continuing investigation and discomfort incurred with limited life expectancy. Even after post-mortem examination the primary site may remain unknown.

#### Primary sites - likelihood

Primary sites, in order of decreasing likelihood, are lung, pancreas, colorectal, renal, liver, gastric, prostate, ovary, thyroid, adrenal, breast and parotid.

#### Histological types

Adenocarcinoma is the most common histological type (over 50%). Next are poorly differentiated tumours, and minorities include squamous carcinoma, lymphoma, germ cell tumours, melanoma, sarcoma and neuroendocrine tumours. Squamous CUP usually presents with cervical lymphadenopathy, or rarely inguinal lymphadenopathy of disease originating in the anus, vulva or penis.

#### Investigations to localise the primary site

A panel of histochemical and genetic markers may help identify likely primary sites including thyroid transcription factor-1 for lung cancer, CK7 and CK20 cytorelaratius and CdX to differentiate between gastrointestinal, gynaecological and breast origin, oestrogen receptor and progesterone receptor for breast and endometrium and prostate specific antigen for prostate. Serum tumour markers are of low specificity and do not contribute to diagnosis.

Computed tomography (CT) has traditionally proved to be the most successful technique for identifying the primary site in patients presenting with adenocarcinoma of unknown origin. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is advantageous in the pelvis and in identifying occult breast cancers. Fludeoxyglucose positron emission tomography (FDG-PET) and PET-CT to detect occult primary carcinomas outside the head and neck region may be useful (24-71% sensitivity), better, for example, for lung than some tumours. The most frequent primary diagnoses following investigation are non-small-cell lung cancer and pancreatic cancer and not those with the greatest treatment potential. Breast, ovary and prostate are the more treatable origins of adenocarcinoma of unknown primary (ACUP). A primary breast cancer may be found in 40-70% of women presenting with isolated axillary lymph node metastases. MRI is a valuable adjunct to mammography and ultrasound. For most patients presenting with bone metastases, investigation with CT scan of chest, abdomen and pelvis is likely to reveal a primary source in the lung (24%), prostate (17%) or breast or gynaecological organs (16%).

### Prognostic factors

The overall median survival for all patients with CUP varies from 12 to 22 weeks, a better prognosis defined by disease in limited nodal sites or a single site of metastasis, performance status 0-2, weight loss less than 10% and absence of liver metastases on CT.

Patients commonly present with liver metastases. Chemotherapy directed at tumours of gastrointestinal origin can result in a median survival of 7 months, depending upon performance status and serum lactate dehydrogenase levels. Specific chemotherapy regimens even for these traditionally less treatable cancers and other adenocarcinomas of the gastrointestinal tract that present as CUP can give good palliation for 6-12 months. Multiple small-volume lung metastases due to thyroid carcinoma are rare, but response to treatment with radioiodine can often be achieved. Patients with central nervous system disease from CUP have a poor prognosis.

### Possible treatments

Poorly differentiated cancers can be the most treatable, and chemotherapy curable. A distinctive subgroup of patients with poorly differentiated or ACUP have characteristics that differ from patients with well-differentiated adenocarcinoma - a younger age, rapid progression of symptoms (< 30 days) and tumour growth, and tumour expression of germ cell markers a-fetoprotein or human chorionic gonadotropin; lymph nodes within the mediastinum and retroperitoneum are often more infiltrated. This subgroup may include a high proportion of germ cell tumours that may be more responsive to chemotherapy and potentially curable.

Most malignant lymph node masses in the neck are metastatic, arising from primary head and neck tumours (squamous carcinoma of cervical or supraclavicular nodes), although primary lung cancer should be suspected when the lower cervical or supraclavicular lymph nodes are involved. The prognosis here has improved with modern surgery and chemoradiotherapy.

The management of patients presenting with lymph-adenopathy due to metastatic melanoma is primarily surgical. They should be staged by PET-CT scan if the disease is clinically confined to a resectable nodal basin in order to exclude distant metastases. The prognosis following lymphadectomy can be excellent, with 55-58% 5-year survival. The management of isolated deposits of metastatic melanoma in other sites such as lung is also primarily surgical.

If after all efforts the primary site has not been identified, palliative chemotherapy treatment with combination chemotherapy such as carboplatin, gemcitabine and capcitabine can achieve responses in 20-40% of selected patients.

### How would you approach the problem of febrile neutropenia?

Bone marrow suppression is usually the limiting factor of cytotoxic chemotherapy. Drug-induced DNA damage in bone marrow progenitor cells causes falls in neutrophil counts, and patients with neutropenia are at increased risk of sepsis. The timing of neutropenia is predictable; it typically lasts over a 7-14-day period with the nadir usually around day 10, but all patients should be considered to be at risk of sepsis if they have received chemotherapy within the past 28 days. The risk correlates with both the absolute neutrophil count and the duration of neutropenia: a low nadir (< 500/mm3) and protracted duration (> 10 days) are major risk factors.

### Defining febrile neutropenia

Febrile neutropenia refers to a single oral temperature of 38.3degC or above, or 38degC or above for 1 hour or longer, and a neutrophil count of 500/mm3 or less, or a count below 1000/mm3 and predicted to fall below 500/mm3 within 24-48 hours. Any chemotherapy patient presenting with a sustained fever (>= 37.5degC) and low neutrophil count (< 1000/mm3) should be treated as potentially septic.

### Common organisms

These are Gram-positives (mostly) including _Staphylococcus spp., Streptococcus pneumonia_, _Streptococcus tridans_, _Enterococcus faecalis_ and _Cornebacterium_ spp.,

identically. Initial intravenous therapy regimens vary - monotherapy with a broad-spectrum penicillin (piperacillin/tazobactam), third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin or penem are reasonable, and there is no clear advantage of combination therapy in most uncomplicated episodes. Glycopeptide therapy (vanomycin) may be important in cases with catheter-associated infection or mucosal injury but resistant enterococci limit its use to specific indications and not as empirical first-line therapy. The single most important determinant of discontinuation of antibiotics is neutrophil recovery. Colony-stimulating factors (granulocyte colony-stimulating factor) and granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor) stimulate neutrophil precursor recovery and can shorten the duration of neutropenia but there is no clear evidence that adding these agents to empirical antibiotics provides significant benefit although they may have a role in protracted neutropenia where the risk of secondary fungal infection is high, and in patients receiving potentially curative chemotherapy who have experienced a previous episode of severe febrile neutropenia. Prophylactic antibiotics to prevent infection remain contentious.

### What are the non-myelosuppressive complications of chemotherapy?

Non-myelosuppressive complications of cancer therapies (specific targeted therapies as well as conventional cytotoxics) include the following.

#### Hypersensitivity and acute drug reactions

Mild hypersensitivity reactions without hypotension or bronchospasm are managed by stopping the drug, hydrocortisone and chlorpheniramine. More severe anaphylactic reactions require intensive management. Paclitaxel is notorious for hypersensitivity, requiring routine premedication and dexamethasone prophylaxis. Irinotecan provokes an acute cholinergic syndrome with sweating, diarrhoea, tachycardia and palpitations. Oxaliplatin can provoke an acute neuropathy.

#### Extravasation

Attempts should be made to aspirate back an extravasated drug. Ice packs should be applied and steroid creams reduce inflammation. Dexrazoxane is used to treat extravasation.

#### Tumour lysis syndrome

Tumour lysis syndrome is a well-recognised complication of treatment of acute leukaemia and high-grade lymphoma, and less commonly solid tumours. It provokes characteristic metabolic derangements - hyperuricaemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperosphataemia, hypocalcemia - as a consequence of rapid and massive lysis of tumours. These cause muscle weakness, cramps, paraesthesiae, paralysis and arrhythmias. Hyperuricaemia and hyperphosphataemia cause renal failure owing to urate and calcium phosphate crystal formation. Tumour lysis syndrome is treated with high fluid loads. Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase conversion of xanthene to urate and is used where there has been no allopurinol prophylaxis. Rasbruiscase is a recombinant urate oxidase that converts uric acid into allatonin, which is excreted, and is considered in established tumour lysis syndrome. Haemodialysis may be required. Hypocalcaemia should be corrected only if symptomatic, to prevent worsening calcium phosphate deposition.

#### Emesis

If emesis is uncontrolled and oral intake inadequate, intravenous fluid and electrolytes are needed. Highly emetiogenic regimens require pre-medication with high-dose steroids and 5-hydroxytryptamine 3 (5-HT3) antagonists, with follow-on oral steroids and oral anti-emetics such as domperidone or 5-HT3 antagonists. The NK-1 inhibitor appreprint or longer acting 5-HT3 antagonists such as palenosetron should be considered for refractory symptoms.

#### Gastrointestinal toxicity

Castrointestinal toxicity is second only to bone marrow toxicity. The mechanism is similar throughout the gastrointestinal tract, but symptoms depend on the site of damage. Oral mucositis or stomatitis commences about 5-10 days after starting chemotherapy, with erythema followed by ulceration and secondary infection, usually with candidiasis. Dysphagia, dyspepsia and nausea are common. Mucositis is a complex process of epithelial and submucosal DNA damage, rapid cell death, and activation of multiple cascades mediated through generation of reactive oxygen species. Treatment includes antiseptic and analgesic midbrain, and topical and systemic antifungals or antibiotics. Parenteral hydration or nutrition is occasionally required. Keratinocyte growth factor-1, a member of the fibroblast growth factor family, is a recombinant therapeutic agent that significantly reduces severe mucositis when used prophylactically in patients undergoing stem cell transplants. Diarrhoea is the commonest lower gastrointestinal complication, and treatment is usually supportive. Secondary infection can prolong it.

#### Cardiotoxicity

Anthracyclines are cardiotoxic (dilated cardiomyopathy), inducing direct and cumulative myocardial damage, but greater understanding of dose thresholds for cardiotoxicity and modern, less cardiotoxic anthracyclines have reduced this problem. Trastuzumab is a monoclonal antibody targeting the cell surface signalling molecule HER-2, which is overexpressed in some breast and other cancers; HER-2 is also important in cardiac development and in the response to cardiac injury and trastuzumab is cardiotoxic in a minority of patients. Tyrosine kinase inhibitors treat a wide range of cancers; lapatinib, an inhibitor of both HER-1 and HER-2, seems relatively safe but others that target various oncogenic signalling pathways may induce QTc prolongation. 

#### Renal toxicity

Cisplatin is notoriously nephrotoxic, and high fluid intake is required.

#### Skin toxicity

A non-specific dermatitis is very common with chemotherapy. Palmar-plantar erythodysaesthesia or hand-foot syndrome is common; it is due to interference with epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) function, characterised by palmoplantar erythema which can progress to painful desquamation. Skin toxicity associated with agents that interfere with EGFR function appears as an acneform rash predominantly affecting the trunk and face; agents such as cetuximab (a monoclonal anti-EGFR antibody), erlotinib and lapatinib (inhibitors of the intracellular tyrosine kinase activation region of EGFR) may provoke it.

What do you know about tumour angiogenesis in cancer and what strategies may be used to suppress it?

New blood vessel formation (angiogenesis) is a physiological process active in wound healing. It is tightly regulated by many pro- and anti-angiogenic factors. It is vital for cancer growth and metastasis, as tumours cannot grow beyond 200-300 mm in diameter unless they recruit new blood vessels to supply nutrients and oxygen.

#### Tumour angiogenesis - why it occurs

Cancer cells promote a state of pro-angiogenic predominance. Hypoxia as tumour cells outgrow their available blood supply is one reason, with production of hypoxim inducible factor-1 (HIF-1), which controls expression of several genes important in cellular adaptation to hypoxia including vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), endothelial growth factor and platelet-derived growth factor. Tumours with high EGFR expression include head and neck, renal cell, non-small-cell lung cancer, glioma, ovarian, pancreatic, colorectal, breast and gastric. Amplified signalling through phosphoinositide 3-kinase and its downstream target, mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR), enhances HIF-1-dependent gene expression. Another reason for pro-angiogenesis is changes in cell-to-cell interactions that occur in the highly proliferative state, augmented by the inflammatory and immune responses to cancer; further, specific clones of cancer cells with oncogenic mutations that stimulate production of pro-angiogenic factors or deletion of tumour suppressor genes that suppress angiogenesis are positively selected.

#### Tumour angiogenesis - molecular processes involved

VEGF-A is the prototype and most important member of the VEGF family of growth factors, which, together with their receptors, form one of the major pathways in tumour angiogenesis. In this family there are numerous individual growth factors (VEGF A-E and placental growth factors 1 and 2) and tyrosine kinase (TK) receptors (VEGF 1-3, VEGFR 1 and 2 promoting angiogenesis and VEGFR 3 lymphangiogenesis). VEGF TK receptors (VEGFR) have an extracellular ligand-binding domain to which the growth factors attach with an intracellular TK part which activates protein kinase C downstream, thereby interacting with DNA in the nucleus. Neuropilin 1 and 2 are cell surface proteins that bind VEGF and may act as co-receptors. Other factors in angiogenesis and interacting with the VEGF pathway are fibroblast growth factor-2, which induces neovascularization, platelet-derived growth factors (PDGF) expressed by activated endothelial cells, angiopoietin-1 and transforming growth factor a which impact on PDGF function and regulate endothelial integrity, and the Delta/lagged-Notch system, one of whose ligands, Delta-like ligand 4 (DLL4), is strongly expressed in endothelial cells. New blood vessels predominantly sprout from established vasculature or intunsuscpetion when periodontitisheli cells divide an existing vessel into two. Tumour vessels tend to be tortuous, chaotic with poor lumen formation, and have structurally immature leaky walls with reduced cell coverage, their endothelial cells tend to be more dependent on VEGF for survival.

#### Treatments to suppress tumour angiogenesis

Endothelial cells that make up tumour vasculature are essentially 'normal' cells with a stable genome which reduces the chances of resistance to treatment. Anti-angiogenic agents specifically target the growth factors or proliferating endothelial cells without affecting other organs. One approach is blocking the pro-angiogenic pathways such as the VEGF pathway (antibodies to the ligand VEGF, the receptor, TK inhibitors, etc.). The antibody bevacizumab binds VEGF and is beneficial in recurrent colorectal, breast, non-small-cell lung, liver and renal cancer. VEGFR TK inhibitors include sorafenib and sunitinib. The impact of anti-angiogenesis is currently not profound, and this might reflect the large number of growth factors involved in the complex process of angiogenesis and bypassing of VEGF inhibition.

### How might you diagnose and manage bone metastases?

Bone is an extremely common site of cancer metastases, and bone metastases are often painful. Hyperclacaemia and raised alkaline phosphatase levels can be a clue to diagnosis, but are not inevitable. Bone metastases can be detected on a bone scan (but myeloma, renal cell cancer and thyroid cancer characteristically producing lytic lesions that are missed), CT and MRI; plain X-ray films will show abnormalities only when at least half of the bone is involved, and pathological fracture and spinal cord compression should always be excluded. Treatment of metastatic bone pain may include analgesics, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and bisphosphonates, systemic chemotherapy or hormonal therapy in some cases, and surgery in some cases. Radiation therapy, as a single fraction, half-body treatment or as a systemic radiopharmaceutical, can provide effective pain relief with minimal side effects.

## Case 2.50 Tiredness

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic. Please read the following letter from the patient's general practitioner (GP). You may make notes on the paper provided. When the bell sounds, enter the examination room to begin the consultation.

#### Scenario

#### Re: Mr Christopher Brandon, aged 42 years

Thank you for seeing this gentleman with a history of tiredness and overall loss of energy for the last year. He has also been losing weight and feeling nauseated. He takes leuylorvnine and his thyroid-stimulating hormone and free thyroxine suggest adequate replacement. Examination is unremarkable save for a slightly tanned complexion but he says he has always had this. His wife agrees. His full blood count, urea, creatinine, liver function tests and inflammatory markers are normal. His serum sodium is 121 mmol/l and his potassium is 5 mmol/l. His random blood glucose is on the low side at 3.5 mmol/l. Please see and advise on further management.

#### Yours sincerely

Please take a history from the patient (you may continue to make notes if you wish on the paper provided). Your examiners will warn you when 12 minutes have elapsed. You have 14 minutes to take a history from the patient followed by 1 minute of reflection. There will then follow 5 minutes of discussion with the examiners. Be prepared to discuss solutions to the problems posed by the case and how you might reply to the GP's letter. You are not required to examine the patient.

### Patient information

Mr Christopher Brandon is a 42-year-old man who has been feeling tired for around a year. He used to be very active, but since losing his teaching job a few years ago has found he has less energy, with a considerable decline in the last year. He now feels marked waning of energy and some dizziness on standing. He has lost a little weight but attributes this to nausea. He lives with his wife, is a lifelong non-smoker and seldom drinks alcohol. He has always tanned quickly, and is not sure if this has changed over the last year. He takes levothyroxine for an underactive thyroid but his tests indicate adequate dosage. He had a large operation for a maxillary sinus tumour 15 years ago followed by cranial radiotherapy, but was later told he was cured. Recently, his GP thought he might be depressed but he does not feel it. He and his wife would simply like to get back to 'doing things' again.

## How to approach the case

### Data gathering in the interview and interpretation and use of information gathered

#### Presenting problem(s) and symptom exploration

Patients may complain of feeling 'tired all the time', having 'no energy' or feeling 'exhausted'. It is important to try to establish:

* What is meant by tiredness (e.g. a general feeling of fatigue after minimal activity, or weakness in specific muscle groups)
* Whether symptoms are present all the time or are episodic. If episodic, do they occur at specific times of the day (e.g. rheumatoid arthritis causes morning stiffness that improves with activity while mechanical back pain may be worse towards the end of a working day) or after specific activities (e.g. myasthenia gravis causes fatiguable weakness in specific muscle groups)?
* The duration of symptoms
* The mode of onset of symptoms (insidious or sudden, following a specific illness?)
* Symptoms that might suggest a specific cause (Table 2.118).

#### Patient perspective

Establish if he has had any thoughts about what his symptoms might represent.

#### Past medical history

The history of pituitary radiation is potentially highly relevant.

#### Drug and allergy history

Levothyroxine is probably blameless here.

#### Family history

Relevance here is unlikely.

#### Social history

As well as checking his alcohol and smoking history aim to discover how his life has changed as a result of his symptoms, notably what he cannot do now that he could.

## Discussion

### What is chronic fatigue syndrome?

This typically follows an upper respiratory tract infection with incomplete recovery. Many viruses have been implicated as possible causes. There are many possible symptoms, but profound fatigue, worsened by minimal physical or mental exertion, is central. Chronic fatigue syndrome is more common in females but there is no clear link with occupation or social class. Investigations are usually non-specific, although altered lymphocyte function, hypocortisolism and other altered blood parameters have been described in some patients. There may be full recovery, but symptoms may relapse and remit or persist as a chronic illness.

### What is melatonin?

This is an important hormone in sleep-wake circadian rhythm regulation, produced in the pineal gland, ensuring we are awake in the daytime and sleep when it is dark. Melatonin levels increase at night and help induce deep, restorative sleep. Sleep changes occur with age and melatonin therapy is sometimes used in older people with primary insomnia. In all people disruption in getting up and going to bed times, even by an hour (not to mention shift-workers), can be dramatically disruptive to circadian regulatory hormones.

### List some causes of hypoglycaemia

* Diabetic drugs
* Starvation

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} \hline
**Cause** & **Symptoms and clinical clues** \\ \hline Anaemia & Pallor & \\ Per rectal or vaginal bleeding & & \\ Diet & & \\ Hypothyroidism & Preference for warmth & \\ Weight gain & & \\ Dry skin and hair thinning & & \\ Obstructive sleep & Snoring & \\ apnoea–hypopnoea & Obesity & \\ syndrome & & \\ Heart failure & Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, paroxysmal & \\ nocturnal dyspnoea & & \\ Ankle swelling & & \\ Chronic lung disease & Dyspnoea & \\ Inflammatory bowel & Altered bowel habit with blood, mucus & \\ disease & or pus & \\ Diabetes mellitus & Polyuria and polydipsia & \\ Recurrent fungal infections & & \\ Addison’s disease & Postural dizziness & \\ Nausea & & \\ Pigmented skin creases & & \\ Vitiligo & & \\ Chronic kidney disease & Necturia & \\ Oliguria & & \\ Colorectal cancer & Altered bowel habit & \\ Per rectal bleeding & & \\ Weight loss & & \\ Lung cancer & Cough & \\ Haemoprysis & & \\ Weight loss & & \\ Smoking history & & \\ Myopathy/polymylagia & Persistent proximal muscle weakness & \\ rheumatatica & & \\ Myasthenia gravis & Fatigable weakness in certain muscle & groups \\ Periodic paralysis (very rate) & Episodic marked generalised weakness & \\ Depression & Depressed mood & \\ LOSS of enjoyment, interest or pleasure in activities & & \\ Diminished concentration & & \\ Change in weight or appetite & Sleep disturbance & \\ Suicidal ideation / attempts / specific plans & & \\ Aqitated behaviour or reduced activity & & \\ Chronic fatigue syndrome & Fatigue after minimal activity & \\ Antecedent viral illness & & \\ Iatrogenic, e.g. beta & & \\ blockers & & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 2.118: Symptoms that might suggest a cause of tiredness * Addison's disease
* Insulinoma

### How might you approach the problem of medically unexplained symptoms?

The term medically unexplained symptoms is one we all understand, but which may carry false implication that a doctor has failed to diagnose. There are many other terms for a similar notion. Somatisation refers to the physical manifestation of a psychological disorder, and the term somatomform is sometimes used. Hysterical conversion disorder refers to loss of function, whilst the term functional disorder is an often codified, pejorative one. Hypochondriasis refers to severe and unjustified worry regarding disease, whilst malingering refers to the deliberate invention of signs. Functional imaging has been shown to prove this in rare cases, but malingering is an exceptionally pejorative term if misapplied without proof. The term psychologically determined or generated symptoms seems a more acceptable, accurate description for many patients.

Symptoms may be diverse, and may be overemphasised as is common with tingling, or underplayed as is common with weakness when patients might display remarkable 'la belle indifference' to their paralysed arm, or may be multiple and disparate and not add up to any reasonable single pathology.

Examples of possible psychologically involved disorders include fibromyalgia and irritable bowel syndrome, in which it seems likely that symptoms reflect a variety of processes including misperception of normal physiology.

It is commonly underappreciated by doctors that symptoms are merely perceptions, contributed to by a combination of pathology and beliefs. Indeed, the vast majority of symptoms cannot be fully explained by the scientifically biomedical model.

A thorough history is essential, as is appreciation of background attitudes, not just of the patient, but of the doctor. Antennae for mismatch or inconsistencies, for example pain being reported but a facial expression suggesting otherwise, exaggeration, falsification and markers of intentionality such as a limb failing before counterpressure is applied.

These patients should not be seen as time wasting, despite there sometimes seeming to be a sense of futility and a fear that investigation might reinforce or consolidate the patient's notion of disease. However, if investigation is undertaken, it should be packaged to a patient as very likely to exclude the very small possibility of disease.

Alternative medicine tends to focus on symptoms rather than underlying causes, which may be one reason why it appears to be effective where symptoms have more of a belief foundation than a pathological one. Some doctors, too, appear to have a gift of healing almost akin to hypnosis, which may reflect their intuition and ability to influence patient's beliefs and so assuage symptoms.

The cardiovascular system is a system of the form of a system that is composed of a single system, a system that is composed of a

## Chest wall

* Look for a median sternotomy scar consistent with coronary artery bypass grafting, open mitral valvotomy or valve replacement, aortic valve surgery or mediastinal surgery.
* Look for a left lateral/inframammary thoracotomy scar consistent with closed mitral valvotomy.
* Look for the scar of (and palpable) a permanent pacemaker or implantable cardiac defibrillator.

## Palpation

### Arterial pulse

* Note the heart rate and rhythm.
* Palpate the radial or carotid pulse. Important abnormalities in arterial pulse character are shown in Table 3.5.

### Blood pressure

* Take (or ask for) the blood pressure. Ensure you have the correct cuff size. Obese patients need a large cuff; a standard cuff will exert greater pressure to compress the artery and give falsely elevated readings. The point at which you hear the first

Figure 3.1: Normal waveforms of the jugular venous pulse.

Figure 3.2: Measuring the jugular venous pulse.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{p{56.9pt} p{113.8pt} p{113.8pt}} \hline
**Waveform** & **When it occurs** & **What it represents** \\ \hline 'a’ & Presytolic & Venous distension due to right atrial contraction – blood, as well as entering the right ventricle, passes back through the open channel between the right atrium entrance and the jugular vein \\ \hline 'c’ (a flicker in the x descent) & Ventricular systole & Closure of tricuspid valve whose leaflets bulge back towards the right atrium during ventricular systole \\ \hline 'x’ & Synchronous with carotid pulse & Tricuspid valve drawing away from the right atrium as the right ventricle empties in systole (atrial relaxation also contributes to ’x’) \\ \hline 'v’ & _Not synchronous with ventricular systole_ & Venous return to right atrium whilst tricuspid valve still closed \\ \hline 'y’ & Precedes atrial contraction & Opening of tricuspid valve and blood rushing from the right atrium into the right ventricle just prior to ’kick’ of atrial contraction \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 3.3: Normal waveform of the jugular venous pressure Korotkoff sound is the systolic pressure and the point at which the sound disappears (fifth Korotkoff sound) is the diastolic pressure. The fourth Korotkoff sound (muffling) is acceptable in patients in whom sound does not disappear.

Be alert to a wide pulse pressure (aortic regurgitation) and a narrow pulse pressure (aortic stenosis). Be aware of the significance of differences in blood pressure between arms (leaking thoracic aneurysm) and greater than 15-20 mmHg between arms and legs, always palpat

\begin{table}

\end{table}
Table 3.4: Abnormal waveforms of the jugular venous pulse (IVP) ing for radio-femoral delay otherwise silent aortic coarctation is missed.

## Apex

* Aim to locate the apex beat with your middle finger, normally palpable in the fifth intercostal space at the mid-clavicular line, and determine if it is displaced (Fig. 3.3). An absent apex beat may be a sign of obesity, emphysema, pericardial effusion or dextrocardia, but it may be best palpable in the left lateral position. Many terms have been used to describe abnormalities in apex beat character (Box 3.1).

## Right ventricular parasternal lift

* Also known as a right ventricular heave or a left parasternal heave, this is detected by placing your palm over the left lower parasternal edge (Fig. 3.3). It will palpably (and visibly) lift if there is right ventricular hypertrophy, itself usually a consequence of pulmonary hypertension of any cause.

## Box 3.1 Apex beat abnormalities

* A _double impulse_ may be a sign of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
* A _tapping apex_ is the palpable equivalent of a loud first heart sound and should alert you to mitral stenosis.
* A _hyperdynamic_ or _thrusting apex_ should alert you to aortic regurgitation.
* A _sustained apex beat_ should alert you to aortic stenosis.
* A _heaving apex_ is a sign of left ventricular hypertrophy.
* Apex beat abnormalities can be difficult. As a rule of thumb, try to determine whether the apex beat is displaced or undisplaced, and whether or not it is forceful.

## Undisplaced apex

This is normal or implies pressure overload, as in aortic stenosis (raised left ventricular pressure with hypertrophy) and mitral stenosis (raised left atrial pressure with hypertrophy/ dilatation).

## Displaced apex

This implies volume overload, as in aortic regurgitation (left ventricular dilatation) and mitral regurgitation (left atrial and left ventricular dilatation).

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} \hline
**Pulse character** & **Causes** & **Description/what it represents** \\ \hline Normal & & The percussion wave is the dominant wave transmitted along elastic arterial walls \\  & & The dicrotic notch (difficult to detect) is a normal blip on the downstroke of the percussion wave representing aortic valve closure \\ \hline Slow rising, plateau pulse (“anarotic” pulse) & Aortic stenosis & Slow rising with delayed percussion wave and sometimes a palpable udger on the upstroke Characteristicsally associated with narrow pulse pressure (narrow difference between systolic and diastolic pressure) if severe \\ \hline Collapsing pulse & Aortic regurgitation & Very brisk upstroke followed by ‘collapse’ occurs when there is ‘run-off’ from the aorta \\  & Patent ductus & Characteristicsally associated with wide pulse pressure (wide difference between systolic and diastolic blood pressure) if severe \\  & & disease, severe anaemia; a small-volume collapsing pulse (quickly rising but small percussion wave) is associated with ventricular run-off states, e.g. mitral regurgitation, ventricular septal defect & May be visible at brachials or carotids and best felt by raising the patient’s arm, feeling the radial pulse ‘slap’ against your fingertips or palm \\ \hline Pulsus alternans & Aortic stenosis & Alternating large and small beats \\  & & Left ventricular failure & A sign of poor left ventricular function \\ \hline Pulsus paradosus & Cardiac tamponade & Excessive fall in pulse pressure (\textgreater{} 10 mmHg) during inspiration \\  & Acute severe asthma compromising venous return & Not, in fact, a paradox, but an exaggeration of normal physiology \\  & & Difficult to detect \\ \hline Jerky pulse & Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy & Ventricular ejection in ‘stops and starts’ \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 3.5: Abnormalities in arterial pulse character 

### Palpable heart sounds and murmurs ('thrills')

* Feel for any thrills. Thrills are palpable murmurs (like a purring lion cub) usually caused by blood being squeezed through a narrow aperture. They are the tactile manifestation of murmur energy. A systolic thrill at the apex suggests mitral regurgitation. An upper parasternal thrill suggests aortic stenosis (right parasternal edge) or pulmonary hypertension (left parasternal edge).

### Predicting valve abnormalities before auscultation

One of your aims in cardiovascular examination to this point should be to try to predict any valve abnormality before auscultation. Ideally, auscultation should confirm already aroused suspicions. Common examples are given in Table 3.6.

## Percussion

Percussion of the cardiac border or cardiac dullness adds little to clinical assessment. Omit unless other signs suggest the presence of a pericardial effusion.

## Auscultation

### Auscultation sites

* Start at the apex with the diaphragm followed by the bell of your stethoscope, then move through the areas shown in Figure 3.4 with the diaphragm. The bell is a resonating chamber that amplifies low-pitched sounds, such as the diastolic murmur of mitral stenosis or a third or fourth heart sound. If not applied lightly it becomes a diaphragm.
* Next listen at the _apex_ with the patient in the _left lateral position_ and at the _aortic and pulmonary areas_ with the _patient sitting forward_. Which you do first depends upon where you think the pathology lies, but be seen to do both. _In both positions listen in expiration ('breathe in, and out, and hold your breath... and breathe again') and in inspiration if you suspect a right-sided (pulmonary or tricuspid) murmur_.

Inspiration increases the loudness of right-sided murmurs and expiration the loudness of left-sided murmurs.

### Heart sounds

Heart sounds are vibrations caused by closure of heart valves combined with rapid changes in blood flow and tensing within cardiac structures.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c} \hline \hline
**Pulse character** & **Pulse pressure** & **Apex position** & **Diagnosis** \\ \hline Normal (may be atrial fibrillation) & Normal & Undisplaced & Mitral stenosis (or normal!) \\ \hline Large volume & Wide & Displaced & Aortic regurgitation \\ \hline Small volume & Narrow & Undisplaced & Aortic stenosis \\ \hline Normal & Wide/normal & Displaced & Mitral regurgitation \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 3.6: Predicting valve abnormalities before auscultation

Figure 3.3: Determining the position of the apex beat (A) and palpating for a right ventricular heave (B). * Listen for the first and second heart sounds, and any extra third or fourth heart sound.
* **First heart sound (S1).** S1 (_'lub'_) represents mitral (and to a lesser extend tricuspid) valve closure at the onset of ventricular systole. Only one sound is audible. The intensity of S1 may be altered (Table 3.7) by the position of the mitral valve leaflets at the onset of systole, the rate of rise of the left ventricular pressure pulse, the amount of tissue, fluid or air between valve and stethoscope, and mitral valve disease.
* think of the efficient left ventricle contracting swiftly, offloading its stroke volume compared with the lower pressure within the right side of the heart.

There are four types of S2 splitting (Table 3.8).

The intensity of S2 may be altered by disease (Table 3.9).

* **Third heart sound (S3).** This is described in Box 3.2.

### Added sounds

* Listen for added sounds (Box 3.3).

### Murmurs

Heart murmurs result from vibrations in the bloodstream and surrounding heart and great vessels as a result of turbulent flow.
* Try to determine the character, location and radiation of any murmur you hear (Box 3.4).
* Try to grade the intensity or loudness of any murmur you hear (Table 3.10).
* Try to determine the timing (with the carotid pulse) and duration of any murmur you hear (Table 3.11). Whilst this is expected by examiners and straightforward in theory, many candidates find timing difficult; what sounded like the typical whispering decrescendo murmur of aortic regurgitation suddenly becomes mitral regurgitation on the basis of dubious timing when all the evidence is with the former and against the latter. Keep in mind that the character of a murmur is more compellingly helpful than anything else when you might otherwise 'hedge your bets.' 

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} \hline \hline
**Type of splitting** & **Explanation** & **Causes (all splitting best heard at left sternal edge with diaphragm)** \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 3.8: Types of 52 splitting 

### Murmurs and physiological manoeuvres

**Murmurs and respiration.** Right-sided heart murmurs are louder in inspiration because in inspiration blood flow increases through the right side of the heart. Left-sided heart murmurs are louder in expiration because in expiration blood flow increases through the left side of the heart.

**Murmurs and dynamic auscultation.** Subtle murmurs may be enhanced by certain manoeuvres (Table 3.12). Tell the examiners that you would consider them but never ask a patient to perform these unless directed to do so by examiners.

## Additional examination

Complete cardiovascular system examination includes assessment of lower limb pulses and examining for pulmonary, sacral and lower limb oedema.

## Chapter 3.1 MIRAL STENOSIS

### 3.1 Introduction

This 65-year-old woman is thought to have a heart murmur. She is short of breath on exercise. Please examine her pulse, and palpate and auscultate her heart. Tell the examiners the signs you elicit, and discuss your proposed management.

## 3.2 Recognition

Signs of mitral stenosis are shown in Figure 3.5.

### 3.3 Confirm the diagnosis

Listen in the left lateral position and in expiration to accentuate the murmur. The differential diagnosis includes:

* Tricuspid stenosis (rare, louder in inspiration)
* Atrial myxoma (rare, fever, constitutional symptoms, raised inflammatory markers)
* Carey Coombs murmur (acute rheumatic fever)
* Austin Flint murmur (see Case 3.4).

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c c}
**Manoeuvre** & **Effect on murmur** & **Louder and longer** & **Louder because** \\
**Valsalve strain phase** & **Softer and shorter** & **Louder and longer** & **Louder because** \\
**During the strain phase (forceful expiratory pressure against a closed glottis)** & **bloed is flowing** & **earlier click** & **filling in** \\
**there is raised intrathoracic pressure leading to reduced right and left ventricular filling (reduced preload to both ventricles), this leads to reduced cardiac output and blood pressure falls and heart rate rises to compensate.** & **because** & **diastole** \\
**_Note from Table 3.8 that reduced intrathoracic pressure in inspiration augments venous return, the opposite rule to the strain phase of Valsalva, but that both inspiration and the strain phase of Valsala lead to a fall in blood pressure and cardiac output – in inspiration the negative pressure in the lungs’sucks’ blood from the right side of the heart and ’holds’ it back’ from entering the left side of the heart (blood pools in the pulmonary vessels during inspiration for oxygenation purposes); in the strain phase of Valsala, the positive intrathoracic pressure impedes venous return into the thorax, and reduced delivery of blood to the left side of the heart is the natural consequence. Thus in both inspiration and the strain phase of Valsala_ & **the heart is the natural condition at birth at a fall in blood pressure and rise in heart rate, although stretch receptor and baroreceptor autonomic responses contribute to these responses in inspiration and the strain phase of Valsala respectively** & **the Valsala** 
Encountering a 'full-house' of signs is improbable and even when present they are difficult to elicit. Many physicians say they have never heard an opening snap. Atrial fibrillation renders a murmur difficult to time. But the rumbling murmur is characteristic, not unlike heavy rain on a tin roof, the rumble of a subway train or a buffalo stampede!

### What to do next - consider causes

Tell the examiners that causes of mitral stenosis include:
