### Is there a role for neuroimaging in Parkinsonism?

Computed tomography (CT) can be useful in excluding other conditions, such as cerebrovascular disease, and normal pressure hydrocephalus in patients with an early gait disorder. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can sometimes be useful if MSA or PSP is suspected but there is poor sensitivity for the changes suggesting these conditions. Functional imaging with single photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) uses the tracer FP-Cit, otherwise known as a DaT scan. The tracer binds to dopamine transporters on presynaptic dopaminergic neurons and gives an indirect measure of dopaminergic function. It may be used to distinguish Parkinson's disease from essential tremor; in the latter the scan is normal but in Parkinson's disease loss of the 'tail of the comma' is seen bilaterally, representing putaminal degeneration. A DaT scan is capable of distinguishing these two conditions with 97-98% sensitivity and specificity. It cannot reliably distinguish Parkinson's disease from other Parkinsonian syndromes, since it only evaluates integrity of the nigrostriatal pathway, which is affected in all Parkinsonian syndromes, although neuron loss is more likely to be asymmetrical in Parkinson's disease. Interestingly, an increasingly recognised clinical method of distinguishing essential tremor from a Parkinsonian tremor is the thumb extension component in essential tremor to what may otherwise look like a typical Parkinsonian tremor.

### What do you know about non-motor symptoms in Parkinson's disease?

Many neurotransmitters are disrupted in Parkinson's disease, and the diverse array of structures involved, and therefore symptoms, is increasingly appreciated. They are summarised in Table 3.41.

### What are the stages of Parkinson's disease?

Parkinson's disease is a progressive disease requiring a multidisciplinary team approach. It is helpful to consider the four management stages proposed by MacMahon and utilised in National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence (NICE) guidelines:

* Diagnosis
* Maintenance treatment
* Complex disease
* Palliative care.

Diagnosis is around 18 months, usually with observation. It is usually important not to plunge into treatment quickly before getting an idea of the individual pattern of disease. Treatment should always be individualised. Once treatment is started, it is seldom stopped, and most patients receive maintenance treatment for around 5 years before entering the complex stage. Complex may mean more than two drugs or more than four doses or frequent drug changes or side effects. Patients in the complex stage generally attend Parkinson's disease or movement disorder clinics, where they may benefit additionally from multidisciplinary care, e.g. physiotherapists to help with gait and postural instability and activities of daily living, speech and language therapists to assist with speech and swallowing and occupational therapists to assess the need for domiciliary adjuncts. If a patient survives long enough, the priority may no longer be to increase drug doses but to maintain dignity. Important issues may include managing dementia, managing drug-induced psychiatric side effects and appropriate reduction in pharmacotherapy.

### What are the treatments in Parkinson's disease?

Broadly, the treatments for the motor symptoms centre on dopamine replacement and fall into three categories (Box 3.46).

### When should treatment in Parkinson's disease be started, and what agent should be used?

An optimum time to start treatment remains unclear, as does the best starting drug. Generally levodopa is used in older people, or those with comorbidity, and a non-ergot dopamine agonist in those who are younger and otherwise well. Enzyme inhibitors are generally add-on agents to levodopa.

The TEMPO trial with rasagiline (not compared with any other drug) was a delayed start trial, and the first of several ongoing trials as well as the PD Med trial on early treatment. The ADAGIO study demonstrated significant longer term improvement in those who commenced rasagiline at entry compared with those who started treatment later. However, the notion of any drug being neuroprotective is still without a clear evidence base, and further head-to-head studies of different agents as monotherapy in early disease are needed, although the use of a monoamine oxidase-B inhibitor in early disease is favoured by some because of the relative convenience of a once-daily drug and relatively few side effects.

### What impulse control disorders do you know of in Parkinson's disease?

These include gambling, hypersexuality and compulsive eating or shopping. Funding refers to perseverative behaviours such as removing clothes from the wardrobe and refolding them neatly, returning them to their usual place, and then doing this all over again; and sometimes again, and again,. Excessive hobby-ism, such as playing computerised chess, is also common. Dull, but there you have it. All of these are associated with aberrant or excess stimulation of dopaminergic reward systems, and with loss of control, continued engagement in such activities despite adverse psychological, social or financial consequences, and withdrawal resulting in craving. They are associated particularly, but not exclusively, with dopamine agonists. Dopamine dysregulation syndrome refers to compulsive use of escalating doses of levodopa despite adequate motor control (akin to substance addiction), 

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Neurocyschiatric symptoms & Depression/anxiety & Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (not with selegiline) \\  & Triyclic antidepressants (anticholinergic side effects) \\ Cognitive behavioural therapy \\  & Hallucinations/dementia & Review anti-Parkinsonian medication \\  & Cholinesterase inhibitors, e.g. rivastignine \\ Psychiatric support \\ Sleep disorders & Restless leg syndrome & Low-dose dopaminergic therapy \\  & Notcuminal akinesia & Modified release levodopa \\  & Periodic limb movements \\  & Insonnia \\  & Vivid dreams & Review anti-Parkinsonian medication \\  & Rapid eye movement sleep behaviour disturbance & Clonazepam \\  & Daytime somnolence & Modafinil \\  & Sudden sleep attack \\ Autonomic symptoms & Bladder disturbance (urgeny, notcuria, frequency) \\ Sweating \\  & Saliorrhoea & Encourage swallowing of saliva \\  & Speech and language therapy \\  & 1\% sublingual atropine ophthalmic solution \\  & Salivary gland botox injection or radiation \\  & Sexual dysfunction (hypersexuality/rectile \\ dysfunction) & Beware dopamine agonists \\  & Avoid alpha blockers \\  & Frectile dysfunction therapies \\  & Orthostatic hypotension & Modify medication \\  & Elevate head of bed \\  & Encourage fluid and salt intake \\  & Fludrotcortisone/midodrine \\ Gastrointestinal symptoms & Dysphagia & Speech and language therapy \\  & Videofluoroscopy \\  & dietitian \\  & Enteral or percutaneous gastrostomy feeding \\  & Choking \\  & Weight loss \\  & Nausea and vomiting \\  & Constipation & Adequate fluid intake/diet/exercise \\  & Laxatives/enemas \\  & Faecal incontinence \\ Sensory symptoms & Pain \\  & Paraesthesia \\  & Olfactory loss \\  & Blurred vision \\ Other & Falls & Multifactorial assessment \\  & Fatigue \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 3.41: Non-motor symptoms and strategies in Parkinson’s disease and may occur in isolation or be associated with impulse control disorders. Although now notorious, most patients do not experience impulse control problems and it may be that some who do have underlying dopamine receptor polymorphisms. Treatment is without an evidence base, although reduction, withdrawal or switching of dopamine agonist seems a sensible measure.

Why do patients with Parkinson's disease develop complications after a period of time on levodopa, and what are these complications?

Dopamine release is not continuous in normal brains, with background basal release and bursts on top, but these are not as dramatic as the swings after a few years of levodopa. Levodopa causes dopamine levels in the brain to swing up and down, leading to changes in phosphorylation and activation of receptors, particularly glutamate receptors, so that the basal ganglia fire in bursts rather than smoothly with non-physiological stimulation of postsynaptic dopamine receptors. This results in breakthrough periods of involuntary movements (dyskinesias) and wearing 'off' periods. Other factors such as disease duration and levodopa dosage are important.

### Why is there a period of smooth control before wearing off develops?

The brain is probably able to buffer for a while, surviving neurons able to take up levodopa, metabolise it to dopamine and release it. It may be that in time less neurons are able to do this and non-dopaminergic neurons also take up levodopa and release it more chaotically.

### When do patients treated with levodopa start to show signs of wearing off?

Half of all patients show signs within 5 years. The time-scale depends upon the patient, and younger patients with a genetic basis may develop motor complications within a year or two. Older patients seem less prone to the motor symptoms of dyskinesia and wearing off, but more prone to non-motor complications including cognitive impairment. This said, wearing off is probably under-recognised, especially in older people, and especially in those in care homes.

### What are the signs of wearing off?

Early signs of wearing off may be very subtle, whilst later signs are overt, with dramatic and unpredictable on-off phases.

### How may wearing off be minimised or prevented?

Longer acting levodopa preparations may have a role, with continuous dopaminergic stimulation. Some experts recommend combination therapy early in disease, providing 24-hour dopaminergic stimulation. Neuroprotective drug strategies theoretically should prevent dyskinesia and wearing off. None of these strategies are evidence based. The ELLDOPA study showed that higher daily doses of levodopa (600 mg) significantly increase the risk of dyskinesia, hypertonia, headache, nausea and infection. Adjuvant therapies should probably be considered at levodopa doses of around 400 mg daily.

### How may wearing-off symptoms be managed?

The most effective strategies to reduce motor fluctuations are probably deep brain stimulation of the subthalamic nucleus (STN-DBS), levodopa infusions, and apomorphine. Careful surveillance is needed from when patients are entering this new, more complex stage of illness. The aim is for smooth control of symptoms, but simply increasing the dose of levodopa will bring with it an inherent risk of dyskinesias, and levodopa-sparing therapies should probably be considered when patients are still in the maintenance phase of disease. In cognitive impairment and those without healthy looking MRI, STN-DBS poses greater surgical complications. DBS of the internal pallidum (GPi) may pose lower risk. Surgery may improve dopa-responsive symptoms, and will not improve patients beyond their best response to levodopa, except in a small number of patients with levodopa refractory tremor that can be helped by thalamic or subthalamic DBS (or gamma knife thalotomy in those at high risk of surgery).

### Which therapies are recommended for first-line adjuvant treatment?

The NICE guidelines allow most things. It is all about tailoring treatment to the individual, and sharing several drug options, with surgical therapies always in the back of the mind. Most dopaminergic drugs, like levodopa, can cause postural hypotension, confusion, hallucinations and nausea.

#### Catechol-O-methyl transferase (COMT) inhibitors

Entacapone must be given with each dose of levodopa, often as a combined preparation (Stalevo). It prolongs the action of levodopa, although some think this is only by half an hour to an hour or so. It is not always very effective, but this may reflect ineffective taking. Diarrhoea is a side effect.

#### MAO-B inhibitors

Selegiline and rasagiline are convenient as once-daily doses. The LARGO study showed that rasagiline reduces mean daily off-time and improves symptoms in levodopa-treated patients with motor fluctuations, an effect similar to entacapone. The LARGO-Ancillary Study showed that rasagiline also improved motor performance during the off period before the first morning dose of levodopa, probably because it has continuous clinical effect throughout the day and night from a once-daily dose. Rasagiline is generally well tolerated. Selegiline may be more likely to cause postural hypotension, confusion and hallucinations, but some say rasagiline is only better tolerated because it is less effective. Others are firm supporters of rasagiline and anecdotal evidence suggests it also improves gait and freezing disorders. Rasagiline appears to be gaining predominance.

#### Dopamine agonists

These are generally less well tolerated as adjuvant therapy in older people and should be avoided in confusion and dementia, which are exacerbated by these drugs.

#### Deep brain stimulation, continuous levodopa (Duodopa) and apomorphine

Deep brain stimulation is generally considered before Duodopa gel. Apomorphine can be very effective.

### How may morning motor function problems be tackled?

Dispersible levodopa can be used before a patient gets out of bed. A COMT inhibitor at night does not seem to be as useful as a MAO-B inhibitor. Some would use controlled-release levodopa. Some would use a dopamine agonist, but sleep disturbance is common. Some would consider surgery when off periods in the morning start to become very troublesome. 

### What do you know about the patient perspective in Parkinson's disease?

Parkinson's disease is a condition in which individualised care is vital, and patients should be engaged in day-to-day management. Unmet needs are common, impacting on day-to-day care, and a Parkinson's Disease Society survey, Life with Parkinson's Today - Room for Improvement, the largest survey of its type, found many people were not given clear information on diagnosis and treatment. Furthermore, many patients are still not referred for specialist assessment, despite NICE guidance that all need specialist input.

### What do you know about dementia in Parkinson's disease?

Parkinson's disease dementia (PDD) affects the vast majority of people with Parkinson's disease at 20 years and is distinguished from dementia with Lewy bodies by its time of onset. Both PDD and dementia with Lewy bodies have the pathological hallmark of Lewy bodies, but in PDD Lewy bodies progress subcortically to cortically from a Parkinson's disease background whereas in dementia with Lewy bodies they progress cortically to subcortically to produce early cognitive impairment and later Parkinsonism.

### What do you know about palliation in Parkinson's disease?

Many patients in care homes have Parkinson's disease, and these patients must have access to high-quality end-of-life care. It should be recognised that at all stages Parkinson's disease warrants symptom control and palliation, and the value of the array of therapists and specialist nurses is paramount.

### Case 3.33 Cerebellar disease

#### Instruction

Please examine this patient's speech and upper limbs and discuss your findings.

## Recognition

There is slurred speech dysarthria. There is _upper limb ataxia with an action tremor, inability to judge distance (dysmetria) with past pointing_ and _dysdiadocholinesis_.

### Interpretation

#### Confirm the diagnosis

Tell the examiners that you would examine for nystagmus and for heel-shin ataxia and for a wide-based gait.

#### What to do next - consider causes

Unilateral cerebellar disease causes ipsilateral signs. Midline (vermis) lesions cause midline or truncal ataxia. Cerebellar hemisphere lesions cause appendicular or limb ataxia. These principles may suggest whether cerebellar pathology is unilateral or bilateral, midline, hemispheric or pancreebellar. This should narrow the differential diagnosis to structural lesions or more generalised insults. Causes of cerebellar disturbance are shown in Figure 3.76.

#### Consider severity / decompensation / complications

This depends upon the underlying cause.

Figure 3.76: Cerebellar ataxia. 

[MISSING_PAGE_POST] 

### What to do next - consider causes and assess other systems

An _anterior cord syndrome_ refers to complete motor paralysis (corticospinal tracts) and sensory anaesthesia (spinothalamic tracts). _Brown-Sguard syndrome_ is an _incomplete_ cord syndrome. A _central cord syndrome_ is typically due to an extension injury to an osteoarthritic spine; the central grey matter is mostly affected and motor loss tends to be greater in the arms than the legs (fibres to the lower body are more peripheral) with variable sensory sparing. Cord lesions may be non-compressive or compressive.

Lesions which can appear to be spinal because of tetraplegia but are not include bilateral cortical lesions, para-sagittal meningiomas and syringomyelia/bulbia.

#### Non-compressive spinal cord disorders

These are outlined in Table 3.42.

#### Compressive spinal cord disorders

Extradural lesions include cervical spondylosis (myelo-thy or radiolopathy), cervical/lumbar disc prolapse, tumours and trauma. Intradural lesions may be intramedullary such as meningiomas or neurofibromas or extramedullary such as astrocytomas.

### Consider severity/decompensation/complications

Tell the examiners that you would ask about sphincter disturbance (bladder or bowel symptoms, urinary catheter) and initially exclude acute cord compression, a neurosurgical emergency.

### Consider function

Tell the examiners you would determine the level of disability and, as well as identifying and correcting any correctable cause, work closely with physiotherapy and occupational therapy colleagues to optimise function.

## Discussion

### How does the blood supply to the spinal cord influence clinical features of infarction?

The anterior cord is supplied by the anterior spinal artery, artery of Adamkiewicz, a radicular artery that contributes significantly to the lower thoracic and lumbar cord and multiple radicular arteries. The posterior cord is supplied by the posterior spinal arteries supplemented by posterior radicular arteries. Infarction may occur in watershed areas between the anterior and posterior systems, e.g. after cardiac arrest. The dorsal columns are invariably preserved, with normal proprioception and sensation of vibration and light touch.

### What is the prognosis for recovery in spinal cord infarction?

This is generally poor if there is no improvement in the first 48 hours.

### Where do spinal arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) tend to occur?

Dural AVMs are mostly thoracolumbar, and lie in the spinal dura and nerve roots, supplied by branches of the intercostals and lumbar arteries.

### What is spinal muscular atrophy?

This is the second commonest hereditary neuromuscular disorder after Duchene muscular atrophy (1 in 25 000 live births). It has many forms, localised to the _SMN_ gene on chromosome 5, which has a role in RNA processing. Spinal muscular atrophy is classified according to age (childhood or adult), distribution (proximal, distal, bulbar) and mode of inheritance (dominant, recessive, X-linked).

### What is hereditary spastic paraparesis?

This is a typically, but not exclusively, an X-linked recessive disease developing at any age with difficulty walking. Paradoxically, the earlier the presentation the milder is the disease. The spastin gene is one of numerous that can be at fault.

### Is myelopathy common in human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)?

It occurs clinically in 7% of patients and at post-mortem in 50%, and may be vacuolar or multinucleate giant cell, the former more common and a marker of late disease (CD4 count < 200/ml). The mechanism is uncertain and may involve microglial neurotoxicity, gp120 protein myelin toxicity or B12 deficiency. There is no treatment. Myelitis may occur at seroconversion. Human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1), endemic in parts of the world, is transmitted similarly to HIV and may cause insidious spastic paraparesis, peripheral neuropathy, ataxia or optic atrophy. HIV may also cause myelitis via a range of opportunistic infections, usually with marked immunosuppression.

### What investigations might you consider when investigating a spinal cord syndrome?

These include magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), cerebrospinal fluid examination for microscopy and culture, protein, glucose, oligoclonal bands and polymerase chain reaction testing (for viruses), electrophysiology, erythrocyte sedimentation rate and autoimmune profile, B12, viral serology and syphilis serology (if suspicion).

### How might MRI further the diagnosis?

It can exclude cord compression and sometimes identify signal change consistent with myelitis. Cord atrophy is possible in neurodegenerative and nutritional causes, and signal change may occur in subacute combined

## Recognition

The slowly expanding central canal of syringomyelia results in those signs in Figure 3.78.

* The spinothalamic tracts are always affected because their fibres cross near the centre of the cord leading to _loss of hot/cold discrimination and pain sensation in a 'cape' distribution_. This is often the earliest sign.
* There is bilateral _wasting and weakness of the small muscles of the hands_ due to bilateral compression of T1 ventral nerve roots. The syrinx generally originates in the cervical cord. Other anterior horn cells and nerve roots are affected as it elongates, resulting in _loss of upper limb reflexes and flacedi tone_.
* The dorsal columns are spared because they are too far from the site of disease. Because vibration, joint position and fine touch sense are preserved but pain and temperature sense are lost, the sensory loss is referred to as _dissociated sensory loss_.
* The corticospinal tracts may rarely be affected in late disease, resulting in spastic paraparesis or tetraparesis.

## Interpretation

### Confirm the diagnosis

Diabetes (foot and ankle joints) and tubes dorsalis (knee joints) can also cause Charcot's joints. The clue to syringomyelia should be the dissociated sensory loss.

### What to do next - assess other systems

Other features that may be present are Horner's syndrome, Charcot's joints and other skeletal abnormalities:

* Horner's syndrome is common because the syrinx disrupts the sympathetic nerve fibres, especially at C8, T1
* In longstanding disease loss of pain sensation causes Charcot's joints
* Other skeletal abnormalities include kyphoscoliosis and cervical ribs. _La main succinate_ is the term given to the cold, swollen, dystrophic hands that may occur in syringomyelia as a result of autonomic vasomotor disturbance.

### Consider severity /

#### decompensation / complications

In syringobulbia the syrinx originates in the brainstem or extends upwards into it. There may be:

* Facial dissociated sensory loss (the decussating pain and temperature fibres from the descending trigeminal nucleus are affected)
* Wasting and weakness in the bulbar muscles giving rise to a bulbar palsy
* Nystagmus and cerebellar ataxia.

### Consider function

Tell the examiners you would determine the level of disability and, as well as identifying and correcting any correctable cause, work closely with physiotherapy and occupational therapy colleagues to optimise function.

## Discussion

### What is the differential diagnosis of syringomyelia?

Springomyelia is rare and has an equal sex incidence and an age of onset in early middle age. Intramedullary tumours of the spinal cord may cause a similar clinical picture to syringomyelia.

## Case 3.36 Absent ankle jerks and extensor plantars

### Instruction

Please examine this patient's legs (you may be asked specifically to examine reflexes and sensation) and discuss your findings.

## Recognition

This implies no more than a combination of lower and upper motor neurone disease, described classically in:

* Subacute combined degeneration of the cord
* Taboparesis
* Friedreich's ataxia.

But most commonly these signs are visible when two common conditions occur together, e.g. cervical myelopathy causing extensor plantar responses and diabetes causing peripheral neuropathy with loss of ankle reflexes. Motor neurone disease and lesions at the conus medullaris may also cause a combination of upper and lower motor neuron signs.

## Interpretation

### Confirm the diagnosis

A positive Romberg's sign (unsteadiness with the eyes closed or sensory ataxia) confirms dorsal column disease.

### What to do next - consider causes

The three classic causes are differentiated by the clinical signs in Table 3.43. Though classic, they are not inevitable. It is perfectly possible, for example, to have Friedreich's ataxia with flexor plantars and without dorsal column involvement. * [19] A. 

### Consider severity /

#### decompensation / complications

The spectrum of severity in each of the conditions is highly variable. Note in the final row of Table 3.43 the additional complications that may occur.

#### Consider function

Tell the examiners you would determine the level of disability and, as well as identifying and correcting any correctable cause, work closely with physiotherapy and occupational therapy colleagues to optimise function.

## Discussion

### What causes subacute combined degeneration of cord (SACDC)?

Vitamin B12 is a co-enzyme for transmethylation in DNA and myelin protein synthesis. Vitamin B12 deficiency may cause an array of insidious neurological problems including SACDC, pure peripheral neuropathy (often precedes SACDC) and dementia.

### What are the stages of syphilis?

These are outlined in Box 3.48.

Syphilis is caused by the spirochaete _Treponema pallidum_, which can reach the central nervous system early in the course of infection, even in the primary stage. In meningovascular syphilis, leptomeningeal exudates, granulomatous and arteritis produce progressive spastic paraparesis and radicular pain is common. In tubes dorsalis, a cellular reaction in the dorsal roots causes retrograde sensory loss up to 20 years after infection; characterised by lancinating pain that may be viscerally sited, then hypoalgesia.

### How is syphilis diagnosed?

Dark-ground microscopy (DGM) allows prompt diagnosis of syphilis. Polymerase chain reaction for _Treponema pallidum_ in genital ulcers is available but should not replace DGM or serological tests (various). Two groups of serological tests are available, treponemal or specific (enzyme immunoassay to detect immunoglobulin G/M, _T. pallidum_ haemagglutination assay, _T. pallidum_ particle agglutination assay, fluorescent terpenomel antibody absorbed test, _T. pallidum_ recombinant antigen line immunoassay) and non-treponemal or non-specific (Venereal Diseases Research Laboratory carbon antigen test, rapid plasma reagin test), outlined in Table 3.44. Patients with symptoms or signs of possible neurosyphilis should have cerebrospinal fluid examination.

### How is syphilis treated?

The treatment of choice is penicillin.

### What do you know about Friedreich's ataxia?

This constitutes 50% of all inherited ataxias. It is an autosomal recessive spinocerebellar degeneration (referring not to spinocerebellar tracts in isolation, but to degeneration of multiple tracts within the spinal cord together with cerebellar degeneration) caused by an increased number of GAA trinucleotide repeat sequences at a gene encoding the protein frataxin on chromosome 9-9q13. Diagnostic criteria alongside gene testing include progressive ataxia starting under 25 years of age, absent lower limb reflexes and electrodiagnostic evidence of axonal neuropathy.

### What are the spinocerebellar tracts?

These tracts convey unconscious proprioceptive information to the cerebellum. This is in contrast to the dorsal columns transmitting conscious sensation directly to the sensory cortex.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c c}
**EIA** & **TPPA** & **RPR** & **FTA** & **Diagnosis** \\ \hline + & – & – & + & Early primary syphilis \\ \hline + & + & – & + & Treated syphilis or untreated latent (early or late) syphilis \\ \hline + & – & + & + & Primary syphilis \\ \hline – & – & + & – & Biological false positive \\ \hline + & – & – & – & Likely false positive \\ \end{tabular}

Table 3.44: Serological tests in syphilis 

###### Abstract

The development of a new, high-resolution, UMN signs above the LMN signs. By the time patients meet criteria they are, by definition, at an advanced stage.

#### Red flags to diagnosis

* Sensory signs are not present in any form of MND.
* Motor neurons serving the extraocular muscles and pelvic floor are spared in all forms of MND so that ocular palsies and sphincter disturbance are never present.
* Weakness out of proportion to wasting or that is atypical in pattern should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses. Uncommonly MND may be associated with extra-motor features.

### What to do next - assess other systems

Tell the examiners that you would assess respiratory function (spironometry) and swallowing (speech and language therapist).

### Consider severity/

#### 3.1.1 decomposition/complications

Prognosis in MND is wide. Muscle wasting leads to progressive weakness, with respiratory function, swallowing and nutrition importantly affected. Death typically occurs 3-5 years from diagnosis, most often from respiratory failure.

Fifty per cent of patients with ALS die from respiratory failure, almost always peacefully, at a mean of 40 months from symptom onset, 20% are alive at 5 years and 10% at 10 years. Ten per cent of ALS is associated with a front-temporal dementia and the rare Guamanian variant has additional extrapyramidal features.

A pure LMN form of MND confined to arms or legs for many years has a better prognosis, whilst more generalised LMN forms can be rapidly progressive.

PLS is often associated with long-term survival of beyond 15-20 years.

Predictors of long-term survival are shown in Box 3.50.

### Consider function

This would be individually determined.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c c} Region & Brainstema & Cervicalt & Thoracic & Lumbosacralt \\ Body part & Face & Neck & Back & Back \\ \hline \multirow{2}{*}{Jaw} & \multirow{2}{*}{Arm} & \multirow{2}{*}{Abdomen} & Abdomen & Abdomen \\ \cline{3-5}  & & & & Leg \\ \cline{3-5}  & & & & Foot \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \hline \multirow{2}{*}{Lower motor neuron signs} & \multirow{2}{*}{Weakness} & \multirow{2}{*}{Weakness} & \multirow{2}{*}{Weakness} & \multirow{2}{*}{Weakness} \\ \cline{3-5}  & & & & \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \hline \multirow{2}{*}{Upper motor neuron signs} & \multirow{2}{*}{Clonic jaw} & \multirow{2}{*}{Brisk reflexes} & \multirow{2}{*}{Loss of abdominal reflexes} & \multirow{2}{*}{Brisk reflexes} \\ \cline{3-5}  & & & & \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \hline \multirow{2}{*}{Exaggerated snout reflex} & \multirow{2}{*}{Hoffman reflex} & \multirow{2}{*}{} & \multirow{2}{*}{Extensor plantars} \\ \cline{3-5}  & & & & \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \hline \multirow{2}{*}{Pseudobulour palsy} & \multirow{2}{*}{Spasticity} & \multirow{2}{*}{} & \multirow{2}{*}{Preserved reflex in weak and wasted} \\ \cline{3-5}  & & & & \\ \cline{2-5}  & & & & \\ \hline \multirow{2}{*}{Spastic tone} & \multirow{2}{*}{} & \multirow{2}{*}{} & \multirow{2}{*}{lower limb} \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 3.45: Upper motor neuron (UMN) and lower motor neuron (LMN) signs in the four central nervous system regions 

## Discussion

### How common is MND?

MND has an incidence of 1-2/100000 and a prevalence of approximately 6/100000. Onset is typically in late middle age. Increasing age, male sex (1.6:1) and the inheritance of a genetic susceptibility are the only proven risk factors. Approximately 90% is sporadic, the remainder inherited.

### What causes MND?

This is not known. About 10% of MND is familial and most familial MND is autosomal dominant. Mutations to a ubiquitous antioxidant enzyme, Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase (SOD1), are responsible for approximately 20% of inherited MND but the mechanism of how the mutations promote motor neuronal death is unclear. Overexpression rather than loss of enzyme function in microglia (the main immune cells of the nervous system) may cause disease. Other responsible mutations include TAR DNA-binding protein (TARDBP) and fused in sarcoma or FUS, both of whose gene products are likely to have roles in RNA processing. TARDBP 43 is the main component of the pathological hallmark protein inclusions found in surviving motor neurons in sporadic MND (also in front-temporal dementia and PMA). Astrocytes, cells which support neuronal structure and function, are responsible for reuptake of excitotoxic neurotransmitters such as glutamate, receptors for which may be aberrant in MND (the basis for riluzole therapy).

### Which investigations may be considered in diagnosing MND?

All suspected MND patients should undergo electrophysiological testing which allows identification of LMN features of MND in both clinically affected and as yet clinically silent regions. Typical electromyographic features include evidence of active denervation (positive sharp waves, fibrillation potentials, fasciculation potentials) and chronic denervation evidenced by large motor unit potentials. In pure LMN syndromes evidence of alternative diagnoses, such as conduction block and demyelination, is sought. Conduction block can be particularly difficult to demonstrate. Interval studies may be needed. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spinal cord should be done in all limb-onset ALS patients without bulbar involvement as mixed cord and root compression is responsible for some 30% of ALS misdiagnoses. When symptoms and signs are isolated to the bulbar region MRI can identify infiltrative lesions of the tongue and pharynx. Some differential diagnoses of MND are listed in Table 3.46.

### Summarise the management of MND

MND is best managed within specialist centres offering multidisciplinary care.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} \hline
**Form of MND** & **Mimic** & **Clues in differentiation** \\ \hline Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis & Multilevel spinal cord and root compression by discs, osteophytes or tumour & Sensory symptoms and pain common UMN often caudal to LMN signs MRI spine \\ \hline  & Combined peripheral neuropathy and cervical myelopathy & MRI spine and electrophysiology \\ \hline  & Paraneoplastic syndromes (especially lymphoma) & Computed tomography chest/ abdomen Tumour markers Cerebrospinal fluid studies \\ \hline  & Thyroidciosis & Non-motor symptoms and signs Thyroid function tests \\ \hline  & Indusion body myositis & Rare Pattern of weakness affects long finger flexors and quadriceps early Muscle biopsy \\ \hline  & Radiation myelopathy & History MRI spine \\ \hline  & Structural lesions of the bulbar region & Failure to progress beyond region MRI \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 3.46: Differential diagnoses of motor neurone disease (MND) 

#### Disease-modifying therapy

Riluzole is the only disease-modifying therapy in MND, evidenced in ALS. It reduces presynaptic glutamate release. Other neuroprotective effects are likely to contribute. On average riluzole extends life by 3-4 months although subsets of patients may benefit more.

#### Other therapy

This is outlined in Table 3.47. Nutritional and respiratory management of MND patients have an impact upon prognosis and require careful multidisciplinary specialist input.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c}
**Form of MND** & **Mimic** & **Clues in differentiation** \\ Progressive muscular atrophy & Chronic idiopathic demyelinating & Electrophysiology identifies demyelination \\ Multifocal motor neuropathy & Weakness out of proportion to wasting \\ NCS can identify conduction block & Anti-GM1 antibodies may be raised \\ Acute motor axonal neuropathy (a Guillain–Barré variant) & Acute onset and progression \\ Compressive focal neuropathies & Sensory involvement common \\ Failure to extend beyond territory of one nerve \\ Electrophysiology helpful \\ Lead poisoning & Extramator clinical features, e.g. constipation, nail and mouth signs \\ Lead levels \\ Porphyria & Extra-motor clinical features \\ Episodic & Family history \\ Stoel testing \\ Lyme disease & Non-neurological symptoms and signs, e.g. rash \\ Borrelia testing \\ Hereditary motor neuropathies & Pure LMN syndrome \\ Slower progression \\ Family history \\ Kennedy’s disease & Possible gynaecomastica, sensory symptoms or peri-oral fasciculations \\ Slower progression \\ Genetic testing \\ Spinal muscular atrophy & Can be adult-onset \\ Pure LMN syndrome \\ Slower progression \\ Genetic testing \\ Benign cramp fasciculation syndrome & Predominantly middle-aged men \\ Largely calf involvement \\ Failure to progress \\ No active denervation on EMG \\ Post-polio syndrome & Pure LMN syndrome \\ Past history or EMG confirms previous polio \\ Primary lateral sclerosis & Multiple sclerosis & Non-motor symptoms and signs, e.g. eye, bladder, cerebellar, sensory MRI brain and spine \\ Spinal cord compression & Sensory involvement and pain common \\ MRI spine \\ Hereditary spastic paraplegia & Prominent bladder symptoms \\ Family history \\ Genetic testing \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 3.46: Differential diagnoses of motor neuron disease (MND)—cont’d 

## Case 3.38 Cervical mJycloradiculopathy

### Instruction

Please examine this patient's arms and legs and then discuss your findings.

## Recognition

There is _weakness_ and _increased tone in the lower limbs_ with _enhanced reflexes_ and _extensor plantar responses_. There may be sensory impairment (compression of dorsal columns). There may be inversion of the biceps and supinator reflexes. In middle-aged to older patients the most likely diagnosis is cervical spondylosis.

### 3.51 Inverted reflexes

This is a phenomenon caused by combined spinal cord and root disease. It simply means absent reflexes at the segmental level of the lesion and enhanced reflexes below. With an inverted biceps reflex (C5/6 segment lesion), when the biceps tendon is tapped there is no biceps reflex but the triceps (C7) may be stimulated by the manoeuvre. In an inverted spinator reflex (C5/6) there is no spinator response but finger flexion (C8) may be seen to have been stimulated.

### 3.52 Inverted reflexes

This is a phenomenon caused by combined spinal cord and root disease. It simply means absent reflexes at the segmental level of the lesion and enhanced reflexes below. With an inverted biceps reflex (C5/6 segment lesion), when the biceps tendon is tapped there is no biceps reflex but the triceps (C7) may be stimulated by the manoeuvre. In an inverted spinator reflex (C5/6) there is no spinator response but finger flexion (C8) may be seen to have been stimulated.

In an inverted spinator reflex (C5/6) there radiculopathy but not myelopathy because the spinal cord has terminated at the lower level of the L1 vertebra.

## Case 3.39 CAUDA EQUINA SYNDROME

### Instruction

Please examine this patient's legs and then discuss your findings.

### Recognition

There is _flacid weakness_ in the lower limbs with _loss of the knee and ankle reflexes_ and _normal plantar responses_. There is _demandonal sensory loss below L1_. These findings suggest a complete cauda equina lesion.

### Interpretation

#### Confirm the diagnosis

A lesion in the spinal canal below the conus medullaris can cause a cauda equina syndrome. Because the spinal cord ends as the conus medullaris at the lower end of the L1 vertebra, the lumbar and sacral roots pursue a long course (unlike cervical roots) within the lumbar vertebral canal and can be injured anywhere within it to their intervertebral foramina exit sites. Cauda equina syndrome results from dysfunction of multiple sacral and lumbar nerve roots in the lumbar vertebral canal (rather than single root lesions), and by definition the term cauda equina syndrome is used only when dysfunction includes impairment of bladder, bowel or sexual function, and perinatal or 'saddle' numbness (Box 3.52).

Flacid weakness and reflexia in the lower limbs can be the result of Guillain-Barre syndrome. Tell the examiners that you would take a history for ascending symptoms over a few days. Over sensory loss is not a feature of Guillain-Barre syndrome.

### What to do next - consider causes/assess other symptoms

Cauda equina syndrome may be classified by location, presentation or clinically.

#### Location

* Midline, with bilateral symptoms
* Lateral, often with unilateral root symptoms

#### Presentation

* Acutely as the first symptom of lumbar disc herniation
* As the endpoint of a long history of chronic back pain with or without sciatica
* Insidiously with slow progression to numbness and urinary symptoms (primary or metastatic spinal neoplasms are often accompanied by marked pain, often chronic)

It is postulated that there is relative hypovascularity at the proximal portion of the cauda equina such that reduced blood supply from nerve root pressure here may be of greater importance than elsewhere, with rapid changes allowing less adaptation than those of a slower onset.

#### Clinically

* Cauda equina syndrome with urinary retention
* Incomplete cauda equina syndrome, with reduced urinary sensation, loss of desire to void,

Figure 3.80: Spinal cord, roots and compression. or a poor stream, but no established retention or overflow

Most clinicians now classify cauda equina syndrome clinically. Often the slower the presentation, the better tolerated the symptoms are and the less likely the patient to be alarmed. Those with pre-existing bladder problems resulting from other disease may also present late. Cauda equina lesions and their causes and clinical features are outlined in Table 3.48. Lumbar intervertebral disc prolapse causing isolated radiolopathies is very common. For example, the highly prevalent L4/5 and L5/S1 disc prolapses compress the L5 or S1 roots at their exit foramina (although these roots originated from their cord segment at a much higher level). The term cauda equina syndrome suggests more widespread damage arising within the canal involving multiple nerve roots and with associated sphincter disturbance and perianal numbness. Only a small number of herniated discs are likely to cause cauda equina syndrome, although because disc herniation is so common overall it is probably the commonest cause. Pain in the distribution of L4, L5 or S1 without sphincter disturbance or perianal numbess may reasonably be attributed to lumbosacral disc disease, but widespread root disturbance, root pain in unusual dermatomes or evidence of higher lumbar disc disease or lower sacral root damage is unusual, and should ring alarm bells to possible cauda equina syndrome.

### Consider severity/

#### decompensation/complications

Tell the examiners that you would ask about sphincter disturbance (bladder or bowel symptoms, urinary catheter) and initially exclude acute cord compression, a neurosurgical emergency. Tell the examiners that a full neurological examination (not to be performed in PACES) of the legs, and assessment of perianal sensation and of anal tone should be performed (Table 3.49).

### Consider function

Tell the examiners you would determine the level of disability and, as well as identifying and correcting any correctable cause, work closely with physiotherapy and occupational therapy colleagues to optimise function.

## Discussion

### What is the conus medullaris?

This is the terminal portion of the spinal cord, at vertebral level L1, and from it emerge the lower sacral roots.

### What is the cauda equina?

The spinal cord is shorter than the vertebral column, its roots sloping inferiorly from their levels of origin to their
