## Chapter 5.4 Blistering skin disorders 

## Case 5.5 Facial Rash

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

[1] Re: Ms Harriet Tortoise, aged 45 years

This woman is being investigated in your clinic for joint pains. She now has a facial rash.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 45-year-old woman being investigated in the general medical clinic for joint pains, the cause of which is not yet clear. Recently you developed a rash on your cheeks that appears to be made worse by sunlight. You have also noticed some pusults around the nose and are surprised to be developing what you believe to be acne. Your general practitioner prescribed some steroid cream, which seemed to make things worse.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about any symptoms
* Ask about the duration of the rash.
* Ask whether there is a rash elsewhere.
* Ask about any provoking (e.g. sunlight) and relieving factors.
* Ask about treatments.

#### Initial examination

* Be aware of the most likely causes of a facial rash listed below.

#### Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)

There is a _'butterfly distribution'_ _malar rash_ (Fig. 5.10). There are _raised or flat patches of malar erythema, sparing the nasolabial folds_. Other areas such as the forehead and neck are often affected. There may be marked _photosensitivity_. There may be _mouth ulcers_.

#### Discoid lupus erythematosus (DLE)

DLE is a skin-limited disease, with well-defined erythematous papules or plaques on light-exposed areas including the head, neck, hands and arms. Mostly affected are the cheeks, nose and forehead (Fig. 5.11). Like SLE, the rash is photosensitive, and more common in females. Lesions are scaling and hyperkeratosis of hair follicles gives these scales a dotted, 'nutmeg' appearance. If you were to remove the scales and inspect their undersurface, you would see spicules projecting from them. This is known as follicular keratosis or plugging (also known as the 'carpet track' sign) and no other scaling condition does this; it is specific to lupus. There may be patches of depigmentation. Once affected areas heal and the scab falls off, the skin beneath is often scarred, hypopigmented and atrophic.

#### Rosacea

There is an _erythematous, 'acneiform'_ _papular eruption_ on the _flush areas_ or convexities of the face - _forehead, nose, cheeks,

Figure 5.11: Discoid lupus erythematosus.

Figure 5.10: Malar rash. _chin_ (Fig. 5.12). The distribution is usually symmetrical; occasionally one side of the face is affected, and, rarely, other sites - the "V" of the chest, upper arms and back.

There are two main clinical components to rosacea, vascular (episodic flushing, erythema and telangiectasia) and inflammatory (erythematous papules and pusules). _Pusules_ within the eruption are almost pathognomonic. Later in disease, there is sebaceous hyperplasia, but seborrhoea and comedones (features of acne) are not features.

#### Seborrhoeic dermatitis

Seborrhoeic dermatitis is discussed in Case 5.2.

#### Dermatomyositis

There is a _heliotrope_ (referring to the distinctive iliac colour of the flower so named) _rash_ around the _eyelids_ (Fig. 5.13). It may also affect the malar region, limb extensors, knuckles and trunk.

_Gotton's papules_ may be present on the dorsum of the hands (notably metacarpophalangeal joints and inter-phalangeal joints) and occasionally elsewhere. _Nail-fold erythema_ due to _dilated capillaries_ (_telangiectasia_) is often present, and the cuticles may be ragged.

#### Lupus perrio

There are _purple_/_red_/_violaceous plaques on the nose, cheeks and earlobes with _telangiectasia over and around the plaques_ (Fig. 5.14). The plaques may have a yellowish translucency. They tend to flatten with time.

#### Lupus vulgaris

This is the skin manifestation of tuberculosis and is rare in the UK. The lesion has an 'apple jelly' consistency when a translucent slide is rested upon it. 

### Pulmonary hypertension

This is another cause of a malar rash.

#### Xanthelasmata

There are multiple _xanthelasmata_ (see Fig. 5.94, p. 870).

Other acute causes of facial erythema, unlikely in PACES, include ersipelas, acute flares of dermatitis, polymorphic light eruptions and phototoxic eruptions. And causes of swollen eyelids specifically include allergic contact dermatitis, nephrotic syndrome, hypothyroidism and superior vena cava obstruction.

### Further assessment

* Be aware of some distinguishing features between the facial rashes and of some important associations and complications of the facial rashes (Table 5.10).
* Explore concerns.

### Feedback to patient

I think you may have a condition called lupus. This is a condition in which the body's immune system and an almost 'acneiform' eruption on the flush areas of the face.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The 'butterfly' rash with photosensitivity and a history of joint pains would suggest lupus. However, the nasolabial folds are spared in lupus and the pusules here characteristic of rosacea [Box 5.2]. Adult onset 'acne' is not likely. Furthermore, whilst a lupus rash is helped by topical steroid, rosacea is often made worse, certainly on withdrawal.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

I would value the opinion of a dermatologist. Treatments for the rash of lupus include moderately potent topical steroids and hydroxychloroquine. Sunscreens are essential. A subacute form of skin lupus may occur in an older person who is photosensitive but provoked by drugs such as thiazides, calcium channel blockers, antifungals and biologicsis. However, for rosacea, corticosteroids should not be used. They are not effective in the long term and tend to cause skin addiction and 'flares' of severe rebound erythema, which do not easily settle. Avoidance of precipitating factors is seldom helpful except for the use of sunscreens. Topical treatments include metronidazole or erythromycin gel, with an embilient. Systemic treatments include tetracycline (often prolonged as low-dose maintenance treatment), minocycline and doxycycline. Although remissions with treatment occur, long-term treatment is usually needed. Topical tretinoin helps vascular features and, in severe cases, pulsed dye laser therapy is used.

### Case 5.6 SCLERODERA, VITILIGO AND AUTOIMMUNE SKIN DISEASE

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the cardiology ward. You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 79-year woman recovering in hospital from a heart attack. You seldom have cause to see a doctor but for some years have noticed that the skin over your hands, face and feet has become more tight and shiny, and you suffer considerably from cold hands and feet. You wonder what might be wrong. You are on a range of medications but have not been given beta blockers.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms, e.g. pain, tightening skin, shiny skin, Raynaud's phenomenon (and clarify that she is not taking beta blockers).
* Ask about the duration of symptoms.

#### Initial examination

* Look at the face and hands.

#### Scleroderma - face

There is facial _telangiectasia_. The skin is _smooth, shiny_ and _tight_. There is _period skin puckering_ (Fig. 5.15).

#### Scleroderma - hands

There is _sclerodactyl, nail-fold erythema due to dilated nail-fold capillaries_ (telangiectasia), nail-fold infarction, ragged cuticles, digital ischaemia, pulp atrophy, calcinosis_ and _Raynaud's phenomenon_.

#### Further assessment

* Distinguish limited from diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis. The latter extends above the elbows or knees or involves the trunk. You would confirm the diagnosis and stratify risk using hallmark antibodies and evidence of other organ disease.
* Ask about gastrointestinal symptoms (gastro-oesophageal reflux leading to Barrett's oesophagus) and kidney problems.
* Examine the chest for evidence of interstitial lung disease and signs of pulmonary hypertension.
* Skin signs are common in autoimmune connective tissue diseases (Box 5.3, Fig 5.16).
* Explore concerns.

Figure 5.16: Vitligo.

Figure 5.15: Scleroderma. 

## Feedback to enantiomers

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has facial telangiectasia and perioral skin puckering. In her hands there is sclerodactyl and evidence of digital ischaemia, and she reports Raynaud's phenomenon.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She has scleroderma. She may have limited or diffuse systemic sclerosis with multi-organ involvement, although the absence of apparent involvement above the elbows or knees would suggest limited systemic sclerosis.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

I would arrange for her to have a range of blood tests including scleroderma hallmark antibodies and, depending upon symptoms, pulmonary function tests for interstitial lung disease, echocardiography for pulmonary artery hypertension, oesophagogastroduodenoscopy for Barrett's oesophagus and monitoring of renal function.

## Case 5.7 Oral Lesions and

NAIL LESIONS

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Georgina Tate, aged 73 years

This woman is on your ward with iron deficiency anaemia. She has a skin rash around her mouth and hands.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 73-year-old woman in hospital with iron deficiency anaemia, and have been transfused with blood. You have a chronic skin rash around your mouth and on your hands that waxes and wanes. You have a long history of iron deficiency anaemia and have been investigated and given a name for this before, although you cannot remember the name of the condition. You also have odd spoon-shaped nails. You would like to know a little more about the underlying condition.

## 55 Focused history and examination

### 55 Initial history

* Ask about the rash.
* Ask about a longstanding history of iron deficiency anaemia.
* Ask if there is a family history of similar rashes or anaemia.

### 55 Initial examination

* Be aware of common oral lesions seen in PACES, listed below.

#### 55.1 Hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT)

There are _dilated capillaries/venues of the lips and tongue_ and _around the mouth_ (Fig. 55.17), _mail beds_, _hands_ and frequently elsewhere. There may be evidence of recent epistaxis.

#### 55.2 Peutz-Jeghers syndrome

The _lips_ are _pigmented_ in this autosomal dominant disorder characterised by mucocutaneous melanosis (lentigo) and gastrointestinal hamartomas.

#### 55.3 Mouth ulcers and intraoral lesions

Causes are listed in Box 55.4.

#### 55.4 Other

Other oral lesions you could encounter include period dermatitis and Addison's disease pigmentation. The circumoral and mucosal pigmentation of Peutz-Jeghers syndrome is usually less diffuse than that of Addison's disease.

### 56 Further assessment

* Nail lesions are seldom isolated in PACES, but be aware of the range of nail lesions that occur (Table 55.11). This woman probably has koilonychia associated with her iron deficiency anaemia and pallor from gastrointestinal haemorrhage.
* direct connections between arteries and veins
- throughout the gastrointestinal tract, liver, lungs and nervous system. Consider the possible presence of hepatic or chest fruits. Neurological problems in HHT may result from an intracranial arteriovenous malformation (subarachnoid haemorrhage is one of the most serious complications of HHT) or from an embolic stroke arising from a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation.
* Explore concerns.

## 57 Feedback to patient

This looks like a condition called hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia, in which small blood vessels can be friable and leak small amounts of blood, giving rise to blood loss and a deficiency of iron. Interestingly, this can also give rise to the shape of your nails. It may be inherited. There is no specific treatment other than maintaining blood levels as far as possible with iron supplementation and blood transfusion if necessary. Further investigation is probably not necessary if this is a diagnosis that has been established previously and if there are no other conditions or complications in your past that might relate to this condition. I'd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

## 58 Feedback to examiners

### 58 Summarise the key history and examination findings

This lady has telangiectasias of the lips and tongue and around the mouth and hands together with koilonychia and she would thus appear to be iron deficient.

Figure 55.17: Telangiectasia.

Figure 5.18.: Oxychomycosis.

### Case 5.8 SHIN LESIONS

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Ms Jane Tiller, aged 22 years

This woman has presented to the acute medical unit with some lesions on her legs.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a previously well 22-year-old woman who has recently been given antibiotics by her general practitioner for a sore throat. You have now been admitted to the acute medical unit because of some very tender, bruise-like lesions on your legs that have developed over the past 24 hours. You are very worried about the possible cause and also the appearance - you are getting married in 2 weeks and are worried that it may spread to more conspicuous areas.

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms of the shin lesions, notably if they are painful.
* Ask about past medical history and recent illnesses or drugs.

### Initial examination

* Be aware of the most likely causes of a shin rash listed below.

#### Erythema nodosum

There are _erythematous_ (progressing through the colour changes of a bruise), _warm, nodular_ (flatten with healing) _lesions over the anterior aspect of both shins_ (Fig. 5.19). They

Figure 5.19: (A–C) Erythema nodosum. may be single or multiple, of various sizes, and may occur elsewhere. Ask if they are _tender_ (before you examine them!). Lesions evolve slowly, changing from acute red nodules to residual bruises over a period of weeks.

#### Pyoderma gangrenosum

Several variants of pyoderma gangrenosum exist. _Classic pyoderma gangrenosum_ is a _deep ulcer_ with a _well-defined border_ that is usually violet or blue (Fig. 5.20). The ulcer edge may be undermined (worn and damaged) and the surrounding skin may be erythematosus and inducrated. The ulcer often starts as a red papule or nodule or collection of papules, which break down to form small ulcers with a 'cat's paw' appearance; these coalesce and the central area becomes necrotic and forms the ulcer. Lesions are painful. Pyoderma gangrenosum can occur at any site, but the face and legs are common sites. Healing often leaves a papery scar.

Variants include _peristomal_ pyoderma gangrenosum (around 15% of all cases), _pustular pyoderma gangrenosum_ (a rare, superficial variant), _bullous pyoderma gangrenosum_ (a superficial variant that tends to affect the upper limbs or face and often associated with haematological malignancy) and _vegetative pyoderma gangrenosum_ (a less aggressive superficial form).

#### Necrobiosis lipoidica (diabeticorum)

There are _well-demarcated (often oval) non-scaling plaques_ on the shins (Fig. 5.21). Characteristically they occur on the legs but they may occur anywhere else. They have a _shiny, atrophic surface, red/brown margins_ and _yellow wavy centres_. _Surface telangiectasia_ is characteristic. Trauma often results in persistent ulceration (Fig. 5.22).

#### Pretibial myxoedema

There are _elevated symmetrical shin lesions_ (Fig. 5.23). Such lesions may occur at other sites. They are _coarse, purplish red or brown_, with _well-defined edges_. The _skin is shiny_, with an _orange peel appearance_. The lesions may be asymptomatic or painful. Coarse hairs tend to occur in the vicinity of the lesions.

### Further assessment

* Be aware of the presence of a confirmatory feature (Box 5.5).
* Consider possible causes of erythema nodosum and pyoderma gangrenosum (Table 5.12). Pretibial myxoedema is specific to Graves' disease and the superficial layer of the skin is infiltrated with mucopolysaccharides and hyaluronic acid.

Figure 5.20: Pyoderma gangrenosum.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c}  & Erythema & Pyoderma \\ nodosum & gangrenosum \\ Infection & Viral & Uncommon, but \\ _Streptococcus_ (store throat) & & pyoderma \\ _Salmonella_ or & & gangrenosum should \\ _Campylobacter_ & & be in the differential \\ (enteritis) & & diagnosis of any \\ Primary tuberculosis & & ulcer – also consider \\ Leptoxy & & infection, chronic \\ Fungi & & venous insufficiency, \\ peripheral vascular & & disease, vasculitis, \\ neuropathy (often with & & neuropathy (often with \\ & & Charact’s joints), sickle \\ cell disease and & & malignancy \\ Drugs & Oral contraceptive pill & Uncommon \\ & & Pulphonamides \\ Tetracyclines & & \\ Penicillin & & \\ Inflammation & Ulcerative colitis & Ulcerative colitis \\ Crohn’s disease & & Crohn’s disease \\ \hline Other & Sarcoidosis, & Rheumatoid arthritis \\ systemic disease & commonly with & Ankylosing spondylitis \\ arthalgia and & & Chronic active hepatitis \\ bilateral hilar & & Primary biliary cirrhosis \\ lymphadenopathy & & Sclerosing cholangitis \\  & & Sarcoidosis \\  & & Diabetes mellitus \\ Thyroid disease \\ \hline Malignancy & lymphoma & & Lymphoproliferative and \\  & & myeloproliferative \\ disorders & & disorders \\ \hline Other & Pregnancy & & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}

I'd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has tender spontaneous bruise-like lesions on her legs characteristic of erythema nodosum.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The likely trigger is infection or antibiotics.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

It should settle spontaneously, although it would be sensible to arrange blood tests (including clotting) and a chest X-ray to exclude hilar lymphadenopathy.

## CASE 5.9 NEUROFIBROMATOSIS AND TUBEROSE SCLEROSIS

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr John Evans, aged 40 years

This man has been admitted to hospital with a first seizure. He has a significant background medical history.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 40-year-old man who has been admitted to hospital with a first seizure. You have neurofibromatosis with cafe-au-lait spots and freckling and peripheral neurofibromas and Lisch nodules and wonder if the seizure could be related to this condition. You are also concerned because you have not seen anyone about the condition for many years and have numerous concerns such as likely prognosis and how it is inherited. 

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about the seizure symptoms.
* Ask about driving.

#### Initial examination

* Recognise the neurofibromatosis. There are multiple _cafe-au-lait spots_, each more than 15 mm in greatest diameter. There is _axillary and skin-fold flexbling_. There are multiple _neurofibromas_. These may be soft mobile subcutaneous lumps or nodules along peripheral nerves (Fig. 5.24 and Fig. 5.25 Neurofibromatosis type 1).

#### Further assessment

* NF1 and NF2, different at molecular and clinical levels. The type of peripheral nerve tumour (neurofibroma versus schwanoma), the presence or absence of cafe-au-lait spots and skin-fold flexbling and the presence of certain ophthalmic features characterise each type.

#### Epidemiology

NF1 is one of the most common autosomal dominant conditions, with an incidence of around 1 in 3000 births. Its major features - neurofibromas, cafe-au-lait spots and Lisch nodules - are not generally associated with other problems. NF2 is much less common with an incidence of around 1 in 30 000 to 1 in 40 000.

#### Genetic basis

Inheritance of both NF1 and NF2 is autosomal dominant, but about 50% of cases of NF1 and NF2 are first cases within a family; penetrance is 100% by age 5 such that if no cafe-au-lait spots are present by then it can be inferred that NF1 has not been inherited. Around 10% of people without neurofibromatosis have one or two cafe-au-lait spots. A small number of people with NF1 have fewer than six spots, and disease can be identified by a positive family history and observation to teenage years for neurofibromas, or by genetic testing. The genes affected in both types normally act as tumour suppressors. The _NF1_ gene is on chromosome 17 and encodes the protein neurofibrombin, a GTPase-activating protein for the p21 ras protein family that normally inhibits ras by converting active ras-GTP to inactive ras-GDP. Neurofibrombin absence thus leads to cell growth activation. The _NF1_ gene is large, with many mutations with slightly varying clinical manifestations, whereas _NF2_ tends to cause predictable features within a family. The _NF2_ gene is on chromosome 22 and encodes the protein merlin (schwanomoin - a negative growth regulator affecting cell signalling), part of the Erin-Radixin-Moexin (ERM) sub-group of the protein 4.1 super family.

#### Diagnostic criteria and clinical features

The diagnostic criteria for NF1, based on the 1987 National Institutes of Health consensus, are shown in Box 5.6, as are the Manchester criteria for NF2, although it will not be necessary to know these (nor, indeed, the genetics beyond autosomal dominance, but it is important to keep the geeks happy).

And so NF1 is very much a condition of cafe-au-lait spots and flexbling, peripheral neurofibromas and Lisch nodules, the frequencies of these features are high and are detailed in Table 5.13. Plexiform neurofibromas (fusiform enlargement rather than subcutaneous nodules) are

Figure 5.24: Cafe-au-lait spots, neurofibromas and axillary flexbling. common and may be large lesions on the head and neck, limb or trunk, associated with skin and bone hypertrophy. Optic gliomas are quite common. Around 50% of people with NF1 develop complications. By far the commonest are learning problems, usually minor. Spinal neurofibromas are not uncommon. Seizures are not that common. All other complications are unusual, with a frequency under 5%. These include aqueduct stenosis, orthopaedic complications (scoliosis, pseudoarthrosis of tibia and fibula, sphenoid wing dysplasia), malignancy and other tumours (malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumours, pelvic rhabdomyosarcomas, gastrointestinal neurofibromas, phaechromocytomas, duodenal carcinoid, glomus tumours of nail beds), vascular complications (renal artery stenosis, cerebrovascular disease) and interstitial lung disease. Complications cannot be predicted, even within families.

NF2, conversely, is characterised by its hallmark of bilateral vestibular schwannomas (the term preferred to acoustic neuromas here since they are schwannomas histologically arising from the vestibular branch of the eighth nerve). Meningiomas and other cranial nerve and spinal root schwannomas are also common. NF2 does not cause skin-fold freckling, but does cause a unique skin lesion: the NF2 plaque. NF2 also causes ophthalmic complications. NF2 is thus easy to distinguish from NF1, except in

Figure 5.25: Neuofibromatosis type 1. a small number of cases of NF2 with a few cafe-au-lait spots and peripheral nerve tumours indistinguishable from those in NF1. However, histologically these are schwannomas in NF2. Unlike NF1, NF2 is associated with significant morbidity and mortality in all patients.

#### Differential diagnosis

Another condition, tuberose sclerosis, occasionally appears in PACES. It is also an autosomal dominant condition (_TSC1_ gene at 9q34 and _TSC2_ gene at 16p13.3) in which hamartomas occur in multiple organs. There are often multiple small skin lesions on the face - flesh coloured or pink papules on the nose and cheeks are _angofibromata_ (Fig. 5.26) (once incorrectly called adenoma sebacecum), very similar to adenoma sebacecum and tending to be symmetrical, e.g. nasolabial folds, butterfly rash. Leathery, lumpy, flesh-coloured _shagreen patches_ may be present, often on the trunk. There may also be hypopigmented macules in an oval or mountain ash leaf configuration. Periungual fibromata arise from the proximal nail folds of the fingers or toes. Other organs that can be affected by the hamartomas include the brain (epilepsy, learning dif ficulties), retina, kidney (cysts), heart, lung (pneumothorces) and bone.

## Feedback to patient

Seizures are not that common in neurofibromatosis and are quite common in the general population and so it is by no means certain that the neurofibromatosis and the seizure are linked. However, just as nerve thickening can occur elsewhere, it is possible for it to occur in the brain. I think we should arrange, as well as blood tests, a magnetic resonance brain scan to look at things in more detail, and seek the advice of a neurologist about whether or not to start medication to prevent further seizures. The main features of your neurofibromatosis are these pale brown spots called cafe-au-luit spots, freckling, thickening of peripheral nerves known as peripheral neurofibromas, and the nodules in the eye (not always seen) known as Lisch nodules. These features suggest that you have the most common type of neurofibromatosis called NF1. This generally has a much better outlook than the other type of neurofibromatosis, NF2. Both types are inherited with a one in two chance. We could certainly ask an expert in neurofibromatosis - usually a genetics expert - to see you to talk about the condition and its future in more detail. Otherwise I'd be happy to try to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This man has multiple cafe-au-liatt spots, each more than about 15 mm in greatest diameter. There is axillary and skin-fold freckling. There are multiple neurofibromas.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

He has neurofibromatosis type 1, NF1, much different from NF2, which is characterised by vestibular schwannomas and a greater malignant potential. NF1 can certainly be complicated by minor learning problems and sometimes by seizures, although most other complications are uncommon.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Genetic testing is not routine because clinical diagnosis is usually straightforward and there is a large array of possible genetic mutations. It may be used in prenatal counselling or to establish diagnosis in rare situations where NF1 diagnostic criteria are fulfilled but with very limited evidence of disease (NF1 limited to a segment with just six cafe-au-liatt spots and axillary freckles, known as mosaic NF1 and which may not be inheritable as gonadal tissue may be unaffected, or a rare homozygous recessive disorder of mismatch repair genes in which there are six or more cafe-au-liatt spots and an affected sibling, but no other features).

NF1 management in adults is largely education based, teaching patients about possible complications. NF2 is managed in multidisciplinary specialist centres.

### Case 5.10 Neoplastic Skin Lesions

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Anna Watson, aged 40 years

This woman is in your outpatient clinic because of diabetes. However, she mentions that she has a skin lesion on her leg.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 40-year-old woman with stable diabetes in the outpatient clinic. You mention to the ward doctor that you have a brown lesion on your leg. It has been enlarging over the past few months. You have spent considerable time in the sun. You are worried about melanoma.

Figure 5.26: Angiotensin. 

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

* Ask about the duration of the lesion.
* Ask about symptoms, e.g. itch, bleeding.
* Ask about any provoking and relieving factors.
* usual or excessive, e.g. outdoor occupation

### Initial examination

* Be aware of the wide-ranging skin manifestations of malignancy (Box 5.7).

### Box 5.7 Skin manifestations of malignancy

### Primary skin malignancy

Important skin neoplasms arising in the epidermis are malignant melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma (often with a keratotic surface) and basal cell carcinoma (Fig. 5.27, a rolled penalty edge characteristic, but may be raised red patches, ulcerated and even deeply invasive if untreated but metastases are more likely with malignant melanoma and squamous cell carcinoma). Less common skin tumours arise from blood vessels (e.g. angiosarcoma) or connective tissue (e.g. dermatofibromascarcoma).

### Premalignant sun-related lesions

These include solar keratoses, cutaneous horns, actinic cheitis, disseminated superficial actinic parakteratosis and Bowen's disease.

### Direct or metastatic spread to skin

The skin is an uncommon site of distant metastases, but primary sites include lung, colon and oral squamous cell cancer.

### Paraneoplastic syndromes involving the skin

There is a wide range, e.g. amyloidosis, carcinoid syndrome, cryoglobulinaemia, dermatomyostis, migratory erythema, migratory thrombobelitis, paraepoplastic pemphipus, pruritus, prodyema gangrenovan and Sweet's syndrome.

### Skin manifestations of haematological malignancy

Itch is common with lymphomas and myeloproliferative disorders. Infections are common, especially in immunocompromised people - bacterial, herpes virus, cytomegalovirus, fungal and yeast infections such as candidiasis and phytiasis versicolor. Autoimmune diseases, notably thrombotic thrombocytopenia purpura, haemolytic anaemia, viiiiltigo, alopecia, Raynaud's phenomenon and vasculitis, are more common in myelodysplastic syndromes. Drug-induced skin disease is common, such as toxic erythema, and vitiligo around the eyes from tyrosine kinase inhibitors. Neutrophil dermatoses include pydedema gangrenovan and Sweet's syndrome (well-defined red or plum-coloured plaques), and may be associated with myeloid leukaemias. Leukaemia cuts is infiltration of neoplastic leucocytes in the skin presenting as deep-of purple plaques and can occur in acute myeloid leukaemia. Adult T-cell leukaemia/lymphoma associated with human T-cell leukaemic virus type-1 retrovirus infection involves the skin early. Plasma cell disorders including myeloma can cause amyloid deposition in blood vessels leading to purpura. * Ask whether the lesion has changed, ask about risk factors, and perform ABCDE examination (Table 5.14).

### Further assessment

Types of melanoma are listed in Table 5.15. The mean age of presentation is 55 years, but this may depend on the thickness and histological subtype; superficial spreading melanomas, usually thinner, are more common in younger people and nodular melanoma and lentigo maligna melanoma in older individuals.

## Feedback to patient

You certainly have a pigmented lesion on the leg. The vast majority of pigmented lesions (all commonly referred to as moles) are either known as benign naevi or atypical naevi. There are a number of features that make a naevus or mole atypical, including colour variation. However, the concern is always whether or not an atypical naevus might be a melanoma. This mole of yours looks small and has irregular colouring within it and a slightly irregular odeng. The most compelling reason to have it removed is that, although it is small, it has changed in size. I think we should arrange for you to be seen in our pigmented lesion clinic in the very near future to have it removed, if you are in agreement. That would hopefully be the fastest way too, to build in reassurance, and in the event that it were cancerous then it would hopefully have been removed in entirety before causing any complications. I'd be happy to discuss any further concerns that you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has a pigmented lesion on her leg with a number of features of concern - asymmetry, irregular edge, colour variation and evolution in size.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Assessment & Features of concern \\ \hline Lesions that could be melanoma & New lesion (almost two-thirds of melanomas arise _de novo_), especially over the age of 30 \\ \hline Risk factors for melanoma & Risk factors include: & \\  • Significant sun exposure (ultraviolet exposure in the UK underestimated) – the major risk factor, and while & \\  • Indidence is highest in parts of the world with the longest hours of sunlight, the pattern of exposure is important & \\ • Previous sunburns & \\ • Type I skin (red hair, burns easily, never tans) & \\ • Personal or family history of melanoma (family history confers a modestly increased risk) & \\ • Immunosuppression. & \\ • The presence of naevi is the most powerful predictor of risk & \\ •  • (familial) dysplastic neevis syndrome (apical naevi are 3–15 mm in diameter with variable colour, and an irregular edge) increases the risk of melanoma & \\ • More than 100 common, benign naevi (\(\lesssim\) 2 mm in diameter) or more than two atypical naevi (\(\gtrsim\) 5 mm in diameter) carry a five- to 20-fold increased risk & \\ • More than 50 atypical naevi increases the risk almost 200-fold. The atypical mole syndrome affects around 2\% of the population with a lifetime risk of melanoma of around 1 in 20. Alypical naevi, unlike melanoma, are usually symmetrical and do not have a sharp edge with a geographical border, and remain static, whilst melanoma usually changes in size, shape or colour over months & \\ • Congenital naevi, especially giant naevi \(\gtrsim\) 20 cm, carry considerable risk. & \\ \hline Examination findings & Modified American Cancer Society ABCD system: & \\ \(\begin{array}{ll}\text{A}&\text{Asymmetry}&\text{Divide lesion into four quadrants; asymmetry if lack of mirror image in any of the quadrants}\\ \text{B}&\text{Border}&\text{Irregularities of border, with ‘coves’ resembling a coast or place to port a boat; this geographical border has a sharp cut-off from normal skin}\\ \text{C}&\text{Colour variation}&\text{\(\geq\) 2 colours within lesion}\\ &\text{Lack of even pigmentation throughout}\\ &\text{Blue or black pigment highly suspicious; rarely non-pigmented red nodule, more likely on hands or feet}\\ \text{D}&\text{Diameter}&\text{>\) 6 mm in diameter (or any change in size over weeks or months)} \\ \(\text{(E)}&\text{Evolution of lesion/ elevation/ erythema}\\ \end{array}\) & \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}

Arrowed captions, what to do/instructions to the patient; white text captions, what it tests/detects. 

## Further assessment

### Symptoms

Common rheumatoid symptoms are pain, stiffness and joint swelling (Table 5.17).

### Autoimmune tests

Diagnosis in autoimmune rheumatic disease may take time, the clinical picture often emerging over months or even years. The detection of autoantibodies to a variety of antigenic targets plays an important role in the diagnosis of autoimmune rheumatic disease. When the history and examination meet a number of diagnostic criteria, autoantibody tests can seek to confirm the diagnosis. This is the purest view, but in practice tests are often requested in the absence of clinical assessment meeting defined criteria. For example, an 18-year-old woman might develop pericarditis as the first manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and positive autoantibodies are then highly suspicious. Rheumatoid factor, antinuclear antibodies and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies have been the key in the diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), SLE and systemic vasculitis for many years. Antibodies against cyclic citrullinated peptides are specific for RA and may predict development of erosive disease and are increasingly used in RA assessment. There is increasing interest in the use of anti-C1q antibodies in detecting and monitoring lupus nephritis as this assay appears to be more sensitive than anti-DNA antibodies. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISAs) are now being used in place of both radioimmunoassay and immunofluorescence because they are readily automated and less dependent on experienced technicians.

Antinuclear antibodies (ANAs) are autoantibodies (usually immunoglobulin G) directed against cellular proteins or nucleic acids; despite their name, several of these antigens are confined to the cytoplasm. The most common ANAs react with DNA-protein or RNA-protein complexes. Immunoblotting (though ELISA is increasingly used) may show a predominantly homogeneous (e.g. SLE - double-stranded DNA), speckled (e.g. Sjogren's syndrome - Ro, La; mixed connective tissue disease - U1RNP), nucleolar (scleroderma - Scl-70) or centromere (scleroderma) pattern. A titre >=1:160 is regarded as high. Subsequent detection of antibodies to extractable nuclear antigens is useful in diagnosing specific diseases (Table 5.18).

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} \hline \hline
**Symptoms** & **Causes** \\ \hline Diffuse pain not localised to joints and without stiffness & Chronic pain syndrome (Polymylglia rheumatic causes diffuse symptoms but is characterised by stiffness) \\ \hline Pain and stiffness localised to joints without joint swelling & Osteoarthritis (Tenortilis may give rise to pain in the region of a joint, and bursitis to swelling in the region of a joint) \\ \hline Pain and stiffness with joint swelling (Arthralgia with arthritis/synovits) & Monoarticular Painlromic rheumatoid, usually short lived (days) and occasionally the first manifestation \\ \hline Fetal & The skin (warmth) and surrounding tissues (fluid, soft tissue or bone) & Crystal arthropathy \\ \hline The joints (at rest) & Polynarticular symptoms & Rheumatoid arthritis \\ Move & The joints Movements may be _active_ (involving active muscle contraction) or _passive_ (especially if active movement limited) & Spendinguria (unlike antibodies to single-stranded DNA) \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}

## EXAMINATION OF THE HANDS AND ARMS

### The hands and wrists

* Be careful before shaking a patient's hand. Ask if there is pain before examining, and preferably examine the hands rested on a pillow on your seated patient's lap.
* Proceed to look, feel and move (Table 5.19; Figs 5.29 and 5.30). * Ask the patient to point to any site of pain, although this is not a reliable guide for localising the structure from which it arises. Shoulder pain may be referred from cervical roots, thoracic viscera and from structures adjacent to the diaphragm. Patterns of stiffness or loss of movement are more helpful diagnostically; the removal of a garment requires external rotation at the glenohumeral joint, for example.
* Proceed to look, feel and move (Table 5.21).

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c}
**Look** & At the skin & Erylthema suggests acute inflammation, e.g. crystal arthropathy, septic arthritis, tendon sheath infection, soft-tissue infection \\  & Look for palmar erythema, vasculitis, digital ischaemia, telangiectasia, purpura (corticosteroids) or scars (arthrodees or previous capal tunnel release) \\  & Look at the nails for pitting or evidence of vasculitis – nail-fold erythema or infarction \\ \hline For swellings & Swelling of the IP joints or MCP joints suggests synovitis; there is loss of interdigital indentation of the MCP and IP joints when the fingers are flexed (loss of mountains’ sign) \\ \hline For deformities & Swan neck and boutonnier deformities, Z-shaped thumbs and ulnar deviation of the MCP joints are typical of rheumatoid arthritis \\  & Anterior (volar) displacement of the wrist from partial dislocation (subluxation) is also common in rheumatoid arthritis \\  & A mailer finger or thumb, in which the terminal phalanx cannot be actively extended, is caused by rupture of the extensor tendon, usually from trauma or rheumatoid arthritis \\  & Arachnodactyl suggests Marfan’s syndrome \\ \hline \multirow{2}{*}{**Feel**} & For muscle wasting & Look for wasting of the thenar or hypothenar muscle groups or both \\  & The skin and surrounding tissues & Feel for warmth \\ \hline The joints (at rest) & Bony swellings of the distal or proximal IP joints are likely Heberden’s or Bouchard’s nodes of osteoarthritis \\  & & “Squaring” of the wrist is due to osteophytes at the first CMC joint \\  & Soft-tissue swelling suggests synovitis, a fusiform swelling \\  & Feel the flexor tendon sheaths for localised swelling or tenderness; if present look for triggering or locking during extension \\  & & feel for repelus of the opening and dosing fingers with your index finger \\ \hline
**Move** & The joints & Make a ‘prayer sign” & Test wrist extension to 90° \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.19: Examining the hands and wrists 

### Subacromial disease - rotator cuff disease

This is common under 50 years of age, often _de novo_ or due to repetitive strain. The rotator cuff tendons may tear, often partially, or become inflamed, and often a combination of tearing and inflammation gives rise to symptoms. Chronic inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis can also lead to atrophy of the rotator cuff muscles, with symptoms. The rotator cuff muscles and tendons are:

* allows abduction and elevation of the shoulder via the deltoid
* allows external rotation of the shoulder
* allows internal rotation of the shoulder.

Supraspinatus tendonitis is the most common problem. Rotator cuff tears tend to cause pain at the deltoid insertion, often worse at night, with weakness of overhead activity and outreach fatigue. There may be wasting of rotator cuff muscles over the scapula (supraspinatus and infraspinatus) and restricted movement, particularly active movement. The rotator cuff, notably supraspinatus, initiates abduction. Its presence between the glenohumeral and subacromial joints means that during abduction of the humeral head the tendon has to slide under the acromion where it rubs against the bony undersurface of the latter. Any tear or damage or focus of inflammation in the tendon will be exacerbated by the constant rubbing against bone, and a vicious cycle is set up. Because of the

Figure 5.30: Examining for synovitis. Press both sides of the joint with the index finger and thumb of one hand and any swelling will ‘bulge up’. Now press on the swelling (it has a spongy feel) with the fingers of your other hand. You should normally be able to feel the bony margins of each metacarpophalangeal joint and the gaps between them, especially when the patient makes a fist, like mountain peaks and valleys. In inflammatory joint disease the valleys are filled with synovial fluid.

Figure 5.29: Rheumatoid hands – synovitis.

restricted space, the tendon may reach a point of being stuck ('impingement'), particularly as the humeral head and acromion come together. But once the tendon has passed through the space, the remainder of abduction is painless and easier. This contrasts with acromiodavicular joint disease, usually more symptomatic with high arc abduction. Plain X-rays may show tendon calcification, and atrophy demonstrated by subacromial space narrowing (decreased vertical height between the humeral head and the acromion). Ultrasound or magnetic resonance imaging is helpful. Treatments include steroid injections, physiotherapy and surgery, although a degree of rotator cuff pathology is almost inevitable in most people at some stage, not necessarily symptomatic.

### Glenohumeral disease - adhesive capsulitis ('frozen shoulder') and osteoarthritis

Distinguishing these two common differential diagnoses is outlined in Table 5.22.

## Summary

A summary of the hands and arms examination sequence is in the Summary box.

\begin{table}

Table 5.22: Glenholumenal disease – adhesive capsulitis versus osteoarthritis 

## Examination of the Legs

### The hip

The hips are less likely to be involved in inflammatory arthritis and are usually a source of pain, rather than swelling, that is often referred to the groin or downward to the knee.

* Ask whether there is pain.
* Proceed to look, feel and move (Table 5.23).

### Thomas's test

A flexion deformity of the hip can be masked if the patient tilts the pelvis forwards and increases the lumbar lordosis. Thomas's test can be used to obliterate the lumbar lordosis and unmask hip flexion (Fig. 5.33). The unaffected hip is flexed to its limit (do not do this if there is a hip replacement, as it may dislocate) to straighten the lumbar spine. An affected hip rises from the couch to reveal the degree of abnormal flexion.

### Trendelenberg's test

Standing on one leg on the side of a diseased hip may cause the contralateral hemipelvis to fall below the horizontal.

## The knee

* Ask whether there is pain.
* Proceed to look, feel and move (Table 5.24). 

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} \hline \hline
**Look** & At the skin & Look for scars, sinuses or erythema \\ \cline{2-3}  & For swellings & Look for any obvious swellings or effusion including prepatellar and infrapatellar bursitis and popliteal (Baker’s) cysts; if the latter ruptures, swelling may track into the calf \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.24: Examining the knee 

## The ankles and feet

* Ask whether there is pain.
* Proceed to look, feel and move (Table 5.25).

## Summary

A summary of the legs examination sequence is in the Summary box.

## EXAMINATION OF THE SPINE

Neurological examination of the limbs for signs referable to the spinal cord is described on Station 3. The look, feel and move approach (Table 5.26) is also appropriate for examining the spine.

## Further assessment

Nerve stretch tests for sciatic and femoral nerve impingement are an important part of spinal examination. In the _sciatic nerve stretch test_ the supine patient's straight leg raising is limited by pain produced by tension of nerve roots supplying the sciatic nerve (L4-S2), commonly tensed over a prolapsed disc (commonly at vertebral level L4/5 or L5/S1). Pain may be in the back of the leg, sometimes radiating to the lumbar region. Tension is increased by dorsiflexion of the foot (which tugs the distal component of the sciatic nerve, the posterior tibial nerve) and relieved by flexion of the knee. In the _femoral nerve stretch_ test the prone patient's femoral nerve roots (L2-4) are tightened by flexion of the knee, causing pain radiating into the back. The femoral roots may be further tensed by extending the hip joint, producing increasing pain.

## Summary

A summary of the spine examination sequence is in the Summary box.

## Summary Box - SPINE EXAMINATION

**Feel** & The skin and surrounding tissues & Feel the midline spinous processes and confirm alignment. Prominence, especially in the thoracic spine, may indicate anterior wedge compression of a vertebra \\  & Feel the paraspinal soft tissues for tenderness \\ \hline The joints (at rest) & Lightly percuss the spine for tenderness \\ \hline \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}

## Chapter 5.11 Rheumatoid hands and Rheumatoid arthritis

### 5.11 _Checks_

#### 5.11 _Checks_

The _Checks_ is a _Checks_ that are based on the _Checks_. The _Checks_ are based on the _Checks_. 

## Box 5.8 Examination findings in rheumatoid hands

### Skin

There may be _palmar erythema_. _Cutaneous vasculitis_ appears as crops of small brown spots at the nail folds, and as digital pulp infarcts.

### Swellings

There may be marked soft-tissue swelling of the _metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints and wrists_. _The proximal intephalangeal (PIP) joints_ may be swollen (the interphalangeal joint of the thumb behaves as a PIP joint). The distal intephalangeal (DIP) joints are spared. There may be active synovitis with fusiform spongy swelling of joints.

Soft-tissue swelling around a joint is the hallmark of inflammatory joint disease and associated stiffness is worse after inactivity and relieved by movement. Pain, stiffness (early morning lasting > 30 minutes) and warmth are the other features of synovial inflammation, but erythema is rare. Pain originates predominantly from the joint capsule, abundantly supplied with pain fibres.

### Deformities

There may be:

* _Ullnar deviation_ of the fingers due to subluxation or dislocation at the MCP joints (ulnar deviation also results from displaced extensor tendons)
* _Swan neck deformities_ of the fingers (flexion deformity of the DIP joint, with a hyperextended PIP joint)
* _Boutomniere deformities_ of the fingers (extended DIP joint caused by rupture of the extensor tendon slip, with a flexed PIP joint)
* _Z-shaped thrumbs_ (flexed MCP joint, with extended IP joint). These deformities are shown in Figures 5.34 and 5.35.

### Muscle wasting

This is usually generalised, predominantly from disuse. There is thenar and hypothenar wasting (compare with isolated median or ulnar neuropathies), interosseous wasting is very common.

### Box 5.9 Diagnostic criteria for rheumatoid arthritis (RA) (American College of Rheumatology)

RA is a chronic inflammatory, symmetrical, predominantly small joint, polyarthritis. It is an autoimmune disease, mediated by pro-inflammatory cytokines. There is a strong HLA DDH association. It affects around 1% of people and its onset is commonly in the fifth decade. Diagnostic criteria are:

* Morning stiffness > 1 hour*
* proximal intephalangeal, metacarpophalangeal, wrist, elbow, knee, ankle, metacarpophalangeal*
* Arthritis of hand joints*
* Symmetrical arthritis
* Rheumatoid nodules
* Rheumatoid factor positivity
* Radiological features (e.g. joint erosions). Four criteria are required (* must be present for at least 6 weeks). The usual pattern is insidious, over weeks to months, starting with fatigue, anorexia and vague musculoskeletal symptoms. Less frequently it appears over a week or two, and rarely over a few days. A palindrome refers to episodic features with complete resolution in between, such as acute, self-limiting swelling of a knee. Extra-articular onset is recognised.

The Guideline Development Group (GDG) for the National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence accepts a clinical diagnosis of RA as being more important than the 1987 American Rheumatism Association (which would become the American College of Rheumatology) classification criteria because an early persistent synovitis in which other pathologies have been ruled out needs to be treated as if it is RA to try to prevent damage to joints. International committees are addressing the diagnostic criteria for early RA. The GDB categorises RA into two categories: "recent onset" (disease duration of up to 2 years) and "established" (disease duration of longer than 2 years).

Figure 5.34: Finger deformities in rheumatoid arthritis. of patients and are generally associated with rheumatoid factor positivity. Asks if she is aware of any symptoms other than in the hands.
* Explore concerns and function. Function can be assessed by a simple screen of pincer, grip and arm movements and by asking about routine activities such as fastening buttons, using keys, writing, taking coins out of pockets, holding cups, opening jars, cleaning teeth, combing hair, getting dressed and reaching into kitchen cupboards. Specific ways of assessing disease activity include health assessment questionnaires, pain assessments, tender joint scores, swollen joint scores, duration of morning stiffness and monitoring of inflammatory markers. Numerous factors influence prognosis in rheumatoid arthritis (Box 5.10).

## Feedback to patient

This is almost certainly a flare-up of the rheumatoid arthritis. It would be sensible for me to discuss treatment with a rheumatologist. It may be that something other than the methotrexate will be needed in the short term to settle things down such as steroids. It may also be worth your rheumatologist discussing other disease-modifying drugs with you. methotrexate can of course suppress the immune system and make infection more likely, such as with the infected ulcer, although it may be a balance of risk as most disease-modifying drugs for rheumatoid

Figure 5.35: (A–D) Finger deformities in rheumatoid arthritis.

Figure 5.36: Rheumatoid nodules: (A) elbow, (B) thumb. 

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt}} System or feature & Possible manifestations & Comment \\ \hline Cardiovascular & Accelerated cardiovascular disease & Severe RA carries a 5-year survival rate similar to three-vessel coronary artery disease. Although increased mortality has been linked with disease severity, disability and extra-articul disease, accelerated atherosclerosis remains the main culprit. Traditional risk factors are partly to blame, but chronic inflammation and endothelial damage associated with rheumatoid disease are important contributors that may enhance traditional factors, such as the association between systemic inflammation and arterial wall stiffness in hypertension, or the pro-atherogenic lipid profile (high LDL and lipoprotein(a), low HDL) seen with rheumatoid disease activity. Patients with RA have a higher incidence of diastolic hypertension, angina and stroke, and subclinical vascular disease such as shown by a higher prevalence of carotid disease, peripheral arterial disease and electrographic abnormalities. Deaths from CVD occur earlier than in the general population. There are many reasons why CVD is increased in RA: * Oxidised LDL (catLDL) antibodies to catLDL are present at higher levels in active disease. QoL is abundant in atherosclerotic lesions, and prolongs the inflammatory response by altering apoptotic cell clearance. QoL is produced in the inflamed rheumatoid joint, where it similarly prolongs inflammation * CRP is raised in acute coronary syndromes, involved in initiation and progression of atherosclerotic lesions, and independently predicts the risk of future myocardial infarction, stroke and death. Higher levels of CRP are associated with CVD in non-RA patients, and treatment of CVD with statins or angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors can lower CRP levels. Rather than simply a marker of systemic inflammation, CRP has direct pro-inflammatory effects at many sites, e.g. it activates vascular endothelial cells to pepres adhesion molecules * Elevated homocysteine levels are associated with CVD and present in higher concentrations in RA joints. Homocysteine is probably an epiphenomenon rather than cause of CVD * RA causes physical deconditioning as a result of disability, often with obesity \\ \hline Lung (findings more common at autopsy than clinically) & Pleuritic pain & Methetrexate also causes fibrosis and nodules \\ \hline Neurological & Entrapment neuropathies (capital tunnel syndrome, foot and wrist drops) & Entrapment by synovial swelling \\ \hline Cervical & Subluvation of the atantoaaxial joint occasionally occurs owing to laxity of the transverse ligament of the atlas (synovitis of facet joints may also contribute to instability); subluation is shown by flexion and extension radiographs demonstrating increased distance between the posterior aspect of the C1 vertebral body and the anterior aspect of the odortoid process of C2 \\ \hline Dysphonia (rare) & Cricoartenoid arthritis \\ Hearing loss (rare) & Synovial involvement of osicides \\ Sensory polyneuropathy & Caused by mononeuritis multiplex \\ \hline Vasculitis (small vessel) & Usually restricted to skin, e.g. nail folds & Rheumatoid factor highly positive \\ Digital gangrene & Cutaneous ulceration including pyroderma gangrenosum Peripheral neuropathy \\ Mononeuritis multiplex & Coronary, renal, cerebral or mesenteric vasculitis (all very rare) \\ Ramoud’s phenomenon & \\ \hline Rheumatoid nodules & Sites: Extensor tendons of hand (soft-tissue nodules, not to be confused with tendon xanthoma) & Rheumatoid factor invariably positive \\ \hline Rheumatoid nodules (soft-tissue nodules, not to be confused with tendon xanthoma) & Trigger finger (thickening, usually without nodules, of flexor aspect) \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.27: Extra-articular features of rheumatoid disease

The other consideration I think is whether or not the ulcer could be related to the rheumatoid arthritis, which can sometimes be the case. We will aim to get these answers quickly, and of course do all we can to relieve the pain and other symptoms quickly. Are there any questions you have?

## Feedback to examiners

### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This woman has soft-tissue swelling of the metacarpophalanagel joints and proximal interphalanged joints with evidence of active synovitis. She has typical hand deformities of rheumatoid arthritis including ulnar deviation, swan neck and Boutonniere deformities and Z-thumbs.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

Given her past medical history rheumatoid arthritis seems likely, and whilst psoriatic arthropathy can occasionally produce similar changes the presence of nodules further supports rheumatoid arthritis as well as providing prognostic information.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Investigations in rheumatoid arthritis [Box 5.11] may include blood testing for rheumatoid factor or anti-cyclic citrulinated peptide, synovial fluid analysis and radiology, but these may not be necessary here. Treatment goals in rheumatoid arthritis are pain relief, reduction of inflammation, limitation of joint damage, control of systemic disease and maintenance of function. No treatment is curative. Analgesics, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and cyclo-oxygenase-2 inhibitors help pain but do not control inflammation. Potential side effects are peptic ulceration, interstitial nephritis, fluid retention and worsening of asthma with non-steroidal antiinflammatory drugs and cyclo-oxygenase-2 inhibitors; a proton pump inhibitor should be considered with these drugs and these drugs should be avoided if already on aspirin. Disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs including methotrexate and sulfaselazine and biological agents have a place early in disease to limit progression [Box 5.12]. Short-term systemic, intramuscular or intra-articular glucocorticoids are used for symptom relief. They attenuate the acute-phase response but do not appear to modify disease and use must be carefully balanced against side effects. Physiotherapists, occupational therapists, podiatrists and surgeons may have important roles.
