\end{table}
Table 5.27: Extra-articular features of rheumatoid disease—control 

## Rheumatoid factor (RF)

RFs are antibodies against the Fc portion of immunoglobulin G. They may be of any isotype but only immunoglobulin M was traditionally detected by the now superseded latex agglutination test. An easy way to consider RF is to first consider that antibodies directed against other antibodies is a normal phenomenon. New antibodies are created continuously in response to an infinite variety of antigens in the environment; some antibodies, when newly formed, may also be seen by the immune system as foreign and 'anti-antibodies' are produced against them. Sometimes a cascade of antibody against antibody may be triggered. Some of the anti-antibodies produced in this way are RFs and, hence, RFs are not specific to rheumatoid arthritis. They may be detected transiently after many acute infections, may be present in low tires in chronic infections, and are sometimes found in chronic inflammatory disorders such as systemic lupus erythematosus and scleroderma. RFs are detectable in 5% of the normal population and in up to 25% of elderly people - simply because elderly people have been exposed for a longer time to the environmental stimuli that engender such antibodies. Up to 70% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis are RF positive

### Anti-cyclic citrulinated peptide (anti-CPP)

Anti-CPP antibodies are detected in many RF-negative patients, a more sensitive test than RF itself. Anti-CPP antibodies occur in 80% of patients with established rheumatoid arthritis and have a specificity of 95% and a sensitivity of around 65%. Like RF, they also seem to predict development of erosive rheumatoid arthritis.

### Box 5.12 Drugs in rheumatoid arthritis

#### Disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs (DMARDs)

DMARDs (methotrexate, sulfasalazine, hydroxychlorouine, gold, eidosoprin, lefhomomide) delay disease progression and reduce subsequent disability, and should be introduced early in disease. All agents are of similar efficacy at optimum dosage except for hydroxychlorouine and aurantium, which appear slightly less effective. Many DMARDs can cause bone marrow suppression and skin rashes. Gold and penicillamine may cause nephrotic syndrome. Penicillamine is associated with drug-induced lupus and autoimmune disease. Methotrexate and sulfasalazine are most widely used but monotherapy with any agent wanes in effect with time. Combinations are therefore used to sustain benefit:
* In newly diagnosed active RA, methotrexate and at least one other DMARD (plus short-term glucocorticoids) are used, ideally within 3 months of the onset of persistent symptoms. Monotherapy is considered where comorbidities or pregnancy make it safer.
* In the first 2 years, provided satisfactory and sustained control, step-down combination therapy may be considered cautiously, although it may not maintain long-term benefit. After 2 years, if stable, drugs and doses may be cautiously withdrawn, but with prompt return of drugs if there are flare-ups.

### Synovial fluid

Fluid is often turbid, with low viscosity, high protein concentration, low or normal glucose concentration, low complement levels and an elevated white cell count (5-50 000 cells/ml). There is seldom a place for synovial fluid examination other than to exclude infection.

### Some radiological features of rheumatoid arthritis

X-rays of the hands and feet early in the course of the disease in people with persistent synovitis in these joints is important. Features include:

* Soft-tissue swelling together with javta-articular osteoporosis in early disease
* Joint space narrowing
* Joint erosions
* Cyst formation
* Joint destruction
* Subluxation/dislocation.

### Monitoring

* C-reactive protein and key components of disease activity (using a composite score such as DAS28) should be measured regularly in people with rheumatoid arthritis to inform decision making about increasing treatment to control disease, cautiously decreasing treatment when disease is controlled.

### Glucocorticoids

* Short-term treatment with glucocorticoids helps manage flares in people with recent-onset or established disease by rapidly decreasing inflammation.

### Biological agents

Rationale
* T helper type 1), macrophages and fibroblasts with secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines [ Interleukin-1 (IL-1), L1-6 and tumour necrosis factor (TNF)-a] and metalloproteinases, which mediate tissue damage. TNF-a mediates proliferative and inflammatory aspects, and IL-1 mediates cartilage destruction and bone erosion. IL-1 mediates many actions of TNF-a and IL-1 but also recruits B cells. Most T-helper type 1 cells are memory cells but some mediate B-cell help, enhancing local auto-antibody formation. Plasma cells secreting rheumatoid factor are also present.

### Agents

* Three monodonal antibody antagonists to TNF-a are available: adalimumab (a fully humanised monoclonal antibody), etanercept (a recombinant TNF receptor immunoglobulins G fusion protein) and infliximab (a chimeric immunoglobulin G antibody).
* Rituximab, a B-cell-targeted treatment acting on CD20, is considered for patients with severe active rheumatoid arthritis failing to respond to DMARDs and TNF-a modification therapy.
* Abatacept inhibits the co-stimulatory signal for T-cell activation.
* Risks are outlined in Case 2.46.

### TNF-a inhibitors

The TNF-a inhibitors are recommended for patients with active rheumatoid arthritis as measured by a disease activity score (DAS28) greater than 5.1 confirmed on at least two occasions, 1 month apart, who have tried two DMARDs, including methotrexate (unless contraindicated). A trial of a DMARD is defined as 6 months, with 2 months at standard dose, unless toxicity has limited the dose or duration of treatment.

TNF-a inhibitors should normally be used in combination with methotrexate. Where a patient is intolerant of methotrexate or methotrexate is inappropriate, adalimumab and etanercept may be used as monotherapy.

Treatment with TNF-a inhibitors should be continued only if there is an adequate response at 6 months following initiation of therapy, defined as improvement in DAS28 of 1.2 points or more. After initial response, treatment should be monitored no less frequently than 6-monthly with assessment of DAS28 and withdrawn if an adequate response is not maintained. Treatment should normally be initiated with the least expensive drug.

### Case 5.12 ANKYLOSING SPONDYLITIS AND SPONDYLOAARTHROPANTHES

### Candidate information Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

* We: Mr Anthony Hoskins, aged 40 years This man is on your ward with a flare-up of ulcerative colitis, but mentions a painful spine.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 40-year-old man on the ward with a flare-up of ulcerative colitis and mention to the doctor that you have a painful neck and buttocks. You attributed it to 'wear and tear' previously as you have worked as a builder for many years and carried heavy loads. Your pain is worse in the morning after a period of rest. Your upper spine, particularly, has become curved. You are self-employed and now worried that work is becoming more difficult, even getting in and out of trucks.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about stiffness of the lumbar or dorsal spine, and those features of back pain listed in Box 5.13. The back pain of spondyloarthritis, unlike mechanical back pain, has typical features.
* Ask about alternating buttock pain. * Ask about a past history of psoriasis and inflammatory bowel disease.
* Ask about a family history of similar problems.

All of the above symptoms are common to ankylosing spondylitis (the primary spondyloarthritis) and the other spondyloarthritis. There is considerable overlap of features between the spondyloarthritis (Box 5.14).

Some differentiating articular signs of spondyloarthritis from rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis are shown in Table 5.28.

### Initial examination

* There may be _hypothesis_ with a characteristic _question mark_ posture (Fig. 5.37).
* There may be _restriction of spinal movement in all directions_ (extension, lateral flexion, rotation) and there would be a _positive modified Schober's test_ (marking the level of the posterior iliac spines and sacral dimples, and a point 10 cm above this in the midline, when the patient flexes forward the distance between the two points normally increases to at least 15 cm but does not in a positive test). Upper spine involvement is demonstrated by the _wall-occupit_ test (Fig. 5.38). 

## Chapter 5.16 The seronegative spondylioarthopathies

### 5.17 Ankylosing spondylitis

Ankylosing spondylitis is the primary spondylioarthopathy affecting 0.2-0.5% of the population with symmetrical pain and absence of features to suggest one of the other conditions. Age of onset is 15-40 years and the male-female ratio is around 3:1. The modified New York criteria for diagnosis require the presence of at least one clinical criterion plus one radiological criterion:

* Low back pain of at least 3 months' duration, with inflammatory characteristics
* Limitation of lumbar spine mobility in both sagittal and frontal planes
* Limitation of chest expansion relative to normal age- and sex-matched values
* Unilateral sacrolitifs (grades 3-4) or bilateral sacrolitifs (grade 2)

(grade 0 - normal; grade 1 - suspicious changes; grade 2 - minimal sacrolitifs; grade 3 - moderate sacrolitifs; grade 4 - ankylosis).

Criteria for early diagnosis, based on the co-occurrence of inflammatory spinal pain and demonstration of sacrolitifs by magnetic resonance scanning, are emerging.

### 5.18 Psoriatic arthritis

Psoriatic arthropathy (Fig. 5.39) affects up to 10% of patients with psoriasis (Fig. 5.40) and may precede or follow skin disease by months or even years. There are, in theory, five types of arthropathy (Case 5.1), radiologically, there may be erosions and severe joint destruction.

### 5.19 Reactive arthritis (including Reiter's syndrome)

Reactive arthritis refers to arthritis after any infection. It may affect small or large peripheral joints, with features of a spondylioarthopathy. Reiter's syndrome, a form of reactive arthritis, often follows urethritis/cervicitis (e.g. _Chlamydia_) or dysenteric infections (e.g. _Shigela_, _Salmonella_, _Camylobacter_, _Tersinia_ spp.). The classic triad is arthritis, urethritis and conjunctivitis/americuri uveitis. The classic triad is not always complete, and common additional findings include mouth ulceration, cironate balanitis, persistent plantar fasciitis and keratoderma blemorrhagica (hyperkeratotic vesicles, often on soles or palms).

### 5.20 Enteropathic arthritis

Associated with inflammatory bowel disease.

Figure 5.37: Ankylosing spondylitis.

Figure 5.38: Modified Schober’s test and wall–occipit test. spondylitis, 70% with Reiter's syndrome and up to 50% with other spondyloarthritis. The interleukin-23 receptor may also play a role. Serology is not used widely in diagnosis, which should rely on clinical and radiological assessment. Overlap of features may occur between spondyloarthritis, e.g. a patient with psoriatic arthritis may develop anterior uveitis.
* Vertebral osteoporosis is not uncommon, and cardiovascular disease and renal impairment may complicate severe ankylosing spondylitis.
* Consider his concerns about work.

## Feedback to patient

I think this may be a condition known as ankylosing spondylitis, in which inflammation in the spine sometimes causes fusion of the vertebrae. The pain and other symptoms that you describe are very typical of this. It might be wise to obtain some X-rays of the spine and pelvis to look for any features of this condition, and if so then to discuss things with a rheumatologist who could advise on the best longer term treatment. If this is ankylosing spondylitis then the pain should respond to anti-inflammatoryies, which we can start now, but it will also be important to consider longer term treatments to tackle the condition itself, and there is a wide range of effective options which the rheumatologist could discuss with you. If be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man describes features of an inflammatory arthropathy with symptoms of spondylitis and sacroiliitis.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

He could well have ankylosing spondylitis - there does not appear to be any evidence of psoriasis or recent

Figure 5.39: (A–C) Psoriatis. infection but he has inflammatory bowel disease - although overlap features of the various spondyloarthritis is common.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis is based on clinical and radiological evidence. The radiological features of established spondylitis and sacroiliitis are useful but in acute disease X-rays are normal; symptomatic sacroiliitis can precede radiological changes by years and magnetic resonance imaging of the sacroiliac joints is increasingly used to detect early disease. Inflammatory markers are often elevated in active disease.

A dramatic improvement with non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs is typical in early disease. Good posture, physiotherapy and home exercises including extension exercises are important. Disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs similar to those used in rheumatoid arthritis may help large joint arthritis, but seem to have less effect on axial (spine disease.

Ankylosing spondylitis persists through life and fatigue may be a major symptom that is difficult to treat. Furthermore, there is no evidence that non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs or physiotherapy alter progression. Evidence for the benefit of 'biologists' is mostly with ankylosing spondylitis but also for psoriatic arthritis, prompting guidelines by the Assessment in Ankylosing Spondylitis Working Group and the British Society of Rheumatology. Tumour necrosis factor-a mRNA has been identified in inflamed sacroiliac joints in patients with ankylosing spondylitis and joints of patients with psoriatic arthropathy. As with rheumatoid arthritis, adalimumab, etanercept and infliximab may have roles in seronegative arthropathy, especially for axial disease. The National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence recommends adalimumab or etanercept in severe ankylosing spondylitis with active spinal disease assessed on two separate occasions 12 weeks apart, and failure of at least two non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. Infiliximab is not recommended for people with ankylosing spondylitis. Etanercept, infliximab and adalimumab are all recommended for the treatment of active and progressive psoriatic arthritis, based on specific criteria and monitoring of the Psoriasis Arthritis Response Criteria and the Psoriasis Area and Severity Index.

## Case 5.13 Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Loma Fieldhouse, aged 29 years

This young woman has been admitted to hospital with an episode of pericardits. Prior to this she was under investigation for a facial rash and joint symptoms.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 29-year-old woman in hospital with chest pain and a subsequent diagnosis of pericarditis. Recently you have been seen in the medical outpatient clinic with a facial rash and joint pains, with a provisional diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Your test results and follow-up with respect to this are awaited. Furthermore, you wish to become pregnant in the near future and wonder about the implications of your diagnosis.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about current symptoms, and if she is feeling better from the symptoms of pericarditis.
* Ask about her SLE symptoms and what she has been told so far about the SLE diagnosis.
* drug-induced lupus (e.g. minocycline, hydralazine, procainamide, penicillamine) is more common in males, tends to spare the nervous system and kidneys, resolves on stopping the drug and is anti-double-stranded DNA negative but histone positive.

#### Initial examination

* Look for the raised or _flat patches of malar erythema ('butterfly rash'), sparing the nasolabial folds_ (Fig. 5.41) that are typical of the rash in SLE. The widely recognised presentation of a young woman with both a butterfly rash and inflammatory arthritis is only part of the spectrum of disease. Non-specific symptoms such as fatigue, photosensitive skin rashes, mild hair loss, Raynaud's phenomenon, oral ulcers, dry eyes and mouth, arthralgia or pleuritic chest pain are equally likely presentations.

#### Further assessment

SLE is a diverse autoimmune disease principally affecting women during childbearing years. The female-to-male ratio is 9:1. Although almost all patients have skin and joint disease, between 30% and 50% will develop renal, lung, heart or central nervous system involvement. SLE has a prevalence of approximately 20-200 per 100 000, ocurring most commonly in the Afro-Caribbean population. The American Rheumatism Council criteria for diagnosis are outlined in Box 5.17.
* It is worth being aware of this spectrum of disease and asking about some key areas of symptoms (Table 5.29). Disease damage and activity indices can be assessed by various validated disease activity scores, e.g. the SLE disease activity index.
* Explore her pregnancy expectations.

### Feedback to patient

This episode of pericarditis probably supports the possible diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus for which you are being investigated. It can cause a range of symptoms including the facial rash and the joint pains, and also pericarditis. Having ensured that the pericarditis symptoms are settling with medication, we should chase the results of all of the tests you have had so far in clinic. I will also let your rheumatoidogist know that you are here, and if the results do confirm the diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus then we should think about treatments to improve things for you. In general, the outlook for pregnancy in systemic lupus erythematosus is good, but the condition should be under good control from the outset of pregnancy, and this is something for the rheumatoidogist to be aware of and advise on. I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has had a rash and arthropathy symptoms consistent with systemic lupus erythematosus, and now has had an episode of pericarditis.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She is likely to fulfil the criteria for a diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus, especially if autoantibodies for it are positive.

#### Outline an investigation and management plan

Systemic lupus erythematosus pathogenesis is a complex combination of genetic, hormonal and trigger factors (e.g. infection, ultraviolet radiation, drugs). There is an increased prevalence of human leucocyte antigen A1 BB DR2 and DR3 in whites with systemic lupus erythematosus. Deficiencies in the classical complement pathway components C1q, C2 and C4 are strongly associated with lupus-like disease. ImmunoblotG (IgG) receptors on phagocyte cells clear IgG and IgG-containing immune complexes from the circulation, and low-affinity receptors have been found in African American patients. Circulating immune complexes are characteristic in systemic lupus erythematosus

Figure 5.41: Malar rash typical of systemic lupus erythematosus.

II, mesangial proliferative IN; III, focal LN; IV, diffuse IN; V, membranous UN; VI, advanced sclerosis IN & Oral dosage is used in resistant skin disease \\ CNS lupus/neuropyschiatric SLE & Traditionally classified as seizures or psychosis Now the ARC classifies CNS lupus into 19 syndromes, with clear distinction between manifestations of lupus and those due to antiphospholipid syndrome Manifestations include strokes, transient ischaemic attacks, seizures, movement disorders (e.g., chorea), headaches including migraine, cognitive disorders, visual loss, transverse myelopathy and demyelination syndromes (acute demyelinating polyadenidopathy), pleoxopathy, craniical neuropathy Seizures due to lupus cerebral vasculitis rare & Lupus nephritis toopathy or quarterly intravenous ‘pulse’ indicator \\ Haematological & Around two-thirds have anaemia, usually normochromic normocytic Around one-third have antiphospholipid antibodies Antiphospholipid syndrome & & \\ Cardiovascular & Pericardiitis commonly subclinical; symptoms in 25\% at some stage Myocardial arteries Acelerated atherosclerosis (e.g. coronary, cerebrovascular), independent of traditional CVD risk factors, now recognised as major cause of morbidity and mortality; \end{table}
Table 5.29: System review in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{p{42.7pt} p{42.7pt} p{42.7pt} p{42.7pt}} System & Possible features & & \\ Non-specific & Fever, fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, lymphadenopathy, CRP usually normal in SLE & Skin rashes and sunkcensus & Sinkcensus & Patients with isolated cutaneous, including thyroidopathy, lupus are sulfuronic \\ Skin (skin involvement affects most patients) & Surrely rash Vasculitis & & & \\ Atopecia & Atopecia Raynaud’s phenomenon Visculitis Discoid lupus & & & \\ Musculoskeletal (arthralgia affects most patients) & Symmetrical, lifting and polyarticular arthralgia common than erosive arthritis & & & \\ Auscultic necrosis of the hip & & & & \\ Osteoporosis (vitamin D deficiency, menstrual dysfunction, corticosteroids) & & & & \\ Renal (affect 30\%) & Six types of IN: I, minimal mesangial IN; II, mesangial proliferative IN; III, focal LN; IV, diffuse IN; V, membranous UN; VI, advanced sclerosis IN & & \\ CNS lupus/neuropyschiatric & Traditionally classified as seizures or psychosis Now the ARC classifies CNS lupus into 19 syndromes, with clear distinction between manifestations of lupus and those due to antiphospholipid syndrome & & & \\ Lung-term high-dose & Lupus nephritis toopathy or quarterly intravenous ‘pulse’ indicator & & & \\ cyclophosphamide & & & \\ Traditional & Short courses low-dose pulse & & & \\ Pulse & & & \\ cyclophosphamide & followed by & & \\ azithromycin similar results with less toxicity & & & \\ Mycophenolate motif & for induction and maintenance therapy in severe profiferative lupus nephritis promising & & & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.30: Treatments in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and are formed or deposited in the skin, kidney and brain; these activate complement and certain complement fragments (e.g. C3a) and induce release of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Autoantibodies may be present for many years before symptoms but often rise in titre with significant B-cell activation just before symptoms develop. Defective clearance of apoptotic cells may also contribute to the pathogenesis.

An antinuclear antibody titre > 1:80 is present in around 95% of patients. Antibodies to double-stranded DNA are the most common antibodies to an extractable nuclear antigen, positive in most patients. Anti-Sm antibody is highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus but only positive in around 10% of patients. Antibodies to single-stranded DNA, nucleosomes, C1a, Ro and RNP are also found commonly. Antibodies to double-stranded DNA and nucleosomes probably contribute to the pathogenesis of the disease. Anti-C1q antibodies are important in lupus nephritis and may be useful in detecting flares. Anti-Ro antibodies correlate with photosensitive skin rashes and anti-Ro and La with Sjogren's syndrome. A normal C-reactive protein with a raised erythrocyte sedimentation rate is characteristic in systemic lupus erythematosus.

Mild disease with skin or joint involvement is treated with topical treatments or hydroxychloroquine, moderate disease with non-life-threatening organ involvement (e.g. pericarditis) with corticosteroids and/or steroid-sparing agents and severe disease with life-threatening organ involvement, such as lupus nephritis or central nervous system lupus, with high-dose immunosuppressive [Table 5.30]. The prognosis has improved in the past 50 years from 50% 4-year survival to around 85% 10-year survival. This improvement has been brought about in part by optimising the use of hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids and immunosuppressive (azathioprine, cyclophosphamide and more recently mycophenolate). With an increased knowledge of pathogenesis, biological therapies are emerging (e.g. B-cell depletion, anti-B-cell stimulating factors).

In general, pregnancy outcomes are good if systemic lupus erythematosus is in remission at conception, but there may still be a higher risk of complications. Risk is higher if antiphospholipid antibodies are present. Risk is much higher if active disease, hypertension of lupus nephritis is present at conception, and pregnancy should be deferred until remission. Complications include recurrent early pregnancy loss, fetal death, pre-eclampsia, intrauterine growth restriction and preterm delivery. Maternal thrombosis is increased postpartum, especially in the puerperium.

## CASE 5.14 SCLERODERMA

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 67-year-old woman recovering in hospital from pneumonia. You seldom have cause to see a doctor but for some years have noticed that the skin over your hands, face and feet has become more tight and shiny, and you suffer considerably from cold hands and feet. You also felt insidiously more breathless for some months prior to development of the pneumonia. You wonder what might be wrong and what should be done.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms, e.g. pain, tightening skin, shiny skin, Raynaud's phenomenon.
* Ask about the duration of symptoms.

#### Initial examination

* Examine the face and hands (Box 5.18) for signs of scleroderma.

#### Size (Fig. 5.42)

There are _dilated capillaries_ (_telangiectasia_), and the _skin appears smooth, shiny and tight_. The nose may appear beaked. There may be _perinal skin_ pucketing with microstonia and restricted mouth opening.

#### Hands (Fig. 5.43)

The _skin over the fingers is also smooth, shiny and tight (_deerodactyla_). There may be _dilated nail-fold capillaries_ (_telangiectasia_), _nai-fold infarcts, ragged cutcles, evidence of digital ischaemia with ulceration, infarcts and pulp atrophy_ (_+_/_-_ amputations), and calcinosis (with subcutaneous calcium deposits in the digits and fingertips). 

### Further assessment

* Ask about gastrointestinal symptoms and examine the chest for evidence of interstitial lung disease and signs of pulmonary hypertension. Important complications of scleroderma are listed in Box 5.19.

Although the terms scleroderma and systemic sclerosis (SSC) are often used synonymously, SSc is a heterogeneous disease within the spectrum of scleroderma disorders, SSc being used to describe conditions involving fibrosis of the skin and internal organs with microvasculopathy. SSc is relatively rare with a female-male ratio of 5:1. Scleroderma disorders share several clinical features, notably skin thickening from dermal fibrosis and a high incidence of episodic peripheral vasospasm. The classification of scleroderma is shown in Table 5.31. Figure 5.43 (A-C) Scleroderma.

In Raynaud’s phenomenon there is evidence of \\ microvasculopathy on nail-fold acilmicroscopy and/or serum antibodies against & nuclear antigens (autoimmune Raynaud’s phenomenon), and there is a 10–15\% chance of developing a connective tissue disease, including SSc, in the long \\ term. Raynaud’s phenomenon affects virtually all patients with SSc, with variable severity. In limited cutaneous (LC) SSc, it typically precedes other features, but in diffuse cutaneous (DC) SSc is more likely to be contemporaneous. Raynaud’s phenomenon may be secondary to a range of other conditions, including other autoimmune connective tissue disorders, haematological disorders (myeloproliferative, phytrombinotic, paraprotein) and exclusive arterial disease. \\ Systemic scleroderma now & This is typically preceded by a long history, often many years, of Raynaud’s phenomenon, leading to what used to be referred to as the acronym CREST syndrome (calonics, Raynaud’s phenomenon, oesophageal dysmotility, siderodactyly and telangiectasia); the acronym is misleading because it ignores important manifestations of LC5sc such as pulmonary hypertension and gastrointestinal disease. \\ Medical & Diffuse cutaneous (DCSSC) & This is defined by sclerosis proximal to the elbows or knees or involving the trunk. \\ LC5sc or DC5sc & & \\ depending upon & SSc, sine scleroderma & This refers to a small group of patients with vascular symptoms and SSc-specific antibodies and major organ complications in the absence of significant skin \\ involvement. ## Case 5.15 CRYSTAL ARTHROPATHY

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Robert Warrell, age 64 years

This man has presented to the acute medical unit with a painful, swollen.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 64-year-old man who has been referred to the hospital with an exquisitely painful, swollen foot, especially around the first metatarsal joint. You have had this once in the past, though not as severe, and it responded to anti-inflammatory drugs. You are known to have to have topheceous gout. You have taken allopurinol for tophaceous gout in the past and decided to restart this when the foot became painful, but it seemed to make the foot worse.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about previous symptoms or any history of confirmed gout.
* Ask about any injury to the foot, historic or recent, and in particular any breach of skin (septic arthritis).
* Ask about any treatment received.

#### Initial examination

* In acute gout there is _swelling of the first metatasophalangeal (MTP) joint_ (or knee, ankle or other joints). The patient reports that the joint has been _exquisitely painful and tender_ (ask before examining!). Weight bearing is very painful.
* cheesy, chalky lesions that may ulcerate
- at extra-articular sites (helix, olecranon/patellar bursa, ulnar surface of forearm, Achilles tendon, etc.). Polyarticular involvement is also common, e.g. knees, first MTP joint, ankles, hands.

An acute monoarthritis that is exquisitely tender and with associated erythema is invariably the result of gout or septic arthritis. Acute gout is the result of an intense inflammatory response in joints (tophaceous gout is non-inflammatory) and there may be fever and systemic upset. The crystal arthropathies include gout, pseudogout and, rarely, calcium phosphate arthropathies (Table 5.34).

#### Further assessment

* Enquire about causes and risk factors for gout (Table 5.35), although many cases are idiopathic.
* Explore his understanding of allopurinol.

#### Feedback to patient

I think this is almost certainly acute gout, which is due to uric acid crystals in one or some of the joints. There is a small possibility of an infected joint. We should arrange some bloods and an X-ray of the foot, and monitor for signs of fever. If symptoms are settling with treatment (anti-inflammatory) then we can be reassured but if things are getting worse then I would ask one the orthopaedic specialists to have a look at the joint and possibly take a sample of fluid from it to exclude infection. The tophaceous gout that you have had is not necessarily associated with acute gout (many people with tophaceous gout will never get acute gout) but is worth taking

Figure 5.44: (A, B) Tophaceous gout. preventure medication for, although we should not start any preventive medicine until the acute gout is cleared up. Indeed allopurinol, whilst good at preventing gout and preventing progressing of chronic tobacco gout, can make acute gout worse. I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has evidence of tohaceous gout and has probably had an episode of acute gout, possibly worsened acutely by allopurinol.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

Sepsis is important to exclude, and pseudogout can often mimic gout clinically although the knee and ankle are more commonly affected in pseudogout than the toes.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Synovial fluid should be aspirated whenever possible (large joint) and demonstrates inflammation (often with a very high white cell count); polarised microscopy reveals needle-shaped, negatively birefringent intracellular (especially acute) and extracellular (especially ulcerated tophi) crystals. Serum urate levels can be misleading. Inflammatory markers are raised; a C-reactive protein in the range 50-150 is typical; higher levels suggest sepsis. X-rays may show perinticular punched-out erosions and eventually joint destruction. Tophi are usually radiolucent but sometimes calcified.

Rehydration is important. Any implicated drug should be stopped. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs alleviate symptoms; indomethacin 50 mg four times daily for 3 days, decreasing by 50 mg every 2 days thereafter, is very effective. Cyto-oxygenase 2 agents

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Attribute & Crystal & Notes \\ \hline \hline Gout & Urate in joint or soft tissues & Described in text. \\ Pseudogout & Calcium pyrophosphate (shed from joints) & This can mimic gout. It often affects the wrist, knee or shoulder but may be polycrudrudrud. Chondracalionosis (calcium deposition in cartilage) is an essential precursor, but also occurs in gout, primary hyperathyroidism, hypothyroidism and haemochromatosis – states promoting cartilage calcification. Synovial fluid is similar to that of gout but crystals are polymorphic, often homoblot of rectangular, sometimes needle shaped, and positively birefringent. It is very rare under the age of 50 years. Treatment is as for acute gout, but prophylaxis is unavailable. \\ Basic calcium phosphate & Calcium hydroxyapatite & Calcifc periarthritis is a rotator cuff problem that tends to occur in midlife. Milwaukee shoulder is a very severe, rapidly destructive calcium \\ periarthritis, Milwaukee shoulder & hydroxyapatite shoulder arthritis in older people. \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 34: The crystal arthropathies

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Cause or risk factor & Examples \\ \hline \hline
5ex & Male \textgreater{} female \\ \hline Family history & Genetics and ethnicity, e.g. more common in Maori population (gout was also common in Tyranosaurus real) \\ \hline Metabolic syndrome & Obesity, increased cholesterol and hypertension within metabolic syndrome \\ \hline Drugs & Alcohol \\  & Diuretics \\  & Allopurinol \\  & Low-dose aspirin /salicy/dates \\  & Ciclosporin /cytotoxic \\  & Levodopa \\  & Ethmbutol, pyrazinamide \\ \hline Diet (usually in setting of underexcretion) & Excess purines, e.g. beer, shellfish, liver \\ \hline Dehydration \\ \hline Underexction (the major group of causes) of urate & Sepsis (lactic acid competes with urate excretion) Chronic kidney disease (creatinine clearance \textless{} 30\% of normal) Hypothyroidism Drugs \\  & Drugs \\ \hline Overproduction of urate & Myeloproliferative or lymphoproliferative disease Tumour lysis/chemotherapy Chronic haemolysis \\  & Inborn errors increasing purine levels \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 35: The crystal arthropathies (equricoxib) are of proven efficacy but contentious. Colchicine is useful if non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are contraindicated, e.g. 0.5 mg twice or thrice daily (not more frequently as was once recommended). Prednisolone 15 mg, reducing over 10 days, is also effective, and corticosteroid joint injections can be used. Biological treatments offer novel possibilities as the pathophysiology becomes better understood. Painful acute attacks would last several weeks without treatment.

As well as tackling reversible causes or risk factors, alloqurium is effective for recurrent gout. Treatment should be covered for 6 weeks or so with colchicine or non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs because allopurinol can precipitate acute gout. Alloqurinol titration to serum urate levels is currently favoured, a typical dose 300 mg but up to 600 or 900 mg may be needed and a reduction to 100 mg in patients with chronic kidney disease. Alloqurinol use should be decided with the patient, but two to three attacks is generally a convincing reason as joints become irreversibly damaged by repeated attacks of inflammation. Other indications include a single polyarticular attack, tophaceous gout, urate calculi and renal disease. Nephrolithiasis and renal failure are caused by urate crystals formed by prolonged hyperuricaemia and hyperuricuria. New drugs such as febuoxstat may prove useful for those who cannot tolerate allopurinol.

## Case 5.16 Osteoarthritis

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Katherine Grimshaw, aged 76 years

This woman is on your ward recovering from a myocardial infarction. She asks if she can have some treatment for her painful hands.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 76-year-old woman in hospital following a heart attack. You suffer from chronic pain in your hands and knees that you have always attributed to arthritis and want to know if some painkillers can be prescribed. You are also concerned because you have had considerable knee pain in the past and seen an orthopaedic surgeon who said you had some arthritis of the knee but that it did not warrant surgery and that your X-rays were really not that bad.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms, e.g. pain, swelling, distribution, duration.
* Ask about any provoking and relieving factors.
* Ask about any treatment.

#### Initial examination

* There is _bony swelling_ of the _distal interphalangeal joints (Heberden's nodes)_ (Fig. 5.45) and _proximal interphalangeal joints (Bouchard's nodes)_. There is not usually evidence of synovitis. There may be crepitus and restricted range of movement. There is no apparent systemic upset.

#### Further assessment

* Consider the features in Table 5.36 to confirm the likelihood of osteoarthritis. Osteoarthritis pain is worse with activity. Any morning stiffness is usually brief, unlike inflammatory arthritis. and the knees. Risk factors for and causes of are listed in Box 5.20.
* Consider other complications of osteoarthritis, including ankylosis, peripheral nerve entrapment (e.g. carpal tunnel syndrome) and cervical spondylosis with cervical myeloradiculopathy.
* Explore her concerns about her knee pain.

### Non-pharmacological

Education

Weight reduction

Physiotherapy (exercise is probably more beneficial than anything, with emerging evidence of joint improvement)

### Oral, topical and intra-articular drugs

Paracetamol

Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) or cyclo-oxygenase-2 inhibitors, co-prescribed with a proton pump inhibitor

Topical NSAIDs for hand or knee arthritis

Intra-articular steroids

Intra-articular viscosupplementation not deemed beneficial

### Dietary

Glucosamine sulphate (synthetic cartilage matrix molecule) and chondroitin - modest questionable benefit, to be discontinued if no improvement

### Surgery

Total joint replacement

Arthroscopic lavage and debridement not deemed useful unless mechanical locking

### Disease modification

So far elusive, but some evidence for strontium canelate

### Feedback to patient

This is absolutely osteoarthritis in the hands, due partly to 'wear and tear' of the joints, although we don't yet understand the exact cause of the condition. As you say painkillers can help, in the first instance, relatively simple ones often all that is necessary. The knee pain may well be due to osteoarthritis, and it would be sensible for me to review the summary from the orthopaedic surgeon. The peculiar thing about osteoarthritis is that the degree of symptoms does not relate to the degree of damage seen on X-ray and some people can have very minor structural changes in the knee and yet an awful lot of pain. In these circumstances it is always worth considering the possibility of other diagnoses although I am reasonably confident that there is nothing more sinister going on. However you and your general practitioner may need to think about the range of treatments now available for osteoarthritis pain should the knee become more troublesome again in future. I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has Heberden's and Bouchard's nodes. 

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She has osteoarthritis.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Structural abnormalities are exceedingly common radiologically. Almost all people over 50 years of age will have some osteophytes; progression to loss of joint space and subchondral bone sclerosis and ultimately a damaged looking joint are characteristic of osteoarthritis. Prevention is important. Some types of physical activity are associated with an increased risk of arthritis in certain joints. Previous knee injury, surgery and even occupational or habitual kneeling and squatting can, for example, predispose to osteoarthritis. Treatments include physiotherapy and drug therapies [Box 5.21].

## Case 5.17 Paget's disease

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Peter Hancock, aged 65 years

This man is in hospital receiving treatment for a soft-tissue infection but mentions that his hearing has not been as good in the past few months.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 65-year-old man in hospital with a deep skin infection, but recovering well. You have Paget's disease although have not been on any treatment. You feel that in the past few months your hearing has been less good in both ears and mention this to the doctor.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask whether the hearing loss occurred suddenly or slowly, and if it seems to be unilateral or bilateral.
* Ask about associated symptoms such as vertigo, facial sensory loss or facial weakness or headaches.
* Ask about a previous diagnosis of Paget's disease and ask about bone pain.

#### Initial examination

* Look for _enlargement of the skull_ (Fig. 5.46) or if there is _bowing of the tibia_ (Fig. 5.47), and feel if the skin over the leg is warm. There may be kyphosis.

#### Further assessment

* Consider other features and complications of Paget's disease (Box 5.22). Check for the bounding pulse of hyperdynamic circulation.
* Explore the functional consequences of his deafness.

## Feedback to patient

There is a possibility that the change in hearing is due to Paget's disease. This can occur if enlarged bone is pressing on nerves controlling hearing. There are many other possibilities but if due to Paget's then this needs to be identified as soon as possible and treatment for the Paget's considered. In the first instance we should examine the ears with an otoscope (torch) and arrange an audiogram or special hearing test. If these confirm that the problem is likely to be due to compression of the nerves and not something else such as a build-up of wax or fluid, then we should arrange a scan of the auditory apparatus and seek specialist opinions from ear, nose and throat and rheumatology experts. I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has bony skull enlargement together with bilateral hearing loss that is probably sensorineural.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

He has Paget's disease.

#### Outline an investigation and management plan

Serum and urine biochemical changes in Paget's disease include raised alkaline phosphatase, raised serum osteocalit (but often normal, and not used in practice) and raised urinary hydroxyproline and pyridinolone (markers of bone resorption, and not used in practice). Other causes of raised alkaline phosphatase include hyperparathyroidism, bone metastases, growth, osteomalacia and liver disease. Figure 5.46 (A, B) Paget's disease of the skull. 

## Case 5.18 MARFAN'S SYNDROME

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr David White, aged 29 years

This man is in your clinic because his general practitioner is concerned about the possibility of Marfan's syndrome. He has recently seen a cardiologist because of a murmur.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 29-year-old man in clinic because your general practitioner (GP) is concerned about the possibility of Marfan's syndrome. You have seen a cardiologist who mentioned the possibility of mitral valve prolapse, and an echocardiogram has been arranged. You are previously very healthy and went to see your GP originally because of ankle pain. You are now very worried.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

Explore what he has been told so far by the cardiologist.

Ask about the ankle pain, and a history of musculoskeletal problems including joint laxity.

Ask about a family history of tall stature and long arms and fingers.

#### Initial examination

He is _tall,_ with a _thin body habitus, long limbs, arandomactly_ and a _pectus deformity_. There is a _high-arched palate_ with dental crowding (Fig. 5.48). 

### Further assessment

* Ask about eye problems.
* Examine briefly for a high-arched palate.
* Ausculate the precordium for evidence of aortic regurgitation or mitral valve prolapse (very common, often with regurgitation).

To formally confirm a diagnosis of Marfan's syndrome, reference is made to the Ghent diagnostic nosology devised in 1996 in which clinical features are assessed as part of seven body 'systems' (Table 5.37) to determine whether that system provides a major criterion or simply system involvement. Plainly this is not to be memorised, unless you are exceptionally unimaginative with little better to do, have a peculiarly agile memory, or are odd in other ways.

Differential diagnoses of Marfan's syndrome on the basis of skeletal, cardiac and ocular manifestations include a range of genetic disorders such as homocystinuria, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, ectopia lentils, Beal's syndrome, Marshall-Stickler syndrome and the MASS phenotype. There is a range of Marfan-like disorders with the presence of fibrillin mutations without Marfan's syndrome. These have a good prognosis, and include MASS (mitral valve prolapse, mild non-progressive aortic dilatation, skin and skeletal features).

## \(\bullet\) Explore his concerns.

### Feedback to patient

There is a possibility that you have a condition called Marfan's syndrome as your general practitioner suggested to you. This is a genetic disorder of elastic fibres that occur in various tissues of the body, including the skin, and sometimes the heart and eye. The most common heart problem is mitral valve prolapse, which I suspect you have, but mitral valve prolapse, in which part of one of the heart valves is a little floppy, is a very common finding in the general population and often of no significance. The other thing to mention is that Marfan's syndrome has a wide spectrum from being very mild to being much more severe, and the fact that you have reached 29 years of age without it being diagnosed is noteworthy; some people only ever have very limited manifestations. The first things to do, I feel, are to confirm whether or not there is any trouble with one of the heart valves by way of the heart scan, and to try to determine if the possibility of Marfan's syndrome is confirmed with further tests. It is very much a specialist condition and we would arrange for an expert in it to see you and talk it through with you in some detail. I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail as best I can or answer any questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has some evidence of joint laxity, and is tall with long arms and fingers. He has evidence of mitral valve prolapse.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

These features would suggest a need to investigate further for a diagnosis of Marfan's syndrome, and there are detailed criteria embracing skeletal, eye, heart, lung and skin manifestations together with genetics. Some people have a limited condition called the MASS phenotype with mitral valve prolapse, mild non-progressive aortic dilatation, skin and skeletal features. However there are more serious manifestations of Marfan's syndrome such as valve regurgitation and aortic dilatation.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Referral to a clinical geneticist is important, as is liaison with the cardiologist about his valvular condition. He needs expert guidance about diagnosis

Figure 5.48: Marfan’s syndrome.

## Case 5.19 Ehlers-Danlos Synborne

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Donald Brown, aged 35 years

This man has been admitted to the acute medical unit with acute chest and back pain. His symptoms have settled somewhat, he is cardiovascularly stable and his electrocardiogram is normal. Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 35-year-old man with known Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. You developed severe chest and back pain this morning and have been admitted to the hospital. You are feeling better after analgesia and the pain is now localised to the mid-thoracic spine. You will also mention to the doctor concerns about increasing falls as a result of the ligament laxity in your feet.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about past medical history and confirm Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.

#### Initial examination

There may be a combination of three broad clinical features:

* _Purpura_ and skin fragility with poor 'tissue-papers' wound healing (Fig. 5.49)
* _Skin'stretchiness'_ which later becomes lax with extensive folds and wrinkles
* _Joint hypermobility,_ and kyphoscoliosis.

Ehlers-Danlos syndrome refers to a heterogeneous range of disorders with autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive or X-linked inheritance. In classic Ehlers-Danlos syndrome with gross joint laxity and skin hypersensitivity, disease is principally the result of mutations in genes encoding collagen type V (_COL5A1_ and _COL5A2_). In a vascular type of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, collagen type III (_COL3A1_) results in arterial rupture, e.g. gastrointestinal bleeding.

### Further assessment

The differential diagnosis of hypermobile joints includes osteogenesis imperfecta, Marfan's syndrome, joint hypermobility syndrome and pseudoxanthoma elasticum. Here, you may take the Ehlers-Danlos syndrome as an established diagnosis and consider whether the chest and back pain might be associated.

* Ask about the nature of the pain (central tightness, pleuritie, worse with movement), site, onset

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Aortic root dilatation & Beta blockers reduce the elastic fibre degeneration in the aorta that causes progressive dilatation, aortic valve incompetence, aneurysms and aortic dissection. The type of fibrillin mutation determines the severity and rate of progression and, if medical treatment fails and the aortic root dilates to 5.5 cm or more, prophylactic surgery is considered. As patients are sunving longer, second aneurysms and second operations are not uncommon. Life expectancy remains 45 +/− 17 years, with scope for improvement. Aortic dissection is associated with an aortic diameter exceeding 5.5 cm, a rate of dilatation exceeding 5% or 2 mm per year or a history of aortic dissection. The major risk in pregnancy is aortic dissection, greatly increased, especially if the aortic root diameter exceeds 4 cm, probably because of collagen and elastin deposition inhibition by oestrogens and hyperdynamic circulation. \\ Ocular manifestations & Bilateral ectopia lentils occurs in around 25% of patients. Lens dislocation may occur. Myopia is associated with an increased risk of retinal detachment. Regular ophthalmic assessment is needed. \\ Skeletal manifestations & Common problems are joint laxity with arthralgia (\textgreater{} 50\% of cases), scoliosis and reduced bone mineral density. Avoidance of contact sports protects joints and the lens of the eye from dislocation. \\ Respiratory manifestations & Petus excavation occurs in two-thirds of patients, and can sometimes cause restrictive ventilation requiring surgery. Spontaneous pneumothorax occurs in up to 10% of patients, and patient awareness is essential. Scuba diving should be avoided because of the risk of pneumothorax. \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.38: Management of complications in Marfan’s syndrome and duration and any precipitating or relieving factors.
* Ausculatte the precordium. Potentially serious complications of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome include mitral valve prolapse and aortic dissection.
* myopia, recurrent dislocations and falls.
* Explore concerns.

## Feedback to patient

It is not yet clear what the cause of the chest and back pain was. However, we always consider the more serious of possibilities first of all and attempt to exclude these. One uncommon but important association with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is called aortic dissection, which refers to a tear in the aorta or large blood vessel exiting the heart. We should attempt to exclude this as soon as possible with a chest X-ray and scan of the chest. It could also be that you have fractured a vertebra in the back and we should look for that on X-rays, too, and then consider treatment. With respect to your falls it will be important when you are a little better to consider further input from the orthopaedic team of physiotherapists and occupational therapists. I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This man has skin 'stretchiness', joint hypermobility and kyphoscoliosis.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

He has Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. Aortic dissection must be considered as a cause of back pain although he looks well now after analgesia and the pain is probably musculoskeletal in origin - a vertebral compression fracture should be considered, and his kyphoscoliosis might be contributory. Cardiac pain and pain from pulmonary emboli should always be considered, but I suspect these unlikely.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

X-rays of chest and thoracolumbar spine (and possibly scans) and an electrocardiogram are essential and management will depend upon cause.

## CASE 5.20 Osteogenesis Imperfecta

### Candidate information

### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

### Scenario

Re: Mr James Fulton, aged 40 years

This man has been admitted to the acute medical unit with acute back pain.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 40-year-old man with known osteogenesis imperfecta of moderate severity with previous finger fractures. You developed severe back pain this afternoon whilst gardening and have been admitted to the hospital. You are not taking any medications. 

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

* Ask about past medical history and confirm osteogenesis imperfecta.
* Establish whether he takes any treatment for bone protection.

### Initial examination

* Look for a _blue tinge_ to the _sclera_ (Fig. 5.50).
* There may be multiple finger _deformities_ consistent with previous fractures.

The most commonly used classification for osteogenesis imperfecta is that of Sillence, modified by Glorieux (Table 5.39). In most cases, the underlying genetic abnormality is in genes encoding type 1 collagen (_COL1A1_ and _COL1A2_). Several hundred mutations have been described and none is common.

### Further assessment

* A likely problem is a vertebral fracture causing the acute back pain.
* Also look at the long bones for deformities and the teeth for dentinogenesis imperfecta with variably coloured, somewhat translucent teeth. Patients with dentinogenesis imperfecta may have accelerated dental caries and are at increased risk of basilar invagination, where the odontoid peg indents the brainstem. Ligament laxity also affects many patients.
* Explore any concerns.

### Feedback to patient

Given the increased risk of bone fractures in osteogenesis imperfecta, and given the sudden back pain, I think we must consider the possibility of a fractured vertebra. Firstly we should get you some pain relief for the back pain, either tablets or injection if needed, and obtain some X-rays of the spine. If there is a fracture then it is most likely to heal without any interventional treatment or surgery, and there are various treatments to help protect the bones from recurrent fractures in the future. I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has blue-tinged sclera and multiple bony deformities.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

He has osteogenesis imperfecta and may now have had an acute vertebral compression fracture.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Spinal radiology can confirm a fracture. Radiology is also helpful in osteogenesis imperfecta to document the degree of limb deformity and any skull appearances that precede basilar invagination. Management of osteogenesis imperfecta is multidisciplinary - medical therapy centres on bisphosphonates, surgery helps limb deformity, physiotherapy increases muscle strength and stability, occupational therapy selects aids and optimises fine motor skills, orthotics may be needed and pain may be chronic and unremitting and require specialist pain management.

Figure 5.50: Osteogenesis imperfecta – blue sclera.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} Type & Severity & Features \\ I & Mild & Blue sclera, lightening with age \\  & & Minimal bowing of long bones \\  & & Dentinogenesis imperfecta may be present \\ II & Lethal & Blue sclera, Severe fractures \\ III & Very severe & Blue sclera, Triangular face, ocular \\  & & prominence \\  & & Fractures _in utero_, limbs bowed at birth \\  & & Dentinogenesis imperfecta \\ IV & Moderate & Frequent fractures, including vertebral, \\  & & some deformation \\ V & Moderate/severe & Progressive deformation, hypertrophic \\  & & callus formation \\ VI & Severe & Progressive deformation; growth retardation \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.39: Types of osteogenesis imperfecta



## 5.2 Evaluation of the eyes

### 5.3 Evaluation of the eye

The eye has two separate visual faculties, served by different parts of the retina:

* Central vision is served by the central retina, dominated by the macula and a super-specialised area of the macula called the fovea.
* Peripheral vision is served by the peripheral retina.

Unlike a camera photographing images in which everything is reproduced with the same detail, the eye fixes on objects using the central retina and everything in the periphery is appreciated in less detail. Whilst central and peripheral visions appear to merge imperceptibly, the retina's functions are discrete. This is why it is possible to lose central vision and preserve peripheral vision and vice versa.

Central image perception is dominated by the cone photoreceptors in the fovea, particularly sensitive to colour. Peripheral images are perceived by rods, which are particularly sensitive in the dark.

The retina comprises an inner layer of photoreceptors, the neuroretina adjacent to the vitreous, and an outer layer of pigment, the pigment retina, which receives its blood supply from the vascular choroid beneath (Fig. 5.51).

A normal eye brings parallel rays of light to a focus on the retina. Conventionally, beyond 6 m is infinity. Normal distance vision or visual acuity (VA) is said to be 6/6 - meaning that the eye sees at 6 m what is should see at 6 m. 6/24 means it sees only at 6 m what it should be able to see at 24 m. Near vision (<6 m) is made possible by contraction of the ciliary muscle and alteration in the shape of the lens to shorten the focal length. This is necessary because rays of light entering the eye have diverged slightly from the parallel, and without refractive adjustment by the lens would converge to a focal point beyond the retina (too long a focal length). Long sight occurs when the focal length is too long for the eye. In other words, the eye is too short. Short sight occurs when the focal length is too short for the eye. In other words, the eye is too long. Long- and short-sightedness are merely refractive errors.

### 5.4 Fundoscopy

Neuro-ophthalmological disorders are discussed elsewhere; here the focus is on fundal abnormalities. The retina is the only part of the body where blood vessels and nervous tissue are visualised directly. Fundoscopy (Fig. 5.52) is ideally performed in a darkened room with dilating drops. Examiners appreciate the limits of fundoscopy and PACES abnormalities are likely to be overt. temporal side. This should ultimately bring the optic disc straight into view. Tilting your head sideways gives both the patient and yourself 'breathing space' and enables you to get close to the patient's eye. The closer you are, the easier it is to angle your ophthalmoscope into each quadrant and the bigger your field of view of the fundus.
* Before looking at the optic disc, focus through the anterior chamber, lens and vitreous as you proceed. Find '0' on the ophthalmoscope. West of '0' are negative numbers and east of '0' positive numbers. Dial clockwise so that you start with a short focal length focusing on the cornea. Dialling progressively anticlockwise, through progressively longer focal lengths, will focus through the anterior chamber, lens (detecting any cataract), vitreous and finally onto the fundus. This takes practice. Remember that short-sighted people have long eyes and '0' will focus on the vitreous; long-sighted people have short eyes and '0' will come to a focus behind the eye; if a patient is wearing their glasses then '0' will come to a focus on the retina.

### Optic disc

* Now examine the optic disc. This full moon-like circle is the optic nerve's head. Note its _margin, cup_ and _colour_. If only blood vessels come into immediate view, follow them backwards against the angles of their branches. This should bring the optic disc into view.
* Be aware of some normal appearances of the optic disc, which confuse many candidates (Box 5.23).

### Blood vessels

* Next pursue the four major vessels into each quadrant.

### Macula

* Remember to look temporally at the macula. This can be done by asking your patient to look directly at the light. The _macula_ is found two disc diameters from the temporal margin of the disc and appears as a pale yellow spot on a slightly darkened area of retina. It can be difficult to see (Box 5.24).

## Summary

A summary of the fundoscopy examination sequence is in the Summary box.

## Summary Box - Fundoscopy Examination Sequence

Check for the red reflex and look for cataracts. Look at the optic disc and be aware of normal variants. Follow the blood vessels into each quadrant, noting any abnormalities. Look at the macula.

Figure 5.52: Fundoscopy. 

### Box 5.24 Macula, optic disc, central scotoma and blind spot

Candidates get very confused about the _macula_, the _optic disc_ (the optic nerve's head), central _scotomas_ and _blind spots_. What especially confuses (and is seldom explained in books) is how disease either at the macula or at the optic disc can produce a central scotoma.

The retina comprises a nerve cell (photoreceptor) layer and a deeper pigment layer, its pigment layer lying over the highly vascular choroid. The nerve fibres from the photoreceptors run in an orderly fashion to the optic disc (where they become tightly packed, their millions of converging axons giving rise to the disc's pale appearance) and become the optic nerve. The most important area of the retina is the macula, an area crowded with photoreceptors, whose axons form the most important bundle of fibres entering the optic nerve, the _papillomacular bundle_.

The optic disc, although an area of densely packed nerve fibres, is an area of retina without photoreceptors to receive visual images. The optic disc' cannot see and its corresponding visual axis is termed the blind spot (Fig. 5.53). The macula, on the other hand, is an area of densely packed photoreceptors (the fovea being its centre of excellence) and its corresponding visual axis is the area of central vision. Lesions of the macula therefore classically result in a central scotoma (Fig. 5.53).

Sometimes it is not just the macula that is damaged but a wider area of receptors and axons situated between the macula and the optic disc. In such cases the scotoma is large, extending between the visual axes of the macula and the optic disc, and this is called a caccoccal central scotoma.

Optic atrophy implies damage to and degeneration of axons entering the optic disc. Because the papillomacular fibres are densely packed and thus vulnerable to a heavy casualty rate, central vision disturbance is marked, and can even result in a central scotoma or caccoccal scotoma.

In the early stages of optic disc swelling (Case 5.23) there is no visual disturbance. Later, retinal oedema may cause blind spot enlargement. Haemorrhages or oedema extending to the macula may cause abrupt central vision disturbance. Chronic scarring resulting from optic disc swelling may cause secondary optic atrophy with a central scotoma and/or progressive visual field restriction as fibres from the periphery entering the optic disc also degenerate.

Arotaic scotomas result from a wide variety of lesions. Arcuate scotomas are scotomas corresponding to damage to a bundle of nerve fibres entering the optic nerve. Primary open-angle glaucoma causes a special type of arcuate scotoma that radiates from the blind spot. This is because raised intraocular pressure damages nerve fibres at the disc margins.

## Scenario

Re: Mrs Zoe Jackson, aged 35 years

This woman is in hospital for treatment of cellulitis and unstable blood sugars. She asks if you might check on her chronic eye condition.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

## Patient information

You are a 35-year-old woman in hospital for an unrelated problem but you have diabetes and background diabetic retinopathy and ask if the doctor might check on this as you missed your last screening appointment. You have poor glycaemic control, having a busy family and not always being concordant with your insulin regimen. You are also currently 3 months pregnant.

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

* Ask about the visual symptoms (visual acuity and any loss) and any previous treatment.

Figure 5.53: The optic disc, the macula, the blind spot and central scotomas. * Ask about the level of diabetes control and current medication.
* neuropathy (peripheral neuropathy, mononeuroopathy/femoral neuropathy), nephropathy and macrovascular complications, e.g. ischaemic heart disease, stroke.

### Initial examination

* Perform fundoscopy. Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a microvascular process in which high retinal blood flow induces microangiopathy in capillaries, arterioles and venules, causing vessel occlusion and leakage of plasma contents into the retina itself. DR may be staged separately from diabetic maculopathy, and may be non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR), which includes background diabetic retinopathy (BDR) and preproliferative diabetic retinopathy (PPDR), proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) or advanced eye disease.

#### Background diabetic retinopathy (BDR)

There are _microneuurysms (dots), hard exudates_ and _'dot and blot' haemorrhages_ (Box 5.25 and Fig. 5.54).

#### Preproliferative diabetic retinopathy (PPDR)

There are, in addition to background changes, _cotton wool spots_ (Box 5.26, Fig. 5.55) and larger blot or 'blotch' haemorrhages. There is _dilatation and heading of retinal veins_ (sausage appearance). BDR and PPDR are now often replaced by the term NPDR, classified in Box 5.27.

### Microaneurysms

These are bulges in weakened vessel walls and appear as red dots scattered throughout the fundus, but most commonly temporal to the macula. They arise as a direct result of capillary occlusion.

### Hard (leakage) exudates

These are discrete yellow protein-lipid-rich deposits. They represent lipid which has leaked from vessels and been engulfed by macrophages. They commonly occur at the edges of microvascular leakage and may form a citrate pattern around a leaking microaneurysm. They may coalesce to form sheets.

### Haemorrhages (dot blot)

Haemorrhages from microaneurysms and weakened vessels appear red but their shape depends upon their site. Deep haemorrhages are round (blots) because they are confined by the tightly packed layers of the deep retina. Superficial haemorrhages are larger and blotchy because they follow the pattern of nerve fibres.

Figure 5.54: (A, B) Microaneurysms, hard exudates and blot haemorrhages. Note that the hard exudates are close to the macula and that the haemorrhages are of different sizes. Sometimes it is difficult to differentiate between smaller ‘blot’ and larger ‘blotch’ haemorrhages. 

### Box 5.28 Neovascularisation

Neovascularisation occurs in response to occlusive ischaemia (which stimulates angiogenic factors) and affects veins, not arteries. New vessels are usually 'wild' in appearance. They tend to loop off veins and can look like fronts of seaweed.

#### Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR)

There is, in addition to NPDR changes, _neovascularisation_ (Box 5.28, Fig. 5.56). There may be extensive _laser photo-coagulation therapy scarring_ (Fig. 5.57).

#### Advanced eye disease - retinal haemorrhage and retinal detachment

There is, in combination with any of the above features, _preretinal_ or _vitreous haemorrhage_ and /or (tractional) _retinal detachment_ and /or _rubeosis irida_ (Box 5.29, Fig. 5.58).

#### Diabetic maculopathy

There is evidence of diabetic eye disease near the macula (Box 5.30). This usually takes the form of _hard exudates_ (tending to form _circinate rings_) and _oedema_ (Fig. 5.59). There may be _neovascularisation_.

### Further assessment

Diabetic eye disease is usually easy to recognise, the main dilemma being to differentiate it from hypertensive

Figure 5.56: Diabetic retinopathy changes throughout the fundus with neovascularization at the optic disc. Neovascularisation is also seen notably at 7 o’clock and 11 o’clock (where new vessels have tended to form loops). Note also the large haemorrhage between the optic disc and the macula.

Figure 5.57: Photocagulation scars. Note neovascularization at 8 o’clock.

Figure 5.55: Detection of diabetic retinopathy changes throughout the fundus with neovascularization at the optic disc. Neovascularisation is also seen notably at 7 o’clock and 11 o’clock (where new vessels have tended to form loops). Note also the large haemorrhage between the optic disc and the macula. retinopathy. Microaneurysms are almost pathognomonic of diabetes, but may occur in venous occlusion. They must be present to diagnose diabetic retinopathy. This said, general physicians cannot possibly be aware of the vast array of retinal and other eye diseases that are the preserve of ophthalmologists. Cotton wool spots, for example, usually in the superior pole and lasting around 6 weeks but changing over time, have the same appearance regardless of the wide range of underlying causes, which include diabetes, hypertension, lupus, vasculitis, heavy smoking, anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy and human immunodeficiency virus infection. Haemorrhagic retinopathy has causes as disparate as Behcet's disease, tuberculosis and leukaemia, and viral infections like herpes simplex, herpes zoster and cytomegalovirus, which can cause acute retinal necrosis.
* cataracts (early onset age-related or snowflake cataracts in poorly controlled type 1 diabetes) or visual changes caused by osmotic alteration in lens shape, ocular nerve palsies (especially a sixth-nerve palsy), increased risk of infection (conjunctivitis, sytes, herpes zoster, hypopyon
- also in HLA-B27-related disorders, Behcet's disease and leukaemia) and central retinal artery and vein occlusion.
* Explore the issues around her concordance with treatment.

## Feedback to patient

I think this is still background diabetic retinopathy, but digital photography through the diabetic screening service is important to be sure of this. We can make sure, before you are discharged from hospital, that an appointment is arranged. I appreciate the reasons for your erratic insulin taking, but I think it very important that we arrange further review by the diabetes team before you go home. Good control of diabetes is vital for three reasons here: Firstly, the level of diabetic control (HbAto) is associated with the likelihood of eye as well as other complications including the infection that brought you into hospital.

Secondly, rapid worsening of eye disease may occur in pregnancy.

Thirdly, worsening retinopathy may occur with rapid tightening of glycaemic control.

I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

There are microaneurysms, hard exudates and small dot haemorrhages in both eyes.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence
