This is probably still background diabetic retinopathy, but for reasons mentioned (poor control, pregnancy, sepsis) it is very important to involve the diabetes team urgently and obtain formal screening soon.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Diabetic eye disease is the commonest cause of blindness in the UK for patients of working age. Two per cent of the UK population have diabetes, 10-13% of whom have sight-threatening retinopathy. Type 1 diabetes patients generally have 5-10 years of disease before developing eye signs but eventually nearly all succumb. Type 2 diabetes patients may have eye signs at diagnosis and are more prone to maculopathy.

Risk factors for diabetic retinopathy include disease duration, poor glycaemic control, pregnancy, hypertension and nephropathy.

Figure 5.58: Subhyloid (preretinal) haemorrhage in diabetic retinopathy. Blood is in the posterior vitreous space and tends to settle inferiorly and outline the attachment of the vitreous to the retina. The appearance has been likened to a bird’s nest. A subhyloid haemorrhage may also be seen with a subarachnoid haemorrhage. Note how a subhyloid haemorrhage obscures retinal vessels (compare with a choroidal haemorrhage in Fig. 5.74).

Figure 5.59: Hard exudates forming a citrate ring at the macula. Note how bright and crisp leaking exudates are compared with cotton wool spots. Note also haemorrhages of varying size. 

## Box 5.31 Management of diabetic retinopathy

* Non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR) with microaneurysms alone requires annual screening with good overall diabetic control. All other grades of NPDR warrant ophthalmological referral (non-urgetury) except for very severe). In addition to strict diabetic control, a decision must be made about prophylactic laser photocoagulation therapy.
* Proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) and advanced eye disease require urgent ophthalmological referral for laser photoacoagulation therapy. Parentieth photocoagulation (scatter laser treatment) is used to create hundreds or thousands of tiny burns to the retina which aims to destroy inelepionet areas of retina (not concerned with vision) and thus attenuate synthesis of angiogenic factors. In advanced PDR burns are used to destroy abnormal blood vessels and can reduce the risk of severe vision loss by 50%. Patients may lose some peripheral, colour and night vision.
* Viteous haemorrhage requires urgent referral and can be treated with vitrectomy.
* Maculopathy requires ophthalmological referral. Focal laser therapy may be indicated but must be balanced against potential risk to eloquent tissue. Intravitreal triamcinolone acetonide decreases macular oedema and is very effective but the effect is transient, lasting up to 3 months, necessitating repeated injections. Complications include cataract, glaucoma and endophthalmitis.
* Other treatments are now becoming available, e.g. kinase inhibitors and anti-vascular endothelial growth factor. Avoiding smoking and treating hypertension are very important.

Screening with digital retinal photography and appropriate management [Box 5.31] has been shown to prevent blindness in 80% of patients with proliferative diabetic retinopathy and 50-60% of patients with maculopathy. Routine screening of all diabetic patients is mandatory at least annually.

## Case 5.22 Hypertensive Retinopathy

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Paul Richards, aged 56 years

This man is in hospital with a hypertensive crisis. As part of your assessment for target organ damage you are keen to check his eyes.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 56-year-old man in hospital following an episode of severe hypertension. You are known to have a chronic kidney condition. You have not been concordant with antihypertensive medication because of side effects (ankle swelling and headaches from a non-rate-limiting calcium channel antagonist). You cannot recall the names of the other tablets you have tried in the past. The doctor wishes to assess your eyes for evidence of hypertensive damage.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about any visual symptoms including visual loss.
* cardiovascular disease, cerebrovascular disease, renovascular disease and peripheral vascular disease.
* Ask about medication.

#### Initial examination

* Perform fundoscopy.

Hypertensive retinopathy may progress through a number of stages:

* _Focal arteriolar attenuation and arteriovenous (AV)_ _nipping_ represent the presence of arteriosclerosis of any cause. Thickened arteriolar walls appear like _'silver'_ or _'copper'_ wires (Fig. 5.60).
* Narrowing of arterioles leads to localised areas of infarction of the superficial retina (_cotton wool spots_)

Figure 5.60: Hypertensive retinopathy. Note arteriovenous nipping, the ‘wire’ appearance of some vessels, leakage (hard) exudates, haemorrhages and blurring of the optic disc margin. and _flame-shaped haemorrhages_. Leakages from these vessels result in _hard exudates_ (Fig. 5.60).
* _Optic disc swelling_, along with cotton wool spots, flame-shaped haemorrhages and hard exudates, indicates what has been termed _'malignant hypertension'_. Although defined by disc swelling rather than an absolute blood pressure measurement, blood pressure is often above 200/140 mmHg. _Accelerated hypertension_ refers to a recent increase over previous hypertensive levels associated with vascular damage on funduscopy but without disc swelling. Beware of diagnosing hypertensive retinopathy if disc swelling is unilateral or if it occurs in the absence of other signs of hypertensive retinopathy. These signs are explained in Box 5.32. Additionally, both central retinal artery and central retinal vein occlusion may occur in hypertension with or without hypertensive retinopathy.

### Further assessment

* Explore the issues around his concordance with treatment.

### Feedback to patient

There appears to be some evidence that the high blood pressure has affected the backs of the eyes. Blood vessels supplying the retina are subject to pressure effects as with any other blood vessels in the body. It would be advisable for us to obtain digital photographs of each retina as a baseline for comparison in the future, and the best solution to preventing future damage is to control blood pressure rigorously, in collaboration with your kidney specialist. It is going to be very important in future to do all we can to keep the blood pressure well controlled and there are plenty of alternative medications which will not cause you to have headaches or ankle swelling. If'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

There is, in both eyes, the presence of focal arteriolar attenuation and arteriovenous nipping, cotton wool spots and flame-shaped haemorrhages, and hard exudates

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

This is hypertensive retinopathy.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

It may be that he has chronic kidney disease from a primary kidney condition with secondary hypertension, or it may be that his chronic kidney disease is driven by hypertension and renovascular disease, or it may be a combination of both. Whichever way, I would gather information from his renal specialist to determine whether further investigations are warranted and which antihypertensive agents might now be considered. Depending upon the severity of his chronic kidney disease and any prior exposure, attempts to block the angiotensin system might be valid.

### Case 5.23 SWOLLEN OPTIC DISC AND PAPILLOEDEMA

### Candidate information

### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Sarah Dunne, aged 35 years

This woman is in hospital with headaches. As part of your assessment you are keen to check her eyes.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient. You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 35-year-old woman in hospital for investigation of headaches. This has been a problem for a few months. You are usually well but your own doctor has been concerned that you are overweight. You take the combined oral contraceptive pill. The doctor wishes to assess your eyes. You are anxious to get home as soon as possible because you have young children.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about any visual symptoms including visual loss.
* Ask about any past medical history.
* Ask about medication.

#### Initial examination

* Perform fundoscopy.

There are many causes of optic disc swelling. Papilloedema, by definition, is swelling of the optic disc due to raised intracranial/cerebrospinal fluid pressure. This should be distinguished from other causes of disc swelling. A sequence of optic disc signs results from evolving optic disc swelling:

* The veins become dilated and tortuous and capillary dilatation leads to hyperaemia of the disc.
* The centre of the disc (the _cup_), becomes _pinker than normal_ and _less distinct_ as it swells. The _wessels seem to disappear_ suddenly in the oedema before reaching the cup. The _disc margins blur_ (Fig. 5.61) as the nerve fibre layers well.
* The entire disc eventually becomes swollen and raised and the origins of vessels completely disappear. At this stage (Fig. 5.62), retinal _haemorrhages, exudates_ and _cotton wool spots_ may also appear, although any haemorrhages are never as dramatic as in central retinal vein occlusion. _Loss of venous pulsation_ implies raised intracranial pressure and is a feature of _papilloedema_. It is difficult to detect and is a normal finding in many individuals. However, _spontaneous venous pulsation excludes papilloedema_.

The pathophysiology of papilloedema remains obscure but involves transmission of elevated cerebrospinal fluid pressure along the optic nerve sheath, followed by impaired venous drainage from the eye. Papilloedema may take weeks or months to develop, and is therefore a late sign of raised intracranial pressure. Papilloedema may be detected in its early stages by fluorescein angiography.

### Further assessment

* Check for enlargement of the blind spot by visual field testing. Unless there is also pathology in the optic nerve itself, the visual fields should otherwise be normal. Chronic untreated hypertension may result in macular oedema (with macula stars), which can rapidly disturb central vision.
* Consider the causes of optic disc swelling (Box 5.33); see also Fig. 5.63.
* Explore concerns.

## Feedback to patient

There appears to be some evidence that high fluid pressure has affected the backs of the eyes. With the headaches it may be that you have a condition called idiopathic intracranial hypertension in which there is a problem with drainage of fluid around the brain. But we do need to consider other possibilities as well, and I would think that a scan of the brain is sensible in the first instance. I'd be happy to answer any questions you may have, although it will be important to understand exactly what is wrong quickly so that we are in a better position to advise you about what to do, and of course get you home without delay. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension, for example, is a condition treatable by removing some spinal fluid.

Figure 5.62: Swollen optic disc (more advanced case). 

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has headaches and bilateral papilloedema.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She probably has idiopathic intracranial hypertension. She could conceivably have a space-occupying lesion. Other causes of optic disc swelling include accelerated hypertension and central retinal vein occlusion, although she has no signs of the latter.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Brain imaging is necessary in the first instance to exclude a space-occupying lesion. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension is possibly caused by microthrombin compromising dural venous drainage, although the cause is not really clear. The condition usually affects young to middle-aged females and has been associated with the oral contraceptive pill and corticosteroids. Raised cerebrospinal fluid pressure is a central feature, causing the papilloedema and headaches, and the condition responds to therapeutic cerebrospinal fluid drainage. A neurologist should be involved in her management.

### Case 5.24 optic atrophy

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the neurology ward.

#### Scenario

[1] Re: Mrs Linda Jones, aged 35 years

This woman is in hospital with an exacerbation of a chronic neurological condition. She has had worsening visual acuity recently.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 35-year-old woman in hospital with a relapse of multiple sclerosis, presenting initially with transverse myelitis. Your vision has deteriorated recently. You are aware that multiple sclerosis affects the eyes and have asked the doctor to examine your eyes for signs of damage.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* central or peripheral, abrupt or insidious.
* Ask about past medical history and the main pattern and complications of the multiple sclerosis (including any previous eye involvement), whilst preparing for fundoscopy.

Figure 5.63: Swollen optic disc. Note the fiery red burst emanating from the optic disc, often seen in retinal vein thrombosis. 

### Initial examination

* Perform fundoscopy. The _optic disc_ flashes in to view, _bright, white_ and _crisp,_ like a bright full moon in a dark sky (Fig. 5.64).
* Assess for the five signs of optic nerve damage (Box 5.34).

Optic atrophy is not an all or nothing phenomenon. There are 'shades of white' depending upon the degree of axonal degeneration, and hence degrees of visual disturbance. Patients with multiple sclerosis who have recurrent episodes of optic neuritis develop progressive worsening of the five signs. Episodes of demyelinating optic neuritis (Box 5.35) in multiple sclerosis are by far the commonest cause of optic atrophy in the UK. While a single episode of optic neuritis (without other preceding neurological features) does not necessarily imply multiple sclerosis, a high proportion (up to 75%) of patients will develop other signs of multiple sclerosis over time.

### Further assessment

* Consider the causes of optic atrophy (Table 5.40).
* Explore concerns.

## Feedback to patient

The optic discs - these are the areas in each eye where the optic nerves that carry the signals for vision join onto the retina - do appear to be paler than normal. This can occur if nerve fibres of the optic nerve have been affected by the demyelination of multiple sclerosis. It will be important to get the advice of the neurologist and in all probability an ophthalmologist on this, as firstly we need to consider how recent or longstanding this process is thought to be, and secondly we should decide what treatment might be best. I'd be happy to talk about any of this in more detail or answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This woman has a history of multiple sclerosis and now has decreased visual acuity and attenuated colour vision with pale optic discs.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She has bilateral optic atrophy.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Visual loss during each episode of optic neuritis may be subtle, often described as a 'steamed up' sensation and often only noticeable when the good eye is closed. A key associated symptom is pain, especially on eye movement, often preceding visual disturbance. Recovery from an episode of optic neuritis is usual within a few weeks, although, even when the field defect resolves, a defect in sharp colour vision persists. Visual evoked potential testing usually detects delayed conduction consistent with demyelination and neuronal loss. Optic neuritis may be treated with corticosteroids, usually intravenously.

## Case 5.25 Retinitis Pigmentosa and Chorioretinitits

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the stroke unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Jane Horsley, aged 70 years

This woman is in hospital with a stroke and your junior doctor colleague has asked you to examine her fundi, which he believes to be abnormal.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

## Patient information

You are a 70-year-old woman in hospital following a stroke with dysplasia. You are progressing well. You have retinitis pigmentosa, which has severely affected your vision, but have been unable to communicate this diagnosis to the doctors on account of your current word-finding difficulty.

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

* On discovering the dysplasia, seek permission to examine the eyes.
* Ask whether there is a known eye condition and following examination attempt to confirm this with the patient.

### Initial examination

* Perform fundoscopy.

There are webs of _pigment_ in the _peripheral retina_ (Fig. 5.65).

### Further assessment

* Appearances are absolutely characteristic in retinitis pigmentosa, although chorioretinitis can produce pigmentation and pose a trap for the untrained eye; these are entirely separate conditions other than giving rise to retinal pigmentation. In chorioretinitis there is evidence of previous inflammation of the chorioretian layers. Patches of the normal pink chorioretian are stripped away to reveal the underlying white sclera. Typically, there are _multiple small white and black patches_, the black reflecting proliferation of retinal pigment (Fig. 5.66). Retinitis

Figure 5.65: Retinitis pigmentosa. pigmentosa may be associated with optic atrophy, the result of marked narrowing of arterioles and retinal cell ischaemia/loss. Chorioretinitiis is often idiopathic, but may be associated with antecedent infection, e.g. _Toxoplasma_. A pigmented retina as a result of normal racial variation or due to pan-retinal photocoagulation scarring or melanoma can sometimes have similar appearances to the untrained.
* Visual loss occurs in retinitis pigmentosa (funnel visual field loss) and chorioretinitiis (scotomas). Both funnel and tunnel vision refer to the phenomenon of peripheral visual field constriction, with preserved central vision (Fig. 5.67). _Funnel vision_ results from disease of the peripheral retina. _Tunnel, or tubular, vision_ is always non-organic or psychogenic. In tunnel vision, the visual field defect remains the same size (like looking through a tube) regardless of the distance of a target object. This is physically impossible, because a visual field subtends an arc, such that at double the distance an intact field should be doubled in size.

## Feedback to patient

My junior colleague was very thorough and understandably concerned by the retinal appearances, but clearly this is a longstanding condition for you. We will of course focus on improving things from the point of view of the stroke and perhaps, when you are a little better, consider if there is anything further we might do with respect to helping you with your visual loss. We will of course make sure that the nursing staff and therapists take account of the visual problems you have when assessing and treating you.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has webs of black pigment peripherally in both eyes. She appears to have some peripheral visual loss.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She has retinitis pigmentosa. A differential diagnosis would be chorioretinitiis due to _Toxoplasma_ infection, cytomegalovirus infection or granulomatous diseases (e.g. sarcoidosis, tuberculosis). Some conditions associated with retinitis pigmentosa include Laurence-Moon-Biedl syndrome (mental retardation, hypogonadism, obesity, polydactyl, deafness and renal cysts), Kearns-Sagre syndrome (mitochondrial cytopathy with ophthalmoplegia, ptosis and heart block), Refsum's disease (a phytanic acid disorder comprising cerebellar ataxia, peripheral neuropathy and deafness), abteiloproteinaemia (widespread neurological manifestations). User syndrome (sensorineural deafness), Friedreich's ataxia and mucopolysaccharides (Hurler's, Hunter's and Sarfilippo's syndromes.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Retinitiis pigmentosa refers to a group of hereditary, progressive conditions that may be autosomal dominant, autosomal recessive or X-linked and that usually begin in childhood. Visual loss is unhappily not preventable.

## CASE 5.26 CENTRAL RETINAL VEIN OCCLUSION

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

Figure 5.66: Chorioretinitiis.

Figure 5.67: Funnel and tunnel vision. You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 77-year-old man who has been referred to the acute medical unit with sudden, painless, partial loss of vision in your right eye. You have a history of primary open-angle glaucoma and ischaemic heart disease. You are understandably alarmed.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about the visual loss. Patients with retinal vein occlusion typically present with sudden painless loss or distortion of vision, although occlusion of a branch retinal vein may be asymptomatic. If the sight-threatening complication of neovascular glaucoma develops, pain as well as loss of vision may also occur.
* Ask about past medical history.

#### Initial examination

* _Visual acuity_ may be _unilaterally reduced,_ depending on the extent of retinal involvement and if the macula is affected. Confrontational visual field testing may reveal _a peripheral visual field defect corresponding with an area of retinal non-perfusion in a branch retinal vein occlusion._ The affected side may have a _relative afferent pupillary defect_ identified by constriction of both pupils when a bright light is shone into the normal eye, but initial dilation of both pupils when the light is then immediately shone into the affected eye because the stimulus passing through the affected optic nerve is weaker.
* Diagnosis is mainly based on typical abnormal findings on fundal examination. In central retinal vein occlusion there is a flock of _widespread retinal haemorrhages_ in all quadrants of the retina (a 'blood-orange' appearance), which is an inevitable consequence of the pressure effects of occlusion. This may be accompanied by _dilated and tortuous retinal veins, cotton wool spots, macular oedema_ (blurred appearance) and _optic disc oedema_ (blurred swollen optic disc; Fig. 5.68). The overall appearance is like a fiery sunset (Fig. 5.69). In branch retinal vein occlusion only a sector of the retina is covered in haemorrhages though macular oedema may also be seen.
* Central retinal vein occlusion may be ischaemic or non-ischaemic (depending on whether the retina is ischaemic), and it is important to recognise signs of ischaemia because this has implications for prognosis. Box 5.36 lists the main signs of ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion. An ophthalmologist should perform fundus fluorescein angiography,

Figure 5.68: Optic disc swelling in central retinal vein occlusion (in this case caused by hypertension).

Figure 5.69: Central retinal vein occlusion. which can distinguish between ischaemic and non-ischaemic retinopathy. If ischaemia has occurred, angiography typically shows extensive areas of capillary non-perfusion. Fluorescein angiography will also identify areas of capillary leakage. Leakage of dye indicates capillary leakage and therefore retinal oedema. Blockage of dye may be caused by capillary non-perfusion or retinal haemorrhage. The position of the haemorrhages can also help distinguish between these. Optical coherence tomography is used to measure the amount of oedema at the macula.

### Further assessment

* obstruction of the retinal venous system by thrombus formation, external compression, or disease of the vein wall
- is the second most common retinal vascular disease after diabetic retinopathy. The prevalence is around 0.5-2.0% for branch retinal vein occlusion and 0.1-0.2% for central retinal vein occlusion. Compression of the retinal vein by a thickened adjacent retinal artery (typically by atherosclerosis or arteriosclerosis) is the most common cause of retinal vein occlusion. Retinal vein occlusion may occur in the central retinal vein, where the central retinal vein exits the eye or proximal to the lamina cribrosa of the optic nerve, or in a branch retinal vein at an arteriovenous crossing. Branch retinal vein occlusion may represent the end stage of arteriovenous nipping, in which the vein is compressed by the crossing artery without being completely occluded. If the superior or inferior branch of the central retinal vein becomes obstructed, a hemicentral retinal vein occlusion occurs and the corresponding half of the retina is affected. Retinal vein occlusion typically occurs in people aged over 50 years, with an equal sex distribution. Table 5.41 summarises the main risk factors. People over the age of 50 with high blood pressure, and those under 50 with hypercholesterolemia, are particularly at risk.
* Conditions that can mimic retinal vein occlusion include papillombleitis and diabetic papillitis, in both of which the swollen optic disc is the main feature, with localised retinal haemorrhages around the optic disc.
* Explore obvious concerns.

### Feedback to patient

The symptoms you describe suggest a blockage to the retinal vein. This blockage has probably arisen as a result of a blood clot, which may have arisen because of the glaucoma or because of hardening of the blood vessels in the eye. I would like to arrange some blood tests and ask an ophthalmologist to see you urgently to see if there is anything that can be done now. I'm afraid I cannot tell you at this stage what the prospects are for recovery of vision in the eye, but we will do all we can to help you, and without delay. I'd be happy to answer any questions that I can.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has a flock of haemorrhages throughout the retina, retinal vein dilatation, and optic disc swelling.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

He has a central retinal vein occlusion.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

The cause is likely to be arteriosclerosis or glaucoma, but hyperviscosity states should also be considered and investigated for. He needs immediate assessment by an ophthalmologist [Box 5.37]. 

### Optical management

Investigations in all patients include full blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate or plasma viscosity, urea, electrolytes, creatinine, blood glucose and lipids, thyroid function, myeloma screen and electrocardiography. Other tests might include C-reactive protein, a thrombophilia screen and autoantibodies.

Medical management is aimed at the identification and treatment of modifiable risk factors (commonly blood pressure, lipids and diabetes), aiming to prevent further non-ocular target organ damage and recurrence of venous occlusion, particularly in the fellow eye. Aspirin is indicated in patients with hypertension if the 10-year risk of coronary heart disease is > 15% or the 10-year risk of total cardiovascular disease is > 20%, providing blood pressure control is satisfactory and there is no contraindication. Patients taking antiplatelet agents or anticoagulants when they develop a central retinal vein occlusion tend to have more severe haemorrhages and a worse visual outcome and the Royal College of Ophthalmologists does not recommend aspirin otherwise.

Oestrogen-containing hormone replacement therapy is relatively contraindicated in women with retinal vein occlusion. However, continued use does not seem to be associated with a higher rate of recurrence. The oral contraceptive pill is the most common underlying association in young women, and should be prescribed with caution in retinal vein occlusion. Rarer underlying conditions such as myeloma and inflammatory disorders should be managed by appropriate specialists.

### Ophthalmological management

Ophthalmological treatments focus on the prevention and management of the main sight-threatening complications - ocular neovascularization and macular oedema. Patients with haemorrhagic retinopathy need early management by an ophthalmologist for better visual outcomes.

### Management of ocular neovascularization

Patients with ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion can develop neovascularization, which may lead to painful neovascularization glaucoma that can be difficult to treat. New vessels can develop on the retina, on the iris and in the angle between the cornea and the iris through which aqueous fluid normally leaves the eye. In non-ischaemic central retinal vein occlusion neovascular glaucoma is rare.

Laser parental photocoagulation is beneficial in central retinal vein occlusion when new iris or angle vessels are visible. Peripheral scatter laser photoacquation of ischaemic retina is beneficial with retinal and disc neovascularization. The precise mechanism of action of photocoagulation is unknown and it may reduce the hypoxic state of the retina by destroying oxygen-consuming photoreceptor cells, but potential adverse effects include reductions in visual acuity, visual field, colour vision, night vision and contrast sensitivity, pain, choroidal neovascularization, haemorrhage, epiretinal fibrosis, and serous detachment of the peripheral retina are possible. More recent evidence indicates that intravitreal injection of anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) agents in combination with parenteral photocoagulation results in dramatic regression of neovascularization. These treatments can be repeated if new vessels recur.

If neovascularization glaucoma is established and the eye is blind, the aim is to keep the eye pain free, usually with topical steroids and atropine. But if the eye has visual potential, it is important to try to control intraocular pressure with topical pressure-lowering agents or surgery to preserve vision. Intracameral (into the anterior chamber) and intravitreal bevacizumab (an anti-VEGF agent) cause regression of new iris vessels and reduce obstruction of the angle.

### Management of macular oedema

Macular oedema, thought to be caused by leakage of fluid from capillaries in the central macular area, is the most common cause of visual loss in patients with retinal vein occlusion.

Laser photoacquation has traditionally been used to treat macular oedema secondary to branch retinal vein occlusion. Grid laser photoacquation in the area of leaving capillaries (recommended 3-6 months after the event and absorption of most of the haemorrhage) is, however, of no benefit on visual acuity in central retinal vein occlusion. Patients with severe visual loss (< 6/60 vision) and those who have had symptoms for more than a year are also unlikely to benefit from laser treatment.

Corticosteroids reduce retinal capillary permeability and inhibit the expression and metabolic pathway of VEGF, a driver of macular oedema. Invariants, a preservative-free form of intravitreal craniomicone, may help over 25% of patients with central retinal vein occlusion but does not appear superior to laser treatment in branch retinal vein occlusion. Dexamethasone is more potent and dexamethasone implants appear beneficial, safe and cost effective in retinal vein occlusion and are approved by the National Institute for Health and Clinical Excellence.

Blocking VEGF appears beneficial. Ranibizumab blocks all isoforms of VEGF and is given by injection and may become more widely licensed. VEGF Trap-Eye is a fusion molecule with anti-VEGF effects. Bevacizumab is a pan-VEGF blocker unlicensed for intraocular use. Intravitreal pegaptain is also being studied. Anti-VEGF drugs increase the risk of stroke and cardiovascular events when used to treat age-related macular degeneration; this may also be true for retinal vein occlusion.

Combinations of laser, corticosteroid and anti-VEGF treatments may have a place.

### Prognosis if untreated or treated conservatively

Not all retinal vein occlusions need treatment. Subsequent visual acuity seems to depend on visual acuity at presentation and eyes with an initial visual acuity of at least 6/12 are more likely to retain good vision, and patients with central retinal vein occlusion under 45 years are likely to have a better outcome. The incidence of new vessels on the iris or neovascular glaucoma in untreated or conservatively managed participants with central retinal vein occlusion is 4.2%, and that of retinal neovascularization (including vitreous haemorrhage) is 7.5%, ischaemia with subsequent neovascularization is not uncommon in branch retinal vein occlusion, but nevascular glaucoma is rare. Branch retinal vein occlusion in the other eye occurs in about 10% of cases over time. 

## Case 5.27 Central Retinal Artery Occlusion

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Harold Sargent, aged 82 years

This man has been admitted with sudden loss of vision.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are an 82-year-old man who has been referred to the acute medical unit with sudden, painless loss of vision in your left eye. You have atrial fibrillation. You take digoxin, but not warfarin. You are otherwise well. You are alarmed to have lost your vision.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* partial or complete, sudden or gradual, other symptoms; there is usually a history of sudden painless visual loss.

#### Initial examination

* Perform fundoscopy.

The _retina is pale_. In the first few days a _cherry red spot_ is present at the fovea because the red choroid is visible at the fovea where the retina is rendered especially thin (Figs 5.70 and 5.71). The entire fundus is otherwise _oedematous_ with a _milky appearance_. The _arterioles_ are _thin_. Neovascularisation ultimately occurs but is never overt, probably because retinal destruction limits the amount of angiogenic factor the retina can produce.

The central retinal artery divides into upper and lower branches on the disc to supply a 'half moon' of retina above and below the horizontal meridian respectively. Branch occlusion as opposed to central occlusion therefore only causes visual field defects in areas corresponding to the retinal supply of the branch and these are limited by the horizontal meridian. tests and ask an ophthalmologist to see you urgently to see whether there is anything that can be done now. I'm afraid I cannot tell you at this stage what the prospects are for recovery of vision in the eye, but we will do all we can to help you, and without delay. I'd be happy to answer any questions that I can.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has a pale retina and thin vessels.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

I think this may be central retinal artery occlusion.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

The cause is likely to be atrial fibrillation. Retinal artery occlusion is the stroke to the transient ischaemic attack that is amaurosis fugaya. Investigations are directed at determining an emblotic source (carotid imaging, electrocardiogram, echocardiography); neuroimaging is usually not necessary. Treatment is with antiplatelet therapy, consideration of carotid endarterectomy and management of any cardioembolic source with anticoagulation. Secondary prophylaxis of hypertension and hypercholesterolemia are important, the latter sometimes responsible for cholesterol emboli. Admission to an acute stroke unit, however, is not necessary as other aspects of stroke care such as prevention of aspiration and early rehabilitation are not relevant and urgent outpatient investigations are often appropriate. He also needs the immediate opinion of an ophthalmologist.

## CASE 5.28 Retinal detachment and vitreous haemorrhage

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 70-year-old woman in the medical outpatient clinic and mention to the doctor that earlier on today you experienced flashing lights in the corner of your eye. The phenomenon lasted for about 10 minutes. Vision does not seem to have been affected. You are short-sighted. You are concerned because a friend lost her vision as a result of blood pressure and diabetes.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Explore the history of any floaters or flashing lights (Box 5.38), and ask about any visual loss.

#### Initial examination

* Perform fundoscopy. This will probably be normal if symptoms have been brief and without visual loss.

#### Further assessment

* Check visual acuity and visual fields for any area of visual field loss or scotoma on an axis corresponding to an area of detached retina. Visual loss will help to differentiate uncomplicated posterior vitreous detachment (PVD) from retinal detachment.

#### Floaters (the perception of mobile spots, lines or haze)

These are usually the result of opacities in the vitreous. They may be caused by:

* Vitreous syneresis (degeneration) and age-related macular degeneration
* Posterior vitreous detachment, which often makes opacities more mobile and hence more noticeable
* Vitreous haemorrhage
* Inflammatory infiltrates, e.g. in posterior uveitis.

#### Flashing lights (photopsin or perception of light not attributable to an incident light)

The origin of light flashes is less well understood, but they probably result from retinal stimulation by vitreretinal traction. Flashes are a classic feature of posterior vitreous detachment (often recurrent, brief events in the peripheral temporal field but may occur anywhere) and incipient retinal detachment, but they may also occur in migraine with aura, and occasionally with choroidal tumours or postural hypotension. * Understand the pathophysiology of PVD, retinal detachment and vitreous haemorrhage, explained below.
* age, myopia, previous trauma (see Table 5.42).
* Explore any concerns.

#### Posterior vitreous detachment (PVD)

Explore the symptoms further. PVD may occur acutely, causing _flashes_ (vitreoreretinal traction) and _floaters_ (glial floaters or light flashes)

* Acute onset suggests posterior vitreous detachment, a retinal tear or retinal detachment
* Visual blurring
* Reduced visual acuity (suggests macular detachment or vitreal haemorrhage) or visual field defect
* Previous history or family history of retinal tear or detachment
* Myopia
* Previous ocular trauma or surgery
* Diabetes in which the posterior vitreous separates from the retina, is the main event predisposing to retinal detachment.

PVD may simply exert traction on the retina and then release it without tearing it. Indeed, PVD without subsequent retinal detachment is very common.

But as the vitreous collapses, traction on the retina (with which it is normally in tight adherence) may cause a retinal tear, which in turn can lead to separation of the neuroretinal and pigment retinal layers and detachment of the neuroretina. If PVD results in retinal detachment or haemorrhage, visual impairment is very likely, and if the macula is torn or obscured, central vision is abruptly lost. The classic symptom of retinal detachment is that of a curtain being drawn across the visual field. Detachment may be visible at ophthalmoscopy, although the narrow field of view does not exclude detachment. The _retina appears opaque_ and its reflex is _grey_ rather than pink (the normally transparent retina becomes opaque in any profile other than its normal attached position and obscures the underlying red choroid) (Fig. 5.72). _Rippling_ may be visible if the detached retina folds into hills and valleys because of any subretinal fluid.

Risk factors for retinal detachment are listed in Table 5.42. Whilst most are _rhegmatogenous_, implying a tear, and of these most are due to PVD as a result of an ageing, shrinking vitreous, less common groups of causes include _tractional_ and _exudative_.

#### Vitreous haemorrhage

Vitreous haemorrhage (Box 5.40) causes floaters and reduced vision. Large bleeds cause loss of the red reflex (other causes of this include cataracts and, rarely, asteroid hyaline bodies and retinoblastoma).

### Feedback to patient

Flashing lights suggest that a small piece of the jelly inside the eye has pulled away from and tagged on the retina. It is a common symptom, often more likely in people who are short-sighted, and the symptom does not in itself suggest a full-blown retinal detachment. It is reassuring that vision seems to be unaffected. However, it would be sensible for us to send you along to the same-day eye clinic for a thorough check on the retina by an ophthalmologist. I can reassure you that there are absolutely no changes in the eye suggesting eye problems from blood pressure or diabetes. Let me know if that all makes sense, and I'd be happy to answer any other questions that I can.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has experienced isolated flashing lights for a brief period in one eye.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The absence of substantial floaters or loss of vision suggests posterior vitreous traction or detachment without retinal tearing or detachment.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

She should see an ophthalmologist urgently to confirm that there has not been a retinal tear. Laser photocapalation or cryotherapy may be used to repair a tear and is very successful although evidence for prophylactic treatment of asymptomatic tears preventing retinal detachment is lacking. Once detached, common treatment options include scleral buckling and primary vitrectomy with gas tampomade. New tissue adhesives are being developed. Treatment of a detached retina is seldom possible if the neuroretinal layer has become ischaemic and fibrosed.

Case 5.29 AGE-RELATED MACULAR DEGENERATION AND OTHER RETINAL PROBLEMS - DRUSEN, ANGIOID STREAKS AND MYELINATED NERVE FIBRES

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the stroke unit. 

## Scenario

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

## Patient information

You are a 65-year-old man in hospital following a stroke. You are progressing well. A diligent junior doctor examined your eyes because you mentioned that your vision has been a little less good over the past 2 years, and has asked his senior to do so as well.

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

* partial or complete, sudden or gradual, other symptoms; there is usually a history of gradual central painless visual loss.

### Initial examination

* Perform fundoscopy.

_Drusen_ in the area of the macula, individually or in clusters (Fig. 5.73), mask the normally transparent retina and its underlying red choroid. There may also be frank _macular atrophy_ exposing the white sclera beneath. There may even be areas of _haemorrhage_ that mask all underlying structures. The disrupted pigment retinal layer may exhibit areas of _hypopigmentation_ or _hyperpigmentation_. The overall appearance of these various changes can be like a _splodge of mixed paints_ split over the macula (Fig. 5.74). The overall consequence is loss of _central vision_.

### Further assessment

* Ask about visual distortion. This is a specific and sensitive symptom of choroidal neovascularization seen in 'wet' age-related macular degeneration (AMD).
* Explore any concerns.

AMD is the commonest cause of central vision disturbance and registered blindness in the UK. Causes include genetic predisposition, age and smoking. Because cataracts and AMD are common in older people, it may be difficult to diagnose AMD with fundoscopy as even early cataracts may obscure adequate fundoscopy; but typically patients with cataracts find that reading is easier when a light is shone directly onto the page and visual enhancement with such a simple measure suggests that AMD is not present (of course it cannot exclude AMD). Visual loss in AMD may occur from neovascularization abnormalities and haemorrhage ('wet' AMD) (Fig. 5.75) or non-neovascular abnormalities ('dry' AMD) (Box 5.41).

Occasionally angioid streaks or myelinated nerve fibres may be mistaken for AMD.

### Angioid streaks

These are _dark red trails_ or _streaks with irregular edges_ (Fig. 5.76). They represent cracks in the retina and Bruch's membrane through which the choroid is exposed. They are uncommon. Many patients with angioid streaks have _pseudoxanthina elasticum_, and they can also be a feature of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Paget's disease or sickle cell

Figure 5.74: More advanced age-related macular degeneration with choroidal neovascularization and haemorrhage. This patient would have marked impairment of central vision. Note that with choroidal haemorrhage the overlying retinal vessels can be clearly seen (compare with preretinal haemorrhage in diabetic retinopathy).

Figure 5.73: Typical driven (heralding age-related macular degeneration). disease. Appearances are characteristic. Visual loss does not occur. The examiners are unlikely to know more about angioid streaks!

#### Myelinated nerve fibres

These are _bright white patches with frayed edges_ (Fig. 5.77). They can look like an exploding white firework emanating from the optic disc or a bright comet trailing across the retinal sky! Myelination of optic nerve fibres is normally arrested before such fibres enter the eye. Failure of arrest during development results in myelinated (or medullated) nerve fibres. Appearances are characteristic. There may be visual field defects but frequently myelinated nerve fibres are asymptomatic.

Figure 5.76: Angioid streaks.

Figure 5.77: A, B) Myelinated nerve fibres. 

## Feedback to patient

My junior doctor was concerned about the possibility of a condition called age-related macular degeneration, which can show up as a series of small deposits at the back of the eye. Briefly it means that some of the retinal cells are less effective than they once were and a little more prone to breakdown. On examining your eye I think you may have very early signs of where pockets of cells have degenerated. I don't think any action is needed at all immediately but routine referral to an eye specialist would be appropriate. I'd be happy to try to answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This man has significant areas of drusen formation, possibly with some atrophic patches. There does not appear to be any neovascularization and he reports mild loss of central vision without visual distortion.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

He has drusen and possibly dry age-related macular degeneration.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

He probably requires only surveillance by the eye clinic. Laser photocoagulation therapy can deliver thermal energy under topical anaesthesia to areas of neovascularization, but because this treatment also destroys the retina it often cannot be delivered to the areas of greatest need (most patients needing treatment have choroidal neovascularization extending under the central retina). The closer the treatment to eloquent areas of retina, the higher is its risk-benefit ratio. In an attempt to solve this dilemma, photodynamic therapy emerged as a favoured alternative. The principle of this therapy is that a drug that can be photoactivated to destroy tissue locally is given intravenously. When it circulates into the new choroidal vessels, photoactivation is applied. Treatments inhibiting vascular endothelial growth factor (e.g. pegaptanib, ranibizumab, bevacizumab), some administered intravitraly, are now proving optimal, perhaps combined with photodynamic therapy; pegaptanib specifically blocks vascular endothelial growth factor 165, the major angiogenic form of vascular endothelial growth factor.

In addition to regular outpatient follow-up, patients with drusen may be taught to screen themselves frequently using such techniques as looking at the fine lines on a piece of graph paper for any black spots or areas of distortion which may indicate underlying neovascularization.

## Case 5.30 RED Eye and Other Anterior Eye Problems - Uveitis, Cataracts, GLAUCOMA

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Andrew Ellis, aged 70 years

This man is in hospital and appears to have developed a red eye.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 70-year-old man in hospital with an exacerbation of inflammatory bowel disease. You have developed a painful, red eye. You have a background history of mild cataracts and chronic glaucoma and are very concerned that you seem to have so many problems with your eyes.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* onset, painful or painless.
* partial or complete, sudden or gradual, other symptoms. Visual loss is variable with cataract and glaucoma.
* Ask about past medical history.

#### Initial examination

* Look at the eye and attempt fundoscopy (this may be thwarted by cataracts).

#### Red eye

Either there is evidence of previous eye disease with scarring (Fig. 5.78) or the eye is _red, predominantly close to the corneoscleral limbus_. Ask about pain. Simple conjunctivitis causes predominantly peripheral, conjunctival redness and does not extend to the limbus and tends to be irritating rather than painful. 

### Cataract

There is _loss of the red reflex_ and the lens appears cloudy (Fig. 5.79).

### Glaucoma

There is _cupping_ of the _optic disc_ (Fig. 5.80). Primary open-angle glaucoma is insidious, often being without symptoms until considerable ocular damage has been sustained. Patients may present because of visual field loss but are frequently referred from optometrists following the detection of raised intraocular pressure.

### Further assessment

* Be aware of the causes of red eye, and a little about cataracts and primary open-angle glaucoma. These are explained below.
* Explore concerns.

### Red eye

A distinguishing feature between most of the serious, sight-threatening causes of red eye from non-threatening conjunctivitis is that in the former the redness dominates at the corneoscleral limbus (where blood vessels emerge from the depths of the eye). This is sometimes called ciliary injection. Furthermore, serious causes are usually painful. The causes of a painful, red eye that are medical emergencies are uveitis (iritis), acute closed-angle glaucoma and keratitis (Box 5.42).

### Cataracts

These may be subtle but symptoms and ophthalmoscopy are usually sufficient for more overt cataracts. Most cataracts arise because of ageing of the crystalline lens. The lens continues to grow through life and new fibres are laid down and old ones not replaced. Yellowing of the nucleus is common with ageing (nuclear sclerosis). The transparency of the lens is a result of many factors, including its chemical consistency. Extrinsic causes of cataract are listed in Box 5.43.

Symptoms of different types of cataract are described in Box 5.44.

### Chronic primary open-angle glaucoma

In primary open-angle glaucoma there is resistance to outflow through the meshwork owing to partial obstruction and additional poorly understood mechanisms. There are three cardinal features of primary open-angle glaucoma (Box 5.45). Risk factors include the level of intraocular pressure, age, genetics and family history, Afro-Caribbean origin, a thin cornea, a large cup to disc ratio and myopia.

Normal intraocular pressure is 10-21 mmHg. Raised intraocular pressure with normal visual fields and discs is called ocular hypertension. The mechanisms behind the triad of features in primary open-angle glaucoma can be explained as follows.

1. The pathophysiological changes in primary open-angle glaucoma have traditionally been attributed to raised intraocular pressure causing damage to the optic nerve head (optic disc). Increasingly, vascular and toxic damage to optic nerve fibres are thought to contribute, and 'primary open-angle glaucoma' with normal intraocular pressure is recognised. Ageing has a role, and the optic nerve, which at birth contains approximately 106 neurons, loses about 104 of these per year.
2. A normal optic disc contains a central cup, an indentation or cavity from which the blood vessels emanate. It is normally shallow and the ratio of the

Figure 5.78: Old iris.

Figure 5.80: Glaucoma with cuping.



### 5.42 Causes of a painful red eye

#### 5.42.1 Useitis / iritis

The usual tract comprises the anterior urea (iris and ciliary body) and the posterior uveal tract (chiroids - the vascular layer which supplies the outer pigment retina). Uveitis may affect the anterior or posterior uvea or both (pannevitis). Uveitis typically produces a painful, watery, photophotic red eye with blurred vision due to the presence of inflammatory cells. The pupil is small and in spasm in iris and adhesions between the iris and anterior lens capsule (posterior synchae) may render it irregularly shaped. There may be keratinic precipitates (clumps of white cells on the inner lining of the cornea and floating in the anterior chamber appear like dust in a beam of light) and precipitation of neutrophils in the anterior chamber may result in a hypopyn. Anterior uveitis is classically associated with human leucocyte antigen (HLA) B27 conditions such as ankylosing spondylitis (up to 20% of patients), Refer's syndrome and psoriatic disease. It occurs in most patients with Babetz's disease (a multisystem vasculitis also causing oral and genital ulceration). Inflammatory bowel disease, sarcoidosis and tuberculosis can cause anterior or posterior uveitis (chiroteritinibs), as can a wide range of other infections including toxoplasmosis, toxocariosis, human immunodeficiency virus and the herpes simplex and zoster viruses.

### Acute (closed-angle) glaucoma

Normally the ciliary body secretes aqueous, which flows into the posterior chamber between the lens behind and the iris in front, and then through the pupil into the anterior chamber. It leaves the eye through the trabecular meshwork, flowing into Schlemm's canal and into episcleral veins. In acute glaucoma, there is complete obstruction to outflow caused by sudden closure of the drainage angle; apposition of the lens to the back of the iris prevents flow of aqueous from the posterior chamber to the anterior chamber. This is more likely when the pupil is semi-dilated at night (iris shortened and fattened). Aqueous then collects behind the iris and pushes it forwards onto the trabecular meshwork and acute block to drainage occurs, leading to rapidly rising intraocular pressure. Acute glaucoma usually arises in long-sighted (hypermetopic) people, who tend to have shallow anterior chambers or short eyeballs. It may also occur in tuboisels of the iris and in anterior uveitis (inflammation of the eye) when adhesions may develop between the lens and iris (posterior synchae). It may be precipitated by dilating drops and myriatics such as tricylics. It is very painful. The cornea is steamy/cloudy/stormy with a mid-dilated unreactive pupil, and the anterior chamber is narrow. It is an emergency.

#### 5.43.1 Keratitis

The cornea may be damaged by trauma (corneal abrasions or penetrating eye injury), foreign bodies and infection. Keratitis and ulceration caused by herpes simplex or zoster infections may assume a puncture or dendritic pattern. Keratitis usually presents with pain, redenes and immidization. Bacterial ulceration may result in a hypopyn. Keratitis may also arise as a sterile immunological response to antigens. An example is marginal keratitis where staphylococcal exotoxin triggers marginal inflammation of the cornea with ciliary injection. It is often the result of staphylococcal overgrowth in patients with pleharitis. When assessing a corneal problem, the eye should be examined completely with appropriate stains, and a safe rule is that topical steroids should never be commenced without ophthalmological recommendation. A rate 'corneal melt' condition is an immunological keratitis associated with autoimmune conditions such as Wegener's granulomatous.

Scleritis may be a feature of vasculitis, sarcoid, rheumatoid disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, inflammatory bowel disease and ankylosing spondylitis. Patients with rheumatoid disease may also develop scleromalacia perforans, a necrotizing skleritis which gives the eye a grey-blue tone. The grey-blue tone results from thinning of the sclera, faintly exposing the choroid pigment beneath. Osteogenesis imperfecta, Eliers-Danios syndrome and Marlan's syndrome may also cause blue sclera.

Episcleritis produces superficial redness, usually without significant pain. It may be associated with rheumatoid disease but is usually an idiopathic self-limiting condition.

Sloose (in 

### Nuclear cataracts

These produce gradually reduced contrast and colour intensity and ultimately difficulty seeing faces, number plates and so forth. It is like looking through a rippled window. Reading is often surprisingly good.

### Cortical cataracts

These scatter light from localised opacities and disrupt its smooth transmission. This produces a whitewash behind the window or dirty window sensation, glare when driving, especially from car headlights at night, and difficulty reading. Sunlight is uncomfortable on a winter's day when low in the sky.

### Subcapsular cataracts

Opardication of the posterior cortical zone occurs in a posterior subcapsular cataract. These are visually disabling in good lighting but less troublesome in low light when the pupils are dilated. There is difficulty with daytime driving and reading.

### Box 5.45 Three cardinal features of primary open-angle glaucoma

1. Raised intraocular pressure > 21-22 mmHg (> 30 mmHg warrants urgent referral to an ophthalmologist)
2. A cupped optic disc with an increased cup-disc ratio
3. Visual field loss

### Acuate scotoma. Patients may start bumping into things.

### Feedback to patient

There is certainly a degree of redness emanating from the centre of the eye and l am concerned about the possibility of a condition called uveitis in which the front of the eye becomes inflamed. It can be associated with inflammatory bowel disease. I think we should ask an eye specialist to have a look at this now and consider what treatment might be best to settle it down. It is almost certainly unrelated to the cataracts or the glaucoma, both of which are very common conditions in their own right. I would not expect the uveitis to affect your vision if we can settle it down quickly. I'd be happy to go into more detail if you'd like me to, and answer any questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has evident cataracts and also a history of glaucoma, although cupping of the disc or field defects cannot be accurately assessed because of the cataracts. He now also has bilateral red and painful eyes, with the redness close to the limbus.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

He almost certainly has uveitis, associated with inflammatory bowel disease. Acute glaucoma is not likely in someone with a background history of chronic glaucoma and is seldom bilateral. Keratitis and scleritis are conceivably possible causes.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

He should see an ophthalmologist urgently. The uveitis may then be best settled by treating the inflammatory bowel disease and with topical steroids after careful eye examination. Once the uveitis is settling further assessment of his cataracts and chronic glaucoma will be possible although it will be important for his intraocular pressures to be checked now.

To treat his cataracts, routinely, phacoemulsification with lens implantation can be performed as day surgery and is increasingly 'sutureless'. 'Vttrium-aluminium-garnet (YAG) laser capsulotomy is also used to eradicate a posterior capsule opacity.

Figure 5.81: Cupping of the optic disc and arcuate scotoma in primary open-angle glaucoma. IOP, intraocular pressure. * Ask the patient to sit comfortably at the edge of the bed or on a chair. Stand back and observe, paying particular attention to the face.
* Look at the thyroid gland from the front. Note any scars indicating previous surgery.
* Ask whether there is any pain or tenderness, and then feel the thyroid gland from behind (Fig. 5.82). Be gentle, because thyroid examination can be uncomfortable for patients. Feel the isthmus, which overlies the thyroid cartilage, with your right middle and index fingers, and then feel the lobes. Note any diffuse goiture or area of focal nodularity. Note the gland's size and texture.
* Consider asking the patient to swallow a glass of water if you find a goiture, to see and feel whether it is readily mobile.
* supraclavicular, submandibular, postauricular, subocipital. If there are palpable nodes, note if they are separate and tender (reactive hyperplasia), hard and clustered together (carcinoma), soft and rubbery (lymphoma), mobile or fixed.
* Auscutate the thyroid gland bilaterally for fruits.
* Examine the eyes for those features in Box 5.47 (p. 844).
* Observe the face, skin and hair for signs of hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism.
* Feel the pulse.
* Inspect the palms for erythema and feel for sweat. Note any clubbing. Note any tremor with the arms outstretched.
* Ask your patient to abduct their shoulders to assess for proximal muscle weakness.
* Look at the legs for pretibial myxoedema.
* Test the ankle reflexes.
* You will probably discover early in the course of the examination if you are dealing with a thyroid lump, hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism, directing your assessment accordingly.

## Summary

A summary of the thyroid/endocrine examination sequence is given in the Summary box.

## Summary Box - Thyroid/endocrine system examination

Examining the endocrine system in PACES will usually entail examining the thyroid (above), or the face and hands for diagnoses such as Cushing's syndrome or acromegaly.

## Case 5.31 Hyperthyroidism, Thyroidoxicosis and graves' disease

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Angela Stapleton, aged 35 years

This woman has been admitted to the acute medical unit with tachycardia and tremor.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

Figure 5.82: Examining the thyroid and thyroid status. 

### Patient information

You are a 35-year-old woman admitted to the acute medical unit with tachycardia and tremor. Your general practitioner suspected an overactive thyroid and sent you in. You have also noticed weight loss and heat intolerance and experienced diarrhoea and generalised anxiety over the past few months. You have slight protrusion of the eyeballs with occasional redness. You are usually well. You are especially concerned because you have just discovered that you are pregnant.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms of hyperthyroidism or thyrotoxicosis (Box 5.46).

#### Box 5.46 Symptoms of thyrotoxicosis (of any cause)

* Weight loss
* Increased appetite
* Heat intolerance
* Preference for cold and sweating
* Diarrhoea
* Tremor
* Hyperactivity, irritability, insomnia or anxiety
* Depression (especially elderly)
* Oligomeronrhoea and loss of libido
* Polyuria
* Weakness and fatigue

#### Box 5.47 Signs of thyroid eye disease (TED)

* Swelling and redness of the eyelids, periorbital tissues and conjunctiva (chemosis refers to corneal oedema)
* lower sclera visible between cornea and lower lid (lower or upper lid retraction refer to visible sclera between the cornea and the lid)
* Impaired lid closure and corneal ulceration, especially if levator muscle affected
* Moving your finger downwards quite fast and asking the patient to follow it, while fixing their forehead with your other hand, will elicit lid lag
* Note that lid retraction and lid lag are due to excess sympathetic activity and not specific to Graves' disease
* Ophthalmolegia causing diplopia (often a complex ophthalmmolegia involving numerous ocular muscles)
* Visual loss due to compression of the optic nerve at the orbital apex (exophthalmos is not always present in this sight-threatening situation and does not correlate well with disease severity; indeed, patients with minimal exophthalmos may be at higher risk of optic nerve compression because displacement of the eye forwards helps to decompress the optic nerve)

Common early symptoms of TED included altered appearance, eye surface symptoms (gritinees, photophobia, lacimination), diplopia (especially at extremes of gaze), blurred vision (due to tear film abnormalities, refractive problems or subtle extraocular palsy - but blurred vision that does not improve with blinking, refraction/pinhole or by occluding either eye may be due to optic nerve compression) and retro-orbital ache. The natural history of TED comprises an initial progressive phase lasting a few months before reaching a peak before it improves spontaneously. These changeable phases can last 1-2 years until a chronic, 'burnt-out' stage is reached when no further change is likely. Atypical features requiring orbital imaging include unilateral disease, no evidence of thyroid disease, absence of upper lid retraction, divergent strabismus, diplopia alone and diplopia that is worse towards the end of the day. The differential diagnosis includes myasthenia gravis, orbital myystis, chronic progressive external ophthalmmolegia, orbital tumours, carotid-cavemous fistula and any inflammatory orbitopathy (e.g. Wegener's granulomatosis). well-defined edges_ (Fig. 5.84). Lesions may be _modular or plaque-like, purplish red or brown_ and the _skin is shiny_, with a _thickened, orange-peel appearance_. Lesions can occur at other sites. They are usually painless. Coarse hairs tend to occur in the vicinity. Look also for thyroid acropachy (clubbing) and onycholysis (Plummer's nails), which affect a small minority of patients.

### Further assessment

* Be aware that thyrotoxicosis and hyperthyroidism are not synonymous (Box 5.48) and consider the causes of thyrotoxicosis (Table 5.43).
* Explore concerns.

### Feedback to patient

All of your symptoms are very suggestive of an overactive thyroid gland. The eye problems can also be caused by this. We should aim to confirm this diagnosis with blood tests and then settle things down with treatment. In terms of treatment we could firstly settle down the immediate symptoms with medication (beta blockers) and then get expert advice on treating the problem in the longer term; options usually include tablets or a radioactive treatment to the thyroid gland, and occasionally surgery. Most patients will settle with the more straightforward of the options, though the best treatment is determined by a number of considerations, not least pregnancy. In pregnancy drugs treatment is going to be the safest option and the condition can be managed very effectively but best under the supervision of a specialist in thyroid problems. I'd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

Figure 5.84: Pretibial myxoedema (note also acropachy).

Figure 5.83: (A, B) Thyroid eye disease. 

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has numerous symptoms and signs of hyperthyroidism with thyrotoxicosis, a diffuse goûte and exophthalmos.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She almost certainly has Graves' disease.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Thyroid function tests are needed [Table 5.44]. Serum autoantibodies against thyroglobulin and thyroid peroxidase are present in 80% of patients with Graves' disease but also in many healthy people, especially women (peroxidase is an enzyme needed for thyroid hormone synthesis). Thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor-stimulating antibodies are not widely available, but diagnosis of Graves' disease is usually clinical.

Thyroid scintscanning with technetium-99m or iodine-131 is useful when thyrotoxicosis without

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c}  & **Disease** & **Causes** & **Distinguishing features** \\ Primary & Graves’ disease & Thyroid-stimulating IgG antibodies are TSH receptor antibodies that bind to and stimulate the TSH receptor & Painless diffuse goûte in \textgreater{} 90\% \\  & Toxic nodular thyroid disease (toxic & Unknown disease (toxic & Pretibial myxoedema \\  & multinodular goûte) & Unknown & Painful goûte Most common cause in older people \\  & Toxic adenoma & Solitary secreting adenoma & Thyroidoxicosis without goûte \\ Other & Metastatic thyroid cancer & Rare & Consider if above three causes not confirmed \\  & Genetic & & \\  & Ectopic thyroid tissue & & \\ Secondary & TSH-sereting & Possibly part of wider pituitary disease \\  &pituitary adenoma & TSH detectable despite high FT4 and FT3 \\  & Gestational & Hypertension & \\  & thyrotoxicosis & & \\  & Thyroid hormone & Thyroidoxicosis uncommon & Elevated FT4 and FT3 due to peripheral resistance to action \\  & resistance syndrome & & \\ Thyroidoxiosis & Destructive & Subacute (De Queruain’s) & Viral \\  & without & thyroiditis & Small, tender goûte \\  & hyperthyroidism & & High ESR \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & Postpartum thyroiditis & Similar to viral thyroditis but goûte painless \\  & & Drug-induced (amiodarone, lithium, other drugs inducing autoimmunity or inflammatory thyroiditis) & Onset abrupt \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\  & & & \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.43: Causes of thyrotoxicosis

hyperthyroidism is suspected, because isotope uptake is low or absent compared with all types of hyperthyroidism in which it is high. It can identify a toxic adenoma by demonstrating uptake in the adenoma with complete suppression of uptake in the rest of the gland. Such hot spots are important to identify as they are less responsive to carbamazole.

Treatment of hyperthyroidism [Box 5.49] may be with antithyroid drugs (carbamimezole) to inhibit thyroid hormone production via peroxidase, and beta blockers to relieve symptoms. Radioactive iodine (331) acts slowly but is very effective; contraindications include pregnancy, lactation and active ophthalmopathy. Indications for subtotal thyroidectomy include a large goiture and relapse. Hypothyroidism is a common side effect of treatment. Management of thyroid eye disease includes avoiding smoking, corneal lubricants and steroids.

Graves' disease is significant in pregnancy because thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor-stimulating antibodies can cross the placenta and cause fetal hyperthyroidism and tachycardia. Antithyroid drugs can also cross the placenta and cause fetal hypothyroidism. Where necessary, the lowest possible dose is used. Breastefeeding is possible with a low-dose titration regimen. For all of these reasons this woman should be treated and monitored by an expert endocrinologist.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c} FT4/FT3 & TSH & Interpretation & Background Information \\ \hline Normal & Normal & Normal & Thyroid hormones have a diverse range of roles in metabolism and development. Hypothalamic thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH) stimulates release of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) from the anterior pituitary, which in turn directs production of and release of T4 and T3. T4 is the major hormone released from the thyroid, subsequently converted to biologically active T3. The hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis maintains levels of circulating T4 and T3 within relatively tight limits through compensatory alterations in TRH and TSH secretion such that TSH is usually a sensitive indicator of thyroid status and an ideal initial screening test for thyroid disturbance. T4 and T3 are unsuitable as a screening test as they may be maintained within normal limits by TSH in subclinical hypothyroidism or subclinical hyperthyroidism, but T4 or T3 (for sometimes both) should be checked if TSH is abnormal. Total T4 or T3 (T14, T13) levels are unsuitable, as alterations in thyroid hormone-binding carrier proteins such as thyroid-binding globulin and albumin may affect measurement of T14 or T18 levels; for example, estrogen can increase levels of thyroid-binding globulin. Thus, free T4 or T3 (FT4 or T3) levels should always be used. Checking TSH alone may be misleading in a small number of situations including non-thyroidal illness, recent treatment for hyperthyroidism, central hypothyroidism (hypothalamic or pituitary disease), a TSH-screening adenoma or resistance to thyroid hormone. Interpretation of TFTs is usually straightforward, the combined measurement of TSH with T4 or T3 usually enabling a diagnosis of hyperthyroidism or hypothyroidism to be made. A small number of results offer confusion. A guide to interpreting TFTs is given here.

\end{table}
Table 5.44: Interpreting thyroid function tests (TFTs) 

### Box 5.49 Treatment of thyrotoxicosis

Distinguishing the cause of thyrotoxicosis is an essential initial step in management, once the diagnosis has been confirmed biochemically. In particular, antithyroid drug treatment can cure around 50% of patients with Graves' disease, will only control but not cure nodular thyroid disease, and is useless in destructive thyroditils. Radioiodine is generally used to treat patients with toxic nodular gotre and Graves' disease that has relapsed, but caution is necessary in those patients with ophthalmopathy and pregnancy and breastfeeding are absolute contraindications. Special care is needed in the management of Graves' disease in pregnancy; the lowest dose of antithyroid drug should be used and measurement of thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) receptor antibodies in the mother can be used to predict the likelihood of the fetus developing neonatal hyperthyroidism.

### Antithyroid drugs

Antithyroid drugs, such as carbamazole and proythituouracil, inhibit iodide organization by thyroid peroxidase, reducing T3 and T4 production. They also reduce TSH receptor-stimulating antibody levels. Carbumazole is most widely used and patients should be warned about symptoms of infection and rashes; the earliest symptom of incipient granulomatous is usually a sore throat but fever or mouth ulcers are also common and patients should stop treatment and seek an urgent full blood count.
