Neutropenia occurs in around 0.1% of patients. More common side effects include rash, fever and cholestasis. Carbumazole may be started in one of two main regimens:

### Titration or reducing regimen

30-60 mg of carbumazole is taken daily in three or four divided doses, reduced over 4-8 weeks as the patient becomes euthyroid and guided by free thyroxine (FT4) levels, usually to 5-15 mg daily.

### Block-replace regimen

40 mg carbumazole is taken daily and maintained, adding T4 when the patient becomes euthyroid, usually at around 1 month. Fever clinic visits are required and biochemical control is often smoother. Treatment is usually continued for 18-24 months, but around half of patients will eventually relapse when it is discontinued.

### Beta blockers

Carbimazole takes about a month to improve thyroid status. Symptom control is achieved in the interim with non-cardioselective beta blockers (e.g. propranolol 80 mg thrice daily, weaning to 40 mg twice daily over 4-6 weeks and ceased once carbimazole has taken effect).

### Radioactive iodine

Radioactive iodine (191) acts slowly, and is very effective, but can cause early transient hypothyroidism, and later permanent hypothyroidism at a rate of around 10% in the first year and a small percentage increase per year thereafter. It can also cause transient thyroditils, and although antithyroid drugs can be used to reduce this they increase the need for higher doses of 191. Lifelong thyroxine replacement is often needed after treatment. Contraindications to 191 include pregnancy, lactation and active ophthalmopathy. Patients with ophthalmopathy should be offered corticosteroids following 191 to reduce the risk of exacerbation of eye disease. 1911 is the treatment of choice for toxic multithodular gotte, except for large gotites and those with retrostermal extension requiring surgery.

### Surgery

Surgery is now much less commonplace given the efficacy and safety of radiolodine but indications for subtotal thyroidectomy in Grave' disease may include a large gotite, disease relapse and situations in which 1911 is contraindicated, unavailable or unacceptable to the patient. Hypothyroidism is a common side effect.

### Thyroidoxic crisis or 'storm'

This is uncommon, but has a mortality of around 50% and should be recognised and treated urgently. Treatments include tropythituouracil 600 mg then 200 mg 6-hourly, stable iodine (Lugol's iodine) at least 1 hour later, propranolol 600 mg 4-hourly, dexamethasone and intravenous fluids. Precipitating causes include thyroid surgery, radiolodine, withdrawal of drugs and acute illness.

### Monitoring treatment

Thyroid function tests should be checked annually if stable, and after 2 months following any dose adjustment. It takes several weeks for results to reflect changes in treatment.

### Thyroid eye disease (TED)

Ensuring there are no sight-threatening complications - corneal ulceration or optic neuropathy - is a first consideration. Corneal lubricants (artificial teardrops, especially gels and ointments such as hypromellose eye dry, Vyectase gel and Lori-Lube ointment) offer corneal protection and symptom relief. Smoking increases the risk of developing TED by seven- to eight-fold and cessation is essential. Correcting thyroid dysfunction is usually followed by improvement of eye symptoms over several months. A small risk of exacerbation of TED has been reported after radiolodine, especially in smokers, whilst antithyroid drugs and thyroidectomy seem to be neutral. Radiolodine may be administered once TED is inactive or easier with a short course of steroids. The main medical treatment is steroids. Pulse therapy with intravenous methylenediisolone (initially 500-1000 mg weekly, for 10-12 weeks) is more beneficial than oral steroids. Orbital irradiation may consolidate the effects of steroids. Either treatment is only effective in active TED. Surgery may have a role in all phases of TED. Patients with optic neuropathy who do not respond to or who cannot tolerate steroids must be considered for urgent orbital decompression. Rehabilitative reconstructive surgery may be considered one TED is inactive. Prisms (Special lenses fitted to spectacles to refract light and thus achieve binocular vision when eyes are misaligned) are helpful in diplopia. Rituximab (humanised mouse monoclonal antibody to CD20) may have a role. TED should be referred urgently to specialists (European Group on Graves' Orbitopathy recommendations) if there is visual loss or change in intensity or colour, a corneal opacity, inability to close the eyes fully to cover the cornea or a swollen optic disc. Other troubling or progressive problems should be referred electively. 

## Case 5.32 Hypothyroidism

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Marjorie Dawe, aged 70 years

This woaman has been admitted to the acute medical unit with a possible non-ST elevation myocardial infarction and is stable but reports to the doctor that she has become extremely tried in the past few months.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 70-year-old woman admitted to the acute medical unit with a possible non-ST elevation myocardial infarction. You are now stable. You have also noticed extreme tiredness, weight gain and cold intolerance and experienced constipation and mood change over the past few months. You are usually well, but you are always so tired and wish you could have more energy.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms of features of hypothyroidism (Box 5.50).

#### Box 5.50 Symptoms of hypothyroidism

* Tiredness/exhaustion/somnolence
* Weight gain
* Preference for warmth/intolerance of cold
* Mood change including depression, or slow cognition
* Constipation
* Mentrust disturbance including menorrhagaja
* Myalgia, especially calf discomfort and stiffness
* Carpal tunnel syndrome
* Hearing impairment

#### Initial examination

* puffiness especially below the eyes, and sometimes _hoarseness_), _dry skin, and fine, brittle hair with loss of the outer eyebrows_ (Fig. 5.85). There may be delayed relaxation of ankle and other deep tendon reflexes.

#### Further assessment

* Consider the possible causes of hypothyroidism (Box 5.51). The commonest cause in developed countries is autoimmune thyroiditis, which may be associated

Figure 5.85: Hypothyroidism. with goitre (Hashimoto's thyroiditis) or, with equal frequency, thyroid atrophy.
* serous effusions (pleural, pericardial, peritoneal, joint), heart failure, bradycardia, dyslipidaemia (raised total cholesterol and low-density lipoprotein with increased cardiovascular disease risk), anaemia (iron deficiency, macrocytic, pernicious, normochromic, normocytic), cerebellar dysfunction, psychiatric manifestations, and impaired consciousness ( myxoedema coma).
* Explore concerns.

## Feedback to patient

All of your symptoms are very suggestive of an underactive thyroid gland. We should aim to confirm this diagnosis with blood tests and then settle things down with treatment. This is a very common condition and treatment is usually successful with a small dose of medication. One thing for us to consider, especially if this turns out to be a small heart attack, is cholesterol levels since an underactive thyroid can provoke high cholesterol levels. You will of course be feeling under the weather as a result of the heart attack but it is very likely that an underactive thyroid has been making you feel chronically tired and hopefully treatment would help this. I'd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has numerous symptoms and signs of hypothyroidism.

### Box 5.53 Treatment of hypothyroidism

### When to treat

Treating hypothyroidism is generally considered straightforward and mostly carried out in primary care; however, up to 50% of patients are overtreated or undertreated and a small but significant proportion of patients continue to feel unwell despite optimum treatment.

### Over hypothyroidism with thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) > 10 mU/l

Symptomatic people with TSH concentrations > 10 mU/l should be treated. Except in drug-induced hypothyroidism or the recovery phase of thyroiditis there is little chance of spontaneous recovery; treatment is therefore lifelong. TSH levels should be confirmed on a second sample. Adults with symptoms usually feel better treated, and treatment may also reverse dyslipidaemia and so benefit vascular risk.

Subclinical (mild) hypothyroidism: TSH between 5 and 10 mU/l (free serum thyroxine (T4) in reference range)

Treatment of subclinical hypothyroidism with levothyroxine is controversial. A 20-year follow-up study showed only small risk of progression to overt hypothyroidism, correlating with TSH levels and the presence of thyroid peroxidase antibodies. Some meta-analyses have shown links between subclinical hypothyroidism and cardiovascular morbidity and mortality but studies have failed to show benefit from leon/styroxine replacement and it remains unclear as to whether vascular risk can be ameliorated by early thyroid hormone replacement. Further, in people aged more than 85 years, evidence suggests that subclinical hypothyroidism is associated with longevity. An expert panel did not support routinely treating subclinical hypothyroidism. In practice the TSH level should be re-measured, along with testing for thyroid peroxidase antibodies, within 3 months of the initial test to ascertain the persistence of the abnormal TSH and the tempo of thyroid failure. If there are symptoms consistent with hypothyroidism and the increased TSH persists, a therapeutic trial of leothyroxine for 3-6 months is reasonable. If the patient feels better - a third to half do - it is reasonable to continue treatment. If there are no symptoms and the TSH level seems stable with thyroid peroxidase antibodies present, the risk of progression to overt hypothyroidism is just under 5% per year and yearly TSH surveillance is satisfactory. If there are no symptoms and the TSH level seems stable with thyroid peroxidase antibodies absent, the risk of progression to overt hypothyroidism is around 2% per year and TSH surveillance every 3 years is recommended. In pregnancy, or in someone trying to conceive, mild hypothyroidism should always be treated.

### Symptoms of hypothyroidism but normal TSH

Although a normal TSH level excludes primary hypothyroidism, without a serum free T4 assay secondary hypothyroidism from pituitary or hypothalamic disease cannot be excluded. Secondary hypothyroidism is almost always associated with other hormone deficiencies and clinical clues, e.g. low gonadotrophin levels in a postmenopausal woman, amenorrhoea, galactorrhea, erectile dysfunction. Because of the possibility of secondary adrenal failure, full evaluation of pituitary function is important before starting leukotrivane in secondary hypothyroidism.

### How to treat

Levothyroxine is the treatment of choice. Although commonly titrated upwards from 25 to 50 mg daily, this is unnecessary for most patients who may receive full replacement of 75 or 100 mg from the outset. Exceptions are those aged more than 60 years or those with ischaemic heart disease. The dose requirement depends on lean body mass, a daily dose of 1.6 mg/kg body weight rendering most patients euthorol. This equates to 100 mg daily for a 60 kg woman and 125 mg daily for a 75 kg man. Combination therapy (T3 tri-iodothyronline with T4 levothyroxine) for primary hypothyroidism is not beneficial.

### Monitoring treatment

Seum TSH measurement is used to monitor treatment except in pituitary disease. In longstanding untreated hypothyroidism there is pituitary thyrotroph hyperplasia, so TSH levels commonly take 3-6 months to fall into the reference range, even with full-dose levothyroxine. After starting levitvoflurane, TSH and free T4 levels should be measured at 8-12 weeks with dose adjustments made accordingly. Measuring TSH annually is sufficient for those on a stable dose of levothyroxine. Pregnancy, to a lesser extent oestrogen use, and large changes in body weight lead to more unpredictable TSH fluctuations. Dose requirements for levothyroxine tend to decrease with age with reduced clearance of T4 and reduced body mass.

### The target TSH level

The aim of levothyroxine treatment is symptom improvement, and the dose should usually be adjusted to maintain TSH levels within the lower half of the reference range, around 0.4-2.5 mU/l. If a patient feels perfectly well with a level in the upper half of the reference range, adjustment is unnecessary. If persistent fatigue, somnolence, or subtle cognitive problems persist then it is reasonable to increase the dose by 25 mg daily, or on alternate days. A fully suppressed serum TSH level (< 0.1 mU/l) should be avoided and is not associated with improved well-being, although a low level (0.1-0.4 mU/l) may be acceptable in a younger person. Low TSH levels in older people (> 60 years) should trigger a small dose reduction of 25 mg daily, or on alternate days since low levels of TSH (0.1-0.4 mU/l) increase the risk of osteoporosis and may contribute to increased risk of atrial fibrillation.

### A persistently raised TSH level despite levothyroxine replacement

Four possibilities should be considered:
1. _Non-concordance with levothyroxine therapy_. Larger doses of levothyroxine than 150 mg daily are seldom necessary. It has a half-life of 7 days in the bloodstream and, if one tablet is missed, there will be no noticeable effect. In most people it is safe to recommend a double dose on the day after a missed tablet (exceptions being active ischaemic heart disease and atrial fibrillation). Taking levothyroxine weekly (seven times the daily dose taken once a week) is a safe regimen. Levothyroxine should be taken on an empty stomach to maximise absorption.
2. _Drugs_. Some drugs prevent absorption of levothyroxine (calcium salts, ferrous sulphate, aluminium hydroxide, cholestyramine). Some drugs increase clearance of levothyroxine (pherytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbitone, rifampicin).
3. _Malabsorption_. Coeliac disease and autoimmune gastritis should be considered.
4. _Interference with laboratory assay_. The presence of heterophil antibodies can lead to artefacts.

### Persistence of symptoms despite a well-controlled TSH level (0.1-2.5 mU/l)

It may take several months before symptoms of hypothyroidism fully resolve (especially muscle weakness, stiffness, or cognitive defects) after biochemical correction. If symptoms persist, other diagnoses should be considered, as mild abnormalities of thyroid function are common and may be incidental. If a patient clearly reports feeling worse on levothyroxine, this suggests Addison's disease. Other autoimmune conditions, notably perilcious anaemia, may coexist with hypothyroidism. Sleep apnea may have non-specific symptoms superletially similar to hypothyroidism (tiredess, weight gain, poor concentration), and these may have triggered testing of thyroid function. If the TSH level is over 1.5 mU/l, a small increment of 25 mg on alternate days should be considered or a change to nocturnal dosing, which may provide a subtle increase in absorption. Some people continue to feel symptoms despite adequate replacement and absence of other identifiable conditions.

### Hypothyofilm in pregnancy

Matemal hypothyroidism in pregnancy is associated with adverse obstetric outcomes and long-term developmental sequelae. Specialist management is needed.

### Myxoedema coma

This is an uncommon complication seen in older people, often precipitated by illness such as infection. Reduced consciousness and hypothermia are common, not necessarily with coma. Heart failure, hypotension, hypoventilation and hyponatraemia also occur. Treatment is supportive, with intravenous fluids, slow re-warming, ventilation, antibiotics and intravenous T3 followed by oral or nasoogastric T4 once improving. Hydrocortisone is given if hypodrenalism is also possible.

### Screening

Routine screening for thyroid disease is not recommended except for patients with Down's syndrome and Turner's syndrome, patients taking certain drugs (e.g. amiodarone, lithium, thalidomide, interfering, suntitis, rifampicin), patients who have received radioloding treatment or neck radiotherapy, patients who have had subtotal thyroidectomy and patients with type 1 diabetes and autoimmune Addison's disease. detectable in over 90% and about 70% of patients, respectively.

Hyprothyroidism in older patients and those with ischaemic heart disease should always be managed with caution. Because longstanding hypothyroidism may cause bradycardia, which can mask substantial asymptomatic coronary artery disease, levothyroxine should be replaced cautiously in patients over 60 years or those with known ischaemic heart disease. Particular caution is needed in profound and longstanding hypothyroidism (TSI > 50 mU/l). Here, or in active angina or following a recent acute coronary syndrome, the starting dose of levothyroxine should be 12.5 or 25 mg daily, increased every 3-6 weeks until euthyroidism is achieved.

## CASE 5.33 GOITRE AND NECK LUMPS

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor from the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Sue Richards, aged 40 years

This woman was admitted to the orthopaedic ward for a carpal tunnel release operation. She mentioned to the orthopaedic doctors that she has a swelling in her neck and they have asked for the opinion of a physician. You were asked by your consultant to assess the suspected swelling in her neck.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 40-year-old woman on the orthopaedic ward and you mentioned to the admitting doctor that your neck seemed swollen. One of the medical doctors has been asked to see you about this. You are having a short admission to have surgery for carpal tunnel syndrome. You have not mentioned the neck swelling to a doctor before but your sister, who has an underactive thyroid, has been commenting on it for a few years. You do not have any of the symptoms your sister had when her thyroid was underactive. Indeed you feel well. Your weight is stable. Your skin and hair are normal. You do not seem to feel the heat or the cold any more than anyone else. Your bowel works normally. You do not have any problem with swallowing. You want to know why your neck seems swollen and whether there might be something wrong with your thyroid gland and what should be done.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms of the neck lump. Most are asymptomatic and most are euthyroid (endocrine glands generally comprise much redundant tissue). Pressure symptoms (dysphagia, dysphagia, stridor) or pain (especially with bleeding into an adenoma) are possible.
* Ask about symptoms of hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism (Cases 5.31 and 5.32).

#### Initial examination

* Note if there is a diffuse goitre, a multinodular goitre or a single thyroid nodule (Fig. 5.86).

#### Further assessment

* Consider causes (Box 5.54). The key issue is to exclude malignancy (Table 5.45) with further investigations.
* Explore concerns.

Figure 5.86 (A, B) Neck lump. 

## Feedback to patient

You have a swelling of the thyroid gland, sometimes known as goitre. These swellings do not usually give rise to symptoms of an overactive or underactive thyroid but of course the swelling itself can be uncomfortable and we should aim to establish what has caused it and then treat it. Usually thyroid lumps are benign but occasionally not and I think we should arrange a thyroid scan in the first instance to assess it in more detail. We should also arrange some blood tests to make sure that the thyroid gland is not becoming overactive or underactive. I'd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This lady has a thyroid lump, possibly an asymmetric or possibly a large single nodule.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The possible causes are wide ranging and include a simple non-toxic goitre, Graves' disease, autoimmune thyroiditis, lymphoma or a large nodule.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

She will require imaging to determine the nature of the mass and thyroid function tests to determine thyroid status. Thyroid lumps are common, often silent, and increasingly prevalent with age. Nodules may be solid, cystic, mixed (e.g. cystic degeneration within an adenoma) or calcified. The key issue is to exclude malignancy, although most thyroid lumps are benign. It is not possible without scanning to determine if a single palpable nodule is part of a multinodular thyroid. Measurement of size and exclusion of tracheal compression are best assessed by computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging of the neck and thoracic inlet. Radionuclide imaging is less often used these days as it does not absolutely exclude malignancy, and although most cold nodules are benign and malignant nodules hot the latter may sometimes be cold. Fine-needle aspiration is the gold standard for diagnosis short of open excision. It is not possible to distinguish between a follicular adenoma and a follicular carcinoma on cytology and all follicular lesions should be excised. Indications for surgery include malignancy, concern about malignancy (e.g. large cysts are more likely to be malignant), continuing enlargement, pressure symptoms (dysphabia, dysphonia, stridor or pain) and cosmetic reasons.

## Case 5.34 Hypopitiuitarism

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Graham Ellis, aged 76 years

This man is in the medical outpatient clinic. He has been feeling very brief over the past few months. Ten years ago he had radiotherapy to the pituitary gland for a benign condition.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan. 

### Patient information

You are a 76-year-old man in the medical outpatient clinic. You have been feeling very tired over the past few months, and sometimes lightheaded, and have noticed generalised loss of body hair. Your family thinks that you have a pale, sallow complexion. Ten years ago you had radiotherapy to the pituitary gland for a benign condition. You wonder if this might be relevant.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* syndrome of inappropriate ADH and diabetes inspiidus
- are discussed elsewhere.

#### Initial examination

Classic signs of hypopitiutarism include _pallor, fine facial skin, decreased axillary and public hair, breast atrophy_ in females and other signs of _hypogonadism_, reflecting the fact that gonadotrophins tend to be the early hormones lost. Hypopitiutarism is shown in Figure 5.87.

### Further assessment

Causes of hypopitiutarism are listed in Box 5.55.

The three common secreting adenomas are outlined in Table 5.47, prolactinomas the most common. Non-functioning adenomas are also common. Rarer functioning adenomas secrete TSH or gonadotrophins. Adenomas are usually solitary and monoclonal, and in some activating mutations in G proteins have been identified as in many cases of acromegaly in which there is mutation of the G protein linking the growth-hormone-releasing hormone receptor to adenylate cyclase in growth hormone-secreting cells. Pituitary adenomas are sometimes part of multiple endocrine neoplasia. The four possible groups of ways in which a pituitary tumour might present are also outlined in Table 5.47.

#### Feedback to patient

Your symptoms would suggest the possibility of an underactive pituitary gland, possibly but not necessarily contributed to by the previous

Figure 5.87: Hypopitiutarism.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c} \hline
**Deficient** & **Symptoms and** & **Tests of** \\
**hormone** & **signs** & **deficiency** \\ \hline Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) & Fatigue Cold intolerance & Low TSH or thyroxine \\ Weight gain & & \\ Dry skin & & \\ \hline Adtenocorticotrophic hormone (ACH) & Fatigue Nausea and vomiting & Low morning cortisol (\textless{} 100 nmol/l) \\ Hypoglycaemia and hypotension & & with normal or low ACTH \\ (presyncope) & & Hypoglycaemia Insulin-induced hypoglycemia (failure to stimulate ACTH) \\ \hline Growth hormone & Loss of well-being Altered body composition with increased fat mass and central fat & Low insulin-like growth factor-1 \\  & Reduced muscle strength & \\ \hline Lutteinising hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) & Women – oligo / amenorrhoea, infertility, breast atrophy & Low estradiol with normal or reduced basal LH or FSH \\ \hline Men – loss of libido, exercise dysfunction, infertility, loss of body hair & Low testosterone with normal or reduced basal LH or FSH \\ \hline Antidiuretic hormone & Diabetes inspiidus & Water deprivation test (see Case 2.30) \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.46: Symptoms and signs of hypopitiutarism 

#### Pituitary adenoma

Causes destruction or pressure on pituitary cells, especially macroadenoma

Other masses of the pituitary, sella or suprasellar cistern

Craniopharyngioma

Meninjoma

Metastases

Cyrts

Aneuvsm

Optic glioma

Granulomatous disease, e.g. sarcoidosis

Infection

An enlarged sella filled with cerebrospinal fluid post-infarction, surgery or radiotherapy is known as empty sella syndrome.

### Previous pituitary radiotherapy or surgery

#### Pituitary haemorrhage (apoplexy) or infarction

e.g. Sheehan's syndrome - acute pituitary infarction due to hypovolaemia or septic shock as in meningococcalaemia 

## Chapter 5 Empirical Evaluation

### 5.1 Introduction

The purpose of this thesis is to provide a comprehensive overview of the experimental results and the experimental results.

The experimental results are presented in the following chapters.

* _The experimental results are presented in the following chapters.
* _The experimental results are presented in the following chapters. 

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Diagnosis is by combined pituitary function testing and magnetic resonance imaging of the pituitary and sella region. The individual missing hormones should be replaced [Table 5.48].

### Case 5.35 ACROMEGALY

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Harold Ford, aged 55 years

This man has been feeling generally unwell over the past few months, and noticed an increase in denture size.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 55-year-old man in the medical outpatient clinic. You have had symptoms for a few months (and maybe years when you think back as they have been so insidious) of fatigue, sweating, joint pains, headaches and a need for increasing sizes of shoes and dentures. You have also recently been diagnosed with early diabetes, which troubles you because your friend has diabetes and has to take insulin and has a multitude of problems. You wonder what might be wrong with you and what should be done.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms of acromegaly. Some of the terms once used are rather old fashioned, even insulting ('spade-like hands'), and of little practical value. Newly diagnosed patients have often had symptoms for years, including increased sweating, increased size of shoes, gloves, rings or dentures, fatigue, joint pains and headaches. It typically becomes apparent between the ages of 40 and 60 years.

#### Initial examination

* Recognising acromegaly may be straightforward, although signs can be subtle as disease slowly and insidiously evolves. Examine the hands and face.

#### Face (Fig. 5.88A,B)

There are _prominent supraorbital ridges_. There is _prognathism_ (protrusion of lower jaw), _soft-tissue enlargement of the nose, tongue and ears_. The _intendental distances are increased_. There is _general coarsening of features_. The voice may be husky.

#### Hands (Fig. 5.88C,D)

These are _'spade-like'_, squared and large. The skin is _thick_, often with a rubbery texture and moist.

#### Further assessment

* Acromegaly is a rare, chronic, debilitating condition resulting from hypersecretion of growth hormone from a macroadenoma, which, if untreated, not only causes significant morbidity but also reduces life expectancy by about 10 years. Complications may be from the acromegaly itself or there may be symptoms arising from hypofunction of the compressed anterior pituitary such as hypogonadism or from local compressive symptoms (Table 5.49). State that you would check blood pressure and plasma glucose, and examine for bitemporal hemianopia.
* Explore concerns.

### Feedback to patient

Your symptoms would suggest the possibility of a condition called acromegaly. The pituitary gland at the base of the brain produces numerous hormones including hormones for growth and well-being, and sometimes hormone production becomes overactive, sometimes as a result of a benign growth in the hormone production site. Acromegaly is the result of overproduction of growth hormone. We should arrange blood tests to check the levels of all your pituitary hormones, and a scan of the pituitary area, as well as seeking the advice of a hormone

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Complications of acromegaly itself & Hypertension \\ Impaired glucose tolerance and diabetes \\ \ specialist/endocrinologist. We need to understand a little more about what is happening first of all but if this does turn out to be acromegaly then various treatments are possible, from tablets and radioactive therapy to, in occasional circumstances, surgery. I should mention that diabetes is often caused by this condition and I would hope that in treating the underlying condition we might be able to avoid the diabetes becoming a problem. I'd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has noticed increased sweating, increased size of dentures and fatigue. He has prominent supraorbital ridges, prognathism, soft-tissue enlargement of the nose, tongue and ears, and general coarsening of features. His hands are squared and large.

Figure 5.88: (A–D) Acomegaly. 

### 5.36 Cushing's syndrome

#### 5.36 Cushing's syndrome

#### 5.36 Cushing's syndrome

##### 5.36 Cushing's syndrome 

### 5.56 Initial clues to Cushing's syndrome (in order of frequency)

* Obesity/weight gain (centripetal distribution)
* Facial plethora
* Round face
* Decreased litho
* Thin skin
* Menstrual disturbance (amenorrhoea and infertility)
* Hypertension
* Hirsutism
* Depression/emotional change
* Easy brusing
* Glucose intolerance
* Weakness (proximal myopathy)
* Osteopenia on X-ray
* Nephrolithiasis

Most discriminating are thin skin, easy brusing and proximal muscle atrophy.

### 5.57 Initial examination

* As for acromegaly, there are lengthy lists of signs to be found in textbooks, some insulting ('buffalo hump' and 'moon face'), which may best be summarised as signs of corticosteroid excess. Recognising these is often not hard (Fig. 5.89).

### 5.58 Further assessment

* Consider iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome. Most patients with Cushing's syndrome are taking long-term corticosteroids for a chronic inflammatory disease such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, asthma or inflammatory bowel disease, or a renal transplant. Cushing's syndrome results from prolonged exposure to excessive circulating glucocorticoid, and, except where the diagnosis is iatrogenic, should be confirmed biochemically.
* The three non-iatrogenic or endogenous causes of Cushing's syndrome to consider are shown in Table 5.51. Endogenous Cushing's syndrome is more common in women. Rarely, pseudo-Cushing's syndrome may result from obesity, depression or sometimes alcohol excess.
* Explore concerns.

## 5.6 Feedback to patient

Your symptoms would suggest the possibility of a condition called Cushing's syndrome. This can cause all of the symptoms you have been experiencing and lead to high blood pressure and diabetes, and also ultimately osteoporosis. It is due to excess secretion of a hormone called cortisol. There are numerous causes for this phenomenon. The pituitary gland at the base of the brain produces numerous hormones including a hormone that stimulates excess cortisol secretion. We should arrange blood tests to check the levels of cortisol and related hormones in order to confirm that this is Cushing's syndrome, and then consider a scan of the pituitary area, and potentially other tests, to try to identify the specific cause, as well as seeking the advice of a hormone specialist/endocrinologist. We need to understand a little more about what is happening first of all but if this does turn out to be Cushing's syndrome then various treatments are possible, from tablets and radioactive therapy to surgery. Ifd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

## 5.7 Feedback to examiners

### 5.7 Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has been gaining weight, becoming hypertensive with impaired glucose tolerance, and

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c c c}  & Cause & Features \\ Addenocorticotrophic hormone (ACTH) dependent (80% of endogenous Cushing’s syndrome) & Cushing’s disease (80% of  has thin skin, easy bruising and proximal muscle atrophy.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She does not take steroids and almost certainly has non-iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome - most likely Cushing's disease, although ectopic adrenocorticotrophic hormone and adrenal adenomas also need to be considered.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

There are two steps to investigating clinically suspected non-iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome [Table 5.52]:

1. Diagnosing Cushing's syndrome (low-dose dexamethasone suppression test or assessment of circadian rhythmicity or 24-hour urinary free cortisol)
2. Establishing the cause (adrenoocorticotrophic hormone detectable - Cushing's disease if partly suppresible so pituitary imaging needed, ectopic adrenocorticotrophic hormone if not suppresible; adrenocorticotrophic hormone not easily detectable - adrenal tumour so adrenal imaging needed)

For Cushing's disease trans-sphenoidal hypophysectomy by an experienced surgeon is the treatment of choice, but potential complications are cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhea, diabetes insipidus, hypopitiutarism, visual field disturbance, persistent or recurrent disease and death. Laparoscopic bilateral adrenalectomy is

Figure 5.89: (A–E) Cushing’s syndrome. * [19] A. increasingly used in refractory cases. Lasting remission without other pituitary hormone deficiency is achievable in over 50% of patients. Pittsulatory radiotherapy is the next best choice, and used if there is persistent hypercortisolism after surgery. The main complication is progressive anterior pituitary failure, which may be subtle and protracted, sometimes not detectable for as much as 10-20 years. Cortisol levels have usually normalised within a few years, and later growth hormone deficiency, gonadotrophin deficiency and hypoadrenalism are not uncommon. Drugs are sometimes used before surgery or after failed surgery, and include metyrapone and ketaconazole, which inhibit cortisol synthesis. Metyrapone causes an increase in androgen precursors and hirsutism. Mittane is sometimes used in adrenocortical carcinoma. There is interest in agents that might inhibit adrenocorticotrophic hormone release. Unilateral adrenalectomy is the treatment of choice for an isolated adrenal adenoma and bilateral adrenalectomy is reserved for hypercortisolism in Cushing's disease where primary evacuation or treatment is not possible; it may be complicated by Nelson syndrome, in which adrenocorticotrophic hormone levels rise causing melanin-induced hyperpigmentation.

### Case 5.37 Hypoadrenalism and Addison's Disease

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

* Re: Mr Michael Fragmore, aged 39 years This man has weight loss and weakness. He has noticed increased skin pigmentation.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 39-year-old man who has been losing weight over recent months and feeling nauseated and very tired. You have also noticed an increase in skin pigmentation on your palms and around your mouth and face. You do not feel dizzy when you stand up. You do not have any past medical history or take any medications. You wonder what is wrong with you, as you are not normally someone who feels under the weather.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms of hypoadrenalism (Box 5.57), which can be insidious. Hyperpigmentation is the result of elevated melanocyte-stimulating hormone and adrenocorticotrophic hormone and suggests primary adrenal failure, usually Addison's disease.

#### Initial examination

* Look for _hyperpigmentation,_ especially of _skin creases_ (_palms, elbows_), the _lips_ and _mouth,_ and _surgical scars_ (Fig 5.90). There may be _vitiligo,_ and sparse _axillary_ (and public) _hair. Postural hypotension_ is possible.

#### Further assessment

* Consider other causes of hypoadrenalism/adrenal insufficiency (Table 5.53).
* Adrenal crises are associated with haemodynamic instability and life-threatening metabolic disturbances. State that you would measure blood pressure. Hyponatraemia and hyperkalaemia with type IV renal tubular acidosis are common, owing to mineralocorticoid deficiency. Hypoglycaemia is less common but possible. Addison's disease carries a two-fold increased risk of mortality.
* Explore concerns.

#### Feedback to patient

Your symptoms would suggest the possibility of a condition called Addison's disease. This can cause all of the symptoms you have been experiencing and lead to low blood pressure and alterations in blood chemistry. It is due to deficiency of hormones produced by the adrenal gland, a gland above the kidneys that produces hormones called cortisol and fluorocrtisone, which are lacking in Addison's disease. We should arrange blood tests to confirm that this is Addison's disease, as well as tests on blood chemistry. This is a very treatable condition, and we would expect you to feel well again with treatment, but we need to establish the diagnosis first of all. I'd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has symptoms and signs suggesting Addison's disease, notably the skin pigmentation associated with tiredness, nausea and weight loss.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

Other causes of hypoadrenalism should always be considered, although the pigmentation suggests Addison's disease. Hypoptititarism sometimes seems clinically similar. Addison's disease tends to appear over months or up to 2-3 years with hyperpigmentation, whereas hypoptititarism can take a more protracted course over many more years especially if radiotherapy is a cause, and pallor predominates. An adrenocorticotrophic hormone level usually resolves the issue, although levels of other pituitary hormones might be indicated.

#### Outline an investigation and management plan

A serum cortisol level <= 100 nmol/l at 09.00 h is diagnostic of hypoadrenalism, and elevated adrenocorticotrophic hormone is suggestive. The teratocarcinoma or short Synatechen test is used to confirm a suboptimal response to synthetic adrenocorticotrophic hormone: 250 mg is administered intramuscularly and serum cortisol is measured at 0, 30 and 60 minutes. A normal response is a peak of more than 550 nmol/l, although definitions vary. The result may be less easy to interpret, with a suboptimal response, for people who have just stopped or are on low-dose, long-term prednisolone. Switching to dexamethasone is a potential way of assessing in these circumstances.

Adrenal antibodies to adrenal enzymes are present in over 90% of patients with adrenal autoimmunity, although most people with adrenal antibodies without symptoms do not develop adrenal

Figure 5.90: Addison’s disease: hypepignmentation of (A) palms, (B) mouth, (C) elbows and (D) knees. insufficiency. Elevated recumbent renin with low or normal aldosterone may be a sign of impending hypoadrenalism where a cause is not confirmed. Other tests include chest and abdominal imaging.

Adrenal "incidentalomas" are asymptomatic findings on 2-8% of scans. Most are adenomas, and these may be non-functioning or lead to subclinical autonomous glucocorticoid hypresecretion, Conn's syndrome or phaeochromocytoma. Other incidentalomas are metatases, carcinoma, myelolipoma and many less common types.

Biochemical tests to aid diagnosis include the low-dose dexamethasone suppression test for hypercortisolism, renin and aldosterone levels for aldosteroneism and plasma or urinary catecholamines or metanephrines for phaeochromocytoma.

Computed tomography scan can distinguish between adenomas and non-adenomas based on lipid content. The adrenal cortex secretes the glucocorticoid cortisol, the mineralocorticoid aldosterone, and undergens.

Aldosterone secretion is predominantly under the control of renin-angiotensin, but around 10% is controlled by adrenocorticotrophic hormone.

Glucocorticoid, mineralocorticoid and undergens are reduced in adrenal failure, albeit not until around 90% of functioning tissue is lost, whereas only glucocorticoid activity is affected by adrenocorticotrophic hormone deficiency.

Treatment of hypoadrenalism involves identification and management of any underlying remediable cause and adrenal replacement therapy with both glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid. Hydrocortisone is administered at a starting dose of 10 mg on waking (to mimic the physiological peak), 5 mg at lunchtime and 5 mg at 18.0. In an increased dose may be needed in pregnancy. Fluorocorticosine is used to achieve normal sodium homeostasis, as evidenced by normal plasma renin levels. Androgen replacement remains contentious. Increased doses of hydrocortisone are needed in intercurrent illness, generally doubled orally but with high doses intravenously in unstable patients. An adrenal crisis requires immediate intravenous physiological saline, and 100 mg hydrocortisone iv followed by 50 mg ads.

Corticosteroid withdrawal can precipitate iatrogenic hypoadrenalism. If prednisolone has been used for under 3 weeks at doses of < 40 mg, it can usually be withdrawn abruptly. If this is not the case, gradual withdrawal is needed. After a few weeks of treatment it could be decreased in 2.5 mg decrements for a few days to 7.5 mg and then more slowly, e.g. 2.5 mg every week or every few weeks. After an unknown period it could be decreased in 2.5 mg decrements once or twice monthly to 7.5 mg and then more slowly, e.g. 1-2.5 mg monthly. If disease is likely to recur then reduction is often by 1 mg monthly.

## Case 5.38 Hirsutism and Polycystic Ovarian Syndrome

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Michelle Short, aged 34 years

This overweight woman is being treated in hospital for pneumonia, and getting better. She mentions to you that she has noticed weight gain and hirsutism over recent months.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient. You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 34-year-old overweight woman recovering in hospital from pneumonia. You mention to the doctor that you have noticed weight gain and hirsutism and male pattern baldness over recent months. You have recently been diagnosed with hypertension and impaired glucose tolerance. You are particularly concerned about the abnormal hair distribution, and about problems with fertility.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms and duration.
* Ask about past medical history and drugs.

#### Initial examination

* There is _hirsutism_, or excessive hair growth, particularly over the face and limbs (Fig. 5.91). There may be _acne_. There may be other _signs of hyperandrogenism_.

Hypertrichosis refers to excess body hair. Hirsutism refers to excess hair in androgen-dependent areas (male pattern). Hyperandrogenism or 'virilisation' refers to male pattern hair loss and other physical changes including voice change, breast atrophy and clitoromegaly. Hirsutism may be constitutional, or it may be the result of adrenal or ovarian disorders, such as androgen-secreting tumours in either of these organs. It may be caused by congenital adrenal hyperplasia - a group of autosomal recessive disorders, the commonest defect being 21-hydroxylase deficiency, this enzyme defect resulting in a deficiency of glucocorticoids or mineralocorticoids leading to hyperstimulation of the adrenal gland, which can only respond by producing excess hormones from unaffected pathways - testosterone precursors and testosterone. There can be a spectrum of clinical manifestations from severe ambiguous sexual differentiation at birth and precocious puberty in males, to mild, late-onset signs resembling polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) with virilisation in females. One of the most common causes of hirsutism is PCOS.

#### Further assessment

* Ask about fertility.
* Ask about hypertension and diabetes.
* Explore concerns.

### Feedback to patient

The hair growth, weight gain, problems with blood pressure and blood sugars, and problems with fertility may all be part of a wider condition called polycystic ovarian syndrome in which insulin, the hormone controlling blood sugar and growth, does not work effectively. Sometimes there are cysts on the ovaries. There are treatments for the various aspects of the condition but it is a highly specialised area with a number of alternative diagnostic possibilities. I would prefer not to jump to conclusions at this stage and for us to consider running some blood tests and possibly an ovarian scan and seeking a more senior and specialist opinion. I can see that there is a range of very important concerns that you have and it is essential that we take things forward and help you. I'd be happy to answer any further questions that I can.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This woman has hirsutism, some signs of hyperandrogenism and features of the metabolic syndrome. She is particularly concerned about fertility.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She probably has polycystic ovarian syndrome. The possibility of a virilising adrenal tumour causing Cushing's syndrome should always be considered in the differential diagnosis. 

### Box 5.58 Polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS)

PCOS is the commonest endocrine disorder in women. The aetiology and pathophysiology remain unclear, but both genetic and environmental factors are involved.

PCOS typically presents with symptoms of anovulation due to hyperplasia of ovarian thecal cells and multiple cysts, associated with clinical and/or biochemical evidence of androgen excess. The spectrum of presentation, however, includes hyperhaemoglobin with amenorrhoea, oligonomethrose or regular periods. As well as being the most common cause of anovulatory infertility or subletrility, PCOS is a major risk factor for development of type 2 diabetes.

Diagnosis is made primarily clinically, but typical biochemical features are raised serum concentrations of testosterone (and ardrosethene) and elevation of serum luteinising hormone, particularly in anovulatory women, with a raised luteinising hormone to follicle-stimulating hormone ratio and abnormalities in the usual pulsatile secretion of gonadotropin-releasing hormones. Gestradiol levels are usually normal or elevated, and mild hyperolotaneemia may occur. In overweight and obese women an oral glucose tolerance test should be undertaken to detect impaired glucose tolerance reflecting insulin resistance with hyperinsulinaemia, and high-density lipoprotein cholesterol levels are low.

Management of PCOS includes treatment of infertility, menstrual regulation in those who do not desire pregnancy and treatment of associated symptoms of hyperadenogramism. Diet and lifestyle change in overweight and obese women with PCOS is important to improve fertility and prevent or reduce possible long-term consequences of the metabolic disturbances characteristic of anovulatory women with PCOS.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Polycystic ovarian syndrome is characterised by a number of clinical and biochemical abnormalities and there is a range of treatment considerations [Box 5.58].

### Case 5.39 Gynaecomastia and Hypogonadism

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr William Gale, aged 70 years

This man is being treated in hospital for pneumonia, and getting better. He mentions to you that he has noticed breast enlargement recently.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 70-year-old man recovering in hospital from pneumonia. You mention to the doctor that you have noticed bilateral breast enlargement over the past few months. A year ago you were in hospital with heart failure and since then have been on treatment with ramipril and spironolactone. You are more curious than alarmed, as having excessive breast tissue does not worry you too much at your age.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms and duration.
* Ask about past medical history and drugs.

#### Initial examination

* There is _gynaecomastia_ (Fig. 5.92).

Gynaecomastia may be difficult to distinguish from normal breast tissue but the patient's account of a change should be given credence. Gynaecomastia is caused instead by a decreased androgen-oestrogen ratio and so galactorrhoea (usually due to hyperprolactinaemia) is not expected.

### Further assessment

Gynaecomastia has many causes (Box 5.59).

A full hormone, including gonadotrophin, profile would be important, together with assessing for features of testosterone deficiency, in instances where a clear cause is not evident. Testosterone-deficiency symptoms should be assessed where a clear cause is not evident.

Testosterone deficiency is increasingly recognised, and not uncommon. It may result from a specific hypogonadal

Figure 5.92: Gynaecomastia. disorder or age-related decline. It may be hypergonadotrophic or hypogonadotrophic. Hypergonadotrophic hypogonadotropin refers to primary testicular failure, often with elevated follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinising hormone (LH) levels. Acquired causes include mumps, trauma, torsion, surgery, radiotherapy, drugs (spironolactone, chemotherapy, marijuana) and myotonic dystrophy. Congenital disorders include Klinefelter's syndrome and 5a-reductase deficiency (androgen resistance). Hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism refers to gonadal failure secondary to hypothalamic/pituitary disease, usually with low or normal FSH and LH levels. Systemic disorders including chronic kidney or liver disease, and sickle cell disease can also cause testosterone deficiency. Chronic alcohol excess is a common cause. Testerostence deficiency can present in many ways, most commonly as reduced libido, reduced muscle mass, reduced bone mass with an increased risk of osteoporosis, reduced facial, body or public hair, small testes or non-specific cognitive changes such as poor concentration or tiredness.
* Explore concerns.

## Feedback to patient

This looks like a condition called gynaecomastia, which refers to benign enlargement of breast tissue. It can sometimes be produced by drugs like spironolactone and depending upon whether or not you would wish us to try to tackle the problem we could alter your medication. However, it would be sensible to review your cardiac assessment from a year ago before considering any switch in treatment. I'd be happy to answer any further questions or concerns that you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This man has gynaecomastia. He is unconcerned.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The cause is likely to be spironolactone, and there are no symptoms of a wider problem.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Further investigation should be necessary only if the gynaecomastia progresses or if new features develop.

## CASE 5.40

### Candidate information

### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

### Scenario

Re: Mr Ralph Lloyd, aged 27 years

This young man is recovering from an exacerbation of asthma in hospital and has a lifelong abnormality of his hands.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 27-year-old man with pseudohypoparathyroidism and shortened fourth and fifth fingers of both hands. You have lived with this problem without difficulty and your general practitioner monitors your blood tests. You are currently in hospital with asthma and take the opportunity to ask the doctor about whether or not this condition affects calcium levels, as you have heard there is an association.

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

* Ask about the condition and the details known to him.

### Initial examination

* There are _short fourth and fifth metacarpals_ on both hands. There is short stature, obesity, a round face with frontal bossing of the skull and a short neck (Fig. 5.93). 

### Further assessment

Calcification can be a complication of pseudohypopapathyroidism, as might be learning difficulties, hypothyroidism and hypogonadism. Short stature has many causes (Box 5.60).

* Explore concerns.

### Feedback to patient

Pseudohypoparathyroidism is a condition in which a hormone in the body, parathyroid hormone, cannot work effectively because of a block to its action. This potentially causes disturbances in the body's calcium levels, which may be low, which is why your general practitioner monitors your blood tests. It is also associated, in some forms, with shortened metacarpal bones on the fourth and fifth fingers.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has short fourth and fifth metacarpals on both hands and short stature.

#### Create

##### Case 5.41 CHEST PAIN

POST-MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION

##### Candidate information

##### Role

You are a doctor on the cardiac ward.

##### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The syndrome with short fourth and fifth metacarpals is likely to be the result of type 1a pseudohypoparathyroidism or pseudopsyendothypoparathyroidism. Hypoparathyroidism is a biochemical condition comprising low levels of parathyroid hormone and calcium and raised phosphate. Pseudohypoparathyroidism is the result of target organ resistance to parathyroid hormone, with high levels of parathyroid hormone, low calcium and raised phosphate (type 1a or Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy produces this phenotype although there is also a morphologically normal phenotype). Pseudopsyendothypoparathyroidism has the morphological appearance of type 1a pseudohypoparathyroidism with normal biochemistry.

##### Outline an investigation and management plan

This is a specialist area but after diagnosis is made simple surveillance may be all that is required.

##### Case 5.41 CHEST PAIN

POST-MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION

##### Scenario

##### Re: Mr Thomas Carter, aged 84 years

This man has chest pain after a recent heart attack.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the

Figure 5.93: Pseudohypoparathyroidism. diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are an 84-year-old man admitted to hospital 3 days ago with a small heart attack. You have a long history of ischaemic heart disease and heart attacks and your cardiologist has performed angiograms in the past that have shown considerable disease and you and he have jointly agreed to manage things with tablets and not to consider coronary artery bypass grafting, and coronary stenting is not feasible. You are quite pragmatic, and have pain not infrequently, and prefer to continue with the 'conservative' treatment strategy. Indeed your main concern is that the doctors will want to do more. You currently take aspirin 75 mg, clothological 75 mg, optimum statin therapy, optimum beta blockade therapy and optimum angiotensin blockade therapy. Your subcutaneous heparin was stopped yesterday. Your pain is not as severe as that which brought you into hospital but of the same character and came on at rest.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Establish that the pain is likely to be cardiac.
* Get a sense of how things have changed symptomatically.

#### Initial examination

* Examine for signs of heart failure.
* Xanthelasmata (Fig. 5.94) are more common in people with coronary disease, reflecting dyslipidaemia, but are not present in most people with coronary disease.

#### Further assessment

* Consideration of other causes of chest pain is always wise (pulmonary embolus if pleuritie, pericarditis if positional) but coronary disease is the likely cause.
* Explore concerns.
* Respect his thoughts, but make sure that his decision for symptomatic treatment only is based on informed knowledge. Chest pain after recent myocardial infarction suggests ongoing coronary instability (chest pain after recent coronary intervention suggests possible restenosis, warranting urgent re-evaluation of coronary anatomy).

### Feedback to patient

I suspect you are having further angina or just possibly another heart attack. We should repeat your ECG. We'll give you some oxygen and a nitrate spray just now, and restart the heparin injections. Some morphine might help, too, if the pain isn't settling quickly. Then I'd hope to keep things settled with some additional treatment (e.g. nitrate orally, nicorandil). I'll also arrange for you to have a blood test in a few hours to see if a further small heart attack has occurred. We can also let your cardiologist know that you have had further pain. I'd be happy to try to answer any questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has typical cardiac ischaemic pain at rest post-myocardial infarction.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The diagnosis on history alone is probably post-coronary syndrome unstable angina or a further myocardial infarction.

#### Outline an investigation and management plan

His decision for non-escalation of investigation, particularly invasive investigation, is probably based on previous information and discussion with his cardiologist. This should be respected, although it is always important to ensure that decisions are made with appropriate information. 

## Case 5.42 Sudden Breathlessness

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs June Havelock, aged 70 years

This woman is recovering from pneumonia in hospital and has become more breathless.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 70-year-old woman in hospital with pneumonia. You have been recovering well over the past few days, but have been kept in hospital because of your unstable diabetes and because you developed a problem with your platelet count when being given heparin injections for prevention of blood clots. Today you have suddenly started to feel more breathless. You are usually well apart from the diabetes and only take medication for this. You wonder what might be wrong, are a little worried about worsening pneumonia and wonder what should be done as you have had antibiotics already.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about the onset (abrupt or gradual).
* Ask about other symptoms (e.g. cough, sputum).
* Ask about pleuritic pain.
* Ask about past medical history and drug history.
* Ask about a personal or family history of blood clots.

#### Initial examination

* Note whether she is tachyonoeic. Examine her chest, notably through auscultation.
* You would wish to know her observation recordings including saturations.

### Further assessment

Explore concerns.

#### Feedback to patient

We need to find out why you are feeling more breathless. My chief concern is that you might have developed a blood clot on the lung. I think we should arrange a chest X-ray and then a scan of the lungs to see if there is evidence of further infection or if a blood clot on the lung might be a possibility. It is particularly important for us to answer this question without delay because of the problems you have had with the heparin injections. On that basis I will talk with the radiologists now about the X-ray and scan, and also speak with our haematology expert about a safe alternative to heparin. I'd be happy to try to answer any other questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman is suddenly more breathless after a period of medical illness and could not take heparin.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

Pulmonary embolus must be considered more likely than recurrence or exacerbation of infection. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is much more commonly complicated by thromboembolic complications than bleeding.

#### Outline an investigation and management plan

A computed tomography pulmonary angiogram is necessary, and immediate discussion with a haematologist about an alternative to heparin.

#### Case 5.43 Upper Gastrointestinal Bleeding

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Mr Malcolm Freeman, aged 35 years

This man has had an episode of haematemesis and melanoma.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 35-year-old solicitor. Over the past few days you have been vomiting. Initially you thought it had been provoked by a meal, but the vomiting persisted and in the past 24 hours you have been vomiting with a mixture of blood. You have no other symptoms but you felt lightheaded and your general practitioner noted your blood pressure to be low and arranged urgent admission to hospital, where you have just arrived. You have recently taken over-the-counter non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for stomach pains. You cannot accept a blood transfusion on religious grounds.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about dyspepsia, haematemesis and melaena.
* Ask about symptoms of postural hypotension.
* Recognise that gastrointestinal bleeding and presyncopal symptoms warrant urgent assessment.
* After initial haemostasis, repeat endoscopies with further band ligation are indicated, with a non-selective beta blocker until all varices are obliterated.
* Balloon tamponade is potentially dangerous (significant risks of aspiration pneumonia and oesophageal rupture). It should be used as rescue therapy only in actively bleeding patients when haemorrhage continues despite endoscopic therapy; it may stop bleeding temporarily until definitive endoscopic, radiological, or surgical treatments are applied.
* Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic stent shunting (IP5S) should be considered for uncontrolled varical bleeding.
* TIPSS should be considered to prevent oesophageal relbeding in patients with contraindications to endoscopic or pharmacological treatment, and in patients who bleed despite endoscopic and drug treatments.

#### Lower gastrointestinal bleeding

* After resuscitation, the site and cause may be determined by endoscopy or computed tomography (CT). CT angiography or digital subtraction angiography are preferred in massive active bleeding.
* Colonoscopic haemostasis is an effective means of controlling haemorrhage from active diverticular or post-polypectomy bleeding.
* For bleeding uncontrolled by colonoscopy, arterial embolisation may be indicated.
* When bleeding fails to respond to endoscopy or interventional radiological procedures, localised segmental intestinal resection or subtotal osteotomy may be indicated.

#### Initial examination

* Look at the observations, especially heart rate, lying and standing blood pressure and respiratory rate.

A normal or low heart rate and hypotension may occur in acute haemorrhage, often after gastrointestinal surgery - the first response to maintain arterial pressure despite a falling cardiac output involves tachycardia and baroreceptor reflex vasoconstriction; a second phase, however, occurs when about a third of circulating volume has been lost, in which sympathetically mediated vasoconstriction and cardiac drive fall abruptly and cardiac vagal drive increases.

#### Further assessment

* Consider the causes of upper gastrointestinal bleeding (Case 2.3).
* Haematemesis (bright red, dark clots or coffee-ground) or melaena indicate an upper gastrointestinal bleed. Haematemesis suggests a source of bleeding proximal to the ligament of Trietze (jejunum). Small bowel (e.g. angiodysplasia, investigated by wireless capsule endoscopy or computed tomography angiography) and right-sided colonic disease may present with melaena. The Rockall score (see Case 2.3) is a clinical and endoscopic predictor of risk of re-bleeding and death. The Blatchford score (Case 2.3) is a risk score that does not rely on endoscopy; its place is more to predict the need for intervention than to predict mortality, and so it has greater emphasis in less severe bleeding.
* Explore concerns.

### Feedback to patient

You are almost certainly bleeding internally, probably from the stomach, possibly from an ulcer. This could be due to one of a number of things, or a combination, including the anti-inflammatory. In the first instance we need to put a 'drug' into you, take a blood sample and give you some fluid intravenously and some medication to help settle things. There is a small possibility, if your blood count is very low, that you would need a blood transfusion to stabilise things. I appreciate that you would not wish to accept this on religious grounds and we would respect this (assuming capacity). We should, when things are more stable, with or without a blood transfusion, arrange an endoscopy test - a camera test looking into the stomach. You should be aware that if there is bleeding that cannot be controlled or if the blood count is unsafely low, then not having a transfusion could be a decision that would put you at serious risk. I'd be happy to try to answer any further questions.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summaries the key history and examination findings

This man has had an upper gastrointestinal bleed.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

Possible causes are peptic ulceration, oesophagitis, erosive disease, varices or a Mallory-Weiss tear.

#### Outline an investigation and management plan

He needs stabilisation and then oesophagoastroudonedoscopy. [Management strategies for upper and lower gastrointestinal bleeding are described in Box 5.61 (and further discussed in Cases 2.3 and 2.6, respectively).]

#### Case 5.44 NON-BLANCHING RASH

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Amanda Baker, aged 34 years

This woman is in hospital recovering from pyelonephritis. She has developed bruising on her legs.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

#### Patient information

You are a 34-year-old woman, usually well, recovering from a kidney infection. You have been on antibiotics. You have developed a bruising type of rash on your legs. It is not painful and you feel well. It has progressed over the past 2 days. You have finished your course of antibiotics. You wonder what might be wrong with the legs, and are worried about leukaemia as a friend died of this last year presenting with bruising initially.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about symptoms such as pain.
* Ask about the onset and duration of the rash, and if she has ever had a similar rash before.
* Ask whether she feels generally well despite it.
* Ask about past medical history and drug history. Ask particularly about any known bleeding tendency.

#### Initial examination

* Ask whether it is painful or tender (erythema nodosum).
* Note a _non-blanching, purpuric rash_. This is skin vasculitis, and the purpura _may be palpable_ (Fig. 5.95).

#### Further assessment

The clinical appearance of vasculitis is usually obvious, but the type may need to be confirmed by biopsy.

* Consider causes of skin vasculitis (Box 5.62).
* Explore concerns.

#### Feedback to patient

I think this may be something called a leucocytoclastic vasculitis, a condition well recognised after infections and antibiotics, and one which invariably settles down. However, we should check your temperature, arrange some blood tests and obtain a chest X-ray, and I'd also like to ask a dermatologist - a skin specialist - to have a look at the rash. If it is not settling it may be that the or she would wish to take to a biopsy (a small piece of tissue) from the rash and consider some treatment to settle it down more quickly. I'd be happy to try to answer any questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has a non-blanching rash of her legs and feet that has progressed over days rather than hours and she feels well without other symptoms.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She probably has a localised skin vasculitis secondary to the infection or the antibiotics. Less likely possibilities include erythema nodosum from the same triggers (but she does not have the typical tender bruising pattern) or a clotting disorder. The skin vasculitis is probably a leukoevodastic vasculitis but other possibilities include immunoglobulin A vasculitis and urticarial vasculitis. Immunoglobulin A vasculitis is an immunoglobulin A-mediated vasculitis causing a linear immunofluorescence pattern histologically. It is seen in immunoglobulin A disease such as Henoch-Schonlein purpura. Urticarial vasculitis looks like urticaria but lasts for over 24 hours, turns to bruises, and is painful rather than itchy. It is seen in autoimmune disease such as lupus.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

I would check urinalysis, renal function and obtain a chest X-ray. Autoimmune testing for systemic vasculitis is usually negative in patients with isolated skin vasculitis who feel well. Leukoevodastic vasculitis is a cutaneous vasculitis that may occur in isolation or in association with systemic disease including anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody-associated vasculitis, connective tissue disease, infection (e.g. infective endocarditis, hepatitis B virus, hepatitis C virus), inflammatory bowel disease or paraproteinaemia. Consideration of causes is important but the rash usually settles.

### Case 5.45 Skin and Software

#### Tissue Infection and Acute Dermatology

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

Figure 5.95: Vasculitis: (A) hand, (B) dorsum, (C) soles of the feet. 

#### Scenario

* Re: Ms Julia Summers, aged 39 years This woman has a painful red leg. Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 39-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes. You have developed a spreading, red, tender calf and thigh over the past few days. You have developed a small area of skin breakdown that is very uncomfortable, but the skin is now very tender even where intact. Ten days ago you were bitten by a 'mozzie'. You wonder what might be wrong with the leg, are worried about infection and wonder what should be done. You are a nurse.

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

* Ask about the leg and how painful it is.
* Establish whether there are any systemic or febrile symptoms.
* Ask about the stability of the diabetes.

### Initial examination

* There is _erythema_ over the anterior aspect of the lower leg, which is _warm,_ and slightly swollen (Figs 5.96 and 5.97).

The main differential diagnosis of unilateral leg swelling is deep venous thrombosis (DVT), which causes circumferential swelling of the leg, often with erythema or dislocation with calf predominance and calf tenderness; Wells' criteria for diagnosing DVT are discussed in Case 2.36. Bilateral erythema in the setting of lower limb oedema, although commonly treated as soft-tissue infection, is more often a problem with venous stasis, as bilateral swollen legs seldom imply infection.

### Further assessment

* Cellulitis is usually caused by staphylococcal or streptococcal infection. The term espsielas is often used to describe deeper, often streptococcal, infection with greater demarcation of erythema.
