In practice it may be hard to distinguish the type of soft-tissue infection. However you should always also consider necrotising fascitiis (Fig. 5.98). Pain and tenderness are disproportionately predominant and visible signs may be minimal, especially early on. Necrotising fascitiis refers to infection that spreads rapidly through fascial layers, often extending through a limb within hours. Erythema is minimal and not always present. Buliae or vesicles are common, but late, signs. Black, necrotic skin is also a later sign, and often more obvious than erythema. Although traditionally streptococcal organisms are responsible, many cases of necrotising fascitiis are of mixed growth, and some cases may be caused by less rapidly progressive organisms (relatively) such as streptococcal group G organisms. Both creatine kinase and C-reactive protein serum levels may be raised, but are unreliable in excluding necrotising fascitis if normal.
* Explore concerns.

## Feedback to patient

* think this is cellulitis. It is certainly a very nasty case. We should take a series of blood tests and start you on intravenous antibiotics. If things are not settling soon we may also need to consider a scan of the leg, and we should certainly have you on a prophylactic dose of subcutaneous heparin. Given the degree of tenderness and the ulcer, I'd also like to just play on the safe side and ask our microbiology team and our surgical team to have a

Figure 5.96: Cellulitis. look at the leg. Occasionally infection can run through the deeper layers of tissue leading to more serious problems if not tackled early. I don't think this is the case but I would prefer to treat this situation aggressively than watch and wait. I'd be happy to try to answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has diabetes with spreading erythema on her leg following an inset bite 10 days ago. There is a small area of ulceration [Box 5.63] that looks infective.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She probably has cellulitis, with circumferential redness. A deep vein thrombosis is possible but this looks much more like cellulitis, although if there is any doubt about symptoms and signs setting quickly with antibiotics an ultrasound scan of the leg will be indicated. However the degree of tenderness makes me just a little edgy about the possibility of necrotising fascitis - an emergency [Table 5.54].

Figure 5.97: (A–D) Erythema multiforme due to or with secondary soft-tissue infection.

Figure 5.98: (A, B) Necrotising fascitis. 

#### Types of ulcer

An ulcer is a breach in the skin and may be caused by trauma, infection or vasculitis, and perhaps most commonly in older people with pressure, venous insufficiency, arterial compromise, oedema or lymphoedema.

#### Pressure ulcer

This is a breach in the skin and underlying tissue secondary to unrelieved pressure, shear or friction. Pressure may be high for a short time or low for a longer time, as in sacral pressure ulcers caused by immobility in a chair or bed. Friction pressure ulcers commonly occur at the heels, elbows and knees, and may be associated with poor manual handling or positioning, and are common in agitated patients. Shearing, when gravity overcomes friction as when a patient slides down the bed, commonly causes pressure ulcers on the buttocks, sacrum or back. Other factors conspire to increase pressure ulcer risk or worsen pressure ulcers such as poor nutrition, body weight, incontinence and comorbidity such as cardiovascular disease. The European Pressure Ulcer Advisory Panel classification system describes four grades of pressure ulcer:

* _Grade I: Non-blanachable erythema of intact skin; discoloration, warmth, oedema, induction or hardness_
* _Grade 2: Partial-thickness skin loss involving epidermis, dermis or both; superficial, appearing as abrasion or blister_

Grade 3: Full-thickness skin loss involving damage or necrosis of subcutaneous tissue that may extend down to, but not through, fascia_

* _Grade 4: Extensive destruction, tissue necrosis, or damage to muscle, bone or supporting structures with or without full-thickness skin loss._

#### Treatments

Treatments for ulcers depend upon the cause, e.g.:

* _Pressure: Pressure-relieving devices, modern dressings, e.g. hydrocolloids, hydrogels, foams, films, aginates, silicones; antimicrobials_
* _Venous: Appropriate compression bandages, especially in prevention (light, moderate, high or extra-high compression) Arterial Surgery, vasodilators, e.g. pentoxyline, lipoprost Vasculartic Treatment of underlying cause, immune suppression_
* _Ulcer with lymphoedema: Compression bandages; intermittent pneumatic compression device_
* _Euding cavity: Vacuum-assisted closure_
* _Ulcer with slough and non-viable tissue: Debridement, e.g. sharp, mechanical, enzymatic, biosurgery (magogots)_
* _Nerobosis lipoidea:_ _P_oralen and ultraviolet light therapy.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} Infection & Cellulitis \\ Erysipelas \\ Impetigo (blistering superficial staphylococcal infection) \\ Infected blisters, e.g. insect bites \\ Necrotising fasciitis \\ Tinea \\ Scabies \\ Pyoriasis & Erythrodemic/generalised pustular \\ Guttate \\ Atopic dermatitis & Secondary infection \\ Eczema herpeticum \\ Autoimmune bullous diseases & Pemphigoid/pemphigus \\ Urlcaria & This refers to wheels (bedema) surrounded by flares of erythema. It may be caused by drugs, heat, cold, pressure, water, food substances, infections, insect bites or dematographism. More severe reactions may lead to angioedema. Urlcaria pigmentosa refers to litchy red-brown macules/papules, which are the dermal manifestation of systemic mastocytosis. \\ Angioedema & Food allergens, e.g. peanuts \\ Drug reactions, e.g. ACE inhibitors, mediated by bradykinin \\ C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency \\ Photosensitivity & Sunburn \\ Polymorphic light eruption – more utricarial than sunburn, common in young females, semi-confluent \\ Drug-induced phototoxicity \\ \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.4: Acute dermatology 

### Outline an investigation and management plan

The should receive intravenous antibiotics guided by the microbiology team. If necrotising fascitis is considered a possibility, urgent surgical review with a view to urgent fascotomy is needed to establish the diagnosis, and extensive surgery is the only potential cure, aided by appropriate antimicrobials.

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{c c} \hline Vasculitis & Cutaneous vasculitis and Henoch–Schönlein purpura \\  & Meinigococcal infection \\ \hline Tumours & Cutaneous metastases \\  & Paraneoplastic eruptions \\ \hline Drug eruptions & An inexhaustible list of drugs can be the cause of a skin eruption, e.g. ACE inhibitors, allopurinol, antibiotics, anteipileptic drugs, NSAIDs, thiazides. Morphologically, the eruption may be: \\  & Morbilliform (exanthematous, maculopapular) – extensive and variably itchy, onset a few days after drug, usually lasts under a week, responds to moderate potency topical steroid \\  & Urtcalial or angioelectomatous – treatment with antihistamines, epinephrine (adrenaline) and possibly systemic steroids \\  & Eczematous \\  & Psoriasisiform \\  & Lichen planus-like \\  & Bulleus \\  & Pustular \\  & Acne-like \\  & Vasculitric \\  & Pipriasis rose-like \\  & Photosensitive \\ \hline Life–threatening drug eruptions are: \\  & — TEN due to, for example, antibiotics, terbinafine, nevriapine, NSAIDs, antiepileptic drugs (lamotrigine, valproate low risk, allopurinol, gold or sulfasalazine. \\  & Stevens–Johnson syndrome, a cousin of TEN but also provoked by infections, e.g. herpes simplex virus \\  & Drug reaction with eosinophilsilia and systemic symptoms (DRESS), a rare, multisystem disease with rash, fever, lymphadenopathy, pneumonitis, hepatitis, eosinophilia and atypical lymphocytes \\ \hline Erythrodezine & \\ \hline Erythrodezine is a clinical syndrome with extensive red skin and systemic manifestations which may be life threatening. If 70–90\% of the skin is red it is termed ‘sub-erythrodezine’. There are many causes, e.g. eczema, psoriasis, drugs, cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, staphylococcal scaled-skin syndrome, phriasis rubra pliers. \\ \hline Saxena-skin shedding & \\ \hline Bacterial scaled skin syndrome is the most quoted cause, but TEN due to drugs (any) or autoimmunity is another important cause. Glucagonomas can cause a nerolytic migrating erythema. \\ \hline Hen & \\ \hline Primary skin diseases causing itch include all types of dermatitis, lichen planus, blistering disorders, especially dermatitis herpetiformis (pemphigoid does not itch), fungal infection or mite infestation, flea/ tick bites and ichthyroesis (dry skin with fish-like scales, with a genetic basis or associated with malignancy). Systemic causes include drugs, cholestasis, uaemia, diabetes, hyper/hypothyroidism, polycythaemia vera, lymphoma, parasitrophobia and certain drugs. Dermographism, in which itchy linear lines appear after scratching, is common, as are heat- and cold-induced urticaria, the former often after a hot shower or bath, or after exercise. Aquagenic itch caused by water of any temperature is recognised, as is neurogenic itch in which neuropathic pain is rather manifested as itch. \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.54: Acute dermatology—cont’d 

## Scenario

* Re: Mrs Abigail Booker, aged 38 years This woman complains of pain in her left calf and she feels it may be swollen.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 38-year-old woman who thought your left calf was a little swollen and tight feeling over the past 2 days and today it is quite painful when you walk. You went on holiday to Egypt 3 weeks ago and you returned home 1 week ago. Your left calf had been slightly uncomfortable the whole time you were away. You thought it was probably due to the heat although you were surprised that only one leg was affected. Now, since coming home, the problem has got worse. You tend to have mild discomfort in the left knee from a cartilage injury when you played tennis some years ago. You do not feel breathless nor do you have any chest pain. You have no other symptoms or problems. Your only treatment is the oral contraceptive pill. You are now on the acute medical unit and wonder what might be wrong with the leg, are a little worried about a clot and wonder what should be done.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about the leg symptoms.
* Ask about risk factors (flight, oral contraceptive pill, any family history).
* Ask about possible pulmonary embolus symptoms (chest pain, breathlessness).

#### Initial examination

* Examine the calves.

#### Further assessment

* If deep vein thrombosis seems likely, seek any possible contraindications to anticoagulation.

## Feedback to patient

I think there is a reasonable possibility that you do have a blood clot in the leg, known as a deep vein thrombosis. The flight may have been a risk factor for this. The oral contraceptive pill does modestly increase the risk although usually not enough to provoke a blood clot on its own. Some people do have an increased tendency to clot, which is why I asked about any family history. We need to arrange for you to have a scan of the leg to confirm our suspicions but in the mean time it is important to start some blood-thinning medication (an injection of heparin) pending the result. I'd be happy to try to answer any other questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has a swollen calf without convincing signs of cellulitis.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

She has risk factors for a deep vein thrombosis and this must be the first diagnosis to exclude.

#### Outline an investigation and management plan

A Doppler ultrasound scan of the leg veins is necessary with low-molecular-weight heparin at treatment dose administered pending the result.

### Case 5.47 Focal Neurological SYMPTOMS - AMAUROSIS FUGAX

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the medical ward.

#### Scenario

* Re: Mr Alexander Miller, aged 59 years This man, who is an ex-smoker and has stable type 2 diabetes and peripheral vascular disease, has had an episode of transient loss of vision whilst on your ward recovering from cellulitis.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are a 59-year-old man in hospital with a foot infection. You also have atrial fibrillation and diabetes. You have just had an episode of sudden painless loss of vision, like a curtain coming across your left eye. It lasted for around 15 minutes and slowly the vision came back to normal. You take aspirin, a statin and metformin. You are an ex-smoker. You are worried about the implications for driving as you live in an isolated area.

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

Pick out some key words or points from the information you have been told. The vision loss must be explored. You are told that the diabetes is stable and that he is recovering from cellullitis and a focused history would not tend to explore these areas although you would note the diabetes and smoking (a focused history would not be troubled with how many he smoked) as risk factors, along with the atrial fibrillation.

* Confirm sudden, painless, unilateral and transient visual loss.
* Ask if it is a first episode.
* Exclude any symptoms of retinal tearing or posterior vitreous detachment (flashes or floaters).
* Exclude any other focal neurological symptoms (limb weakness or sensory loss, speech disturbance).

### Initial examination

* Confirm that there are no other obvious focal neurological features, and auscultate the carotid arteries for bruits and possibly the heart briefly.
* Note blood pressure.
* Perform a quick bedside visual acuity and fundoscopy.

### Further assessment

* Explore his concerns about driving.

### Feedback to patient

This sounds very much like a transient ischaemic attack or TIA ('mini-stroke'). It is usually caused by a blood clot going into the retina of the eye. The clot then dissolves and the symptoms improve. Clots usually arise from the carotid artery in the neck or from the heart, and the irregular rhythm in the heart may be a source of blood clots, or the previous smoking, which can lead to furfing up of arteries in the neck. We should organise a scan of the neck (carotid duplex) and a brain scan, and I would be keen to discuss things further with the stroke team. If the neck scan showed a significant blockage in the relevant artery then a procedure to unblock that blockage may need to be considered by a specialist in vascular surgery. And it may be that following the tests we should consider a more powerful treatment to thin the blood such as warfarin; in the meantime we should increase the dose of aspirin that you take. If, as we suspect, this has been a TIA then you would be required to not drive for a month providing you remain well [it does not matter if you do not know the driving restrictions after a TIA - do not hazard a guess! - and simply tell him that there is likely to be a restriction for a limited time and that you will find out what that is]. I'd be happy to try to answer any questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has had transient spontaneously reversible monocular visual disturbance, and has a history of atrial fibrillation.

### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The diagnosis is almost certainly amaurosis fugax and is unlikely to be anything else with this constellation of symptoms. Transient visual loss for a few minutes is usually ischaemic. If very brief (seconds), or if bilateral, it is often a reflection of perfusion, common in presymcope. He is at risk of further transient ischaemic attacks or strokes, particularly in the next few weeks.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

He should have a carotid duplex scan (urgently) and possibly a brain and heart scan, and I would be keen to discuss things further with the stroke team. If the carotid scan showed significant stenosis in the relevant artery then he should be discussed with the vascular team for consideration of endartectomy. Following tests he should be considered for warfarin rather than aspirin and in the meantime his antiplatelet therapy should be enhanced (aspirin 300 mg plus dipyridamole, although there is increasing evidence for clopidogrel monotherapy), with consideration to intensifying his lipid-lowering therapy and adding antihypertensive therapy to reach target levels. He is advised not to drive for a month after a transient ischaemic attack.

### Case 5.48 Transient loss of consciousness

#### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor in the medical outpatient clinic.

#### Scenario

[1] You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan.

### Patient information

You are an 84-year-old man in the medical outpatient clinic. Last week you had an episode of loss of consciousness without warning. You have had numerous episodes of feeling light-headed. You take three antihypertensives for blood pressure. Your wife does not drive.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Ask about the circumstances of the loss of consciousness and any prodromal symptoms.
* Ask if it was witnessed.
* Ask about duration (brief with rapid recovery suggests syncope, prolonged suggests a seizure).
* Ask if any injury was sustained.
* Ask how he felt on recovery (back to normal quickly suggests syncope, post-ictal period suggests seizure).
* Review medications.

#### Initial examination

* Check blood pressure and auscultate the precodium.

#### Further assessment

Loss of consciousness is usually either syncope (Case 2.41) or a seizure (Case 2.42) or sometimes metabolic (usually hypoglycaemia). Most loss of consciousness is due to syncope, and both syncope and seizure become more common with age. Syncope is usually due to one of three main groups of causes: cardiac syncope, either electrical (e.g. sinus pauses) or structural (e.g. aortic stenosis); orthostatic hypotension, due to either drugs (e.g. antihypertensive) or disease (e.g. diabetes autonomic neuropathy); reflex syncope, either vasovogal syncope or carotid sinus syncope. Cardiac syncope is often without warning. Orthostatic hypotension is often suggested by the drug or disease history. Reflex vasovogal syncope has a typical prodrome and carotid sinus syncope is often suggested by rubbing or movement of the neck such as shaving or reaching up.

* Enquire into these areas.
* Explore concerns.

### Feedback to patient

It sounds as though you had a blackout. This could be due to one of a number of reasons - sometimes a problem with the heart's rhythm or one of its valves, sometimes a drop in blood pressure from medications, and sometimes a fainting attack for other reasons. I think that we should look into this in a bit more detail and in the first instance I would like to arrange for you to have an ECG heart tracing and some blood tests. We should check your blood pressure lying and standing up to see if there is a significant drop when you stand. I would then like to arrange for you to have a heart recording over 24 hours to monitor the heart's rhythm (you can wear a small device) and a heart scan. We can take things from there but I'm afraid that whilst we are waiting for answers the DVLA rules are that you should not drive. I'd be happy to discuss any of this in more detail and answer any questions you may have.

### Feedback to examiners

#### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This man has had an episode of syncope.

#### Provide a diagnosis or differential diagnosis with supporting evidence

The possible causes are cardiac syncope (electrical or structural), orthostatic hypotension (drugs or disease) or reflex (neurocardiogenic) syncope (vasovagal or carotid sinus syncope).

#### Outline an investigation and management plan

Unless the cause is clearly orthostatic hypotension, and certainly if his ECG is abnormal, he should proceed to ambulatory ECG monitoring and echocardiography. Further tests will depend upon results and ongoing symptoms but could include more prolonged ECG monitoring, tilt testing and carotid sinus message. He should not drive whilst the diagnosis is uncertain. Though should also be given to falls risk and bone protection.

## Case 5.49 VERITGO AND HEARING LOSS

### Candidate information

#### Role

You are a doctor on the acute medical unit.

#### Scenario

Re: Mrs Deborah Kent, aged 68 years

This woman has been admitted to hospital with disabling vertigo.

Your task is to assess the problem by means of a focused history and focused examination. You should then advise the patient of your probable diagnosis and plan, and address any questions or concerns raised by the patient.

You have 10 minutes. The examiners will alert you when 6 minutes have elapsed, stop you at 8 minutes, and in the remaining 2 minutes ask you to report your findings, the diagnosis or differential diagnosis, and a management plan. 

### Patient information

You are a 68-year-old woman who developed a sudden severe sense of the world moving whilst getting out of bed this morning. This has waxed and waned throughout the day but is very debilitating. The symptom is worsened by any movement. You have no other symptoms, and notably no headache, hearing loss or other neurological symptoms. You feel nauseated but have not vomited. You are usually well. You do not know what is wrong and are rather alarmed.

### Focused history and examination

#### Initial history

* Confirm that the symptom is vertigo (Box 5.64).
* Ask about acute hearing loss, other neurological features (e.g. diplopia, facial numbness or weakness, dysphagia, dysphagia, ataxia, limb weakness) and headache.

#### Initial examination

* The first step is to decide whether a peripheral or central cause of vertigo is likely. A central cause is suggested by hearing loss (Box 5.65), other

#### Box 5.64 Vertigo

Vertigo is the illusion of movement (e.g. the world spinning as if just getting off a playground roundabout) caused by disorders of the labyrinth or its central connections. The vestibular organ detects head motion, so abnormal activity in the vestibular nerves may be interpreted by the brain as self-motion. Nausea and vomiting often accompany vertigo since there are connections between the vestibular nuclei and brainstem centres mediating nausea and vomiting.

#### Box 5.65 Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL)

Sensorineural hearing loss is popularly attributable to an acoustic neuroma in the cerebellum angle. These are overall rare than perceived as a cause of SNHL and often slowly progressive. Other central causes, which may present abruptly, include demyelination and stroke, each described, but unusual causes of isolated SNHL. The commonest form of SNHL occurs insiduously with age. Sometimes, SNHL is 'diglobulin' (INSHU) and abrupt, postulated as viral, autoimmune or ischaemic and prompting a standard battery of haematobochemistry, inflammatory markers, autoimmune screening and a syphilis test. Magnetic resonance imaging is mandatory. SNHL is usually unilateral, occasionally bilateral if autoimmune, and has a variable prognosis. Adjacent cranial nerves are not affected. Treatment is with high-dose steroids and, where available, hyperbearic oxygen; acidori is recommended if herpes simplex or zoster are even remote possibilities.

As well as hearing loss, unilateral SNHL produces problems with localisation of sound as stereosound is lost, a problem that the brain may slowly adjust to.

SNHL is vital to distinguish from conductive hearing loss such as wax in the external ear or fluid in the middle ear, conductive hearing loss being much more amenable to treatment. Abrupt SNHL is an emergency.

#### The audiogram in SNHL

An audiogram has two axes, the x-axis on the top representing increasing frequency from 0 and 250 Hz (very low rumbles) on the left to 8000 Hz, the point at which humans start to stop hearing but beyond which dogs can still detect even higher frequencies or pitches, on the right. Little useful hearing in adults occurs beyond 6000 Hz and in age-related SNHL it is these higher frequencies that are impaired first of all. On the y-axis downwards is the intensity of sound, in decibels (dB), a logarithmic scale going from 0 at the top to around 100 at the bottom.

An audiologist in a silent room will produce sound at various frequencies and decibels to determine the minimum threshold in decibels that a sound is heard at a particular frequency. Gradually an average map is built up for each ear. As a very general rule, normal hearing detects most frequencies at around 0 dB but it is common to see the threshold a bit higher, at say 5-10 dB, with a dysphagia, dysphagia, ataxia, limb weakness) and headache.

#### Initial examination

* The first step is to decide whether a peripheral or central cause of vertigo is likely. A central cause is suggested by hearing loss (Box 5.65), other

#### Box 5.64 Vertigo

Vertigo is the illusion of movement (e.g. the world spinning as if just getting off a playground roundabout) caused by disorders of the labyrinth or its central connections. The vestibular organ detects head motion, so abnormal activity in the vestibular nerves may be interpreted by the brain as self-motion. Nausea and vomiting often accompany vertigo since there are connections between the vestibular nuclei and brainstem centres mediating nausea and vomiting.

#### Box 5.65 Sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL)

Sensorineural hearing loss is popularly attributable to an acoustic neuroma in the cerebellum angle. These are overall rare than perceived as a cause of SNHL and often slowly progressive. Other central causes, which may present abruptly, include demyelination and stroke, each described, but unusual causes of isolated SNHL. The commonest form of SNHL occurs insiduously with age.

As a general rule, sounds heard below 30 dB give rise to adequate hearing, sounds audible at 50-60 dB may be corrected to around 30 dB with a hearing aid, and sounds detected only below 50-60 dB are usually not amenable to treatment without significant sound distortion when sound is amplified.

The audiogram illustrates the amazia spectrum of frequency and loudness detected by the ear, even individual spoken words comprising a vast array of internal differences; hearing words is a function not just of the spectrum of capability of the hearing mechanism but also of an individual's learned interpretation of how these diverse sounds come together to form words.

#### Vertigo and SNHL

Vertigo, in a sense, represents a 'screaming', diseased nerve that settles when the nerve is more severely damaged and the brain adjusts. It is more likely continuous than paroxysmal when present. Longer term 'swimmingness' may occur if only one vestibular apparatus is working, especially when tired.

#### Tinnitus

Tinnitus is often ascribed to Meniere's disease but in fact is a common feature of any severe SNHL or damage to the vestibularochedar nerve. It may be a high-pitched whine or his or a washing machine whit. It can be alarmingly disabling, a fact often underpredicted since many benign noises in the ear such as awareness of the pulse are often inadvertently reported as tinnitus. Tinnitus may be due to the brain detecting background noise previously blotted out by normal hearing from ear structures; other theories suggest it is due to the hearing compartment in the brain still being receptive whilst its sensory nerve has dwindled, analogous to the sensory experiences of a phantom limb. Rehabilitation training can overcome tinnitus just as ticking clocks can become inaudible with time, and hearing aid technologies can help. neurological features or headache. Rubbing fingers close to the ears is a crude test of hearing (a formal audiogram is a necessary test if hearing loss is a possibility). Vertigo is not usually an isolated symptom in central causes as the brainstem is a tightly packed grid of long tract and cranial nerve fibres running up and down and across like busy New York City, making it unlikely for an insult to affect only the vestibular nerve or nucleus in isolation. If a peripheral cause is likely, the likely diagnoses are benign paroxysmal positional vertigo or acute vestibular neuronitis. Tell the examiners that you would perform a Dix-Hallpike test and a head impulse test (Box 5.66).

### Further assessment

* Further refine the cause of vertigo. Differentiating features are given in Table 5.55.
* Explore concerns.

Figure 5.100: Head impulse test.

Figure 5.99: Dix–Hallpike test. 

\begin{table}
\begin{tabular}{p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt} p{56.9pt}} \hline Type & Disease & Cause & Vertigo & Other features & Natural history \\ \hline Peripheral (three classic causes + rare causes, e.g. supernovae & & Common in later life (but may paroxysmal positional & & See Box 5.67 & Nausea common but vomiting unusual likely to be due to debris in inner canals & Responds to Epley manoeuvre if Du-Hallpike test positive \\ ear disease, vestibular & & Acute vestibular neutrits (Talphyrinitis’ or acute cases may respond to cinnarazine & Uncertain Viral prodrome in 50\% & Symptoms may persist for days or weeks & Nausea and vomiting Hearing loss unusual vestibular compensation, which precedes nerve regeneration Miller symptoms may recur but over subsequent weeks \\ \hline Menière’s disease & & Uncommon and cause unknown time & Vertigo at any time Chronic and insidious & Tinnitus Hearing loss & Tends to persist within days with compensation, which precedes nerve regeneration Miller symptoms may recur but over subsequent weeks \\ \hline Central & Disease of vestibular connections in brainstem (vertigo is isolated  cerebellar cerebellar recurrent disease and disease and causes should not be lumped with those of nystagmus) & & Vertigo & Red flags pointing to central rather than persists and does not prevent overlap of include: & Red flags pointing to central rather than \\ \hline \end{tabular}
\end{table}
Table 5.55: Causes of vertigo and differentiating features 

## Feedback to patient

You have vertigo, a condition that is usually caused by a problem affecting the nerve for balance inside the ear. Occasionally it is caused by a problem in the brain itself, but on the basis of your symptoms I think a problem arising in the ear is much more likely. It may well be due to a condition with a complicated name - benign paroxysmal positional vertigo - in which small crystals within the fluid in the ear are affecting its nerve signals and balance. This is easily diagnosed and often easily remedied by a series of head movements we could perform now. I'd be happy to try to answer any questions you may have.

### Box 5.67 Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV)

'Dizziness' is a common symptom, usually caused by presyncope, vertigo or disequilibrium. Balance is a complex mechanism with vestibular, visual and proprioceptive sensory inputs feeding into the brain itself, and with age neuronal reserve is often depleted. Factors that can predispose to balance problems also include depression, ischaemic heart disease, poly-pharmacy, diabetes, postural hypotension and any abnormal gait.

### Vestibular input to balance

Vestibular anatomy includes a utricle of semicircular canals (SCCs) positioned in three reciprocal pairs, and in the planes of the extraocular muscles. These are lined with hair cells and filled with endolymph fluid and each has a widened portion called the ampulla. The SCCs act as angular accelerometers, allowing the vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR) to keep the eyes stable when the head moves. Nerve signals from the hair cells sensing endolymph movement lead into audiovestibular nerve fibres and into the brain, along with information from the cochlear subserving hearing.

### BPPV

An abnormal VDR gives rise to the sign of nystagmus and the symptom of vertigo. BPPV is probably the most common cause of vertigo. It is a complex disorder, with subclassification based on the affected SCC. The posterior SCC is affected in most cases.

### Classic symptoms

BPPV is characterised by vertigo with nystagmus (character and direction depending upon the part of the inner ear affected) elicited by provocative positions. Classic BPPV is usually triggered by sudden movement from the erect to supine position while angling the head 45deg towards the side of the affected ear. Being in the provocative position is not enough - the head must actually move to it. After reaching the provocative position, a latency period of 1-40 seconds occurs before symptoms. When BPPV is triggered, patients feel thrown into a rolling spin toward the side of the affected ear. Symptoms start abruptly and dissipate within 10-60 seconds. This sensation is triggered again upon sitting erect but the direction of the nystagmus is reversed. The first onset is typically sudden. Many patients wake up with it. Symptoms are paroxysmal and positional, most patients experiencing them when rolling over in bed, or reaching upwards or putting their head back as at the dentist. Symptoms and signs fatigue with repeat testing. Attacks may continue for days to weeks, and occasionally months or years. Between attacks, there may be few or no symptoms but in extreme cases the slightest head movement may cause symptoms.

### Pathophysiology

Each inner ear contains three SCCs, which mediate spatial orientation, oriented in three perpendicular planes. Each canal consists of a tubular arm (curval) spotting from a large barrel-like compartment, like the handle sprouting from a mug. Each arm has a dilated (ampular) end near the top that houses the nerve receptors or stria amplularis. The crista amplularis has a sail-like tower, the cupula, that detects fluid (endolymph) flow within the SCC. If a person turns to the right, fluid within the right horizontal canal lags behind, causing the cupula to be deflected left towards the ampulla, translated into a nerve signal indicating the head is turning right. Simply, the cupula acts as a three-way switch, in the neutral position reflecting no motion, and in either direction producing a sensation of movement.

Particles (usually calcium carbonate crystals) in the canal can slow and even reverse movement of the cupula switch, creating signals incongruous with head movements. This mismatch results in vertigo. Two theories in BPPV explain what happens:

1. Cupololithiasis (cupula stones) refers to densities adhered to the cupula of the crista ampularis that are not free floating. The situation is analogous to a heavy object on a pole, the extra weight making the pole or cupula unstable and prone to clunking from side to side producing vertigo and nystagmus when tilted backward, especially when the posterior SCC is affected.
2. Canalithiasis (canal stones), proposed by Epley, refers to particles that are free floating and mobile in the posterior SCC. When the head is upright, the particles sit in the canal at the most gravity-dependent position but when tilted supine the particles rotate up along the arc before (after a momentary lag) gravity pulls them down again. This is analogous to pebbles inside a tyre, picked up momentarily when the tyre is rolled before tumbling down again with gravity. This tumbling of particles triggers fluid flow away from the ampulla causing cupula deflection and nerve-ending stimulation. This produces vertigo and nystagmus. Sitting back up causes reversal of cupular deflection with vertigo and nystagmus beating in the opposite direction. The canalithiasis theory better explains the latency, transient nystagmus and reversal on return to upright that is seen in dassic BPPV. Canlithiasis is probably the cause of most cases of BPPV.

Much of this is theory. Perhaps the simplest way to think about BPPV is that hair cells rest in SCCs like sea anemones on the seabed. The anemone tentacles move softly in a watery freeze and the direction they move tells you the direction of that freeze. The brain is able to determine precise body position by integrating information from many anemones in many different planes. If some little crabs or shells or pebbles get into the tentacles of an anemone then it will disrupt tentacle movement and send confusing signals to the brain.

### Causes

A few factors predispose patients to BPPV, including inactivity, trauma, ear disease (e.g. Meniere's disease, otosclerosis, sudden sensorineural hearing loss) and central disease. But age is a key factor, with increased otolith cell turnover. Both hair cells and audiovestibular nerve fibres degenerate with age.

### Diagnosis

The Dix-Hallplike test (Fig. 5.99) is best performed relatively quickly and with the patient's eyes focused on the nose of the examiner. This provides the best chance of crystal movement. A positive right Dix-Hallplike test is when the patient turns to the right and upbeating, right torsional (rotatory) nystagmus is noted in association with symptoms. By upbeating, right torsional nystagmus we mean that the top part of the iris is seen to flicker up and to the right. It is sometimes called geotropic nystagmus, which means that (in the position the patient is in) the movement is towards the ground. The nystagmus is sometimes referred to as clockwise or anticlockwise, but this is confusing. The opposite (downbeating, left torsional nystagmus) is seen when the patient sits up again. The converse applies for a positive left Dix-Hallplike test.

Classic BPPV involves the posterior SCC and is characterised by:

* the top pole of the eyes rotates with the fast phase toward the untormost (affected) ear
* Latency (a few seconds)
* Limited duration (< 20 seconds)
* Reversal upon return to upright position
* Response define upon repetitive provocation.

### Outline an investigation and management plan

Neuroimaging is indicated in vertigo if any of the following are present:

* Acute deafness
* Other neurological features

Purely horizontal nystagmus indicates horizontal canal involvement. Sustained or non-fatiguing nystagmus may indicate cupulithiasis. Because the type of BPPV is defined by the type of nystagmus, defining it is important.

### Treatment

A canalith repositioning procedure (CRP) is favoured treatment. Two major manoeuvres are Epley and Semont, with many minor variations of each. Both involve movements of the head to disperse particles. Semont involves rapid, vigorous, side-to-side head and body movements, and carries concern about complications of vertebral dissection or cervical damage. The Epley manoeuvre is gender. Research into multivarial positioning devices that can perform CRP in the proper plane of the SCCs is ongoing.

The Epley manoeuvre (Fig. 5.101) is as follows:

* Start from the neutral position.
* The patient is recitned slowly to the supine position of the affected side. The rate is titrated to the point of no nystagmus and no symptoms. This usually takes approximately 30 seconds. The patient is recitned further to the Dix-Hallplike position (15deg Trendelenburg, head turned 45deg towards the affected side). This usually takes 10 seconds. Another 20 seconds are spent in the Dix-Hallplike position with the affected ear down. Epley's first two steps are thus the same as a Dix-Hallplike test.
* The patient's head is turned slowly from position B towards the opposite, unaffected side; this moves crystals a little.
* The body is rolled towards that unaffected side so that the shoulders are aligned perpendicularly to the floor, and the affected ear is uppermost. The head looks 45deg below the plane of the horizon to the floor; this moves crystals a lot.
* The patient is raised back to the sitting position with the head still turned away from the affected side looking over the shoulder. Finally, the head is turned back to the midline.

A Dix-Hallplike test is performed immediately afterwards. If nystagmus is observed, the Epley manoeuvre may be repeated. Then, the patient is instructed to avoid lying completely flat and to avoid agitation of the head for around 48 hours while the particles settle.

Watchful waiting of BPPV may be reasonable if symptoms are infrequent and relatively non-troublesome. Vestbulousuppresent medication is seldom effective. Vestbular rehabilitation can have success after lengthy periods and may include Cawthorne exercises. Surgery is occasionally undertaken in those for whom CRP fails. your legs just give way. You are generally more rigid and your family has noticed that you tend to shuffle along with small steps. You are generally slower. On this occasion you find it difficult to mobilise. You are a little more forgetful. You have a history of hypertension. You have no pain. Your daughter is with you. You are both worried that you keep falling although expect that another urinary tract infection is to blame.

## Focused history and examination

### Initial history

Ask about duration of symptoms of falling. Ensure that there is no pain on this occasion suggesting a fracture (e.g. hip, public ramus, humerus). Ask about weakness and rigidity and slowing up (or down!) Ask about walking. Ask about memory and if there has been any forgetfulness. Ask about past medical history and drug history, noting the hypertension.

### Initial examination

* Examine gait if she is able and agrees.

### Further assessment

* Consider all causes of falls. The rule is that multiple causes are likely in any individual and if you find one, do not stop there but look for the next. In a frail older person insidious brain failure will collude with diminution of all facets of the neuromuscular system including sensory organs, and generalised sarcopenia.
* abbreviated mental test score (AMTS) or mini-mental state examination
- is useful, and here a modified AMTS should suffice.
* Take a brief social history.
* Explore concerns.

### Feedback to patient

I am not exactly sure just yet what is causing you to fall. But one of the concerns I have is that you have been becoming less able to use your legs properly over a considerable period of time. It may be that some hardening of the arteries in the brain is contributing in part to your tendency to balance trouble, and it may be that other factors are at play, too, such as nerves and muscles and joints generally becoming less efficient than they once were. And I am a little concerned to hear that your daughter feels that your memory is not as sharp as it used to be. I think at this stage we should keep an open mind and arrange a series of blood tests, a scan of the brain and a test of the memory, I also think we should ask our physiotherapists and occupational

Figure 5.101: Epley manoeuvre. therapists to assess your walking and balance and your overall safety on your feet. We should think, too, about whether or not we might build in some protective treatment for the bones to reduce any risk of fracture in the event that you were to fall again. I'd be happy to answer any questions you may have.

## Feedback to examiners

### Summarise the key history and examination findings

This woman has gait disturbance with an upright shuffling gait and some memory disturbance, and a history of hypertension.

### What subcortical VaD is

Subcortical VaD is a predominantly microvascular brain disease that has profound effects on gait and ultimately cognition.

### Why subcortical VaD is important

Subcortical VaD is a hugely underappreciated and ill-understood and yet exceedingly common 21st century health-care problem. It is one of the commonest reasons for admission (as 'off legs' or 'collapse') to medical assessment units and for delay in discharge from hospital. Despite this, formal recognition of this problem has not occurred, and patients continue to be diagnosed with alternative diagnoses such as urinary tract infections.

Whilst dementia due to Alzheimer's disease and other dementiates including other forms of VaD has attracted prominent attention, subcortical VaD has not. Unlike strokes (marcovascular brain disease), more infectious small-sweiser brain disease has received less attention. And yet microvascular disease in other organs is widely appreciated. Chronic kidney disease (CKD), usually due to underlying vascular disease from hypertension or diabetes, 's greatly understood, and CKD is even staged, and this underlying kidney vulnerability is often a background factor in episodes of acute kidney injury. Acute coronary syndromes often arise from chronic atherosclerotic disease. Yet the notion of brain vulnerability from underlying chronic microvascular brain disease is not widely recognised. The concepts of chronic kidney disease and heart failure are routinely appreciated, but the concepts of chronic brain disease and brain failure are given little emphasis, especially in older people with gait disturbance. When an older person falls, attention is rightly given to any comorbidities, drugs and environmental factors, and the legs are often examined to exclude musculoskeletal causes and injury. Urinalysis and heart and chest examination are often undertaken, but seldom consideration of brain disease. This is analogous to examining the wheels of a car that are not turning, without considering that the problem might have arisen at a higher control level.

### Why subcortical VaD is underappreciated

* Subcortical VaD is characterised by early gait disturbance and falls, and cognitive disturbance tends to be apparent later, and because the concept of a dementiating process is almost universally one of cognitive disturbance, when people present with a gait problem or falls a brain problem is understandably seldom considered.
* Subcortical VaD has become an accelerated problem as the population demographic has shifted to a much larger group of older people (notably those over 85 years of age).
* Subcortical VaD is not necessarily an easy disease to grasp without understanding the underlying pathology, the location of pathology and the clinical features that arise from that pathology.

### What we mean by 'subcortical'

Subcortical literally means 'beneath the cortex', like the core of an apple. White matter tends to predominate in these areas. 'Subcortical' refers to deep brain structures where there is much white matter with aggregates of grey matter.

White matter comprises bundles of myelinated nerve cell processes (axons) which connect areas of grey matter (nerve cell bodies) of brain to each other, carrying insulated nerve impulses rapidly between neurons. Whilst grey matter can be thought of as the computing areas of the brain, white matter represents the networks of cables connecting these. The brain can adapt somewhat to white matter damage by finding alternative routes. White matter forms the bulk of the deep parts of the brain, and parts of the spinal cord. Aggregates of grey matter such as the basal ganglia (e.g. caudate nucleus, putamen, globus pallidus, subthalamic nucleus) and brainstem nuclei (red nucleus, substantia nigra, cranial nerve nuclei) are spread within white matter.

Subcortical areas include:

* Components of the forebrain including the basal ganglia (motor control), thalamus (relay station for sensory information), limbic system and hypothalamus
* Components of the midbrain including the superior and inferior colliculi involved with eye movement and localisation and hearing, and the red nucleus, which aids motor control
* Components of the hindbrain, including the cerebellum, pons and medulla.

### Pathology in subcortical VaD

With ageing, loss of tissue density in grey matter (polioaraioisi) and white matter (leucoaraioisi) reflects neurodegenerative change, and progresses concurrent with decline in brain perfusion. Rates of polio- and leucoaraiosis accelerate after age 60, correlating with cortical and subcortical atrophy and ventricular enlargement. In particular, leucoaraiosis correlates with advancing age, hypoperfusion of white matter, and cognitive impairment. Leucaraiosis is detectable in some older 'normal' subjects but virtually always present in VaD, and normal subjects destined for later cognitive decline may have early excessive leucoaraiosis.

### Pathophysiology of subcortical VaD - lacunar state and arteriosclerosis (Binswanger's disease)

VaD refers to a broad category of cognitive decline attributed to cerebrovascular disease. Dementia may occur in approximately one-quarter to one-third of stroke cases. Strategic infarcts in critical areas (e.g. hippocampus, thalamus and caudate nucleus), and intracranial haematomas or mixed forms of VaD are well recognised. Subcortical VaD is due to subcortical ischaemic vascular disease (5IVD). Small-vessel disease and hypoperfusion result in focal and diffuse ischaemic white matter lesions and incorporate two pathophysiological mechanisms:

* Occlusion of an arterial lumen leading to complete infarct (lacunar infarct, often with lipophilosis or micro-leakage of blood) with disruption of a neural pathway
* Critical stenosis and hypoperfusion of multiple arterioles (arteriosclerosis or hypertensive microangiopathy) resulting in widespread areas of incomplete infarction of the deep white matter and consequent functional disruption of neural networks.

The end stages of the two pathways are the lacunar state and Binswanger's syndrome, which, in practice, usually converge. Cerebral autosomal dominant arteriopathy with subcortical infarcts and leucoencephalopathy (CADASIL) is a rare, familial cause of SVD in a younger age group, often with migraine history.

### Clinical features of subcortical VaD and how these are explained

#### 'Subcortical' areas relating to subcortical VaD

Various subcortical structures, including long motor and sensory tracts in transit between cortex and brainstem, and the extrapramidal system, which aids smooth motor output (and the cerebellum and thalamus, which aid smooth sensory input), generally become 'moth-eaten' by small-vessel disease. This leads to impaired neuronal information exchange between the outside world (via long tracts and cranial nerves) and the cortex. All parts of the body are represented cortically in the homunculus - face, arm and leg, for example, interestingly upside down and present in all animals, and so a little dormouse has a tail up at the top and smooth down below. There is both a motor and sensory homunculus bilaterally, the motor predominantly in the posterior frontal lobes and the sensory in the parietal lobes. The following analogy may be useful:

* Motor fibres start as millions of little roads in the cortex which join bigger highways as they descend downwards, eventually converging onto a motorway within the subcortex and internal capsule, before continuing their journey through the brainstem to supply the contralateral face, trunk and limbs.
* Sensory information, conversely, enters the brainstem and ascends through subcortical areas like the thalamus before being relayed onwards to the sensory areas of the cortex.
* unilateral weakness or sensory loss
- if affecting the internal capsule or thalamus. But multiple areas of microvascular damage in the subcortex from lacunar infarcts or arteriosclerosis produce a situation analogous to snow or ice on these motorways. Unless there is a pivotal motorway cerebrovascular 'accident', traffic is sluggish but does not stop unless the snow is intense (analogous to chronic kidney disease rather than acute kidney injury, or chronic angina rather than an acute coronary syndrome).

### Clinical features of subcortical VaD

Subcortical VaD therefore gives rise to the groups of clinical features in Table 5.56 and Fig. 5.102 - neurologically, a little of everything and not much overtly of anything. Clinical features are often insidious and significant decline over months once subcortical VaD is recognised is often the tip of a much longer developing iceberg - often a history of progressive gait problems and short-term memory loss over a few years, but gait disturbance tends to precede cognitive disturbance. In the author's experience the mean age of recognition of this type of dementia is 88-89 years.

There is no definition or set of criteria for diagnosing subcortical VaD. Cerebrovascular disease is common in older people and causes heterogeneous impairments, which range in severity. Definitions of cerebrovascular cognitive impairment are hampered by the fact that cerebrovascular disease is not easily linked to cognitive syndromes either clinically or pathologically and the presence of coincident Alzheimer's pathology is common. Furthermore, there are no universally accepted diagnostic criteria for Alzheimer's dementia and many proposed ones for other forms of VaD; these tend to be used in research but not in clinical practice.

### Why these features are not Parkinson's disease

These patients are slow and rigid and so it is commonly suggested that they might have Parkinson's disease (PD). In fact subcortical VaD embraces what used to be known as vascular Parkinsonism. Here are four differences from PD:

1. Older age (average age of onset of PD 63)
2. Leg failure more than arm failure (PD patients have arm problems more than leg problems and do not present 'off legs' until many years down the track), with an upright rather than stopped shuffling posture
3. Symmetry (PD patients have asymmetric disease)
4. Non-sustained response to levodopa (if tried).

Furthermore, despite traditional thinking, subcortical VaD is much more common than PD. If subcortical traffic is slow in subcortical VaD, and the legs are affected more than the arms, one might ask why in PD the arms are affected more than the legs (albeit all four limbs being affected to some degree in both conditions). One thought is based on primate evolution - that swinging and fine motor control in the hands and arms is more important than walking. If this is so, then one might expect the arms to fare worse in PD - largely an extrayramidal biochemical disturbance, and one might expect the legs to fare worse in subcortical VaD because upper limb nerve fibres would have a more generous blood supply. Of course another reason is that subcortical VaD is not a direct replacement in terms of location to PD but with different pathology, but rather may affect areas of gait control unaffected in PD. We simply do not know.

### Subcortical vascular disease versus normal ageing

Neuroimaging often shows small-vessel disease and atrophy, often reported as normal for age. But this is not so. What we are seeing is chronic brain disease, potentially ultimately leading to brain failure. This is no difference in importance from conditions we recognise routinely such as CKD and heart failure and, even where asymptomatic, we would not refer to stage 2 CKD or a reduced cardiac ejection fraction as normal. We might say these features are not unexpected for age, but that is not the same as saying they are not pathological, and these features are by no means inevitable with age and the causes might be modifiable.

### Treatment for subcortical VaD

There is no theoretical reason why risk factor intervention, notably treating hypertension, might not offer benefits that are preventive or delay progression, for both gait and cognitive features, but research is lacking. Diagnosis enables appropriate involvement of the multidisciplinary team. Diagnosis is important to patient and family, as is appreciation that preceding non-cognitive problems such as gait disturbance and falls are part of a dementia process before cognitive disturbance prevails. This allows advance planning of care and personal affairs. Recognising and sharing with the patient and family that consequences of dementia such as muscle wasting, falls, poor nutrition and pneumonia may be explained by brain disease, rather than being the prime reasons for decline in a health-care setting, is vital.

### Important tips

Tip 1: Use precise terminology - not 'off legs' or 'collapse'

These are imprecise terms, and never diagnoses. It may be useful to consider 'off legs'/collapse within a 'falls - unable to stand' diagnostic framework.

Tip 2: Have a differential diagnosis list for falls

Falls may occur without or with loss of consciousness (often syncopal). Most falls are without loss of consciousness and in older people multifactorial, with both intrinsic and environmental factors at play. Clinicians should consider whether features of subcortical VaD are present.

Tip 3: Appreciate the role of the brain in 'off legs' patients

Urinary infections and other 'simple' diagnoses should not be applied liberally without appreciation of what else might be going on. It is implausible that a positive urine dip without symptoms, without evidence of systemic upset and without a rise in inflammatory markers could produce falls, substantial confusion or loss of consciousness. Figure 5.102: Subcortical vascular dementia (numbers 1, 2 and 3 refer to Table 5.56).

Weakness and rigidity with prominent gait disturbance & An upright ‘marche a petit pas’ gait with small, shuffling steps may occur (‘vascular’ perfusion’) before mobility stops altogether (an ‘off legs’ scenario) & The critical mass of long motor tracts damaged is sufficient to produce generalised weakness but insufficient to produce overt focal upper motor neuron signs of pyramidal weakness (all motorways on all routes are ‘narrowed to one or two lanes’ in subcortical dementia rather than completely blocked on one route as in a stroke) \\ This, in concert with wider subcortical damage including damage to basal ganglia, produces gait disturbance & This, in concert with wider subcortical damage including damage to basal ganglia, produces gait disturbance \\ Rigidity (and facial hypomínia) & Some rigidity is present due to extrapyramidal damage, similar to that seen in Parkinson’s disease, although Parkinson’s disease has a unique pattern of progression and tends to affect a younger age group \\

The thesis is based on the work of the 

## BEFORE PACES

### Timing

1. Many candidates feel that MRCP exams should be sat (and failed) as often as possible in the belief that passing will be the reward of negative but repetitive experienced With the right preparation you can easily pass PACES. Take it when you feel ready and do not listen to pessimistic colleagues.

### What the examiners are looking for

2. The examiners want to be satisfied that you are a safe, competent doctor, suitable to enter specialist training.
3. They are not looking for specialist knowledge.
4. They are not looking for the next Professor of Medicine.
5. They want to know that, as your general medical consultant, they could have a sensible discussion with you about the care of a patient.
6. They want to know that, as your consultant, they could entrust the care of their patients to you when away from the wards.
7. They respect a friendly and confident manner, but not arrogance.
8. Many candidates adopt 'victim mode' with head down and victim posture throughout PACES. This concerns examiners, who wonder how a nervous candidate might cope in an emergency. Examiners are likely to be more impressed by the candidate who stands with an upright, confident posture, hands by sides or behind back, and makes good eye contact.

### It's in your hands!

1. Passing PACES is well within your grasp.
2. The idea that passing is in your examiners' hands is a negative one.
3. It is in your hands!
4. You can pass with the right preparation.
5. Success is about discipline and desire. You must work to pass, and want to pass.
6. Passing is easier than you think and you probably need to know less than you think. Reading larger textbooks is not a good use of time. Seeing patients is.

### Practice

1. Most candidates are competent enough to pass PACES by the exam date. Invariably lack of confidence is the problem. The aim of practice is to build a natural confidence into your already adequate competence.
2. Practise your examination technique for each system at every opportunity (whenever possible in front of senior colleagues).
3. Practise the history-taking skills taught in this book with patients at every opportunity.
4. Practice your communication skills at every opportunity. As well as discussing management and ethical dilemmas with patients and colleagues, some candidates find it helpful to form discussion groups.
5. Be alert to any topical issues in the news and in general medical journals that could be examined and discussed.
6. Read widely. Reading strengthens your communication prowess.
7. Practice does not automatically make perfect. Perfect practice does! 

## Appendix - 100 tips for passing PACES

When you go into the exam you should appear as though you have performed your examination of each system hundreds of times before, ideally because you have!

## ON THE DAY OF PACES

### Some formalities

* PACES requires sustained energy and you must not be so tired through stress and studying as to lose momentum on the day. Meerkats get plenty of sleep each night to be alert and vigilant each day! Try not to work continuously in the run-up to PACES. Have some early nights and avoid caffeine and alcohol.
* Dress professionally.
* Arrive at the exam venue in good time.
* Take your stethoscope. Other equipment should be provided but you may prefer to carry your own ophthalmoscope, tendon hammer and red pin. A case of 'James Bond' gadgets will not impress examiners.
* Thank your patients and examiners after each case.

### At the start

* Keep in mind that there should be nothing particularly rare and therefore nothing to be particularly scared of throughout the five stations. However, even good candidates find some cases much more difficult than others. A few poor performances can still mean an overall pass and you should be determined from the start to put any difficult cases behind you and give the next one, with different examiners, your best shot.

### The patients are more important than the examiners!

* The patients are your prime concern. Thinking that the outcome of the exam is in the hands of the examiners is not helpful. The examiners will pass or fail you on the strengths and weaknesses of your performances with patients. Focus on your patients and the exam will take care of itself.
* Do not hurt patients on any account! This is one of the easiest ways to fail PACES.
* Be considerate to patients. This is one of the easiest ways to pass PACES. It may seem obvious, but the notion of treating other people as we would like to be treated ourselves is a remarkably effective guiding principle in PACES, as it is in medical practice.

### Examiners

* Examiners come with the full range of personalities. The best advice is not to worry about what sort of examiners you get. Focus instead on doing what you do as well as you can do it.
* The examiners are not trying to catch you out but want to find out what you know.

### Examining patients

* Be nice to patients! This is worth reiterating. Treat them with respect at all times. A considerate (but not obsequious) approach to patients is the single biggest determinant of success.
* Ask each patient for permission to examine and if they are in any pain.
* Ensure that each patient is positioned correctly before you begin.
* Observation is a vital part of examination!
* Don't wait for the examiners to keep telling you what to do. Do it spontaneously, using the _Recognition-Interpretation-Discussion (RID)_ sequence.
* Don't look for feedback from the examiners. You will not get it, and looking for it will unnecessarily unnerve you. A good candidate, without appearing to rush, will elicit signs and gather information considerably, swiftly and spontaneously.
* Remember that you are 'on stage'. Let the examiners see your performance. As in a driving test, your actions should be natural but clearly visible.
* Remember that from the examiner's perspective you do two things in each case. Firstly, you _examine_ your patient. Secondly, you _present_ your findings. From your perspective, you are doing much more. While examining, you are looking for signs, deciding how best to interpret these and thinking about how you are going to present your findings. A structured approach is essential. The examination schemes in this book can be performed automatically with practice, allowing you to concentrate on recognising and interpreting signs and to use this same framework for presenting.
* At the end of each case ensure that your patient is as comfortable as you found them.
* Don't forget to thank them.

### Examining and presenting

* such that even if their final diagnosis is incorrect, they may at least have reported some correct findings. This is risky.
* A running commentary slows you down and fragments the smooth flow of your examination. Examiners do not want to be told that you are examining the pulse to check whether it is collapsing. They want to see you doing it. Examiners are human and easily bored. They will forgive you for omitting things here and there but may be irritated if you do not 'just get on with it'. Constant stops and starts are more likely to draw the attention of examiners to mistakes. Conversely, candidates with competent examination skills who 'get on with it' tend to impress examiners, often lulling them into a sense of security - switching them from hawk to dove mode and blinding them to mistakes.
46. Talking while examining interferes with your _rapport_ with a patient. You may think rapport is something you cannot develop within a few minutes but patients in PACES generally have a very good idea of how well a candidate performs, simply because what impresses patients is what impresses examiners - a natural, considerate and considered approach. Be attentive to your patient, not the examiners, while examining. Be completely absorbed by your patient. Tell them what you are doing as you are doing it, smile, look and listen for the subtle cues of cooperation, and above all show that you care. Rapport will come naturally and your examiners, at this stage, should fade into the background.
47. The 'absence' of the examiners allows you to pool all of your mental energy into the case, and should reduce anxiety.
48. The opposite applies when presenting your case. Be decisive in finishing your examination and politely turn away from your patient to give your undivided attention to the examiners. At this point, some candidates are so relieved if they have a diagnosis that they blurt it out and wait for feedback - 'Graves' disease! Talk through the _Recognition-Interpretation_ sequence instead.
49. When presenting, avoid lots of 'ums' and avoid reporting lists of negative findings.
50. Conversely, if you have elicited a sign, mention it. Candidates surprisingly often fail to mention signs they have clearly elicited. Do not assume the examiners know that you have elicited a sign.
51. Never make up signs to conform to an anticipated diagnosis. It is a sure way to fail.
52. If you know the diagnosis, state it and then give the relevant supporting findings.
53. If you do not know the diagnosis, describe what you found.
54. The key to impressing examiners is in ever striving 'to go further'. Examiners prefer candidates who spontaneously present their findings and thoughts.
55. Candidates who lack confidence often look back at their patient when presenting or asked questions as if the findings or answers will magically appear. Try to avoid this.
56. Avoid uncomfortable silences (where no marks can be awarded) by saying something intelligent. For example, if you find a mass in the left upper quadrant and are not sure if it is a spleen, you could describe findings you might expect in support of it being a spleen and findings you might expect in support of it being a kidney. You could also suggest an ultrasound scan to resolve the uncertainty.
57. The key is to try to keep talking and saying sensible things.
58. Don't always expect a full house of signs. Patients are not from textbooks and examiners frequently disagree about signs before the exam starts. Try to keep calm. Simply blurring out a diagnosis about which you are unsure is hazardous. Remember that describing your findings, even if your diagnosis is incorrect, will allow points to be awarded.
59. There will always be cases where the diagnosis is not clear. Report your findings, telling the examiners which other signs you might have expected to find in support of the important differential diagnoses and what else you could do to resolve the uncertainty. Examiners seldom fail candidates who can demonstrate logical approaches to dilemmas. What often happens is that candidates fail themselves by 'going blank' when they are uncertain.
60. _PACES for the MRCP_ has tried to strike a balance between structure and flexibility. It provides a framework for diseases likely to be encountered in PACES, but every patient is different. The same disease is exhibited in different ways in different patients and its impact is individually determined. You should tailor your knowledge to specific patients. _PACES for the MRCP_ is not a substitute for seeing as many patients as possible on the wards, and _Recognition-Interpretation-Discussion_ is not something to which you need always adhere. It is simply a map to guide you through cases and bring you back on course if things go wrong.
61. Remember that 'common things are common! You will have seen most things before.
62. Remember that double pathology can occur.
63. Diagnoses can be difficult. Many candidates struggle especially with neurology and heart valve cases. In cases where you are unsure: **(i)**: Describe what you found. Be honest. Never make up signs. **(ii)**: Tell the examiner two or three differential diagnoses. **(iii)**: Describe what else you might have expected to find in support of these. **(iv)**: State some things you could do to help resolve the issue, e.g. dynamic auscultation, arrange echocardiogram.
64. Before this point, many candidates have failed themselves, believing the signs too confusing and their diagnosis to be wrong. Practice often falls far short of theory. 

## Appendix - 100 tips for passing PACES

### History-taking skills

Use the history-taking skills described on Station 2.

#### Communication skills

* Awareness of the history-taking and communication skills described in this book allows you to further improve your history-taking and communication skills.
* Watch good communicators at work. See how they handle situations.
* Try to bring discussion with examiners to life. Add sparkle by appearing enthusiastic and interested in the topic for discussion.
* Watching politicians and debate programmes on television is a way of seeing negotiation at work. You do not have to like the politicians or agree with their arguments but take an interest in how they communicate. They are usually effective speakers.
* that of asking yourself what you would want if you were in your patient's situation.

### Answering examiners' questions

* Listen to the question!
* Answer the question asked!
* Try not to repeat the question asked!
* Take a moment or two before answering it.
* Look at the examiner and not at the floor. Eye contact is very important.
* Try to appear confident, yet reflective, giving a well-structured answer. Your examiners will be as impressed by your communication skills as your knowledge.
* Speak clearly and succinctly. Try to avoid speaking too quickly, and rambling.
* Some candidates speak very quietly. It is unusual for candidates to speak very loudly. Unless you are one of the latter, it may help to speak up slightly, more _authoritatively_.
* Do not think of the examiners as examiners. This is easier said than done. Try to think of the examiners as colleagues with whom you are discussing interesting ward cases. Some candidates find it helpful to imagine themselves on a ward round.
* Curiosity keeps us alert. Let your enthusiasm, curiosity and interest shine through.
* Difficult questions often reflect a good performance. Examiners will challenge and probe and sometimes make you feel uncomfortable or inadequate.
* Be confident, if you think you are right, but never argue!
* Be confident, not arrogant.
* Avoid casual phrases and abbreviations.
* Avoid emotive words like 'tumour' in front of patients.
* Do not be afraid to retract statements you feel are wrong.
* If you do not know the answer to a question, be honest in your ignorance.
* Above all, avoid saying anything that would pose a danger to patients.
* 'Would you like to explain that? You have some opportunity to take control of the areas to be discussed.

### Answering examiners' questions at the communication skills and ethics station

* Don't be too dogmatic. It can be dangerous to go down one route when discussing ethical dilemmas. It is wiser to state the broad range of issues an ethical dilemma creates, and that there are often numerous viewpoints to consider.
* Remember that you are not expected to have all the answers. Examiners may be impressed by a mature attitude of self-awareness and a need to discuss an issue further with colleagues, a medical defence organisation and so on. Willingness to communicate with colleagues is considered a strength.
* While there is often no right or wrong answer, you can state what you think is the best course of action. Examiners may challenge you but it does not mean that your argument is flawed. Provided you feel that your argument is 'safe', run with your convictions. The examiners are usually checking for consistency of your argument, ensuring that it is genuinely your belief, and that you can justify it.
* Sometimes examiners' questions will be hard! They may be searching, probing and uncomfortable. Again, this does not necessarily mean that you are doing anything wrong. It may mean that you are performing well.
* Try to be 'patient centred' in your responses.

### When you think things are going badly

* Do not expect feedback from your examiners. They do not give it. This said, a confident, respectful and competent candidate with a friendly but not overly familiar manner is someone examiners warm to immediately.
* Difficult cases may be genuinely difficult to all candidates (and to examiners!).
* Difficult questions often reflect a good performance!
* Examiners may play on your nerves and ask 'Are you sure?' in response to your answers. Often, they are simply testing your confidence and your answer is correct.
* Each station and case is marked separately. Try not to lose hope if you feel you have performed badly in a particular case. Examiners allow numerous mistakes (provided these would not have overtly dangerous clinical consequences) and a poor performance does not necessarily result in overall failure.
